Analyst-Identified Gaps - All five benchmarks are offline, curated datasets with known gold answers; no evaluation in live multi-agent, streaming, or production tool-calling settings is provided, leaving real-world latency and correctness behavior unverified. - No statistical significance testing is reported anywhere; given small absolute differences between systems on some benchmarks (e.g., LoCoMo EM scores uniformly below 12 for most systems), several findings may not be robust to noise. - Potential author-affiliation bias: MemOS, A-MEM, and several reference systems originate from SJTU or MemTensor, which are the authors' institutions, but the paper does not acknowledge or control for this. - The LLM backbone ablation tests only 6 systems × 4 backbones on a single benchmark (LoCoMo); the interaction between backbone and architecture type is not factorial, so conclusions about backbone robustness are partially confounded. - Operational cost analysis omits memory/storage footprint, which is critical for edge and mobile deployment where RAM is the binding constraint, not latency. - The study captures a snapshot of 12 systems as of mid-2026; the preprint does not acknowledge that the benchmark and system set may already be dated relative to faster-moving open-source development in this area. Bottom Line Any practitioner selecting, designing, or benchmarking agent memory infrastructure will gain directly actionable findings here that are unavailable elsewhere — specifically the workload-to-architecture mapping, the operational scaling rule, and the evidence that conservative maintenance outperforms aggressive summarization — making this the most useful empirical reference in the space despite its lack of statistical rigor and real-deployment validation.

CLI-Universe: Towards Verifiable Task Synthesis Engine for Terminal Agents

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CLI-Universe, a three-stage synthesis pipeline that generates high-quality, verifiable terminal-agent training tasks by combining structured capability taxonomy, evidence-guided deep research over real technical materials, and multi-stage executable verification, producing CLI-Universe-6K, a dataset of 6,000 distilled trajectories. - Why it's important: Open-source terminal agents lag far behind proprietary systems on benchmarks like Terminal-Bench 2.0, and the bottleneck is not model capacity but training data quality; existing synthesis pipelines scale task volume without guaranteeing that tasks are genuine, non-trivial, or unambiguously verifiable. - How it does it: Each task candidate is first defined by sampling a four-dimensional taxonomy (domain, skill type, capability, engineering pillar), grounded in specific tools and failure modes via an agentic deep research pass over real repositories and documentation, then instantiated in a Docker environment and subjected to role-separated test and solution agents with rubric-gated test construction, hint-conditional filtering, and fail-to-pass checking. - The good: Fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on only 6,000 CLI-Universe trajectories achieves 33.4% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, outperforming all open-source models at or below 32B parameters and surpassing several open-weight models an order of magnitude larger (e.g., 480B Qwen3-Coder at 23.9%, 1T Kimi-K2-Instruct at 27.8%). - The missing: The dataset contains only 6,000 trajectories across domains defined by human curation, and the quality ceiling of both task synthesis and solution generation is bounded by the capability of the underlying LLM agents, so systematic blind spots in the frontier models propagate into the training set. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on terminal-agent training data, agentic benchmarking, or synthetic data pipelines; the evidence-guided refinement and multi-stage verification design translate directly to other agentic domains beyond CLI.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CLI-Universe, a three-stage synthesis pipeline that generates high-quality, verifiable terminal-agent training tasks by combining structured capability taxonomy, evidence-guided deep research over real technical materials, and multi-stage executable verification, producing CLI-Universe-6K, a dataset of 6,000 distilled trajectories. - Why it's important: Open-source terminal agents lag far behind proprietary systems on benchmarks like Terminal-Bench 2.0, and the bottleneck is not model capacity but training data quality; existing synthesis pipelines scale task volume without guaranteeing that tasks are genuine, non-trivial, or unambiguously verifiable. - How it does it: Each task candidate is first defined by sampling a four-dimensional taxonomy (domain, skill type, capability, engineering pillar), grounded in specific tools and failure modes via an agentic deep research pass over real repositories and documentation, then instantiated in a Docker environment and subjected to role-separated test and solution agents with rubric-gated test construction, hint-conditional filtering, and fail-to-pass checking. - The good: Fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on only 6,000 CLI-Universe trajectories achieves 33.4% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, outperforming all open-source models at or below 32B parameters and surpassing several open-weight models an order of magnitude larger (e.g., 480B Qwen3-Coder at 23.9%, 1T Kimi-K2-Instruct at 27.8%). - The missing: The dataset contains only 6,000 trajectories across domains defined by human curation, and the quality ceiling of both task synthesis and solution generation is bounded by the capability of the underlying LLM agents, so systematic blind spots in the frontier models propagate into the training set. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on terminal-agent training data, agentic benchmarking, or synthetic data pipelines; the evidence-guided refinement and multi-stage verification design translate directly to other agentic domains beyond CLI. Problem & Motivation: LLM-based terminal agents must execute multi-step workflows in real command-line environments, but open-source models remain substantially weaker than proprietary systems on benchmarks like Terminal-Bench 2.0 (33.4% for the best open-source 32B model vs. 57.8% for Claude-Opus-4.5). The gap is not primarily a capacity problem: as the paper shows, base Qwen3-32B scores only 3.4% on TB-2.0 without targeted training data. Existing synthesis pipelines address the volume problem by converting surface-level artifacts (repository code, Docker traces, documentation templates) into tasks, but produce ambiguous instructions, shallow execution paths, and brittle tests that provide weak learning signal even when they run successfully. The result is that adding more trajectories from these pipelines yields diminishing returns, because failures and non-solutions pollute the supervision signal. Key Idea: The core insight is that task quality, not task volume, is the binding constraint, and that quality can be systematically enforced by (a) generating each task from a structured capability specification so it exercises a specific, identifiable skill, (b) grounding each specification in real technical evidence so the task is realistic rather than abstract, and (c) applying a multi-stage executable filter that independently verifies both the task itself and the solution trajectory, retaining only examples where an unsolved environment demonstrably transitions to a solved state. Methodology: CLI-Universe operates in three stages. Stage 1 (Task Blueprint Construction) seeds task ideation by sampling anchor points across four orthogonal dimensions: Domain (16 categories from Software Engineering to Cryptography), Skill Type (8 categories from Algorithmic to Deployment), Capability (11 reasoning behaviors such as Error Recovery, Long-Horizon Planning, Constraint Satisfaction), and Engineering Pillar (6 activity types such as Debugging, DevOps, Feature Creation). For each anchor, an LLM brainstorms task ideas scored on creativity, technical groundedness, and feasibility; top candidates proceed to evidence-guided refinement, where a dedicated research agent iteratively searches repositories, documentation, issue threads, and usage examples to ground the task in specific tools, realistic constraints, known failure modes, and concrete input/output contracts. Refined candidates are compiled into blueprints (instruction, internal hint, environment checklist) and validated by human and LLM judges using quality rubrics covering clarity, difficulty, and robustness; acceptance rate rises from 72% to 91% (human) and 75% to 93% (LLM) post-rubric review. Stage 2 (Environment Realization) materializes each blueprint into a Docker image: assets are acquired from public sources and adapted (format normalization, fault injection, parameter adjustment) or synthesized from scratch when unavailable; environments undergo smoke tests for dependency installation, service startup, and filesystem layout. Stage 3 (Executable Verification) applies three sequential filters: (i) role-separated test construction, where a test agent and a solution agent operate independently (neither sees the other's output) and the test agent iteratively refines tests against correctness, determinism, and edge-case rubrics; (ii) hint-conditional filtering, where a hint-free agent attempt that succeeds causes the task to be discarded as trivially solvable, retaining only tasks where the hint is genuinely necessary; (iii) fail-to-pass checking, where the synthesized tests must fail on the initial environment and pass after executing the hinted solution trajectory. End-to-end retention is 33.6%: 30% of ideas are rejected at ideation, 14% yield invalid blueprints, 14% fail environment construction, and 8.4% are filtered by fail-to-pass. Training uses multi-turn supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on Kimi-K2.6-generated trajectories with Qwen3 (8B/14B/32B) students, 32 NVIDIA H200 GPUs, AdamW with peak LR 1e-5, 5 epochs, 64K context. Key Results: CLI-Universe-32B achieves 33.4% avg@4 on Terminal-Bench 2.0 under the Terminus 2 scaffold with 200 turns, outperforming SkillSynth-32B (29.6%), Nemotron-Terminal-32B (27.4%), TerminalTraj-32B (22.0%), and the 480B Qwen3-Coder (23.9%) and 1T Kimi-K2-Instruct (27.8%). Gains are monotonically increasing with model size: 8B achieves 10.9 (+8.4 over Qwen3-8B baseline of 2.5), 14B achieves 23.0 (+19.0 over 4.0), 32B achieves 33.4 (+30.0 over 3.4). On out-of-domain benchmarks, CLI-Universe-32B improves over Qwen3-32B by +11.3 on BFCL v4 (58.0 vs 46.7) and +11.6 on VitaBench (27.0 vs 15.4). Per-category breakdown on TB-2.0 shows largest gains in Data Processing (+62.5), Machine Learning (+50.0), Data Querying (+50.0), and Model Training (+43.8); Video Processing and Games show no gain. The proprietary gap remains large: Claude-Opus-4.5 scores 57.8% on TB-2.0. Ablation on a 1k-task subset with Qwen3-32B shows the full pipeline scores 26.7; removing the asset strategy costs 6.2 points (down to 20.5), removing query rubrics costs 3.4 points (down to 23.3), and removing test-case rubrics costs 3.9 points (down to 22.8). Success-only filtering (6k trajectories) outperforms keeping all 10k trajectories including failures and incomplete runs by +5.2 points (33.4 vs 28.2), confirming quality filtering dominates volume. Teacher model comparison: Kimi-K2.6 reaches 33.4 and DeepSeek-V4-Pro reaches 31.2 with matched trajectory counts, indicating the pipeline is not locked to a specific frontier model. Data scaling shows no saturation at 6,000 trajectories on Qwen3-8B. Synthesized tests agree with Terminal-Bench 2 official tests at 91% pass rate (official solutions on CLI-Universe tests) and 88% semantic match (Codex/GPT-5.4 judge). Evidence-guided refinement increases task difficulty by 3.45× in solver turns and reduces pass rate by 13.3 points relative to unrefined tasks. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuinely novel element is the combination of structured capability specification with evidence-guided deep research and role-separated multi-stage verification; each component individually has precedents (taxonomy-based generation in SkillSynth and Nemotron, Docker-based environments in TerminalTraj, fail-to-pass checking in code synthesis), but the integration of an agentic evidence-grounding pass that grounds abstract capability anchors in specific real-world technical constraints before environment construction has not been demonstrated in prior terminal-agent synthesis work. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover three pipeline components and two data-side choices (trajectory selection and teacher model), each isolated cleanly. The comparison of synthesized tests against Terminal-Bench 2 official ground truth (91% pass rate, 88% semantic match) provides external validation of test quality. Error attribution uses an independent LLM judge (Codex/GPT-5.4) on two rollout attempts per task. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported on any TB-2.0 numbers; avg@4 reduces variance but no confidence intervals are given. The CLI-Universe-6K dataset and pipeline code are not released as of the preprint; no repository URL is stated, which limits reproducibility. The ablation is conducted on a 1k-task subset rather than the full 6k, which may not fully represent the pipeline's behavior at scale. The paper is a preprint (arXiv 2606.22883v1, 2026-06-23) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — the paper describes a built synthesis pipeline and evaluates fine-tuned models by deployment on Terminal-Bench. - Practical: The fine-tuned models (Qwen3-8B through 32B) run on commercially available hardware; the 32B model was trained on 32 NVIDIA H200 GPUs, which is large but accessible at cloud providers. - Efficient: The synthesis pipeline discards two-thirds of candidates, making generation cost approximately 3× the cost of the retained data; pipeline cost per retained trajectory is not stated, and the compute cost of evidence-guided refinement (multi-agent research loops) is not quantified. - Effective: The models demonstrably improve on TB-2.0 by 30 points at 32B scale, outperform 10-100× larger open-source baselines, and transfer to two out-of-domain benchmarks; the evidence for effectiveness is strong within the tested scope. - Scalable: The data scaling curve for Qwen3-8B shows no saturation at 6,000 trajectories, suggesting the pipeline can continue generating useful supervision; whether the pipeline itself scales to 100k+ trajectories without degradation in task quality or diversity is not tested or analyzed. The paper delivers the CLI-Universe-6K dataset as an artifact; the pipeline code and fine-tuned model weights are not released in this preprint. Strengths: - The fail-to-pass + hint-conditional filtering combination is a principled approach to measuring task non-triviality and solution validity simultaneously, avoiding the common failure mode of keeping easy tasks that provide no learning signal. - The evidence-guided refinement step is empirically validated to increase genuine difficulty (3.45× solver turns, -13.3 pass rate), distinguishing it from taxonomy expansion that merely broadens surface topic coverage without raising task quality. - The role-separated test and solution agents (neither sees the other's output) directly address the test-contamination problem in self-supervised pipeline design, where the solution agent could otherwise write tests that trivially pass its own approach. - The error attribution study on TB-2.0 failure modes (9-mode taxonomy across Execution, Coherence, Verification) provides actionable diagnostic information: CLI-Universe-32B's failure profile shifts from Verification-dominated (frontier models: 47-60%) to Execution-dominated (44%), with step repetition at 23% vs 0-7% for frontiers, identifying the specific capability gap that remains. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note three limitations. First, all pipeline stages — ideation, environment construction, solution generation, and test construction — rely on LLM agents, so the quality ceiling of the synthesized data is bounded by the capability of the underlying frontier models. Second, CLI-Universe narrows but does not close the gap to proprietary systems (33.4% vs 57.8% for Claude-Opus-4.5 on TB-2.0), and whether larger open-source base models or reinforcement learning can close this gap is unexplored. Third, the current dataset is only 6,000 trajectories, and scaling the pipeline to larger and more diverse task pools may unlock further gains but is left to future work. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Compute cost of the synthesis pipeline is never reported. Given that evidence-guided refinement involves multi-agent research loops and two-thirds of candidates are discarded, the cost per retained trajectory could be substantial relative to simply sampling more trajectories from a teacher model; this matters for reproducibility and practical adoption. - The ablation is conducted on a 1k-task subset and reports TB-2.0 scores on Qwen3-32B only; it is unclear whether the relative importance of pipeline components holds at 8B and 14B scales or at the full 6k trajectory count. - No baseline compares CLI-Universe-6K against a matched-volume sample of trajectories from Kimi-K2.6 on pre-existing high-quality task sets (e.g., hand-curated Terminal-Bench 1 tasks), so it is not possible to isolate how much of the gain comes from the synthesis pipeline versus the teacher model and SFT recipe. - The Video Processing and Games categories show zero gain at 32B, but the paper does not analyze why; if the taxonomy does not produce diverse-enough tasks in these categories, the failure is a systematic gap in coverage rather than a model limitation. - Human evaluation of blueprint quality uses accept/reject rubrics but not calibration on inter-annotator agreement, so the 91% human accept rate carries unknown variance. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or training terminal agents gains immediately actionable methodology from this work: the evidence-guided grounding + role-separated verification design is the most disciplined synthesis pipeline yet demonstrated for this domain, and the 30-point gain at 32B on only 6,000 trajectories makes a strong case that task quality dominates task volume; the lack of released code or weights is the main obstacle to direct adoption.

Neuro-Symbolic Artificial Intelligence: A Reference Model for the Design and Development of Neuro-Symbolic Systems

brandoncolelough.com · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a descriptive Neuro-Symbolic Reference Model — comprising a common architecture, an ordinal Level 0–7 integration scheme, six role-preserving architectural families, and a build-order argument — derived empirically from 85 independently reproduced Neuro-Symbolic systems. - Why it's important: The Neuro-Symbolic AI field currently lacks a grounded reference model that can guide design decisions; existing surveys describe themes and trends but cannot establish which architectural choices are necessary for a system to function, since they rest on unverified self-reports rather than systems that were actually rerun. - How it does it: The authors conduct a PRISMA-guided systematic literature review across nine databases, apply a same-artifact reproducibility audit to 455 code-bearing studies, extract normalized architectural records from the 85 verified survivors, and synthesize recurring roles, coupling patterns, and build dependencies into the reference model. - The good: The reproducibility gate is the strongest distinguishing feature: only systems whose released code was independently rerun and whose main published claim was confirmed (±5% absolute error or within the reported 95% confidence interval) contributed evidence, making the reference model's structural claims traceable to confirmed operational systems rather than paper descriptions. - The missing: The build-order argument — the paper's most operationally prescriptive contribution — is grounded in what worked across the 85 verified systems but has not been prospectively tested against new system development, so its prescriptive validity remains undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Researchers designing Neuro-Symbolic systems or studying reproducibility in AI should read it; the combination of a large-scale reproducibility audit (18.68% reproduction rate across 455 attempts) and a grounded reference model is more methodologically rigorous than any prior NSAI survey.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a descriptive Neuro-Symbolic Reference Model — comprising a common architecture, an ordinal Level 0–7 integration scheme, six role-preserving architectural families, and a build-order argument — derived empirically from 85 independently reproduced Neuro-Symbolic systems. - Why it's important: The Neuro-Symbolic AI field currently lacks a grounded reference model that can guide design decisions; existing surveys describe themes and trends but cannot establish which architectural choices are necessary for a system to function, since they rest on unverified self-reports rather than systems that were actually rerun. - How it does it: The authors conduct a PRISMA-guided systematic literature review across nine databases, apply a same-artifact reproducibility audit to 455 code-bearing studies, extract normalized architectural records from the 85 verified survivors, and synthesize recurring roles, coupling patterns, and build dependencies into the reference model. - The good: The reproducibility gate is the strongest distinguishing feature: only systems whose released code was independently rerun and whose main published claim was confirmed (±5% absolute error or within the reported 95% confidence interval) contributed evidence, making the reference model's structural claims traceable to confirmed operational systems rather than paper descriptions. - The missing: The build-order argument — the paper's most operationally prescriptive contribution — is grounded in what worked across the 85 verified systems but has not been prospectively tested against new system development, so its prescriptive validity remains undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Researchers designing Neuro-Symbolic systems or studying reproducibility in AI should read it; the combination of a large-scale reproducibility audit (18.68% reproduction rate across 455 attempts) and a grounded reference model is more methodologically rigorous than any prior NSAI survey. Problem & Motivation: Neuro-Symbolic AI practitioners apply the "Neuro-Symbolic" label to systems that differ substantially in task setting, operating environment, and design requirements. Existing survey and taxonomy work describes themes, trends, and application areas but does not establish a common architectural account grounded in verified, runnable systems. Without such grounding, the community cannot clearly delineate which design choices are necessary for a Neuro-Symbolic system to function, cannot compare systems on a principled basis, and cannot identify what must be built before what. The gap is both definitional (what counts as a functional Neuro-Symbolic system?) and structural (what components are required, and in what order must they be established?). Key Idea: The core insight is that grounding a reference model in systems that have been independently reproduced — not merely peer-reviewed or code-released — separates structural claims supported by confirmed operational evidence from architectural descriptions that have never been verified to run. The architectural decomposition that follows from this evidence base (Problem-Set Domain → Neuro-Symbolic Environment → Neural component + Symbolic Store + Reasoning Process, coordinated by a Neuro-Symbolic Controller) is not proposed a priori; it emerges as a recurring pattern across the 85 verified systems, making it descriptively grounded rather than normatively imposed. Methodology: The methodology proceeds in five stages. First, a 2024 PRISMA-guided scoping review across five sources established the initial search vocabulary and thematic baseline from 158 retained papers. Second, an updated 2025 systematic review across nine sources (Web of Science, Scopus, PubMed, EI Compendex, IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, SpringerLink, Google Scholar, arXiv) retrieved 5,497 records, deduplicated to 2,479 unique records, retained 1,365 on-topic records after title/abstract screening, applied full-text eligibility exclusion (removing 61 records), and applied a public-artifact gate (removing 849 records with no verifiable public code artifact), leaving 455 code-bearing studies for attempted rerun. Third, each of the 455 studies entered a same-artifact reproducibility audit with a staged protocol: repository verification, environment reconstruction, executable-integrity testing, result re-execution, post-rerun data extraction, author-contact procedures, quality-control review, and log archival. Inter-rater reliability was established via a ten-paper calibration pilot (Cohen's κ = 0.82). Outcome labels O1 (fully reproduced) through O5 (irrecoverable defects) were assigned; only O1 and O2 outcomes (48 and 37 systems respectively, yielding 85 total) contributed to the model corpus. Fourth, each of the 85 verified systems was converted into a normalized architectural record using a fixed extraction vocabulary covering Problem-Set Domain, Operating Environment, Neural/Sub-Symbolic Component, Symbolic Store, Reasoning Process, Controller/Orchestration, System Input/Output, Verification/Validation Mechanism, Architectural Family, and Primary Neuro-Symbolic Level; roles received active, no-op, or absent status codes. Fifth, three reconciliation passes across all 85 normalized records abstracted recurring roles, information flows, coupling patterns, and build dependencies into the reference model. Critical design decisions include: (a) requiring same-artifact rerun (not just code availability) as the evidence threshold, which is well-justified because a code link alone does not verify that the released workflow executes or that the rerun reproduces the published claim; (b) using no-op role coding to preserve structural completeness for lower-boundary systems without crediting roles with no functional contribution, which is well-justified as it preserves the full observed range of integration depth; (c) resolving abstraction ambiguities conservatively toward lower Neuro-Symbolic levels, which is methodologically defensible but may systematically undercount integration depth. Key Results: Of 455 attempted reruns, 85 systems were verified as functional (18.68% of attempted reruns; 6.52% of the 1,304 eligible full-text records). The reproduced corpus spans Levels 0 through 6 of the ordinal scheme; Levels 1 and 3 are modal at 18 systems each, Level 5 has 1 system, Level 6 has 2, and no primary Level 7 systems were observed. Domain distribution: structured prediction and knowledge dominates at 24 systems (28%), formal reasoning and verification at 16 (19%), language/dialogue/QA at 14 (16%), vision and multimodal at 11 (13%), planning/control/robotics at 9 (11%), scientific discovery at 6 (7%), software and code at 5 (6%). Environment distribution: benchmark and dataset-defined environments dominate at 48 systems (57%), formal symbolic runtimes at 14 (17%). Verification regime: empirical benchmark evaluation is most common at 39 systems (46%), hard symbolic or formal checking and constraint/consistency checking each at 16 (19%). The 370 failed reruns (81.3% of the attempted pool) document the scale of the reproducibility deficit in the Neuro-Symbolic literature. No quantitative baseline comparison to prior reference models or taxonomy frameworks is reported, as none with comparable empirical grounding existed to compare against. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (systematic methodology for grounding a reference model in reproduced systems), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (18.68% reproduction rate across 455 attempted reruns; ordinal distribution of verified systems across Levels 0–6 with no Level 7 observed), NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (the Neuro-Symbolic Reference Model itself, the ordinal Level 0–7 scheme, the six architectural families, and the build-order argument as design artifacts). The architectural components named (neural component, symbolic store, reasoning process, controller) individually appear in prior NSAI survey work; the genuine novelty is the reproducibility-gated empirical grounding and the formal extraction methodology that makes cross-system structural comparison tractable without collapsing implementation differences. Rigor & Reproducibility: The reproducibility audit methodology is documented in detail with explicit outcome label definitions, permitted and prohibited repair categories, and a fidelity criterion (±5% absolute error or within the reported 95% confidence interval). Inter-rater reliability (Cohen's κ = 0.82) was established on a ten-paper calibration pilot before full auditing. The audit protocol is described in a companion reproducibility study (reference [19]: Colelough et al., 2025, manuscript under review at ICSME, RENE track) rather than this paper, which means the full audit protocol and its justification are not independently verifiable from this paper alone. The extraction vocabulary was refined iteratively against the full verified corpus, meaning the final role set is fitted to the 85 systems from which it was derived; this is acknowledged as a potential limitation but no cross-validation against held-out NSAI systems is performed. No ablation studies test which extraction fields are necessary versus redundant for the reference model to hold. The companion reproducibility study ([19]) is under review, not yet published, which limits independent verification of the audit protocol at time of reading. Code for the systematic review and extraction procedure is not reported as released in this paper. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — the paper delivers a built reference model, ordinal scheme, architectural family taxonomy, and build-order argument evaluated against 85 reproduced deployments. - Practical: The reference model is a conceptual and organizational artifact requiring no special hardware; it is applicable by any practitioner designing or evaluating a Neuro-Symbolic system. - Efficient: The cost of applying the reference model to a new system is the cost of filling out the normalized extraction record (10 fields), which is low; the cost of reproducing the model from scratch (conducting a new systematic review and same-artifact audit) is high, involving eight annotators and 455 attempted reruns over an extended period. - Effective: The reference model correctly classifies all 85 verified systems into the common role vocabulary without requiring paper-specific slots, which the authors take as evidence of structural completeness; no prospective evaluation on new Neuro-Symbolic systems not in the corpus is reported. - Scalable: The corpus covers 85 systems from 2019–2025; the authors note the role vocabulary may require extension for domains not represented among the 85 verified systems, and Level 7 self-improving systems are posited but unobserved, so the scale coverage has a known ceiling at current verified NSAI practice. The paper delivers the reference model in document form with the companion extraction table (Table A1, listing all 85 systems with their normalized architectural records); no software tool or API for applying the reference model is reported. Strengths: - The reproducibility gate is a genuine methodological advance over prior NSAI surveys: using same-artifact rerun as the evidence threshold separates structural claims supported by confirmed operational behavior from architectural descriptions that may never have been verified to run, directly addressing the construct validity problem in reference model derivation. - The normalized extraction vocabulary and role-status coding (active / no-op / absent) are thoughtfully designed to allow comparison across systems using different research communities' terminologies without collapsing genuine architectural differences into naming artifacts, which is the specific failure mode of prior taxonomy work applied directly to paper text. - The six architectural families are characterized by dominant coupling pattern rather than by task domain or application area, which is the correct level of abstraction for a design reference model: the same coupling pattern (e.g., proposal-verification-repair loop) recurs across theorem proving, code repair, and drug discovery, showing that family membership is structurally predictive, not domain-specific. - The build-order argument, grounded in observed dependencies across 85 verified systems, provides a practitioner-facing design implication that prior surveys do not: the Problem-Set Domain must be specified before the Environment, and both must be established before the inner components can function coherently. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) the corpus is restricted to systems with publicly released code, likely excluding proprietary and industrial Neuro-Symbolic systems that may exhibit different structural patterns; (2) the reference model is fitted to the 85 verified systems and may require revision as higher-level systems (particularly Level 7 self-improving systems) emerge in the literature; (3) the build-order argument is grounded empirically in what worked across the 85 verified systems but has not been prospectively tested against new system development; (4) the public-artifact bias is intentional but does limit the scope of inference. The authors confront these limitations directly, which strengthens the paper's credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The companion reproducibility study ([19]) on which the audit methodology rests is under review at ICSME, not yet published; readers cannot independently verify the full audit protocol, the permitted/prohibited repair categories, or the fidelity criterion justification from this paper alone. The conservative tie-breaking rule (lower Neuro-Symbolic levels preferred when ambiguity remains) is methodologically defensible but is not validated: no sensitivity analysis tests how many systems would change level assignment under a less conservative resolution rule, so the ordinal distribution reported may systematically understate integration depth. The six architectural families are described as exhaustive over the verified corpus, but no formal coverage claim or negative result (a system that required a seventh family) is reported. The build-order argument presents a dependency ordering (domain → environment → components → controller) as derived from structural observation, but the paper does not distinguish this from a logical necessity argument: it is possible that all 85 verified systems happen to follow this order because researchers who failed to specify the domain first never produced a publishable (and therefore verifiable) result — selection bias in what enters the literature could produce the observed pattern without the order being causally necessary. Finally, the 46% dominance of empirical benchmark evaluation as the primary verification regime (vs. 19% hard symbolic checking) is reported descriptively; the reference model does not take a position on whether empirical benchmark evaluation is a weaker verification regime for Neuro-Symbolic claims, which is a design-relevant question the data could support. Bottom Line: A practitioner or researcher designing or studying Neuro-Symbolic systems should read this paper: it is the first reference model for Neuro-Symbolic AI grounded in systems that were independently reproduced rather than merely described, the 18.68% reproduction rate across 455 attempts is itself a significant empirical finding about the state of the field, and the combination of common architecture, ordinal scheme, six architectural families, and build-order argument provides more actionable design guidance than any prior NSAI survey.

SpecAlign: Efficient Specification-Grounded Alignment of Large Language Models via Synthetic Data

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SpecAlign, a framework that synthesizes DPO preference-pair training data directly from provider-authored model specification documents, enabling an LLM to be fine-tuned to comply with a specific policy without manual data curation. - Why it's important: Model providers (OpenAI, Meta, Anthropic) increasingly deploy the same base model under different behavioral policies that are long, structured, and frequently updated; no existing alignment pipeline can rapidly adapt a model to a new or revised specification without costly human labeling. - How it does it: SpecAlign annotates each spec rule with semantic metadata, samples diverse 8–13-rule subsets as training contexts, and runs a Planner-Attacker-Defender multi-agent loop with an experience pool and role switching to generate hard boundary-violating negatives paired with compliant positives for DPO training. - The good: Across all three backbone models (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-8B, GPT-oss-20B) and all ten independently evaluated Model Specs, SpecAlign raises Rule Compliance Score (RCS) over the base model without exception, while simultaneously reducing over-refusal (XSTest-ORR) and preserving general capability (IFEval, MT-Bench, SimpleQA). - The missing: Every Model Spec in the evaluation derives from a single source (OpenAI's published spec); there is no test of cross-provider generalization, no frontier-scale model (GPT-4 class), and no assessment of whether SpecAlign-trained models maintain compliance when the governing spec is later updated without retraining. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on enterprise or product-specific LLM deployment and need a way to operationalize structured policy documents as training signals; skip it if your interest is in general safety alignment or theoretical guarantees, as this is purely an empirical systems contribution.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SpecAlign, a framework that synthesizes DPO preference-pair training data directly from provider-authored model specification documents, enabling an LLM to be fine-tuned to comply with a specific policy without manual data curation. - Why it's important: Model providers (OpenAI, Meta, Anthropic) increasingly deploy the same base model under different behavioral policies that are long, structured, and frequently updated; no existing alignment pipeline can rapidly adapt a model to a new or revised specification without costly human labeling. - How it does it: SpecAlign annotates each spec rule with semantic metadata, samples diverse 8–13-rule subsets as training contexts, and runs a Planner-Attacker-Defender multi-agent loop with an experience pool and role switching to generate hard boundary-violating negatives paired with compliant positives for DPO training. - The good: Across all three backbone models (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-8B, GPT-oss-20B) and all ten independently evaluated Model Specs, SpecAlign raises Rule Compliance Score (RCS) over the base model without exception, while simultaneously reducing over-refusal (XSTest-ORR) and preserving general capability (IFEval, MT-Bench, SimpleQA). - The missing: Every Model Spec in the evaluation derives from a single source (OpenAI's published spec); there is no test of cross-provider generalization, no frontier-scale model (GPT-4 class), and no assessment of whether SpecAlign-trained models maintain compliance when the governing spec is later updated without retraining. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on enterprise or product-specific LLM deployment and need a way to operationalize structured policy documents as training signals; skip it if your interest is in general safety alignment or theoretical guarantees, as this is purely an empirical systems contribution. Problem & Motivation Model providers maintain a single base LLM but deploy it under many distinct behavioral regimes, each codified in a long, hierarchically organized, frequently updated specification document (e.g., OpenAI's Model Spec, Meta's Acceptable Use Policy, Anthropic's Constitution). The alignment challenge in this setting is not safety from scratch but rapid, policy-conditioned fine-tuning: when a spec changes or a new deployment context is added, the provider needs a way to generate specification-adherent training data without convening human annotators. Existing approaches—Constitutional AI, principle-driven self-alignment, and synthetic data pipelines—assume short, fixed rule sets and do not scale to the length, structural heterogeneity, or conflict resolution requirements of real provider specifications. Deliberative Alignment (Guan et al., 2025) integrates policy documents at inference time but provides no training-time mechanism for internalizing them. Key Idea The core insight is to treat a model specification not as background guidance but as a structured generative grammar for training data: first annotate each rule with semantic attributes that support coherent subset sampling and conflict resolution, then use a multi-agent adversarial loop where the attacker's job is to find prompts that cause specification violations and the defender's compliant response to those same prompts becomes the positive in a DPO pair. The adversarial loop produces hard negatives—responses close in surface form to the compliant response but crossing a specific rule boundary—rather than the generic safe/unsafe dichotomy of prior red-teaming datasets. Methodology SpecAlign operates in three stages. First, Rule Annotation: each raw rule r_i is enriched offline with four structured tags—Direction (positive/negative), Stage (Essence, Request, Content, Vetting), Domain (safety, helpfulness, privacy, truthfulness), and Family (k-means cluster index over text-embedding-3-small embeddings, K_fam = floor(sqrt(|R|))). Direction and Domain are annotated by GPT-4o-mini with human validation showing 97.6% and 94.6% agreement respectively; Stage is annotated manually by two annotators. Second, Specification Generation: each training iteration samples a coherent subset S of 8–13 rules from the annotated set, enforcing four constraints—stage coverage (one rule per pipeline phase), direction balance (limiting prescriptive rule proportion), theme coverage (maximizing distinct domains), and family diversity (discouraging semantically similar rules co-occurring). Within S, rules are priority-sorted via a lexicographic ordering over (stage, direction, exception, specificity) to resolve conflicts deterministically. Third, Multi-Agent Adversarial Data Synthesis: a Planner (GPT-4o) constructs an attack strategy given the spec and seed prompt plus up to k=3 examples retrieved from an experience pool via MMR (lambda=0.7); an Attacker (GPT-4o) generates an adversarial query q_t; a Defender (GLM-4.5-Air, selected for highest attack success rate and Jaccard similarity tradeoff among seven candidates) generates a response a_t; a Safety Judge (GPT-5.2) produces a binary violation verdict v_t in {0,1}; and a Quality Judge (GPT-5.2) scores helpfulness h in [0,1] to reject degenerate prompts. When v_t=1, y^- = a_t and y^+ = the target model's response under explicit spec constraints. Attacker and Defender swap roles after each successful violation to prevent strategy fixation. Training uses three-stage SFT+DPO: SFT on Alpaca (~4k samples), DPO warm-up SFT on chosen responses (2k), then full DPO at a 1:3 SFT:DPO ratio. DPO temperature beta=0.1; max sequence length 512 tokens. Key Results Across 10 Model Specs and 3 backbone models, SpecAlign consistently improves RCS: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct from mean 0.73 to 0.80 (gains 1.8%–26.9% per spec), Qwen3-8B from 0.89 to 0.93 (1.9%–10.4%), GPT-oss-20B from 0.83 to 0.92 (3.1%–32.3%). On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, SpecAlign (RCS 0.86) outperforms GRPO (0.85), DebateGPT (0.82), RLOO (0.80), and SPIN (0.75), with the improvement over GRPO and RLOO indicating the gain is not attributable to optimizer choice alone. Safety robustness on Beaver-Unsafe generally decreases (fewer unsafe responses) or remains stable; FalseReject improves consistently; XSTest-ORR decreases in most settings, indicating less over-refusal. IFEval and MT-Bench improve across most specs; SimpleQA is mostly maintained. Cost analysis reports $0.0382 per successfully generated DPO instance at 2026 API prices. Ablation shows that removing the multi-agent framework, experience pool, or role switching each degrades RCS, with the full system achieving the highest score. LLM-as-judge reliability against human evaluators: 95.3% three-way agreement on Spec Compliance, and Fleiss' kappa of 0.91 on synthetic triplet label correctness across two CS PhD students and an independent GPT-5.2 judge. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM (specification-grounded alignment as a distinct paradigm), NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (the SpecAlign framework and toolkit), NEW METHOD (structured rule annotation + constraint-aware spec sampling + adversarial synthesis loop with experience pool and role switching). The individual algorithmic components—DPO, multi-agent red-teaming, MMR-based diversity, k-means family clustering—are well-established; the novelty lies in the specific integration architecture that makes provider spec documents a primary control signal for data generation rather than background context. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations covering three framework components are presented and show clear monotone degradation when each is removed. Error bars (mean ± std) are reported for main RCS results. Human evaluation with two independent CS PhD students validates both the LLM-as-judge reliability (three-way agreement 95.3%–100% across benchmarks) and triplet label correctness (mean pairwise agreement 95.6%, Fleiss' kappa 0.91). Code and documentation are publicly available at specalign.readthedocs.io. Key methodological concerns: (1) All 10 Model Specs are derived from OpenAI's single published spec—this is not 10 independent specifications but 10 sampled subsets of one document, so cross-provider and cross-domain generalization is untested. (2) The RCS judge (GPT-4o-mini) is used for evaluation but the Planner and Attacker in data generation also use GPT-4o, creating partial circular evaluation risk. (3) The SFT:DPO ratio of 1:3 is tuned on the same distribution being evaluated on. (4) Sequence truncation to 512 tokens limits applicability to specs requiring long multi-rule reasoning chains. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.16276v2, June 2026). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS (a built framework evaluated by deployment on three models across ten spec configurations). - Practical: Training runs on Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen3-8B with standard HuggingFace/PyTorch stack; data generation requires API access to GPT-4o, GPT-5.2, and GLM-4.5-Air. - Efficient: Data generation costs $0.0382 per instance; the number of instances needed to train 8B-class models is not stated in the main text, so total training cost is not fully quantifiable from this paper. - Effective: RCS gains are consistent across all 30 model-spec combinations tested, and the gains coincide with maintained or improved general capability, supporting the claim that the method solves specification compliance without sacrificing helpfulness. - Scalable: Only 8B and 20B models are tested; the framework's behavior at 70B+ or frontier model scale is not evaluated and not claimed. The 512-token sequence truncation is a hard constraint that would require architectural changes for very long-context specification documents. The work delivers a software toolkit (specalign.readthedocs.io) and a reusable data generation pipeline, not just a paper artifact. Strengths - The structured rule annotation (direction, stage, domain, family) combined with constraint-aware sampling is a genuinely useful formalization that separates SpecAlign from prior ad hoc red-teaming approaches; it directly addresses the controllability and conflict-resolution gaps the authors identify. - The role-switching mechanism in the multi-agent loop is a simple but well-motivated design decision to prevent strategy collapse, and the ablation confirms it contributes measurably to RCS. - The simultaneous improvement in compliance and reduction in over-refusal (XSTest-ORR decreases) is a credible result that directly contradicts the common failure mode of alignment methods becoming more conservative; this is the strongest empirical claim in the paper. - Human evaluation at Fleiss' kappa 0.91 on synthetic triplet labels provides meaningful evidence that the generated pairs carry genuine compliance signal rather than surface-level artifacts. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge three limitations: (1) effectiveness is bounded by spec quality—incompleteness or inconsistency in the source document propagates into training data; (2) the divide-and-conquer sampling approximates, rather than holistically applies, the full specification, and curriculum learning to bridge this gap is left as future work; (3) evaluation benchmarks may not capture long-term behavioral drift under prolonged real-world deployment. The authors also note ethical risks of centralizing normative control and the possibility that biased specs propagate faithfully into model behavior. These limitations are confronted directly, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All 10 "independent" Model Specs are sampled subsets of a single document (OpenAI's published spec); the framework has not been tested on distinct provider specifications from Meta or Anthropic, or on domain-specific specs (e.g., a medical or legal deployment spec). The generalization claim to "provider-authored specifications" broadly is unsubstantiated by the experiments. - The primary evaluation metric (RCS) is computed by the same class of model (GPT-4o-mini) that participates in data generation (GPT-4o Planner and Attacker), raising a partial Goodhart's Law concern: the system may be optimizing for outputs that score well under GPT-4-family judges specifically. - The defender model (GLM-4.5-Air) was chosen precisely because it generates more violations at higher rates than more capable models (GPT-5.2 produces fewer usable violations); this selection criterion means the positive examples in DPO pairs are responses from a weaker, less well-aligned model, which could limit the ceiling on what behavior the model learns to imitate. - No experiment shows what happens when a spec is updated: does SpecAlign-trained model retain compliance under the previous spec, or does new training overwrite it? This is the core deployment scenario the paper motivates but never directly evaluates. - The 512-token truncation excludes cases requiring long contextual reasoning about rule interactions; real specifications routinely produce such cases. Bottom Line Practitioners building product-specific or policy-conditioned deployments on open-weight models will find SpecAlign's pipeline immediately actionable, but they should treat the compliance gains as specific to OpenAI-spec-derived rule sets until cross-provider experiments are published.

Safety, Security, and Cognitive Risks in Neuro-Symbolic AI

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a unified threat model for neuro-symbolic AI (NeSy) systems, comprising three formal definitions (NeSy Attack Surface, Symbolic Integrity Violation, Cross-Layer Amplification Ratio), an 11-extension MITRE ATLAS mapping, a five-profile attacker taxonomy, and three empirical experiments validating symbolic-layer attack vectors on synthetic domain artefacts. - Why it's important: NeSy systems are being deployed in safety-critical domains (healthcare, cybersecurity, enterprise automation) where the symbolic layers create attack surfaces — KG poisoning, ontology-merging attacks, inference-engine subversion — absent from existing MITRE ATLAS and OWASP catalogs, leaving deployers without a structured threat-modeling framework. - How it does it: The Cross-Layer Amplification Ratio (X) formalizes the ratio of symbolic conclusion flip rate to neural decision flip rate under adversarial perturbation; its decomposition into neural-caused and autonomous symbolic sensitivity components reveals that the symbolic layer amplifies adversarial impact independently of neural decision-boundary crossings. - The good: PGD-10 at epsilon = 0.01 produces X = 5.884 (95% CI [4.64, 8.00], p_bootstrap < 0.0001) on a DistilBERT + ProbLog pipeline, confirmed adversarially specific by a matched-random baseline (E_rand = 0), with the decomposition showing 75.8% of symbolic flips arise without any neural flip — the dominant amplification pathway is autonomous symbolic sensitivity, not neural error propagation. - The missing: All three experiments use synthetic artefacts (a 205-entity medical KG, 1,000 synthetic medical claims, 30 OWL templates); the paper has not demonstrated X > 1 or the detector recall targets on any production-scale KG (SNOMED-CT, ATT&CK), and the cognitive-risk hypotheses (H1–H3) remain unvalidated pending IRB approval. - Read it or not: Worth reading for AI security practitioners and NeSy system builders who need a structured, quantified threat taxonomy with measurable acceptance criteria; less relevant for ML researchers seeking novel algorithmic contributions to model robustness.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a unified threat model for neuro-symbolic AI (NeSy) systems, comprising three formal definitions (NeSy Attack Surface, Symbolic Integrity Violation, Cross-Layer Amplification Ratio), an 11-extension MITRE ATLAS mapping, a five-profile attacker taxonomy, and three empirical experiments validating symbolic-layer attack vectors on synthetic domain artefacts. - Why it's important: NeSy systems are being deployed in safety-critical domains (healthcare, cybersecurity, enterprise automation) where the symbolic layers create attack surfaces — KG poisoning, ontology-merging attacks, inference-engine subversion — absent from existing MITRE ATLAS and OWASP catalogs, leaving deployers without a structured threat-modeling framework. - How it does it: The Cross-Layer Amplification Ratio (X) formalizes the ratio of symbolic conclusion flip rate to neural decision flip rate under adversarial perturbation; its decomposition into neural-caused and autonomous symbolic sensitivity components reveals that the symbolic layer amplifies adversarial impact independently of neural decision-boundary crossings. - The good: PGD-10 at epsilon = 0.01 produces X = 5.884 (95% CI [4.64, 8.00], p_bootstrap < 0.0001) on a DistilBERT + ProbLog pipeline, confirmed adversarially specific by a matched-random baseline (E_rand = 0), with the decomposition showing 75.8% of symbolic flips arise without any neural flip — the dominant amplification pathway is autonomous symbolic sensitivity, not neural error propagation. - The missing: All three experiments use synthetic artefacts (a 205-entity medical KG, 1,000 synthetic medical claims, 30 OWL templates); the paper has not demonstrated X > 1 or the detector recall targets on any production-scale KG (SNOMED-CT, ATT&CK), and the cognitive-risk hypotheses (H1–H3) remain unvalidated pending IRB approval. - Read it or not: Worth reading for AI security practitioners and NeSy system builders who need a structured, quantified threat taxonomy with measurable acceptance criteria; less relevant for ML researchers seeking novel algorithmic contributions to model robustness. Problem & Motivation NeSy systems pair neural perception with symbolic knowledge bases, ontologies, and logic rules to deliver auditable reasoning in high-stakes domains including clinical decision support, cybersecurity operations center (SOC) tooling, and enterprise workflow automation. Their hybrid five-layer architecture — neural perception, symbolic knowledge bases, reasoning engines, agent orchestration, data stores — multiplies the asset inventory and attack surface beyond purely neural systems. MITRE ATLAS (16 tactics, 84 techniques as of 2024) and the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications cover neural adversarial examples, prompt injection, data exfiltration, and agentic context manipulation, but neither catalogs symbolic-substrate attacks: malicious KG triple injection, ontology-merging attacks that corrupt concept hierarchies, inference-engine subversion via crafted rule inputs, or supply-chain compromise of reasoning libraries. Beyond technical threats, NeSy systems produce structured logical explanations that — unlike opaque neural outputs — map onto Kahneman's System 2 reasoning and may amplify automation bias, authority bias, and sycophantic reinforcement of false beliefs. No prior framework addresses all three threat dimensions (technical, adversarial, cognitive) with formal definitions and quantitative empirical grounding. Key Idea The central technical insight is autonomous symbolic sensitivity: in a decoupled NeSy pipeline where a neural component passes soft probabilities or logits to a probabilistic reasoning engine (e.g., ProbLog), a sub-perceptual adversarial perturbation that does not change the neural model's argmax classification can nonetheless shift logit distributions enough to cross ProbLog's probabilistic firing threshold, changing downstream symbolic conclusions without any neural decision-boundary crossing. This means X > 1 is an inherent property of probabilistic decoupled architectures, not a consequence of neural errors, and defenses focused solely on neural robustness are structurally insufficient. Methodology The paper formalizes three constructs. Definition 1 (NeSy Attack Surface) is the set of interfaces across which an adversary with access level alpha can inject, modify, or exfiltrate information, with a proof sketch that |AS(S, alpha)| > |AS(N, alpha)| for grey-box and above access because symbolic layers add new interface classes absent in purely neural systems. Definition 2 (Symbolic Integrity Violation, SIV) captures any unauthorized change to the knowledge base that causes the reasoning engine to produce a different conclusion on a safety-critical query. Definition 3 (Cross-Layer Amplification Ratio) defines X(delta) = E^R(delta) / E^N(delta), decomposed into neural-caused symbolic flip rate E^{R|N} and autonomous symbolic sensitivity rate E^{R|not-N}. Three experiments validate the model: E1 simulates targeted, random, and stealth KG poisoning on a synthetic 205-entity, 622-triple medical contraindication KG across budgets B = 1–50, testing three detectors (cryptographic provenance signing, statistical anomaly detection, canary monitoring) over 8 seeds. E2 measures X on a DistilBERT (hidden dim 768) + ProbLog hazard-reasoning pipeline using PGD-10 and FGSM attacks at epsilon in {0.005–0.08} (l_inf, applied to pre-LayerNorm token embeddings), with a matched-random baseline to isolate adversarial directionality, and a depth sweep d = 1–10 (up to 21 ProbLog rules). E3 constructs 30 adversarial OWL axiom templates (15 clinical-safety, 15 STIX cyber-threat) evaluated under Pellet + HermiT dual consistency checking, with in-sample vs. held-out detection via a differential safety-query detector. Bootstrap tests use B = 10,000 resamples; E2 applies Holm-Bonferroni correction across five simultaneous epsilon-level tests. Key Results E1: Targeted injection reaches SIV > 5% at B = 5 (mean 7.6%, 95% CI [6.3%, 8.9%]), 14.6× more efficient than random injection (0.52% at B = 5). Stealth attacks achieve deterministically zero SIV by avoiding the contraindicated relation to evade provenance signing — an architecture-specific stealth/SIV mutual exclusion under this KG schema. No single detector covers all three attacker profiles: canary monitoring covers targeted attacks at moderate budget (B ≤ 10) with recall 0.738 at B = 10, but recall is 0 for stealth at all budgets; provenance signing achieves 1.00 recall on targeted/random but 0 on stealth; statistical anomaly detection fires only at high budget. E2: PGD-10 at epsilon = 0.01 produces X = 5.884 (95% CI [4.64, 8.00], p_bootstrap < 0.0001, one-sided). The matched-random baseline produces E^R_rand = 0 at all epsilon, confirming adversarial specificity (additive excess delta-X_adv = 5.884). The decomposition reveals E^{R|N} = 1.000 (all 43 neural-flipped queries propagate to symbolic flips) and E^{R|not-N} = 0.758 (75.8% of symbolic flips arise without a neural flip). X declines monotonically from 5.884 at epsilon = 0.01 to 1.042 at epsilon = 0.05. The depth sweep d = 1–10 is a confirmed null result: X is depth-invariant because PGD perturbations do not enter multi-hop chained inference regions. Tight coupling (argmax labels only to ProbLog) fully suppresses X to 0.000, confirming probabilistic marginalization as an architectural defense. FGSM fails entirely (E^N = 0 at epsilon ≤ 0.05) due to DistilBERT's subword embedding discretization. E3: 28 of 30 templates induce SIV (93.3%, 95% CI [78.7%, 98.2%]) with 100% Pellet-consistency stealth. In-sample detection recall is 100%. Held-out detection is 67.9% overall (95% CI [49.3%, 82.1%]), with a pronounced domain gap: clinical held-out recall 85.7% (95% CI [60.1%, 96.0%]) vs. STIX held-out recall 50.0% (95% CI [26.8%, 73.2%]) — statistically indistinguishable from random guessing. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION (Definitions 1–3: NeSy attack surface, SIV, and the first formal measure of cross-layer adversarial amplification in decoupled pipelines), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (first empirical measurement of X > 1 at realistic perturbation budgets; the identification of autonomous symbolic sensitivity as the dominant amplification pathway at 75.8%), NEW METHOD (five-profile NeSy attacker taxonomy extending MITRE ATLAS; 11 NeSy-specific tactic extensions). The MITRE ATLAS extension and OWASP mapping are application of existing frameworks to a new domain. The formal definitions are the genuine contribution; prior adversarial ML work studied neural robustness and KG embedding attacks separately, but Definition 3 is the first measure bridging them in a decoupled pipeline. Rigor & Reproducibility The paper applies bootstrap significance testing (B = 10,000) throughout, reports Wilson 95% CIs on all proportions, uses 8 independent seeds for E1, and applies Holm-Bonferroni correction for five simultaneous epsilon-level tests in E2. The matched-random baseline in E2 directly addresses the alternative hypothesis that X reflects general perturbation sensitivity rather than adversarial directionality — this is a methodological strength. Limitations are openly disclosed in Table 11: all artefacts are synthetic, the X measurement is PGD-specific and DistilBERT-specific, the depth sweep is a null result, and STIX detector failure is unresolved. Code and data are available from the author upon reasonable request but are not publicly deposited. This is an arXiv preprint (v1, June 2026); no peer review is declared. One methodological concern: the E2 DistilBERT model was chosen for computational tractability and because it establishes a conservative lower bound on X — the claim is that domain-adapted models with tighter decision margins (BioERT, ClinicalBERT) would show higher X, but this is not measured, leaving the headline result anchored to a single, deliberately weak neural model. The E3 claim of 93.3% SIV is explicitly scoped to equivalence and subclass axioms; the paper does not test property restrictions, cardinality constraints, or other OWL axiom types. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: E2 runs DistilBERT (66M parameters) plus ProbLog on synthetic data executable on commodity GPU hardware; E1 and E3 use Owlready2, Pellet, and HermiT — all open-source, CPU-deployable OWL reasoners. - Efficient: No throughput, wall-clock, or memory figures are reported for any experiment; relative compute cost of NeSy vs. neural-only pipelines is not evaluated. - Effective: All three experiments demonstrate that attacks succeed at low budget with high stealth — confirming the threat — but no defended end-to-end pipeline is evaluated; the practical checklist specifies acceptance criteria (e.g., ≥85% SIV detection recall) that exceed E1's demonstrated performance for multi-profile coverage. - Scalable: All experiments use toy-scale artefacts (205-entity KG, 1,000 training claims, 30 OWL templates); scaling to SNOMED-CT, real EHR KGs, or STIX/ATT&CK is deferred as explicit future work with no preliminary evidence. The paper delivers experimental scripts and synthetic datasets available on request; no software library, CLI tool, or trained model artifact is released. Strengths - The Cross-Layer Amplification Ratio decomposition (Definition 3) is a genuinely novel measure: distinguishing neural-caused from autonomous symbolic sensitivity pathways gives practitioners a quantitative diagnostic for whether to harden the neural component, the symbolic layer, or the coupling mechanism — and the E2 result (E^{R|not-N} = 0.758 dominating E^{R|N} = 1.000 × 0.134) shows the coupling architecture is the primary risk factor, not neural vulnerability alone. - The E1 stealth/SIV mutual exclusion finding — that avoiding the single contraindicated relation achieves perfect detection evasion at the cost of zero impact, while targeted attacks are 14.6× more efficient than random — is a concrete, KG-architecture-dependent result that directly informs defense prioritization and illustrates why no single detector suffices. - The paper's scope discipline is creditable: the cognitive risk section (Section 7) explicitly labels all three hypotheses (H1–H3) as theoretical predictions grounded in literature but not yet empirically confirmed, and defers E4 to IRB-approved future work rather than asserting results it has not measured. - Aligning mitigations with NIST AI 600-1 and EU AI Act acceptance criteria (Table 4) bridges the gap between academic threat modeling and actionable governance, addressing the gap between threat awareness and regulatory compliance that most security papers ignore. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront limitations directly in Table 11 and the broader-impact section. E1 uses a synthetic KG with a single safety-critical relation type, and the stealth/targeted trade-off may not generalize to KGs with multiple safety-critical relation types. E2's X = 5.884 is PGD-specific at epsilon = 0.01 and declines to 1.042 at epsilon = 0.05; the depth sweep is a null result because the tested perturbations do not trigger multi-hop chained rules. E3's 93.3% SIV is scoped to single-axiom equivalence and subclass attacks; STIX held-out detection at 50% is explicitly characterized as a detector failure requiring STIX-specific template augmentation or domain-agnostic syntactic detection on OWL axiom structure. The five attacker profiles are stated as threat hypotheses, not empirically calibrated against any real-world NeSy incident corpus. The broader-impact section acknowledges dual-use risk from publishing the attacker taxonomy and states that operational attack details (specific KG injection payloads, step-by-step rule-poisoning procedures) were deliberately omitted. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The proof sketch for |AS(S, alpha)| > |AS(N, alpha)| (Definition 1) rests on the assumption that symbolic layers are not fully sandboxed from the API output channel. This is stated as a structural assumption rather than a variable measured across deployment configurations; real production deployments may sandbox the KG behind the API, making the attack-surface enlargement claim deployment-conditional rather than architectural. - X = 5.884 is a lower bound only if domain-adapted models (BioERT, ClinicalBERT) have tighter decision margins than DistilBERT — a plausible but untested claim. If those models are also more robust to l_inf embedding perturbations (e.g., due to domain-specific normalization), X could be lower rather than higher, reversing the conservative-baseline interpretation. - Definition 3 is explicitly restricted to decoupled NeSy architectures and does not cover fully differentiable NeSy systems (DeepProbLog, Logical Neural Networks, Neural Theorem Provers) where the neural-symbolic boundary is implicit. These tightly coupled architectures may represent the systems with the highest real-world deployment growth and the most complex amplification dynamics, and the paper offers no evidence that the X > 1 threat extends to them. - The practical checklist (Table 4) specifies an aspirational ≥95% SIV detection target described as requiring ensemble engineering beyond E1 results; E1 shows no single detector exceeds 85% recall across all three attack profiles, and the ensemble acceptance criterion is undemonstrated by any experiment in the paper. - The reference list includes 13 blog posts and industry white papers alongside peer-reviewed work; several claims in Sections 6–7 cite blog posts (references 8, 9, 10, 12, 13, 24, 26–33) rather than peer-reviewed empirical studies, which limits the evidentiary weight of some threat-category descriptions. Bottom Line A practitioner building or threat-modeling NeSy systems in healthcare, cybersecurity, or enterprise automation gains the first formal vocabulary, a structured attacker taxonomy, and three quantified attack demonstrations for symbolic-layer threats absent from MITRE ATLAS; the finding that 75.8% of cross-layer amplification arises via autonomous symbolic sensitivity rather than neural error propagation is directly actionable for architecture selection (probabilistic vs. hard coupling) and makes this worth reading despite the entirely synthetic evaluation and single-author arXiv status.

GateMem: Benchmarking Memory Governance in Multi-Principal Shared-Memory Agents

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GateMem, a benchmark that evaluates LLM memory agents across three joint governance dimensions — authorized utility, access-control enforcement, and post-deletion non-recovery — in multi-principal shared-memory settings spanning medical, office, education, and household domains. - Why it's important: Shared institutional deployments (clinical teams, enterprise staff, campus communities, households) require agents that simultaneously serve authorized users and reliably withhold or forget information from unauthorized ones; existing benchmarks measure only recall, leaving governance failures entirely unmeasured. - How it does it: Constructs 91 long-form multi-party interaction episodes with 2,218 hidden checkpoints, evaluates seven baseline memory architectures across six backbone LLMs using an LLM-as-judge protocol, and scores systems with a multiplicative Memory Governance Score (MGS = U × (1−A) × (1−F)) that prevents masking one failure mode with excellence on another. - The good: No evaluated method simultaneously achieves strong utility and reliable governance across any domain-backbone combination; LONG-CONTEXT leaks >20% in multiple settings despite high utility, a finding that recall-only benchmarks structurally cannot surface. - The missing: All 91 episodes are LLM-generated rather than drawn from real institutional logs, which risks systematic blind spots in the attack distribution and limits ecological validity. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building or evaluating memory-augmented LLM agents for multi-user institutional settings; the benchmark, leaderboard, and harness are publicly released and ready to use.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GateMem, a benchmark that evaluates LLM memory agents across three joint governance dimensions — authorized utility, access-control enforcement, and post-deletion non-recovery — in multi-principal shared-memory settings spanning medical, office, education, and household domains. - Why it's important: Shared institutional deployments (clinical teams, enterprise staff, campus communities, households) require agents that simultaneously serve authorized users and reliably withhold or forget information from unauthorized ones; existing benchmarks measure only recall, leaving governance failures entirely unmeasured. - How it does it: Constructs 91 long-form multi-party interaction episodes with 2,218 hidden checkpoints, evaluates seven baseline memory architectures across six backbone LLMs using an LLM-as-judge protocol, and scores systems with a multiplicative Memory Governance Score (MGS = U × (1−A) × (1−F)) that prevents masking one failure mode with excellence on another. - The good: No evaluated method simultaneously achieves strong utility and reliable governance across any domain-backbone combination; LONG-CONTEXT leaks >20% in multiple settings despite high utility, a finding that recall-only benchmarks structurally cannot surface. - The missing: All 91 episodes are LLM-generated rather than drawn from real institutional logs, which risks systematic blind spots in the attack distribution and limits ecological validity. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building or evaluating memory-augmented LLM agents for multi-user institutional settings; the benchmark, leaderboard, and harness are publicly released and ready to use. Problem & Motivation Existing memory benchmarks (LoCoMo, LongMemEval, PersonaMem, MemBench, EverMemBench, and others) treat agent memory as a private cache owned by a single user, where success equals recall accuracy. Real institutional deployments operate on a fundamentally different model: a clinical assistant shared by physicians, nurses, patients, and family members writes and queries a common memory pool under role-specific permissions; an enterprise assistant serves managers, contractors, legal staff, and executives with overlapping but non-identical access scopes; a campus assistant handles professors, students, advisors, parents, and registrars with fluid and sometimes conflicting authorization states. In these settings, high recall without governance is a security liability — an agent that accurately recalls a deleted lab result for an unauthorized family member has failed, regardless of factual correctness. The specific gap is that no prior benchmark jointly evaluates authorized utility, access-control boundary enforcement, and post-deletion non-recovery in a genuinely multi-principal shared context. Key Idea The core insight is that memory governance in shared deployments is inherently tripartite and cannot be decomposed: a system must simultaneously serve authorized queries (Utility), withhold information from unauthorized or over-scoped requesters (Access Control), and cease retrieving or confirming information after explicit deletion (Active Forgetting). These three objectives are in tension — full-context prompting boosts utility but surfaces information that should be withheld; policy-aware retrieval reduces leakage but causes over-refusal of authorized queries; structured external memory systems introduce organization but do not automatically enforce access boundaries or respect deletions. The Memory Governance Score (MGS = U × (1−A) × (1−F)) operationalizes this coupling multiplicatively: a system cannot compensate for severe leakage with high utility, nor for over-refusal with perfect forgetting compliance. Methodology GateMem is constructed through a three-stage pipeline. First, domain and policy design defines four institutional domains — Medical, Office, Education, Household — each with a distinct principal set, role taxonomy, and scope-specific access rule graph. Medical episodes involve up to 15 principals per episode (physicians, nurses, patients, family, pharmacists, schedulers) with permissions defined over lab results, medication details, treatment plans, and clinical interpretations. Office episodes involve project managers, engineers, finance staff, legal staff, contractors, and executives with access scopes over project confidentiality, commercial terms, staffing decisions, and incident details. Second, LLM-assisted episode construction generates 91 long-form interaction traces, each averaging 223 turns. Each turn records speaker, timestamp, turn type (ordinary dialogue, note update, portal message, lab result, scheduling change, deletion request), and natural-language content. Turn types are expressed in natural language and not exposed to the evaluated agent as explicit memory operation labels. Episodes are designed with utility memory anchors, access-overreach distractors, and deletion-to-recovery gaps. Third, 2,218 hidden checkpoints are inserted at turn boundaries. Each checkpoint contains a visible input (episode ID, turn boundary, authenticated requester identity, natural-language query) and a hidden annotation (checkpoint category, expected normalized action from {answer, answer_redacted, refuse, no_memory}, judge specification, and leak targets). The three checkpoint categories are Utility (728 checkpoints), Access Control (727 checkpoints), and Active Forgetting (763 checkpoints). Leak targets are string or regex patterns that must not appear in agent outputs for access-control and active-forgetting checkpoints. A four-stage quality control pipeline validates every episode: schema consistency, chain-of-evidence validation (gold answers must be supported by the preceding interaction history), deletion-chain closure (sensitive values must appear before deletion and only be targeted afterward), and manual leak-target inspection. Evaluation uses GPT-4o as judge with a structured output protocol. Effective Utility U requires both correct action and factual completeness per judge spec. Access-Control Violation A fires when the agent's response contains or clearly implies any leak target, or when the agent fails to take a valid restrictive action. Active Forgetting Failure F fires when deleted information is recovered, confirmed, or reconstructed. MGS = U × (1−A) × (1−F) is the primary composite metric. Seven baseline configurations are evaluated: LONG-CONTEXT (full history in prompt, up to 300 turns), RAG-NAIVE (top-k=20 similarity retrieval), RAG-POLICY (top-k=20 with requester and policy metadata filtering), A-MEM (Zettelkasten-style agentic notes with graph retrieval), MEM0 (production memory with add/update/delete operations), REMEM-I (episodic graph with iterative tool-based reasoning), and REMEM-S (episodic graph with single-step retrieval). Six backbone LLMs are tested: GPT-5.4, Deepseek-V4-Pro, Llama-4-Maverick, GPT-5-mini, GPT-4o-mini, and Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite. Key Results No method achieves simultaneously high utility and low governance failures across any domain-backbone combination. LONG-CONTEXT achieves the highest MGS in the majority of domain-backbone blocks: under GPT-5.4, Medical MGS = 80.1 (U=91.4, A=10.4, F=2.3); under Deepseek-V4-Pro, Medical MGS = 70.6 and Education MGS = 71.0. Despite this, LONG-CONTEXT access-control or active-forgetting failures exceed 20% in multiple domains and backbone settings, with Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite showing active-forgetting failure rates of 26% on Medical and 64% on Education under LONG-CONTEXT.
