RAG-POLICY substantially reduces unauthorized disclosures relative to RAG-NAIVE but induces over-refusal on legitimate queries: over-refusal rates of 63.3% (RAG-POLICY) vs. 44.8% (RAG-NAIVE) vs. 24.8% (LONG-CONTEXT) on Medical with GPT-4o-mini. Dedicated memory systems (A-MEM, MEM0, REMEM) do not consistently outperform simpler baselines on MGS across completed runs — their structured memory organization does not inherently enforce access boundaries or deletion compliance. Efficiency diverges sharply: LONG-CONTEXT averages 4.22 seconds/checkpoint and 4.04k tokens/checkpoint on Medical; REMEM-I averages 122.95 seconds/checkpoint and 1.06k tokens/checkpoint. REMEM-S averages 113.91 seconds/checkpoint. Policy-aware retrieval (RAG-POLICY) adds minimal overhead over RAG-NAIVE while providing safety benefits. Human validation on a stratified sample of 579 Medical checkpoints (approximately 50% of one evaluation run) finds maximum 1.04 percentage-point difference from GPT-4o judge labels across U, A, F, and MGS, with field-level agreement ≥97.7% and Cohen's κ ≥ 0.937 across action correctness, utility correctness, access leakage, and deletion leakage fields. Attack-type failure analysis on Medical with GPT-4o-mini reveals that family overreach (n=55) and indirect inference (n=22) are among the highest-failure access-control categories, while yes-or-no confirmation (n=48) and post-delete recovery (n=27) dominate active-forgetting failures. Policy-aware retrieval (RAG-POLICY) mitigates most soft-overreach attack types, while persistent memory systems remain brittle on retrieved-but-deleted content. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW FORMULATION. The formulation of multi-principal shared-memory governance as a joint tripartite problem — combining utility, access-control enforcement, and active forgetting into a multiplicative composite score — is genuinely new in the memory-agent evaluation literature. The benchmark itself (91 LLM-generated multi-party episodes, 2,218 hidden checkpoints, four institutional domains with distinct principal topologies, curated attack taxonomy, and public leaderboard) is a substantive dataset contribution. The evaluation methodology adapts LLM-as-judge to governance-specific rubrics with leak-target auditing. No new agent architecture is proposed; the experimental contribution is a systematic comparative study of existing architectures on the new benchmark. Rigor & Reproducibility Code, dataset, and leaderboard are publicly released (links on page 1 of the paper). The four-stage quality control pipeline (schema consistency, chain-of-evidence validation, deletion-chain closure, leak-target inspection) is described in sufficient detail to replicate. Judge reliability is validated with rigorous human annotation (κ ≥ 0.937 across all fields on a stratified Medical sample). Retrieval depth sensitivity is tested (top-k ∈ {5, 10, 20, 30, 40}) and over-refusal rates are separately reported to distinguish governance from paralysis. Two methodological concerns merit attention. First, all 91 episodes are LLM-generated; the paper does not report inter-annotator agreement for episode construction quality, only for judge labels on a single domain. Second, not all 7 baseline × 6 backbone × 4 domain combinations are reported; the paper states main tables cover the subset of completed runs, which makes cross-backbone comparisons for some methods incomplete. Third, human validation covers only Medical checkpoints from one evaluation run; generalization of judge reliability to Office, Education, and Household is asserted but not measured. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL (benchmark paper with experimental comparison of multiple existing memory systems). - Practical: The evaluation harness runs on commercial APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic-compatible, HuggingFace embeddings) with 4-8 worker parallelism; no specialized hardware is required. The benchmark is ready to use for any team with API access. - Efficient: Token cost is 1k-7.6k tokens/checkpoint depending on method; wall-clock time ranges from 4 seconds/checkpoint (LONG-CONTEXT) to 267 seconds/checkpoint (REMEM-I on Education). Running all 7 baselines across 6 backbones and 2,218 checkpoints represents substantial API spend, but individual method-backbone evaluations are tractable. - Effective: GateMem successfully discriminates between architectures on governance dimensions that recall-only benchmarks would classify as equivalent; the finding that no method achieves MGS > 80 in any domain-backbone combination is credible and non-trivial. The multiplicative scoring genuinely prevents high-utility-but-leaky systems from scoring well. - Scalable: The benchmark is fixed at 91 episodes; the paper does not test how episode quality or checkpoint validity would hold if the episode set were expanded by 10× using the same generation pipeline. The evaluation harness supports resumable runs and streaming to disk. The work delivers a public dataset (episodes.jsonl, checkpoints.jsonl), evaluation harness, trained judge prompts, and live leaderboard. Strengths - The problem formulation is both correct and important: shared-memory governance is structurally different from recall, and the multiplicative MGS enforces that distinction rather than allowing partial credit across dimensions. - The attack taxonomy for both access-control (family overreach, indirect inference, authority pressure, label-existence probe, cross-patient confusion, impersonation) and active-forgetting (yes-or-no confirmation, social engineering, split reconstruction, update-delete conflict, post-delete recovery) goes well beyond obvious adversarial cases and tests the realistic soft-boundary violations that dominate real institutional failures. - Judge validation is unusually rigorous: stratified human re-annotation of approximately 50% of one domain's checkpoints yields κ ≥ 0.937 and ≤1.04 percentage-point metric difference, providing stronger evidence than most LLM-judge papers that the evaluation signal is reliable. - The efficiency profiling (Table 4) reveals a non-obvious tradeoff: LONG-CONTEXT is fast (4 seconds/checkpoint) but token-expensive and leaky; REMEM is token-cheap (1k tokens/checkpoint) but 30–60× slower; this directly informs system design choices for practitioners. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that GateMem tests interface-level forgetting (the agent's response behavior) rather than certified physical erasure from internal databases, vector indexes, caches, or model parameters — a meaningful scope boundary given that machine unlearning typically requires retraining or weight updates. They note that episode generation is LLM-assisted rather than drawn from real institutional logs. They acknowledge that not all baseline runs are complete and that the benchmark does not propose new agent architectures. These limitations are confronted directly in the text and treated as design decisions rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All episodes are generated by LLMs with the same general architecture family as the evaluated agents; the attack distribution may be systematically biased toward patterns that LLM-based agents can recognize, underestimating failure rates in real deployments where adversarial requesters craft attacks outside the LLM's training distribution. - The benchmark has no existing method that was designed specifically for multi-principal shared-memory governance — all baselines are recall-oriented systems repurposed. This means low MGS scores cannot yet distinguish between "current architectures are fundamentally limited" and "no governance-aware training signal has been applied"; a fine-tuned or RLHF-trained governance agent is absent from the evaluation. - The multiplicative MGS treats all three dimensions as equally weighted. In high-stakes domains (medical), access-control violations and active-forgetting failures likely warrant higher penalties than equal weighting implies; a practitioner applying GateMem to medical agent development has no guidance on domain-appropriate weighting. - Human validation covers only the Medical domain from one evaluation run; whether GPT-4o judge reliability holds equally for Office (with its more ambiguous delegation and role-adjacency attacks) or Household (with fluid family-member authorization) is not demonstrated. - The paper evaluates only English-language episodes; multi-lingual institutional deployments (common in medical and educational settings) are not covered and may exhibit different failure modes. Bottom Line A practitioner building memory-augmented LLM agents for any shared institutional setting gains both a ready-to-run benchmark and a sobering empirical result — no current architecture reliably governs shared memory — that makes the case for governance-aware agent design as an open research problem.

Noise-resistant discovery of identifier-sound object-centric process models with dynamic identity relations

link.springer.com · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces extended object-centric process tree and Petri net formalisms plus a noise-resistant discovery algorithm that automatically constructs identifier-sound process models capturing ten types of dynamic identity relations between business objects from object-centric event logs. - Why it's important: Existing object-centric discovery algorithms either ignore identity relations (producing imprecise models that permit impossible object combinations) or lack soundness guarantees, blocking reliable conformance checking and deviation detection on ERP and CRM logs. - How it does it: It extends the DF²-Miner with noise thresholds over multiplicities, activity counts, and edge frequencies, then checks all language-equivalent candidate trees for the ten identity relation types in a partial-order-driven sequence, wrapping each detected relation in a joint τ-transition construction proven to preserve identifier-soundness by induction. - The good: Identifier-soundness is proven formally by induction across all ten relation types and all translation cases, and the algorithm runs in under 60 seconds on 9 of 10 tested logs (up to 38,528 events, 12 object types) across all 36 parameter combinations. - The missing: No conformance-checking experiment quantifies the precision improvement that is only proven theoretically; object splits and merges were never detected on any evaluated log, leaving that sub-formalism practically unvalidated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for process mining researchers and practitioners who need formally guaranteed sound models for object-centric ERP processes; safely skippable for those working in flat-trace process mining or needing large-scale validated precision numbers.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces extended object-centric process tree and Petri net formalisms plus a noise-resistant discovery algorithm that automatically constructs identifier-sound process models capturing ten types of dynamic identity relations between business objects from object-centric event logs. - Why it's important: Existing object-centric discovery algorithms either ignore identity relations (producing imprecise models that permit impossible object combinations) or lack soundness guarantees, blocking reliable conformance checking and deviation detection on ERP and CRM logs. - How it does it: It extends the DF²-Miner with noise thresholds over multiplicities, activity counts, and edge frequencies, then checks all language-equivalent candidate trees for the ten identity relation types in a partial-order-driven sequence, wrapping each detected relation in a joint τ-transition construction proven to preserve identifier-soundness by induction. - The good: Identifier-soundness is proven formally by induction across all ten relation types and all translation cases, and the algorithm runs in under 60 seconds on 9 of 10 tested logs (up to 38,528 events, 12 object types) across all 36 parameter combinations. - The missing: No conformance-checking experiment quantifies the precision improvement that is only proven theoretically; object splits and merges were never detected on any evaluated log, leaving that sub-formalism practically unvalidated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for process mining researchers and practitioners who need formally guaranteed sound models for object-centric ERP processes; safely skippable for those working in flat-trace process mining or needing large-scale validated precision numbers. Problem & Motivation Real business processes recorded in ERP and CRM systems involve multiple interacting objects of different types simultaneously. In an order-to-cash process, for example, the exact set of items associated with a sales order during placement should be the same set invoiced, packed, delivered, and collected. These identity-based constraints between object types form dynamically during process execution rather than being fixed in advance. Object-centric process mining must discover models that reflect both the control flow of each object type and these inter-type identity constraints. Without identity relations, discovered models accept behaviors where arbitrary combinations of objects interact — producing analytically useless models. Prior approaches either ignore identity relations entirely, treat them as statically pre-specified (which cannot account for identities first observed mid-execution), or model them without guaranteeing identifier-soundness — the property that the model has no deadlocks, no dead transitions, and that all objects can reach a terminal state. Violation of identifier-soundness blocks the applicability of downstream conformance-checking and deviation-detection techniques. Key Idea The core technical insight is that any dynamic identity relation between two sets of object types T₁ and T₂ can be uniformly encoded in an extended object-centric Petri net as a pair of joint τ-transitions — one that fuses individual object tokens into a combined object-set token (enforcing the coupling), and one that later splits them back — and that this before/during/after construction preserves identifier-soundness by construction if the subnet it wraps is already identifier-sound. By restricting the embedding of identity-relation operators in process trees to exactly the configurations that admit this inductive argument, the extended tree formalism guarantees identifier-soundness for every tree that can be written in the language, trading some modeling flexibility for a structural soundness certificate that does not require runtime verification. Methodology The approach has five layers. First, ten identity relation types are formally defined over object-centric event logs via first-order conditions on event-object involvement sets: strict synchronization (exact same object sets across a group of activities), partitioning and overlapping subset synchronizations (subsets of the strictly synchronized objects used in additional activities), general implication (identities of T₁ determine identities of T₂ but not vice versa), temporally ordered implication (one-to-many unfolding sequentially), temporally concurrent implication (one-to-many unfolding simultaneously), k-batched implication (bounded concurrent capacity), and object split/merge (objects of one type consumed and new objects of another type created). Second, object-centric Petri nets are extended by allowing places to hold object-set tokens and transitions to carry functions mapping input to output token combinations; object-centric process trees gain ten new parameterized identity-relation operator nodes with structural restrictions enforced to ensure the inductive soundness proof closes. The proof proceeds via four lemmas covering identity-relation operator translations, split/merge translations, control-flow operator translations, and leaf node translations, concluding in Theorem 1. Third, the DF²-Miner is extended with a single noise threshold μ₁ ∈ [0,1] applied uniformly to object multiplicity properties, activity node retention, and edge frequency filtering — a deliberate simplification trading parameter richness for practical usability. Fourth, because the DF²-Miner does not guarantee a syntactically normal-form tree, the algorithm uses published language-preserving transformation rules to enumerate all syntactically distinct language-equivalent candidate trees. Fifth, for each candidate tree the algorithm recursively checks all pairs of candidate type sets for identity relations under a second noise threshold μ₂, following the detection order induced by the formal partial-order among relation types (strict synchronization detected before subset synchronizations, which are detected before implications, which are detected before splits/merges). The candidate tree with the most unique identity relations is returned. Fitness equality and precision improvement under μ₂ = 1 are proven analytically by induction on the replay semantics. Key Results Runtime: applied to 10 public OCEL 2.0 logs (1,798 to 38,528 events, 532 to 23,771 objects, 3–12 object types, 9–24 activities) on an Intel Core i9-13900 HX with 64 GB RAM. Across all 36 parameter combinations per log (μ₁, μ₂ ∈ {0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 0.99}), average runtimes ranged from 2.85s (PM4PY Github) to 20.84s (Hinge Production), with a maximum of 126 seconds. All synchronization and implication subtypes were observed across the 10 logs. No object split or merge was detected on any log. Identity relation counts ranged from 2.98 per log (μ₁ = 0.5 strictest retention) to 6.71 (μ₁ = 0.99), consistent with the expectation that retaining more activities creates more detectable relation opportunities. Stricter μ₂ produced fewer but more restrictive relation types. The example analysis on a recruiting log (10, 6,980 events) at μ₁ = 0.9 found a synchronization between applicants and applications at μ₂ = 0.7 indicating roughly 30% of applicants are linked to multiple applications simultaneously, flagging a rework pattern. On an order management log (01, 21,008 events) at μ₁ = 0.9, strict synchronization between items and orders was found during order confirmation and payment at μ₂ = 1.0, but a subset synchronization appeared at lower thresholds, indicating items in the placement step differ from items in fulfillment — an analytically actionable anomaly. No quantitative precision improvement is measured; the formal guarantee is cited in lieu of an experiment. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMALISM. The paper is genuinely novel on three dimensions: (a) the first complete formal taxonomy of ten dynamic identity relation types in object-centric event logs derived from a systematic literature survey rather than invented ad hoc; (b) extended Petri net and process tree formalisms that represent all ten types while guaranteeing identifier-soundness by construction — no prior formalism with automated discovery achieves this; (c) the first discovery algorithm that constructs identifier-sound object-centric process models with dynamic identity relations from noisy logs. Individual building blocks (DF²-Miner, object-centric process trees, the extended Petri net token model) are prior work by the same group; the integration, soundness proof, and noise handling are new. Rigor & Reproducibility Theorem 1 is proven by induction with four supporting lemmas; the proofs are structural and presented in sufficient detail within the paper. Fitness and precision guarantees are analytic, not empirical — no experiment measures actual precision improvement because object-centric conformance checking is currently too slow on large object sets (explicitly acknowledged). No ablations isolate the contribution of individual identity relation types. Runtimes are single-run measurements with no error bars or repeated trials. Code and all object-centric logs are publicly available at github.com/Nik314/Dynamic-Identity-Relations; source logs come from the OCEL 2.0 public library (ocel-standard.org). The paper is peer-reviewed (received December 2025, accepted June 2026, published Process Science 2026). A methodological concern: the detection order among relation types is asserted to be the uniquely reasonable ordering based on formal partial-order relationships, but no experiment tests alternative orderings on real data. The failure to detect object split/merge in any log is noted but not resolved — the authors cannot distinguish between structural over-restriction and genuine absence. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS — a Python implementation is evaluated on real logs. - Practical: Runs on a commodity desktop (Intel i9, 64 GB RAM); publicly available Python implementation; input format is OCEL 2.0, an adopted standard with library support. - Efficient: Median runtime under 20 seconds across all tested configurations. Maximum 126 seconds. No asymptotic complexity analysis; runtime correlates empirically with activity-to-object-type connectivity density. - Effective: The algorithm consistently detects 3–7 identity relations per log and the example analyses generate actionable hypotheses about rework and anomalies. Formal fitness preservation is proven; precision improvement is claimed but not measured. - Scalable: Tested up to 38,528 events and 12 object types, which is moderate for process mining. No experiment at 10× scale; no analysis of how runtime grows with log size, object type count, or activity count beyond the empirical scatter. The work delivers a Python software artifact and a public GitHub repository. No trained model or dataset is contributed beyond re-packaging OCEL 2.0 public logs. Strengths - The identifier-soundness guarantee by construction is a hard formal result proven by induction, not an empirical claim. It is the first such guarantee for a discovery algorithm covering dynamic identity relations, which makes this work directly useful for any downstream analysis that requires sound models. - The taxonomy of ten relation types is the most systematic coverage of the design space to date, grounded in a comparative literature survey (Table 1) that maps prior formalisms to relation types — making the paper a useful reference even independent of the algorithm. - The noise-resistant DF²-Miner extension addresses a practical necessity for ERP data: three qualitatively different noise sources (multiplicity, activity count, edge frequency) are handled without requiring separate parameterization per dimension. - The language-equivalent candidate tree enumeration is a non-obvious step that prevents the syntactic structure of the initial DF²-Miner output from artificially limiting which identity relations can be detected — this is a subtle but important correctness contribution. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The structural restrictions of extended object-centric process trees may be too strict, potentially preventing detection of identity relations that are genuinely present but incompatible with the allowed embedding structure; the failure to detect any object splits or merges in all 10 evaluated logs is directly attributed to this as a plausible cause. Conformance checking to quantify actual precision improvement is computationally infeasible on object-centric logs with large object sets using current methods, so the precision improvement proof cannot be validated experimentally. Future work is identified as relieving structural restrictions while preserving identifier-soundness and developing efficient conformance checking for identity relations. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The evaluation uses exclusively public OCEL 2.0 benchmark logs, which are small to moderate (max 38,528 events, 12 object types) and may not reflect production ERP log complexity. No experiment on a real SAP or Oracle production log is included, so generalizability to logs with hundreds of object types or millions of events is uncharacterized. - A single threshold μ₁ governing three qualitatively different noise phenomena (multiplicities, node frequency, edge frequency) will over-filter in some dimensions while under-filtering in others on any real log. No experiment teases apart which noise dimension drives the observed identity-relation count sensitivity. - No head-to-head comparison against any alternative approach (OC-DECLARE, typed Jackson nets, synchronization-sensitive OCPNs) on the same logs, even informally. The related work table (Table 1) establishes positioning but without experimental corroboration. - Runtime is reported as single measurements without variance; on the logs that reached 126 seconds, it is unclear whether this is deterministic or variable, and the scaling behavior with log size is not characterized beyond observing a correlation with activity-type connectivity. - The partial-order-driven detection priority is justified formally but not empirically: it is possible that on some logs a different priority order yields more informative models (e.g., detecting a coarser implication rather than a stricter synchronization that absorbs it). This is unexamined. Bottom Line A process mining researcher working on object-centric analysis of ERP or CRM logs gains the first formally guaranteed identifier-sound discovery algorithm with dynamic identity relations, backed by a public implementation — but should note that claimed precision improvements are unquantified experimentally and the evaluation scale is modest.

Qwen-AgentWorld: Language World Models for General Agents

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Qwen-AgentWorld-35B-A3B and Qwen-AgentWorld-397B-A17B, the first native language world models (LWMs) capable of simulating seven agent-interaction domains (MCP, Search, Terminal, SWE, Android, Web, OS) within a single model, trained via a three-stage CPT→SFT→RL pipeline on more than 10M environment interaction trajectories, and demonstrates two complementary downstream applications: a decoupled environment simulator for Sim RL and a unified agent foundation model. - Why it's important: LLM agents have focused almost exclusively on the policy side of the agent–environment loop, leaving environment simulation as an unresolved bottleneck that limits agent training to whatever real-world interactions are available and feasible; a general-purpose world model provides infinite, controllable, scalable training signal. - How it does it: The core trick is framing world modeling as the primary training objective from continual pre-training onward — not as a post-hoc addition — and sharpening simulation fidelity through a hybrid rubric-plus-rule RL stage that resists reward hacking via content-type classification and rule-based binary anchors. - The good: Single-turn, non-agentic LWM RL warm-up (predicting next environment state, no tool calls) transfers to multi-turn, tool-calling agentic benchmarks across seven diverse tasks with consistent gains (Terminal-Bench 2.0 +6.30, Claw-Eval +11.28, BFCL v4 +8.96), including +11.28 and +9.67 on two domains entirely absent from LWM training data. - The missing: The paper reports no statistical significance tests for the AgentWorldBench main results, and the margin over the best frontier model (58.71 vs. GPT-5.4's 58.25) is too narrow to be meaningful without error bars; GUI domain performance clearly lags Claude Opus 4.6 (59.69 vs. 61.74) because the model is text-only, a gap the paper acknowledges but does not close. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on agent training infrastructure, Sim RL, or world-model-augmented agents; the AgentWorldBench construction methodology and the reward-design ablations are the most transferable contributions.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Qwen-AgentWorld-35B-A3B and Qwen-AgentWorld-397B-A17B, the first native language world models (LWMs) capable of simulating seven agent-interaction domains (MCP, Search, Terminal, SWE, Android, Web, OS) within a single model, trained via a three-stage CPT→SFT→RL pipeline on more than 10M environment interaction trajectories, and demonstrates two complementary downstream applications: a decoupled environment simulator for Sim RL and a unified agent foundation model. - Why it's important: LLM agents have focused almost exclusively on the policy side of the agent–environment loop, leaving environment simulation as an unresolved bottleneck that limits agent training to whatever real-world interactions are available and feasible; a general-purpose world model provides infinite, controllable, scalable training signal. - How it does it: The core trick is framing world modeling as the primary training objective from continual pre-training onward — not as a post-hoc addition — and sharpening simulation fidelity through a hybrid rubric-plus-rule RL stage that resists reward hacking via content-type classification and rule-based binary anchors. - The good: Single-turn, non-agentic LWM RL warm-up (predicting next environment state, no tool calls) transfers to multi-turn, tool-calling agentic benchmarks across seven diverse tasks with consistent gains (Terminal-Bench 2.0 +6.30, Claw-Eval +11.28, BFCL v4 +8.96), including +11.28 and +9.67 on two domains entirely absent from LWM training data. - The missing: The paper reports no statistical significance tests for the AgentWorldBench main results, and the margin over the best frontier model (58.71 vs. GPT-5.4's 58.25) is too narrow to be meaningful without error bars; GUI domain performance clearly lags Claude Opus 4.6 (59.69 vs. 61.74) because the model is text-only, a gap the paper acknowledges but does not close. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on agent training infrastructure, Sim RL, or world-model-augmented agents; the AgentWorldBench construction methodology and the reward-design ablations are the most transferable contributions. Problem & Motivation: LLM agents operating in language environments (terminals, browsers, IDEs, MCP servers, Android) need both a policy (states → actions) and a world model (states × actions → next states), yet almost all current research targets the policy alone. In practice this means agent training is bounded by what real environments can provide: they are expensive to run at scale, irreproducible, unsafe for certain domains (proprietary APIs, healthcare, finance), and incapable of systematically exposing rare failure modes. Agents trained on uncontrolled real-environment rollouts therefore remain brittle at edge cases. Prior work on language world models either covers a single domain (web, terminal, or SWE in isolation) or fine-tunes a general LLM post-hoc, which fails to inject the deep environment-transition knowledge needed for high-fidelity multi-turn simulation. Key Idea: The single core insight is that world modeling should be the training objective from the first stage of pre-training, not an auxiliary loss bolted on after a general LLM is trained. Concretely, this means the continual pre-training (CPT) stage uses environment trajectories as the primary corpus and learns next-state prediction via standard language modeling, so state-transition dynamics are embedded in the base representations before SFT or RL ever run. RL then sharpens micro-level fidelity (byte-level arithmetic, URL identifiers, cross-turn schema consistency) via a multi-dimensional rubric reward that the model could not learn from SFT alone because SFT data is too sparse and surface-level. As a downstream consequence, a model that has genuinely internalized environment dynamics can act as both a simulator (decoupled) and as an agent whose thinking trace now includes mental simulation of consequences before committing to actions (unified). Methodology: The training pipeline has three strictly disjoint data pools and three sequential stages. Stage 1, Continual Pre-Training (CPT), trains on environment trajectories from three sources — a dedicated agent-infrastructure pipeline (containerized sandboxes for Terminal/SWE/MCP, persistent Android/browser/desktop environments for GUI), open interaction traces cleaned via a multi-agent pipeline, and in-house agentic trajectories — plus specialized-domain world-knowledge corpora covering cybersecurity, medicine, finance, and legal domains. The objective is standard next-token prediction, but with turn-level information-theoretic loss masking: each (action, observation) turn is characterized by four statistics (Overlap OL, Novelty Nov, Jaccard Jac, length ratio R), assigned to one of seven semantic categories (retrieval, expansion, action, transform, boilerplate, echo, other), and masked at ratios from 5% to 100% so that boilerplate API echoes do not dominate gradients. System prompts for all seven domains are optimized via AutoResearch (12 parallel optimization runs × 10 iterations each, yielding 12 distinct template styles per domain). Stage 2, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), activates explicit next-state-prediction reasoning: starting from 10,250 candidate trajectories, three rollouts per query are generated and scored by an independent judge; only the highest-quality trajectory is kept, and queries below a minimum threshold are discarded, yielding 7,094 trajectories (69.2% retention). Stage 3, Reinforcement Learning (RL), applies GSPO with a hybrid reward combining a five-dimensional LLM judge (Format, Factuality, Consistency, Realism, Quality, each scored 1–5 and summed, range [5,25]) and rule-based binary verifiers (scaled to [0,25]) at a 9:1 rubric-to-rule ratio. Critical design decisions: (i) restricting RL trajectories to exactly one randomly sampled turn per multi-turn trajectory prevents the "Echo Trap" reward-collapse failure mode caused by shared long prefixes; (ii) content-type classification in the judge prompt enforces exact matching for deterministic content, nullifying self-praise in deterministic regions; (iii) strict tag extraction prevents the model's thinking block from inflating rubric scores. For downstream Sim RL (decoupled), the world model generates synthetic environments from seed scenarios through two controllability modes: environment adaptation (targeted perturbations — injecting API errors, paginated responses, partial failures) and fictional-world construction (fully invented, self-consistent environments with no real-world counterpart). For the unified application, the same model undergoes LWM RL on single-turn next-state prediction with no tool calls, then is evaluated directly on multi-turn agentic benchmarks with no additional fine-tuning. Key Results: On AgentWorldBench (2,170 samples across seven domains from nine established benchmarks, judged by GPT-5.4 with Spearman ρ = 0.92–0.99 cross-judge consistency), Qwen-AgentWorld-397B-A17B achieves an average score of 58.71, versus GPT-5.4 at 58.25 and Claude Opus 4.8 at 56.59. On text domains Qwen-AgentWorld-397B-A17B scores 58.07 average, leading GPT-5.4 (56.84) by 1.23 points, with the largest advantage on SWE (68.49 vs. 66.29) and Terminal (57.73 vs. 53.69). On GUI domains, Claude Opus 4.8 (60.93) and Claude Opus 4.6 (61.74) outperform Qwen-AgentWorld-397B-A17B (59.69), a deficit the paper attributes to multimodal pre-training absent from the text-only world model. Rule-based verification (Table 10) shows GPT-5.4 ranking first overall at 72.93, with Qwen-AgentWorld-397B-A17B second at 67.12. World-model training provides consistent lift over Qwen base checkpoints: at 35B the gain is 8.66 points (47.73 → 56.39), while Qwen3.6-Plus (50.81) and Qwen3.6-Max-Preview (52.42) without LWM training score well below. Cross-domain generalization (RL on Terminal only): MCP gains +5.0, SWE gains +11.5, Search gains +11.8, all within 100 RL steps, despite Terminal and MCP having syntactically unrelated outputs. As a decoupled simulator: Claw-Eval improves from 65.4 to 69.7 (+4.3) and QwenClawBench from 47.9 to 55.0 (+7.1) when using Qwen-AgentWorld-397B-A17B versus Qwen3.6-Plus as simulator; uncontrolled Sim RL provides essentially zero gain (Tool Decathlon drops from 32.4 to 31.5), whereas controlled Sim RL raises Tool Decathlon to 36.1 (+3.7) and MCPMark from 21.5 to 33.8 (+12.3); WideSearch F1 by Item rises from 34.02 to 50.31 (+16.29) at 35B via fictional-world Sim RL. As an agent foundation model (LWM RL warm-up): Terminal-Bench 2.0 +6.30, SWE-Bench Verified +3.39, SWE-Bench Pro +5.24, WideSearch F1 Item +12.79, Claw-Eval +11.28, QwenClawBench +9.67, BFCL v4 +8.96 — all on benchmarks strictly disjoint from LWM training data. Environment prediction accuracy in thinking traces improves from 69.9% to 78.3% (+8.4%) after LWM RL, providing direct mechanistic evidence for the generalization. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (Qwen-AgentWorld models and training pipeline), NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (AgentWorldBench), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (cross-domain transfer of LWM RL warm-up). The training pipeline (CPT→SFT→RL for world modeling) extends the standard three-stage recipe but is genuinely novel in applying it to a single model covering seven domains simultaneously, starting from environment modeling as the pre-training objective rather than fine-tuning a general LLM. The unified agent application (LWM warm-up for downstream agentic tasks) is a new empirical finding with a mechanistic explanation. The fictional-world construction for Search Sim RL is independently interesting: fully invented self-consistent environments produce agents that generalize to real search tasks. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations on reward design (reference-reward vs. Turing-test reward vs. five-dimensional rubric) justify the chosen hybrid and identify concrete failure modes. Training dynamics across 440 RL steps are tracked and reported per dimension, revealing that Factuality (the hardest dimension) shows the largest relative improvement (11.3%) yet remains the lowest-scoring throughout, a credibility-positive finding. AgentWorldBench evaluation uses three judge models for calibration (Gemini 3 Flash, Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.2) and confirms rank-level consistency (Spearman ρ = 0.92–0.99), reducing concern about judge choice. The paper is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2606.24597v1, June 23, 2026), not yet peer-reviewed. No statistical significance tests (p-values, confidence intervals, bootstrap) are reported for the main AgentWorldBench results; the margin over GPT-5.4 (0.46 points on a 0–100 scale) is asserted without uncertainty bounds. Code and model weights are listed as available (GitHub and HuggingFace links on the title page), though the specific repos are not stated in the text. SFT and RL training data draw exclusively from in-house accumulated trajectories, so independent reproduction requires replicating the infrastructure. The numbers for downstream agentic benchmarks come from 3–5 independent rollouts for most benchmarks, which is reported but limited. Several baselines for the agent foundation model comparison are self-reported vendor model-card numbers. Deployability: Profile: EMPIRICAL (system with experiments). - Practical: The 35B-A3B model (mixture-of-experts with 3B active parameters) can run on commercially available hardware at inference; the 397B-A17B model requires multi-GPU infrastructure consistent with large-scale MoE deployment, which is commercially available but not consumer-grade. - Efficient: At inference the 35B-A3B model uses 3B active parameters per forward pass; context windows up to 256k tokens are required for long agentic trajectories, so memory at inference is non-trivial. Training cost is not quantified in the paper. - Effective: Evidence is strong for text-based domains where the model outperforms all frontier models including GPT-5.4; evidence for GUI domains is weaker, with the model ranking fifth out of evaluated models on Android (60.20 vs. Claude Opus 4.8 at 61.50). - Scalable: The paper demonstrates two model scales (35B-A3B and 397B-A17B) and shows consistent gains at both, which is positive. The fictional-world Sim RL approach scales environment diversity to 1k–4k constructed scenarios, but stress-testing at 10× or 100× the current domain count or trajectory volume is not evaluated. The paper delivers trained model weights as a deployable artifact. Strengths: - The unified trajectory schema across seven structurally heterogeneous domains (text-based and GUI, stateless and stateful) is a genuine infrastructure contribution that enables cross-domain transfer, confirmed empirically by the Terminal-only RL cross-domain experiment. - The fictional-world construction for Search Sim RL is the most surprising finding: agents trained entirely on invented, factually isolated search environments generalize to real-world search tasks at meaningful gains (+16.29 F1 by Item at 35B), directly proving that the learned behaviors (query reformulation, multi-source cross-referencing, iterative aggregation) are structural rather than content-dependent. - The turn-level information-theoretic loss masking is a principled and tool-agnostic technique for filtering low-information turns from multi-turn RL trajectories, motivated by the concrete failure mode (boilerplate echoes dominating gradients) rather than by heuristic. - The AgentWorldBench construction principles — widely-used queries, frontier-agent trajectories, real ground-truth observations, out-of-distribution partitioning at the data-source level — address the most common evaluation validity concerns for world-model assessment. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: GUI domain performance trails frontier models with multimodal pre-training, attributed to the text-only nature of Qwen-AgentWorld's GUI representation (accessibility trees and UI hierarchies rather than pixel frames). The authors list multimodal extension as explicit future work. The paper also notes that Sim RL effectiveness degrades without a sufficiently detailed initial state, and that current agents fail to leverage the world model as a foresight tool (citing Qian et al. 2026), motivating the native pre-training approach. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The AgentWorldBench benchmark is constructed from trajectories of five frontier models including Qwen-family models; despite the out-of-distribution partition claim, the benchmark inherently reflects the difficulty distribution of frontier-agent behavior on existing benchmarks, potentially undersampling the long-tail failure modes that a world model would most need to cover. The 0.46-point lead over GPT-5.4 on the main benchmark is reported as a headline result without confidence intervals; given the judge model variability (Gemini 3 Flash scores systematically higher, GPT-5.2 scores systematically lower), this margin could easily flip under a different judge choice despite high rank consistency. The agent foundation model results use a single base checkpoint (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-SFT) for the warm-up experiment; it is not shown whether the same transfer gains hold from a stronger or differently trained base. The controllable Sim RL results on Tool Decathlon and MCPMark use a single evaluation run without rollout averaging, making it hard to assess variance. Search remains the hardest domain for all models (best score 37.82), but the paper does not analyze whether this reflects a fundamental limit of text-based world modeling for evolving web content or a data-coverage issue. Bottom Line: A practitioner designing agent training pipelines for tool-use or multi-step agentic tasks gains substantively from this work: the fictional-world Sim RL result and the agent-foundation-model warm-up finding are concrete, reproducible techniques that can improve agent performance without requiring additional real-environment interaction, and the AgentWorldBench evaluation framework is the most rigorous public benchmark for language world model assessment to date.

Data Recipes for Agentic Models

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a six-stage SFT data curation pipeline (OpenThoughts-Agent) validated through 100+ controlled ablation experiments, along with a 100K-example training set and OpenThinkerAgent-32B, the strongest open-data agentic model at ≤32B scale averaged across seven diverse benchmarks. - Why it's important: Prior open data efforts (SWE-Smith, SERA, Nemotron-Terminal) each target a single benchmark, leaving the community without actionable guidance on which data choices drive generalization across diverse agentic tasks — a gap that makes it nearly impossible to improve broadly capable open agents systematically. - How it does it: Each of six pipeline stages (task sourcing, mixing, augmentation, filtering, teacher selection, rollout filtering) is ablated independently by training Qwen3-8B on 10K trajectories and measuring z-score-normalized accuracy across three core benchmarks; a synthetic task augmentation strategy then overcomes the task-diversity bottleneck that causes upsampling to plateau. - The good: Task source selection accounts for up to 30pp variation on SWE-Bench Verified-100 and the final model reaches 54.0% on SWE-Bench Verified and 26.2% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, outperforming Nemotron-Terminal-32B (41.9% and 25.1%) while also generalizing to five held-out OOD benchmarks. - The missing: The RL investigation is limited to 8B scale due to compute constraints, so whether the SFT+RL recipe transfers to 32B is untested; the base model contribution (Qwen3 pretraining) is never isolated from data pipeline effects. - Read it or not: Essential for anyone building or studying open-data agentic model training pipelines; the empirical prescriptions are directly actionable and would be expensive to rediscover independently.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a six-stage SFT data curation pipeline (OpenThoughts-Agent) validated through 100+ controlled ablation experiments, along with a 100K-example training set and OpenThinkerAgent-32B, the strongest open-data agentic model at ≤32B scale averaged across seven diverse benchmarks. - Why it's important: Prior open data efforts (SWE-Smith, SERA, Nemotron-Terminal) each target a single benchmark, leaving the community without actionable guidance on which data choices drive generalization across diverse agentic tasks — a gap that makes it nearly impossible to improve broadly capable open agents systematically. - How it does it: Each of six pipeline stages (task sourcing, mixing, augmentation, filtering, teacher selection, rollout filtering) is ablated independently by training Qwen3-8B on 10K trajectories and measuring z-score-normalized accuracy across three core benchmarks; a synthetic task augmentation strategy then overcomes the task-diversity bottleneck that causes upsampling to plateau. - The good: Task source selection accounts for up to 30pp variation on SWE-Bench Verified-100 and the final model reaches 54.0% on SWE-Bench Verified and 26.2% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, outperforming Nemotron-Terminal-32B (41.9% and 25.1%) while also generalizing to five held-out OOD benchmarks. - The missing: The RL investigation is limited to 8B scale due to compute constraints, so whether the SFT+RL recipe transfers to 32B is untested; the base model contribution (Qwen3 pretraining) is never isolated from data pipeline effects. - Read it or not: Essential for anyone building or studying open-data agentic model training pipelines; the empirical prescriptions are directly actionable and would be expensive to rediscover independently. Problem & Motivation Training broadly capable agentic language models requires high-quality (task, trajectory) pairs for supervised fine-tuning, but publicly documented curation pipelines are scarce. Existing efforts — SWE-Smith, SERA, Nemotron-Terminal, OpenSWE — optimize for a single benchmark (SWE-Bench or Terminal-Bench) and provide little detail on data choices. As a result, the open research community cannot understand which curation decisions drive generalization across the diverse agentic tasks agents encounter in practice: software engineering, terminal use, tool calling, healthcare, finance, and general assistance. The gap is illustrated by DeepSeekV4, whose 50-page paper describes training data in only two paragraphs despite open-sourcing its weights. Key Idea The central insight is that task source selection is the dominant lever in agentic SFT pipelines — causing a 30pp spread on SWE-Bench Verified-100 across 95 sourcing strategies, larger than any other pipeline stage — and that stronger teacher models are not better teachers. GLM-4.7-AWQ, a weaker benchmark performer, outperforms GPT-5.3-Codex by roughly 5pp on Terminal-Bench 2.0 when used as the trajectory-generation teacher. A complementary insight is that task-description diversity, not rollout volume from fixed sources, determines whether performance continues improving beyond ~32K examples: synthetic augmentation of task descriptions unlocks continued gains to 100K, while upsampling plateaus. Methodology The pipeline has six stages, each ablated independently with Qwen3-8B fine-tuned on 10K trajectories (160 GPU-hours per run on GH200s), evaluated by computing per-benchmark z-scores and averaging across three core benchmarks (SWE-Bench Verified-100, OT-TBLite, Terminal-Bench 2.0). Stage 1 (Source Tasks) evaluates 95 task generation strategies spanning synthetic issue resolution, human-written infrastructure questions, competitive programming, and more; top performers include SWESmith, StackExchange SuperUser/Tezos, and IssueTasks. Stage 2 (Mix Tasks) evaluates top-N mixing with uniform sampling; top-4 to top-8 outperforms single-source by avoiding over-specialization. Stage 3 (Augment Tasks) tests LLM-driven augmentation (Constrain→Harden, multi-source mixing, trace hints); no intervention reliably outperforms the un-augmented baseline. Stage 4 (Filter Tasks) uses GPT-5 response length as a difficulty proxy; selecting the longest-response tasks yields +3pp average improvement over random. Stage 5 (Teacher Model) ablates five proprietary or semi-open teachers; GLM-4.7-AWQ outperforms GPT-5.3-Codex by ~5pp on Terminal-Bench 2.0. Stage 6 (Filter Rollouts) applies heuristics; retaining trajectories with ≥5 turns outperforms timeout-filtering and subagent-trace removal, and a compute-controlled ablation (Table 15, matched ~145M token budget) confirms the gain reflects trajectory quality rather than additional training compute. The final 100K dataset uses synthetic instruction rewriting to expand the Tezos subset from 997 to 21K+ unique task descriptions, resolving the diversity bottleneck. For RL, the paper ablates eight data sources on 8B models using RLOO with binary rewards from verifier success; pymethods2test (competitive programming recast as Python contracts) ranks first. The SFT+RL combination starts from a moderately strong SFT checkpoint, consistent with prior findings that undertrained SFT models benefit more from RL. Key Results OpenThinkerAgent-32B achieves 44.8% average across seven agentic benchmarks: 54.0% on SWE-Bench Verified (500 tasks), 26.2% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, 85.9% on BFCL-Parity, 47.8% on MedAgentBench, 23.6% on GAIA-127, and 44.0% on FinanceAgent-Terminal. This is 3.9pp above Nemotron-Terminal-32B (40.9%) and exceeds all open-data ≤32B models in Table 1 on the average. Scaling curves show the OT-Agent dataset outperforms Nemotron-Terminal-Corpus at every training set size in compute-controlled comparisons (Figure 1). At 8B scale, OT-Agent-ColdSFT+RL-8B achieves 27.9% average across seven benchmarks, above the strongest existing ≤8B baselines. Task source ablation produces the largest spread of any pipeline stage: Terminal-Bench 2.0 accuracy ranges from 10.9% (worst source) to 0.4% (best source) when varying only source, confirming sourcing dominates. Run-to-run RL reproducibility is characterized at ~1.6pp ID and ~2.0pp OOD spread, substantially above benchmark evaluation noise and substantially below the reported SFT and RL gains. The behavioral analysis of RL (Appendix F) reveals that pymethods2test induces genuine exploratory expansion (think tokens +116%, tool calls +31%, self-correction +81%) while llm-verifier-freelancer induces compaction, and an LLM judge (gpt-5-2025-08-07) prefers the post-RL hero policy on 83.3% of 30 same-task trace pairs. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The pipeline applies established supervised fine-tuning and RLOO over existing task sources; what is genuinely novel is the breadth of ablation coverage (95 task sources, five teacher models, six pipeline stages, eight RL sources) and the empirical findings that contradict common assumptions: stronger teachers are not better teachers, LLM augmentation of task descriptions does not help, and task-source diversity is the binding constraint at scale. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations report standard error across three stochastic re-runs per benchmark. The compute-controlled ablation (Table 15) properly disentangles token-budget effects from trajectory quality effects by equating training tokens, confirming the min-turns filter finding is causal. Five held-out OOD benchmarks are evaluated only after pipeline decisions are fixed, preventing leakage into stage selection. Run-to-run RL reproducibility is formally characterized with three near-replicate runs (Table 20). Code, pipeline, training data, and model weights are publicly released at openthoughts.ai, with pinned commit hashes for all frameworks (Appendix D.8). Two methodological concerns: (1) All SFT ablations use Qwen3-8B as the base model, so stage rankings may not transfer to other model families. (2) The paper could not fully reproduce the published OpenSWE 62.4% SWE-Bench Verified number (measured 44.0% on SWE-Bench Verified-100 subset) due to unpublished scaffold components, flagging a reproducibility gap in the broader ecosystem rather than in this paper's own work. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile (SFT training, RL training, benchmark evaluation on live agent harnesses). - Practical: Training requires 24 nodes of 4×GH200 per node for 32B SFT (~5h wall time at 100K scale) and 24×A100-80GB for RL (~46h); beyond single-GPU access but within reach of HPC-equipped academic groups. - Efficient: Each 10K ablation costs 160 GPU-hours on GH200s, enabling dozens of parallel ablations; the final 100K run is proportionally more expensive but the pipeline is fully described and reproducible. - Effective: OpenThinkerAgent-32B achieves best open-data performance on a 7-benchmark diverse suite, providing evidence that the pipeline solves the stated generalization problem rather than only optimizing proxy benchmarks. - Scalable: Synthetic task augmentation overcomes the upsampling plateau at 31.6K and continues improving to 100K; behavior at multi-million-trajectory scales is explicitly flagged as untested. The paper delivers software artifacts: training sets, data pipeline code, and trained model weights at openthoughts.ai. Strengths - The 95-source task ablation is the most comprehensive public ranking of agentic data sources, providing empirically grounded guidance for a decision that is typically made opaquely or not documented. - The teacher-model inversion finding — GPT-5.3-Codex is the worst teacher despite being the strongest benchmark performer — is well-controlled across five candidates and directly actionable, contradicting a widespread implicit assumption in the field. - The compute-controlled ablation (Table 15) isolating trajectory quality from token-budget effects is methodologically sound and necessary; without it the min-turns filter finding would be confounded. - The mechanistic behavioral analysis of RL learning dynamics (Appendix F, Tables 21-22) traces per-trace shifts in think tokens, tool calls, self-correction, and task-completion rate, providing a causal account of why pymethods2test induces exploration while llm-verifier-freelancer induces compaction — going well beyond accuracy-number reporting. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: RL investigation is limited to 8B scale due to compute; whether the RL recipe transfers to 32B is open; the base model choice (Qwen3 family throughout) is not ablated so its contribution to final performance is not isolated; the largest training set is 100K trajectories and whether trends extrapolate to multi-million-trajectory regimes is untested. These are confronted directly in the conclusion, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All SFT ablations use Qwen3-8B as base, so the pipeline stage rankings (e.g., which sources are top-4, which filters help most) may not transfer to Llama-4, Mistral, or other families — the paper cannot claim universally optimal recipes. - The teacher model ablation covers five models, all proprietary or semi-open; the effect of fully open-weight teachers at similar capability tiers is unstudied, limiting actionability for resource-constrained settings. - The "stronger model ≠ better teacher" finding lacks a mechanistic explanation — the paper establishes the effect empirically but does not explain why GLM-4.7-AWQ generates better training signal (trajectory length distribution, error diversity, instruction-following fidelity?) than GPT-5.3-Codex. - The RL section uses a single algorithm (RLOO) from a single SFT starting point; interactions between SFT data quality and RL algorithm choice (e.g., PPO, GRPO with different KL settings) are not explored. - Task augmentation (Stage 3) is tested only with LLM-driven instruction rewriting and constraint/hardening; retrieval-based augmentation or cross-task interpolation are not evaluated, leaving open whether augmentation is fundamentally unhelpful or just the specific LLM-driven variants tested. Bottom Line Any researcher building or studying open-data agentic model training should read this paper — it is the most systematic public audit of SFT and RL data pipeline choices for agentic models, and its findings (task-source dominance, teacher-model inversion, augmentation futility, diversity as the scaling bottleneck) are directly actionable and would be expensive to rediscover independently.

Rethinking the Role of Efficient Attention in Hybrid Architectures

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper shows that efficient attention modules (sliding-window attention and recurrent sequence mixers) in hybrid LLMs act as optimization priors that shape how quickly full-attention layers develop long-range retrieval heads, and demonstrates that applying NoPE to the full-attention layers of a small-window SWA hybrid substantially improves long-context performance at no short-context cost. - Why it's important: Hybrid architectures are now standard in deployed frontier models (Gemma 3, GPT-4o, Qwen3, MiniMax-01), yet the design principles governing their long-context capability are poorly understood, leading practitioners to assume that more capable efficient-attention (wider windows, more expressive recurrence) automatically improves long-context performance — an assumption this paper overturns. - How it does it: Scaling-law fits across 7 architectures and 5 model sizes (15M–477M parameters), combined with four mechanistic experiments (receptive-field masking, layer-wise NIAH probing, gradient influence profiling, retrieval-head tracing), all converging on the same mechanistic account. - The good: SWA-128-NoPE improves RULER NIAH average from 65.91 to 82.31 at 0.66B parameters with no meaningful short-context regression (41.31 vs 41.32 ShortAvg), supported by four independent mechanistic evidence streams for the underlying causal claim. - The missing: The largest trained model is 0.66B parameters on ≤105B tokens — far below frontier scale — and the prevailing short-context pretraining followed by long-context extension recipe is not evaluated, so the conclusions may not transfer. - Read it or not: Practitioners and researchers designing hybrid LLM architectures should read this — the reframing of efficient attention as an optimization prior rather than a capability carrier is both non-obvious and immediately actionable.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper shows that efficient attention modules (sliding-window attention and recurrent sequence mixers) in hybrid LLMs act as optimization priors that shape how quickly full-attention layers develop long-range retrieval heads, and demonstrates that applying NoPE to the full-attention layers of a small-window SWA hybrid substantially improves long-context performance at no short-context cost. - Why it's important: Hybrid architectures are now standard in deployed frontier models (Gemma 3, GPT-4o, Qwen3, MiniMax-01), yet the design principles governing their long-context capability are poorly understood, leading practitioners to assume that more capable efficient-attention (wider windows, more expressive recurrence) automatically improves long-context performance — an assumption this paper overturns. - How it does it: Scaling-law fits across 7 architectures and 5 model sizes (15M–477M parameters), combined with four mechanistic experiments (receptive-field masking, layer-wise NIAH probing, gradient influence profiling, retrieval-head tracing), all converging on the same mechanistic account. - The good: SWA-128-NoPE improves RULER NIAH average from 65.91 to 82.31 at 0.66B parameters with no meaningful short-context regression (41.31 vs 41.32 ShortAvg), supported by four independent mechanistic evidence streams for the underlying causal claim. - The missing: The largest trained model is 0.66B parameters on ≤105B tokens — far below frontier scale — and the prevailing short-context pretraining followed by long-context extension recipe is not evaluated, so the conclusions may not transfer. - Read it or not: Practitioners and researchers designing hybrid LLM architectures should read this — the reframing of efficient attention as an optimization prior rather than a capability carrier is both non-obvious and immediately actionable. Problem & Motivation: Hybrid language models interleaving full softmax attention with efficient-attention modules — sliding-window attention (SWA) and recurrent sequence mixers such as Mamba-2, Lightning Attention, and Gated DeltaNet — are now widely deployed. The implicit design assumption is that stronger efficient-attention (wider SWA windows, more expressive recurrent state) produces better long-context models. This assumption has not been tested in a controlled, unified setting: existing studies compare architectures with different training setups or confounded parameter counts and lack mechanistic explanations for why one hybrid outperforms another. The specific gap is the absence of a principled account of how the choice of efficient-attention module shapes long-context capability and training dynamics. Key Idea: Efficient attention does not carry long-range information at inference time; full attention layers do. The efficient-attention module instead functions as an optimization prior: it controls how much gradient pressure propagates to the full-attention layers to develop retrieval heads during pretraining. A large SWA window covers enough local context that the model can predict next tokens without activating full-attention retrieval, weakening the gradient signal pushing those layers to learn long-range lookups — a phenomenon the authors term Large-Window Laziness. This single insight explains both why different hybrids converge to similar long-context levels under sufficient training (they share the same full-attention component) and why their convergence rates differ (efficient attention modulates how fast full attention learns retrieval). Methodology: Seven architectures are trained: a full-attention Transformer baseline and six layer-wise hybrids with a 1:1 full-to-efficient ratio — SWA-128, SWA-512, SWA-2048, Lightning, Mamba-2, and Gated DeltaNet (GDN). All architectures are parameter-matched by adjusting FFN width or SSM state dimensions in the efficient layers, not the backbone. Five model sizes span 15M–477M parameters excluding embeddings (S1–S5); models train on a 1:1 mix of long and short documents at 16K context length using the Muon optimizer and a Warmup-Stable-Decay schedule. Scaling laws L(N,D) = aN^{−α} + bD^{−β} are fit to (i) C4 validation loss and (ii) log(LongPPL) — a perplexity proxy for long-context capability that selects tokens whose prediction benefits from long context, using Llama-3.1-8B on GovReport as reference. S4 is held out for scaling-law verification. Four mechanistic experiments are applied to S4/1000N checkpoints: (1) inference-time receptive-field restriction (capping full or efficient attention to ≈2048 tokens and measuring log(LongPPL) change); (2) layer-wise logistic-regression probing on a 10K-sample NIAH classification task at 16K; (3) gradient influence profiling G(d) = E[‖∂s(x)/∂e_{T−d}‖₂] using Llama-3.1-8B as a proxy for training-signal strength over distance; (4) retrieval-head tracing over densely saved S4 checkpoints up to D=200N tokens, tracking attention entropy H(t) and Q/K weight distance d^{QK}(t). Design experiments compare the 1:3 full-to-efficient ratio, head-wise vs. layer-wise mixing, and NoPE applied exclusively to full-attention layers of SWA-128. Critical design decisions: (a) using log(LongPPL) rather than discrete benchmarks for scaling, justified by its smoothness; (b) the choice of Llama-3.1-8B as the LongPPL reference, an external dependency assumed to be model-agnostic; (c) excluding GDN's auxiliary short 1D convolution to isolate the recurrent mechanism, validated by an ablation showing the convolution's LongPPL advantage vanishes at D≥500N. Key Results: Validation loss scaling curves for all seven architectures overlap almost exactly at S5 (N=0.48B), confirming efficient-attention choice has negligible effect on short-context perplexity. By contrast, log(LongPPL) shows large early-training gaps: SWA-2048 is the worst outlier, while recurrent mixers and small-window SWA converge faster; however, all hybrids approach similar log(LongPPL) levels under sufficient training. Receptive-field ablation: restricting full attention to ≤2048 tokens raises log(LongPPL) sharply across all hybrids (e.g., GDN: 1.49 → 4.66); restricting GDN's theoretically unbounded recurrent state causes only a minor increase (1.49 → 1.54). Layer-wise probing: incremental probing accuracy on NIAH concentrates almost exclusively at odd-indexed full-attention layers, with even-indexed efficient-attention layers contributing near-zero or negative gain. Retrieval-head tracing: SWA-2048's attention entropy remains high and Q/K weight convergence is substantially slower than all other hybrids, with its Q-projection gradient norm not activating until approximately step 1500. NoPE design: at S4 (0.22B, ~100B tokens), SWA-128-NoPE achieves RULER NIAH average 67.81 vs. 49.58 (SWA-128) and 35.95 (Full); at S5 (0.66B), 82.31 vs. 65.91 and 67.14. LongBench averages follow the same direction. Short-context averages are within 0.15 points across all three variants at both scales. Head-wise mixing shows no advantage over layer-wise and slower log(LongPPL) convergence. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The scaling-law fitting framework extends Chinchilla-style power-law fitting to a new target (log(LongPPL)) and a new comparison space (seven hybrid architectures); this is methodologically incremental. The mechanistic tools — layer-wise probing, retrieval-head tracing, gradient influence profiling — are all borrowed from prior work. What is genuinely novel: the unified account of efficient attention as an optimization prior rather than a capability carrier, empirically demonstrated to hold across both SWA and recurrent-mixer families; the Large-Window Laziness phenomenon with four converging independent lines of evidence; and the actionable finding that NoPE applied selectively to full-attention layers of small-window SWA hybrid achieves large long-context gains at zero compute cost. Rigor & Reproducibility: Four mechanistically distinct experiments converge on the same conclusion — this multi-pronged design is a genuine strength that reduces the chance of any single confound driving the result. Scaling law fits report R² values for both training (S1–S3) and held-out (S4) sets, generally 0.95–0.99. The GDN conv1d ablation (Table 7) validates an architectural choice that could otherwise confound results. Code is released at the URL rethinking-hybrid-attention (full path not stated in the paper). No error bars or multiple seeds are reported for the downstream benchmarks in Table 2 — standard practice in LM pretraining evaluations but a formal gap. The gradient profiling uses Llama-3.1-8B as a proxy for the dependency structure of the pretraining corpus, which is an unverified assumption — if dependency structure differs substantially across corpora, G(d) may not faithfully reflect the training signal seen during hybrid model pretraining. NoPE is evaluated only at S4 and S5 scales; the scaling law behavior for SWA-128-NoPE is shown only for S1–S4, not extrapolated to larger scales. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Training at 0.66B parameters, 16K context length is feasible on standard GPU clusters; NoPE is a zero-parameter, zero-FLOP change to the positional encoding configuration. - Efficient: NoPE adds no computational overhead at training or inference; the small-window SWA base already reduces KV cache relative to full attention, making the combined design cache-efficient by construction. - Effective: Within the tested range, SWA-128-NoPE consistently leads on RULER and LongBench at both 16K and 32K context while maintaining short-context parity — the evidence is internally consistent and uses established benchmarks with held-out test sets. - Scalable: Experiments top out at 0.66B parameters and ~105B training tokens; frontier models train at 10×–1000× this scale using short-context pretraining followed by long-context extension, a regime not studied here, and extrapolation is explicitly disclaimed by the authors. The work delivers a code repository; no pretrained model weights are released. Strengths: - The mechanistic claim is simultaneously supported by receptive-field ablation, layer-wise probing, gradient influence profiling, and retrieval-head tracing — four independent experiments with different failure modes, making the core finding robust to any single confound. - Controlled parameter matching across 7 architectures under identical training hyperparameters removes the most common confound in hybrid architecture comparisons (one model having more parameters or better hyperparameters). - The NoPE design recommendation is immediately actionable: zero additional parameters, zero FLOPs, and a measured +16-point improvement in RULER NIAH at 0.66B scale. - The use of log(LongPPL) as a continuous scaling target enables principled scaling law fitting for long-context capability, which prior work could not do with discrete benchmarks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge that the largest model (0.66B, ~100B tokens) is well below frontier scale and that pretraining directly at 16K differs from the prevailing short-first, extend-later recipe — both may limit the applicability of conclusions to industrial systems. They also note that the efficient-attention coverage excludes RWKV-7 and Kimi-Linear, and that the Section 6 design choices are mechanistic validations rather than a comprehensive architecture search. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The gradient influence profiling relies on Llama-3.1-8B trained on a different corpus as a proxy for the pretraining data's dependency structure; this assumption is asserted but not empirically validated — if dependency profiles differ significantly across corpora, the Large-Window Laziness argument loses quantitative grounding. The NoPE ablation covers only SWA-128; it is unstated whether NoPE applied to recurrent-mixer hybrids or to SWA-512/2048 would also improve, be neutral, or hurt, leaving a gap between the mechanism claim (strengthen full attention retrieval) and the design prescription (use NoPE only with small-window SWA). The LongBench absolute scores remain low across all models (average ≤19.46 at 32K), with several tasks near random (triviaqa: 0.0–13.08, vt: 0.5–8.2) — floor effects may obscure architectural differences and reduce the discriminative value of this benchmark at this training scale. Inference-time cost tradeoffs (KV cache reduction, throughput relative to full attention) are not analyzed despite being a primary motivation for hybrid architectures. Bottom Line: This is a well-executed mechanistic study that reframes the design problem for hybrid LLMs — shifting focus from the capability of the efficient-attention module to its effect on full-attention training dynamics — with four converging lines of evidence and one clean, zero-cost design recommendation; practitioners designing sub-billion hybrid models should read it, with the caveat that frontier-scale validation remains open.

Neuro-Symbolic Software Verification: Hyper-charging Local Language Models with Symbolic Reasoning at Scale

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VerIbmc, a neuro-symbolic pipeline that pairs locally-deployable open-weight LLMs (7B–120B parameters) with the ESBMC formal verifier to synthesize loop invariants for C programs, achieving 431/499 benchmark solves (86.4%) without any cloud API. - Why it's important: Industrial formal verification of safety-critical software cannot send source code to proprietary cloud APIs, yet every prior high-performing LLM-augmented loop invariant tool (LaM4Inv, Clause2Inv, LORIS) depends on them; a privacy-preserving, locally-executable alternative removes this deployment blocker. - How it does it: A three-phase pipeline first screens trivially decidable programs with ESBMC, then symbolically enumerates and verifies atomic invariant candidates without any LLM call, then iteratively queries a local LLM using the accumulated verified-atom store as structured prompt context—every accepted invariant is formally discharged by ESBMC before the pipeline reports success. - The good: The best single configuration (GPT-OSS-120B, Basic strategy) solves 431/499 problems and is competitive with Clause2Inv (356) and LORIS (351) on four shared benchmark families, while uniquely handling the full SV-COMP loop set (154 programs) that frontier API tools cannot even parse. - The missing: Evaluation is dominated by single-loop scalar integer programs; heap allocation, concurrency, floating-point arithmetic, and multi-procedure control flow are untested, and benchmark contamination from LLM pre-training on the public datasets is acknowledged but unquantified. - Read it or not: Researchers in LLM-augmented formal verification or practitioners deploying verification toolchains in privacy-sensitive industrial settings should read it; those seeking coverage of realistic production C code will find the benchmark scope narrow.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VerIbmc, a neuro-symbolic pipeline that pairs locally-deployable open-weight LLMs (7B–120B parameters) with the ESBMC formal verifier to synthesize loop invariants for C programs, achieving 431/499 benchmark solves (86.4%) without any cloud API. - Why it's important: Industrial formal verification of safety-critical software cannot send source code to proprietary cloud APIs, yet every prior high-performing LLM-augmented loop invariant tool (LaM4Inv, Clause2Inv, LORIS) depends on them; a privacy-preserving, locally-executable alternative removes this deployment blocker. - How it does it: A three-phase pipeline first screens trivially decidable programs with ESBMC, then symbolically enumerates and verifies atomic invariant candidates without any LLM call, then iteratively queries a local LLM using the accumulated verified-atom store as structured prompt context—every accepted invariant is formally discharged by ESBMC before the pipeline reports success. - The good: The best single configuration (GPT-OSS-120B, Basic strategy) solves 431/499 problems and is competitive with Clause2Inv (356) and LORIS (351) on four shared benchmark families, while uniquely handling the full SV-COMP loop set (154 programs) that frontier API tools cannot even parse. - The missing: Evaluation is dominated by single-loop scalar integer programs; heap allocation, concurrency, floating-point arithmetic, and multi-procedure control flow are untested, and benchmark contamination from LLM pre-training on the public datasets is acknowledged but unquantified. - Read it or not: Researchers in LLM-augmented formal verification or practitioners deploying verification toolchains in privacy-sensitive industrial settings should read it; those seeking coverage of realistic production C code will find the benchmark scope narrow. Problem & Motivation Loop invariant synthesis is the central bottleneck in deductive formal verification: without a sufficiently strong inductive invariant, k-induction and bounded model checkers cannot discharge the proof for loops of unbounded depth. The problem is undecidable in general, and classical symbolic approaches (abstract interpretation, constraint-based synthesis, IC3/PDR) either lack expressiveness for complex arithmetic or require manual guidance. Recent LLM-augmented tools—LaM4Inv, Clause2Inv, and LORIS—achieve high solve rates on established benchmarks but transmit source code to proprietary API endpoints (GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4.1). For safety-critical domains (automotive, healthcare, finance), code confidentiality and regulatory compliance make this approach infeasible. No prior work had rigorously demonstrated that open-weight models, served entirely on local infrastructure, could match cloud-API performance for loop invariant synthesis at meaningful scale. Key Idea The core technical insight is to decompose the LLM's verification task down to atom-level granularity: rather than treating each LLM-generated invariant candidate as a monolithic conjecture that either proves the program or is discarded, VerIbmc decomposes each candidate into its atomic sub-formulas, checks each atom individually for inductivity (initiation and consecution, without sufficiency), and accumulates provable atoms into a growing verified store across iterations. Each subsequent LLM prompt includes this verified partial invariant as structured context—the LLM is never asked to re-derive what ESBMC has already established, but is steered toward the specific predicates still needed to close the proof. This transforms the LLM from a one-shot guesser into an incremental co-prover operating above a formally-guaranteed floor. Methodology The pipeline has three phases. Phase 0 runs ESBMC unaided on the unannotated program; trivially decided programs exit immediately. Phase 1 (symbolic candidate generation) enumerates all pairwise comparisons between loop-entry variables and constants using six relational operators (N_atoms ≤ 6(|V|² + |V|·|C|)), submits each to ESBMC in atomic-inductivity mode (checking pre→φ and φ∧g∧body→φ′ without the post-condition), classifies each into provable (P), disprovable (D), or unknown (U) stores, then tests the conjunction ∧P for whole-program sufficiency—resolving 75 problems without any LLM call.
