At 7B scale (OLMoE-1B-7B, 15.7B tokens, 4×H200): g=4 tying reaches loss 2.825, accuracy 47.5% with 3.50B parameters vs. baseline 2.820 / 47.4% / 7.12B — an effective match at half the memory; the g=4, 2× width variant (4.71B) reaches 2.81 loss and 48.2% accuracy, beating the baseline. Training throughput improves by 23.7% at 7B scale (51,777 vs 41,859 tokens/sec on DDP 4×H200). Baselines are current and appropriate: OLMoE, Qwen3-MoE, and DeepSeekMoE are 2024–2025 production systems. A comparison against the official OLMoE-1B-7B-0924 checkpoint is included as a reference point but explicitly flagged as uncontrolled (different tokenizer, data, optimizer). The downstream suite — ARC-Easy, ARC-Challenge, HellaSwag, PIQA, WinoGrande, OpenBookQA — is standard commonsense/factual, not reasoning-heavy. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The specific configuration of tying experts while keeping attention and routing per-layer is not novel in isolation (Megrez2 productionizes it; MoEUT, ReXMoE, PathMoE, and MoUE explore adjacent designs). The genuine contributions are: (a) the controlled multi-architecture, multi-granularity study that isolates which component keeps tied layers distinct — identifying attention, not routing, as the critical differentiator — and (b) the heterogeneous width expansion reframing expert tying as a depth-vs-width design axis rather than pure compression. The implementation novelty (pointer alias, LR scaling) is engineering, not research. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation structure is thorough: three tying modes × five topologies × two expert granularities provide a clean factorial design. The routing-vs-loss decoupling is established by introducing a dedicated metric (cross-loop agreement) and showing the two effects are orthogonal across all cells. No error bars or significance tests are reported for the main production-architecture experiments; within-sweep CUDA nondeterminism is stated to be below 0.005 in final loss for the ablation runs, but this is not verified for the larger production runs. The token budget (~15.7B tokens at 7B scale) is short by production standards, which limits confidence in downstream accuracy comparisons. Code is public (github.com/epfml/looped-moe), and full hyperparameters are provided in appendices A and B, giving reasonable reproducibility. The Megrez2 comparison is absent from empirical tables — the paper's closest prior work is only discussed in related work, not benchmarked against. No model weights are released; the code is promised on acceptance, so full reproducibility is currently partial. This is a preprint. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (pretraining experiments on production MoE architecture variants). - Practical: The method runs on 4×H200 GPUs with standard PyTorch DDP and HuggingFace transformers; implementation is a single pointer assignment and an optimizer LR-divisor, deployable in any existing MoE codebase. - Efficient: g=4 tying cuts total FFN parameters by 43–52%, reduces optimizer state proportionally, and delivers 15.7–23.7% training throughput gains; active compute per token is unchanged; inference memory is halved at g=4 (3.50B vs 7.12B parameters at 7B scale). - Effective: The evidence shows the method solves the stated problem — preserving quality while reducing memory — at the 7B scale tested; the iso-parameter width-expansion variant strictly outperforms the untied baseline across all three architectures. - Scalable: The paper tests up to 7B total parameters at 15.7B tokens; frontier MoE models operate at 100B–700B parameters and hundreds of billions of tokens — whether the quality gap widens or whether the throughput gains scale with NVLink topology and collective communication at multi-node scale is not evaluated, only claimed. The work delivers a public code artifact but no trained model weights. The method is a drop-in modification for existing codebases. Strengths: - The component ablation cleanly decouples three confounded variables (which component to tie, tying topology, expert granularity) with a dedicated diagnostic metric (cross-loop agreement) that is well-designed and adds mechanistic insight beyond loss curves alone. - Results are consistent across three architecturally distinct production MoE families (OLMoE, Qwen3-MoE, DeepSeekMoE) with different expert counts, depths, and routing schemes, substantially reducing cherry-picking risk. - The reframing as depth-vs-width trade-off (width expansion beats the untied baseline at iso-parameter count on all three architectures) is a genuine conceptual contribution that changes how practitioners should think about expert tying. - The LR scaling analysis (1/√g derivation and empirical validation against 1/g and no-scaling) is a practically important engineering detail usually omitted in papers of this type, improving reproducibility. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: experiments are limited to 7B parameters at a fixed token budget, leaving frontier scale and longer training horizon untested; width expansion is competitive but not uniformly dominant; PyTorch implementation without tied-layer-aware kernels means efficiency gains are a lower bound on what optimized kernels would achieve. These limitations are confronted directly in the conclusion rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The downstream evaluation suite (ARC, HellaSwag, PIQA, WinoGrande, OpenBookQA) covers commonsense and factual recall but no reasoning-intensive tasks (MATH, GSM8K, HumanEval) — the paper's own theoretical framing positions looped/tied architectures as benefiting reasoning, yet this claim goes untested. The comparison against official OLMoE-1B-7B-0924 is acknowledged as uncontrolled but still prominently featured; a fairer same-data same-optimizer comparison against Megrez2 (the architecturally closest prior work) is absent from the empirical tables entirely. The token budget at 7B scale (~15.7B tokens) is roughly 10-20× below what production models use; the smooth loss gap at 10.5B and 15.7B tokens does not rule out divergence at production compute. The throughput comparison at 7B involves a confounded batch-size difference (tied model can run larger local batch due to reduced memory, baseline runs out of memory at that batch size), which is disclosed but complicates clean apples-to-apples throughput attribution. Finally, inference-time memory measurements are conspicuously absent — the paper motivates the work partly on inference memory, but only training throughput is benchmarked. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or fine-tuning MoE models who wants to cut memory and training cost by up to 2× with minimal quality loss should read this paper — the method is simple to implement, the ablation design is unusually clean for the area, and the iso-parameter width-expansion finding is actionable immediately, though the evidence base is thin at production scale and on reasoning tasks.

Kairos: A Native World Model Stack for Physical AI

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Kairos, a 4B-parameter world model stack combining a three-stage cross-embodiment data curriculum, a hybrid linear temporal attention architecture (sliding-window + dilated sliding-window + gated linear attention), and deployment-aware quantization and distillation for physical AI. - Why it's important: Existing world models either generate plausible video but lack action grounding, or are far too compute-heavy for real-time closed-loop robot control — Kairos targets both gaps simultaneously in a single system. - How it does it: Kairos replaces full softmax attention for long sequences with a hybrid of local sliding-window attention, mid-range dilated attention, and O(N) gated linear attention that maintains a persistent compressed memory state, backed by formal error-accumulation bounds and distilled to 4B parameters with FP8/INT4 inference. - The good: On 720P 5-second generation, Kairos-4B achieves 43s / 9s latency on 1 GPU / 4 GPUs, versus 2526s / 687s for Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B and 5525s / 1436s for Lingbot-28B — a 28×–85× speedup at 2.3 vs. 156.5 PFlops — while matching or beating those models on WorldModelBench-robot (9.30 vs. 9.26), DreamGen Bench (0.618 avg), and LIBERO-Plus (89.0 average success rate). - The missing: All baseline comparisons reproduce competitor numbers in-house rather than using authors' reported checkpoints; no ablation isolates the contribution of the hybrid attention architecture itself versus the data curriculum, so it is impossible to disentangle the source of the empirical gains. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on embodied AI, video world models, or efficient long-sequence Transformers — Kairos delivers a rare combination of deployment-grade efficiency and top-tier benchmark results; skip it if you need peer-reviewed certainty, since this is a preprint with self-reproduced baselines.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Kairos, a 4B-parameter world model stack combining a three-stage cross-embodiment data curriculum, a hybrid linear temporal attention architecture (sliding-window + dilated sliding-window + gated linear attention), and deployment-aware quantization and distillation for physical AI. - Why it's important: Existing world models either generate plausible video but lack action grounding, or are far too compute-heavy for real-time closed-loop robot control — Kairos targets both gaps simultaneously in a single system. - How it does it: Kairos replaces full softmax attention for long sequences with a hybrid of local sliding-window attention, mid-range dilated attention, and O(N) gated linear attention that maintains a persistent compressed memory state, backed by formal error-accumulation bounds and distilled to 4B parameters with FP8/INT4 inference. - The good: On 720P 5-second generation, Kairos-4B achieves 43s / 9s latency on 1 GPU / 4 GPUs, versus 2526s / 687s for Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B and 5525s / 1436s for Lingbot-28B — a 28×–85× speedup at 2.3 vs. 156.5 PFlops — while matching or beating those models on WorldModelBench-robot (9.30 vs. 9.26), DreamGen Bench (0.618 avg), and LIBERO-Plus (89.0 average success rate). - The missing: All baseline comparisons reproduce competitor numbers in-house rather than using authors' reported checkpoints; no ablation isolates the contribution of the hybrid attention architecture itself versus the data curriculum, so it is impossible to disentangle the source of the empirical gains. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on embodied AI, video world models, or efficient long-sequence Transformers — Kairos delivers a rare combination of deployment-grade efficiency and top-tier benchmark results; skip it if you need peer-reviewed certainty, since this is a preprint with self-reproduced baselines. Problem & Motivation Robotic and physical AI systems require world models that can (1) acquire generalizable physical knowledge from heterogeneous data sources including passive video and robot trajectories, (2) maintain coherent state over long generation horizons (seconds to minutes of video, hundreds of robot steps), and (3) run at low latency on server and consumer hardware in closed-loop control. Prior work partitions into three silos — pixel-level video generators (Cosmos, Wan, LingBot), predictive latent-space models (V-JEPA family), and interactive environment simulators (Genie 3, Dreamer) — each optimizing one of these axes at the expense of the others. Pixel-level generators are slow and expensive; latent predictors lack actionable output; interactive simulators assume fixed environments. No single system trains natively on cross-embodiment data while also targeting real-time inference and joint world-action modeling. Key Idea The core insight is that long-horizon temporal consistency requires architecturally persistent global memory, and that this memory can be made both theoretically justified and computationally cheap by factorizing temporal attention into three ranges: sliding-window attention for local appearance continuity, dilated sliding-window attention for mid-range dynamics, and Gated Linear Attention (O(N) complexity) as a contractive recurrent state that never discards far-past context. The paper proves formally (Theorems 1 and 2) that bounded sliding-window attention produces irreducibly higher prediction risk whenever the optimal predictor depends on supra-window events, while the hybrid factorization limits error accumulation to ξ/(1-ρ) asymptotically, where ρ < 1 is the contraction factor and ξ is the per-step perturbation. This provides theoretical grounding usually absent from engineering-focused systems papers. Methodology Kairos is structured in three interconnected layers: Architecture (Section 2): A Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) Diffusion Transformer whose temporal attention blocks alternate between (a) Sliding Window Attention (SWA) with RoPE for local frames, (b) Dilated Sliding Window Attention (DSWA) with larger receptive field, and (c) Gated Linear Attention (GLA / Gated DeltaNet) maintaining a compressed O(d²) state matrix. Each Hybrid Linear Temporal Block fans through all three paths. The design is unified across world understanding (VLM encoder, Qwen3.5-2B), generation (video diffusion), and prediction (action head), sharing the same backbone weights. Pre-training Curriculum (Section 3): Cross-Embodiment Data Curriculum (CEDC) runs three sequential stages: Stage I trains on "hundreds of millions" of open-world video clips for physical regularities via causal chain-of-thought grounding; Stage II adds "over 100,000 hours" of human-centric behavioral data (egocentric, instructed tasks); Stage III injects robot-specific data (AgiBotWorld-Beta, Droid) with joint video generation and action prediction objectives. A key design choice is that action grounding is native — not a post-hoc fine-tune — motivated by the 23.2-point LIBERO-Plus gain from joint vs. action-prediction-only training. Deployment System Co-Design (Section 5): Timestep distillation compresses multi-step diffusion into fewer function evaluations. Attention layers use FP8 quantization with per-thread INT8/INT4 Q/K and FP8 P·V; the text encoder uses INT4 weight-only compression. A tiled GLA with streaming access avoids OOM on long sequences. These optimizations are targeted specifically at closed-loop control latency, not just offline throughput. Key Results All evaluations are preprint-stage; competitor results marked with * were re-run by the authors rather than taken from original papers. Efficiency (Table 5): At 720P, 5s generation, Kairos-4B uses 2.3 PFlops and 23.5 GB memory, achieving 43s (1 GPU, A800) and 9s (4 GPU). Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B uses 156.5 PFlops and 2526s/687s; Lingbot-28B uses 347.4 PFlops and 5525s/1436s. Against the parameter-comparable Wan2.2-5B (16.6 PFlops, 201s/85s), Kairos delivers ~4.7× speedup at lower compute. WorldModelBench-robot (Table 6): Kairos-4B scores 9.30 total, topping Cosmos3-Nano-16B (9.26) and Lingbot-28B (9.04), with perfect scores on Newton, fluid, and gravity sub-metrics. DreamGen Bench (Table 7): Kairos-4B best overall (0.618 avg score) and best physical adherence (0.538), second in instruction-following (0.698 vs. 0.703 for Wan2.2-14B). PAI-Bench-robot (Table 8): Best among <10B models with domain score 88.59 and overall 82.57, beating Cosmos3-Nano-16B (82.62 — nearly tied), Wan2.2-5B (78.63), and several 14B models. Long-horizon 15s (Table 18): Overall 79.9 vs. Wan2.2-5B 77.8, Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B 76.2, showing smaller degradation from 5s to 15s generation. VideoPhy (Table 17): 45.55 average score vs. Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B 45.16, Wan2.2-5B 38.85. WAM — LIBERO-Plus (Table 12): 89.0 average (best WAM category); joint training (Kairos-joint) reaches 90.8. WAM — RoboTwin 2.0 (Table 11): 96.1 average clean+randomized, first among WAMs (MotuBrain 96.0, SANTS 94.4). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM / NEW METHOD. The contribution is genuinely novel in the combination of (a) formal theoretical justification for a specific hybrid attention factorization targeting world models, (b) a native three-stage pre-training curriculum that treats robot grounding as Stage III of a continuum rather than fine-tuning, and (c) deployment-aware inference co-design validated on robot benchmarks. Individual ingredients (GLA, SWA, DSWA, diffusion distillation) are drawn from prior work; the integration and the physical AI framing are novel. The theoretical analysis (Theorems 1 and 2) is the most genuinely new intellectual contribution — formal error-accumulation bounds for hybrid attention in a world-model context have not appeared in prior work. Rigor & Reproducibility Code and model weights are publicly released at https://github.com/kairos-agi/kairos-sensenova and https://huggingface.co/kairos-agi. Ablations are present but narrow: human-centric data ablation (+6 LIBERO points, Table 13), joint training ablation (+23.2 points, Table 14), and VLM encoder choice (Table 9). No ablation isolates GLA vs. SWA vs. DSWA — the architectural contribution stands only on the theoretical argument plus end-to-end system comparison. Baselines marked with * are reproduced by the authors' own team, not taken from official checkpoints; given that some competitor numbers differ from published reports, this introduces a potential reproducibility concern. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported for the robot policy benchmarks (LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin), where variance can be high. The theoretical bounds (Theorem 2) require specific assumptions (contractive gated delta update, exact factorization of the Bayes predictor) that are never empirically verified. The paper is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — Kairos is a deployed inference system evaluated on server and consumer hardware. - Practical: Kairos runs on NVIDIA A800 and RTX 5090 (consumer GPU), generating 480P video in 11–12s single-GPU and 3–6s with 4 GPUs, qualifying as practically usable on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: At 2.3 PFlops and 23.5 GB memory for 720P 5s generation, it is 68× fewer PFlops than Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B while using 67% less memory, representing a strong efficiency gain; the RTX 5090 memory footprint (13.9 GB at 480P) is within reach of a $2K–3K consumer GPU. - Effective: Evidence across WorldModelBench, DreamGen, PAI-Bench, LIBERO-Plus, and RoboTwin 2.0 consistently shows Kairos matching or exceeding models with 3×–7× more parameters, which credibly supports the claimed efficiency–capability trade-off; however, all baselines are author-reproduced, and only a subset of results include human evaluation. - Scalable: The paper shows linear latency scaling from 480P 5s to 720P 15s (versus exponential for competitors), and the 15s PAI-Bench results directly test this axis; scaling beyond 15s or to 4K resolution has not been evaluated and remains a claim rather than a demonstration. Kairos delivers a full software artifact: model weights, inference code, and training infrastructure released publicly. Strengths - The theoretical treatment of persistent memory necessity (Theorem 1 + Corollary 1) is the strongest formal justification for hybrid attention in world models published to date, going beyond empirical motivation. - The efficiency gains are empirically striking and backed by specific hardware numbers (PFlops, GB, latency) rather than FLOPs estimates; 28×–85× over Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B at comparable quality is a publishable finding in its own right. - The CEDC curriculum's staged data strategy — treating internet video, human behavior, and robot data as three progressive curriculum stages rather than mixed pooling — is a principled design choice validated by the +23.2 ablation from joint generation+prediction training. - Evaluation coverage is unusually broad: five benchmarks (WorldModelBench, DreamGen, PAI-Bench, LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin 2.0), long-horizon testing at 15s, and human evaluation against anonymized baselines. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge virtually no limitations explicitly. The conclusion pivots immediately to future work (self-evolution, generalist embodied substrate). There is no discussion of failure modes, out-of-distribution generalization bounds, the theoretical gap between the mathematical model and actual training dynamics, or the limited diversity of robot morphologies tested. This is a credibility negative — the absence of limitation discussion reads as vendor-style reporting rather than scientific disclosure. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No architectural ablation: the paper never compares GLA-only vs. SWA-only vs. full hybrid in an end-to-end setting, making it impossible to attribute the performance gains to the architectural choice versus the data curriculum. - Competitor results re-run by the authors: several baseline numbers differ from published reports; no independent verification is offered, and the paper does not disclose which hyperparameters or prompts were used in re-running. - Theoretical assumptions go unverified: Theorem 2 assumes the gated delta update is contractive (ρ < 1) and that the Bayes predictor factorizes over the three temporal scales — neither property is empirically measured. - Action evaluation is narrow: LIBERO-Plus and RoboTwin 2.0 are simulated benchmarks; no real-robot deployment results appear. - Long-context scaling claim is partial: 15s is tested, but the "infinite horizon" claim (arbitrary-length generation via recurrent state passing) is not empirically validated. - Human evaluation used 10 volunteers on a proprietary pool of generated samples, which is a weak statistical basis for the claim that Kairos achieves 65–87% win rates. Bottom Line Kairos is a credible and practically significant system paper — its efficiency numbers are the most compelling argument for reading it, and the theoretical treatment of hybrid attention genuinely advances understanding — but the absence of architectural ablations, self-reproduced baselines, and lack of real-robot validation mean practitioners should treat the benchmark rankings as indicative rather than definitive until independent replication is available.

Fantastic Pretraining Optimizers and Where to Find Them II: Hyperball Optimization

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Hyperball, an optimizer wrapper that constrains weight matrix Frobenius norms and update Frobenius norms to fixed constants, replacing the implicit norm control of decoupled weight decay with an explicit hypersphere constraint. - Why it's important: The speedup of matrix-based optimizers like Muon over AdamW collapses from ~30% to ~10% as model size and data scale grow under standard weight decay, limiting the practical value of these optimizers at production scale. - How it does it: For each constrained matrix, Hyperball takes a step of length η_t R in the normalized update direction and immediately projects back onto the Frobenius sphere of radius R, decoupling weight magnitude from directional optimization and making the angular learning rate an explicit, directly controlled quantity. - The good: MuonH sustains 20–30% token-equivalent speedup over AdamW at 1.2B parameters across Chinchilla ratios 1×–8× (growing with training duration), compared to ~10% for MuonW, and also reduces optimal learning rate drift across depth L∈[4,512] and width d∈[128,2048] to ~1.4× versus 2–4× for AdamW/MuonW. - The missing: The theory rests on isotropic Gaussian gradient assumptions that ignore anisotropy and the signal in E[g_t]; systematic experiments beyond 1.2B are absent (the 8B result is a single manually-tuned run), and no code or checkpoint is released. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on LLM pretraining optimizers or training dynamics, as it provides a clean theoretical account of what weight decay actually does in scale-invariant networks and a practical wrapper with consistent empirical gains.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Hyperball, an optimizer wrapper that constrains weight matrix Frobenius norms and update Frobenius norms to fixed constants, replacing the implicit norm control of decoupled weight decay with an explicit hypersphere constraint. - Why it's important: The speedup of matrix-based optimizers like Muon over AdamW collapses from ~30% to ~10% as model size and data scale grow under standard weight decay, limiting the practical value of these optimizers at production scale. - How it does it: For each constrained matrix, Hyperball takes a step of length η_t R in the normalized update direction and immediately projects back onto the Frobenius sphere of radius R, decoupling weight magnitude from directional optimization and making the angular learning rate an explicit, directly controlled quantity. - The good: MuonH sustains 20–30% token-equivalent speedup over AdamW at 1.2B parameters across Chinchilla ratios 1×–8× (growing with training duration), compared to ~10% for MuonW, and also reduces optimal learning rate drift across depth L∈[4,512] and width d∈[128,2048] to ~1.4× versus 2–4× for AdamW/MuonW. - The missing: The theory rests on isotropic Gaussian gradient assumptions that ignore anisotropy and the signal in E[g_t]; systematic experiments beyond 1.2B are absent (the 8B result is a single manually-tuned run), and no code or checkpoint is released. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on LLM pretraining optimizers or training dynamics, as it provides a clean theoretical account of what weight decay actually does in scale-invariant networks and a practical wrapper with consistent empirical gains. Problem & Motivation: Language model pretraining practitioners using matrix-based optimizers (Muon, Moonlight) observe that the token-equivalent speedup over AdamW degrades from roughly 30% at small compute budgets to approximately 10% at 1.2B-parameter scale and beyond under standard decoupled weight decay. The root cause is that weight decay implicitly controls the angular learning rate — how fast weight directions change — through an equilibrium Frobenius norm that grows with learning rate and shrinks with weight decay. As model size or training duration changes, this implicit calibration shifts, undermining the optimizer's advantage. The paper targets this specific failure mode in scale-invariant Transformer weight matrices, where the loss is unchanged by rescaling individual weight matrices, making the matrix direction the only optimization-relevant quantity. Key Idea: Weight decay in scale-invariant networks does not regularize in the classical ℓ₂ sense; instead, it drives the Frobenius norm toward an equilibrium radius R ∝ √(η/λ), and that equilibrium radius determines the angular step size η^ang = ‖u_t‖_F / R. Hyperball makes this mechanism explicit: instead of letting (η, λ) jointly and indirectly calibrate R* and the relative update length, it fixes R = ‖W₀‖_F and normalizes updates to unit Frobenius norm, turning the learning rate η directly into an angular step-size controller. This is a three-line modification to any base optimizer loop. Methodology: Hyperball is a stateless wrapper (Algorithm 1): given a base optimizer update u_t, it computes û_t = u_t/‖u_t‖_F, takes W̃_{t+1} = W_t − η_t R û_t, then projects back: W_{t+1} = R · Normalize(W̃_{t+1}). The projection costs O(N²) per matrix (elementwise norm), compared to O(N³) for spectral projection. It is applied only to attention and MLP weight matrices in a prenorm Transformer; embeddings, normalization gains, and scalar parameters use Adam without the constraint, since their norms carry semantic information. The paper instantiates AdamH (Adam + Hyperball) and MuonH (Muon + Hyperball) and also combines it with KL-SOAP. Critical design decisions: (1) Frobenius rather than spectral norm — computationally cheaper and motivated by the theory; the choice is justified by arguing high stable rank (the singular value spectrum is not dominated by a single direction) makes Frobenius and spectral control equivalent up to a slowly varying factor, which is plausible but not directly verified per-experiment. (2) Radius set once at initialization and held fixed — this is well-justified by the expressivity argument (a trainable normalization gain can absorb any scale change). (3) Embedding and norm parameters excluded — correctly identified as non-scale-invariant; no ablation is provided for this boundary choice. The theory section (Section 4) establishes, under Assumptions 4.1–4.2 (infinite history optimizer, isotropic Gaussian i.i.d. gradients), closed-form expressions for the equilibrium Frobenius norm R*, cosine proxy between weight and update, and angular step size η^ang for AdamW, SVD Muon, and Moonlight. The key result (Theorem 4.6) is that weight decay sets both the equilibrium radius and the angular step size, with the base optimizer entering only through the update norm U and update autocorrelation sum C_α. This formalizes and extends prior rotational equilibrium analyses (Kosson et al. 2024a) to Muon/Moonlight, introducing the SVD Muon sign kernel κ_{p,q}(ρ). Five empirical phenomena validate theory predictions on held-out training runs. Key Results: On Qwen3-style models at 1.2B parameters across Chinchilla ratios 1×–8×: MuonH achieves 20–30% token-equivalent speedup over AdamW (vs. ~10% for MuonW at this scale), with the advantage growing with training duration. On the Marin speedrun (FineWeb-Edu, 1× Chinchilla), AdamH and MuonH match weight-decay baselines that are ~10% larger in parameter count. On an 8B model trained over 159B tokens (single run, manually chosen hyperparameters), MuonH achieves 0.04 lower validation loss than the AdamW baseline, with norm divergence in the layer-9 value projection under AdamW. On the modded-nanogpt Track 3 public benchmark: AdamW reaches loss 3.2790 in 5625 steps; MuonH reaches 3.2782 in 3325 steps; KL-SOAP-H reaches 3.2780 in 3125 steps. Learning rate drift across depth L∈[4,512] (fixed d=128, 10B tokens/run): ~1.4× for AdamH/MuonH vs. ~3× for AdamW and ~4× for MuonW at L=512. Same ~1.4× drift for width d∈[128,2048] (fixed L=4). In the 130M overtrained sweep (1B–128B tokens), MuonH fits an asymptote L∞=3.065 vs. MuonW's L∞=3.079. Baselines are current and appropriate (AdamW, MuonW, NorMuonW); the modded-nanogpt comparison uses public logs from a community benchmark. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The Hyperball wrapper itself is a genuine algorithmic contribution: the specific combination of simultaneously fixing weight Frobenius norm and update Frobenius norm as an optimizer wrapper, motivated by the scale-invariance insight, distinguishes it from prior norm-constraint work (nGPT fixes column norms, anGPT bounds rows, Weight Normalization reparameterizes but does not fix the norm, AdamP projects updates without fixing weight norms). The theoretical analysis extending rotational equilibrium to Muon/Moonlight via the SVD sign kernel κ_{p,q}(ρ) is new. The empirical finding that weight decay should be understood as an indirect angular step-size controller — not a capacity regularizer — consolidates several prior observations into a single, testable framework with quantitative predictions that are validated. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present: fixed ηλ product ablation (Fig. 10) validates the theory prediction that the product controls training loss; separate depth and width transfer sweeps disentangle the two axes; the overtrained 130M sweep provides asymptotic loss comparison. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported for the headline 1.2B results; the 8B comparison is a single pair of runs with manually chosen hyperparameters. The modded-nanogpt results use a public community benchmark with single runs. No code, model weights, or checkpoints are released. The isotropic Gaussian gradient assumption (Assumption 4.2) is intentionally idealized and acknowledged as ignoring anisotropy and gradient signal — the theoretical equilibrium radius and angular step size formulas are ansätze, not proven convergence guarantees. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.16899v1, June 2026); results are not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on standard TPU and GPU hardware (experiments use Google TRC TPUs); the wrapper adds only an elementwise Frobenius normalization per matrix per step, no additional memory beyond the base optimizer state. - Efficient: O(N²) per matrix per step for the projection — same order as the forward/backward pass element count — versus O(N³) for spectral alternatives; the overhead is negligible relative to optimizer state computation at 1B+ scale. - Effective: Evidence shows Hyperball consistently achieves lower validation loss than weight-decay baselines at 130M, 1.2B, and 8B parameters on multiple benchmarks, with token-equivalent speedups growing with scale and training duration; the evidence is convincing for the tested settings. - Scalable: Systematically tested up to 1.2B parameters; a single 8B run is included but not scaled across configurations; the claim that the benefit grows with training duration is supported within the 1.2B sweep (1×–8× Chinchilla) but the 10B+ regime is untested. No software artifact is released — the paper stops at the experimental report form. Strengths: - The core theoretical contribution — deriving explicit closed-form expressions for equilibrium radius and angular step size for both AdamW and Muon under a unified framework, and validating five distinct predictions empirically — is the most intellectually credible part of the paper. It converts a folk observation about weight decay into a precise, testable mechanism. - The Hyperball update is trivially implementable (three lines) and optimizer-agnostic: the paper demonstrates it works with Adam, Muon, NorMuon, and KL-SOAP, ruling out entanglement with a specific base optimizer. - The learning rate transfer result is underappreciated relative to the speedup result: ~1.4× drift across 7 octaves of depth and 4 octaves of width is a practically significant improvement for scaling recipes that need to sweep hyperparameters infrequently. - The expressivity argument (Theorem 4.1) is clean and preempts the natural objection that fixing the norm reduces representational capacity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that Assumption 4.2 (isotropic Gaussian gradients) is intentionally idealized and ignores anisotropy, layer-specific structure, and the signal in E[g_t]. They acknowledge the Frobenius vs. spectral norm choice is unresolved and flag a spectral Hyperball variant as a natural future direction when singular value concentration makes Frobenius control too loose. The conclusion section openly frames the paper as a proof of concept for removing weight decay via explicit norm control, not a finished recipe. The 8B experiment uses manually chosen hyperparameters — the authors do not claim this is a principled comparison. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 8B experiment (Fig. 3, middle/right) is a single run pair with manually tuned hyperparameters, which makes it difficult to attribute the 0.04 loss gap to Hyperball versus tuning differences; a systematic sweep at 8B is absent. No error bars or seed replication are reported for any 1.2B result — given the tight loss differences being compared (~0.01 on the modded-nanogpt benchmark), statistical significance is unclear. The boundary decision of which parameters get Hyperball vs. plain Adam is not ablated: the paper applies it to attention/MLP weights and excludes embeddings and norms, but whether this specific boundary is optimal is untested. The theory assumes exact scale invariance, while the paper notes that real LLM weight matrices are only approximately scale invariant; the practical implications of this approximation for the theory's quantitative predictions are not quantified. Interaction with learning rate schedules (WSD vs. cosine vs. constant) is not systematically explored beyond the settings used — the 1.2B results use cosine-like schedules but the modded-nanogpt benchmark uses a different setup. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or studying LLM pretraining infrastructure will find concrete value here: Hyperball is a trivial code change with well-documented empirical gains at 1.2B scale and a theory that cleanly explains why weight decay works as it does in normalized Transformers — making it worth reading for anyone working on optimizer design or hyperparameter transfer.

PI-Hunter: Automated Red-Teaming for Exposing and Localizing Prompt Injections

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PI-Hunter, an automated agentic auditing framework that proactively exposes and localizes latent indirect prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agent systems by evolving source-aware test cases through genetic-algorithm-style feedback loops. - Why it's important: LLM agents ingest content from untrusted external sources (emails, web pages, databases) where malicious instructions lie dormant until specific retrieval patterns activate them — developers currently have no systematic way to find these hidden paths before deployment, and existing defenses detect but do not map vulnerabilities. - How it does it: The framework performs a one-time static analysis of the agent's tool surface, seeds one LLM-generated test case per high-risk interface, runs the target agent and audits execution trajectories with a rubric-guided evaluator, mutates test cases and the mutation operators themselves based on failure signals, then transiently patches each discovered injection path to force exploration of previously hidden attack surfaces. - The good: Across five backbone models (Gemini-3.1-pro, Gemini-2.5-pro, GPT-5.4-mini, Claude-4.6-sonnet, DeepSeek) and two benchmarks, PI-Hunter consistently and substantially improves both source recall (e.g., 0.255 → 0.834 under the AgentVigil attack with Gemini-3.1-pro on AgentDojo) and discovery diversity (Source Diversity 0.11 → 0.84 for Gemini-3.1-pro on AgentDojo), including under defenses where the baseline degenerates to zero recall. - The missing: The evaluation compares against a single custom baseline rather than published automated red-teaming systems (GOAT, AutoRedTeamer, SIRAJ), and performance in live production agents with dynamic, unconstrained environments remains entirely untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and security practitioners building or auditing LLM agent systems, as it offers a concrete and well-motivated shift from attack-success measurement to proactive ingestion-path mapping — but expect no code release and calibrate confidence accordingly.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PI-Hunter, an automated agentic auditing framework that proactively exposes and localizes latent indirect prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agent systems by evolving source-aware test cases through genetic-algorithm-style feedback loops. - Why it's important: LLM agents ingest content from untrusted external sources (emails, web pages, databases) where malicious instructions lie dormant until specific retrieval patterns activate them — developers currently have no systematic way to find these hidden paths before deployment, and existing defenses detect but do not map vulnerabilities. - How it does it: The framework performs a one-time static analysis of the agent's tool surface, seeds one LLM-generated test case per high-risk interface, runs the target agent and audits execution trajectories with a rubric-guided evaluator, mutates test cases and the mutation operators themselves based on failure signals, then transiently patches each discovered injection path to force exploration of previously hidden attack surfaces. - The good: Across five backbone models (Gemini-3.1-pro, Gemini-2.5-pro, GPT-5.4-mini, Claude-4.6-sonnet, DeepSeek) and two benchmarks, PI-Hunter consistently and substantially improves both source recall (e.g., 0.255 → 0.834 under the AgentVigil attack with Gemini-3.1-pro on AgentDojo) and discovery diversity (Source Diversity 0.11 → 0.84 for Gemini-3.1-pro on AgentDojo), including under defenses where the baseline degenerates to zero recall. - The missing: The evaluation compares against a single custom baseline rather than published automated red-teaming systems (GOAT, AutoRedTeamer, SIRAJ), and performance in live production agents with dynamic, unconstrained environments remains entirely untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and security practitioners building or auditing LLM agent systems, as it offers a concrete and well-motivated shift from attack-success measurement to proactive ingestion-path mapping — but expect no code release and calibrate confidence accordingly. Problem & Motivation: LLM agents connected to external tools continuously consume content from untrusted sources — email inboxes, web search results, document stores, databases — that can embed malicious instructions designed to remain dormant until activated by specific user queries, retrieval patterns, or reasoning trajectories. This indirect prompt injection threat differs fundamentally from direct injection through user prompts: the attack surface is not the user's message but the agent's interaction with its environment. Existing defenses (Spotlighting, MELON, PIGuard, SecAlign) address detection and blocking at inference time and assume injections are known. Existing automated red-teaming tools (GOAT, AutoRedTeamer, AgentVigil) optimize for attack success rate — they measure whether an agent can be compromised, not where in its operational reach it is vulnerable. Agent builders therefore lack tools to proactively audit which external sources, interfaces, and interaction patterns introduce exploitable ingestion paths before deployment. Key Idea: The single core insight is that exposing latent prompt injections requires treating the auditing problem as a co-evolutionary search: the auditor generates source-targeted test cases, observes which trajectories produce suspicious behavior, uses rubric-graded diagnostic feedback to mutate both the test cases and the mutation operators themselves, and structurally eliminates discovered paths through transient patching — forcing the search into genuinely unexplored attack surfaces. This patch-and-reexplore mechanism is the distinguishing mechanism: rather than relying on randomness or coverage metrics to drive diversity, it removes each discovered vulnerability from the accessible landscape, making continued exploitation of easy paths impossible and pushing exploration toward sparse, hidden injections. Methodology: PI-Hunter implements a three-stage pipeline operating on a black-box target agent A with tool set I and external sources S. Stage 1 — Static Analysis: A one-time inspection of tool schemas and API interfaces identifies high-risk ingestion channels — specifically interfaces that consume unstructured external content (emails, HTML pages, documents, repository listings) or that can perform privileged actions (send_email, execute_command, modify_files). This produces the seeding surface for Stage 2. Stage 2 — Evolutionary Exploitation Loop (Algorithm 1, T iterations): (a) Source-aware meta-seeding: the LLM-powered Generator creates one seed query per identified high-risk source, designed to embed plausible benign user intent, activate a specific high-risk interface, and elicit verbatim reproduction of retrieved content (quoting, listing, summarizing). (b) Trajectory execution and evaluation: test cases are submitted to the target agent; the trajectory-aware Evaluator audits retrieved contents, tool call sequences, reasoning traces, and outputs against five rubric criteria: intent adherence discrepancy, third-party instruction presence, authority confusion, abnormal interface usage, and sensitive action attempts. The Evaluator returns structured diagnostic feedback, not binary labels. (c) Feedback-driven mutation: the Generator selects and applies mutation operators based on trajectory signals. Seven general-purpose operators are defined (volume_expansion, implicit_assumption, parameter_fuzzing, instruction_hierarchy_override, role_inversion, delimiter_hijacking, encoding_obfuscation), supplemented by domain-specific operators for workspace, banking, travel, Slack, shopping, GitHub, and daily-life scenarios. (d) LLM-guided meta-mutation: a "Hyper-Mutation Engine" prompt refines the mutation operators themselves based on historical effectiveness across iterations. Default configuration: 5 seed queries, 10 iterations. Stage 3 — Verification & Co-evolution (Patcher): Newly discovered injection paths are passed to the LLM-powered Patcher, which applies lightweight transient mitigations (blacklisting injections in the system prompt, restricting vulnerable interfaces, temporarily isolating compromised sources) to reshape the exploration landscape and prevent redundant rediscovery. Objective function: maximize Source Recall and Instruction Recall (precision/recall over compromised source localization and malicious payload recovery) and entropy-based Diversity across source categories and attack consequence categories. Instruction matching uses exact string comparison. Critical design decisions: (1) Source-aware seeding over generic domain prompts — well-justified by the ablation showing Source Recall 0.48 vs. 0.19 (generic); (2) trajectory-level rubric evaluation over binary attack labels — justified conceptually (injections manifest in intermediate reasoning, not only final outputs) but not ablated against simpler evaluators; (3) transient patching for coverage expansion — not ablated independently, leaving its specific contribution to diversity unquantified. Key Results: Tested on AgentDojo (4 domains: banking, Slack, workspace, travel) and AgentDyn (7 domains, 60 user tasks, 560 injection test cases), across ReAct and Planner-Executor architectures, 5 backbone models, 5 attack types (direct, ignore_previous, system_msg, important_inst, agentvigil), and 4 defense settings (None, Spotlighting, MELON, PIGuard). Source Recall improvements (PI-Hunter vs. baseline): Gemini-3.1-pro on AgentDojo under agentvigil: 0.255 → 0.834; under direct attack: 0.248 → 0.881. GPT-5.4-mini on AgentDyn under direct attack: 0.314 → 0.688. Claude-4.6-sonnet on AgentDyn under ignore_previous: 0.356 → 0.889. Diversity improvements: Gemini-3.1-pro, AgentDojo: Source Diversity 0.11 → 0.84; GPT-5.4-mini, AgentDojo: 0.18 → 0.69. Baseline diversity is frequently near zero (0.00–0.15), indicating the baseline repeatedly rediscovers the same injection patterns. Under defenses (Table 3, Gemini-2.5-pro, agentvigil): Under PIGuard (strongest defense), the baseline achieves 0.000 Instruction Recall while PI-Hunter reaches 0.194. Under Spotlight, Source Recall improves from 0.300 to 0.456, Instruction Recall from 0.220 to 0.390. Ablation results (Table 4): Source-aware seeding achieves Source Recall 0.4796 vs. 0.1918 (Generic), 0.3453 (Holistic). Source Precision 0.7794 vs. 0.2338. Table 5: Smart mutation achieves Source Recall 0.4796 vs. 0.3228 (Fixed), 0.3578 (Random). Architecture generalization: Instruction Recall for Planner-Executor agents (baseline 0.22 → 0.67) benefits more than ReAct (0.27 → 0.49), suggesting multi-stage pipelines expose broader attack surfaces. Convergence: Performance saturates around 8–10 iterations (Figure 3). Computational cost: 70–127 queries and 5,759–11,007 tokens per audit run (Table 18), though whether target agent calls are included is unstated. Baseline: a single custom "free interaction" LLM baseline (static analysis → open-ended multi-turn conversation → post-hoc extraction). No comparison to GOAT, AutoRedTeamer, SIRAJ, or SafeSearch as auditing systems. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The auditor-side framing of injection exposure (mapping ingestion paths rather than optimizing attack success) and the specific combination of source-targeted seeding, trajectory-rubric evaluation, and patch-driven exploration diversity are the genuine novelties. The individual components — LLM-powered test case mutation, genetic algorithm exploitation loops, rubric-guided evaluation — are established techniques (AgentVigil uses MCTS mutation for the attack side; rubric-based evaluators are standard); the contribution is their assembly and reorientation toward proactive vulnerability mapping rather than attack maximization. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations on seeding strategy and mutation strategy are present and quantitative. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed runs are reported for any table; all numbers are point estimates from stochastic LLM-based systems, and the sensitivity of results to randomness is unknown. No code, model weights, or data are released (no URL mentioned). The exact-match payload recovery metric (explicitly defined in Appendix B) is conservative and will miss semantically equivalent paraphrases, potentially undercounting true recall. The custom baseline is implemented by the authors and not validated against published systems; this is a significant gap, as GOAT, AutoRedTeamer, and SIRAJ could plausibly be adapted as auditing baselines. Domain-specific mutation operators are hand-crafted for each benchmark domain, introducing tuning that may not generalize. This is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2606.12737v1, June 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS): - Practical: PI-Hunter operates as a black-box auditor requiring only API access to the target agent and an LLM for the Generator/Evaluator/Patcher roles — both commercially available. No internal agent access required. - Efficient: Claimed 70–127 queries and ~6,000–11,000 tokens per audit run (Table 18); however, these counts appear to cover only the auditor's own LLM calls, not the target agent's LLM calls, making the true compute cost unclear. No wall-clock timing or dollar cost estimates are provided. - Effective: Improvements are large and consistent, but absolute recall values for PI-Hunter remain below 1.0 in nearly all conditions and fall as low as 0.252 Source Recall under PIGuard on AgentDyn — meaning a substantial fraction of injections remains undiscovered even after 10 iterations. The framework finds more, not all. - Scalable: Tested exclusively on curated benchmark environments. Performance on production agents with hundreds of tools, dynamic content, and real-world variability is explicitly acknowledged as untested. No experiments at scale beyond the benchmarks. No software artifact is released alongside the paper. Strengths: - The ingestion-path framing (source × tool intersection as the unit of vulnerability) is more actionable for practitioners than attack success rate — knowing which external source and which interface introduce risk directly informs remediation. - The meta-mutation mechanism (evolving mutation operators based on accumulated feedback) is a non-obvious and technically sound contribution that operationalizes continual adaptation without manual operator design per deployment. - The patch-and-reexplore co-evolution loop is a structurally clever diversity driver: eliminating discovered paths from the exploration landscape guarantees monotonically increasing coverage, unlike coverage metrics or random restarts. - Evaluation breadth is above average for this literature: five backbone models, two benchmarks, four defense settings, two agent architectures, five attack types, and four injection-scenario configurations, with ablations on the two most critical design choices. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state (1) performance in live, unconstrained real-world environments remains unverified, since production systems present dynamic and uncontrollable variables; and (2) the framework identifies vulnerabilities but proposes no remediation solutions, leaving the gap between discovery and mitigation as future work. The limitations are acknowledged directly and without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The comparison baseline is a single custom implementation; there is no comparison to published automated red-teaming systems (GOAT, AutoRedTeamer, SIRAJ) as auditing-side competitors, which would be the natural comparison class. (2) No error bars or significance tests are reported for any quantitative result, despite all measurements coming from stochastic LLM systems — some reported improvements may fall within noise. (3) The token cost in Table 18 likely excludes target agent calls, making the cost analysis potentially misleading for practitioners estimating audit budgets. (4) Exact-match instruction recovery will miss paraphrased or reformatted payloads that achieve the same attack effect, creating systematic undercounting of true recall. (5) Domain-specific mutation operators are hand-crafted for each benchmark domain, which constitutes implicit tuning to the evaluation environments and may not generalize to novel deployment domains. (6) No code release makes reproduction and extension impossible, a significant gap for a paper presenting a framework rather than a theoretical result. Bottom Line: PI-Hunter makes a genuine methodological contribution by reframing prompt injection security as proactive ingestion-path mapping and introducing the patch-and-reexplore co-evolution mechanism, but the single-baseline comparison and absence of a code release prevent confident assessment of where it stands relative to the broader red-teaming literature, making it a compelling prototype that warrants follow-up rather than immediate deployment.

SHIP: SRAM-Based Huge Inference Pipelines for Fast LLM Serving

openreview.net · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents SHIP (SRAM-based Huge Inference Pipelines), a production LLM inference system built on Groq's Language Processing Unit (LPU) that stores both model weights and KV caches entirely in on-chip SRAM across thousands of chips, serving hundreds of billions of tokens daily. - Why it's important: GPU-based serving is fundamentally memory-bandwidth-bottlenecked during decode, forcing operators to choose between latency and throughput; SRAM's order-of-magnitude higher bandwidth breaks this tradeoff, enabling consistently low TTFT and TPOT simultaneously. - How it does it: The LPU's deterministic, statically-compiled execution model enables synchronous lock-step operation across thousands of chips via a low-diameter QuadFour topology (300 ns per hop), combined with dynamic chunked prefill (down to chunk-size=1), fused context-batch scheduling, and two-tier prefix caching across SRAM and host DDR4. - The good: In controlled experiments serving Qwen3-235B-A22B, SHIP maintains stable per-user decode throughput (ot/s/u) across a wide range of prefill:decode ratios and context lengths where SGLang/B200 shows pronounced TPOT degradation, and achieves lower mean TTFT than the B200 DGX across all tested scenarios including production traffic samples. - The missing: Aggregate system throughput (tokens/second) at low prefill:decode ratios and long context is substantially worse than the B200 baseline, and the paper provides no ablation isolating individual architectural contributions; peak FLOPs utilization is also acknowledged to be lower than GPU systems. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on LLM serving infrastructure or accelerator co-design; it is the most detailed public account of an SRAM-based production inference deployment and surfaces non-obvious scheduling and topology decisions that matter at scale.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents SHIP (SRAM-based Huge Inference Pipelines), a production LLM inference system built on Groq's Language Processing Unit (LPU) that stores both model weights and KV caches entirely in on-chip SRAM across thousands of chips, serving hundreds of billions of tokens daily. - Why it's important: GPU-based serving is fundamentally memory-bandwidth-bottlenecked during decode, forcing operators to choose between latency and throughput; SRAM's order-of-magnitude higher bandwidth breaks this tradeoff, enabling consistently low TTFT and TPOT simultaneously. - How it does it: The LPU's deterministic, statically-compiled execution model enables synchronous lock-step operation across thousands of chips via a low-diameter QuadFour topology (300 ns per hop), combined with dynamic chunked prefill (down to chunk-size=1), fused context-batch scheduling, and two-tier prefix caching across SRAM and host DDR4. - The good: In controlled experiments serving Qwen3-235B-A22B, SHIP maintains stable per-user decode throughput (ot/s/u) across a wide range of prefill:decode ratios and context lengths where SGLang/B200 shows pronounced TPOT degradation, and achieves lower mean TTFT than the B200 DGX across all tested scenarios including production traffic samples. - The missing: Aggregate system throughput (tokens/second) at low prefill:decode ratios and long context is substantially worse than the B200 baseline, and the paper provides no ablation isolating individual architectural contributions; peak FLOPs utilization is also acknowledged to be lower than GPU systems. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on LLM serving infrastructure or accelerator co-design; it is the most detailed public account of an SRAM-based production inference deployment and surfaces non-obvious scheduling and topology decisions that matter at scale. Problem & Motivation: LLM inference providers must simultaneously minimize time-to-first-token (TTFT) and time-per-output-token (TPOT) across workloads that vary continuously in prefill:decode token ratio and context length. GPU systems using HBM face a fundamental tension: decode is memory-bandwidth-bound, so batching improves FLOP utilization at the cost of TPOT, while prefill-prioritized scheduling stabilizes TTFT at the cost of decode latency. This tension worsens for reasoning models that generate 2–10× more tokens per prompt and require 10× longer output sequences. MoE architectures add further stress because data-dependent expert routing prevents full batching benefits. The paper addresses the question of whether SRAM—offering >10× the memory bandwidth of HBM but far lower density—can serve as the primary storage tier for LLM weights and KV caches in a commercially viable way at scale. Key Idea: The central insight is that LLM decode is bandwidth-limited, not compute-limited, and SRAM's bandwidth advantage allows efficient inference at batch size 1 (no batching needed to amortize memory access), fundamentally reframing the serving problem from throughput maximization to latency minimization. This shifts the bottleneck from bandwidth to capacity, which can be addressed by scaling the number of chips. The enabling technical realization is that a statically compiled, deterministic execution model can synchronize thousands of chips without host or router involvement, making cycle-accurate collective pipelining feasible at low batch sizes where GPU NCCL overhead (1–10 µs per collective) would dominate. Methodology: The system is organized as a pipeline combining tensor parallelism (TP) and pipeline parallelism (PP). The LPU chip (GlobalFoundries 14 nm) integrates 230 MB of SRAM and 235 GB/s of chip-to-chip (C2C) I/O across 11 logical ports, with SIMD units operating on 320-element vectors. Eight LPUs form a node in a K8 clique; nodes are chained in a linear sequence with 32 inter-node links (QuadFour topology), achieving 3-hop diameter for a 72-LPU TP partition and 853 GB/s bisection bandwidth. The compiler statically schedules all instruction dispatch including network transfers, enabling lock-free C2C transfers at 300 ns per hop and cycle-level compute/communication overlap. Three critical software mechanisms address the capacity-constrained SRAM environment: (1) PagedAttention with 128–512 token pages, managed via a compact mask-encoding forwarded pipeline-wide from the host CPU; (2) two-tier prefix caching using a BlockTrie across SRAM and host DDR4 (up to 72 TB aggregate in a 72-node gpt-oss-120B instance), with LRU eviction; (3) speculative decoding where a small draft model is inserted as an extra pipeline stage, trading draft overhead for reduced KV cache pressure. Pipeline scheduling uses dynamic chunked prefill (chunk sizes from 1 to full pipeline capacity, adapted per step based on observed P:D ratio), fused context-batch (B×C dimensions fused and partitioned into N chunks driven by an N×N mask from the scheduler to maintain uniform stage latencies), and capacity-filling prefill (decode prioritized, prefill backfills idle slots). The design decision to execute MoE experts per-token rather than batched is well-argued for first-gen LPU: it preserves pipeline balance at the cost of OI=1 in expert MatMuls, justified by SRAM's ability to sustain high utilization even at OI=1. Key Results: The primary quantitative comparison pits a large SHIP instance (TP=16, PP=95, max concurrency=380) running Qwen3-235B-A22B against an 8×B200 DGX node running SGLang v0.5.8 at two mixed-batch token budget (MBTB) settings (2Ki and 16Ki). Results are presented as normalized throughput curves across P:D ratios from 1–256 and context lengths 2Ki–8Ki. SHIP maintains ot/s/u within 20% of peak across all P:D values at CL≤4Ki and maintains stable decode throughput under production traffic where SGB200 shows pronounced TPOT instability depending on MBTB tuning. On raw end-to-end latency (TTFT+n×TPOT) from OpenRouter data over one month (Sep–Oct 2025), SHIP is measured as fastest across five models tested. SHIP shows substantially lower system throughput than SGB200 at MBTB=16Ki in high-P:D conditions (SGB200 reaches near-peak; SHIP's normalized curve is lower). Collective bandwidth reaches 50% saturation at 32 KiB tensor size and 90% at 80 KiB; GPU NCCL requires much larger tensors to reach comparable efficiency. LPU provisioned power is 388 W per chip vs 1,788 W per GPU for B200 DGX. Prefix cache DRAM hit rates of 50–75% are reported from production for two model instances over a 7-day window. No absolute token-throughput numbers comparing SHIP to GPU are published; all throughput comparisons use normalized relative curves. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The QuadFour topology and its synchronization protocol constitute a novel interconnect design tailored for low-batch, latency-sensitive inference. Dynamic chunked prefill at chunk-size=1 and fused context-batch are novel scheduling mechanisms. The demonstration that SRAM-based inference at production scale is commercially viable is a genuine empirical finding, not merely a claimed benefit. The individual serving algorithms (PagedAttention, prefix caching, speculative decoding) are adaptations of prior work, not novel inventions. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are presented that isolate the contribution of dynamic chunked prefill, fused context-batch, or prefix caching individually. No error bars or confidence intervals appear on any throughput figures. The controlled comparison uses a single model (Qwen3-235B-A22B) on a single SHIP configuration; no results for dense models or smaller SHIP sizes appear in the main text. The paper is a peer-reviewed MLSys 2026 Industry Track publication, warranting moderate confidence—but industry-track systems papers are typically evaluated for deployment plausibility rather than algorithmic rigor, and vendor-favorable framing is likely. The normalized throughput presentation obscures absolute performance differences: SHIP's peak throughput and SGB200's peak throughput are both set to 1.0× on their respective systems, making direct comparison of absolute output tokens/second impossible from the figures. Code and model weights are not released; the LPU is proprietary hardware. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile): - Practical: The system requires proprietary Groq LPU hardware and is accessible only via Groq's public cloud API; no path exists for independent deployment. - Efficient: At 388 W per LPU vs 1,788 W per B200 GPU, per-chip power is 4.6× lower, but a 72-chip SHIP instance for a single 120B-parameter MoE model consumes ~28 kW before host overhead, and peak FLOPs utilization is acknowledged to be lower than GPU systems. - Effective: The evidence shows SHIP solves the stated problem of stable, low-latency decode under dynamic traffic; TTFT is lower than B200 across all tested scenarios, and ot/s/u stability is substantially better. - Scalable: The paper demonstrates deployment at thousands of chips and hundreds of billions of daily tokens; scaling to longer context (>8Ki) and lower P:D ratios degrades throughput, and next-generation LPUs are cited as the remediation path rather than software solutions. The work delivers a deployed cloud service, not a software artifact. Strengths: - The production-scale SRAM inference deployment is the first of its kind publicly documented in sufficient technical depth; the QuadFour topology design rationale (diameter vs. bisection bandwidth trade-off) is clearly articulated and original. - Fused context-batch is a non-obvious scheduling primitive that addresses pipeline bubble formation from heterogeneous context lengths without requiring context-length-aware batching algorithms. - The paper honestly quantifies where SHIP underperforms GPU systems (low P:D, long context, absolute peak FLOPs efficiency) rather than cherry-picking favorable comparisons. - The propagated synchronization scheme for distributed clock recovery across thousands of chips—enabling compiler-visible synchrony without dedicated router hardware—is technically novel and well-described. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that SHIP's system throughput at low P:D and long context is degraded relative to peak and inferior to GPU configurations, citing on-chip SRAM capacity as the binding constraint. They acknowledge that peak FLOP/s efficiency does not match NVIDIA GPU systems. They note that LPUv1's C2C bandwidth (235 GB/s vs NVLink 1,800 GB/s) is a key limiting factor for collective efficiency and defer improvement to next-generation LPUs. They acknowledge that MoE per-token expert execution sacrifices OI for pipeline balance and will require a different strategy at higher batch sizes. Adaptive scheduling under mixed reasoning/non-reasoning traffic is flagged as future work. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The normalized throughput presentation prevents readers from computing absolute tokens/second comparisons—a material omission given the paper's claim of superior performance. The SGB200 comparison uses a single GPU node (8×B200) against a massive SHIP instance (TP=16, PP=95); no iso-cost or iso-power comparison appears. Total cost of ownership analysis is qualitative (BOM and power arguments) without actual dollar figures. The claim of "state-of-the-art end-to-end latency" rests on OpenRouter third-party latency rankings, which measure the full provider stack including network and queuing effects, not isolated hardware throughput. Speculative decoding results (Fig. 8) are measured on Llama3.3-70B, a different model from the main Qwen3-235B-A22B comparison, making the SD efficiency claims difficult to integrate with the overall evaluation. No multi-seed or time-of-day variation data is provided for throughput measurements. Bottom Line: Systems engineers building or evaluating LLM inference infrastructure at scale will find SHIP's topology, scheduling, and memory management design decisions directly actionable, but the evaluation's normalized-throughput presentation and lack of iso-cost comparison prevent a clean conclusion that SRAM serving dominates GPU serving on overall efficiency.

Agentic Neuro–Symbolic Planning and Commissioning for Human-in-the-Loop Industrial Robotics with Digital Twins

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a neuro-symbolic multi-agent framework (Specifier-Designer-Inspector, SDI) that translates natural-language assembly commands into physically verified robot trajectories via a Unity3D digital twin, achieving 100% task success across 70 benchmark commands on a dual-arm ABB robotic station. - Why it's important: LLM-only planners cannot reliably satisfy the hard geometric and kinematic constraints (gravity, collision, reachability, gripping feasibility) required in safety-critical industrial manufacturing, leaving a gap between linguistic fluency and physical executability. - How it does it: LLMs are confined strictly to language understanding (Specifier) and structure generation (Designer), while all constraint verification, assembly sequencing, and execution use deterministic symbolic modules; LangGraph-based routing is reserved only for failure-recovery branching where context-awareness adds value. - The good: An ablation replacing the symbolic Inspector with an equivalently-prompted LLM critic collapses success from 98.1% to 3.8% with a 4× generation-time blowup — a stark, quantitative demonstration that LLM-as-evaluator is insufficient for physical feasibility checking. - The missing: The 70-command benchmark is entirely self-constructed and self-evaluated by the same team with no independent third-party benchmark, and the physical experiment covers only 12 commands without multi-run variance — so the 100% headline should be read as a proof-of-concept, not a deployment-ready claim. - Read it or not: Worth reading for robotics researchers and engineers building LLM-integrated industrial assembly systems who need a principled, reproducible architecture for constraint-verified planning with human oversight; can be skipped by those seeking advances in motion planning algorithms or general-purpose LLM reasoning.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a neuro-symbolic multi-agent framework (Specifier-Designer-Inspector, SDI) that translates natural-language assembly commands into physically verified robot trajectories via a Unity3D digital twin, achieving 100% task success across 70 benchmark commands on a dual-arm ABB robotic station. - Why it's important: LLM-only planners cannot reliably satisfy the hard geometric and kinematic constraints (gravity, collision, reachability, gripping feasibility) required in safety-critical industrial manufacturing, leaving a gap between linguistic fluency and physical executability. - How it does it: LLMs are confined strictly to language understanding (Specifier) and structure generation (Designer), while all constraint verification, assembly sequencing, and execution use deterministic symbolic modules; LangGraph-based routing is reserved only for failure-recovery branching where context-awareness adds value. - The good: An ablation replacing the symbolic Inspector with an equivalently-prompted LLM critic collapses success from 98.1% to 3.8% with a 4× generation-time blowup — a stark, quantitative demonstration that LLM-as-evaluator is insufficient for physical feasibility checking. - The missing: The 70-command benchmark is entirely self-constructed and self-evaluated by the same team with no independent third-party benchmark, and the physical experiment covers only 12 commands without multi-run variance — so the 100% headline should be read as a proof-of-concept, not a deployment-ready claim. - Read it or not: Worth reading for robotics researchers and engineers building LLM-integrated industrial assembly systems who need a principled, reproducible architecture for constraint-verified planning with human oversight; can be skipped by those seeking advances in motion planning algorithms or general-purpose LLM reasoning. Problem & Motivation Industrial robot automation increasingly demands that operators specify tasks in natural language, but no existing system closes the full pipeline from free-form language to physical execution with human oversight and closed-loop failure recovery. Classical task-and-motion planning (TAMP) provides formal guarantees but requires hand-coded domain descriptions and cannot accept natural-language input. LLM-based planners such as SayCan and Code as Policies lack constraint verification and recovery. Multi-agent LLM frameworks (AutoGen, CrewAI) operate purely in language space with no connection to physical systems or digital twins. LangGraph-based orchestration that delegates all control-flow decisions to the LLM yields unpredictable behavior and difficult debugging in practice. The core gap is that no prior system unifies NL intent, symbolic physical feasibility, human-in-the-loop inspection via a digital twin, and closed-loop execution-level failure recovery in a single deployable pipeline. Key Idea The key technical insight is that the Evaluator role in software engineering's Planner-Generator-Evaluator (PGE) harness pattern cannot be filled by an LLM in physical robotics — geometric and kinematic feasibility require exact numerical checking against hard thresholds, not probabilistic language model judgment — and so it must be replaced by a deterministic symbolic verifier (the Inspector). This single substitution transforms an unreliable LLM-driven loop into a formally verifiable generate–verify–repair cycle, while the LLM is retained where it genuinely adds value: interpreting ambiguous natural language (Specifier) and generating creative spatial layouts (Designer). Methodology The pipeline has four modules orchestrated by a LangGraph state machine over phases {PLAN, DESIGN, INSPECT, HUMANREVIEW, PREEXECUTE, EXECUTE, REPLAN}. Module 1 (Structure Design Generation): The Specifier skill expands a raw NL command into a structured specification; the LLM Designer generates a candidate 3D layout (per-object ID, position p∈ℝ³, orientation q∈SO(3), size s∈ℝ³); the symbolic Inspector verifies against six constraints: gravity (objects placed bottom-up), reachability (cuboid workspace check), collision (AABB disjointness), contact (non-ground objects must have structural support), gripping feasibility (at least one unblocked lateral approach direction), and assembly sequence precedence. Two design strategies are instantiated: an adaptive constraint-guided loop (fixed control flow, one LLM call per retry, only violated constraints fed back as targeted prompt augmentation) and an agentic skill-based loop (LLM dynamically selects composite or granular verification skills via function calling). A final safety gate always re-verifies before the layout is passed downstream. Module 2 (Human-in-the-Loop): The verified layout is rendered in a Unity3D digital twin; the operator can inspect, translate/rotate/insert/remove objects by direct manipulation; any modification triggers automatic re-verification by the Inspector before proceeding. Module 3 (Symbolic Sequencing): A DAG is built from object support relations (Constraint 1 antecedent); topological sort by iterative sink-node removal yields a disassembly order that is reversed to produce the assembly sequence σ*; gripping feasibility (Constraint 5) is enforced at each sink selection step. Module 4 (Motion Planning and Monitoring): MoveIt with RRTConnect generates collision-free joint trajectories; the digital twin streams both planned (Q^plan) and executed (Q^exec) trajectories; deviation metric δ = max_{r,t} ‖q^plan_r(t) − q^exec_r(t)‖₂ triggers pause, human escalation, or replanning when a threshold is exceeded. Two-tier failure recovery: structure-level failures trigger LangGraph-routed context-aware replanning (Designer ξmodify or geometric recovery skills ξflatten/ξshift that bypass the Designer and feed corrected layouts directly to the Inspector); execution-level geometric failures invoke deterministic recovery skills. All retry and escalation decisions remain with the Orchestrator. Three critical design decisions: (1) LLMs restricted to language/generation — justified by the 98.1% → 3.8% LLM-critic ablation; (2) LangGraph routing applied only to failure-recovery branching, not nominal control flow — reduces hallucinated routing; (3) targeted constraint feedback (only violated constraints, not full K) — reduces prompt complexity and repair iterations. Key Results Evaluated on 70 self-constructed natural-language assembly commands in five difficulty tiers (A: 3–15 steps, explicit; B: 5–15 steps, vague/semantic; C: 7–16 steps, enclosed/dense; D: 6–20 steps, open-ended + 3D packing; E: 13–25 steps, stress-scale) against 10 baselines spanning single-pass LLM, adaptive feedback loops, single-agent tool-use, and multi-agent orchestration variants. Hardware: dual-arm ABB IRB120 + IRB1600, GPT-4o-mini (temp 0.3), Ubuntu 22.04, ROS 2, MoveIt 2, Unity3D. Ours-Hybrid achieves 100% overall success (58/58 Groups A–D; 12/12 Group E), the only method to do so. Best non-agentic baseline is LLM-AdaptLoop and SAMS, both at 93.1% overall, with Group D dropping to 83.3% and 88.9% respectively; weakest baseline (LLM-FullPrompt) reaches only 74.1% overall with 55.6% on Group D. Ablation: removing the Specifier drops Group D from 100% to 95.5%; removing LangGraph routing drops Group C from 100% to 83.3%; replacing symbolic Inspector with LLM critic collapses success from 98.1% to 3.8% with generation time rising from 85.3s to 319.0s. Failure recovery: Hybrid recovers 9/9 human-modification violations and 9/10 injected deterministic execution failures (LGOrch achieves 10/10, surpassing Hybrid on the narrow-corridor scenario where Specifier over-constrains the search space). Physical deployment: 12/12 commands produce valid IK solutions and collision-free trajectories; dual-arm commands achieve up to 1.7× speedup. Average generation time for Hybrid is 85.3s versus 14.9s for LLM-Direct, reflecting multi-agent LLM API overhead. Baselines are moderately strong — LLM-AdaptLoop with targeted feedback and SAMS with 8 granular tools are genuine comparators — but no baseline represents a fully optimized neuro-symbolic prior system (e.g., PDDLStream + LLM grounding), which would be the most relevant comparison. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The primary novelty is the adaptation of the PGE software-engineering harness to physical robotics by substituting the LLM Evaluator with a deterministic symbolic Inspector (SDI), combined with selective LangGraph routing and two-tier failure recovery. The individual components — LLMs, LangGraph, MoveIt, AABB constraint checking, Unity3D digital twins — are all existing technologies; novelty lies in their principled composition and the empirical demonstration that LLM-as-evaluator fails categorically for physical feasibility tasks. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study covers all key architectural axes (Specifier, LangGraph, Inspector, recovery skills) and is the strongest part of the paper — the LLM-critic ablation in particular is a necessary and decisive experiment. No error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported; each of the 70 commands is apparently run once. No code, dataset, or trained model release is mentioned. The 70-command benchmark is entirely self-authored and self-evaluated, raising Goodhart's Law concerns — a system tuned on this task class, evaluated on the same task class, with no held-out or third-party benchmark. Physical experiments cover 12 commands from a single lab with two specific ABB robots. The paper is a preprint submitted to IEEE, not yet peer-reviewed. GPT-4o-mini is the only LLM evaluated; sensitivity to model choice is untested.
