Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built and deployed pipeline evaluated on physical hardware. - Practical: Runs on commercially available hardware (ABB IRB120/1600, OnRobot grippers, Ubuntu 22.04, ROS 2, Unity3D, GPT-4o-mini API) with no specialized compute requirements. - Efficient: 85.3s average per command due to multi-agent LLM API latency; authors acknowledge this and propose lighter models or cached Specifier outputs as mitigations, but no numbers are provided. - Effective: Evidence from 70 benchmark commands and 12 physical trials supports feasibility for block-stacking assembly with known object positions; the benchmark is not independent, so the 100% success rate should be treated cautiously. - Scalable: Tested on structures up to 25 assembly steps with axis-aligned bounding boxes and known supply-area positions; scalability to heterogeneous parts, visual perception, larger workspaces, or more complex geometry is claimed as future work but not demonstrated. The system delivers a deployed software pipeline (ROS 2 + Unity3D + Python) but no public code release is mentioned. Strengths - The LLM-critic ablation (98.1% → 3.8% success, 4× time blowup) is a genuinely compelling empirical argument for the neuro-symbolic split — not a claim, a measured result that would change a practitioner's design decisions. - The two-tier failure recovery architecture (structure-level LangGraph routing vs. execution-level deterministic geometric recovery skills) is a principled distinction not present in prior LLM robotics systems, and Tables V–VI provide per-scenario detail that exposes where each mechanism succeeds and fails. - The formal specification of the pipeline (Algorithms 1–4, operator chain notation) gives enough detail to replicate the approach without ambiguity. - Real dual-arm physical experiments, while limited (12 commands), validate that the planned trajectories are kinematically feasible on actual hardware — a step beyond simulation-only evaluation that most comparable papers omit. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) known supply-area positions are assumed and visual perception for object localization was not integrated (stated as out of scope); (2) 85.3s per-command average is slow for latency-sensitive deployments, with lighter models or cached Specifier outputs proposed as mitigations. The authors confront these limitations directly in the Discussion section, which is a credibility positive, though they do not acknowledge the self-constructed benchmark or the absence of statistical testing. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 70-command benchmark is entirely authored and evaluated by the same team with no third-party benchmark — this is the most significant methodological gap; systems papers of this type typically include at least one independent benchmark (e.g., Assembly-PlanBench, RoboSuite, or standard TAMP domains). - Physical experiments run only 12 commands with no repeated trials — success/failure counts at this scale carry high variance and do not establish deployment reliability. - No sensitivity analysis on LLM choice; GPT-4o-mini may not be representative of weaker or stronger models, and the generalization of the SDI harness to other LLMs is untested. - The Specifier-relaxation recovery mechanism (reverting to raw command after repeated failures) is described but not ablated separately from the geometric recovery skills, making it impossible to attribute recovery credit precisely. - Axis-aligned bounding box collision checking is a significant geometric simplification — real industrial objects with irregular shapes or tight tolerances would require a different verifier, and the system's performance in such cases is unknown. - LGOrch (no Specifier) outperforms Hybrid on the narrow-corridor recovery scenario (10/10 vs. 9/10), suggesting the Specifier's ambiguity-grounding benefit systematically conflicts with recovery flexibility in constrained spaces — a deeper architectural tension that the paper identifies but does not resolve. Bottom Line The SDI ablation result — LLM-critic substitution collapsing success by 94 percentage points — is a genuinely useful finding for any practitioner building LLM-integrated physical robot systems, but the self-authored benchmark and absence of multi-run statistics mean this should be treated as a well-argued design blueprint rather than a validated production system.

On Subquadratic Architectures: From Applications to Principles

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper provides the first controlled head-to-head comparison of xLSTM, Mamba-2, and Gated DeltaNet on tasks with complex long-range dependencies (code pre-training, Transformer distillation, time-series forecasting), and derives a unified gating framework that attributes xLSTM's consistent advantage to two architectural primitives — accumulation and finite-state tracking. - Why it's important: Subquadratic operators are actively displacing softmax attention in hybrid foundation models (Jamba, Samba, Nemotron Nano, OLMo Hybrid), yet prior comparisons used generic English-web benchmarks where performance differences are too small to differentiate architectures, leaving practitioners without principled guidance on operator selection. - How it does it: All three architectures are rewritten in a common input-gate / forget-gate / state-update notation; this formulation predicts which architectures can accumulate over unbounded lengths and which can perform finite-state tracking, and those predictions are validated on six controlled synthetic tasks (AnBn, AnBnCn, Majority, Parity, Mod. Arith. Z5, S3) with 4× and 16× length extrapolation. - The good: xLSTM[1:1] is the only configuration that length-generalizes on both primitive families simultaneously — perfect state tracking (1.000 on Parity, Mod. Arith., S3 at all lengths up to 2048) and meaningful counting extrapolation (0.834 AnBn, 0.742 Majority at 2048) — directly confirming the mechanistic prediction. - The missing: The language model pre-training comparison is confined to 400M parameters with a single training run per configuration and no error bars; the claimed three-way comparison is incomplete in distillation (Mamba-2 is excluded due to architectural incompatibility with the recipe). - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML researchers and engineers selecting or designing subquadratic sequence operators — the unified gating analysis and synthetic task dissection provide mechanistic insight that generic benchmark comparisons do not; less relevant for practitioners targeting multi-billion-parameter regimes, where the advantage may not hold.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper provides the first controlled head-to-head comparison of xLSTM, Mamba-2, and Gated DeltaNet on tasks with complex long-range dependencies (code pre-training, Transformer distillation, time-series forecasting), and derives a unified gating framework that attributes xLSTM's consistent advantage to two architectural primitives — accumulation and finite-state tracking. - Why it's important: Subquadratic operators are actively displacing softmax attention in hybrid foundation models (Jamba, Samba, Nemotron Nano, OLMo Hybrid), yet prior comparisons used generic English-web benchmarks where performance differences are too small to differentiate architectures, leaving practitioners without principled guidance on operator selection. - How it does it: All three architectures are rewritten in a common input-gate / forget-gate / state-update notation; this formulation predicts which architectures can accumulate over unbounded lengths and which can perform finite-state tracking, and those predictions are validated on six controlled synthetic tasks (AnBn, AnBnCn, Majority, Parity, Mod. Arith. Z5, S3) with 4× and 16× length extrapolation. - The good: xLSTM[1:1] is the only configuration that length-generalizes on both primitive families simultaneously — perfect state tracking (1.000 on Parity, Mod. Arith., S3 at all lengths up to 2048) and meaningful counting extrapolation (0.834 AnBn, 0.742 Majority at 2048) — directly confirming the mechanistic prediction. - The missing: The language model pre-training comparison is confined to 400M parameters with a single training run per configuration and no error bars; the claimed three-way comparison is incomplete in distillation (Mamba-2 is excluded due to architectural incompatibility with the recipe). - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML researchers and engineers selecting or designing subquadratic sequence operators — the unified gating analysis and synthetic task dissection provide mechanistic insight that generic benchmark comparisons do not; less relevant for practitioners targeting multi-billion-parameter regimes, where the advantage may not hold. Problem & Motivation Transformer attention scales as O(T²) in sequence length, motivating subquadratic alternatives based on linear attention, state-space models, and recurrent mechanisms. Recent hybrid models (Samba, Nemotron Nano, Kimi Linear, OLMo Hybrid) already use these operators as drop-in replacements for many attention layers, making operator choice a practical engineering decision. However, existing comparisons of xLSTM, Mamba-2, and Gated DeltaNet have been conducted almost exclusively on standard English-language modeling and commonsense reasoning benchmarks, where inductive biases between architectures are not stressed. Prior work (Deletang et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023) shows that architectural differences emerge sharply on data with structured, long-range dependencies. Code, which carries syntax trees, variable scopes, and call graphs, and time series, which requires inferring partially observed dynamics over long histories, are both natural stress tests — yet no controlled, matched comparison existed across these domains. The gap this paper closes is simultaneously empirical (which architecture wins under matched conditions) and mechanistic (why). Key Idea The core insight is that xLSTM's hybrid design structurally separates two capabilities that other architectures conflate or sacrifice. The matrix-state mLSTM component (xLSTM[1:0]) uses an exponential input gate that acts as a softmax over time, enabling flexible accumulation without overwriting old state; this alone handles counting-style tasks. The nonlinear recurrent sLSTM component (xLSTM[0:1]) uses recurrent weight matrices Ri, Rf, Rv that incorporate previous hidden state, enabling finite-automaton-style state tracking. Mamba-2 ties its input and forget gates through a single projection w_Δ, making it structurally analogous to a GRU and preventing independent control of write and erase — a known failure mode for counting (Weiss et al., 2018). Gated DeltaNet adds an orthogonal projection (I − kt⊗kt / ‖kt‖²) that explicitly overwrites old key-direction state, benefiting retrieval but inherently losing the ability to accumulate across repeated keys. Mixed xLSTM[m:s] architectures are therefore the only design that naturally combines both primitives. Methodology The paper proceeds in three phases. Phase 1 is empirical: (1) Code-focused LM pre-training — 400M-parameter inter-layer hybrid models (24 layers, 3 softmax attention layers, 21 subquadratic layers) trained with lm-engine on Nemotron-CC-Code-v1 at 20B and 100B tokens and a mixed Nemotron-CC-Code-v1 + FineWeb-Edu corpus at 20B tokens; compared xLSTM[7:1], Mamba-2, Gated DeltaNet (plus ablation variants xLSTM[1:0], xLSTM[11:1], GatedDeltaNet[-1,1]); evaluated on HumanEval pass@k (k∈{2,8,16,64}) and five reasoning/commonsense benchmarks. (2) Transformer distillation — Qwen3-4B-Instruct teacher linearized using a two-stage recipe: Stage I minimizes per-layer MSE between teacher and student attention outputs; Stage II minimizes 0.9CE + 0.1KL(top-256 sparse teacher distribution) for 10,000 steps at sequence length 4,096; plug-in operators compared are xLSTM[1:0] and Gated DeltaNet (Mamba-2 and xLSTM[0:1] excluded due to incompatibility); evaluated on HumanEval, HumanEval+, MBPP, MBPP+ (pass@1) and GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME 2024. (3) Time-series foundation model pre-training — matched protocol from TiRex (Auer et al., 2025) on ~47.5M time series (Chronos corpus + GIFT-Eval pretraining + KernelSynth); 1M/4M/10M/40M/80M parameter scales; evaluated zero-shot on GIFT-Eval with MASE and CRPS (geometric-mean aggregated). Phase 2 unifies the three architectures in a shared linear-attention-with-gating notation, exposing differences in gate coupling and state overwriting. Phase 3 tests the resulting predictions on six synthetic formal-language tasks trained at length 128 and evaluated at 128/512/2048, run over 5 seeds with the maximum reported. Key design decisions: (a) using HumanEval pass@k at multiple k to separate capability from luck — well-justified; (b) the plug-in distillation comparison isolates xLSTM[1:0] vs. Gated DeltaNet but cannot include Mamba-2 — justified but limits scope; (c) reporting maximum-of-5-seeds for synthetic tasks — non-standard and inflates apparent performance. Key Results Code pre-training (400M): xLSTM[7:1] leads at every pass@k in all three training configurations. At pass@64: +1.43 points over next-best (Gated DeltaNet) at 20B code tokens (12.13 vs. 10.70); +0.90 points at 100B code tokens (15.84 vs. 14.94); +1.81 points on mixed corpus (9.50 vs. 7.69). Reasoning/commonsense margins are <0.1 points over competing models at 20B and 100B code-only tokens — confirming that broad benchmarks cannot differentiate these architectures. Code distillation (4B-parameter students from Qwen3-4B-Instruct): xLSTM[1:0] averages 0.768 across HumanEval/HumanEval+/MBPP/MBPP+ vs. 0.755 for Gated DeltaNet and 0.756 for GatedDeltaNet[-1,1]; xLSTM[1:0] trails on MBPP+ by 0.014. Math distillation: xLSTM[1:0] 0.645 average (GSM8K 0.876, MATH-500 0.726, AIME pass@8 0.333) vs. Gated DeltaNet 0.625. TSFM (1M–80M): xLSTM[3:1] achieves 0.733 MASE / 0.508 CRPS at 10M vs. 0.767 / 0.525 for Mamba-2; advantage narrows substantially at 80M (xLSTM[3:1] 0.715/0.490, Mamba-2 0.715/0.485 — Mamba-2 leads CRPS by 0.005). Synthetic tasks: xLSTM[1:1] achieves 1.000 on all three state-tracking tasks at lengths up to 2048 and reaches 0.834/0.716/0.742 on AnBn/AnBnCn/Majority at 2048. Mamba-2 collapses on every task (AnBn drops from 1.000 at 128 to 0.241 at 2048; Parity never exceeds 0.352 even in-distribution). Gated DeltaNet[-1,1] recovers in-distribution state tracking (1.000 Parity at 128) but fails to length-generalize (Parity 0.472 at 2048, S3 0.667 at 2048) and does not improve counting. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD (analytical framework). The head-to-head comparison under matched conditions across code and time-series domains is the first of its kind for these three architectures. The unified gating notation bridging SSM and linear-attention representations is a methodological contribution that yields a falsifiable prediction. What is not novel: the individual architectures are all prior work; the insight that GRU-like tied gates impede counting draws on Weiss et al. (2018) and Merrill et al. (2024); negative-eigenvalue Gated DeltaNet is from Grazzi et al. (2025). The main novelty is the connection between architectural gating choices and empirically observed task performance, demonstrated in a controlled way. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for the xLSTM layer ratio (xLSTM[1:0], [7:1], [11:1]) and for the Gated DeltaNet negative-eigenvalue variant across all settings. No error bars are reported for the main language model pre-training or distillation experiments (single runs per configuration). Synthetic tasks run 5 seeds and report the maximum, not the mean — this is non-standard and optimistic; reporting maximum over seeds exaggerates confidence in capability claims. No code, trained models, or datasets are released (not mentioned in the paper). The distillation comparison is limited to one teacher (Qwen3-4B-Instruct) with no variance estimate. The pre-training comparison uses one data regime and one compute budget. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.12364v1, June 2026) with no peer review. HumanEval data contamination in Nemotron-CC-Code-v1 is not discussed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All experiments run on commercially available hardware (8×H100 for 400M LM pre-training; 4×A100 for TSFM; single H100 for synthetic tasks), making the methods accessible to research-scale institutions. - Efficient: Inference compute costs, FLOPs, and wall-clock training times are not reported; the sequential sLSTM component in xLSTM[m:s] is not parallelizable over the sequence dimension, which could create inference latency overhead relative to purely parallelizable architectures like Mamba-2, but this trade-off is not quantified. - Effective: Evidence supports xLSTM advantage on HumanEval code generation (margins of 0.90–1.81 pass@64 points) and GIFT-Eval time-series forecasting (1M–40M scale), with the synthetic tasks providing mechanistic validation; the advantage on broad reasoning benchmarks is negligible (<0.1 points). - Scalable: Language model experiments are confined to 400M parameters — one scale — and the time-series advantage disappears at 80M; no experiments at 1B+ parameter scale are reported, making extrapolation to larger models unsupported by evidence in this paper. The work delivers no software artifact, trained model weights, or dataset; it stops at paper form. Strengths - The unified gating notation (Equations 1–4) cleanly expresses all three architectures in commensurable form, makes the predicted capability gap explicit and falsifiable, and is technically accurate. This is the strongest intellectual contribution. - Multi-domain convergent evidence: the same ranking (xLSTM > Gated DeltaNet > Mamba-2 on most complex-dependency tasks) holds across from-scratch LM pre-training, Transformer distillation, and time-series forecasting, which substantially strengthens the conclusion beyond any single experiment. - The synthetic task design is precise: counting tasks (AnBn, AnBnCn, Majority) and state-tracking tasks (Parity, Mod. Arith. Z5, S3) each isolate exactly one of the two predicted primitives, and the 4× and 16× length extrapolation tests go well beyond the training distribution. - Testing Gated DeltaNet[-1,1] (negative-eigenvalue variant) across all settings — code pre-training, distillation, time series, and synthetic tasks — rules out the simplest fix and shows that recovering state tracking in Gated DeltaNet does not recover counting, cleanly separating the two capability axes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) code LM pre-training is at 400M parameters only; (2) distillation uses a single teacher; (3) only the time-series experiments include a parameter-scale sweep; (4) several subquadratic families covered in Beck et al. (2024) are excluded. These limitations are presented directly in the conclusion rather than minimized — a credibility-positive practice. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The "first head-to-head comparison" claim is overstated for distillation: Mamba-2 is excluded from that experiment due to architectural incompatibility, so the three-way comparison only holds for pre-training and time series. - Synthetic experiments report maximum over 5 seeds, not mean ± std. For binary outcomes like Parity at 2048, reporting the maximum of 5 binary-looking runs inflates apparent capability; mean and variance would better characterize reliability. - Compute cost comparison is entirely absent. xLSTM[7:1] includes a sequential recurrent layer that cannot be parallelized; this is a real inference cost that practitioners need but the paper does not address. - HumanEval contamination in training data is not discussed. Nemotron-CC-Code-v1 is a large code corpus whose overlap with HumanEval problems is unknown and unacknowledged. - The time-series advantage disappears at 80M parameters — a result that directly challenges the paper's conclusion but receives only brief treatment; it remains unknown whether xLSTM retains any advantage at language-model scales (1B+). - The distillation experiment initializes xLSTM[1:0] and Gated DeltaNet from teacher QKV weights, which may favor xLSTM[1:0] if its feature maps are more compatible with softmax attention weights at initialization; this potential confound is not ablated. Bottom Line A practitioner designing hybrid subquadratic models for code or time-series tasks will find actionable guidance and a mechanistically grounded explanation for why xLSTM[m:s] outperforms Mamba-2 and Gated DeltaNet on complex-dependency tasks — but the evidence base is limited to ≤400M parameters and the advantage is modest in magnitude, making this a directional recommendation rather than a settled result.

Beyond Query Memorization: Large Language Model Routing with Query Decomposition and Historical Matching

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DecoR, a retrieval-based LLM routing framework that decomposes each query into a structured capability profile (Skills, Knowledge, Difficulty) and selects the cheapest sufficient model by matching against historical query-response logs rather than learning a fixed query-to-model mapping. - Why it's important: Existing routing methods overfit to training query distributions and degrade to near or below random-selection accuracy on out-of-distribution (OOD) queries, making them unreliable in production deployments where query diversity is high and model pools change over time. - How it does it: A three-stage hierarchical sifting pipeline — Jaccard-based hard filtering on capability dimensions, BGE-M3 embedding re-ranking, and a GRPO-trained Log Evaluator that reads context and outputs valid representative log IDs — estimates per-model success probability empirically from matched historical outcomes rather than via learned parametric mappings. - The good: On OOD benchmarks (Math500, MT-bench, MBPP) where all six baseline routers degrade to or below the random-selection baseline, DecoR (DeepSeek-V3.1 fallback) achieves 86.49% average accuracy at 5.0x the cheapest-model cost, near the best single-model performance. - The missing: The paper provides no experiment varying historical log size or domain coverage, so it is unclear whether OOD robustness comes from log matching or simply from always triggering the expensive fallback model — a confound the authors do not disentangle. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers building multi-LLM deployment systems who need OOD-robust routing; the capability-profile abstraction is practically useful, but the absence of latency measurements and log-coverage analysis leaves key production questions unanswered.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DecoR, a retrieval-based LLM routing framework that decomposes each query into a structured capability profile (Skills, Knowledge, Difficulty) and selects the cheapest sufficient model by matching against historical query-response logs rather than learning a fixed query-to-model mapping. - Why it's important: Existing routing methods overfit to training query distributions and degrade to near or below random-selection accuracy on out-of-distribution (OOD) queries, making them unreliable in production deployments where query diversity is high and model pools change over time. - How it does it: A three-stage hierarchical sifting pipeline — Jaccard-based hard filtering on capability dimensions, BGE-M3 embedding re-ranking, and a GRPO-trained Log Evaluator that reads context and outputs valid representative log IDs — estimates per-model success probability empirically from matched historical outcomes rather than via learned parametric mappings. - The good: On OOD benchmarks (Math500, MT-bench, MBPP) where all six baseline routers degrade to or below the random-selection baseline, DecoR (DeepSeek-V3.1 fallback) achieves 86.49% average accuracy at 5.0x the cheapest-model cost, near the best single-model performance. - The missing: The paper provides no experiment varying historical log size or domain coverage, so it is unclear whether OOD robustness comes from log matching or simply from always triggering the expensive fallback model — a confound the authors do not disentangle. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers building multi-LLM deployment systems who need OOD-robust routing; the capability-profile abstraction is practically useful, but the absence of latency measurements and log-coverage analysis leaves key production questions unanswered. Problem & Motivation LLM deployment practitioners face a cost-quality trade-off: simple queries do not require frontier models, but routing systems must decide dynamically which model to invoke. Current routers (RouterDC, KNN Router, EmbedLLM, MODEL-SAT, HybridLLM) learn a parametric mapping from query embeddings to model IDs via supervised training on query-response pairs. This paradigm memorizes surface-level query features rather than task-intrinsic capability requirements. When query distributions shift — new domains, phrasings, or task types — the router's internal embedding-to-model mapping fails, in extreme cases performing worse than random selection. A second practical limitation is that end-to-end training couples router logic to a specific model pool, requiring full retraining whenever models are added, removed, or updated. Key Idea The core insight is that two textually dissimilar queries that require the same underlying reasoning skills (e.g., "count fish in aquarium" and "distribute chocolates across days") should route to the same model. Rather than learning a surface-form-to-model mapping, DecoR abstracts each query into a structured capability profile (Skills, Knowledge Domain, Difficulty level) and then treats routing as a retrieval problem: find historical queries with matching capability profiles, observe which model succeeded on those, and select the cheapest model that empirically succeeds. This abstraction decouples routing decisions from specific query phrasing and domain vocabulary, enabling zero-shot generalization to new task types provided that the required skill combination exists in historical logs. Methodology DecoR operates as a four-stage pipeline. (1) Query Deconstruction: A Qwen3-0.6B model SFT-trained on GPT-5-synthesized data (filtered by unanimous consensus from 3 CS PhD students) converts each raw query q into a Capability Profile p = {S, K, D}, where S is an open-vocabulary set of atomic skills (e.g., "arithmetic reasoning", "summarization"), K is a set of knowledge domains (e.g., "medicine", "basic mathematics"), and D ∈ {D0, D1, D2, D3} encodes 4-level cognitive difficulty. Skills and knowledge categories are dynamically generated, not predefined. (2) Substage A — Character-level Sifting: An inverted index retrieves candidate logs; an alignment score ScoreA = Simsk × w is computed, where Simsk is Jaccard overlap on S and K sets summed, and w(Di, Du) is a difficulty-deficit penalty (1.0 if Di ≥ Du, else reduced by 0.25 per level below). Logs with ScoreA < τ=0.5 are discarded; zero-match immediately triggers an OOD fallback to a high-performance default model. (3) Substage B — Fine-ranking: BGE-M3 encodes the concatenation of query text and its string-serialized capability profile; cosine similarity ranks surviving candidates; top-k=3 are retained. (4) Substage C — Log Evaluator: A second Qwen3-0.6B model trained with GRPO (via VERL) reads a context window C = [query_user, p_user] || [q1, p1, ..., qk, pk] and outputs a set V of valid representative log IDs, or V=∅ for OOD. The GRPO reward (Eq. 13) assigns +6 for perfect match, −2|V| for false positives when ground truth is empty, −6 for total miss, and a precision-weighted partial credit otherwise, incentivizing high recall while penalizing hallucination. (5) Empirical Decision: Average performance V̄_j and cost C̄_j per candidate model are computed over matched logs V, normalized to [0,1], and combined as Uj = λ·V_norm_j + (1−λ)·C_norm_j with λ=0.5; argmax model is selected. Three critical design decisions: open-vocabulary skill generation is well-motivated but its consistency is never measured; the 0.25/level difficulty penalty is a heuristic without ablation; GRPO over SFT for the Log Evaluator is justified by the need for generalizable judgment but the advantage over SFT is not isolated. Key Results On the CodaSet ID test set (Table 1), DecoR (DeepSeek-V3.1 fallback) achieves 95.59% on GSM8k, 80.36% on MMLU-Pro, 93.89% on BBH, 87.56% on IFEval — 89.35% average at 2.1x the cheapest-model cost. The strongest single model (Qwen3-235B-A22B) achieves 90.60% at 5.0x cost; the best baseline (MODEL-SAT) achieves 87.50% at 7.8x cost. On the OOD test set (Table 3), DecoR achieves 85.51% on Math500, 76.72% on MBPP, 97.24% on MT-bench — 86.49% average at 5.0x cost. All six baselines degrade below DecoR: KNN Router falls to 81.53%, EmbedLLM to 83.72%, MODEL-SAT to 83.60%, and several perform at or below the Random Router (82.47%). One counter-intuitive finding: DecoR's MT-bench OOD score of 97.24% is identical to the DeepSeek-V3.1 single-model score, strongly suggesting the fallback fires for that entire benchmark rather than genuine routing occurring. Ablation (Table 2) shows removing Stage A causes the largest drops (e.g., GSM8k accuracy 95.54% → 91.70%, cost 1.1× → 2.1×); removing B or C causes smaller but consistent accuracy drops with negligible cost increase. Backbone scaling from Qwen3-0.6B to 4.0B yields only +0.11% ID and +0.89% OOD accuracy at marginally higher cost, supporting the lightweight 0.6B choice. The LLM pool contains 8 frontier models from 2024–2025; baselines are current and relevant. All results averaged over 3 independent trials; accuracy-based metrics use majority voting across 5 runs. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.25558v1); results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The capability-profile abstraction as the matching key for routing is genuinely novel: replacing learned parametric query→model mappings with instance-based retrieval over structured skill/knowledge/difficulty decompositions has not appeared in prior routing literature. CodaSet, combining 4 ID and 3 OOD benchmarks with a consistent evaluation protocol for routing generalization, is a new contribution. Individual components — GRPO, SFT, BGE-M3, Jaccard overlap, log-based empirical estimation — are all standard; the novelty lies in their composition and the capability-profile abstraction. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover all three sifting substages across three benchmarks, which is adequate but not exhaustive — no ablation isolates the capability-profile representation itself (e.g., S+K without D, or raw embedding without profile concatenation). Three independent trials are reported; accuracy uses majority voting over 5 runs; no confidence intervals or significance tests are provided. Code and data are available at https://github.com/lvbotenbest/DecoR. Reproducibility is partially constrained: the Query Deconstructor training data was synthesized by GPT-5 (closed, commercial), and MT-bench evaluation uses GPT-5.1 as judge — both create external dependencies. The τ, k, and λ hyperparameters were tuned on the CodaSet validation split partitioned from the same benchmarks used for testing, creating a mild Goodhart's Law concern for those parameters. No analysis of Query Deconstructor consistency (whether the same query produces stable profiles across runs) is provided, which is a methodological blind spot given that profile stability is load-bearing for Substage A. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the system is run end-to-end against 8 live LLMs via DeepInfra API with real cost measurement. - Practical: The routing backbone (two Qwen3-0.6B models + BGE-M3 + inverted index) runs on commodity hardware; the API inference and log database are operationally standard. - Efficient: Routing overhead includes two 0.6B LLM forward passes plus an embedding call before every query; wall-clock routing latency is not reported anywhere in the paper, making it impossible to assess whether the cost savings outweigh the added per-query latency for latency-sensitive applications. - Effective: On 7 benchmarks the system achieves near-top-model accuracy at 2.1–5.0x minimum cost versus baselines that degrade; evidence of effectiveness is credible, though the OOD improvement is partly the fallback mechanism rather than routing. - Scalable: No experiment varies historical log size, density, or domain coverage; the system's quality as a function of log size is entirely uncharacterized — a significant gap for production deployments starting with sparse logs. The work delivers a software artifact (code + CodaSet data at GitHub). Strengths - The capability-profile abstraction is principled and addresses the memorization trap at the right level of abstraction: matching on (Skills, Knowledge, Difficulty) rather than raw embeddings is genuinely more invariant to surface-form variation and does not require retraining when the model pool changes. - The GRPO-trained Log Evaluator explicitly reasons about why a historical query is or is not representative (producing a Thought before V), and its V=∅ output triggers a safe fallback — an important property for production systems where silent routing errors are expensive. - The OOD empirical finding is striking and important: all six current baselines degrade to at or below random selection on OOD benchmarks, a result the paper demonstrates clearly with a pool of 8 frontier 2024–2025 models and a fair evaluation protocol. - The framework supports zero-retraining model-pool updates — adding or removing models requires only log updates, which is a meaningful operational advantage over end-to-end trained routers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) historical log scoring depends on LLM-as-judge quality, limiting fully automated real-time log updates; (2) no deduplication mechanism exists for highly similar queries already in the repository, creating redundancy. These are presented directly and honestly. The authors do not acknowledge the routing latency issue, the open-vocabulary consistency problem, or the confound between fallback behavior and genuine OOD routing. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Fallback-vs-routing confound: In OOD Table 3, DecoR's MT-bench score (97.24%) is identical to the DeepSeek-V3.1 single-model score, and MBPP (76.72%) is very close to DeepSeek-V3.1's 75.66%. This pattern strongly implies the fallback fires for most or all OOD queries on these benchmarks, meaning the OOD "routing" result is largely just running the fallback model at full cost. The paper never reports fallback trigger rates per benchmark, which would be essential to separate these effects. - No log coverage analysis: No experiment examines how routing quality degrades as historical log coverage decreases. A system that always falls back (empty log) trivially matches the fallback model's performance; the added value of routing only materializes at some coverage threshold that is never characterized. - Routing latency unreported: Two 0.6B forward passes + embedding + index lookup add latency before every query. For interactive applications, this overhead may dominate savings from routing cheaper models and must be reported to evaluate real-world utility. - Open-vocabulary skill consistency unmeasured: Jaccard matching on dynamically generated skill labels ("arithmetic" vs. "arithmetic reasoning" vs. "calculation") is fragile to paraphrase. No inter-run consistency metric or taxonomy analysis is provided for the Query Deconstructor's outputs. - Training data contamination risk: The Query Deconstructor is trained on GPT-5-synthesized data derived from CodaSet's own benchmark distributions, and the Log Evaluator training uses results from Substage A/B filtering of the same benchmarks. The boundary between training signal and evaluation target is not cleanly enforced. Bottom Line Practitioners building multi-LLM deployment systems will find DecoR's capability-profile matching approach and its OOD robustness results directly useful, but the absence of routing latency measurements, log coverage analysis, and a clear accounting of when the fallback fires versus genuine routing limits the paper's actionability for production deployment decisions.

VASO: Formally Verifiable Self-Evolving Skills for Physical AI Agents

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VASO, a closed-loop framework that converts formal model-checker counterexamples into natural-language textual gradients to iteratively refine reusable LLM-generated robot skill contracts — each represented as a coupled LTL specification, proposition-aligned labeling function, and plan template — without updating model weights. - Why it's important: LLM-generated robot skills can pass every sampled execution test while still violating temporal safety specifications on untested conditions; for physical robots, even rare safety violations (e.g., a drone briefly exceeding its altitude envelope) are unacceptable, and trace-level feedback cannot close this gap. - How it does it: Each skill is compiled into a NuSMV transition system via a proposition-aligned labeling function that bridges symbolic model checking and LLM planning; when verification fails, the counterexample is rendered as a textual gradient that updates the skill contract via LLM-AutoDiff, leaving foundation model weights frozen. - The good: On real Clearpath Jackal and PX4 quadcopter hardware, VASO achieves 97.2% specification compliance across 11 LTL specs in fewer than 100 optimization samples and under 20 minutes per skill, beating the strongest learning-based baseline RLVF (94.3% safety score) at one-fourth the training time. - The missing: The proposition-aligned labeling function is itself LLM-generated and unverified, so the formal guarantee is conditional on AI-generated code being correct — a foundational assumption the title's "formally verifiable" framing substantially overstates, and the paper's mitigation evidence (< 1% degradation in one narrow setting) is insufficient. - Read it or not: Worth reading for robotics and safe-AI researchers working at the intersection of LLM planning and formal verification; the architectural insight is real and the real-robot demonstrations are credible, but the narrow evaluation scope (4 task types, proprietary models, no code release) limits immediate replication.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VASO, a closed-loop framework that converts formal model-checker counterexamples into natural-language textual gradients to iteratively refine reusable LLM-generated robot skill contracts — each represented as a coupled LTL specification, proposition-aligned labeling function, and plan template — without updating model weights. - Why it's important: LLM-generated robot skills can pass every sampled execution test while still violating temporal safety specifications on untested conditions; for physical robots, even rare safety violations (e.g., a drone briefly exceeding its altitude envelope) are unacceptable, and trace-level feedback cannot close this gap. - How it does it: Each skill is compiled into a NuSMV transition system via a proposition-aligned labeling function that bridges symbolic model checking and LLM planning; when verification fails, the counterexample is rendered as a textual gradient that updates the skill contract via LLM-AutoDiff, leaving foundation model weights frozen. - The good: On real Clearpath Jackal and PX4 quadcopter hardware, VASO achieves 97.2% specification compliance across 11 LTL specs in fewer than 100 optimization samples and under 20 minutes per skill, beating the strongest learning-based baseline RLVF (94.3% safety score) at one-fourth the training time. - The missing: The proposition-aligned labeling function is itself LLM-generated and unverified, so the formal guarantee is conditional on AI-generated code being correct — a foundational assumption the title's "formally verifiable" framing substantially overstates, and the paper's mitigation evidence (< 1% degradation in one narrow setting) is insufficient. - Read it or not: Worth reading for robotics and safe-AI researchers working at the intersection of LLM planning and formal verification; the architectural insight is real and the real-robot demonstrations are credible, but the narrow evaluation scope (4 task types, proprietary models, no code release) limits immediate replication. Problem & Motivation Physical robot agents that use LLMs for task planning face a trust gap that execution-based feedback cannot close. Systems like Voyager, Eureka, and AutoSkill refine robot skills via successful rollouts, unit tests, or LLM self-critique, but these signals are trace-level: they confirm the skill worked on observed executions, not that it will satisfy temporal safety constraints under all possible conditions. In practice, even the strongest execution-feedback baseline tested in this work leaves more than 1-in-20 generated plans violating formal specifications. Formal verification has been applied to LLM outputs before, but only as a post-hoc pass/fail filter on individual plans — counterexamples are rejection certificates, not improvement signals. The missing piece is a skill representation that allows model-checker feedback to propagate back to the reusable artifact that produced a failed plan, rather than discarding the plan and starting over. Key Idea The core insight is a dual-interface skill representation called a semantic contract: each skill simultaneously exposes a formal interface (an LTL local rule plus a proposition-aligned labeling function L_sk that maps robot states, sensor readings, and control commands to logical propositions) and a planner-facing interface (a text-based plan template). The labeling function is the bridge — it lets the same skill artifact be consumed by a NuSMV model checker (which reasons over propositions) and by an LLM planner (which reasons over natural language). This makes it possible to treat verification failures not as dead ends but as structured optimization signals targeting the reusable skill contract itself. Methodology VASO operates as a two-level verification and refinement loop. At the skill level, the framework checks logical feasibility: given a combined specification Φ_sk = (∧φ∈G φ) ∧ ψ_sk, it constructs a universal transition system M = (2^AP, 2^AP, 2^AP × 2^AP, id) and checks M ⊨ ¬Φ_sk via NuSMV — if true, no satisfying trace exists and the LLM is re-queried to fix the local rule. At the plan level, the planner generates π = F_plan(C_sk, τ); Algorithm 1 compiles this into a transition system A_π by traversing the ordered API call / waypoint sequence and labeling each state via L_sk; NuSMV then checks A_π ⊗ M ⊨ G ∧ ψ_sk. Failure returns a counterexample trace, which is rendered as a textual gradient g (e.g., "violate specification: G(person_observed → X stop)") and fed to F_skill to update the contract: C_sk^(k+1) = F_skill(C_sk^(k), g, τ). This instantiates LLM-AutoDiff's textual gradient descent applied to the skill contract rather than a flat prompt or model parameters. GPT-5-nano serves as F_skill and GPT-4o-mini as F_plan. Three critical design decisions: (1) optimizing the contract artifact rather than model weights — well-justified by the ablation showing structured rep outperforms flat prompt optimization; (2) two-level verification ordering — well-justified to avoid plan-sampling compute waste on contradictory specs; (3) automatic proposition alignment via F_skill rather than hand-engineering — justified empirically by near-parity with handcrafted mappings, though the correctness assumption is load-bearing. Key Results On 11 LTL specifications and 400 generated plans across Clearpath Jackal and PX4 quadcopter platforms: VASO with handcrafted proposition alignment achieves 97.2% safety score (SS) and 85.3% task completion (TC); VASO with automatic proposition alignment achieves 96.8% SS and 86.5% TC. Against zero-shot baselines: LLM-Planner reaches 90.3% SS / 89.3% TC; ReAct reaches 73.3% SS; LLM+P reaches 79.5% SS. Against learning-based baselines: RLVF (fine-tuning with formal feedback) reaches 94.3% SS / 82.5% TC; DSPy reaches 86.8% SS. Convergence: VASO exceeds 90% safety within 7 optimization steps; LAD-VF (flat prompt optimization with verification feedback) plateaus near 85% after 10 steps. Held-out skill reuse: 92–100% SS and 80–96% TC on unseen task prompts within the same skill family. Skill-level feasibility: 89% of initial LLM-generated skills pass feasibility; 97% after one iteration. Training time: approximately one-fourth of RLVF. Evaluation scale is narrow — 4 skill types on 2 real platforms with up to 40 plans per skill. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is the dual-interface skill representation (LTL contract coupled with a proposition-aligned labeling function and plan template as a single reusable artifact) and the closed loop architecture where model-checker counterexamples become textual gradients targeting that artifact specifically. NuSMV model checking, LTL specifications for robotics, and LLM-AutoDiff textual gradient descent are all prior work; VASO's contribution is the integration architecture that makes them mutually compatible. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study is present and isolates four design choices: structured skill representation, automatic proposition alignment, iterative optimization, and skill-mediated planning. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed runs are reported; the convergence plots represent single runs. Task completion for the baseline comparison (Table 1) is evaluated "in simulation" while real-robot safety scores are measured on hardware — the gap between simulation TC and real-robot TC is not assessed. No code, trained models, or skill libraries are released; no repository URL is provided. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.05395v1, June 2026), not peer-reviewed. GPT-5-nano and GPT-4o-mini are proprietary and unavailable for independent replication. The LTL specifications used are simple (single implication formulas, no nested temporal operators), and it is unknown how the approach scales to richer temporal properties. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: Runs on commercially available ground robots (Clearpath Jackal) and drones (PX4) with standard velocity and detection APIs, and uses NuSMV, an open-source model checker; no custom hardware required. - Efficient: Under 20 minutes per skill and fewer than 100 optimization samples — approximately one-fourth the training time of RLVF fine-tuning; NuSMV model checking is fast for the finite plan automata generated here. - Effective: 97.2% specification compliance on 11 LTL specs across two platforms is credible evidence that the approach resolves real safety violations the paper demonstrates concretely, but effectiveness is shown only for 4 narrow task types and simple sequential plans. - Scalable: Untested beyond 4 skill types and 11 simple specifications; the authors explicitly acknowledge the framework does not handle concurrent or interleaved skill executions, and proposition alignment correctness may degrade with richer APIs. The work stops at paper form with real-robot demonstrations; no software artifact (library, CLI, dataset) is released. Strengths - The dual-interface skill representation is a genuine architectural contribution: coupling LTL specs, proposition-aligned labeling, and plan templates into a single artifact that is simultaneously machine-checkable and LLM-plannable is a non-trivial design advance. - Demonstrations on real physical robots (not just simulation) under realistic conditions, including sensor noise (YOLO detection) and physical dynamics, give the results more credibility than simulation-only baselines. - The two-level verification ordering (feasibility check before plan sampling) is an efficient and principled design choice that prevents the optimizer from refining contradictory specifications. - Automatic proposition alignment achieving near-parity with handcrafted functions (96.8% vs. 97.2% SS) substantially lowers the engineering burden and makes the approach usable without formal methods expertise. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify two limitations: (1) the proposition-aligned labeling function is AI-generated and unverified, so errors in proposition mapping can invalidate formal guarantees — demonstrated with a concrete drone altitude example where 2D vs. 3D norm confusion produces a spurious pass; and (2) verification assumes sequential, non-concurrent skill execution, so interleaved or overlapping skill executions are outside scope. The authors propose probabilistic grounding validation and multi-agent extensions as future directions, and they confront the first limitation directly with a numerical bound (<1% safety score degradation from auto vs. handcrafted alignment), which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The title's "formally verifiable" framing is misleading: formal soundness is conditional on AI-generated code correctly implementing propositions, which is precisely the assumption formal verification is meant to avoid. The paper's <1% degradation claim is measured in the same narrow task setting where the alignment was optimized, not on adversarial or out-of-distribution propositions. - The evaluation covers only 4 task types, none drawn from standard LLM-planning benchmarks (VirtualHome, AI2-THOR, ALFRED, BEHAVIOR-1K), making comparison against the broader literature impossible. - Table 1 mixes real-robot SS with simulated TC for baseline comparison without explaining whether simulation TC is a reliable proxy for real-robot TC, particularly for the learning-based baselines. - The held-out reuse experiment uses prompts from "the same task family" with unseen parameterizations — not true cross-task generalization; a skill optimized for bypass-object is never tested on find-object. - No variance or confidence intervals across runs; with GPT models' stochastic outputs and small sample sizes (40 plans per skill), the reported differences between methods could be within noise bounds. - All experiments use proprietary commercial models (GPT-5-nano, GPT-4o-mini); no open-weight model evaluation limits reproducibility. - The 11 LTL specifications are uniformly simple (G(p → Xq) form); behavior under complex specifications with nested operators, fairness conditions, or conflicting global/local rules is untested. Bottom Line VASO delivers a genuine and implementable architectural insight — using the model checker's counterexample to update the skill contract rather than to discard the plan — and backs it with real-robot results, but the narrow evaluation scope, proprietary model dependency, absence of code release, and the foundational tension between the "formally verifiable" framing and the unverified AI-generated labeling functions mean practitioners should treat this as a proof-of-concept requiring significant validation before deployment.

LEAP: Supercharging LLMs for Formal Mathematics with Agentic Frameworks

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LEAP (LLM-in-Lean Environment Agentic Prover), an agentic framework that enables general-purpose LLMs, without any domain-specific fine-tuning, to achieve state-of-the-art formal theorem proving in Lean via hierarchical AND-OR DAG decomposition and iterative compiler feedback. - Why it's important: Specialized prover models (AlphaProof, DeepSeek Prover V2, Goedel-Prover-V2) require expensive fine-tuning on formal corpora; if general-purpose LLMs can match them through scaffolding alone, the barrier to building formal verification systems drops dramatically. - How it does it: LEAP decomposes each proof goal into subgoals via informal natural-language blueprints, translates blueprints into Lean proof sketches, enforces acyclicity in a shared AND-OR DAG to enable lemma reuse across branches, and uses an LLM reviewer to filter formally-valid but mathematically-vacuous decompositions before committing them to the DAG. - The good: LEAP solves all 12 Putnam 2025 problems (100%) while direct-formalization baselines solve 0%, and achieves 70% overall on the new Lean-IMO-Bench, surpassing Aristotle (a specialized IMO Gold-medal system) at 48%. - The missing: LEAP requires up to 3,000 LLM calls per problem (with rollout=2), making it far more expensive than the Pass@128 baselines it is compared against; no compute-normalized comparison is provided, and only one backbone LLM (Gemini-3.1-pro) is evaluated. - Read it or not: Researchers in formal verification, automated theorem proving, or agentic LLM systems should read this — it makes a mechanically-verified empirical case that overturns the prevailing assumption that specialized fine-tuning is indispensable for competitive formal proving.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LEAP (LLM-in-Lean Environment Agentic Prover), an agentic framework that enables general-purpose LLMs, without any domain-specific fine-tuning, to achieve state-of-the-art formal theorem proving in Lean via hierarchical AND-OR DAG decomposition and iterative compiler feedback. - Why it's important: Specialized prover models (AlphaProof, DeepSeek Prover V2, Goedel-Prover-V2) require expensive fine-tuning on formal corpora; if general-purpose LLMs can match them through scaffolding alone, the barrier to building formal verification systems drops dramatically. - How it does it: LEAP decomposes each proof goal into subgoals via informal natural-language blueprints, translates blueprints into Lean proof sketches, enforces acyclicity in a shared AND-OR DAG to enable lemma reuse across branches, and uses an LLM reviewer to filter formally-valid but mathematically-vacuous decompositions before committing them to the DAG. - The good: LEAP solves all 12 Putnam 2025 problems (100%) while direct-formalization baselines solve 0%, and achieves 70% overall on the new Lean-IMO-Bench, surpassing Aristotle (a specialized IMO Gold-medal system) at 48%. - The missing: LEAP requires up to 3,000 LLM calls per problem (with rollout=2), making it far more expensive than the Pass@128 baselines it is compared against; no compute-normalized comparison is provided, and only one backbone LLM (Gemini-3.1-pro) is evaluated. - Read it or not: Researchers in formal verification, automated theorem proving, or agentic LLM systems should read this — it makes a mechanically-verified empirical case that overturns the prevailing assumption that specialized fine-tuning is indispensable for competitive formal proving. Problem & Motivation Despite LLMs' impressive informal mathematical reasoning, they fail to generate machine-verifiable proofs in formal languages like Lean. Human verification of complex proofs is prohibitively expensive (the Kepler conjecture required four years of peer review and a decade of formal effort), motivating automated formal proving. Prior ATP systems predominantly fine-tune specialized models on large formal corpora, which is resource-intensive and produces brittle specialists. Agentic approaches like Hilbert combine general LLMs for informal reasoning with specialized models for Lean steps, but still depend on fine-tuned backends. Crucially, existing benchmarks (MiniF2F, PutnamBench) are increasingly saturated and fail to stress-test multi-step, non-routine reasoning. The gap between general LLMs' strong informal ability and near-zero one-shot formal proving performance is the central problem. Key Idea The core insight is that the one-shot Lean generation bottleneck for general LLMs reflects the difficulty of producing long, syntactically valid, type-correct proofs in a single attempt — not a deficit in mathematical understanding. An agentic loop that decomposes goals into an AND-OR DAG, bridges informal reasoning to Lean via natural-language blueprints, reuses proven lemmas across branches (hierarchical memoization), and filters useless decompositions via an LLM reviewer unlocks general LLMs for formal proving without any fine-tuning. Methodology LEAP operates as a DFS-with-backtracking agent over an AND-OR DAG. Each open goal is first attempted via direct formalization: generate an informal proof, translate it to Lean, submit to the compiler, and iterate up to 20 revision steps using compiler error messages and LeanSearch retrieval. If direct formalization fails, LEAP enters blueprint decomposition: generate an informal blueprint proposing supporting lemmas, translate it to a Lean proof sketch that is sorry-free in the main body but allows sorry placeholders only for newly proposed lemmas, submit to Lean, and have an LLM reviewer assess whether the proposed subgoals meaningfully simplify the parent goal before committing as an AND node. Three design decisions are critical: (1) requiring the main theorem body to be sorry-free preserves AND-OR soundness — once all children are proved, the parent is proved; (2) an acyclicity check before committing sketches prevents circular dependencies; (3) anticipatory lemma planning allows the DAG to accumulate auxiliary lemmas that may prove useful downstream even if not immediately needed. The backbone is Gemini-3.1-pro throughout. No training or fine-tuning is performed. Key Results On Putnam 2025 (12 problems): LEAP solves 12/12 (100%) with rollout=2. Direct formalization baselines Gemini-3.1-pro and Goedel-Prover-V2-32B each solve 0/12 at Pass@128. Hilbert solves 4/12 (33.3%) but requires up to 7-day time limits per rollout due to O((n·b)^d) search complexity. Aristotle (closed-source) solves 9/12 (75%) at rollout=2. Computational cost for LEAP ranges from 46 LLM calls (Problem B2) to 3,000 (Problem A5), with proof lengths from 300 to 2,000 lines. On Lean-IMO-Bench (60 problems, Basic and Advanced sets): LEAP achieves 83.3%/56.7% (Basic/Advanced) vs. Gemini-3.1-pro 20%/3.3%, Goedel-V2-32B 10%/0%, Hilbert 36.6%/6.6%, Aristotle 76.7%/20%. LEAP maintains 100% on Algebra and Number Theory regardless of difficulty tier but achieves only 16.7% and 12.5% on Geometry. Ablation: removing DAG memoization (naive tree) drops performance from 83.3%/56.7% to 73.3%/40.0%. Removing the LLM reviewer causes failure on Putnam A5 even after 8 rollouts, due to the agent repeatedly producing formally-valid but cyclically-restating decompositions. Iterative formalization raises Gemini-3.1-pro's Basic solve rate from 20.0% (Pass@128 one-shot) to 36.6% (Pass@1 iterative with 20 revision steps); Goedel-Prover-V2-32B does not benefit from iteration (10.0% → 6.6%), indicating it lacks the compiler-error interpretation capability needed for feedback loops. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The AND-OR DAG with hierarchical memoization for agentic formal theorem proving is novel as an architectural design, distinguishing LEAP from prior recursive decomposition systems (Hilbert) that suffer exponential blowup. The informal-to-formal bridge via iteratively-refined blueprints extends the "draft, sketch, and prove" paradigm (Jiang et al., 2023) with recursive decomposition and shared proof state. Lean-IMO-Bench fills a genuine gap between saturated undergraduate benchmarks and research-level problems. The finding that general LLMs without fine-tuning can match specialized provers is an empirical finding that challenges a prevailing assumption in the field. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: DAG vs. tree, with/without LLM reviewer, one-shot vs. iterative formalization. However, the LLM reviewer ablation is conducted on a single problem (Putnam A5), not the full benchmark; its contribution across all problems is not quantified. No error bars or significance tests are reported; all results are single-run at rollout=2. Lean proofs are publicly available at https://github.com/google-deepmind/superhuman/tree/main/leap, and Lean-IMO-Bench at https://imobench.github.io — these are genuine reproducibility positives since the Lean compiler provides ground-truth verification. Key concern: the evaluation is asymmetric in compute — LEAP uses rollout=2 with up to 3,000 LLM calls per rollout, while direct baselines use Pass@128 (128 parallel single-pass attempts). A single LEAP rollout on Problem A5 consumes vastly more inference compute than 128 one-shot attempts; no FLOPs- or wall-clock-normalized comparison is provided. This is an arXiv preprint (v2, June 2026); peer-reviewed confidence level is not established. The principal competitor Aristotle is closed-source, making it scientifically unverifiable (the authors acknowledge this). Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Requires only a Lean 4 installation and API access to a frontier LLM; no GPU training infrastructure needed, making deployment accessible relative to fine-tuned prover systems. - Efficient: Costs are high in inference compute — up to 3,000 LLM calls per problem (roughly hours of wall time and substantial API cost), with the hardest problems requiring two full rollouts; geometry problems remain intractable even at this budget. - Effective: All claimed results are mechanically verified by the Lean compiler, providing the strongest possible correctness guarantee — evidence quality is higher than self-reported informal math results. - Scalable: Proof search space grows rapidly with decomposition depth; the paper identifies this as the primary open challenge and provides no experiments at problem scales harder than Putnam/IMO-Advanced, nor any study of how cost scales with problem difficulty beyond the reported per-problem LLM call counts. The work delivers verified Lean proof artifacts and a new benchmark dataset; no software library or packaged CLI is described. Strengths - The AND-OR DAG with lemma memoization is a principled solution to the exponential blowup in naive recursive decomposition, directly validated by comparing against Hilbert (which hits O((n·b)^d) complexity and requires 7-day limits per rollout). - All reported successes are machine-verified by the Lean compiler — a correctness guarantee absent in informal math reasoning benchmarks, making the empirical claims unusually reliable. - The empirical finding that a general LLM without fine-tuning matches or exceeds specialized provers on Putnam 2025 and Lean-IMO-Bench is non-obvious and significant; it is supported by multiple ablations showing the contribution of each design component. - Lean-IMO-Bench is a technically well-motivated benchmark contribution: expert-formalized statements, balanced across four mathematical domains, with a difficulty gradient specifically designed to test long multi-step reasoning where existing benchmarks are saturating. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge that geometry performance is near zero across all methods due to Lean's lack of domain-specific geometric frameworks, and retain it in the benchmark only to evaluate general-purpose reasoning under formalization constraints. They acknowledge the scalability challenge: decomposition grows the search space rapidly, and branch prioritization remains an open problem. The hybrid architecture (general LLM for high-level reasoning combined with a specialized model for local formal steps) is identified as a promising direction explicitly out of scope for this paper. The closed-source status of Aristotle is noted as a limitation on direct comparison. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Compute asymmetry is not addressed: LEAP at rollout=2 with thousands of LLM calls per problem is compared to baselines at Pass@128. A budget-matched comparison (e.g., 128 independent LEAP rollouts vs. Gemini-3.1-pro at Pass@128, or total-tokens-matched) is absent and would substantially change the narrative. - Only one backbone LLM (Gemini-3.1-pro) is evaluated. The claim that this approach works for "general LLMs" is unverified for GPT-4o, Claude, Llama, or smaller open-source models; it is plausible that the approach is highly sensitive to model quality. - The LLM reviewer ablation covers one problem only; the reviewer's contribution to the full Lean-IMO-Bench performance is unknown, making it unclear whether this component is essential or marginal across the broader benchmark. - No analysis of failure mode distribution: it is unknown what fraction of unsolved problems fail due to budget exhaustion versus fundamental inability to decompose the proof. This matters for assessing where more compute or better search would help. - Geometry (16.7%/12.5%) is included in aggregate scores that flatter LEAP relative to geometry-free subsets; since all systems fail geometry, aggregate score differences primarily reflect non-geometry domains. Bottom Line Practitioners in formal verification and AI for mathematics should read this paper — it provides mechanically-verified evidence that agentic scaffolding with general-purpose LLMs can match specialized fine-tuned provers on competition-level benchmarks, a result that challenges a widely-held assumption, though the compute cost and single-model evaluation scope leave important questions open.

End-to-End Context Compression at Scale

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Latent Context Language Models (LCLMs), a family of encoder-decoder context compressors that map long input token sequences to shorter sequences of continuous latent embeddings, trained end-to-end on over 350B tokens at compression ratios of 4×, 8×, and 16× using a 0.6B encoder and 4B decoder. - Why it's important: Long-context LLM inference is bottlenecked by KV cache memory growth, and existing KV cache compression methods either degrade quality substantially at high compression ratios, require full-context prefill before compression, or are incompatible with standard inference engines like vLLM and SGLang. - How it does it: The encoder maps fixed-size windows of N input tokens to a single latent vector via mean pooling over causal hidden states, projects them through an MLP adapter, and feeds the compressed sequence to a frozen-then-fine-tuned decoder; a four-stage training curriculum (adapter warmup → encoder training → end-to-end continual pre-training → SFT) with interleaved compressed/uncompressed spans and auxiliary reconstruction data prevents catastrophic forgetting. - The good: At 16× compression, LCLM achieves 81.05% on GSM8K — a short, information-dense benchmark where all KV cache compression methods collapse to near 0% — while being 8.8× faster to first token than the best KV baseline on RULER@4k. - The missing: At 4× and 8× compression ratios, KVzip matches or outperforms LCLM on RULER and LongBench while LCLMs are faster; the paper does not evaluate on multi-turn or cross-turn cache reuse scenarios where query-agnostic compression is the claimed advantage. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on long-context LLM inference, agentic systems, or efficient serving — it is a credible systems paper with open models, rigorous architecture search, and genuine wall-clock efficiency gains; skip it if you only care about maximum accuracy at low (4×) compression ratios.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Latent Context Language Models (LCLMs), a family of encoder-decoder context compressors that map long input token sequences to shorter sequences of continuous latent embeddings, trained end-to-end on over 350B tokens at compression ratios of 4×, 8×, and 16× using a 0.6B encoder and 4B decoder. - Why it's important: Long-context LLM inference is bottlenecked by KV cache memory growth, and existing KV cache compression methods either degrade quality substantially at high compression ratios, require full-context prefill before compression, or are incompatible with standard inference engines like vLLM and SGLang. - How it does it: The encoder maps fixed-size windows of N input tokens to a single latent vector via mean pooling over causal hidden states, projects them through an MLP adapter, and feeds the compressed sequence to a frozen-then-fine-tuned decoder; a four-stage training curriculum (adapter warmup → encoder training → end-to-end continual pre-training → SFT) with interleaved compressed/uncompressed spans and auxiliary reconstruction data prevents catastrophic forgetting. - The good: At 16× compression, LCLM achieves 81.05% on GSM8K — a short, information-dense benchmark where all KV cache compression methods collapse to near 0% — while being 8.8× faster to first token than the best KV baseline on RULER@4k. - The missing: At 4× and 8× compression ratios, KVzip matches or outperforms LCLM on RULER and LongBench while LCLMs are faster; the paper does not evaluate on multi-turn or cross-turn cache reuse scenarios where query-agnostic compression is the claimed advantage. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on long-context LLM inference, agentic systems, or efficient serving — it is a credible systems paper with open models, rigorous architecture search, and genuine wall-clock efficiency gains; skip it if you only care about maximum accuracy at low (4×) compression ratios. Problem & Motivation Long-context LLM inference in production systems faces a fundamental memory and latency bottleneck: the KV cache grows linearly with sequence length, making million-token contexts prohibitively expensive. KV cache compression methods — which evict or compress cached attention keys and values — suffer from several practical limitations: many require the full prompt to be prefilled before eviction, query-dependent variants produce caches that cannot be reused across turns, and methods that apply non-uniform eviction across attention heads and layers are incompatible with paged-attention engines that assume a shared sequence length. Prior soft-token encoder-decoder compressors exist but are trained on domain-specific data at small scale, are not evaluated on information-dense long-context benchmarks like RULER, and do not convincingly preserve the base LLM's general capabilities. Key Idea The core insight is that encoder-decoder soft-token compression can match or exceed KV cache compression on the accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier if trained at sufficient scale (350B tokens) with a carefully staged curriculum that prevents catastrophic forgetting of the decoder's base capabilities. Crucially, the encoder window size (W=1024 tokens) is set large enough that local context is richly shared before pooling, and training uses interleaved compressed and uncompressed spans throughout the sequence — rather than the standard first-half-compressed split — so the decoder learns to condition on latent context at arbitrary positions. Methodology The LCLM architecture consists of three components: (1) an encoder (Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B) that processes non-overlapping windows of W=1024 tokens with causal attention and pools each block of N tokens to a single latent vector via mean pooling; (2) an MLP adapter (two-layer FFN with RMSNorm and GELU) that projects encoder hidden states to the decoder's embedding dimension; and (3) a decoder (Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507) that consumes the latent sequence in place of the original tokens. Training proceeds in four stages with progressively unfrozen components: Stage 0 trains only the adapter with both encoder and decoder frozen; Stage 1 unfreezes the encoder; Stage 2 unfreezes the decoder at a small learning rate (1e-5) for end-to-end continual pre-training; Stage 3 applies SFT on a curated mixture. The pre-training data mixes Common Crawl text, code, reasoning, and long-context documents, with each sequence partitioned into alternating compressed/uncompressed spans delimited by `<|memory_start|>` and `<|memory_end|>` tokens; loss is computed only on uncompressed tokens. An auxiliary reconstruction objective — asking the decoder to reproduce the original text from the compressed latents — is included in both pre-training and SFT to preserve fine-grained retrieval fidelity. Total data: 283B pre-training tokens (encoder side), 350B+ combined. The architecture is selected via a controlled from-scratch pre-training sweep (38B tokens, Qwen3-0.6B for both encoder and decoder) across pooling operator (mean, concat, token-based), encoder window size (W ∈ {16, 256, 1024}), attention mask (causal vs. bidirectional), and adapter design (MLP vs. attention-based MLP). Mean pooling and causal masking are confirmed superior at scale. Critical design decision: causal masking in the encoder outperforms bidirectional despite the encoder only processing prompts — the paper attributes this to alignment between training and the pre-trained Qwen3-Embedding model's original causal structure, but the mechanism is not fully analyzed. Key Results All evaluations use Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 as the base decoder on a single H200 GPU. At 16× compression, LCLM achieves: RULER@4k 75.06%, RULER@8k 70.23%, RULER@16k 65.91%, LongBench EN-16 macro 39.08%, GSM8K 81.05%. The uncompressed baseline scores 94.41% / 93.58% / 93.74% / 45.21% / 93.25% respectively, so degradation at 16× is substantial for RULER but acceptable on dense tasks. At 4× compression, LCLM scores 91.76% / 91.03% / 89.96% on RULER, approaching the uncompressed baseline. KVzip at 4× achieves 94.43% / 93.28% / 93.93% on RULER — better than LCLM — but near 0% on GSM8K because it cannot handle short, uniformly important prompts. LCLM is 8.8× faster to first token than the fastest KV baseline on RULER@4k and 5.2× faster on LongBench@64k. Peak memory at 512k–1M tokens: LCLM is the only method that does not OOM at 1M tokens. The agentic EXPAND harness raises RULER NIAH average from 72.51% to 92.46% at 8k context and from 70.18% to 89.91% at 16k context at 16× compression, recovering most of the gap to uncompressed. Models and code are open-sourced (huggingface.co/latent-context, github.com/LeonLixyz/LCLM). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty lies in training an encoder-decoder soft-token compressor at scale (350B tokens, full parameter updates to both encoder and decoder) with a carefully designed multi-stage recipe and interleaved data format that preserves general LLM capabilities — this closes a gap prior small-scale or domain-specific soft-token methods could not close. The architecture (encoder → pooling → adapter → decoder) and the individual components (mean pooling, causal masking, MLP adapter) are each drawn from prior work; the contribution is their systematic combination at scale with the multi-stage training recipe and the agentic EXPAND application. Rigor & Reproducibility The architecture search is a genuine strength: it is conducted in a cleanroom setting with randomly initialized weights, isolating the effect of each design choice, and the small-scale findings are validated at full scale (Figures 8–10 confirm the predictive validity). The staged training ablation is implicit (the paper notes direct end-to-end training underperforms without providing numbers), though the decision to use full-parameter training over LoRA is empirically motivated. Error bars and multiple seeds are not reported; all efficiency measurements are mean over 30 runs with 20 warmup steps, which is adequate for TTFT but no significance tests accompany accuracy comparisons. Code and models are open-sourced. One potential concern: the SFT data includes synthetic long-context tasks (repo summarization, document summarization, repo code generation) generated by Qwen3 models that are related to the evaluation setting, which could inflate LongBench and code task numbers — the paper does not ablate the impact of this synthetic data on downstream benchmarks. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (a trained model family evaluated by benchmark performance and wall-clock measurements). - Practical: The 0.6B encoder + 4B decoder runs on a single H200 (141GB); peak memory plateaus near 30–50 GB at 128k–512k context tokens, making it feasible on high-end but commercially available hardware. - Efficient: At 16× compression, TTFT is 8.8× faster than the best KV cache baseline at 4k context and 5.2× faster at 64k; the encoder batch processes 131k tokens per forward pass, yielding favorable GPU utilization; the decoder KV cache is in standard format, compatible with vLLM and SGLang for further acceleration. - Effective: At 16× compression, the method solves the original GSM8K failure mode of KV cache methods and achieves strong long-context QA accuracy; at 4× and 8× compression, KVzip is competitive or superior on RULER/LongBench, so the method is most decisively effective at the highest compression ratios where alternatives collapse. - Scalable: The paper demonstrates operation up to 1M input tokens without OOM, where all KV baselines fail; compression time and memory scaling curves are reported up to 1M tokens. Accuracy at contexts beyond 16k is not evaluated, so quality scaling to million-token contexts is a claim, not a measurement. The scaling experiments (larger encoder or decoder) yield mixed and somewhat confounding results, suggesting the training recipe is not yet optimized for larger model families. The work delivers open-source models, inference code, and a training recipe — a fully reusable software artifact. Strengths - The architecture search is cleanly executed: random initialization isolates design choices from pre-trained model biases, and small-scale findings are validated at full scale, lending unusual credibility to the design choices. - LCLM is the first soft-token compressor to be evaluated on RULER — a synthetic benchmark specifically designed to stress fine-grained long-context retrieval — and achieves competitive results, closing a gap that prior encoder-decoder methods left open. - The systems-realizable efficiency advantage is genuine: unlike many KV cache methods that mask entries without reducing actual memory or requiring specialized kernels, LCLM reduces the sequence length before decoder prefill, producing measurable wall-clock TTFT and memory reductions on standard hardware with standard inference engines. - The agentic EXPAND mechanism is a creative and practically well-motivated use of the LCLM's compression structure, enabling coarse-to-fine retrieval without requiring architectural modifications to the decoder. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that scaling results are mixed: the 8B decoder achieves lower pre-training loss but worse downstream performance, attributed to a mismatch between the training mixture and the 8B model's hybrid-thinking architecture. They also note that the 4B encoder does not uniformly outperform the 0.6B encoder despite lower loss, suggesting the training recipe is tuned around the 0.6B-4B configuration. Future directions — adaptive compression by information density, extending compression to generated state and CoT — are discussed as limitations of the current static approach. These limitations are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - At 4× and 8× compression, KVzip and AM-Fast/Slow are competitive with or superior to LCLM on RULER@4k–8k (KVzip: 90.27% vs. LCLM: 85.42% at 8×); the Pareto frontier claim holds primarily on the time dimension and at the highest (16×) compression ratios.
