The accuracy-efficiency trade-off story should be qualified by compression ratio. - The evaluation does not include multi-turn or cross-turn cache reuse scenarios, which are the primary practical motivation for query-agnostic compression. Without such evaluation, the claim that LCLMs solve the multi-turn limitation of query-dependent methods is untested. - The SFT data includes synthetically generated long-context tasks (repository summarization, document summarization, code generation) produced by Qwen3 models; these overlap thematically with the LongBench subtasks. The potential for data leakage or distribution inflation is not ablated. - The agentic EXPAND results are evaluated only on RULER NIAH (needle-in-haystack retrieval), the easiest possible testbed for this paradigm; how the agent performs on tasks requiring synthesis across multiple retrieved segments is not tested. - GSM8K involves prompts of a few hundred tokens — well within the 16× compression block. The strong performance here is interesting but does not constitute evidence of general short-context compression quality; a broader dense-prompt benchmark (e.g., MMLU in few-shot mode) would better validate this claim. Bottom Line For practitioners building long-context inference pipelines where high compression ratios (8×–16×) and compatibility with standard inference engines matter, LCLM is a meaningfully better choice than existing KV cache compression methods and represents a credible open-source artifact; at lower compression ratios (4×), KVzip is still competitive in accuracy and should be compared before adopting the higher complexity of an encoder-decoder system.

Assessing Automated Prompt Injection Attacks in Agentic Environments

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a systematic empirical evaluation of automated prompt injection attacks against tool-calling LLM agents, adapting GCG (gradient-based white-box) and TAP (tree-search black-box) from jailbreaking to indirect prompt injection in the AgentDojo framework across 80 task pairs and three target models. - Why it's important: LLM agents that autonomously retrieve untrusted external data are exposed to indirect prompt injection, yet no prior work had systematically compared automated attack optimization paradigms in realistic stateful, multi-step agentic environments where success requires executing specific tool calls with correct arguments. - How it does it: GCG optimizes 30 adversarial tokens (prefix+suffix) in tool outputs toward affirmative natural-language confirmation targets; TAP runs a breadth-first tree search using GPT-5 as attacker and GPT-5-mini as judge, with structured per-iteration feedback; both are evaluated against AgentDojo's deterministic environment-state check functions as ground truth. - The good: A random-signal ablation shows that gradient guidance in GCG provides no measurable benefit over random token search on Qwen3-4B (~33% vs. ~25% ASR), and attacker model capability produces a 4.2–11.8× ASR improvement for universal TAP — findings with broad implications for attack methodology assumptions. - The missing: All undefended agent baselines are tested; given that recent defenses (CausalArmor, AgentSentry) report near-zero ASR on AgentDojo, the paper does not establish whether any of these attacks are practically relevant against deployed defended systems. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on LLM agent security or adversarial robustness of instruction-following systems, particularly for the coercive/exploitative attack taxonomy and the counterintuitive findings about gradient utility and universal optimization.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a systematic empirical evaluation of automated prompt injection attacks against tool-calling LLM agents, adapting GCG (gradient-based white-box) and TAP (tree-search black-box) from jailbreaking to indirect prompt injection in the AgentDojo framework across 80 task pairs and three target models. - Why it's important: LLM agents that autonomously retrieve untrusted external data are exposed to indirect prompt injection, yet no prior work had systematically compared automated attack optimization paradigms in realistic stateful, multi-step agentic environments where success requires executing specific tool calls with correct arguments. - How it does it: GCG optimizes 30 adversarial tokens (prefix+suffix) in tool outputs toward affirmative natural-language confirmation targets; TAP runs a breadth-first tree search using GPT-5 as attacker and GPT-5-mini as judge, with structured per-iteration feedback; both are evaluated against AgentDojo's deterministic environment-state check functions as ground truth. - The good: A random-signal ablation shows that gradient guidance in GCG provides no measurable benefit over random token search on Qwen3-4B (~33% vs. ~25% ASR), and attacker model capability produces a 4.2–11.8× ASR improvement for universal TAP — findings with broad implications for attack methodology assumptions. - The missing: All undefended agent baselines are tested; given that recent defenses (CausalArmor, AgentSentry) report near-zero ASR on AgentDojo, the paper does not establish whether any of these attacks are practically relevant against deployed defended systems. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on LLM agent security or adversarial robustness of instruction-following systems, particularly for the coercive/exploitative attack taxonomy and the counterintuitive findings about gradient utility and universal optimization. Problem & Motivation LLM agents process trusted system/user instructions and untrusted tool outputs (emails, file contents, web pages) through a single shared attention context delimited only by special tokens — not architectural separation. An adversary with write access to any external data source the agent retrieves can embed malicious instructions that the model follows as if they were legitimate commands, causing goal hijacking, data exfiltration, or unauthorized financial transactions. While automated adversarial methods (GCG, PAIR, TAP) have achieved high success rates in static single-turn jailbreaking, their effectiveness in the qualitatively harder agentic setting — where the attacker must coerce specific multi-step tool invocations with correct arguments against a stateful environment — was not characterized. Prior prompt injection work relied on manual attacks or evaluated only text-generation settings (e.g., summarization), not tool-executing agents. Key Idea The core technical insight is that prompt injection in agentic settings is a semantic exploitation problem, not a token-optimization problem. The necessary context for a functional exploit (tool schemas, task descriptions, calling conventions) already exists in the agent's input; an effective injection needs only to exploit the model's instruction-following bias via authority framing or contextual prerequisite framing — not to encode system-specific details into adversarial tokens. This explains why black-box TAP (semantic search) consistently outperforms white-box GCG (token-level gradient descent) despite lacking model access, and why the gradient itself provides little benefit over random search in GCG. Methodology The paper formalizes single-task injection as argmax over adversarial token sequence xadv to maximize P(y* | C(xfixed, xadv)), and task-universal injection as the sum of this objective across a corpus of k task pairs. For GCG: prefix+suffix structure with 15 tokens each (30 total), 800 optimization steps, batch size 256, targeting affirmative natural-language confirmations (not JSON) to avoid tokenization fragility — a decode-reencode filter rejects candidates with unstable round-trip tokenization. For TAP: branching factor 3, width 8, depth 5, 3 root nodes; the attacker model (GPT-5) receives structured feedback (score, full agent response, triggered tool calls) and maintains per-branch conversation history; on-topic pruning is omitted as ineffective in the injection setting; refusal mitigation uses defensive-security framing and evaluator penalties for refusals. The framework is extended with a custom TransformersLLM class providing gradient access and multi-GPU parallelism. Evaluation: n=4 independent optimization runs × m=6 evaluation runs, reporting ASR, S@N (N=4), and utility. Success is verified via AgentDojo's deterministic check functions (not LLM-as-judge). Critical design decisions: affirmative target formulation (well-justified: 13% vs. 5% ASR over JSON target); prefix+suffix over single-sided (well-justified: 14% vs. 8% ASR over prefix-only); omission of on-topic pruning (stated but not rigorously quantified). Key Results On Qwen3-4B (undefended): Single-Task TAP 44.6% ASR, Universal TAP 45.2%, Single-Task GCG 23.0%, Universal GCG 24.1%, Direct Instruction ~15–25% (domain-dependent), Random Prefix-Suffix comparable to GCG. Slack suite is most vulnerable (~67% ASR, Single-Task TAP); Workspace most robust. GPT-5 substantially more robust: best attack (Universal TAP) achieves 4.7% ASR but 30.0% S@N — meaning at least one of four attempts succeeds on 30% of task pairs. GCG transfer from Qwen3-4B within family: Qwen3-32B 24.7–36.0% ASR; cross-family to GPT-5, GPT-5-mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5: all below 2%; Gemini 2.5 Flash partial exception at 7.7% universal GCG. Attacker model capability for universal TAP: GPT-5 vs. GPT-5-mini yields 4.2× improvement on Qwen3-4B and 11.8× on GPT-5. Random-signal GCG achieves ~33% ASR vs. ~25% for gradient-guided GCG on Qwen3-4B. LLM judge (GPT-5-mini): 100% recall but 52.3% precision on Qwen3-4B; 85.9% accuracy on GPT-5 (dominated by true negatives). Experiments run on individual NVIDIA H200 GPUs (144GB); single-task GCG ~1.5h/run, universal GCG ~21.9h/run, single-task TAP ~0.6h/run. Baselines are current (Qwen3, Gemma3, GPT-5 at experiment time); open-weights baselines are 4B-parameter instruction-tuned models with function calling, which is small relative to frontier deployment. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The empirical findings — that black-box semantic search outperforms white-box gradient optimization, that gradient guidance in GCG provides no benefit over random search for agent injection, that universal attacks match single-task performance, and the coercive/exploitative attack strategy taxonomy — are genuinely novel. The AgentDojo extension (TransformersLLM class, gradient support, universal optimization infrastructure) is a tool contribution. The adaptation of GCG and TAP to prompt injection is methodologically incremental (prior work by Pandya et al. and Dreadnode existed for each separately); the contribution is the systematic comparative evaluation in a realistic agentic framework. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover: injection structure (prefix+suffix vs. single-sided), optimization signal (gradient vs. random), target formulation (affirmative vs. JSON), attacker model capability (GPT-5 vs. GPT-5-mini), and evaluator accuracy. Error bars (95% bootstrap CIs) are reported on key figures. n=4 seeds × m=6 evaluation runs provide reasonable but not extensive coverage of stochasticity. Code release is stated in the ethical considerations section. AgentDojo is publicly available. Methodological concerns: (1) The 80-task-pair evaluation set is manually selected from AgentDojo's 900+ pairs — selection criteria are not fully specified, and representativeness is asserted rather than demonstrated. (2) GPT-5 is used as both attacker model and frontier target, creating a potential self-knowledge advantage that the paper does not discuss. (3) LLM judge precision of 52.3% on Qwen3-4B means TAP's optimization signal is badly miscalibrated, likely suppressing measured ASR below what a better judge would achieve. (4) Gemma3-4B is excluded from TAP evaluation due to infrastructure limitations, creating an asymmetric comparison across methods. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: GCG requires a single H200 GPU (144GB) for white-box optimization; TAP requires only API access to an attacker LLM — both are commercially available. - Efficient: TAP at ~0.6h per single-task run is substantially cheaper than GCG at ~1.5h; universal GCG at ~21.9h per run makes large-scale automated red-teaming impractical without significant compute. - Effective: TAP achieves 44.6% ASR on undefended Qwen3-4B but only 4.7% ASR (30% S@N) on GPT-5 with medium reasoning; effectiveness is strongly model-dependent and drops sharply against frontier targets. - Scalable: Tested only on 4B-parameter open-weights models for white-box evaluation; cross-family GCG transfer below 2% ASR against frontier models indicates the attack paradigm does not scale across model families — a fundamental barrier for model-agnostic exploitation. The paper delivers an extended AgentDojo codebase and planned public release, but does not include trained models, datasets, or a reusable attack library beyond the framework extension. Strengths - The random-signal ablation (GCG with random token proposals matching gradient-guided GCG) is the most intellectually important result: it challenges the field's assumption that gradient access is the key advantage of white-box attacks, and generalizes the RAILS finding to the agentic injection setting. - The attacker model capability analysis for universal TAP (4.2–11.8× ASR improvement from GPT-5-mini to GPT-5) identifies a critical evaluation variable that prior work conflated with the method itself, improving reproducibility standards for future black-box attack assessments. - The coercive/exploitative attack strategy taxonomy (Section 6.4) provides a mechanistic, qualitative explanation for the GPT-5 robustness gap: authority-override injections fail because GPT-5 ignores them, while domain-native document framing and contextual prerequisite framing exploit the same instruction-following capability that makes the agent useful — a finding with direct implications for detection-based defenses. - Deterministic success verification via AgentDojo's environment-state check functions eliminates LLM-as-judge noise from ground truth, enabling principled comparison across methods. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) 80-task-pair subset may not capture full AgentDojo diversity; (2) LLM judge unreliability introduces optimization bias toward false positives on Qwen3-4B; (3) single source model (Qwen3-4B) for GCG transfer understates potential cross-family rates achievable with multi-source optimization. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Missing from their self-critique: the GPT-5 attacker/target circularity and the absence of defended agent baselines. Analyst-Identified Gaps - GPT-5 serves as both the primary attacker model in TAP and the frontier target for robustness evaluation; any implicit self-knowledge GPT-5 has about its own refusal patterns could inflate or deflate measured ASR in ways the paper does not acknowledge or bound. - No defended agent baselines are evaluated: the paper cites CausalArmor and AgentSentry as achieving near-zero ASR on AgentDojo with high utility preservation, but does not test whether the optimized injections bypass these defenses — leaving the practical threat level entirely unquantified for production deployments. - Gemma3-4B's lower vulnerability is attributed to a capability-robustness tradeoff (lower instruction-following ability → fewer successful injections), but this is not ablated; the paper does not test whether Gemma3 with the same baseline utility as Qwen3-4B would show similar attack susceptibility. - Utility under attack is tracked but not broken down by domain, attack method, or task type, which would reveal whether successful injections are detectable via utility degradation — a key practical concern for deployment monitoring. - The paper evaluates undefended agents in 2026, when a body of published defenses exists; the framing as establishing attack effectiveness without engaging with defenses limits the actionability of the findings for practitioners. Bottom Line Practitioners building or securing LLM agents should read this for the TAP > GCG finding and the transferability analysis, but should treat the reported ASR numbers as upper bounds on threat in defended settings rather than evidence that current production deployments are compromised at these rates.

AI-Enhanced Business Process Simulation for Decision Making Support

iris.uniroma1.it · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis introduces a family of AI-enhanced hybrid business process simulation (BPS) frameworks—RIMS, WISDOM, DRLHSM, and a proactive scheduling method—that integrate deep learning, generative AI, reinforcement learning, and constraint programming into discrete event simulators to improve simulation fidelity, what-if scenario generation, and process optimization. - Why it's important: Traditional BPS models are systematically oversimplified, treating human resources as machines and ignoring trace history in routing decisions, which causes decision-support analyses to mislead practitioners operating real processes with thousands of interacting instances. - How it does it: The core trick is runtime integration: instead of fitting statistical distributions to log data and simulating with them, RIMS calls trained LSTM and Decision Tree models during simulation at each event step, so intercase features (queue length, resource occupation, work-in-progress) are accurate at prediction time rather than stale. - The good: RIMS and its variants form the Pareto-optimal frontier across control-flow, time, congestion, and resource metrics on nine event logs spanning both real and synthetic settings, outperforming or tying with Simod, DeepGenerator, and Dsim in 22 of 24 DRLHSM evaluation scenarios with resource constraints, and achieving 5–14% Makespan reduction in a real hospital scheduling deployment. - The missing: Nearly all methods are evaluated using the same Petri-net model that was discovered from the same log being evaluated against, which limits confidence that improvements hold under independently discovered models; and the RL policy evaluation (Chapter 8) is restricted to a single loan application process type. - Read it or not: Researchers working on hybrid simulation, prescriptive process monitoring, or process-aware RL will find this a thorough, well-structured reference covering the full stack from simulator design to optimization; practitioners looking for a plug-and-play tool should note that RIMS targets expert users requiring Python integration.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis introduces a family of AI-enhanced hybrid business process simulation (BPS) frameworks—RIMS, WISDOM, DRLHSM, and a proactive scheduling method—that integrate deep learning, generative AI, reinforcement learning, and constraint programming into discrete event simulators to improve simulation fidelity, what-if scenario generation, and process optimization. - Why it's important: Traditional BPS models are systematically oversimplified, treating human resources as machines and ignoring trace history in routing decisions, which causes decision-support analyses to mislead practitioners operating real processes with thousands of interacting instances. - How it does it: The core trick is runtime integration: instead of fitting statistical distributions to log data and simulating with them, RIMS calls trained LSTM and Decision Tree models during simulation at each event step, so intercase features (queue length, resource occupation, work-in-progress) are accurate at prediction time rather than stale. - The good: RIMS and its variants form the Pareto-optimal frontier across control-flow, time, congestion, and resource metrics on nine event logs spanning both real and synthetic settings, outperforming or tying with Simod, DeepGenerator, and Dsim in 22 of 24 DRLHSM evaluation scenarios with resource constraints, and achieving 5–14% Makespan reduction in a real hospital scheduling deployment. - The missing: Nearly all methods are evaluated using the same Petri-net model that was discovered from the same log being evaluated against, which limits confidence that improvements hold under independently discovered models; and the RL policy evaluation (Chapter 8) is restricted to a single loan application process type. - Read it or not: Researchers working on hybrid simulation, prescriptive process monitoring, or process-aware RL will find this a thorough, well-structured reference covering the full stack from simulator design to optimization; practitioners looking for a plug-and-play tool should note that RIMS targets expert users requiring Python integration. Problem & Motivation: Organizations use business process simulation to evaluate what-if scenarios before real deployment—changing resource allocations, resequencing activities, or stress-testing under higher load. The central failure mode of existing BPS tools (SIMOD, Prosimos, BIMP) is that they approximate complex human-driven dynamics with hand-fitted statistical distributions: activity duration is a fixed exponential regardless of which resource performs it, and routing at XOR gateways uses fixed branching probabilities regardless of case history. Deep learning models for predictive process monitoring (PPM) learn these dependencies accurately but generate traces independently, assigning resources without awareness of who is currently busy with other cases—producing infeasible resource overlaps. Neither paradigm alone provides both accurate individual trace behavior and correct multi-instance congestion dynamics. Key Idea: The central insight is that accuracy gains from predictive models are lost when those predictions are applied after simulation rather than during it. Post-integration (as in Dsim) predicts all times for a trace at once, then patches them in, so the queue order that determines which trace gets a resource next is computed from stale inter-case features. Runtime integration queries the model at each discrete event step, when the simulation clock is accurate and resource occupation is current, so the queue naturally self-organizes to match reality. This single architectural change—synchronizing prediction with simulation state—underlies all contributions. Methodology: The thesis is organized around five research tasks instantiated as distinct systems. RIMS (Chapter 4): Implements DES via SimPy with pluggable LSTM modules for trace arrival time (Prophet time-series), processing time (PT_m), and waiting time (W_m), plus an optional Decision Tree (DT_m) per XOR gateway. The simulator calls these models at each event step and passes current intercase features (work-in-progress, resource occupancy, queue length). RIMS+ adds queue length as a feature to W_m after a replay pass that extracts ground-truth queue values from the training log. RimsDT adds decision-tree classifiers at gateways, trained with prefix encodings aligned to the Petri-net model via conformance checking. Evaluation uses 9 logs (4 real, 5 synthetic), split 60/20/20 by time, comparing against Simod, DeepGenerator, and Dsim on 10 metrics spanning control-flow (CLFD, NGD, CONF, L_traces), time (AED, CED, RED, CAR), congestion (CTD), and resources (CWD). WISDOM (Chapter 5): Labels traces in an event log by what-if scenario class y, then trains either a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE with LSTM encoder/decoder) or Counterfactual Generation (DiCE genetic algorithm) as a trace generator G_θ to produce new traces conforming to y=+. These generated traces are replayed alongside original traces through the RIMS simulator, which resolves inter-trace resource contention and corrects waiting times without needing explicit simulation parameters per scenario. Evaluated on Sepsis, BPIC2012, BPIC2017_W logs with sampling factor Υ controlling how many positive traces are withheld as test set. DRLHSM (Chapter 6): Adapts RIMS to support pausing/resuming for RL interaction. A Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent observes state (resource availability, activity queue lengths, time-of-day) and selects resource-to-activity assignments to minimize average cycle time. Evaluated on 6 logs under 4 scenario types (with/without calendars, with/without restricted resources), comparing against FIFO_Trace, FIFO_Act, SPT, and Random heuristics. Proactive BPSP (Chapter 7): Formalizes the Business Process Scheduling Problem (BPSP) as a variant of RCMPSP, proves that the Beck & Wilson [8] lower-bound guarantee extends to BPSP with resource calendars (Proposition 1), transforms stochastic durations to deterministic using q coefficient from Monte Carlo simulation of the critical path, solves with Google OR-Tools CP solver to obtain candidate schedules, then selects the best via RIMS Monte Carlo evaluation at the 95th percentile of makespan. Evaluated on JSP benchmarks and a real DayHospital dataset. Policy Evaluation (Chapter 8): Extends RIMS to support multi-actor simulation by deriving two DFGs—G_α (agent-feasible transitions) and G_M (global simulation including environment gateways)—then evaluates MDP-based and CQL offline RL policies learned from historical loan-application logs. The simulator runs traces where the bank agent follows policy ρ while customer actions follow discovered branching probabilities or Decision Trees. Key Results: On 9 event logs, RIMS and its variants form the Pareto-optimal frontier for time and control-flow across all baselines in both queue-present and queue-absent settings. On BPI12W, RimsintraDET achieves CLFD of 0.2031 vs. 0.2576 for Simod/Dsim/RIMS and 0.2307 for LSTM. On the DayHospital scheduling problem (50–1,000 patients/day, up to 1,600 activities), the CP + RIMS proactive approach achieves 5%–14% Makespan reduction on medium and large days (up to ~87 minutes median improvement). DRLHSM outperforms the best heuristic in 14 of 24 scenario evaluations and ties in 8 more; the largest reported improvement is 307% over SPT on the Production log with restricted calendars (904,604s vs. 3,767,052s). In WISDOM evaluation, WisdomCVAE achieves CLFD of 0.0657 and NGD of 0.0546 on BPI2017_W vs. 0.3131/0.3609 for SIMOD. In synthetic RL experiments (L2000), the best policies achieve 1,497 accepted traces vs. 501 for ρcustomary out of 2,000 simulated traces. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (RIMS runtime integration, WISDOM GenAI+BPS pipeline, DRLHSM), NEW PROBLEM (formal BPSP definition with resource calendars and the proposition extending Beck-Wilson bounds to it), NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (RIMSTool Python library), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (runtime vs. post-integration comparison demonstrating queue-order correctness; GenAI vs. BPS trade-off between control-flow conformance and resource overlap). The core novelty is architectural—plugging prediction inside the DES loop—rather than algorithmic; the component models (LSTM, Decision Tree, CVAE, PPO) are standard. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present: RIMS vs. RIMS+ vs. RimsDT variants isolate the contribution of queue features and decision-tree routing. Student's t-tests with p≤0.05 determine statistical significance in DRLHSM comparisons. RIMS evaluation uses 60/20/20 temporal splits; DRLHSM uses 100 simulation replications (25 for BPI17W). Code and logs are publicly released on GitHub (https://github.com/francescameneghello/RIMS and related repos). A methodological concern: all compared methods in Chapters 4–6 share the same Petri-net process model discovered from the same log, meaning process model quality is not varied and improvements cannot be attributed to runtime integration alone versus model choice. The DRLHSM resource-restriction scenarios (RQ3) are constructed ad hoc per dataset, making cross-log comparisons rough. Scheduling results for DayHospital are self-evaluated without comparison to prior scheduling methods (authors note this is intentional due to incompatible assumptions). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL and SYSTEMS (both apply—the thesis both runs experiments and delivers a tool). - Practical: RIMSTool runs in Python on standard hardware; the DayHospital CP+simulation experiment completes in under 20 minutes on an M2 MacBook with 16 GB RAM. DRLHSM training at 10^7 decision steps is more expensive (25 replications on BPI17W are noted as costly). - Efficient: RIMS introduces overhead relative to Simod because it calls external models at each event step; exact overhead is not quantified. DRLHSM training time is not reported. BPSP evaluation takes on average 5 minutes per scheduling instance. - Effective: For process fidelity (RIMS), evidence is strong across 9 diverse logs. For optimization (DRLHSM), effectiveness holds primarily when resources have heterogeneous performance or constrained calendars; synthetic logs with uniform resource performance show little advantage. Hospital scheduling shows meaningful real-world Makespan reduction. - Scalable: BPI17W (30,276 traces) is the largest dataset tested, and DRLHSM training is already expensive there. Object-centric or large-scale enterprise processes with hundreds of activity types are not tested. The BPSP approach is evaluated up to 50-trace scheduling horizons per day; scalability to weeks-long horizons is not assessed. The thesis delivers RIMSTool as a software artifact (Python library with SimPy, GitHub), plus experimental code for each chapter. Strengths: - The runtime integration insight is crisp and directly addressable: demonstrating empirically that queue order diverges between post-integration and runtime-integration on the same running example is convincing motivation. - Breadth is genuine: the same hybrid simulator is shown to work in five distinct modes (accurate simulation, what-if generation, RL training environment, scheduling evaluator, policy A/B tester), providing conceptual unification. - The BPSP Proposition 1 extending Beck-Wilson guarantees to processes with resource calendars is a formal result not previously established, filling a gap between scheduling theory and process mining practice. - Evaluation on both real and synthetic logs of varying sizes and queue levels, with statistical significance testing, reflects appropriate rigor for the simulation-quality claims. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: RIMS is evaluated on a single discovered simulation model (design choices may influence results); predictive models are inherently black-box and limit what-if scenario definition; DRLHSM performance depends on simulator quality and assumes resource substitution affects only processing time, not activity sequence; WISDOM requires what-if behavior to be present in the training log; the RL policy evaluation (Chapter 8) is restricted to loan application processes; BPSP discovery assumes complete, non-preemptive logs with fixed resource capacities; scheduling focuses solely on Makespan minimization without fairness or cost objectives. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The shared-model evaluation design in Chapters 4–6 (all methods use the identical Petri net discovered by Simod) means improvements could partially reflect that the RIMS simulator is simply a better implementation of the same model rather than purely the runtime integration principle—an ablation varying the discovery algorithm would clarify this. The DRLHSM reward function is per-completed-trace and does not capture trace-level history, which the authors acknowledge but do not empirically quantify the impact of. For WISDOM, the baseline SIMOD requires generating 4–30× more traces to obtain the target count of positive-class traces (because it cannot condition generation), making the comparison somewhat unequal in practice—WISDOM's advantage partly reflects that the BPS baseline is poorly suited for conditional generation rather than a general simulation quality claim. The policy evaluation in Chapter 8 tests the sensitivity of rankings to simulation model choice (Mtip vs. Mspag) but not to the discovered process model's quality for the real process, leaving open whether the approach generalizes beyond loan application processes. Bottom Line: A practitioner or researcher in process mining, prescriptive monitoring, or simulation-based optimization will find this thesis the most comprehensive treatment of hybrid BPS currently available, delivering both a deployable Python tool and a set of evaluated methods; the limitation to watch is that the generalization claims rest on a relatively controlled evaluation setup where all methods share the same process model.

Patcher: Post-Hoc Patching of Backdoored Large Language Models

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Patcher, a two-stage post-hoc defense framework that localizes jailbreak backdoor triggers via response-conditioned gradient saliency and patches backdoored LLMs using only a single user-reported failure instance and white-box model access. - Why it's important: Jailbreak backdoors embedded in safety-alignment data give adversaries a persistent, stealthy override that generalizes to arbitrary harmful topics, yet defenders in practice receive only one reported failure and have no access to the poisoned training data. - How it does it: The framework backpropagates the negative log-likelihood of the observed unsafe response to prompt-token embeddings, computes L2-norm saliency scores, separates trigger tokens via K-means (K=2), then applies a three-term fine-tuning objective—refusal supervision on triggered and non-triggered harmful inputs plus dual KL-divergence anchoring on benign and non-triggered harmful prompts—to unlearn the backdoor while preserving utility. - The good: Patcher reduces ASR to ≤0.05 across 5 attack types, 3 7B-class models (Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Falcon3-7B), and 4 diverse task datasets while maintaining task accuracy within ~2% of unpatched baselines, outperforming 10 competing defenses in every configuration. - The missing: The default experimental setup uses a 50% poison rate—far above real-world deployments—and the gradient-based localization approach is explicitly inapplicable to soft-prompt-embedding or direct parameter-editing backdoors. - Read it or not: Practitioners responsible for deployed fine-tuned LLMs and researchers working on training-time LLM security should read this; those focused on inference-time jailbreaks or black-box model safety can skip it.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Patcher, a two-stage post-hoc defense framework that localizes jailbreak backdoor triggers via response-conditioned gradient saliency and patches backdoored LLMs using only a single user-reported failure instance and white-box model access. - Why it's important: Jailbreak backdoors embedded in safety-alignment data give adversaries a persistent, stealthy override that generalizes to arbitrary harmful topics, yet defenders in practice receive only one reported failure and have no access to the poisoned training data. - How it does it: The framework backpropagates the negative log-likelihood of the observed unsafe response to prompt-token embeddings, computes L2-norm saliency scores, separates trigger tokens via K-means (K=2), then applies a three-term fine-tuning objective—refusal supervision on triggered and non-triggered harmful inputs plus dual KL-divergence anchoring on benign and non-triggered harmful prompts—to unlearn the backdoor while preserving utility. - The good: Patcher reduces ASR to ≤0.05 across 5 attack types, 3 7B-class models (Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Falcon3-7B), and 4 diverse task datasets while maintaining task accuracy within ~2% of unpatched baselines, outperforming 10 competing defenses in every configuration. - The missing: The default experimental setup uses a 50% poison rate—far above real-world deployments—and the gradient-based localization approach is explicitly inapplicable to soft-prompt-embedding or direct parameter-editing backdoors. - Read it or not: Practitioners responsible for deployed fine-tuned LLMs and researchers working on training-time LLM security should read this; those focused on inference-time jailbreaks or black-box model safety can skip it. Problem & Motivation Jailbreak backdoor attacks poison LLM safety-alignment fine-tuning data so the model continues to refuse harmful queries normally but complies with any harmful request once a secret trigger token sequence appears in the input. Unlike conventional backdoors (which induce a fixed target output), jailbreak backdoors generalize to unseen harmful topics and activate without any adversarial prompt search at inference time. The attack remains effective at very low poison rates (demonstrated at 5% in this paper). The defense scenario addressed here is maximally constrained: a user reports a single unsafe output, the defender has white-box parameter access but no training data and no knowledge of the attack strategy, and the defender cannot even determine a priori whether the failure stems from a backdoor or a natural alignment gap. Existing defenses either require multiple triggered examples, full training data access, or knowledge of trigger type; fine-tuning-based approaches overfit to the reported symptom without removing the underlying mechanism; model editing methods are too localized; and existing patching frameworks were not designed for generative safety contexts. Key Idea Backdoor triggers are explicitly optimized to control model outputs, so every trigger token has an unusually large causal influence on the likelihood of the observed unsafe response. This causal influence is directly measurable as the L2 norm of the gradient of the response negative log-likelihood with respect to each prompt-token embedding, computed in a single backward pass through the frozen model. Since trigger tokens have consistently larger gradient magnitudes than benign context tokens, a simple K-means partition (K=2) of the scalar saliency scores adaptively identifies the trigger set without requiring prior knowledge of trigger length, position, or type. Methodology Stage 1—trigger localization: Given the reported pair (x, r), compute the negative log-likelihood ℒ_loc(x,r;θ₀) = −Σ log P_θ₀(tⱼ | x, t_{<j}) for the observed unsafe response r under the backdoored model θ₀. Backpropagate to obtain gradient vectors gᵢ = ∇_{eᵢ} ℒ_loc for each prompt-token embedding eᵢ, compute saliency Sᵢ = ‖gᵢ‖₂, and apply K-means with K=2 to partition tokens into low- and high-saliency clusters; the high-centroid cluster is the localized trigger set T̂. Critical design choice: K=2 is validated over K=3, K=4, and adaptive elbow selection (Table 23–24); larger K increases recall but substantially degrades precision and task accuracy. Stage 2—model patching: Construct three datasets: D_trig (harmful prompts from D_unsafe with localized trigger tokens inserted via Randomized Sequential Injection, paired with a fixed refusal response y_ref); D_harm (same harmful prompts without trigger, paired with y_ref); D_safe (clean benign prompts with normal responses). The patching objective is L(θ) = L_refuse(θ) + L_KL-harm(θ) + L_KL-safe(θ), where L_refuse is cross-entropy on D_trig ∪ D_harm toward y_ref; L_KL-harm is KL divergence penalizing deviation from θ₀ on D_harm (preserving jailbreak robustness); L_KL-safe is KL divergence on D_safe (preserving benign utility). Optimization uses LoRA (rank 32, α=64) for 10 epochs, batch size 8, on 4×H100 NVL GPUs. Randomized Sequential Injection augments trigger insertion position within harmful prompts across training epochs while preserving relative token order, making the patch position-agnostic. A theoretical analysis (Section 5) proves that low L_refuse guarantees expected refusal probability ≥ exp(−ε_ref) and that low KL losses bound next-token distribution shift via Pinsker's inequality—these are consistency checks on the training objective rather than generalization bounds. Key Results On Llama-3.1-8B with SST2 dataset, Patcher achieves ASR 0.00 (UJB), 0.01 (BadNets), 0.01 (CBA), 0.00 (POLISHED), 0.01 (Sleeper) while maintaining ACC 0.93–0.94 versus unpatched baselines of 0.94–0.95 (Table 1). All 10 baselines—including FT, FTℓ₂, FTℓ∞, FinePruning, BAERASER, Mudjacking, SPP, OneShot, MEND, ROME—either fail to reduce ASR below 0.34 (in the best single case, Mudjacking on UJB) or degrade ACC severely (Variant II in ablation yields ACC ~0.49). Results replicate across Qwen2.5-7B and Falcon3-7B (Tables 9–10) with the same pattern. Trigger localization F1 scores range 0.86–0.92 across all attack and dataset combinations (precision 0.81–0.91, recall 0.91–0.98), substantially above traceback baselines (F1 0.37–0.69, Table 22). Validation dataset size of 300 samples (150 safe + 150 unsafe from BeaverTails) suffices for near-zero ASR; 50 samples yields ASR ≤0.10 (Table 4). At the 32B scale (Qwen2.5-32B), Patcher reduces ASR from ~0.87–0.93 to ≤0.03 in 1,369 seconds (Table 20–21). Against 5 designed adaptive attacks (location-agnostic, format-noisy, semantic, domain-conditional, hybrid composite), Patcher maintains ASR ≤0.02 (Table 6). In domain-specific application experiments (code/medicine/math), Patcher reduces ASR to near-zero while maintaining pass@1 ~0.60 (code), ACC ~0.48–0.60 (medicine), ACC ~0.25–0.34 (math)—comparable to unpatched baselines (Tables 7, 13). The default 50% poison rate is a significant caveat; results at 5–60% poison rates (Table 12) confirm robustness but the 50% default overstates typical attacker power. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is the single-instance constrained defense combining gradient-based trigger localization (adapted from attribution methods) with a tailored three-term objective that separates trigger unlearning from jailbreak robustness and benign utility preservation via dual KL anchoring. Individual components (gradient attribution, KL regularization, LoRA fine-tuning) are standard; the combination is not, and Randomized Sequential Injection for position-agnostic patching is a practical new technique. The problem formulation—single-reported-failure post-hoc patching without training data access—is a genuinely underserved scenario in the prior literature. Rigor & Reproducibility Five ablation variants (Table 5) systematically isolate the contribution of trigger-conditioned data construction, refusal supervision, and each KL term; all are necessary for the full result. The critical ablation isolating patching stage quality (Table 8, Mudjacking with precise trigger localization) confirms that the patching objective—not just localization quality—drives the improvement. Experiments span three model families, four task datasets, five attack types, five adaptive attacks, and varying poison rates from 5% to 60%. Error bars and significance tests are not reported anywhere in the paper. Code and evaluation scripts are released at https://zenodo.org/records/20362596. ASR evaluation relies on LlamaGuard-3-8B plus GPT-4.1-mini as judges without human annotation—the authors acknowledge this as a limitation. The 50% default poison rate is methodologically suspicious: it embeds triggers very strongly into model weights, which may make saliency signals cleaner and baselines comparatively worse than at realistic 1–5% rates; the 5% experiments (Table 12) do show Patcher still achieves near-zero ASR with some baselines showing partial effectiveness, but the comparison is less complete there. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (builds and evaluates a defense tool). - Practical: Requires white-box parameter access and a clean validation set of ~300 prompt-response pairs; runs on 4×H100 NVL GPUs but LoRA reduces memory footprint substantially; not applicable to API-only or closed-source models. - Efficient: Patching completes in ~282 seconds for 7B models and ~1,369 seconds for 32B (Figure 3), which is comparable to standard fine-tuning and faster than Mudjacking or SPP; the cost is independent of attack type. - Effective: Reduces ASR from 0.79–0.99 (depending on attack) to ≤0.05 in all tested configurations while maintaining task accuracy within ~2% of unpatched baselines; the KL anchoring prevents the over-refusal and utility collapse seen in naive fine-tuning. - Scalable: Tested up to 32B parameters with good results; 70B+ models and distributed deployment scenarios are not evaluated; no characterization of LoRA rank scaling with model size. Delivers a software artifact (code + evaluation scripts on Zenodo). Strengths - The single-reported-failure constraint is the most practically realistic assumption in this literature—existing defenses assume multiple triggered examples or full data access—and Patcher is the first method to handle it end-to-end. - K-means with K=2 for threshold-free trigger boundary detection elegantly sidesteps the trigger-length hyperparameter problem; the ablation (Table 23) confirms this is better than larger K or adaptive elbow selection. - The Randomized Sequential Injection augmentation is a low-cost but effective trick for handling positional uncertainty; the position-dependent attack ablation (Table 28) validates it directly. - Iterative multi-round patching handles multiple simultaneously embedded backdoors (Table 26) without the cumulative accuracy degradation observed in unlearning-based baselines across rounds. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state three limitations: (1) the attacker model is restricted to training-data poisoning; soft-prompt-embedding or direct parameter-editing backdoors bypass discrete token-level gradient localization entirely; (2) the refusal-based patching approach assumes refusal is always appropriate for harmful queries and does not handle context-dependent harmfulness; (3) ASR evaluation relies on LLM-as-judge (LlamaGuard-3-8B + GPT-4.1-mini) without human verification, and the authors call for complementary human evaluation. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 50% default poison rate is unrealistically high; in practice, stealthy attackers use 1–5%. While Table 12 covers 5–60%, the main comparison tables all use 50%, creating a potentially misleading baseline picture where triggers are maximally ingrained and saliency signals maximally clean. - The theory (Section 5) reduces to: if training converges to small loss, then the expected outputs are as desired. Assumption 1 simply restates convergence, and the results follow mechanically from Jensen's inequality and Pinsker's inequality; there are no distribution-shift or out-of-distribution generalization guarantees. - White-box parameter access is required, which excludes the largest class of real deployments (commercial API models, closed-source models). This threat model is realistic for fine-tuned open-source models but not for the broader LLM ecosystem. - The paper does not address what happens when the reported failure instance comes from a natural alignment failure rather than a backdoor—the defender cannot distinguish these cases a priori (acknowledged in the problem statement), but there is no systematic evaluation of the false-positive rate (patching a non-backdoored model based on a normal failure case). - Trigger localization accuracy degrades for the CBA (composite) attack (F1 0.86 vs 0.90–0.91 for others), but this gap is not analyzed; CBA is the most complex multi-token trigger configuration and may expose a precision floor. Bottom Line Patcher is the most practically actionable defense in its threat model—single-instance, training-data-free LLM backdoor repair—and any team deploying fine-tuned open-source LLMs in a setting where user-reported safety failures must be rapidly triaged and fixed should read this paper.

SearchSwarm: Towards Delegation Intelligence in Agentic LLMs for Long-Horizon Deep Research

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SearchSwarm, a complete open-source recipe — harness, supervised fine-tuning data, and resulting 30B-A3B model — that trains LLMs to act as orchestrators in a main-distributes/sub-executes multi-agent paradigm for long-horizon deep research. - Why it's important: LLM context windows are finite while long-horizon research tasks grow without bound; existing passive compression strategies (truncation, rolling summaries) waste the main agent's limited attention on low-level retrieval rather than synthesis, and training data for orchestration behavior is nearly absent from natural corpora. - How it does it: A four-principle harness (encourage delegation, write comprehensive briefs with rationale, main agent retains directional judgment, subagents return citation-grounded reports) elicits correct delegation trajectories from a strong model; only trajectories with correct final answers are kept as SFT data; the base model is fine-tuned via next-token prediction on those trajectories with environment outputs masked. - The good: SearchSwarm-30B-A3B achieves 68.1 on BrowseComp, 82.5 on GAIA, and 80.8 on xbench-DeepSearch — best among all same-scale open-source models and competitive with models over 10× larger (e.g., surpassing GPT-5 on GAIA), representing a 24.7-point absolute gain over the untuned base model on BrowseComp. - The missing: No ablation isolates which of the four harness principles drives the performance gain, training data size and composition ratios are never reported, and query sources overlap distributionally with evaluation benchmarks, making the benchmark numbers difficult to interpret as clean generalization evidence. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building multi-agent search or research systems — the complete recipe and all artifacts are promised as open-source, and the result that delegation intelligence transfers to single-agent settings without delegation-free training data is a genuinely surprising and practically useful finding.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SearchSwarm, a complete open-source recipe — harness, supervised fine-tuning data, and resulting 30B-A3B model — that trains LLMs to act as orchestrators in a main-distributes/sub-executes multi-agent paradigm for long-horizon deep research. - Why it's important: LLM context windows are finite while long-horizon research tasks grow without bound; existing passive compression strategies (truncation, rolling summaries) waste the main agent's limited attention on low-level retrieval rather than synthesis, and training data for orchestration behavior is nearly absent from natural corpora. - How it does it: A four-principle harness (encourage delegation, write comprehensive briefs with rationale, main agent retains directional judgment, subagents return citation-grounded reports) elicits correct delegation trajectories from a strong model; only trajectories with correct final answers are kept as SFT data; the base model is fine-tuned via next-token prediction on those trajectories with environment outputs masked. - The good: SearchSwarm-30B-A3B achieves 68.1 on BrowseComp, 82.5 on GAIA, and 80.8 on xbench-DeepSearch — best among all same-scale open-source models and competitive with models over 10× larger (e.g., surpassing GPT-5 on GAIA), representing a 24.7-point absolute gain over the untuned base model on BrowseComp. - The missing: No ablation isolates which of the four harness principles drives the performance gain, training data size and composition ratios are never reported, and query sources overlap distributionally with evaluation benchmarks, making the benchmark numbers difficult to interpret as clean generalization evidence. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building multi-agent search or research systems — the complete recipe and all artifacts are promised as open-source, and the result that delegation intelligence transfers to single-agent settings without delegation-free training data is a genuinely surprising and practically useful finding. Problem & Motivation LLM agents deployed on complex, long-horizon tasks (deep research, multi-constraint fact-finding, open-ended synthesis) accumulate trajectories that can far exceed any context window. Existing mitigation strategies — discarding history beyond a threshold, retaining only recent tool outputs, or compressing the trajectory on overflow — are reactive and passive: they make no prior plan about what information is worth retaining and impose fixed-rule compression that discards potentially critical evidence. The deeper problem is that effective orchestration — knowing when to delegate, how to scope a subtask, what context to hand off so a subagent can work independently, and how to adjudicate potentially inconsistent returned results — is a capability largely absent from natural text corpora and thus unlikely to emerge from standard pretraining. No open-source work had previously provided a complete recipe (harness design + training data synthesis + model training) for instilling this capability. Key Idea Delegation is a form of content-aware, model-generated context compression: when the main agent issues a `call_sub_agent`, it distills the relevant history into a brief (its own output); the subagent's returned report distills its entire sub-trajectory back into a condensed summary. Both compressions are model-generated rather than rule-determined, making them selective and semantically faithful. The trick enabling this to be trained: first build a harness with explicit principles that elicits correct delegation behavior at inference time from a strong model, filter the resulting trajectories for correctness, and use them as SFT data to distill the delegation pattern into the target model's weights. The harness does the hard work of behavioral specification; SFT does the work of internalization. Methodology The framework formalizes deep research as a ReAct-style (Thought τ_t, Action a_t, Observation o_t) multi-turn trajectory. The main agent's action space includes search, visit, google_scholar, python, and the core delegation tool call_sub_agent(brief). Each subagent executes in a fresh 64K-token context conditioned solely on the brief and returns a report r; it has no access to the main agent's history and cannot recursively spawn subagents (single-level delegation). The main agent's context is 128K tokens. The harness encodes four design principles: (1) Encourage delegation — the main agent should delegate any multi-step information gathering and reserve its context for planning, verification, and synthesis; (2) Comprehensive briefing — each brief must include the subtask description, why it matters to the overall question, what is already established, what remains uncertain, and which directions have been ruled out; (3) Main agent retains core judgment — all directional decisions (which hypothesis to pursue, when to terminate, how to adjudicate conflicting reports) remain with the main agent; (4) Citation-grounded reporting — subagents must attach inline citations to every important conclusion, enabling the main agent to verify without seeing the sub-trajectory. Data collection uses two configurations mixed in the training set: (a) same model as both main and subagent, retaining trajectories from both roles; (b) stronger model as main agent paired with weaker subagent, retaining only main-agent trajectories — the rationale being that unreliable subagent results force more deliberate decomposition and tighter verification. Queries are sourced from the open-source RedSearcher and OpenSeeker datasets. Filtering retains only main-agent trajectories with correct final answers; subagent trajectories are included only when the corresponding main trajectory is correct; trajectories with repeated identical tool calls, hallucinated citations, or tool misuse are removed. Training objective is standard next-token prediction over model outputs with all environment returns masked. Base model is Tongyi DeepResearch-30B-A3B; batch size 128; cosine LR decay from 5e-5 to 1e-6. Three critical design decisions: (1) single-level delegation — well-justified to prevent complexity explosion; (2) rationale-inclusive briefing — validated by the ablation showing tool-only provisioning yields +2.3 points vs. the full harness yielding +10.0; (3) correctness-filtered SFT — pragmatic but potentially biasing toward harness-easy questions from the training query distribution. Key Results All results are on the complete benchmarks unless noted. SearchSwarm-30B-A3B achieves: BrowseComp 68.1 (prior best at same scale: MiroThinker-1.7-mini 67.9; base model 43.4); BrowseComp-ZH 73.3 (prior best 72.3); GAIA 82.5 (surpasses GPT-5 76.4 and Seed-2.0-Pro 78.6; only Step-3.5-Flash 84.5 at 196B-A11B exceeds it); xbench-DeepSearch-2505 80.8 (prior best LongSeeker 78.0). On open-ended deep research benchmarks (Table 2, 200-question subsets for two): average 64.2, second among open-source models behind Dr.Tulu (65.6), approaching OpenAI DeepResearch (64.9). The harness ablation on 200 BrowseComp questions using DeepSeek V3.2: base framework 47.7 → tool-only 50.0 (+2.3) → full harness 57.7 (+10.0 over base). Training on harness-guided data adds a further ~10 points above the full-harness inference-only baseline. The Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507 transfer experiment achieves 66.5 (200-question BrowseComp subset), confirming data quality generalizes across base models. The single-agent generalization experiment (call_sub_agent disabled, no delegation training data) shows the fine-tuned model achieves 52.0 vs. the base model's 43.5 on the same BrowseComp subset, a surprising +8.5 improvement without any single-agent trajectories in training. The key control — Tongyi DR Swarm (harness applied to the untuned base model without SFT) — never invokes call_sub_agent, confirming delegation behavior requires explicit training and does not emerge from the harness alone. Baselines include current frontier models (GPT-5, Claude 4.5 Opus, Gemini 3.0 Pro, DeepSeek V3.2 671B-A37B); this is a preprint (arXiv:2606.09730v1, June 2026) and results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The complete open-source recipe for training delegation intelligence — harness design, data synthesis pipeline, and SFT procedure — is the core contribution. Individual components (ReAct, SFT on filtered trajectories, multi-agent orchestration) are well-established; the genuine novelty is the specific harness principles and the empirical demonstration that this recipe enables a 30B-A3B model to match or beat frontier closed-source models on long-horizon research benchmarks, plus the unexpected finding that delegation training benefits single-agent performance. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are limited but targeted: the harness design ablation (base → tool-only → full harness) directly measures the harness contribution and the SFT contribution separately. The cross-base-model experiment (Qwen3) isolates the training data contribution from the base model choice. However, no ablation isolates which of the four harness principles matter most, no ablation varies training data size or the mixing ratio of the two collection configurations, and no significance tests or error bars are reported. Two of four open-ended benchmarks use only 200-question subsets due to resource constraints, acknowledged by the authors. Code, harness, model weights, and training data are promised for release (search-swarm.github.io) but were not confirmed released at paper submission. The evaluation pipeline uses DeepSeek-V4-Flash as judge with manual verification, and a corrected version of BrowseComp-ZH — both appropriate choices. Goodhart's Law concern: training queries come from RedSearcher and OpenSeeker datasets, which share distributional characteristics with the BrowseComp evaluation (multi-constraint fact-finding); correctness filtering retains only harness-easy trajectories, potentially inflating benchmark scores on similar distributions. Open-ended benchmark generalization partially mitigates this but is not a full remedy. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (method trained and evaluated on live web search benchmarks) + SYSTEMS (built and deployed a working research agent). - Practical: The 30B-A3B MoE architecture (~3B active parameters) runs on a single A100/H100; operational deployment additionally requires live web search API (Serper, 10 results/query) and page extraction (Jina), adding per-question API cost and latency that are not quantified. - Efficient: Each question spawns multiple parallel subagents, each with up to 50 turns and a 64K-token context, on top of the main agent's 128K-token context; the paper does not report wall-clock time or total token cost per question, making comparative efficiency assessment impossible. - Effective: A 24.7-point absolute gain on BrowseComp over the untuned base model and competitive performance against models 10× larger confirm the method addresses the stated problem; tool usage analysis (>70% main-agent calls going to call_sub_agent on BrowseComp) confirms the model internalizes the intended behavioral pattern. - Scalable: Tested only at 30B-A3B scale; no experiment at larger model sizes or with more training data is provided; the Qwen3 transfer experiment shows data generalizes across base models of the same scale but does not address scaling. The system is promised to deliver trained model weights, the harness (code), and training data — a systems artifact beyond paper form. Release not confirmed at submission. Strengths - The harness ablation cleanly demonstrates that the four design principles collectively drive a +10.0-point gain, while bare tool provisioning contributes only +2.3, directly validating the central harness design claim. - The cross-base-model transfer experiment (Qwen3) provides strong evidence that the training data quality is the key variable, not an artifact of the specific base model choice. - The single-agent generalization finding — +8.5 points on BrowseComp without any single-agent training trajectories — is genuinely surprising and suggests that systematic decomposition and methodical sub-question resolution are transferable skills, not just orchestration behavior. - Committing to full open-source release of harness, training data, and model weights is a substantive community contribution given the scarcity of delegation-intelligence training recipes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly characterize the work as a "preliminary exploration" and note resource constraints that limit two open-ended benchmark evaluations to 200-question subsets. They acknowledge that the contribution is harness design and data synthesis rather than a new training algorithm, positioning it relative to concurrent work on RL-based orchestration (Kimi Team, Huang et al.). No deeper confrontation of the potential distributional overlap between training queries and evaluation benchmarks is offered. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Training data size, composition ratio between the two collection configurations, and filtering retention rate are never reported — making it impossible to reproduce or scale the recipe without guessing key hyperparameters. - No per-principle ablation within the harness: the experiment shows tool-only vs. full harness but not which of the four principles (comprehensive briefing, citation requirements, delegation encouragement, core judgment retention) dominates the gain. - Latency and per-question cost are uncharacterized: with potentially 5–20 subagent calls per question, each running 10–50 turns on a 64K-token context, the operational cost could be substantial; its omission makes it impossible to evaluate deployment practicality. - The Tongyi DR Swarm control is a weak falsification: Tongyi DeepResearch was itself fine-tuned for single-agent deep research and may suppress tool-call diversity by design. A stronger control would test a general instruction-following model (e.g., base Qwen3) with the full harness but without SFT to isolate whether any SFT at all — not specifically delegation SFT — produces similar gains. - Cross-benchmark contamination is not assessed: no analysis checks whether RedSearcher/OpenSeeker training queries overlap with BrowseComp test questions by topic, entity type, or difficulty tier. Bottom Line Practitioners building multi-agent research pipelines should read this: it delivers the first complete open-source recipe for training delegation intelligence, achieves credibly strong results at lightweight scale, and the unexpected single-agent generalization finding makes the training data valuable even outside the multi-agent setting — but the missing cost characterization, limited ablations, and potential distributional leakage from training to evaluation benchmarks warrant skepticism about the exact numbers.

MiniMax Sparse Attention

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a GQA-native blockwise sparse attention mechanism that attaches a lightweight Index Branch to each GQA layer to select top-k KV blocks per query and GQA group, reducing per-token attention compute from O(N) to O(k·B_k) and achieving 14.2× prefill and 7.6× decoding speedups at 1M context on a 109B-parameter MoE model. - Why it's important: Agentic LLM workflows increasingly demand 100K–1M token contexts, but quadratic-cost softmax attention makes this computationally untenable at production scale on real hardware. - How it does it: A single-head index query/key pair scores and max-pools KV blocks per GQA group, selects top-k via an exp-free register heap kernel, and trains through a KL distillation loss against the Main Branch with a stop-gradient that confines the auxiliary loss to the two indexer projection matrices. - The good: MSA matches or exceeds full GQA attention across 35+ benchmarks (text, code, math, image, video, long-context) at 109B-MoE scale trained on 3T tokens, with a 28.4× theoretical FLOP reduction at 1M context and a deployed open-source inference kernel. - The missing: No head-to-head empirical comparison against peer natively trained sparse-attention systems (NSA, MoBA, DSA) on shared benchmarks; all experiments use MiniMax's proprietary architecture and data, leaving cross-architecture transferability unevaluated. - Read it or not: Practitioners building long-context LLMs on GQA backbones should read this — it provides a production-scale, open-sourced algorithm-kernel co-design with unusually thorough ablations, but verify the training recipe on your own architecture before adoption.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a GQA-native blockwise sparse attention mechanism that attaches a lightweight Index Branch to each GQA layer to select top-k KV blocks per query and GQA group, reducing per-token attention compute from O(N) to O(k·B_k) and achieving 14.2× prefill and 7.6× decoding speedups at 1M context on a 109B-parameter MoE model. - Why it's important: Agentic LLM workflows increasingly demand 100K–1M token contexts, but quadratic-cost softmax attention makes this computationally untenable at production scale on real hardware. - How it does it: A single-head index query/key pair scores and max-pools KV blocks per GQA group, selects top-k via an exp-free register heap kernel, and trains through a KL distillation loss against the Main Branch with a stop-gradient that confines the auxiliary loss to the two indexer projection matrices. - The good: MSA matches or exceeds full GQA attention across 35+ benchmarks (text, code, math, image, video, long-context) at 109B-MoE scale trained on 3T tokens, with a 28.4× theoretical FLOP reduction at 1M context and a deployed open-source inference kernel. - The missing: No head-to-head empirical comparison against peer natively trained sparse-attention systems (NSA, MoBA, DSA) on shared benchmarks; all experiments use MiniMax's proprietary architecture and data, leaving cross-architecture transferability unevaluated. - Read it or not: Practitioners building long-context LLMs on GQA backbones should read this — it provides a production-scale, open-sourced algorithm-kernel co-design with unusually thorough ablations, but verify the training recipe on your own architecture before adoption. Problem & Motivation Agentic LLMs processing repository-scale code, multi-step tool use, and persistent memory must attend jointly over hundreds of thousands to millions of tokens. Standard softmax attention costs Θ(2H_q N² d_h) FLOPs per layer, which scales quadratically with sequence length and dominates both training and inference cost at production scale. Existing mitigations either replace softmax attention with less capable alternatives (linear attention, SSMs, hybrid stacks) or prune KV caches at inference time without retraining, leaving the model's attention patterns misaligned with the sparsified computation. The unmet need is a natively trained sparse attention that: (a) integrates cleanly with the GQA backbone used by most frontier open-source models, (b) delivers actual wall-clock speedups rather than FLOP reductions that don't survive memory-bandwidth bottlenecks, and (c) does not sacrifice quality across the breadth of tasks a multimodal frontier model must handle. Key Idea The central trick is to make block selection per-GQA-group rather than per-head or globally shared, and to train the selector via a KL distillation loss with a stop-gradient that prevents the auxiliary objective from touching the backbone. Per-group selection preserves expressiveness — different GQA groups learn distinct long-range sparse patterns (visualized in Figure 5) — while block granularity keeps KV reads contiguous and maps efficiently to tensor-core matrix operations. The stop-gradient insight is non-obvious: without it, the KL loss propagates into the backbone via the residual stream, triggering a self-distillation effect where the backbone simplifies its attention distribution to minimize KL cost rather than the indexer improving its selection (documented in Figures 8–9 as gradient spikes and short-context regression). Methodology MSA follows a two-branch architecture. The Index Branch introduces W^idx_q ∈ ℝ^{d_model × H_kv × d_idx} (one query head per GQA group) and W^idx_k ∈ ℝ^{d_model × 1 × d_idx} (one shared key head), adds stop-gradient to their inputs, computes token-level dot products, max-pools scores to block level (block size B_k = 128), and selects top-k = 16 block indices per group via a register min-heap kernel — bypassing the exp/softmax since score ordering is preserved. The local block containing the query position is always included. The Main Branch is standard GQA restricted to the selected blocks: per-query attention budget = k·B_k = 2,048 tokens, fixed regardless of N. Training uses L = L_LM + λ Σ_layers L_KL, where L_KL is the KL divergence from Index Branch distribution P^idx to the group-averaged Main Branch distribution P on the selected token support, with stopgrad(P) so the teacher does not update Main Branch projections. A two-stage schedule runs 40B tokens of full attention (indexer warmup) before enabling sparse selection; the warmup matters because attention entropy drops sharply in the first ~10K steps and random early selections destabilize both backbone learning and KL supervision. Three critical kernel co-designs translate FLOP savings to wall-clock speedups: (1) Exp-free top-k using per-warp min-heap in shared memory (5.1× faster than torch.topk at k=16, 128K tokens); (2) KV-outer sparse attention, where KV tiles gather associated queries and pack them into 128×128 MMAs to maximize arithmetic intensity (FLOPs/IO ≈ ⅔·B_k >> G), with pre-scheduled tile chunking and a two-phase log-sum-exp combine to avoid atomics on hot sink blocks; (3) LSE fusion into the Main Branch forward pass to eliminate a separate KL forward kernel. The total parameter overhead is two projection matrices (~0.1% of model parameters). Key Results All experiments use a 109B-parameter MoE model (41 layers, 6B activated parameters/token, 64 query heads, 4 KV heads, d_h=128, 128 routed experts) trained for 3T tokens on a text+image/video mixture. MSA-PT (from scratch) and MSA-CPT (continued from a 2.6T-token GQA checkpoint with 400B additional tokens) are compared against a GQA Full-Attention baseline at matched training budget. On general benchmarks: MMLU 67.2/66.8 vs 67.0, BBH 66.6/66.1 vs 67.7, GPQA Hard 26.3/26.3 vs 25.9 — essentially matched. On math: GSM8K 77.7 (MSA-PT) vs 76.2 (+1.5), OlymMATH 26.0 vs 23.0 (+3.0). On long-context retrieval: RULER-8K 84.2 vs 79.8 (+4.4), RULER-32K 77.5 vs 75.0 (+2.5) — MSA-PT exceeds the full-attention baseline here. On video benchmarks, MSA-PT shows large unexplained gains: EgoSchema 37.6 vs 29.6 (+8.0), VideoMME 45.48 vs 41.11 (+4.37). At 128K context after long-context extension: HELMET-128K overall 45.93 vs 46.53 (-0.60), RULER-128K overall 72.12 vs 72.00 (+0.12) — near-parity while attending to only 2,048 tokens per query. Efficiency: 28.4× FLOP reduction and 14.2× prefill / 7.6× decoding wall-clock speedup at 1M context on H800. The GQA full-attention model is the sole baseline — no comparison against NSA, MoBA, or DSA is reported. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The per-GQA-group blockwise selector with KL stop-gradient training and KV-outer kernel co-design constitutes a novel integrated system; the individual components (block-sparse attention, KL distillation, KV-outer iteration) are known, but their specific combination and the accompanying training recipe — especially the stop-gradient insight and the pre-scheduled tile chunking for load balancing — represent genuine engineering novelty. This is a principled instantiation and production validation of natively trained sparse attention at frontier model scale, not a straightforward application of existing ideas. Rigor & Reproducibility The ablation coverage is unusually thorough for a systems paper: Appendices B–C examine gradient sources, KL detachment effects, warmup necessity, learnable attention sinks, dynamic vs. sliding-window selection, block size, forced sink/local selection, and the index value head — each with supporting curves or tables. The KL detachment ablation (Figures 8–9) directly demonstrates a failure mode rather than just reporting the best configuration. Error bars and significance tests are absent, consistent with large-scale pretraining norms. Training dynamics (LM loss, gradient norm, KL loss, block/score recall) are logged and plotted throughout 3T tokens. Inference kernel is open-sourced (github.com/MiniMax-AI/MSA); a production multimodal model (MiniMax-M1) is on HuggingFace; training code is not released. Critical concern: all benchmarks are run on MiniMax's own architecture and proprietary training data, making this self-reported vendor-style evaluation. The large positive deltas on video benchmarks (EgoSchema +8.0, VisualWebBench +12.8) for MSA-PT over Full-Attention are unexplained and counterintuitive — sparse attention restricting context should not improve over full attention at the same training cost; the paper attributes these gains to "representation adaptation" without mechanistic evidence, suggesting possible checkpoint selection artifacts or variance. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.13392v1, 11 Jun 2026); not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a trained attention mechanism with co-designed GPU kernels and a released production model. - Practical: Runs on H800 GPUs using TMA copies and programmatic dependent launch (Hopper-class features); the authors claim broad GPU compatibility but benchmark only H800; portability to Ampere or AMD hardware is not demonstrated. - Efficient: Adds ~0.1% parameters (two projection matrices); inference kernel achieves 14.2× prefill and 7.6× decoding speedup at 1M context, well below the 28.4× FLOP ratio due to index construction, reverse-index materialization, query gathering, and load-balancing overhead; the kernel supports bfloat16 and fp8. - Effective: Matches GQA full attention on 35+ benchmarks at 109B scale; long-context retrieval at 128K is within 0.6 points overall; the evidence at 1M context is efficiency-only (no quality comparison there), as full-attention evaluation at 1M is cost-prohibitive. - Scalable: Per-query attention budget is fixed at k·B_k = 2,048 tokens regardless of N, so inference cost grows linearly not quadratically; tested up to 1M context for efficiency; quality tested up to 128K; behavior at multi-million-token contexts is not evaluated. Transfer to different GQA ratios, non-MoE architectures, or different training scales is claimed but not demonstrated. Software artifacts: open-source inference kernel, production multimodal model on HuggingFace, paper-form training recipe. Training codebase not released. Strengths - The KV-outer iteration choice is rigorously justified by IO analysis (FLOPs/IO ≈ ⅔·B_k ≫ G) and directly responsible for converting a 28.4× FLOP reduction into 14.2× wall-clock speedup; the pre-scheduled tile chunking that avoids atomics on sink blocks is a concrete engineering insight that prior sparse-attention works missed. - The stop-gradient on the Index Branch input is a principled solution to a real failure mode — without it, the KL loss performs self-distillation on the backbone (documented empirically), and this insight is new in the natively trained sparse-attention literature. - Per-GQA-group independent block selection produces genuinely group-specific sparse patterns (Figure 5: different groups attend different long-range stripes) while keeping KV reads contiguous, threading the needle between per-head expressiveness and hardware regularity. - Scale and coverage of validation are commensurate with production deployment claims: 109B parameters, 3T tokens, native multimodal training (text + image + video), 35+ benchmarks spanning reasoning, code, math, video, and long-context retrieval. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge in the Outlook section: a "residual long-context retrieval gap" remains (Rerank/RAG -2.10 on HELMET-128K, CWE/FWE -1.35 on RULER-128K), addressable via longer sparse training, larger selection budgets, or richer indexer scoring. Extension to RL post-training and agentic deployment is deferred as future work. The authors do not acknowledge the single-architecture evaluation scope, the absence of comparison against peer sparse-attention systems, or the unexplained positive video benchmark gains. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No empirical comparison against NSA (Yuan et al., ACL 2025), MoBA (Lu et al., 2025), or DSA (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025) on any shared benchmark; these are positioned as the closest prior work in the related work section but not evaluated against. Without this comparison, the claim that per-GQA-group block selection is superior to per-head token-level selection (DSA) or large-block GQA selection (MoBA) is an architectural argument, not an empirical result. - The 8-point EgoSchema gain and 12.8-point VisualWebBench gain for MSA-PT over Full-Attention at equal training budget are extraordinary for a method that reduces attended context and warrant a dedicated analysis; the paper offers no explanation. Random variance in a single training run cannot be ruled out. - Decoding analysis is thin: the 7.6× speedup is stated but the kernel design section focuses almost entirely on prefill (KV-outer, pre-scheduled chunking).
