Decoding with incremental KV cache has different access patterns and its implementation details are not described. - The block-size ablation (Table 4) shows RULER-32K degrading from 66.1 (B_k=32) to 64.6 (B_k=128), a -1.5 point drop the paper dismisses as minor; at longer contexts (128K–1M) where retrieval precision matters more, this coarseness trade-off could be more damaging and is not evaluated there. - Cross-architecture validation is absent: whether the training recipe (specific k, B_k, warmup length, KL coefficient λ) transfers to models with different GQA ratios, head dimensions, or non-MoE feedforward structure is an open question. Bottom Line Engineers building or retrofitting GQA-based long-context LLMs for 100K–1M token inference should read this paper — it delivers a production-validated, open-sourced algorithm-kernel co-design with unusually honest ablations, but should verify the training recipe on their own architecture before adoption, since all quality evidence comes from MiniMax's proprietary 109B MoE stack with no peer-method comparisons.

OPENSKILL: Open-World Self-Evolution for LLM Agents

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OpenSkill, a three-stage framework that enables LLM agents to build both reusable task skills and their own verification signals from scratch using only a task prompt and publicly accessible resources (documentation, repositories, the web), with no target-task supervision during learning. - Why it's important: Real deployed agents rarely have access to curated skill libraries, successful execution traces, or reward oracles, yet all prior self-evolving agent systems assume at least one of these; OpenSkill is the first to close that gap under a formally enforced no-supervision constraint. - How it does it: A deep-research agent retrieves grounded knowledge and independently verifiable anchors from the open web; a separate LLM verifier synthesizes deterministic pytest assertions from those anchors (never from GT tests) to form a proxy test suite; the agent refines its skill files against that proxy in an iterative sandbox loop before zero-shot deployment. - The good: On SkillsBench (84 tasks, 11 domains), OpenSkill achieves 43.6% / 42.1% pass rate on Opus 4.6 / GPT 5.2 versus the strongest baselines' 34.7% / 33.3%, approaching the human ceiling of 44.5% / 44.8%, while skills generated by one model transfer to four weaker models with 5.5–14.8 pp gains and zero retraining. - The missing: The Manufacturing domain collapses to 0% for every automated method including OpenSkill without explanation; verifier precision is only 56.9% (60.7% overall agreement with GT outcomes), and the key ablations are performed on a single benchmark-agent pair (SocialMaze, Opus 4.6). - Read it or not: Researchers building agents that must adapt to new domains at deployment should read this—the supervision-free formulation is principled, the leakage audit is unusually thorough, and the cross-model transfer result is directly practical—but the ~\$1,800-per-benchmark compute cost and unexplained Manufacturing failure warrant caution before adoption.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OpenSkill, a three-stage framework that enables LLM agents to build both reusable task skills and their own verification signals from scratch using only a task prompt and publicly accessible resources (documentation, repositories, the web), with no target-task supervision during learning. - Why it's important: Real deployed agents rarely have access to curated skill libraries, successful execution traces, or reward oracles, yet all prior self-evolving agent systems assume at least one of these; OpenSkill is the first to close that gap under a formally enforced no-supervision constraint. - How it does it: A deep-research agent retrieves grounded knowledge and independently verifiable anchors from the open web; a separate LLM verifier synthesizes deterministic pytest assertions from those anchors (never from GT tests) to form a proxy test suite; the agent refines its skill files against that proxy in an iterative sandbox loop before zero-shot deployment. - The good: On SkillsBench (84 tasks, 11 domains), OpenSkill achieves 43.6% / 42.1% pass rate on Opus 4.6 / GPT 5.2 versus the strongest baselines' 34.7% / 33.3%, approaching the human ceiling of 44.5% / 44.8%, while skills generated by one model transfer to four weaker models with 5.5–14.8 pp gains and zero retraining. - The missing: The Manufacturing domain collapses to 0% for every automated method including OpenSkill without explanation; verifier precision is only 56.9% (60.7% overall agreement with GT outcomes), and the key ablations are performed on a single benchmark-agent pair (SocialMaze, Opus 4.6). - Read it or not: Researchers building agents that must adapt to new domains at deployment should read this—the supervision-free formulation is principled, the leakage audit is unusually thorough, and the cross-model transfer result is directly practical—but the ~\$1,800-per-benchmark compute cost and unexplained Manufacturing failure warrant caution before adoption. Problem & Motivation LLM agents deployed to solve open-ended tasks (software engineering, scientific analysis, cybersecurity) require procedural knowledge that changes across environments and task types. Existing self-evolving systems—Voyager, Reflexion, AutoSkill, SkillRL, Skill Creator—assume access to at least one of: human-curated skills, interaction traces from successful episodes, or a reward/verifier signal during the learning phase. In real deployments none of these may be available; the agent receives only a natural-language task instruction. A harder structural gap also exists: prior self-improvement loops that do use external feedback risk leaking target-task supervision if that feedback is derived from ground-truth tests or solutions. No prior work simultaneously handles cold-start skill acquisition from the open web and builds a supervision-free verification signal, making iterative self-improvement without a grader impossible outside curated benchmarks. Key Idea The core technical insight is that the public internet contains not only the domain knowledge needed to construct skills but also independently verifiable facts—known dataset statistics, documented API output formats, reference values for standard computations—that can anchor a proxy test suite without ever touching hidden ground-truth answers. By retrieving these two orthogonal knowledge types (skill content and verification anchors) in separate passes, OpenSkill builds a self-contained learning loop: the verification anchors ground deterministic pytest assertions that serve as a reliable proxy verifier, and the same open-world resources that teach the skill also supply the criteria for judging it, closing the loop with no target-task supervision. Methodology The pipeline runs in three stages. Stage 1 (Open-World Knowledge Acquisition): An LLM Agent Planner submits a sanitized query (benchmark name filtered out) to a commercial deep-research agent (Gemini deep-research-pro-preview-12-2025, polled asynchronously up to 3600 s) to retrieve background knowledge k_i; a second, lighter pass (gemini-3.1-flash-lite, temperature 0.3) retrieves verification knowledge k_v_i consisting of four anchor classes: hand-computable reference values, dataset statistical invariants (row counts, value ranges, sum-to-one constraints), cross-validation procedures, and known input–output pairs from published benchmarks. A Skill Planner then decomposes k_i into 1–4 SKILL.md documents. Stage 2 (Leakage-Free Skill Evolution): An Independent Verifier—a separate LLM session sharing the Docker container but not the skill creator's conversation—generates a deterministic pytest suite from k_v_i without seeing the GT test directory (tests/ and solution/ are never mounted). The skill creator iterates up to J=3 rounds: when the virtual pass rate r̃ < 1, a Gemini-based LLM classifier diagnoses each failure as SELF-FIXABLE (implementation bug) or NEEDS-DR (knowledge gap), triggering targeted web retrieval in the latter case (capped at 3 searches per task). Stage 3 (Zero-Shot Evaluation): The frozen skill set is copied to the target agent's workspace and evaluated under 5 independent runs against the hidden GT test suite. Three critical design decisions: (1) the leakage barrier is enforced at four independent layers (code API, container filesystem, GT oracle bypass, prompt instructions), which is unusually rigorous and well-justified; (2) deterministic equality assertions anchored to independently verifiable facts are a principled choice but restrict coverage to what the open web can confirm, explaining the 11.1% uncovered GT intents; (3) the J=3 refinement cap is empirically validated via ablation showing performance degrades at J=5 and J=10, suggesting proxy overfitting. Key Results On SkillsBench (primary benchmark, 84 tasks, 11 domains): Opus 4.6—OpenSkill 43.6% vs. Skill-Creator 34.7% (+8.9 pp), Self-Gen 23.9%, AutoSkill 24.7%, Memento 30.1%; human ceiling 44.5%. GPT 5.2—OpenSkill 42.1% vs. CoT 33.3% (+8.8 pp), Skill-Creator 29.2%, AutoSkill 11.2%, Memento 15.6%; human ceiling 44.8%. OpenSkill is best or tied-best in 8/11 domains on Opus 4.6, 7/11 on GPT 5.2. Largest domain gains—Opus: Health 69.6% (vs. 31.2% next best), Software 59.9%; GPT: Energy 80.0% (above human at 53.3%), Cybersecurity 52.5%. Manufacturing collapses to 0% for all automated methods on both agents. On SocialMaze: 82.7% (Opus) and 70.7% (GPT), +0.9 to +2.2 pp over best baselines. On ScienceWorld: 90.0% (Opus) and 85.3% (GPT). Cross-model transfer (Opus 4.6 skills deployed without adaptation on Haiku 4.5, Qwen 3 Coder, DeepSeek V3, Mistral Large 3): improvements over no-skill of 5.5–14.8 pp; AutoSkill skills transfer worse than no-skill on the same models. Virtual verifier quality: 88.9% coverage of GT test intents (120/135) on 15 sampled tasks; 56.9% precision, 80.5% recall, 60.7% agreement with GT on N=84 tasks; Fisher's exact test OR=2.97, p=0.035. Ablation on SocialMaze: open-world query alone +6.1 pp, virtual verifier alone +6.3 pp, both combined +8.2 pp over parametric-only baseline. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM + NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The open-world self-evolution problem formulation—building both skills and verification signal from public resources with no target-task supervision—is a genuinely new setting with a formal definition and an enforced leakage constraint. The specific coupling of deep-web retrieval → skill synthesis → verification-anchor-grounded proxy tests is new as a complete system; individual components (RAG, iterative skill refinement, self-generated test suites) each exist in prior work, but their integration to realize a supervision-free learning loop is the genuine contribution. Code and pipeline are publicly released. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover iteration count (J ∈ {1,3,5,10}) and component contribution (no components, +VV, +DR, +both), but exclusively on SocialMaze with Opus 4.6—a single benchmark-agent pair—raising questions about generality of the J=3 optimum. No error bars or significance tests are reported on the main SkillsBench and ScienceWorld tables; the 5 independent evaluation runs per task reduce variance but no confidence intervals are given. The virtual verifier alignment study uses Fisher's exact test (p=0.035), providing appropriate statistical grounding for the proxy signal claim. Code is released at https://github.com/OpenLAIR/OpenSkill. A methodological concern: third-party baselines (Skill Creator, AutoSkill, Memento) use claude-opus-4-7 as their backbone, while OpenSkill uses claude-opus-4-6; the version difference is documented but not ablated, and could introduce minor confounds. The primary benchmark (SkillsBench) is itself a 2026 preprint specifically designed so skill quality is the bottleneck, which may inflate OpenSkill's relative advantage on it. This is a preprint; results and conclusions have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (a built pipeline evaluated by pass rate on agentic benchmarks). - Practical: Requires access to Gemini deep-research-pro (commercial API), Claude Opus 4.6 or GPT 5.2, and Docker; per-task cost is ~1.14M tokens and ~131 minutes of wall-clock time, totaling ~\$1,800 at list pricing for the 84-task SkillsBench run—prohibitive for most academic labs but potentially acceptable as a one-time skill-creation cost. - Efficient: Skill creation dominates token budget (749K of 1.14M tokens) but skill reuse across models incurs zero creation cost; evaluation-only median run time (368.2 s) is comparable to the no-skill baseline (347.6 s), making deployed skill use cost-neutral at inference time. - Effective: Achieves 43.6%/42.1% vs. human ceiling 44.5%/44.8% on SkillsBench, with consistent gains across two model families and three benchmarks; the complete Manufacturing failure (0%) indicates a hard and unresolved failure mode for at least one task type. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 84 tasks; the one-time creation + reuse argument is plausible but unvalidated at scale; the deep-research API imposes a 3600 s hard timeout per task, which limits throughput. Delivers a software artifact: GitHub repository with Docker-containerized pipeline and SKILL.md skill files as portable deployment artifacts. Strengths - The leakage-free constraint is formally defined and audited at four independent layers (code API, container filesystem, GT oracle bypass, prompt-level instructions), making the no-supervision claim unusually credible compared to prior self-evolving agent work. - The virtual verifier's reliance on independently verifiable anchors (reference values, statistical invariants) rather than LLM-generated guesses is a principled mechanism that achieves 88.9% GT intent coverage without accessing GT tests—a concrete empirical validation of the proxy signal's quality. - Cross-model transferability (same SKILL.md files improve four weaker models by 5.5–14.8 pp with zero retraining) validates the model-agnostic artifact design and provides an immediately practical deployment path. - The breadth of evaluation—two architecturally distinct LLM families, three benchmarks spanning agentic coding, social reasoning, and interactive science, seven automated baselines with identical evaluation harnesses—sets a high bar for comparative rigor in this space. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly flag three limitations: (1) open-world sources may be noisy, outdated, or contradictory, requiring provenance tracking; (2) virtual tasks may overestimate skill quality if too easy, or reintroduce supervision if inadvertently derived from hidden answers; (3) open-world retrieval increases cost and latency over closed-world methods. These are stated directly in a dedicated section, which is a credibility positive. The authors do not acknowledge the Manufacturing domain failure, the verifier's 56.9% precision, or the narrow scope of ablations as limitations. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Manufacturing domain collapse to 0% for every automated method is never diagnosed; no ablation, case study, or hypothesis is offered, leaving a visible failure mode entirely unexplained. - Virtual verifier precision of 56.9% (29.76% of all tasks are false positives: proxy pass, GT fail) is not treated as a limitation; roughly one-third of skills that pass virtual tests fail GT evaluation, and it is unclear whether the J=3 refinement cap adequately contains the resulting proxy overfitting. - Component ablations are performed only on SocialMaze (Opus 4.6); whether the optimal J=3 and the +8.2 pp combined gain replicate on SkillsBench or with GPT 5.2 is not shown. - The backbone version discrepancy (baselines: claude-opus-4-7; OpenSkill: claude-opus-4-6) is documented but unablated; a version-matched baseline would strengthen causal claims. - SkillsBench is specifically engineered so skill quality is the bottleneck, creating a potential circularity: the primary benchmark is designed to reward exactly what OpenSkill optimizes, and it is a 2026 preprint rather than an established community standard. - No wall-clock or token breakdown is reported for baselines, making it impossible to evaluate whether the +8.9 pp gain justifies the roughly 10–100× increase in compute cost over closed-world methods. Bottom Line A practitioner building agents that must adapt to new task domains at deployment time will gain genuine, actionable insight from OpenSkill's supervision-free formulation, leakage audit methodology, and cross-model transfer results—but should weigh the substantial compute cost, the unresolved Manufacturing failure, and the noisy proxy signal against the performance gains before committing to the approach.

SpenseGPT: Practical One-shot Pruning Enabling Sparse and Dense GEMMs for LLM Inference

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Spense, a hybrid sparse-dense weight format that splits each LLM weight matrix into a 2:4 sparse region and a contiguous dense region, along with two one-shot post-training pruning algorithms (SpenseGPT and SpenseGPT+) that populate this format without requiring custom compilers or activation expansion. - Why it's important: Semi-structured 2:4 sparsity is natively accelerated on NVIDIA B200, AMD, and Meta MTIA hardware, but its strict 50% constraint degrades accuracy on challenging reasoning tasks without retraining — blocking practical deployment of sparse inference for large reasoning models. - How it does it: By exploiting the permutation-invariance of GLU-style MLP intermediate indices, Spense rearranges selected "dense" indices into a contiguous block, enabling execution via unmodified cuSPARSELt and cuBLASLt kernels, while SpenseGPT+ scores index importance using a Wanda-style activation-weighted magnitude estimator aggregated across all three MLP projections. - The good: SpenseGPT+ at 25% MLP sparsity on Seed-OSS-36B reaches average score 73.12 vs. dense baseline 73.86 across AIME/GPQA/IFEval/LCB, while achieving up to 1.2× end-to-end VLLM decoding speedup on B200 with FP8 — the first demonstrated one-shot pruning with real serving-level speedup on B200 while preserving model quality. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to two model families on a single GPU generation (B200) with a single serving stack (VLLM/FP8); attention layers are left unpruned, capping the maximum achievable sparsity benefit, and no analysis of calibration-distribution sensitivity is quantified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems engineers and LLM inference practitioners targeting NVIDIA Blackwell or AMD hardware who need a practical, library-compatible path to sparse LLM deployment without retraining.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Spense, a hybrid sparse-dense weight format that splits each LLM weight matrix into a 2:4 sparse region and a contiguous dense region, along with two one-shot post-training pruning algorithms (SpenseGPT and SpenseGPT+) that populate this format without requiring custom compilers or activation expansion. - Why it's important: Semi-structured 2:4 sparsity is natively accelerated on NVIDIA B200, AMD, and Meta MTIA hardware, but its strict 50% constraint degrades accuracy on challenging reasoning tasks without retraining — blocking practical deployment of sparse inference for large reasoning models. - How it does it: By exploiting the permutation-invariance of GLU-style MLP intermediate indices, Spense rearranges selected "dense" indices into a contiguous block, enabling execution via unmodified cuSPARSELt and cuBLASLt kernels, while SpenseGPT+ scores index importance using a Wanda-style activation-weighted magnitude estimator aggregated across all three MLP projections. - The good: SpenseGPT+ at 25% MLP sparsity on Seed-OSS-36B reaches average score 73.12 vs. dense baseline 73.86 across AIME/GPQA/IFEval/LCB, while achieving up to 1.2× end-to-end VLLM decoding speedup on B200 with FP8 — the first demonstrated one-shot pruning with real serving-level speedup on B200 while preserving model quality. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to two model families on a single GPU generation (B200) with a single serving stack (VLLM/FP8); attention layers are left unpruned, capping the maximum achievable sparsity benefit, and no analysis of calibration-distribution sensitivity is quantified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems engineers and LLM inference practitioners targeting NVIDIA Blackwell or AMD hardware who need a practical, library-compatible path to sparse LLM deployment without retraining. Problem & Motivation: Large-scale LLM inference on modern accelerators is memory-bandwidth-bound, and hardware vendors have built sparse tensor core support (2:4 semi-structured sparsity) into NVIDIA B200, AMD, and Meta MTIA chips that can halve effective memory bandwidth and deliver up to 2× GEMM speedup. The barrier to practical adoption is the strict 50%-per-group constraint: post-training pruning to 2:4 without retraining causes significant accuracy degradation on demanding reasoning benchmarks (SparseGPT on Seed-OSS-36B drops avg from 73.86 to 65.18; Wanda and HyperPrune collapse to ~10 avg score). Existing relaxed-sparsity approaches that soften this constraint are either GPU-generation-specific (PATCH requires Triton for Ampere only) or introduce input-activation expansion overhead that eliminates the latency benefit (SlideSparse achieves only 1.04× end-to-end speedup at 25% sparsity on B200). Key Idea: The core insight is that the intermediate dimension of GLU-style MLPs is permutation-invariant — any reordering of (gate, up, down) projection index triplets leaves the MLP output unchanged. This means "important" indices (those that would cause large reconstruction error if compressed) can be identified, gathered into a contiguous block, left dense, and the remaining indices pruned to 2:4 — all while the resulting layout maps cleanly onto two sequential standard GEMM calls (one sparse via cuSPARSELt, one dense via cuBLASLt) with zero runtime overhead beyond the two GEMMs already required. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. First, the Spense format definition: a weight matrix W is split into a contiguous p% dense region and a (100−p)% region compressed to 2:4 sparsity. Two variants handle output-split (for Wgate, Wup) and input-split with accumulation (for Wdown). Second, dense-index selection: SpenseGPT trivially selects the last p% of indices; SpenseGPT+ assigns each intermediate index an importance score by combining normalized Wanda-style scores (|W_ij| * sqrt(H_jj), averaged per row/column) from all three projections and selecting top-pN indices. Third, SparseGPT (OBS-based second-order reconstruction) is applied to the sparse regions, with dense indices placed on the right/bottom to serve as a compensation buffer during left-to-right column processing. Critical design decisions: (a) using Wanda-style per-index scores that assume independence across indices rather than SparseGPT-like scores that assume inter-weight dependencies — ablation shows SparseGPT-based selection achieves avg 27.79 vs. 70.76 for SpenseGPT+, validating this choice; (b) placing dense indices at the back rather than front or in-place — ablation shows front/in-place placement yields avg ~29, back yields 70.76; (c) pruning only MLP layers, leaving attention untouched. Key Results: On Qwen3-32B and Seed-OSS-36B (32B–36B parameter reasoning models), evaluated on AIME2024, GPQA Diamond, IFEval, and LiveCodeBench v6. At 25% MLP sparsity, SpenseGPT+ reaches average scores of 70.76 (Qwen3-32B, dense: 71.68) and 73.12 (Seed-OSS-36B, dense: 73.86), near-lossless compression. SparseGPT at 50% reaches only 60.27 and 65.18. End-to-end VLLM decoding speedup on B200 with FP8: Spense achieves up to 1.2× vs. dense baseline; SlideSparse achieves only ~1.04× at the same sparsity target. Pruning time: SpenseGPT takes 1.33–1.52h (same as SparseGPT); SpenseGPT+ takes 1.87–2.15h on a single B200 GPU. ProxSparse OOMs at this scale. The dramatic sensitivity of AIME score to dense-index placement (76.67 vs. 8.89 with different strategies at the same sparsity) is a notable and counter-intuitive finding. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The Spense format is a new weight storage and execution format; SpenseGPT+ introduces a new importance-scoring heuristic for hybrid sparse-dense index selection. The permutation-invariance observation that enables contiguous dense regions is the genuinely novel element; the underlying pruning engine (SparseGPT + OBS) and the importance-scoring style (Wanda) are existing techniques applied within this new framework. Rigor & Reproducibility: Experiments are run three times with different random seeds and averages are reported, which is appropriate for stochastic reasoning benchmarks. Ablations cover both the scoring strategy and the placement strategy, with clean quantitative comparisons. The baseline set (SparseGPT, Wanda, HyperPrune) is current and relevant; ProxSparse is included but OOMs, which is an honest result. No code or model weights are released (not stated in the paper). The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.10445v1, June 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Evaluation uses EVALSCOPE rather than the standard lm-evaluation-harness, with a footnote acknowledging that harness results for Qwen3-32B were anomalously low — this is a mild reproducibility concern since comparisons against prior work that used different harnesses are not directly validated. Deployability (EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS): - Practical: Runs on a single B200 GPU using standard VLLM with cuSPARSELt and cuBLASLt — both shipping libraries on commercially available NVIDIA hardware; AMD support via hipSPARSELt/hipBLASLt is claimed but not experimentally validated. - Efficient: SpenseGPT+ pruning takes under 2.2h on a single B200 for 32–36B models; runtime inference reduces GEMM cost while adding negligible non-GEMM overhead, in contrast to SlideSparse's activation-expansion overhead that erodes gains at large batch sizes. - Effective: At 25% MLP sparsity, the method preserves average benchmark scores within 1% of the dense baseline on two distinct model families across reasoning, code, and instruction-following tasks; the 1.2× end-to-end speedup is measured in a realistic serving setup (batch=32, seqlen=4096, VLLM). - Scalable: Tested only on 32–36B models; no experiments on smaller or larger models, and no analysis of how accuracy-sparsity tradeoffs change with model scale. Attention layers are excluded from pruning, so the method's benefit is bounded by MLP fraction of total compute. The paper stops at paper form with no released software artifact. Strengths: - The permutation-invariance insight for GLU MLPs is clean and enables a format that requires zero changes to inference kernels — a genuine practical contribution over prior hybrid-sparsity work. - The ablation revealing that placement direction (back vs. front vs. in-place) causes a 70-point AIME swing is a sharp empirical finding that illuminates a subtle interaction between SparseGPT's left-to-right column processing and dense-index placement, providing mechanistic understanding rather than just empirical tuning. - Demonstrated real end-to-end speedup on B200 — a currently deployed, commercially available GPU — closing the gap between theoretical sparse-tensor-core potential and actual serving latency. - Pruning cost (≤2.15h on one GPU) is practical for production model owners. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that (1) speedup depends on the underlying GEMM library efficiency and will vary with batch size, sequence length, hardware backend, and serving configuration; (2) SpenseGPT+ is sensitive to calibration distribution mismatch; and (3) evaluation on non-B200 accelerators (AMD, Meta MTIA) is absent. These are confronted honestly and briefly in a dedicated Limitations section. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Attention layers are excluded from pruning without ablation or justification — for models where attention GEMMs are a significant fraction of total compute, this caps the method's practical benefit and should be quantified. The 1.2× speedup figure is measured at batch=32, seqlen=4096; Figure 1 shows sparse GEMM underperforms dense at small M values, so the speedup under decode-phase autoregressive conditions (batch=1, M=1) would likely be much smaller or negative, yet this is not reported. The Wanda-style independence assumption for SpenseGPT+ scores is acknowledged but not quantified — it is unclear how much accuracy is left on the table compared to an oracle dense-index selection. No cross-model generalization study (e.g., Llama, Mistral families) is presented, limiting confidence in the method's breadth. Bottom Line: A practitioner deploying 30–40B reasoning LLMs on NVIDIA B200 hardware gains a concrete, library-compatible path to ~1.2× serving speedup with near-zero accuracy loss in under 2.2 GPU-hours — making this paper directly actionable for LLM inference optimization work targeting Blackwell-generation hardware.

Simulating Human Memory with Language Models

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a 10-task psychology-derived benchmark with human reference data (N=50) and demonstrates that COMPACTOR — an LLM agent constrained to a 4-slot key-value working memory store — produces score distributions significantly closer to human memory performance than any prompting strategy across nine diverse LLMs. - Why it's important: LLMs deployed as user simulators achieve near-perfect memory on tasks where humans systematically fail, causing simulators to overestimate human retention and producing AI assistants poorly calibrated for teaching, coaching, or interaction with real people. - How it does it: COMPACTOR forces the model to encode its full input into exactly 4 keyed chunks (mirroring Cowan's 4-chunk working memory capacity) via tool calls, then answers exclusively from those chunks — an architectural information bottleneck rather than an instructed one. - The good: COMPACTOR yields humanlikeness gains of up to +0.61 Wasserstein similarity on digit span (Qwen3-8B Standard) and consistent improvements across working-memory tasks and all nine models, while prompting strategies produce near-zero change on most tasks. - The missing: Even the best COMPACTOR condition (Llama 3 8B) achieves less than 70% pairwise reranking accuracy on the educational document task, and COMPACTOR's detailed error patterns diverge markedly from human forgetting patterns (conditional error rate 94.1% vs. 71.2% for humans). - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building LLM user simulators or studying cognitive alignment; practitioners in tutoring or personalization systems will find COMPACTOR directly applicable, though the downstream task results are too preliminary to drive design decisions.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a 10-task psychology-derived benchmark with human reference data (N=50) and demonstrates that COMPACTOR — an LLM agent constrained to a 4-slot key-value working memory store — produces score distributions significantly closer to human memory performance than any prompting strategy across nine diverse LLMs. - Why it's important: LLMs deployed as user simulators achieve near-perfect memory on tasks where humans systematically fail, causing simulators to overestimate human retention and producing AI assistants poorly calibrated for teaching, coaching, or interaction with real people. - How it does it: COMPACTOR forces the model to encode its full input into exactly 4 keyed chunks (mirroring Cowan's 4-chunk working memory capacity) via tool calls, then answers exclusively from those chunks — an architectural information bottleneck rather than an instructed one. - The good: COMPACTOR yields humanlikeness gains of up to +0.61 Wasserstein similarity on digit span (Qwen3-8B Standard) and consistent improvements across working-memory tasks and all nine models, while prompting strategies produce near-zero change on most tasks. - The missing: Even the best COMPACTOR condition (Llama 3 8B) achieves less than 70% pairwise reranking accuracy on the educational document task, and COMPACTOR's detailed error patterns diverge markedly from human forgetting patterns (conditional error rate 94.1% vs. 71.2% for humans). - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building LLM user simulators or studying cognitive alignment; practitioners in tutoring or personalization systems will find COMPACTOR directly applicable, though the downstream task results are too preliminary to drive design decisions. Problem & Motivation LLMs are increasingly used as stand-in human participants in user simulation pipelines — for training AI assistants, evaluating tutoring systems, and running large-scale behavioral experiments. But current LLMs perform at ceiling on memory tasks where humans show systematic, well-characterized failures: a typical human recalls 6.88 digits in a digit span task while tested LLMs recall up to 20. This mismatch means simulators trained or evaluated against LLM stand-ins receive feedback calibrated to superhuman memory, yielding assistants that overestimate user retention. Prior work (de Langis et al., EACL 2026) had characterized LLM memory superiority without attempting to correct it; the user simulation literature (Zhou et al., 2026) had flagged the sim2real gap as a limiting factor for training but lacked targeted interventions for memory specifically. Key Idea Human working memory is capacity-limited to approximately 4 chunks (Cowan 2001), and this bottleneck must be enforced structurally rather than instructed linguistically. Telling a model "you have limited memory" does not degrade its recall; constraining it to exactly 4 key-value slots and allowing it to answer only from those slots does. The key insight is that the information bottleneck must be architectural — not a prompt instruction — to produce systematic forgetting. Methodology The paper has two interleaved components: benchmark construction with human data collection, and evaluation of four intervention conditions. Benchmark: Ten tasks adapted from cognitive psychology covering working memory (digit span, reverse digit span, N-back, word recognition, variable mapping), episodic memory (factual QA from Wikipedia passages processed by GPT-5.1, narrative QA from LLM-generated stories, narrative free recall from the Naturalistic Free Recall dataset), and relational memory (map task and craft task, both generated by GPT-5.1). All tasks are text-in/text-out to permit direct human–LLM comparison. Human data: N=50 Prolific participants (US, native English, 95–100% approval rate, $20 flat rate, IRB-approved), randomized task order, one-hour session. Human-likeness is measured as 1 − W1 where W1 is the normalized 1D Wasserstein distance between human and LLM score distributions. Interventions: (1) TASKPR — vanilla task description; (2) HUMPR — explicit instruction to simulate a human participant in a psychology experiment; (3) MEMPR — HUMPR plus an explicit reminder that humans have limited memory and make mistakes; (4) COMPACTOR — LLM agent with two tool functions (write_memory(key, value) limited to K=4 slots; delete_key(key)), encode phase reads full input and populates the store, recall phase answers from store contents only with no access to the original material. Nine models evaluated: GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.6, Llama 3 8B and 3.3 70B Instruct, Qwen3-8B (Standard and Thinking), Qwen3-30B-A3B (Instruct and Thinking), Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct. 50 trials per model per task per condition. Three critical design decisions: (1) K=4 is grounded in cognitive science and well-justified. (2) Key-value structured memory vs. free-form summary is ablated (TASKSUM, HUMSUM vs. COMPACTOR) — COMPACTOR wins, isolating the bounded slot structure as the operative ingredient rather than compression alone. (3) Wasserstein distance for cross-task aggregation is principled, though it collapses distributional shape into a scalar and does not capture whether the model forgets the same things humans do. Key Results Baseline (TASKPR): All nine models reach ceiling on all ten tasks. Digit span: all models = 20 (maximum tested) vs. human mean 6.88. Word recognition: all models = 100 correct vs. human mean 34.49. Prompting (HUMPR, MEMPR): Near-zero effect for most tasks and models. ΔHUMPR = 0 for digit span across all models. MEMPR occasionally hurts (variable mapping: ΔMEMPR = −0.13 for GPT-5.4). Only narrative free recall shows consistent gains from MEMPR: +0.16 for GPT-5.4, +0.32 for Qwen3-8B Standard, +0.30 for Qwen3-Next-80B. COMPACTOR: Large, consistent gains on working-memory tasks. Digit span: ΔCOMPACTOR reaches +0.61 (Qwen3-8B Standard). Word recognition: up to ΔCOMPACTOR = +0.26. Average ΔCOMPACTor across all tasks ranges from +0.054 (Llama 3 8B) to +0.263 (Qwen3-8B Thinking). For longer-term memory tasks (factual QA, narrative QA), gains are more variable: ΔCOMPACTor = 0 for Claude Opus 4.6 on factual QA but +0.287 for Llama 3 8B. Few-shot analysis (GPT-5.4, digit span + word recognition): In-domain human examples substantially improve humanlikeness; out-of-domain examples from a different working-memory task yield no improvement over TASKPR, ruling out generic exposure to human-like errors as the mechanism. Error pattern analysis (digit span): Even when overall distributions match, COMPACTOR produces error patterns unlike humans. MEMPR models match the true span length on 87% of incorrect trials vs. 55% for humans. COMPACTOR frequently truncates sequences from the end. Conditional error rate p(err|prev wrong) = 71.2% for humans, 56.1% for MEMPR, 94.1% for COMPACTOR. Educational reranking (N=100 humans, 10 biographies × 4 reading conditions): Llama 3 8B with COMPACTOR achieves +0.180 absolute gain in pairwise reranking accuracy vs. TASKPR and +0.147 vs. random selection; gains are inconsistent across models; best model stays below 70% accuracy. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The 10-task benchmark with IRB-approved human reference data is a genuine contribution not previously available. COMPACTOR is a novel (though mechanically simple) cognitive-science-grounded architectural intervention for LLM user simulation. The empirical finding that structural memory bottlenecks consistently outperform prompting for human-likeness across nine models and ten tasks is non-obvious and clearly established; the counter-intuitive finding that frontier models (GPT-5.4) are no better at simulating human memory than smaller models is particularly notable. Rigor & Reproducibility Code and human reference data are released at https://github.com/nickatomlin/simulating-memory. Bootstrapped 95% CIs are reported throughout; statistically significant differences from baseline are flagged in Tables 2 and 3 (, , notation). The COMPACTOR ablation against free-form summarizers (TASKSUM, HUMSUM) is well-designed and isolates the structured-capacity ingredient. Fifty participants and 50 model trials per condition is adequate but not large; some tasks have high variance. Key methodological concerns: (1) Factual QA content is generated from Wikipedia by GPT-5.1, raising contamination risk for GPT-5.4 and other models that may have seen highly similar content during training — this could artificially suppress the gap on QA tasks. (2) Time pressure is simulated for LLMs by instruction ("the human has three minutes to read") rather than enforced; it is unclear this meaningfully changes model behavior. (3) Human tasks were adapted from auditory/manual-response paradigms to visual text presentation — this changes the nature of the task, and the paper acknowledges only a partial version of this concern. (4) Variable mapping allows humans three attempts (best kept); models get one — a slight asymmetry not flagged by the authors. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — methods, experiments evaluated by both human participants and LLM inference. - Practical: COMPACTOR requires only API access to any tool-calling LLM; no special hardware, fine-tuning, or local deployment is needed. - Efficient: Each task instance requires two LLM calls (encode + recall) plus tool-call overhead; roughly 2–3× the cost of baseline single-call inference, which is acceptable for user simulation use cases. - Effective: COMPACTOR substantially improves human-likeness on working-memory tasks but shows inconsistent gains on longer-term memory tasks and produces error patterns that still diverge from human forgetting at the fine-grained level. - Scalable: Tested only on short, controlled tasks with small stimulus sets; no evidence on whether the 4-slot constraint remains effective for multi-turn interactions, longer documents, or more complex memory demands. The work delivers a software benchmark artifact (code + human data at GitHub) and a proof-of-concept COMPACTOR implementation; no trained model weights or fine-tuned checkpoints are released. Strengths - Principled grounding in cognitive science: the task battery, the 4-chunk capacity constraint (Cowan 2001), and the Wasserstein metric for distributional comparison are all carefully motivated, not post-hoc choices. - Breadth of LLM coverage across model families, sizes, reasoning modes, and open/closed weights produces consistent results that are unlikely to be artifacts of a single model's idiosyncrasies. - The ablation study (structured key-value vs. free-form summary) cleanly isolates what makes COMPACTOR work, ruling out the simpler hypothesis that any compression before answering would suffice. - Release of human reference data enables future methods to be benchmarked without re-running the expensive human experiment. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) the benchmark does not cover semantic or visual memory, intentionally restricted to text-in/text-out tasks for comparability; (2) COMPACTOR's score-distribution alignment does not imply distributional human-likeness at the individual-response level, and techniques like verbalized sampling or persona conditioning could address this. These are acknowledged directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - LLM contamination in factual QA: content is Wikipedia-derived and processed by GPT-5.1; GPT-5.4 and other models trained on large web corpora may have seen overlapping material, artificially widening the measured human-LLM gap on that specific task and making COMPACTOR's partial improvement on factual QA harder to interpret. - Temporal constraint validity: HUMPR and MEMPR include statements like "the human has three minutes to read" but no mechanism forces the LLM to simulate reading speed; whether this instruction has any effect on model behavior is untested and likely negligible. - Error pattern analysis is limited to digit span (§6.2); it is unknown whether the observed mismatch in forgetting patterns generalizes across all ten tasks, which would be important for assessing whether COMPACTOR truly simulates human memory or merely matches aggregate score distributions. - The educational reranking corpus is fully synthetic (10 LLM-generated biographies); generalization to naturalistic educational materials with real reading-level variation is untested. - Sensitivity of COMPACTOR to prompt wording for the encode phase is not evaluated; the current prompt is carefully crafted with cognitive-science terminology ("meaningful chunks", "NEVER pack a long run") and may not be robust to simpler phrasings. Bottom Line This preprint makes a genuine empirical contribution by establishing that architectural memory bottlenecks outperform prompting for human simulation across a well-designed benchmark, and the released data and code make it directly useful — but the downstream application evidence is too thin and the error-pattern mismatches too large for the technique to be considered a solved problem rather than a promising first step.

Can Transformers Learn to Verify During Backtracking Search?

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Selective State Attention (SSA), a fixed attention mask that structurally enforces state-equivalence invariance in decoder-only transformers trained on cumulative backtracking-search traces, enabling history-invariant reactive verification without changing training data, objective, or parameters. - Why it's important: Any autoregressive model whose context accumulates a record of its own past actions faces the same failure: next-token training on cumulative traces admits predictors that exploit trajectory features rather than current state, causing the continue-or-backtrack decision to flip on identical states reached by different paths — a failure that silently undermines neural search and LLM chain-of-thought reasoning. - How it does it: SSA zeros attention from each decision block to all prior decision blocks while preserving full access to the problem prefix, combined with block-relative positional encoding that resets position indices at each block boundary, so logits inside the current block are provably invariant to prior history content. - The good: On 28,420 same-state transplant pairs across five seeds, SSA produces identical decisions to floating-point precision (100% argmax agreement, mean symmetric KL = 5.6×10⁻¹⁴) while a cumulative-trained causal baseline agrees only 71.44% ± 7.62% of the time; under state-rebuilt inference (history discarded at deployment) SSA achieves 93.4% ± 2.3% solve rate on 3-SAT n=50 versus 4.8% ± 1.2% for causal. - The missing: All experiments use a 6M-parameter model on instances (n=50 SAT, n=30 GC, 5–7 block planning) that rule-based heuristics solve at 100% with the same symbolic infrastructure — the gap from diagnostic to practical solver is not closed, and extension to pretrained LLMs is suggested but not demonstrated. - Read it or not: Researchers building neural search systems, LLM backtracking pipelines, or process reward models should read this — the diagnosis is formal, the fix is parameter-free, and the two-piece prescription (localize the state in the trace; isolate it in attention) is immediately applicable.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Selective State Attention (SSA), a fixed attention mask that structurally enforces state-equivalence invariance in decoder-only transformers trained on cumulative backtracking-search traces, enabling history-invariant reactive verification without changing training data, objective, or parameters. - Why it's important: Any autoregressive model whose context accumulates a record of its own past actions faces the same failure: next-token training on cumulative traces admits predictors that exploit trajectory features rather than current state, causing the continue-or-backtrack decision to flip on identical states reached by different paths — a failure that silently undermines neural search and LLM chain-of-thought reasoning. - How it does it: SSA zeros attention from each decision block to all prior decision blocks while preserving full access to the problem prefix, combined with block-relative positional encoding that resets position indices at each block boundary, so logits inside the current block are provably invariant to prior history content. - The good: On 28,420 same-state transplant pairs across five seeds, SSA produces identical decisions to floating-point precision (100% argmax agreement, mean symmetric KL = 5.6×10⁻¹⁴) while a cumulative-trained causal baseline agrees only 71.44% ± 7.62% of the time; under state-rebuilt inference (history discarded at deployment) SSA achieves 93.4% ± 2.3% solve rate on 3-SAT n=50 versus 4.8% ± 1.2% for causal. - The missing: All experiments use a 6M-parameter model on instances (n=50 SAT, n=30 GC, 5–7 block planning) that rule-based heuristics solve at 100% with the same symbolic infrastructure — the gap from diagnostic to practical solver is not closed, and extension to pretrained LLMs is suggested but not demonstrated. - Read it or not: Researchers building neural search systems, LLM backtracking pipelines, or process reward models should read this — the diagnosis is formal, the fix is parameter-free, and the two-piece prescription (localize the state in the trace; isolate it in attention) is immediately applicable. Problem & Motivation Backtracking search underlies constraint solvers, planners, and theorem provers. Recent transformer-based reasoning systems (Tree of Thoughts, Self-Backtracking, chain-of-thought scratchpads) replicate the same pattern autoregressively, training on cumulative solver traces under a next-token objective. The optimal continue-or-backtrack verifier is a function of the current search state alone: two trajectories reaching the same propagated state admit identical viable continuations, so trajectory history is irrelevant to the Bayes-optimal decision. A small MLP on per-variable state features solves the 3-SAT verification task at near-ceiling accuracy, confirming the target is state-local. Yet the next-token training objective admits any predictor that fits the joint distribution of states and histories, including history-dependent surrogates that fail to generalize when the trajectory is removed at deployment. The paper targets this mismatch precisely. Key Idea The key insight is that history entanglement is not a data or optimization problem but a representational one: causal attention over a cumulative trace structurally permits the logits at each step to depend on prior-block content, and no amount of data augmentation or regularization reliably eliminates this dependence. The fix must act at the attention level. By blocking cross-block attention entirely — letting each decision block attend only to the problem prefix and its own tokens — the transformer is architecturally prevented from exploiting history, and Proposition 1 guarantees exact prior-history invariance under block-relative positions. The complementary localization fix addresses a separate, orthogonal failure (scattered retrieval): rewriting the trace so that the relevant state features appear contiguously in the current block rather than distributed across the trajectory. Methodology The pipeline has three layers. First, a symbolic search infrastructure handles unit propagation, state serialization into canonical decision blocks (variable assignments, propagated domains, tried-alternative set), and backjump execution; the transformer is responsible only for emitting branch and backtrack tokens. Second, the trace is localized: each decision block explicitly encodes the current partial assignment, propagated domains, and tried alternatives (the enriched format), placing all state features needed for verification within a single block. Third, the SSA mask enforces cross-block isolation: tokens in decision block Bi attend to all of problem prefix P, all slot registers R, and causally within Bi, but not to any Bj with j≠i. Block-relative positional encoding resets position indices at each block boundary, removing the only remaining channel through which prior history could influence current-block logits. The training objective is unchanged (autoregressive next-token cross-entropy). The model is a decoder-only transformer with 6 layers, 256 hidden dimensions, 8 attention heads, and approximately 6M parameters, trained on 5,000 solver-generated traces with AdamW (lr=3×10⁻⁴). A formal aliasing–entanglement decomposition (Appendix B) places scattered retrieval in the aliasing term of the MSE decomposition and history entanglement in the entanglement term, unifying both failure modes in a single framework. The paper proves (Proposition 2) that false-prune errors compound multiplicatively along solution paths — (1−αv)^M survival probability — while false-negative errors contribute only additive overhead, establishing precision as the binding constraint. Key Results Primary domain is 3-SAT n=50 (clause-to-variable ratio α=4.0, planted instances), evaluated over 200 instances × 5 model seeds. Under state-rebuilt inference, SSA achieves 93.4% ± 2.3% solve rate versus 4.8% ± 1.2% for cumulative-trained causal (Δ = +88.6 pp). SSA trained on 5k cumulative traces matches causal trained directly on 100k state-only examples (93.0% ± 2.0%), recovering state-isolated behavior from trajectory data by changing only the attention mask. The state-feature MLP approaches the upper bound at 99.2% ± 0.8%. History-transplant test across 28,420 same-state pairs: SSA argmax agreement 100.000% ± 0.000%, mean symmetric KL 5.6×10⁻¹⁴; causal agreement 71.44% ± 7.62%, mean KL 1.26 ± 0.22. A sweep of 10 alternative interventions (block dropout, sliding window, null history, transplanted donor histories, contrastive regularization, LSTM with block masking) all collapse to near-random performance under state-rebuilt inference; only mask-level cross-block isolation restores transfer. Verifier-swap analysis: SSA + perfect oracle solves 100% of instances; cumulative causal + perfect oracle reaches only 46.3% ± 38.0%, showing history entanglement degrades the branching policy as well. On graph coloring n=30 SSA achieves 98.3% ± 0.6% versus 69.9% ± 1.8% at budget 8192; Blocks World 5-block: SSA 50.7% ± 2.9% versus causal 8.7% ± 2.4% (paired-t p=2.1×10⁻⁵); backtracking parsing: SSA maintains perfect parse success under both protocols while causal drops sharply under state-rebuilt inference. Rule-based heuristics with the same search infrastructure solve every instance at every scale tested (Appendix C.4), placing these results firmly in the diagnostic rather than solver-advance category. The paper explicitly acknowledges this scope limitation. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. SSA as a fixed cross-block isolation mask is a new method; the aliasing-entanglement decomposition and the formal identification of history entanglement as a same-state invariance failure (distinct from imitation-learning distribution shift) constitute a new empirical finding and conceptual framework. Structured attention masks are not new (Block Transformer, System 2 Attention, ETC are cited and differentiated), but applying cross-block isolation specifically to enforce state-equivalence invariance in serialized search trajectories is genuinely novel. The localization prescription (enriched trace format) is a new practical design rule. The application domains (3-SAT, graph coloring, Blocks World, parsing) are standard benchmarks applied in a new diagnostic framing. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are extensive and well-designed: a trace-format × attention-mask 3×2 factorial (Table 10) isolates localization and isolation as independently necessary; a mask × slots 2×2 factorial (Table 22) rules out slot registers as the source of gain; a mask × positional encoding 2×2 ablation (Table 23) isolates the mask's contribution; four model-level confound diagnostics (Table 8) rule out simple repetition, superficial attention effects, checkpoint artifacts, and exposure-without-isolation; a 10-intervention sweep on the verifier-only benchmark (Table 33) shows no alternative works. Error bars are reported throughout (mean ± std over 5 seeds for primary experiments). Bootstrap 95% confidence intervals are reported for key comparisons (Table 27); SSA–causal CIs exclude zero on both domains. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/phuayj/ssa-transformer under MIT license. Methodological concerns: all primary experiments use planted SAT instances (every test instance is satisfiable by construction), which may underrepresent the hardest near-transition cases; the symbolic infrastructure handles propagation and backjumping, so the model learns a narrower target than a complete solver; the paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.22221v1), self-described as a substantially extended version of IJCLR 2025 (Ribeiro et al., 2025). Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The 6M-parameter model and SSA mask run on standard GPU hardware (experiments use TSUBAME4.0); SSA requires zero additional parameters, no training-data changes, and no objective changes — it is a drop-in mask modification. - Efficient: SSA under cumulative inference has identical per-step compute cost to the causal baseline (both process the growing cumulative sequence up to Lcum ≈ 8192 tokens); state-rebuilt inference reduces per-step input to Lstate ≈ 1300 tokens and is cheaper, but requires rebuilding context at each step; the CDCL sidecar adds ~0.1 ms per decision versus ~5 ms for the neural forward pass, negligible overhead. - Effective: SSA achieves 93.4% solve rate under state-rebuilt inference, matching a causal model trained on 20× more state-only data, and the verifier-only benchmark confirms near-ceiling reactive verification accuracy with zero false prunes; proactive verification (locally consistent dead ends) is completely ineffective at the tested scale, and solve rates on the hardest phase-transition instances are substantially lower (25.6% cumulative). - Scalable: Tested only at n=50 (SAT) and n=30 (GC); rule-based heuristics solve all instances at these scales; behavior at solver-relevant scales (n=1000+ SAT, industrial benchmarks) is not evaluated and is not claimed; the paper explicitly frames results as diagnostics. The paper delivers a software artifact (code, training/evaluation scripts, table/figure generation) and a reusable architectural pattern. Strengths - The invariance guarantee is formal and exact: Proposition 1 proves that under SSA with block-relative positions, logits inside Bt depend only on (P, Bt) for any two histories, and the padding-control counterfactual verifies this empirically to floating-point precision across 1800 trials — an unusually strong verification of a structural claim. - The 10-intervention sweep eliminates all obvious alternatives (data-level, objective-level, architecture-level) before claiming that mask-level isolation is necessary, following a strong null-result methodology. - The aliasing–entanglement decomposition (Appendix B) provides a formal unifying framework with three proved theorems (Transplant Variance Identity, Information Bound on History Value, Log-Loss Characterization) that situate the empirical diagnostics in an information-theoretic context. - The practical implication — inference-time context clearing as a protocol-level analogue for pretrained LLMs — is well-motivated by the same formal analysis and does not require architectural modification of existing models. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Proactive verification (detecting locally consistent dead ends before propagation exposes a conflict) is explicitly out of scope; the paper shows learned proactive verifiers produce zero benefit at tested scale, and attributes this to class imbalance, train-deployment state-distribution mismatch, and missing features. SSA requires localized trace format as a prerequisite; it does not replace localization and is ineffective on stripped traces. Block-relative positional encoding is needed for full invariance; under standard absolute positions a small residual KL divergence remains from positional extrapolation. The symbolic infrastructure handles propagation, conflict detection, and backjumping — the model learns only the reactive control decision, not the complete search algorithm. The paper authors directly acknowledge that rule-based heuristics solve every tested instance, framing all results as architectural diagnostics rather than solver advances. These limitations are presented candidly, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps The tested instance scales (n=50 SAT, n=30 GC, 5–7 block planning) are two to four orders of magnitude smaller than practical solver benchmarks; whether history entanglement remains the binding failure mode at larger scale, or is dwarfed by capacity and length-generalization limits, is unknown and not addressed. The method's dependence on a high-quality canonical state representation (provided here by the symbolic infrastructure) is not analyzed — real LLM reasoning traces rarely have clean block boundaries or complete state serialization, and the paper acknowledges this only at a high level. No direct comparison to Self-Backtracking (Yang et al., 2026) or ASTRO (Kim et al., 2025) in terms of end-to-end benchmark performance is provided, making relative practical merit unassessable. The planted-instance evaluation protocol ensures every test instance is satisfiable; performance on UNSAT instances is largely absent. The extension to pretrained LLMs via inference-time context clearing is the paper's most consequential implied application, but no experiment demonstrates it. Bottom Line This is a technically precise paper that correctly diagnoses a structural failure mode in transformer-based search, proves its fix, and backs it with thorough ablations; practitioners building neural search systems or studying LLM backtracking should read it, though the gap from 6M-parameter symbolic-infrastructure-assisted diagnostics to practical LLM-scale search remains fully open.

AI Research Agents Narrow Scientific Exploration

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates empirically that AI research agents—across four frameworks and six LLMs generating 37,802 ideas—produce ideas that are substantially more concentrated in semantic space than human-authored research from the same fields, and remain closer to their seed literature than later human follow-on work. - Why it's important: As AI research agents become integrated into scientific workflows at scale, a systematic narrowing of the explored idea space would create a self-reinforcing loop that crowds out unconventional directions and potentially suppresses the high-variance, high-impact research that drives scientific progress. - How it does it: Citation-defined research areas are constructed via bibliographic coupling and HDBSCAN clustering; AI ideas and human papers are embedded in a shared semantic space (Qwen3-Embedding-4B) and compared along four dimensions: within-area concentration, distance from seed literature, citation impact of nearest human papers, and a research-question vs. technical-method novelty decomposition. - The good: The narrowing effect is consistent across all four agent frameworks (zero-shot, AIScientist, ResearchAgent, AgentLaboratory) and all six LLMs spanning 0.8B to 35B parameters, showing that increasing agent sophistication does not resolve the exploration deficit. - The missing: AI ideas use full proposal text while human papers are represented only by title and abstract—this representation asymmetry almost certainly inflates within-AI-idea similarity and deflates AI-to-human similarity, and the authors do not address it. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or evaluating AI-assisted ideation systems; less useful for those seeking actionable design fixes, which the paper does not provide.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates empirically that AI research agents—across four frameworks and six LLMs generating 37,802 ideas—produce ideas that are substantially more concentrated in semantic space than human-authored research from the same fields, and remain closer to their seed literature than later human follow-on work. - Why it's important: As AI research agents become integrated into scientific workflows at scale, a systematic narrowing of the explored idea space would create a self-reinforcing loop that crowds out unconventional directions and potentially suppresses the high-variance, high-impact research that drives scientific progress. - How it does it: Citation-defined research areas are constructed via bibliographic coupling and HDBSCAN clustering; AI ideas and human papers are embedded in a shared semantic space (Qwen3-Embedding-4B) and compared along four dimensions: within-area concentration, distance from seed literature, citation impact of nearest human papers, and a research-question vs. technical-method novelty decomposition. - The good: The narrowing effect is consistent across all four agent frameworks (zero-shot, AIScientist, ResearchAgent, AgentLaboratory) and all six LLMs spanning 0.8B to 35B parameters, showing that increasing agent sophistication does not resolve the exploration deficit. - The missing: AI ideas use full proposal text while human papers are represented only by title and abstract—this representation asymmetry almost certainly inflates within-AI-idea similarity and deflates AI-to-human similarity, and the authors do not address it. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or evaluating AI-assisted ideation systems; less useful for those seeking actionable design fixes, which the paper does not provide. Problem & Motivation AI research agent frameworks (AIScientist, ResearchAgent, AgentLaboratory, and others) can now generate research ideas, design experiments, and draft papers at scale, and their prompts explicitly instruct models to produce novel, high-impact, unconventional ideas. Prior evaluations of these systems focus on whether individual generated ideas are interesting, feasible, or novel to human judges. What is missing is a distributional account: does repeated AI-assisted ideation broaden the range of scientific directions explored, or does it concentrate output around existing work? This matters because scientific discovery depends on covering diverse and sometimes peripheral directions—not just producing locally coherent proposals near known work. Key Idea The core technical insight is to reframe AI ideation evaluation from per-idea quality judgments to population-level distributional analysis: embed all AI-generated ideas and all human papers from the same research areas into a common semantic space, then compare the spread (pairwise similarity, centroid distance), the directionality (distance from seed vs. distance from follow-on human work), and the composition of novelty (new research question vs. new technical method vs. recombination of existing methods). This distributional lens reveals behavior invisible to per-idea evaluation. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. First, 34,698 papers from ICLR, NeurIPS, and ICML (2019–2025) are collected with DBLP citation data; research areas are defined via bibliographic coupling (papers sharing references are similar), IDF-weighted paper–reference matrix projected to 128 dimensions via truncated SVD, and clustered with HDBSCAN (min_cluster_size=15, min_samples=5), yielding 19 active citation-defined areas spanning GNNs, RL, fairness, Bayesian optimization, and related topics. Second, four AI agent frameworks (zero-shot single-pass, AIScientist iterative self-reflection, ResearchAgent three-stage planning + validation, AgentLaboratory multi-agent postdoc/PhD dialogue) are each run with six LLMs over 2,140 bootstrapped five-paper seed sets, producing 51,360 total runs and 37,802 valid ideas. Third, all ideas and human papers are embedded with Qwen3-Embedding-4B and analyzed via four measurements: (1) pairwise cosine similarity within research area, (2) cosine similarity of ideas to seed papers vs. later human follow-on papers (defined as papers citing ≥2 seed papers), (3) citation statistics of human papers with cosine similarity >0.9 to AI ideas, and (4) research-question and technical-method extraction via Gemma-4-31B-IT followed by semantic matching at a manually calibrated 0.87 threshold. Three critical design decisions: using only title+abstract for human papers but full proposal text for AI ideas (an asymmetry the paper does not justify), restricting seed sets to five papers due to context-window constraints, and using a manually calibrated rather than data-driven novelty threshold. Key Results Within-area pairwise cosine similarity is 0.82–0.84 for AI-generated ideas versus 0.77 for human-authored papers across all agent frameworks and all six LLMs—a gap that persists uniformly (Figure 2). The centroid-distance alternative confirms this: AI ideas sit at mean distance 0.091 from the area centroid versus 0.121 for human papers. For temporal distance from seed literature, AI ideas have mean cosine similarity 0.92 to their seed papers; later human follow-on papers have only 0.88 to those same seeds—and AI ideas and human follow-on papers differ most (0.82), indicating they diverge in different directions. For citation impact, papers matched to AI ideas (2,359 pairs, cosine >0.9) average 50.4 citations versus a same-area baseline of 54.9 (difference: −4.47, 95% CI [−6.41, −2.53], p<0.001); AgentLaboratory shows the largest deficit (−7.98, p<0.001). For novelty composition, 85.1% of AI-generated ideas contain a research question already present in the five seed papers, while only 62.6% contain technical methods already present—AI ideas vary more at the method level (recombination) than at the question level. The narrowing pattern is remarkably stable across model families, parameter scales from 0.8B to 35B, and all four agent architectures. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Prior work (Si et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2024) evaluates individual AI-generated ideas for novelty, feasibility, and quality using human judges; this paper provides the first population-level, distributional characterization of AI ideation behavior across a factorial design of agents and LLMs. The bibliographic-coupling methodology for defining research areas and the research-question/technical-method decomposition lens are applied tools from scientometrics, not invented here, but their combination in this evaluation context is genuinely new. Rigor & Reproducibility The factorial design (4 frameworks × 6 LLMs × 2,140 bootstrapped seed sets) and the consistency of findings across all 24 agent-LLM combinations constitute a strong robustness argument. The centroid-distance analysis (Appendix D) replicates the pairwise-similarity finding. Threshold sensitivity analysis for the 0.87 novelty boundary shows qualitative stability. Confidence intervals and one-sided p-values are reported for the citation comparison. No code, data, or model weights are released (not stated in the paper). Three methodological concerns: (1) AI ideas are represented by full proposal text (hundreds of words) while human papers use only title and abstract—this representation asymmetry will mechanically inflate within-AI similarity and suppress AI-to-human similarity, and the paper does not control for it; (2) the citation impact analysis covers only 2,359 of 37,802 ideas (6.2% match rate at similarity >0.9), and the 93.8% of ideas without a close human analog are entirely excluded—if those ideas differ systematically (more novel or simply lower quality), the citation proxy is severely biased; (3) the 19 research areas total only 1,035 papers, are restricted to AI/ML, and skew toward narrow specialized niches, limiting generalizability. This is a preprint. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The study uses open-weight LLMs (0.8B–35B) run locally; the methodology is reproducible by academic groups with GPU access. - Efficient: 51,360 generation runs represent substantial compute; no wall-clock or GPU-hour figures are stated. - Effective: All four measurement approaches converge consistently, lending credibility to the core finding that AI ideas are more concentrated than human ideas; however, the representation asymmetry is an unaddressed confounder. - Scalable: The study is restricted to 19 AI/ML research areas; whether findings generalize to broader, faster-moving, or cross-disciplinary fields is untested and explicitly acknowledged by the authors as a limitation. The work delivers only empirical findings—no software artifact, dataset, or trained model is released. Strengths - The factorial design at scale (37,802 ideas across 24 agent-LLM combinations) makes the consistency of the narrowing effect convincing; it is not a property of any single model or framework. - The research-question vs. technical-method decomposition is a genuinely useful novelty lens that separates local method recombination from genuine question-level exploration—this distinction is underexplored in the AI ideation literature. - The finding that more sophisticated agentic designs (multi-agent deliberation, iterative self-reflection) do not improve exploration breadth is practically important and challenges the implicit assumption that adding more agentic machinery solves this problem. - Using citation-defined research areas (bibliographic coupling) rather than keyword or venue labels provides a principled, data-driven basis for comparison that is less susceptible to definitional disagreements about what constitutes a research area. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) the corpus is restricted to AI/ML conferences (ICLR, NeurIPS, ICML), so findings may not generalize to biology, physics, or social science; (2) only the ideation stage is evaluated, not full experimental execution or paper writing; (3) the citation proxy is indirect—matched human papers, not actual AI-authored papers, are used; (4) context-window constraints forced five-paper seed sets, which may limit the breadth of input context available to agents. The authors confront these limitations clearly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The representation asymmetry (AI ideas: full proposal text; human papers: title+abstract only) is the most serious unaddressed methodological flaw. Longer, more repetitive proposal text will produce higher intra-corpus similarity for AI ideas regardless of true conceptual concentration. The paper makes no attempt to equate input text length or to test whether the gap persists when human papers are represented by their full text. - The citation-impact proxy covers only 6.2% of AI ideas (2,359 of 37,802). The large majority of ideas—those that do not closely match any existing human paper—are excluded. This is precisely the set that might be most novel (or most incoherent), and its exclusion makes the citation finding uninterpretable as a claim about AI ideation quality broadly. - The AIScientist's literature-search tool is served from a local corpus restricted to papers before time t—a setup that handicaps it relative to production use (where it would access Semantic Scholar). Results for AIScientist may not reflect its behavior in deployment. - No analysis of heterogeneity across the 19 research areas: do smaller, more mature niches show more concentration than broader active ones? If concentration is driven by seed-set diversity (i.e., areas with fewer seed papers force tighter clustering), this would be a confound rather than a property of AI ideation per se. - The paper compares AI ideas against human papers from the same research areas, but the human papers were written after the seed period and have the benefit of actual experimental results, conference peer review, and cross-pollination with other fields. The more appropriate comparison might be human-authored research proposals (grant applications, workshop position papers), which would share the same pre-experimental, text-only format as AI-generated ideas. Bottom Line This paper provides the most comprehensive population-level evidence to date that AI research agents concentrate rather than diversify scientific exploration—a finding robust across agents and LLMs—but the unaddressed representation asymmetry and narrow citation-proxy coverage mean the quantitative magnitudes should be treated as upper-bound estimates rather than precise measurements; nonetheless, the qualitative conclusion is credible and the paper is worth reading for anyone building or deploying AI-assisted research systems.

Scalable Token-Level Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TOKENHD, a pipeline for training lightweight token-level hallucination detectors that operate directly on free-form LLM outputs without requiring step segmentation. - Why it's important: Subtle logical errors in LLM reasoning traces (math, code) are hard to localize; existing Process Reward Models require predefined step boundaries and expensive MCTS sampling, limiting both granularity and scalability in real deployments. - How it does it: Multiple cheap critic models annotate hallucinated text spans, an iterative restoration algorithm aligns those spans to exact token positions, an adaptive ensemble learns critic weights from a high-quality validation set, and an importance-weighted cross-entropy loss trains a small classifier to predict per-token hallucination probabilities. - The good: TOKENHD-0.6B achieves 63.64 Sincor on Math-500, surpassing QwQ-32B (55.05) at 55× smaller model size; TOKENHD-8B achieves AUROC 0.8739–0.9004 on four math benchmarks, exceeding o4-mini by 0.04–0.09 AUROC points. - The missing: The primary evaluation generates test samples with the same GPT-4o-mini policy used for training data, which conflates in-distribution and held-out evaluation; the Gemini-2.0-Flash cross-policy drop (64.55 → 41.42 Sincor on Math-500) reveals the severity of this issue. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on LLM reliability, process reward models, or post-hoc error detection; the lightweight detector and self-correction application are directly practical for math/reasoning pipelines, but readers should weigh the same-policy evaluation design before extrapolating to production settings.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TOKENHD, a pipeline for training lightweight token-level hallucination detectors that operate directly on free-form LLM outputs without requiring step segmentation. - Why it's important: Subtle logical errors in LLM reasoning traces (math, code) are hard to localize; existing Process Reward Models require predefined step boundaries and expensive MCTS sampling, limiting both granularity and scalability in real deployments. - How it does it: Multiple cheap critic models annotate hallucinated text spans, an iterative restoration algorithm aligns those spans to exact token positions, an adaptive ensemble learns critic weights from a high-quality validation set, and an importance-weighted cross-entropy loss trains a small classifier to predict per-token hallucination probabilities. - The good: TOKENHD-0.6B achieves 63.64 Sincor on Math-500, surpassing QwQ-32B (55.05) at 55× smaller model size; TOKENHD-8B achieves AUROC 0.8739–0.9004 on four math benchmarks, exceeding o4-mini by 0.04–0.09 AUROC points. - The missing: The primary evaluation generates test samples with the same GPT-4o-mini policy used for training data, which conflates in-distribution and held-out evaluation; the Gemini-2.0-Flash cross-policy drop (64.55 → 41.42 Sincor on Math-500) reveals the severity of this issue. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on LLM reliability, process reward models, or post-hoc error detection; the lightweight detector and self-correction application are directly practical for math/reasoning pipelines, but readers should weigh the same-policy evaluation design before extrapolating to production settings.
