Problem & Motivation LLMs deployed for reasoning-intensive tasks (mathematics, code, STEM question answering) generate outputs that appear coherent but contain subtle logical errors—incorrect intermediate deductions, wrong arithmetic, unsupported inferences—that propagate to wrong final answers. Detecting these errors post-hoc is difficult because no obvious surface signal distinguishes a wrong step from a correct one. Process Reward Models (PRMs) address this by assigning step-level correctness scores, but they require explicit step boundaries (fragile on free-form text), and sampling-based estimation methods like MCTS incur prohibitive inference cost (repeated policy queries per step). Token-level supervision would enable finer localization and one-pass inference at deployment time, but no scalable pipeline exists for generating such annotations from real policy outputs. Key Idea The core insight is that hallucinated text spans can be reliably annotated at token granularity by aggregating the outputs of multiple cheap critic models—each prompted to identify erroneous text fragments as exact spans—using an adaptive ensemble that weights each critic by its agreement with high-quality ground-truth labels, then training a small binary classifier on the resulting soft token labels with importance weighting to compensate for the extreme sparsity of hallucinated tokens. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. (1) Data synthesis: For each (query, response) pair sampled from GPT-4o-mini, four critic models (DeepSeek-R1-Qwen3-8B, QwQ-32B, GPT-4.1, o4-mini) each produce C=multiple XML-tagged error spans via prompt. An iterative restoration algorithm (up to 3 rounds using o4-mini as a restorer) normalizes paraphrased or reformatted spans to exact verbatim substrings in the original response, achieving 98.10% recovery versus 64.25% direct match. Binary token labels are derived from span overlap; C runs per critic are averaged to soft scores, yielding per-critic soft annotation matrices. (2) Adaptive ensemble: A small validation set D_val annotated by GPT-5 and o3 is used to learn scalar weights w minimizing MSE between the weighted sum of critic soft scores and ground truth, subject to sum-to-one. The learned weights are stable across bootstrap resamples (o4-mini ≈0.60, GPT-4.1 ≈0.30, QwQ-32B ≈0.09) and improve label quality by 8.8–17.95 Sincor points over uniform averaging. (3) Training recipe:* A Qwen3 backbone (0.6B–8B) with an added classification head is trained with importance-weighted binary cross-entropy: the loss up-weights tokens whose soft label exceeds a threshold β (hallucinated tokens) in inverse proportion to their frequency, mitigating class imbalance. Training uses Adam with cosine decay, 1e-5 peak lr, one epoch on ~49,000 filtered samples from Math, AceReason-Math, and Big-Math. Three critical design decisions: (a) separate critic vs. labeler model sets prevents direct distillation and is well-justified; (b) iterative restoration is pragmatically validated at 98.10% recovery; (c) importance weighting is well-motivated by observed token imbalance, with ablations confirming its benefit. Key Results On four math benchmarks (Math-500, AIME-2024, AIME-2025, OlympiadBench-Math), TOKENHD-0.6B achieves Sincor of 63.64/61.41/64.34/64.99 versus QwQ-32B's 55.05/50.59/56.18/52.53 and GPT-4.1's 46.60/50.20/56.24/49.80. TOKENHD-8B achieves 64.55/63.62/68.88/68.45, approaching o4-mini (71.39/67.67/76.09/70.32). On PRM comparison (Appendix F), TOKENHD-8B outperforms Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-72B by 30–57 Sincor points across five benchmarks. AUROC: TOKENHD-8B reaches 0.8739–0.9004 versus o4-mini's 0.7880–0.8489. On STEM generalization (Olym-Phy/GPQA/FinQA), TOKENHD-8B achieves 65.9/65.5/64.4 Sincor despite training only on math. Code zero-shot (Code-Elo/LiveCodeBench-Lite, GPT-4o-mini policy): 49.37/41.30, improving to 73.61/70.16 with math+code mix training. Best-of-N selection: Full-Response Min achieves 35.2% accuracy (62.4% of oracle) versus 25.8% majority-vote baseline. Self-correction with TokenHD hints: 19.9% correction rate (98% of 20.3% oracle) versus 16.9% no-hint baseline. Data scaling saturates around 50% of the training set. Human annotation quality: 4.63/5 (math+STEM), 4.55/5 (code). Baselines are current and strong (o4-mini, GPT-4.1, QwQ-32B as critics; Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-72B as the PRM baseline). Scale of testing: standard benchmarks, not stress-scale; largest backbone tested is 8B (14B mentioned briefly in Appendix). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The integrated pipeline—multi-critic annotation, iterative span restoration, adaptive ensemble weighting, and importance-weighted token-level training—is a genuinely novel combination for hallucination detection. Individual components (importance-weighted loss, adaptive weighting, model merging) apply existing ML ideas to a new problem formulation. The empirical finding that a 0.6B trained detector surpasses a 32B chain-of-thought reasoning model on hallucination detection is non-obvious and has implications for the broader reward-model design space. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for three key axes: ensemble strategy, training objective, and data scaling. Threshold sensitivity is validated (max 1.51 pp mean deviation, Appendix J.2). Ensemble weight stability is confirmed via bootstrap CIs across two independent validation subsets (Appendix J.1). Human evaluation of annotation quality is conducted (Appendix D). Error bars are not reported in main results tables. Code is released at https://github.com/rmin2000/TokenHD; trained model weights release is not stated. The PRM comparison (Appendix F) distributes step scores uniformly to tokens—the weakest possible token-level adaptation, which systematically disadvantages PRMs and does not represent a trained-token-level PRM baseline. Ground-truth labels for evaluation use GPT-5 and o3, which are proprietary and not publicly reproducible. The primary concern is that training and test data both use GPT-4o-mini as the policy model; cross-policy results in Section 5.1 show a severe drop for Gemini-2.0-Flash (41.42 Sincor vs. 64.55 in-distribution), and this confound is not fully resolved. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper trains and evaluates specific models. - Practical: Yes; 0.6B–8B Qwen3 backbones run on a single A100 GPU and integrate as a post-hoc module with no changes to the policy model. - Efficient: The detector runs in one forward pass without generating reasoning text, versus critic models that require full generation; however, no actual latency numbers (tokens/second or time per sample) are reported, so the claimed efficiency advantage is qualitative only. - Effective: Yes on math/STEM (Sincor 63–69, outperforming 32B reasoning models and 72B PRMs); code is weaker zero-shot (41–49) and requires domain-specific training to reach 70+. - Scalable: Tested 0.6B–8B; a brief Appendix note reports ~2-point gain at 14B. Data scaling saturates at ~50% of 49K training samples. No testing at 70B+ backbone sizes or on very long reasoning chains; the >1000-token bin shows a modest Sincor decline for the 1.7B model. The paper delivers a code artifact (GitHub) and a trained-model pipeline; trained weight release is not confirmed. Strengths - The adaptive ensemble provides a 17.95-point label quality improvement over uniform averaging (Comb 1, Table 2), and trained detectors outperform their annotation sources—this is non-trivial and suggests the importance-weighted training objective generalizes beyond simple critic distillation. - The 0.6B vs. QwQ-32B result is the paper's strongest empirical claim: a 55× smaller model consistently wins on all four benchmarks, demonstrating that task structure (binary scoring, no generation) dramatically reduces the required model capacity for this specific problem. - The iterative text restoration algorithm (98.10% recovery vs. 64.25% direct match, Table 4) is a concrete engineering contribution that enables reliable token-level alignment from naturally paraphrased critic outputs and could be reused in other annotation pipelines. - The self-correction experiment (19.9% correction rate, 98% of oracle) directly demonstrates that token-level localization translates into actionable downstream utility, which step-level or response-level signals cannot replicate at the same fidelity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The Conclusion section contains no explicit limitation discussion—it restates contributions in positive framing only. Limitations are scattered through the body: code generalization is weaker zero-shot and requires mix-training; Gemini-2.0-Flash presents a distribution shift challenge; data scaling saturates at ~50% of training data; o4-mini retains a meaningful lead on Math-500 (~7 Sincor points). The authors do not directly confront the same-policy evaluation confound as a limitation. The overall limitation disclosure is minimal, which is a credibility concern. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The primary evaluation table (Table 1) uses GPT-4o-mini as both the training-data policy and the test-data policy. The Gemini-2.0-Flash drop (64.55 → 41.42 Sincor) demonstrates this confound is material, but the main results are presented without this caveat. - The PRM comparison is the weakest possible adaptation: distributing a step's correctness score uniformly to all tokens within that step conflates correctly-written tokens in an incorrect step with actual error tokens. A PRM trained with token-level supervision on the same data would be a more honest baseline. - No inference latency figures are reported despite repeated efficiency claims; the practical speedup over o4-mini is asserted but not measured. - Trained model weights are not stated to be released, which limits reproducibility of the end-to-end pipeline despite code release. - TOKENHD-0.6B's Scor (specificity on correct responses) drops to 89.31–94.83, meaning up to 10% of correct-response tokens are flagged as hallucinations. The implications for downstream applications (e.g., false-positive-driven self-correction) are not analyzed. - The ground-truth labeler models (GPT-5, o3) are proprietary; if they share systematic blind spots with the critic models used for training, the evaluation may undercount errors that TOKENHD also misses, inflating reported performance relative to a fully independent oracle. Bottom Line This is a technically solid preprint that delivers a practical, deployable token-level hallucination detector with genuine efficiency advantages over PRM baselines; the same-policy evaluation design is the primary caveat, and practitioners should validate on their own policy models before deploying—but the pipeline and cross-domain generalization results make it worth reading for anyone building LLM reliability systems.

Toto 2.0: Time Series Forecasting Enters the Scaling Era

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Toto 2.0, a family of five open-weights time series foundation models (4m to 2.5B parameters) built under a single training recipe that produces monotonically improving forecast quality with scale, the first such demonstration for time series foundation models. - Why it's important: Every prior time series foundation model family has exhibited erratic scaling where larger versions sometimes underperform smaller ones, making parameter-count investment unreliable; Toto 2.0 closes this gap and establishes a reproducible scaling template. - How it does it: The key moves are (1) replacing autoregressive decoding with contiguous patch masking for single-pass parallel inference, (2) adopting u-µP to transfer hyperparameters from a cheap 10m proxy to all five target sizes without retuning, and (3) training exclusively on proprietary observability telemetry and synthetic data rather than public time series corpora. - The good: Toto 2.0 takes the top three spots on the contamination-resistant TIME benchmark (CRPS ranks 3.43, 3.51, 3.86) ahead of every external model, without having seen any public forecasting data at pretraining — a strong test of cross-domain generalization. - The missing: No component-level ablations are reported; it is impossible to attribute gains independently to CPM, the quantile head, NorMuon, u-µP, or the data recipe, and the hyperparameter sweep optimized against the GIFT-Eval validation set before GIFT-Eval test evaluation, raising a Goodhart's Law concern. - Read it or not: Practitioners building or deploying time series foundation models should read this — it supplies a concrete, reproducible scaling recipe and open weights; researchers seeking fundamental algorithmic novelty will find the contributions are mostly first-class engineering of recently published building blocks.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Toto 2.0, a family of five open-weights time series foundation models (4m to 2.5B parameters) built under a single training recipe that produces monotonically improving forecast quality with scale, the first such demonstration for time series foundation models. - Why it's important: Every prior time series foundation model family has exhibited erratic scaling where larger versions sometimes underperform smaller ones, making parameter-count investment unreliable; Toto 2.0 closes this gap and establishes a reproducible scaling template. - How it does it: The key moves are (1) replacing autoregressive decoding with contiguous patch masking for single-pass parallel inference, (2) adopting u-µP to transfer hyperparameters from a cheap 10m proxy to all five target sizes without retuning, and (3) training exclusively on proprietary observability telemetry and synthetic data rather than public time series corpora. - The good: Toto 2.0 takes the top three spots on the contamination-resistant TIME benchmark (CRPS ranks 3.43, 3.51, 3.86) ahead of every external model, without having seen any public forecasting data at pretraining — a strong test of cross-domain generalization. - The missing: No component-level ablations are reported; it is impossible to attribute gains independently to CPM, the quantile head, NorMuon, u-µP, or the data recipe, and the hyperparameter sweep optimized against the GIFT-Eval validation set before GIFT-Eval test evaluation, raising a Goodhart's Law concern. - Read it or not: Practitioners building or deploying time series foundation models should read this — it supplies a concrete, reproducible scaling recipe and open weights; researchers seeking fundamental algorithmic novelty will find the contributions are mostly first-class engineering of recently published building blocks. Problem & Motivation: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) have begun to match or exceed tuned statistical baselines across heterogeneous domains, but they have not replicated one of the defining achievements of NLP: reliable scaling where a single training recipe produces predictable improvements as parameters increase. Competing TSFM families such as Chronos, TimesFM, and Moirai exhibit uneven scaling — larger checkpoints sometimes score worse than smaller ones on the same benchmark — making investment in compute unpredictable. Infrastructure operators (e.g., SRE teams, cloud monitoring platforms) need forecasts over CPU utilization, memory, latency, and error rates that are both accurate and fast at inference; prior models' autoregressive decoding grows linearly slow with horizon length. The gap this paper targets is thus twofold: establish monotonic scaling across the full 4m–2.5B range and deliver the speed improvements that make long-horizon probabilistic forecasting practical in production. Key Idea: The central technical insight enabling reliable scaling is u-µP (unit-scaled maximal update parametrization): by reparametrizing hidden weights so that the optimal learning rate is width-invariant, a single hyperparameter sweep on a cheap 10m proxy model transfers zero-shot to all five target sizes. Without this, each of the five sizes would require independent multi-day sweeps, making a coordinated family infeasible. The secondary architectural insight is contiguous patch masking, which converts Toto 1.0's K-step autoregressive decoder into a single-pass decoder — collapsing K forward passes to one and eliminating compounding error across horizon steps. Neither idea is new in isolation (u-µP from Blake et al. 2025; CPM adapted from TiRex/Auer et al. 2025), but their joint application to TSFM training appears to be new. Methodology: The backbone is a decoder-only patched transformer (retained from Toto 1.0) with alternating time-axis causal attention and variate-axis full attention. Patch size is 32 (down from 64), doubling sequence length per context window. Input is normalized via a causal arcsinh transform that preserves small fluctuations and logarithmically compresses large excursions. Linear patch projections are replaced by two-layer SiLU residual MLPs at both input and output ends. The output head predicts nine quantiles {0.1, …, 0.9} trained with the pinball loss averaged over all quantile levels. The optimizer is NorMuon for matrix-shaped parameters and AdamW for input/output projections, biases, and norms; NorMuon extends Muon (momentum + Newton-Schulz orthogonalization) by row-normalizing the update against an EMA of per-row squared magnitudes, reinstating an effective per-neuron β₂ variance mechanism discarded by vanilla Muon. The choice of NorMuon is argued (but not ablated) to pair better with sign-valued pinball gradients than AdamW does. Training data is 42.5% proprietary Datadog observability metrics and 57.5% TempoPFN synthetic data; public time series data is excluded from pretraining. Hyperparameter search uses four sequential Optuna TPE rounds (architecture, data mixture, optimizer, decay schedule) optimized against GIFT-Eval validation MASE and CRPS on the 10m proxy; the same configuration is then applied to all five targets. The five sizes range from 4m (d=256, L=4) to 2.5B (d=2048, L=48), all trained on 4,096-step contexts with patch 32 and 32 variates per sample. Key Results: On BOOM (observability benchmark), all five Toto 2.0 sizes sit on the Pareto frontier: CRPS ranks are 3.88 (2.5B), 3.96 (1B), 4.26 (313m), 5.53 (22m), 7.17 (4m) versus Toto 1.0's 6.94 and the next-best external model Chronos-2 at 7.39. On GIFT-Eval (97 tasks across 23 datasets, zero-shot foundation-model tier), the top three CRPS ranks are 20.3 (2.5B), 21.1 (1B), 21.4 (313m), a 1.7-point gap above the next-best foundation model PatchTST-FM r1 at 23.1. On TIME (98 tasks from 50 fresh datasets designed to resist training contamination), the top three CRPS ranks are 3.43 (2.5B), 3.51 (313m), 3.86 (1B), with Chronos-2 fourth at 4.03. A minor inversion (313m outperforming 1B on TIME MASE and rank metrics) is the one departure from monotonic scaling. The 22m achieves Toto 1.0's (151m parameters) quality with 7× fewer parameters. Inference: a 1,024-step forecast requires 16 forward passes for Toto 1.0 versus 1 for Toto 2.0 single-pass; at 4,096 steps, Toto 2.0 2.5B single-pass remains faster than Chronos-2. The finetuned 2.5B-FT and FnF ensemble reach CRPS ranks 13.4 and 12.8 on the full GIFT-Eval leaderboard (including agentic/ensemble entries). These are vendor self-reported numbers on benchmarks the vendor optimized against (GIFT-Eval validation); the TIME result is the cleanest independent test. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The system contribution is a five-checkpoint model family with open weights and a distributed u-µP training library (dd_unit_scaling). The empirical finding is that time series foundation models exhibit reliable scaling when trained under this recipe. The individual components — u-µP, CPM, NorMuon, quantile heads, TempoPFN synthetic data — are all published prior work; the novelty is their combination, the first application of µP to TSFMs, and the engineering required to make u-µP work under FSDP2/torch.compile at scale. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are reported isolating the contribution of individual components (CPM vs. autoregressive, quantile head vs. SMM, NorMuon vs. AdamW, u-µP vs. per-model tuning, or synthetic vs. observability data). The paper provides qualitative reasoning for design choices (e.g., why pinball gradients favor NorMuon) but no ablation experiments to confirm them. Error bars, multiple seeds, and significance tests are absent throughout; rank metrics aggregate over many tasks which provides some noise averaging, but individual dataset results are not analyzed. A material Goodhart's Law concern: the four-round hyperparameter sweep uses the GIFT-Eval validation set as the optimization objective, and Toto 2.0 is then evaluated on GIFT-Eval test — the model's data mixture and all architectural choices were selected to minimize a proxy of this target. BOOM is Datadog's own proprietary benchmark, making the top-of-BOOM result effectively self-reported on a benchmark the authors control. Code is released (github.com/DataDog/toto, dd_unit_scaling under Apache 2.0) and weights are on HuggingFace; however, the proprietary observability training data is not released, so full pretraining reproducibility is impossible. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL and SYSTEMS — the paper trains, evaluates, and releases a deployed forecasting system. - Practical: The five base models (4m through 2.5B) are released under Apache 2.0 with documented inference code; the 4m is positioned for edge deployment and the 313m runs at roughly the same latency as Chronos-2 on standard GPU hardware. - Efficient: Single-pass CPM reduces long-horizon inference from O(K) to O(1) forward passes; at a 1,024-step horizon, Toto 2.0 sizes are dramatically faster than Toto 1.0, and at a 4,096-step horizon the 2.5B remains faster than Chronos-2, though absolute latency numbers are reported only on unspecified hardware in Figure 9. - Effective: Evidence on three benchmarks — including the contamination-resistant TIME benchmark — indicates the models solve the stated forecasting task, not merely a benchmark proxy, though BOOM is a proprietary benchmark and GIFT-Eval validation was the hyperparameter optimization target. - Scalable: Scaling is validated from 4m to 2.5B parameters (roughly 600×); behavior at 10×–100× the tested scale (25B+) is not studied and not claimed, and the one non-monotonicity at 313m vs. 1B on TIME suggests scaling is not perfectly smooth even within the tested range. The paper delivers a software artifact (five model checkpoints, a training library) alongside the paper. Strengths: - Demonstrating monotonic scaling across a 600× parameter range is a genuine empirical contribution to the TSFM field; prior families consistently exhibit inversions that this work avoids. - The u-µP transfer pipeline is practically impactful: tuning hyperparameters once on a 10m proxy and applying them to a 2.5B model without retuning is a meaningful efficiency gain that the community can adopt. - The TIME benchmark results — top three ranks on a dataset the model was never trained on and that was specifically constructed to avoid training contamination — provide the strongest independent evidence of generalization quality. - The engineering of u-µP for distributed training (FSDP2, torch.compile, data parallelism, sequence-length invariance) addresses real production friction and is released as a general-purpose library. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that (1) the 4m loses long-horizon coherence past its training context, confirming small models have limited extrapolation; (2) even the 2.5B loses structure at 8,192 steps where a properly-fitted seasonal model would extrapolate cleanly; (3) data curation in TSFMs (including their own) remains ad hoc rather than principled; (4) the model treats observability metrics as generic time series, losing structure from histogram/distribution metric types, heterogeneous sampling frequencies, and high-dimensional variate selection problems. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The absence of component ablations is the most consequential gap: the paper asserts NorMuon is better suited to pinball loss than AdamW with a theoretical argument but presents no experiment confirming this. Similarly, the benefit of CPM over autoregressive decoding for quality (not just speed) is unquantified. The GIFT-Eval sweep/test circularity is not flagged by the authors: the hyperparameter selection (data mixture ratios, masking parameters, optimizer settings) was optimized on the GIFT-Eval validation set, so GIFT-Eval test performance is not fully held-out. BOOM is controlled by the authors; an independently administered benchmark where Datadog has no influence would be more convincing. The long-horizon stability experiment uses only synthetic sinusoidal mixtures (three superimposed periods) — real observability signals exhibit regime changes, seasonality drift, and discontinuities that sinusoidal mixtures do not capture, making the stability claim harder to generalize. Finally, the paper compares against models that used public data for pretraining, but does not compare against a Toto 2.0 variant that also used public pretraining data, leaving open whether the "no public data" choice is actually optimal or just matched the sweep. Bottom Line: Practitioners building time series forecasting infrastructure gain two concrete, immediately usable things from this work — five open-weight checkpoints that lead current benchmarks and a distributed u-µP training library — making it worth reading for anyone who trains or deploys TSFMs at scale, despite the absence of component ablations and the self-evaluation concerns around BOOM and GIFT-Eval.

Evaluation of Pipelines for Data Integration into Knowledge Graphs

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces KGI-Bench, a benchmark framework for end-to-end evaluation of knowledge graph (KG) integration pipelines, together with KGI-Bench-Movie, a publicly released dataset instantiation covering 10,000 films in RDF, JSON, and text formats with a DBpedia-derived reference KG. - Why it's important: Existing benchmarks evaluate isolated KG construction tasks (entity resolution, ontology matching) but no benchmark evaluates the quality of the integrated KG produced by complete pipelines, leaving practitioners unable to compare pipeline designs on holistic quality. - How it does it: Pipelines are evaluated by comparing the output KG against a reference KG via three orthogonal metrics — coverage (recall of reference entities/triples), correctness (precision of produced entities/triples), and consistency (ontology constraint violation rates) — after entity alignment using sentence-transformer embeddings. - The good: RDF_llm achieves F1_E=0.973 and F1_T=0.901 and ranks first across all 66 weight permutations of coverage/correctness/consistency, demonstrating that LLM-assisted ontology matching propagates measurably into end-to-end KG quality. - The missing: The text sources are DBpedia abstracts while the reference KG is derived from DBpedia structured data, creating a circularity that makes the text pipeline results (F1_E=0.037–0.074) an artifact of this particular construction rather than a general finding about unstructured source integration. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building or comparing KG integration pipelines who need a principled evaluation harness; the metric formalism is reusable even beyond the movie domain, though the benchmark itself is narrow.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces KGI-Bench, a benchmark framework for end-to-end evaluation of knowledge graph (KG) integration pipelines, together with KGI-Bench-Movie, a publicly released dataset instantiation covering 10,000 films in RDF, JSON, and text formats with a DBpedia-derived reference KG. - Why it's important: Existing benchmarks evaluate isolated KG construction tasks (entity resolution, ontology matching) but no benchmark evaluates the quality of the integrated KG produced by complete pipelines, leaving practitioners unable to compare pipeline designs on holistic quality. - How it does it: Pipelines are evaluated by comparing the output KG against a reference KG via three orthogonal metrics — coverage (recall of reference entities/triples), correctness (precision of produced entities/triples), and consistency (ontology constraint violation rates) — after entity alignment using sentence-transformer embeddings. - The good: RDF_llm achieves F1_E=0.973 and F1_T=0.901 and ranks first across all 66 weight permutations of coverage/correctness/consistency, demonstrating that LLM-assisted ontology matching propagates measurably into end-to-end KG quality. - The missing: The text sources are DBpedia abstracts while the reference KG is derived from DBpedia structured data, creating a circularity that makes the text pipeline results (F1_E=0.037–0.074) an artifact of this particular construction rather than a general finding about unstructured source integration. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building or comparing KG integration pipelines who need a principled evaluation harness; the metric formalism is reusable even beyond the movie domain, though the benchmark itself is narrow. Problem & Motivation: Constructing and continuously updating knowledge graphs requires chaining multiple heterogeneous tasks — extraction, schema alignment, entity resolution, fusion, cleaning — into pipelines. These tasks are developed independently and evaluated in isolation. The missing capability is holistic pipeline evaluation: given two competing pipelines that produce different updated KGs, there is no established methodology to determine which is better. Incremental integration compounds the problem because errors in early stages propagate through downstream steps, making task-level metrics misleading. The field has matured benchmarks for individual subtasks (OAEI for ontology matching, OpenEA for entity alignment, Text2KGBench for extraction) but nothing that assesses the combined output of a complete, multi-stage pipeline. Key Idea: Bypass the intractability of evaluating individual pipeline internals by evaluating the artifact produced — the updated KG — against a reference KG. The key insight is decomposing output quality into three orthogonal dimensions (coverage/correctness/consistency) that individually diagnose different failure modes: low coverage exposes extraction or mapping incompleteness; low correctness exposes fusion or entity resolution errors; low consistency exposes ontology-level defects. This triage structure lets the benchmark serve as a diagnostic tool, not just a ranking mechanism. Methodology: The benchmark assumes a seed KG (KG₀), one or more input sources (S₁…Sₙ) to integrate sequentially, and a reference KG (KG_R) as ground truth. A pipeline φ is modeled as a DAG of task implementations. Evaluation proceeds in two stages: (1) establish an entity alignment A_n ⊆ E(KGₙ) × E(KG_R) using sentence-transformer similarity over entity labels; (2) compute coverage (recall of reference entities with correct types and recall of reference triples via triple matching), correctness (precision of produced entities/triples against aligned reference, penalizing duplicates), and consistency (six ontology violation ratios: disjointness, domain, range, relation direction, datatype, format). Metrics are aggregated into a weighted composite score with tunable weights per quality dimension. The benchmark instantiation (KGI-Bench-Movie) constructs a reference KG by recursively crawling 10,000 DBpedia film entities with their related persons and companies (~336,000 triples), then partitions into a seed (25%) and three source splits (25% each) with controlled ~5% pairwise overlap. RDF sources are generated by namespace renaming; JSON by flattening subgraphs into nested records; text by including DBpedia abstracts. Twelve pipelines are evaluated: six single-source-type (two per format — baseline and LLM-enhanced) and six multi-source-type (all permutations of base pipelines across formats). The LLM-based pipelines use OpenAI's gpt-5-mini-2025-10 at zero-shot inference. Critical design decisions: (a) evaluation-restricted sets exclude seed content from metrics to avoid crediting prior knowledge — well-justified; (b) duplicate-penalized entity correctness counts reference entities in the numerator while counting produced entities in the denominator — sound choice that prevents gaming via duplication; (c) fixed PARIS similarity thresholds (0.95 for entities, 0.8 for relations) across all pipelines — limits threshold sensitivity analysis. Key Results: Among single-source pipelines on the 10k benchmark, RDF_llm achieves the best performance: CovE=0.959, CorrE=0.988, F1_E=0.973, CovT=0.884, CorrT=0.917, F1_T=0.901, near-perfect consistency. RDF_base performs second: F1_E=0.866, F1_T=0.676. JSON_base reaches F1_E=0.807, F1_T=0.600 with notable relation-direction violations (OR=0.842). Text pipelines fail severely: TEXT_base achieves F1_E=0.037, F1_T=0.017; TEXT_llm (1k benchmark only) achieves F1_E=0.074, F1_T=0.069. JSON_llm and TEXT_llm are evaluated only on the 1k version, limiting direct comparability. Multi-source pipelines cluster tightly (F1_E ~0.57–0.59, F1_T ~0.41–0.43) with integration order having minimal effect. In comparative ranking across 66 weight permutations, RDF_llm holds rank 1 stably, RDF_base holds rank 2; JSON pipelines show wider rank ranges (3–11). The key mechanistic finding is that improved ontology matching recall in RDF_llm (OM_r=0.83 vs. 0.63 for RDF_base) propagates into higher fact coverage and F1. Entity linking recall for text sources is 43% for films and lower for persons/companies, constituting the primary bottleneck. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark framework and KGI-Bench-Movie dataset are the primary contributions. The individual metrics borrow from existing KG quality literature (SHACL-style consistency checking, precision/recall for entity resolution); their novelty lies in the combination and application to end-to-end pipeline evaluation rather than isolated task evaluation. The empirical finding that LLM assistance benefits structured (RDF) pipelines but not unstructured (text) pipelines is new at this level of systematic specificity, though directionally anticipated. Rigor & Reproducibility: All code and datasets are publicly available (GitHub: https://github.com/ScaDS/KGI-Bench; Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17246357). Ablations in the traditional sense are absent — no component-level ablations within a single pipeline to isolate which task drives quality. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported; all results are point estimates from single runs. The paper is an arXiv preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. A methodological concern: the LLM-based JSON and text pipelines are evaluated only on the 1k benchmark (not 10k), making direct comparison with RDF pipelines problematic. Thresholds (PARIS at 0.95/0.8, EmbRL at 0.8) were set via preliminary experiments but not systematically swept in the main evaluation. The evaluation alignment itself — sentence-transformer over entity labels — is imperfect and could systematically misattribute errors. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (benchmark construction and pipeline execution). Practical: The benchmark runs on a single workstation (AMD Ryzen 9 9850HX, 64GB RAM, NVIDIA RTX 4070 GPU); text pipelines consume up to 19.8GB RAM, which is within reach of standard research machines. Efficient: LLM-based JSON pipelines are prohibitively slow (7,025 seconds) and expensive (€9.9 per 1k-film run), limiting practical deployment; RDF-based pipelines are fast (65–222 seconds). Effective: The metrics successfully distinguish pipeline quality across format types and detect concrete integration defects (duplicate entities, ontology violations) that correlate with known failure modes. Scalable: The benchmark is tested up to 10,000 films; the full DBpedia corpus contains ~150,000 films and is not tested, leaving scalability of both the evaluated pipelines and the evaluation framework unverified. The paper delivers a software artifact (KGpipe tool, benchmark datasets) and a reusable evaluation methodology. Strengths: - Fills a genuine and well-characterized gap: no prior benchmark evaluates complete KG integration pipeline quality end-to-end against a ground truth, and the paper makes this case clearly with a thorough related work survey. - The three-metric decomposition (coverage/correctness/consistency) is orthogonal by construction and provides interpretable diagnostic signals — each metric points to a distinct failure class, which is more useful for pipeline debugging than a single aggregated score. - The duplicate-penalized entity correctness formulation is a non-trivial and correct design choice that prevents pipelines from gaming precision by under-merging entities. - Public release of all code, datasets, and three benchmark sizes (100/1k/10k films) enables immediate reproduction and incremental adoption. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors limit the scope to a fixed ontology (no dynamic schema evolution). They note that the benchmark is domain-specific to movies and call for extension to other domains. They acknowledge that JSON_llm and TEXT_llm could only be executed on the smaller 1k benchmark, limiting comparability. They note the evaluation alignment is imperfect and that the benchmark is not intended to be exhaustive in consistency constraint types. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant unacknowledged gap is the circularity in benchmark construction: DBpedia abstracts serve as the text source while the reference KG is derived from DBpedia's structured data. This means the text pipeline's entity linker (DBpedia Spotlight) has structural affinity with the ground truth that would not exist in real-world unstructured data integration. The catastrophic text-pipeline results (F1_T=0.017) partly reflect this design choice rather than a general property of LLM or OpenIE extraction. Second, all 12 pipelines are generated by the authors' own tool KGpipe, which means no independent or third-party pipeline designs are evaluated — the benchmark demonstrates usefulness with the authors' own pipelines, not with independently developed ones. Third, the entity alignment procedure (sentence-transformer over labels) used in evaluation is itself imperfect and introduces noise into all downstream metrics; no analysis quantifies this evaluation-alignment error. Fourth, there are no significance tests: with single-run results and no variance estimates, the differences between, e.g., multi-source pipelines (F1_E ~0.566–0.585) may not be statistically meaningful. Bottom Line: A practitioner who needs to compare KG integration pipelines holistically will find the KGI-Bench framework and dataset immediately useful, but should treat the movie-domain results as a proof of concept rather than a general characterization of pipeline behavior, and should be aware that the text-source findings are partly artifacts of the benchmark's DBpedia-derived construction.

Chronicle: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Joint Language and Time Series Understanding

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Chronicle, a 324M-parameter decoder-only transformer trained from scratch jointly on natural language and time series, with both modalities sharing the same transformer backbone and only modality-specific input/output interfaces separated. - Why it's important: All prior multimodal text+time-series models adapt pretrained LLMs post-hoc, forcing temporal data to fit representations never shaped by it; Chronicle tests whether joint training from scratch avoids this fundamental mismatch and whether such models actually outperform unimodal specialists. - How it does it: Text tokens and time series patches occupy positions in a single autoregressive sequence; pretraining uses unimodal-only micro-batches (92% text, 8% time series) so cross-modal capability emerges from shared parameter updates without explicit cross-modal losses, followed by a short alignment stage with 5% interleaved data. - The good: On 24 UCR/UEA datasets with a frozen linear probe, Stage 1 achieves 0.736 accuracy surpassing Moirai-2 (0.714) and all per-dataset supervised baselines except FEDformer; on Time-MMD multimodal forecasting, Stage 2 (LP) achieves NMAE 0.514 vs. the best MM-TSFlib baseline at 0.621. - The missing: The paper never ablates the most critical counterfactual — a Chronicle-architecture model trained on 100% time series data at the same compute budget — so the cost or benefit of joint training for TS quality cannot be isolated from architecture and objective differences. - Read it or not: Researchers building multimodal time series systems should read it for its principled evaluation protocol and the no-catastrophic-interference finding; those seeking production-quality zero-shot forecasting should note it trails dedicated specialists like TimesFM-2.5 by a wide margin on GIFT-Eval.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Chronicle, a 324M-parameter decoder-only transformer trained from scratch jointly on natural language and time series, with both modalities sharing the same transformer backbone and only modality-specific input/output interfaces separated. - Why it's important: All prior multimodal text+time-series models adapt pretrained LLMs post-hoc, forcing temporal data to fit representations never shaped by it; Chronicle tests whether joint training from scratch avoids this fundamental mismatch and whether such models actually outperform unimodal specialists. - How it does it: Text tokens and time series patches occupy positions in a single autoregressive sequence; pretraining uses unimodal-only micro-batches (92% text, 8% time series) so cross-modal capability emerges from shared parameter updates without explicit cross-modal losses, followed by a short alignment stage with 5% interleaved data. - The good: On 24 UCR/UEA datasets with a frozen linear probe, Stage 1 achieves 0.736 accuracy surpassing Moirai-2 (0.714) and all per-dataset supervised baselines except FEDformer; on Time-MMD multimodal forecasting, Stage 2 (LP) achieves NMAE 0.514 vs. the best MM-TSFlib baseline at 0.621. - The missing: The paper never ablates the most critical counterfactual — a Chronicle-architecture model trained on 100% time series data at the same compute budget — so the cost or benefit of joint training for TS quality cannot be isolated from architecture and objective differences. - Read it or not: Researchers building multimodal time series systems should read it for its principled evaluation protocol and the no-catastrophic-interference finding; those seeking production-quality zero-shot forecasting should note it trails dedicated specialists like TimesFM-2.5 by a wide margin on GIFT-Eval. Problem & Motivation: Real-world time series almost always arrive with accompanying text — metadata, domain reports, anomaly descriptions, news — yet time series foundation models process numerical sequences in complete isolation. A growing body of work attempts to bridge this by attaching language models to time series, but every existing approach begins with a pretrained LLM and adapts it post-hoc. Because those backbones were shaped entirely by text before ever seeing temporal data, the time series modality must contort itself into a representational space not designed for it. Compounding the problem, these multimodal models are evaluated almost exclusively against other multimodal or task-specific baselines, never against strong unimodal foundation models in each domain, leaving open whether joint modeling adds value at all. Evidence from Tan et al. (2024) — who found LLM pretraining does not transfer to forecasting — and Merrill et al. (2024) — who found LLMs struggle with text-encoded series — motivates a fundamentally different approach. Key Idea: The core insight is that a decoder-only transformer trained from scratch with modality-specific input/output interfaces but a fully shared backbone, combined with a text-heavy unimodal batch curriculum (92/8), can develop representations suitable for both modalities simultaneously without catastrophic interference. Cross-modal capability emerges passively from shared parameter updates across modalities rather than from architecturally engineered cross-attention or early explicit alignment supervision. Methodology: The architecture is a 16-layer decoder-only transformer (d=1024, 8 GQA query heads, 4 KV heads, SwiGLU, pre-norm RMSNorm, RoPE, 324M parameters total). Time series input: each series is arsinh-normalized (statistics computed over visible values only), segmented into non-overlapping patches of length P=32, and each patch represented by a 4P-dimensional feature vector [time ramp r; normalized values v; binary validity mask m; channel ramp c], projected to d via a single bias-free linear layer + RMSNorm. Text input uses a 131,072-entry BPE vocabulary with tied embedding/LM-head. Output heads are modality-specific: a tied LM head with logit soft-capping (α=15) for text (cross-entropy loss LCE); a 21-quantile head over τ∈[0.05, 0.95] for time series (masked quantile loss LQL). Combined objective: L = LCE + 2.5·LQL (asymmetric weighting to balance gradient magnitudes). Stage 1 (47,683 steps, seq len 2048): 92% text-only batches (FineWeb-Edu + Dolmino-mix), 8% time-series-only batches (GiftEvalPretrain + KernelSynth augmentation); ~150B total tokens. Stage 2 (extended seq len 4096): injects 5% interleaved alignment data from ChatTS synthetic pairs and Merrill et al. descriptions into the TS stream. Optimizer: Muon for transformer block weight matrices; AdamW for embeddings, heads, and norms. Critical design decisions: (1) unimodal batches during bulk pretraining rather than interleaved from the start — justified as preventing modality interference early; (2) 92/8 text-heavy mix — acknowledged as a compute constraint rather than an optimal ratio; (3) channel ramp encoding for multivariate identity — ablated in Appendix E.2 and shown to improve UEA classification by +0.039 accuracy on average. Key Results: NLU (19 tasks, DCLM suite, zero/few-shot): Chronicle Stage 2 achieves 0.406 average, matching Gemma-3-270M-PT (0.406) at comparable scale despite seeing only ~138B text tokens vs. Gemma-3's 6T. On ARC-Easy, Stage 2 scores 0.651 vs. LLaMA-3.2-1B's 0.678 (a model 4× larger trained exclusively on text). GIFT-Eval zero-shot forecasting (97 tasks): Stage 1 = 0.978 MASE / 0.690 CRPS, below all dedicated TSFMs (best: TimesFM-2.5 at 0.705 MASE, PatchTST-FM-r1 at 0.483 CRPS) but above N-BEATS (0.938 MASE), DLinear (1.061), and Auto-ARIMA (0.912 CRPS). Stage 2 regresses to 1.053 MASE. UCR/UEA classification (24 datasets, frozen linear probe, 5 seeds): Stage 1 = 0.736 accuracy / 0.712 F1 vs. Moirai-2 at 0.714/0.692, TimesFM at 0.611/0.563, Chronos-2 at 0.376/0.230. Exceeds all per-dataset supervised DL baselines except FEDformer (0.723). Time-MMD multimodal forecasting (9 domains, LP head on frozen backbone): Stage 2 (LP) = 0.514 NMAE / avg rank 2.56, outperforming BERT+TNet (0.621 NMAE) and GPT2+Moi2 (0.588 NMAE). TimeCAP multimodal classification: Stage 2 LoRA (r=64) = 0.613 F1; Stage 1 LoRA (r=64) = 0.763 AUC — both best across all baselines over 3 seeds. Baselines on GIFT-Eval and UCR are drawn from the current public leaderboard and are strong and up-to-date. Evaluation scale: standard benchmarks, 324M parameters; no stress-scale experiments at larger model sizes. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The from-scratch joint training paradigm is genuinely novel — no prior work has trained a single model on both modalities from random initialization. The cross-domain evaluation protocol against dedicated specialists in each modality is also new to the multimodal TS literature. The architecture itself (decoder-only transformer with patch embedding, quantile output head) is a straightforward combination of PatchTST-FM and GPT-2 style components; the novelty is in the training strategy and evaluation discipline, not architectural invention. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present but incomplete: the Stage 1 vs. Stage 2 trade-off is cleanly documented across all benchmarks; the TS-token repetition sweep (Figure 3) is thorough; the channel-aware vs. mean-channel pooling ablation (Figure 4) covers all 10 UEA datasets. However, the critical missing ablation is a Chronicle-architecture model trained on 100% time series — without it, the cost of joint training for TS quality cannot be isolated from architecture and objective differences relative to PatchTST-FM or TimesFM. Error bars are reported for TimeCAP (3 seeds) and UCR/UEA (5 seeds) but not for GIFT-Eval or NLU (single runs). Code and checkpoints are stated as publicly available on Hugging Face and GitHub, but URLs in the preprint are placeholder strings ([HUGGINGFACE_LINK], [GITHUB_LINK]) — not accessible at paper submission time. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.20268v1); results have not undergone peer review. Training data (FineWeb-Edu, Dolmino-mix) and evaluation suite (DCLM) partially overlap in distribution, which is standard practice but may inflate NLU comparisons against models trained on less curated text. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Stage 1+2 training runs on 2×H100 80GB GPUs in roughly one week; inference is standard autoregressive decoding runnable on a single GPU. - Efficient: 324M parameters is compact relative to the 7–14B baselines it surpasses on multimodal tasks; however, autoregressive next-patch forecasting is slower than one-shot bidirectional TSFMs for long-horizon prediction. - Effective: Evidence is strong for frozen-embedding classification and head-trained multimodal forecasting; zero-shot forecasting is substantially weaker than dedicated specialists, a limitation the paper explicitly measures and explains. - Scalable: No scaling experiments beyond 324M parameters; the paper asserts the forecasting gap is addressable with more compute or a curriculum strategy but provides no evidence at larger scale. The paper delivers stated model checkpoints (URLs pending release) and evaluation code as software artifacts. Strengths: - The finding that 8% time series compute in a 324M joint model does not cause catastrophic interference — quantified against scale-matched LLMs across 19 NLU tasks — is the first clean empirical proof of this claim and directly refutes the implicit assumption underlying all prior post-hoc LLM adaptation work. - The evaluation protocol is the most rigorous in the multimodal TS literature: evaluating against the public GIFT-Eval leaderboard, dedicated TSFM frozen probes, and scale-matched LLMs simultaneously closes a longstanding gap that prior work had not addressed. - The frozen-embedding UCR/UEA classification result (0.736 accuracy, beating Moirai-2 at 0.714 and per-dataset supervised DL baselines with a single backbone) is a substantive empirical contribution, not just a tie. - The TS-token repetition trick (Section 5.4) is a simple, architecture-agnostic solution to the modality-imbalance problem in shared autoregressive backbones, with a clean ablation showing ~3 accuracy and ~4 AUC points of headroom — practically useful for downstream classification tasks with short time series. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront the forecasting gap to dedicated TSFMs, attributing it to two principled design choices: the 8% compute allocation and the causal next-patch objective vs. contiguous patch masking used by PatchTST-FM. They explicitly suggest curriculum strategies (TS-only pretraining followed by joint continued pretraining) and hybrid attention objectives as remedies, and note that Stage 2 covers only 5% of TS tokens with cross-modal supervision — a larger alignment stage is called out as the most promising direction. They also acknowledge the scope restriction to frozen representation learning vs. conversational agents. Overall the limitations section is unusually candid. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most consequential missing ablation is a Chronicle-architecture model trained on 100% time series at the same compute budget as Stage 1 — without this, it is impossible to determine whether the gap to PatchTST-FM is due to (a) joint training hurting TS quality, (b) the causal next-patch objective, (c) the lower TS compute, or some combination; the paper attributes it to (b) and (c) but cannot isolate them. The Time-MMD LP comparison is partially apples-to-oranges: Chronicle's LP head is trained with joint text+TS input (backbone sees both), while FM Fusion baselines freeze both encoders and only train the head — Chronicle thus benefits from cross-modal conditioning during fine-tuning in a way the baselines do not. The NLU comparison favors Chronicle on data quality: Chronicle uses FineWeb-Edu (highly filtered) while GPT-2's ~10B tokens are much lower quality Common Crawl — the 43× fewer token count comparison is accurate but potentially misleading about the actual information advantage. No per-domain analysis on Time-MMD breaks down which domains benefit from text conditioning vs. not, which would be critical for understanding when joint training actually helps rather than just matching well-tuned unimodal baselines. The paper does not evaluate retrieval quality of the time series embeddings or robustness to domain shift. Bottom Line: Chronicle establishes the right evaluation methodology for multimodal time series models and produces the first credible evidence that joint from-scratch training is feasible at compact scale without catastrophic interference; practitioners building systems where time series and text co-occur should read it, but the large zero-shot forecasting gap to dedicated specialists means it is not yet a viable replacement for production forecasting pipelines.

Self-Improving Language Models with Bidirectional Evolutionary Search

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (BES), a search framework that escapes the model's autoregressive probability shell by recombining partial trajectories via four biological-evolution-inspired operators and by recursively decomposing problems into verifiable sub-goals that supply dense intermediate feedback. - Why it's important: On hard tasks, correct solutions lie in low-probability regions of the model's distribution; standard search methods (best-of-N, tree search) are confined to the high-probability entropy shell, causing mainstream post-training algorithms to flatline or degrade on challenging benchmarks. - How it does it: Forward search applies trajectory recombination (combination, translocation, crossover, deletion) to splice segments from different rollouts, while backward search constructs a goal tree by recursively decomposing the root task into leaf sub-goals, scoring partial trajectories against how many sub-goals they address. - The good: On multi-hop reasoning (MuSiQue), BES achieves +3.0% and +3.8% absolute accuracy over the base model on 3B and 8B models respectively, where GRPO degrades (-1.0 to -1.9%) and Tree-GRPO barely improves; on three open mathematical optimization benchmarks it outperforms all open-source frameworks with substantially lower run-to-run variance. - The missing: The backward search component is never ablated independently from the evolution operators — a condition of evolution-only (no sub-goal decomposition) does not appear — so the relative contribution of the two core innovations remains unquantified. - Read it or not: Researchers working on test-time compute, post-training data generation, or agentic search should read this; the entropy-shell theorem and sub-goal complexity result are independently useful, and the empirical results specifically on regimes where baselines fail are the most credible part of the case.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (BES), a search framework that escapes the model's autoregressive probability shell by recombining partial trajectories via four biological-evolution-inspired operators and by recursively decomposing problems into verifiable sub-goals that supply dense intermediate feedback. - Why it's important: On hard tasks, correct solutions lie in low-probability regions of the model's distribution; standard search methods (best-of-N, tree search) are confined to the high-probability entropy shell, causing mainstream post-training algorithms to flatline or degrade on challenging benchmarks. - How it does it: Forward search applies trajectory recombination (combination, translocation, crossover, deletion) to splice segments from different rollouts, while backward search constructs a goal tree by recursively decomposing the root task into leaf sub-goals, scoring partial trajectories against how many sub-goals they address. - The good: On multi-hop reasoning (MuSiQue), BES achieves +3.0% and +3.8% absolute accuracy over the base model on 3B and 8B models respectively, where GRPO degrades (-1.0 to -1.9%) and Tree-GRPO barely improves; on three open mathematical optimization benchmarks it outperforms all open-source frameworks with substantially lower run-to-run variance. - The missing: The backward search component is never ablated independently from the evolution operators — a condition of evolution-only (no sub-goal decomposition) does not appear — so the relative contribution of the two core innovations remains unquantified. - Read it or not: Researchers working on test-time compute, post-training data generation, or agentic search should read this; the entropy-shell theorem and sub-goal complexity result are independently useful, and the empirical results specifically on regimes where baselines fail are the most credible part of the case. Problem & Motivation LLMs and agents need high-quality candidate trajectories both for post-training (as training data) and at inference time. On difficult problems — multi-hop reasoning requiring multiple retrievals, logic puzzles with large variable counts, combinatorial optimization — the model assigns extremely small probability mass to correct solutions. Best-of-N sampling improves coverage only linearly with sample count, while tree search builds candidates one lineage at a time, remaining confined to the model's own distribution. This creates a practical ceiling: on challenging variants of MuSiQue and Knights-and-Knaves, GRPO and MaxRL consistently fail to discover sufficient training positives and either flatline or degrade from the base model. The gap between what existing search can find and what hard tasks demand is the central problem. Key Idea Autoregressive extension of a trajectory stays within a narrow entropy shell because every generated token conditions on the same single prefix lineage — the distribution never mixes across independent rollouts. Recombining blocks from distinct rollouts (analogous to chromosomal crossover in sexual reproduction) produces candidates whose log-probability under the model exceeds the shell boundary by an amount proportional to the block total correlation of the trajectory distribution. This is the entropy-shell escape insight. The second insight, for backward search, is that replacing a single binary terminal verifier with m independent sub-goal verifiers transforms the sample requirement from Ω(1/∏p_i) — exponential in the number of sub-goals — down to O(p_min^{-1} log(m/δ)), because each sub-goal can be satisfied independently and then combined. Methodology BES maintains a candidate pool of partial trajectories and alternates between forward search steps and backward scoring, re-triggering backward decomposition every K_dec forward steps. The forward search applies five operators sampled with fixed probabilities (expansion 0.70, combination 0.10, translocation 0.075, crossover 0.075, deletion 0.05). Expansion samples K ~ Uniform{1,...,K_max} new steps from the policy autoregressively. Combination concatenates the distinct suffixes of two trajectories beyond their shared prefix. Deletion removes one interior step. Translocation transplants one step from trajectory B into trajectory A at position r. Crossover splices the prefix of A with the tail of B at a cut point. Single-parent operators select parents via Boltzmann distribution over backward scores; two-parent operators use a pair score that favors complementary parents maximizing joint sub-goal coverage. Temperature is annealed linearly from τ_0 to τ_end. The backward goal tree starts at a root goal and recursively decomposes unsatisfied leaves; node score s(n,g) = α·V_g(x,n) + (1-α)·mean_{g'∈ch(g)} s(n,g'), with α balancing coarse and fine signals. Verifiers are task-specific: rule-based exact match for K&K, embedding cosine similarity (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, threshold 0.6) for MuSiQue sub-questions, and Python expression evaluators returning [0,1] for open problem solving. For executable-program tasks (open problem solving), evolution operators cannot concatenate tokens directly and are instead realized as LLM-prompted rewrites with operator-specific instructions. Critical design decisions: (1) The 70/30 expansion/evolution split is fixed and not ablated individually per operator — empirically motivated but not justified from first principles; (2) pair score uses max over parents' sub-goal scores, incentivizing complementarity rather than quality, which is well-motivated but sensitive to backward tree quality; (3) for Gemma-3-1B-it (too small for open-ended decomposition), the backward search degrades to template-based scheduling — a significant deviation from the general framework not prominently flagged. Key Results Logical Reasoning (Knights-and-Knaves, Gemma-3-1B-it, 5K training problems): BES improves steadily across 1,000 gradient steps while GRPO and MaxRL show little to no improvement (EMA-smoothed curves in log-accuracy space; exact percentages not reported in text). Multi-Hop Reasoning (MuSiQue, 3-4 hop subset): BES reaches 7.0% (+3.0% over base) on Llama-3.2-3B and 10.4% (+3.8%) on Llama-3.1-8B; GRPO degrades to 2.1%/5.6%; Tree-GRPO (ICLR 2026 baseline, cited as state-of-the-art) reaches 3.9%/7.4%. BES finish ratios (0.97/0.94) substantially exceed Tree-GRPO (0.64/0.71), indicating trained agents learn to actually search rather than guess. Open Problem Solving (GPT-5 backbone, 3 runs each): Circle Packing Square — BES avg 2.623±0.014 vs. GEPA 2.613±0.022; Circle Packing Rect — BES avg 2.349±0.012 vs. GEPA 2.326±0.023; Heilbronn Convex — BES avg 0.026±0.001 vs. GEPA/OpenEvolve 0.025±0.002. BES exhibits dramatically lower standard deviation than all open-source baselines. Best-case scores remain slightly below AlphaEvolve (which uses significantly more compute and is closed-source). BES incurs ~29% more wall-clock time than Tree-GRPO per training step (309s vs. 240s) and 30-43% more API cost than ShinkaEvolve at inference. Baselines are current (GRPO, Tree-GRPO from ICLR 2026; ShinkaEvolve, GEPA from 2025-2026). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The specific coupling of biological recombination operators with backward sub-goal decomposition applied to LLM trajectory search is novel. Individually, the ingredients are known: genetic crossover operators trace to Holland (1992), bidirectional search is classical AI (Hart 1968), and MCTS-guided sub-goal decomposition appears in prior work. The genuine contribution is (a) the entropy-shell formalization proving confinement of autoregressive expansion and the escape guarantee for recombination, and (b) the exponential sample-complexity advantage theorem for backward sub-goal search — neither of which appeared in prior search-for-LLM work. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present but partial: Figure 4 shows that removing evolution operators or answer reweighting each degrades performance on K&K, confirming both components contribute. However, the ablation is conducted only on logical reasoning, not on multi-hop or open problem solving, and does not isolate individual evolution operators or the backward search independently. Multi-hop results are single-number accuracy values (no standard deviation, no multiple seeds). Open problem solving reports mean ± std across 3 runs. Open-source baseline results for open problem solving are taken from SkyDiscover [22] rather than rerun — claimed to use the same backbone and budget but from a third-party system, introducing implementation uncertainty. Code and trained models are released at https://github.com/Embodied-Minds-Lab/BES. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.28814v1); results are not peer-reviewed. The K&K backward search silently falls back to template-based scheduling because the policy model is too small for open-ended decomposition — this is disclosed in Appendix D.1 but not flagged in the main text. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: Post-training uses 2 H200 GPUs plus 1-2 auxiliary GPUs for the backward decomposer and retriever; inference uses GPT-5 via API — commercially accessible configurations. - Efficient: BES adds ~29% wall-clock overhead vs. Tree-GRPO in post-training and 30-43% API cost overhead vs. ShinkaEvolve at inference; the dominant cost is LLM calls for backward decomposition and pair-score computation (quadratic in pool size) — moderate but not prohibitive. - Effective: On post-training, BES demonstrates large gains specifically in the hard regime where baselines degrade; on open problem solving, improvements over best open-source are small in absolute terms (< 0.013 objective units on circle packing) but consistent and with meaningfully lower variance, suggesting better reliability rather than lucky best-case runs. - Scalable: Post-training experiments cap at 8B parameters; no experiments at 70B+ scale. Training collapse at >2 epochs for multi-hop is unresolved and suggests fragility at longer training horizons. Open problem solving uses a single GPT-5 backend with no scaling analysis. The work delivers a software artifact (code + trained models) and a reusable framework applicable to any task with an objective verifier. Strengths - Theorem 4.4 provides a non-trivial, formally rigorous explanation for why expansion-only search is fundamentally limited, going beyond empirical observation to pinpoint the mechanism (block total correlation and entropy-shell confinement). - Theorem 4.5 gives a clean exponential sample-complexity separation between terminal-only and backward-guided search, independently useful for reasoning about verifier design. - The empirical strategy of testing specifically on regimes where baselines fail or degrade is methodologically sound — it isolates the conditions under which BES provides genuine value rather than marginal improvement on already-solved settings. - Substantially lower variance on open problem solving (σ=0.001 for Heilbronn vs. σ=0.002-0.005 for competitors) is a concrete reliability advantage beyond raw performance, and is the most striking open-problem result. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) BES requires an objective reward signal and has not been tested on subjective tasks like academic writing; (2) backward search depends on the policy's decomposition capability and degrades for weak models; (3) post-training experiments are limited to models ≤8B parameters due to resource constraints. These are stated directly in Appendix H and constitute credible self-assessment. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Individual evolution operator ablations are absent: the paper shows evolution operators collectively matter but not which of the four contribute, or whether the 70% expansion probability is tuned or principled — given that translocation is what solves the case study example, its individual contribution deserves isolation. - The backward search is never ablated independently: a condition of evolution operators alone (without sub-goal decomposition, using only terminal verification) would directly test whether the two theoretical claims translate into separable empirical contributions. - The K&K backward search falls back to template scheduling with Gemma-3-1B-it, meaning the backward decomposition result in that experiment is not the same mechanism described in the theory; the boundary conditions for when the general framework applies are unstated. - Open problem solving baselines (OpenEvolve, GEPA, ShinkaEvolve) come from a third-party benchmark system rather than being directly rerun, which introduces implementation heterogeneity that is not quantified. - Assumption 4.3 (linear block total correlation) is theoretically justified but not empirically verified for the Llama or Gemma models used; the shell-escape bound (Eq. 9) only guarantees a strictly positive fraction escapes — the actual fraction is not quantified, leaving the practical exploitation rate of escaped candidates unclear. - Multi-hop training collapses after 2 epochs for all methods, suggesting reward hacking or instability that BES does not fully address; this dynamics question goes unanalyzed. Bottom Line Any practitioner designing search-based post-training pipelines or inference-time compute strategies for hard reasoning tasks will find concrete, implementable contributions here — the entropy-shell analysis sharpens intuition about why evolutionary recombination helps and the MuSiQue results on models where GRPO fails are the strongest evidence — though the partial ablations and small-scale post-training experiments mean this should be read as a strong empirical proof-of-concept rather than a definitive characterization of the method's properties.

Frontier: Towards Comprehensive and Accurate LLM Inference Simulation

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Frontier, a discrete-event simulator for modern LLM inference serving that models disaggregated architectures (Prefill-Decode Disaggregation and Attention-FFN Disaggregation), runtime optimizations (CUDA Graph, speculative decoding, prefix caching), and stateful workloads (multi-round reasoning, agentic tool calls, RL rollouts). - Why it's important: Existing simulators make configuration recommendations that violate real SLAs in practice — average-case models produce up to 51.7% end-to-end latency error and can select configurations that fail SLA constraints on real hardware, undermining the core purpose of simulation-driven capacity planning. - How it does it: The simulator replaces the monolithic-replica abstraction with role-specific cluster workers whose cross-cluster dependencies (KV-cache transfer, activation ping-pong) are materialized as explicit discrete events, and replaces token-count proxies with class-specific ML predictors (random forests over distributional batch features for attention, load-balance statistics for MoE GroupedGEMM). - The good: Frontier reduces E2E latency error from 51.7% to 2.6% under PDD disaggregation and from 44.9% to 6.4% under co-location, compared to the prior state-of-the-art simulators, validated on a real 16-GPU H800 testbed with vLLM v0.10.2. - The missing: Use-case results — including the 30.4% aTTFT reduction from the phase-aware H2Q-BR scheduler and the 2.04× RL rollout throughput gain from dynamic layout switching — are demonstrated entirely in simulation and never validated on actual hardware, which is a significant credibility gap for a tool whose primary value is replacing expensive hardware experiments. - Read it or not: Systems researchers and infrastructure engineers who configure, evaluate, or build disaggregated LLM serving stacks should read this; practitioners looking for validated scheduling algorithms should treat the §6 use cases as design hypotheses, not confirmed results.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Frontier, a discrete-event simulator for modern LLM inference serving that models disaggregated architectures (Prefill-Decode Disaggregation and Attention-FFN Disaggregation), runtime optimizations (CUDA Graph, speculative decoding, prefix caching), and stateful workloads (multi-round reasoning, agentic tool calls, RL rollouts). - Why it's important: Existing simulators make configuration recommendations that violate real SLAs in practice — average-case models produce up to 51.7% end-to-end latency error and can select configurations that fail SLA constraints on real hardware, undermining the core purpose of simulation-driven capacity planning. - How it does it: The simulator replaces the monolithic-replica abstraction with role-specific cluster workers whose cross-cluster dependencies (KV-cache transfer, activation ping-pong) are materialized as explicit discrete events, and replaces token-count proxies with class-specific ML predictors (random forests over distributional batch features for attention, load-balance statistics for MoE GroupedGEMM). - The good: Frontier reduces E2E latency error from 51.7% to 2.6% under PDD disaggregation and from 44.9% to 6.4% under co-location, compared to the prior state-of-the-art simulators, validated on a real 16-GPU H800 testbed with vLLM v0.10.2. - The missing: Use-case results — including the 30.4% aTTFT reduction from the phase-aware H2Q-BR scheduler and the 2.04× RL rollout throughput gain from dynamic layout switching — are demonstrated entirely in simulation and never validated on actual hardware, which is a significant credibility gap for a tool whose primary value is replacing expensive hardware experiments. - Read it or not: Systems researchers and infrastructure engineers who configure, evaluate, or build disaggregated LLM serving stacks should read this; practitioners looking for validated scheduling algorithms should treat the §6 use cases as design hypotheses, not confirmed results. Problem & Motivation LLM inference has become the dominant operational cost for service providers, and the configuration space for modern serving systems is enormous — over 1,000 combinations for a 200B-parameter MoE model, with sweeping 100 configurations on a 64-GPU H100 cluster costing $128,000–$180,000. Simulation is the natural tool for design-space exploration, but existing simulators (Vidur, AIConfigurator, APEX, LLMServingSim2.0) fail in two ways that make them unreliable for production decisions. First, they assume a homogeneous set of monolithic replicas, which cannot represent disaggregated serving architectures (PDD, AFD) where different GPU pools play distinct roles connected by explicit KV-cache or activation transfers. Second, they smooth serving dynamics into average-case models: token-count proxies cause 32.6% mean FlashAttention latency error and 21.0% mean MoE GroupedGEMM error, while "total minus weights" KV-cache budget estimation overestimates the admissible budget by 8.1%–39.73% across configurations — errors large enough to shift TTFT/TPOT estimates across SLA boundaries and recommend configurations that fail on real systems. Key Idea The core insight is that LLM serving is a closed-loop system in which operator runtime errors, KV-cache budget errors, and scheduler admission decisions compound: a 0.1 ms per-operator error propagates across hundreds of decode steps, the scheduler uses the KV-cache budget to bound concurrent decode slots, and batch composition is determined online, making the execution space infeasible to profile exhaustively. The key technical move is to resolve all three sources of error together — materializing disaggregated dependencies as explicit events in the DES graph (so the event ordering enforces correctness), and classifying operators by what actually determines their runtime (token count, per-request length distribution, or expert routing load) so that the right ML predictor feature set is applied to each class. Methodology Frontier is organized into four modules. The Control Plane compiles a serving specification into role-specific Cluster Workers: co-location yields a monolithic cluster C; PDD yields prefill cluster P and decode cluster D; AFD yields P plus separate attention cluster A and FFN cluster F. Parallelism is expressed via a two-domain decomposition with attention sharding tuple (tp_attn, dp_attn) and FFN sharding tuple (tp_ffn, ep_ffn). Five dependency classes are materialized as explicit events: (i) pipeline stage chaining, (ii) PDD KV-cache transfer (KVCacheTransferStart/End causal edges), (iii) AFD per-layer M2N activation transfer ping-pong between A and F, (iv) MoE EP synchronization (AllToAllCombineReady fires only after the slowest rank), and (v) stateful reasoning requeue (ThinkingRequeue event re-admits the request with session affinity for prefix-cache continuity). The Execution Plane lifts production scheduler logic from vLLM and SGLang unchanged, rewiring only the I/O layer; Runtime Adapters inject CUDA Graph (padding to next bin, switching between kernel-only and launch-inclusive timing families), speculative decoding MTP (per-request planned/verified/accepted/committed token tracking), prefix caching (block-hash index), and chunked prefill. The Fidelity Plane applies three operator classes with distinct predictors: token-count operators (linear ops) use linear regression or small random forest on num_tokens; sequence-dependent operators (attention) use random forest over aggregate counts plus distributional statistics of per-request lengths (min, max, percentiles of prefill and decode lengths); routing-dependent operators (MoE GroupedGEMM) use random forest over load-balance statistics (variance and max of token-to-expert counts, expert selection ratio). Memory capacity is estimated via a vLLM-style dummy profile run on a single GPU, measuring weight memory, torch peak increase, and non-torch residency (NCCL workspace, CUDA Graph capture regions); the KV-cache block budget subtracts all three from the requested GPU budget. The Comm. Backend is pluggable (ASTRA-Sim or HTSim), selected by domain scale. The system is implemented in approximately 70K lines of Python, with 808 typed CLI options. Key Results All fidelity experiments use vLLM v0.10.2 (V1 engine) as ground truth on two 8×H800-SXM GPU servers. Operator-level: BF16 p50/p95 relative error is 3.5%/14.2% for attention, 3.3%/6.4% for linear ops, 1.4%/5.3% for MoE GroupedGEMM; Vidur's token-only predictor reaches 55.4%/376.1% for attention and is undefined for GroupedGEMM. FP8 shifts attention p95 to 8.8%. Memory: Initial KV-cache block budget is within 1.89% of vLLM across all four (PP,TP,DP,EP) configurations tested; the analytical baseline overestimates by 14.10%–39.73%. Runtime optimizations: CUDA Graph decode speedup is matched within 1.7% (co-location) and 6.1% (PDD); prefix-cache final hit ratios match exactly (36.98% and 37.11%); MTP speculative decoding p95 errors stay within 11.28% across six configurations (verify lengths {2,8,32} × acceptance ratios {0.3,0.7}). End-to-end: Under co-location, Frontier tracks vLLM within 9.37% across 32 cases (dense and MoE, four workload patterns), with TPOT-p95 and E2E inside 6.4% in all but one; AIConfigurator produces E2E makespan errors up to 170% on dense decode-heavy. Under PDD, all 32 entries are within 10.99% with 20 of 32 inside 3%; AIConfigurator reaches 200% E2E error on both dense and MoE decode-heavy (architectural failure due to missing KV-cache transfer dependency).
