Under AFD (Step3-316B, 16 H800s), TPOT is within 6.4% and throughput within 7.0% for both FFN-TP and FFN-EP layouts. Frontier scales to 1K+ GPU simulations on commodity CPU hardware (wall-clock time vs. cluster size is not benchmarked). Four use cases are presented using ex-situ simulation only: SLA-aware Pareto frontier comparison shows PDD outperforms at loose TTFT ≤3000ms (137.4K tok/s) while AFD is better at tight TTFT ≤500ms (116.2K tok/s vs. PDD 100.7K tok/s); heterogeneous GPU allocation shows H20 decode pool in PDD at P:D=1:1 passes SLA (CE=1.33) but P:D=2:6 fails SLA despite SR=1.69; a phase-aware H2Q-BR scheduler achieves 30.4% p95 aTTFT reduction over vLLM default; dynamic RL rollout reconfiguration halves makespan from 528.8 s to 259.1 s with 2.04× decode throughput. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The disaggregated abstraction — compiling serving specifications into role-specific cluster workers with explicit event-graph dependencies for PDD/AFD/MoE-EP — is genuinely novel as a simulator architecture. The class-specific operator predictor design (distributional batch features for attention, load statistics for MoE routing) is a real methodological contribution to simulation fidelity. Individual components (DES simulation, random forests, vLLM scheduler logic) are not new, but the specific co-design of architectural completeness and decision-grade fidelity with quantified evidence for each failure mode is original and practically significant. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are partial: each fidelity component (operator accuracy, memory budget, CUDA Graph, prefix cache, speculative decoding) is evaluated separately before the end-to-end comparison, which supports attribution. However, error bars and confidence intervals are absent throughout — all numbers are point estimates from single runs. The testbed is 16 H800 GPUs; the claimed 1K+ GPU scalability is stated but not benchmarked (simulation wall-clock as a function of cluster size is not reported). AFD calibration uses an in-house Step3-316B implementation with no publicly available ground truth, limiting independent verification. Code is not announced as publicly released; the paper describes 70K LoC Python but provides no repository. The use-case experiments (§6) are simulation-only with no hardware validation — the H2Q-BR scheduler (30.4% aTTFT reduction) and RL reconfiguration (2.04× throughput) are presented as findings of the simulator, but the simulator itself is the artifact being evaluated, creating a circularity. Comparisons are against current open-source simulator versions as of April 2026, with discrepancies resolved against repositories rather than papers, which is methodologically reasonable but creates an asymmetric characterization of competitors. Deployability (SYSTEMS) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built simulation tool evaluated through deployment fidelity comparisons. - Practical: Frontier can simulate 1K+ GPU clusters on commodity CPUs; single-GPU profiling is sufficient to generate operator and memory predictors for any cluster scale. - Efficient: Single-GPU dummy profile runs replace full-cluster hardware sweeps; the 70K LoC Python implementation may impose non-trivial simulation wall-clock for very large-scale or long-horizon traces, but this is uncharacterized. - Effective: Evidence of solving the stated problem is strong — sub-10% TTFT/TPOT errors across co-location and PDD on real hardware for two model families and four workload patterns; the SLA decision drift experiment (Figure 5) concretely demonstrates that prior simulators recommend configurations that violate SLA on real hardware. - Scalable: 1K+ GPU simulation on commodity CPU is claimed but not measured; the single-representative-rank PP profiling approach is acknowledged to contribute residual errors in pipeline-parallel configurations, and no results above 256 GPU equivalents are presented. The work delivers a software artifact (Python simulator), though no public release is announced. Strengths - The motivation section (§2.2) establishes each failure mode of existing simulators with real vLLM measurements — CUDA Graph padding inflation (22.6%–57.2% extra tokens), KV budget overestimation (up to 39.73%), and MTP sign-mismatches (4 of 5 low-acceptance batches predicted to slow down when they actually speed up) — grounding the design choices in quantified evidence rather than intuition. - The operator classification into token-count / sequence-dependent / routing-dependent classes, each with appropriate ML predictor feature sets, is well-motivated: Vidur's single token-count regressor has 376.1% p95 attention error precisely because attention runtime is shaped by per-request length distribution inside a batch, not aggregate token count, and the paper correctly identifies and fixes this. - The disaggregated abstraction correctly identifies that PDD/AFD are not expressible by adding latency terms to a monolithic-replica model — the event graph changes fundamentally, and AIConfigurator's 200% PDD error is a direct consequence of the missing KV-cache transfer causal edge, not parameter miscalibration. - The four use cases demonstrate analysis that is not reproducible with any prior simulator: the SLA-conditional Pareto frontier reversal (PDD preferred at loose TTFT, AFD preferred at tight TTFT) is a decision that would be made incorrectly with prior tools, and the gate-based heterogeneous GPU analysis (H20 on FFN fails, H20 on attention passes) is a role-level finding that requires the disaggregated cost model. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) Frontier is primarily calibrated around vLLM, with SGLang and TensorRT-LLM support deferred to future work; (2) CPU overhead modeling may lack robustness as it fluctuates with system scale and architectural updates, and CUDA API interception is discussed as a more accurate alternative but debated for scalability across diverse CUDA versions; (3) profiling tools introduce intrusive overhead, requiring strict isolation of fine-grained metrics from system-level metrics; (4) AFD calibration uses an in-house implementation because no public AFD implementation exists. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The use-case scheduling and reconfiguration results (§6.3 H2Q-BR: 30.4% aTTFT reduction; §6.4 layout switch: 2.04× throughput) are validated entirely within the simulator whose fidelity is the subject of the paper — no hardware deployment validates these policies. The paper's own motivation (Figure 5) shows that prior simulators recommend configurations that fail on real hardware; the same risk applies here, yet the use cases present simulation outcomes as findings without this caveat. - The testbed (16 H800 GPUs) is small relative to the claimed use cases (256-GPU and 1K-GPU scenarios), meaning all large-scale results are produced by a tool validated only at 16-GPU scale — extrapolation risk is substantial and unquantified. - Simulation wall-clock time as a function of cluster size, trace length, and workload complexity is not reported, making it impossible to assess whether 1K-GPU simulation is practically feasible for realistic production traces. - The H2Q-BR scheduler comparison is thin: only vLLM default and skip-join MLFQ are compared; no other stateful scheduling baselines are evaluated. - Operator predictor training data size and train/test split methodology are not reported; it is unclear whether predictors generalize to operators and batch compositions outside the profiling distribution. - Results are limited to two MoE model families and one dense model; generalizability to other architectures (different expert counts, routing strategies, dense models at scale) is assumed. Bottom Line A practitioner designing disaggregated LLM serving infrastructure or trying to understand why their simulator's configuration recommendations fail on real hardware will get substantial, quantified value from this paper — but should treat the §6 use-case policy results as simulation-validated hypotheses requiring hardware confirmation before deployment.

SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SkillOpt, a text-space optimizer that trains a frozen agent's natural-language skill document using rollout batches, bounded add/delete/replace edits, a held-out validation gate, a rejected-edit buffer, and an epoch-wise slow/meta update — applying deep-learning training discipline to a portable text artifact. - Why it's important: Weight fine-tuning is unavailable for closed frontier models and expensive for open ones, yet hand-crafted or one-shot-generated skills are brittle; agents operating on procedural tasks (spreadsheet manipulation, embodied navigation, enterprise QA) need reliable iterative domain adaptation without touching model weights. - How it does it: A separate optimizer model reads scored trajectory batches and proposes structured edits; a textual learning-rate budget caps how many edits are applied per step; a held-out selection gate accepts only candidates that strictly improve validation score; rejected edits feed back as negative evidence in the same epoch. - The good: SkillOpt is best or tied-best across all 52 evaluated (model, benchmark, harness) cells, achieving average +23.5 absolute points over no-skill on GPT-5.5 direct chat and demonstrating Codex→Claude Code cross-harness transfer of +59.7 on SpreadsheetBench — the strongest transfer result in the paper and the clearest evidence that the artifact encodes transferable procedural knowledge. - The missing: No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported anywhere; several "wins" in Table 1 are margins of ≤1 percentage point, and the 52/52 best-or-tied claim counts effective ties at that precision — the paper does not distinguish genuinely decisive wins from marginal ones. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners building task-specific agents on closed frontier models who want a reproducible, no-weight-update adaptation procedure; less useful for those seeking theoretical guarantees or working outside the procedural-task regime where the method's gains are largest.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SkillOpt, a text-space optimizer that trains a frozen agent's natural-language skill document using rollout batches, bounded add/delete/replace edits, a held-out validation gate, a rejected-edit buffer, and an epoch-wise slow/meta update — applying deep-learning training discipline to a portable text artifact. - Why it's important: Weight fine-tuning is unavailable for closed frontier models and expensive for open ones, yet hand-crafted or one-shot-generated skills are brittle; agents operating on procedural tasks (spreadsheet manipulation, embodied navigation, enterprise QA) need reliable iterative domain adaptation without touching model weights. - How it does it: A separate optimizer model reads scored trajectory batches and proposes structured edits; a textual learning-rate budget caps how many edits are applied per step; a held-out selection gate accepts only candidates that strictly improve validation score; rejected edits feed back as negative evidence in the same epoch. - The good: SkillOpt is best or tied-best across all 52 evaluated (model, benchmark, harness) cells, achieving average +23.5 absolute points over no-skill on GPT-5.5 direct chat and demonstrating Codex→Claude Code cross-harness transfer of +59.7 on SpreadsheetBench — the strongest transfer result in the paper and the clearest evidence that the artifact encodes transferable procedural knowledge. - The missing: No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported anywhere; several "wins" in Table 1 are margins of ≤1 percentage point, and the 52/52 best-or-tied claim counts effective ties at that precision — the paper does not distinguish genuinely decisive wins from marginal ones. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners building task-specific agents on closed frontier models who want a reproducible, no-weight-update adaptation procedure; less useful for those seeking theoretical guarantees or working outside the procedural-task regime where the method's gains are largest. Problem & Motivation: Frontier LLMs are increasingly deployed as agents inside tool-use harnesses (Codex, Claude Code) and multi-turn execution loops where success requires domain-specific procedures: correct spreadsheet manipulation idioms, structured answer formatting, search-frontier discipline in embodied environments, and precise evidence binding in document QA. None of these procedures are reliably captured by model weights alone, and fine-tuning closed models is infeasible. Hand-crafted and one-shot LLM-generated skills are brittle under harness changes and cannot correct themselves after observing failures. Prior skill-evolution systems (Trace2Skill, EvoSkill) mine trajectories or evolve skill folders but lack validation gates, bounded update controls, and negative-feedback memory, leaving the central question open: if skills are the domain-adaptation layer, how should they be optimized? Key Idea: The core insight is that a natural-language skill document can be treated as the external trainable state of a frozen agent, with the optimizer analogized to weight-space gradient descent: rollout batch size controls evidence noise, a bounded edit budget controls step size, a held-out selection gate plays the role of validation, rejected edits carry negative feedback analogous to gradient history, and an epoch-wise slow/meta update captures long-horizon regularities. The key move is that bounded, validation-gated updates keep adjacent skill versions close enough that the optimizer's history of accepted and rejected edits remains informative — unlike unbounded rewriting, which erases continuity. Methodology: The pipeline operates on three fixed splits (train, selection, test) and two model roles (frozen target, separate optimizer). Each optimization step proceeds as: (1) the target model executes a rollout batch of size B=40 with the current skill; (2) the optimizer model partitions trajectories into failures and successes, analyzes each in minibatches of size Bm=8, and proposes add/delete/replace patch edits; (3) edits from multiple minibatches are merged hierarchically (failure-first priority) and ranked by expected utility; (4) at most Lt edits (the textual learning rate, default 4, cosine-decayed) are applied to form a candidate skill; (5) the candidate is evaluated on the selection split — accepted only if strictly greater than the current selection score, otherwise rejected and logged to an epoch-local buffer; (6) at epoch boundaries, the slow/meta update compares the same tasks under adjacent epoch skills, writes longitudinal guidance into a markup-protected section of the skill document (also validated through the selection gate), and updates an optimizer-side meta skill that guides future reflection calls but is never shipped with the deployed artifact. The deployed output is a single best_skill.md of 300–2,000 tokens. Critical design decisions: (i) bounded edit budget vs. unconstrained rewriting — well-justified, ablations confirm +2.5–22.5 point degradation without it; (ii) strict greater-than acceptance criterion (ties rejected) — well-justified to prevent silent drift; (iii) separate slow-update protected region preventing fast edits from overwriting epoch-level lessons — well-justified, ablation shows −22.5 on SpreadsheetBench when removed. Key Results: On GPT-5.5 direct chat across six benchmarks, SkillOpt lifts SearchQA 77.7→87.3, SpreadsheetBench 41.8→80.7, OfficeQA 33.1→72.1, DocVQA 78.8→91.2, LiveMathematicianBench 37.6→66.9, ALFWorld 83.6→95.5, for a +23.5 average gain over no skill and +5.4 over an oracle that picks the strongest of six competing methods per cell. On GPT-5.5 inside the Codex harness the average gain over no skill is +24.8, and inside Claude Code +19.1. Across all seven direct-chat target models the average per-model improvement is approximately +17.6 points. The cross-harness transfer result is the most striking: a SpreadsheetBench skill trained inside Codex transfers to Claude Code with +59.7 absolute gain over the Claude Code no-skill baseline (22.1→81.8), slightly exceeding the in-domain Claude Code SkillOpt reference of 80.4. All cross-model and cross-benchmark transfer rows are positive. Training cost ranges from 20.8M tokens total (OfficeQA, 1 accepted edit, +39.0 points) to 213.8M tokens (SearchQA, 4 accepted edits, +9.6 points), with 1–4 median accepted edits across benchmarks — evidence that the validation gate filters aggressively. The strongest baselines (GEPA, EvoSkill) are strong competitors: GEPA reaches 73.6 on SpreadsheetBench for GPT-5.5 vs. SkillOpt's 80.7; EvoSkill reaches 75.0 on Codex SpreadsheetBench vs. SkillOpt's 85.0. All evaluated benchmarks are held-out test splits disjoint from the validation split used to gate edits. No error bars or significance tests are reported anywhere in the paper. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is the training-style formulation of skill optimization: the bounded edit budget, strict held-out gate, rejected-edit buffer as negative feedback, and protected slow/meta update are a coherent system that has no prior counterpart for text-artifact training. The empirical finding that 1–4 accepted edits suffice for large gains, and that artifacts transfer across harnesses and model scales, is also new. What is not novel: trajectory-driven reflection (Reflexion, Self-Refine), prompt optimization (TextGrad, GEPA), and skill construction from experience (Trace2Skill, EvoSkill) are all prior work that SkillOpt explicitly positions against. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: learning-rate form, rejected buffer, slow/meta update, training set size, batch size, and schedule are each swept. The ablations are run on three benchmarks (SearchQA, SpreadsheetBench, LiveMath) using GPT-5.5 as both target and optimizer, which is adequate breadth but not comprehensive. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported on any result. Several margins in Table 1 are ≤1 percentage point (e.g., SkillOpt 47.1 vs. LLM skill 46.5 on Qwen3.6-35B-A3B OfficeQA) making the "52/52 best or tied" claim partly a consequence of measurement precision rather than robust superiority. Code is publicly released at https://aka.ms/SkillOpt. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.23904v2, May 2026), not peer reviewed. Several baselines (OfficeQA Pro, LiveMathematicianBench) are themselves 2026 preprints, making the benchmark landscape less settled. The use of GPT-5.5 as both target and optimizer in the primary results introduces a potential same-family confound, though Table 5 partially addresses this with target-matched optimizer experiments. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs entirely via standard LLM API calls on commercially available models (GPT, Qwen); no specialized hardware required for either training or deployment. - Efficient: Training costs 20.8M–213.8M tokens per benchmark per model (a one-time expense); the deployed artifact is a static text file adding zero inference-time overhead, which is a material deployment advantage over methods that require optimizer calls at serve time. - Effective: Held-out test scores confirm that gains generalize beyond the validation split (Figure 3 shows validation checkpoint selection correlates with test performance); the largest absolute gains (+39 points on OfficeQA) appear on procedural benchmarks with strict format requirements where the method's learned rules are most directly applicable. - Scalable: The method is tested on benchmark-sized training pools (35–200 examples per epoch); behavior at 10×–100× scale is not evaluated, and the paper's own limitation note acknowledges that a single skill may be insufficient for highly heterogeneous domains requiring many disjoint procedures. The work delivers a software artifact (released code at https://aka.ms/SkillOpt) plus benchmark-specific best_skill.md artifacts described qualitatively in the paper. Strengths: - The deep-learning analogy is operationally precise rather than decorative: each component (edit budget, selection gate, rejected buffer, slow update) maps to a training-loop primitive with a clear justification and an ablation confirming its contribution. - Transfer experiments across three axes (model scale, harness, benchmark) are the paper's most original empirical contribution; the +59.7 Codex→Claude Code SpreadsheetBench transfer is striking and suggests the learned rules encode domain knowledge that is not harness-specific. - The "edit economy" finding — that +39 points on OfficeQA arises from a single accepted edit — is a surprising and credible result that directly supports the validation-gate design and distinguishes SkillOpt from prompt-inflation approaches. - The qualitative skill inspection (Figure 4, Section 4.5) is unusually informative: verbatim learned rules are procedural, generalizable, and interpretable, providing a sanity check that the optimizer is not simply overfitting to benchmark quirks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge in Appendix B: (1) dependence on automatic verifiers — the method is less applicable to open-ended tasks without reliable feedback; (2) non-trivial training cost despite being amortized at deployment; (3) single-skill design may be insufficient for highly heterogeneous domains; (4) optimized skills can encode training-distribution heuristics that require careful evaluation before transferring to substantially different settings. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 52/52 best-or-tied claim does not distinguish decisive wins from marginal ones (≤1 percentage point differences appear in multiple cells) and no statistical tests are provided — a reviewer would reasonably push back on this as a headline claim. The evaluation is conducted entirely with GPT and Qwen model families in 2026; the method's behavior with open-weight models at the scale of Llama-3 70B or Mistral families is untested. The paper benchmarks against EvoSkill only in the harness settings, not in direct chat (presumably because EvoSkill is specifically a harness-side system), which means the strongest evolution baseline is missing from the largest portion of the main table. Training token costs for SearchQA (213.8M tokens total) and DocVQA (188.2M tokens total) are non-trivial at current API pricing and are not discussed in the context of cost-effectiveness relative to simply fine-tuning a smaller open model. The GPT-5.5 optimizer optimizing GPT-5.5 target creates a potential self-referential advantage that the Table 5 target-matched experiment only partially addresses — a cross-family optimizer experiment (e.g., Claude as optimizer, GPT as target) would be more diagnostic. Bottom Line: A practitioner deploying frontier agents on procedural tasks should read this paper: the method is immediately actionable, the code is public, and the empirical scope is unusually broad, with the cross-harness transfer result being the most practically significant finding — though the absence of error bars and the reliance on 2026-vintage closed models means independent replication will be needed before treating the 52/52 claim as settled.

Quest: Training Frontier Deep Research Agents with Fully Synthetic Tasks

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Quest, a family of open-weight deep research agents (2B–35B parameters) trained using a fully synthetic data pipeline based on hierarchical rubric trees, a structured context management mechanism, and a staged MT→SFT→RL training recipe. - Why it's important: Frontier deep research agents (OpenAI DeepResearch, Gemini) remain proprietary and opaque; existing open alternatives cover only narrow capability slices (fact-seeking or report synthesis, not both), leaving the field without a reproducible general-purpose baseline. - How it does it: The core mechanism is a rubric tree — a hierarchically decomposed, auto-verifiable specification of correctness — that enables generating 8K synthetic training tasks with fine-grained, automated rewards covering fact-seeking, citation grounding, and open-ended report synthesis jointly. - The good: Quest-35B achieves 80.8% on GAIA-Text (vs. GPT-5 at 76.4%), 48.2% on DeepResearch Bench (vs. OpenAI-DR at 47.0%), and 30.7% on Mind2Web 2 (vs. OpenAI-DR at 28.0%), narrowing or closing the open-weight/proprietary gap across three distinct capability dimensions simultaneously. - The missing: The training and inference pipelines depend on proprietary models (GPT-5-mini as context condenser and visit summarizer, Claude Sonnet 4.5 for data generation, Tongyi-DR for trajectory distillation), which means "fully open" overstates reproducibility; no contamination analysis is provided despite training data being crawled from live web content that may overlap with evaluated benchmarks. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or studying autonomous web agents — the rubric-tree synthesis and structured context-state mechanism are genuinely novel components and the full release (models, data, training scripts) provides a credible public reference for future work.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Quest, a family of open-weight deep research agents (2B–35B parameters) trained using a fully synthetic data pipeline based on hierarchical rubric trees, a structured context management mechanism, and a staged MT→SFT→RL training recipe. - Why it's important: Frontier deep research agents (OpenAI DeepResearch, Gemini) remain proprietary and opaque; existing open alternatives cover only narrow capability slices (fact-seeking or report synthesis, not both), leaving the field without a reproducible general-purpose baseline. - How it does it: The core mechanism is a rubric tree — a hierarchically decomposed, auto-verifiable specification of correctness — that enables generating 8K synthetic training tasks with fine-grained, automated rewards covering fact-seeking, citation grounding, and open-ended report synthesis jointly. - The good: Quest-35B achieves 80.8% on GAIA-Text (vs. GPT-5 at 76.4%), 48.2% on DeepResearch Bench (vs. OpenAI-DR at 47.0%), and 30.7% on Mind2Web 2 (vs. OpenAI-DR at 28.0%), narrowing or closing the open-weight/proprietary gap across three distinct capability dimensions simultaneously. - The missing: The training and inference pipelines depend on proprietary models (GPT-5-mini as context condenser and visit summarizer, Claude Sonnet 4.5 for data generation, Tongyi-DR for trajectory distillation), which means "fully open" overstates reproducibility; no contamination analysis is provided despite training data being crawled from live web content that may overlap with evaluated benchmarks. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or studying autonomous web agents — the rubric-tree synthesis and structured context-state mechanism are genuinely novel components and the full release (models, data, training scripts) provides a credible public reference for future work. Problem & Motivation: Web-scale information-seeking tasks — locating obscure facts, synthesizing multi-source reports, and grounding claims in verifiable citations — require autonomous agents that can plan, search, verify, and revise over long horizons. Existing proprietary systems (OpenAI DeepResearch, Gemini Deep Research) handle these tasks well but share neither models nor training recipes. Open-weight alternatives (Tongyi-DR, OpenResearcher, DR Tulu) each address a narrow slice: Tongyi-DR focuses on fact-seeking via single-answer supervision; OpenResearcher concentrates on citation grounding; DR Tulu on report synthesis. None provides a unified recipe covering all three capabilities, and none releases a fully reproducible training pipeline. The deeper problem is that synthesizing training data with verifiable rewards for open-ended research tasks (where there is no single correct answer) has resisted clean solutions — prior single-answer approaches provide binary rewards that are insufficient to guide multi-aspect report quality. Key Idea: The central insight is that any deep research task — whether it has a unique factual answer or requires a multi-page synthesized report — can be formalized as a rubric tree: a hierarchical decomposition of correctness criteria where leaf nodes are directly auto-verifiable (factual match, URL presence, source attribution) and internal nodes aggregate their children's scores. Once this representation exists, a single synthetic pipeline can generate diverse tasks, evaluate agent responses with fine-grained partial credit, and provide rubric-tree-shaped rewards for reinforcement learning — all without human annotation. Methodology: The pipeline has four components. (1) Data synthesis: trending Google keywords seed autonomous web exploration by Claude Sonnet 4.5 (Gsyn), which generates queries, derives verifiable constraints, and organizes them into rubric trees. Objective tasks receive Python evaluation scripts (generated by GPT-5) that programmatically verify each leaf node; open-ended tasks fix four top-level criteria (instruction following, comprehensiveness, readability, insight) and use pairwise scoring against a reference report generated by Gsyn, with the final score defined as J(rcand)/(J(rcand)+J(rref)) to avoid reference-bias inflation. Starting from ~20K raw tasks, strict filtering yields 8,228 high-quality SFT/RL instances. (2) Context management: a Context Condenser (GPT-5-mini) triggers once the active context exceeds a threshold, compressing history into a structured JSON "Context State" with three typed buckets — Trusted (verified facts with source URLs), Untrusted (contradicted claims with reason), and Uncertain (pending hypotheses with follow-up actions). Sessions restart from this state. (3) Training: mid-training (MT) on 300K context summarization examples and 1M+ HTML extraction examples adapts the base model to structured context formats. SFT trains on trajectories from Tongyi-DR distillation (session-level decomposition enables training on 200K-token trajectories on 16 H100 GPUs). RL uses GRPO with a composite reward R = 0.75·s_rubric + 0.25·min(s_fact, s_rubric), where s_fact measures the fraction of inline citations verified as supported by GPT-5-mini. (4) Infrastructure: dual-cache (exact + FAISS semantic fallback) for search/visit calls and a fully asynchronous RL pipeline built on VERL decouple rollout, evaluation, and training, accommodating rubric-tree evaluations that average 4 minutes and spike to 30 minutes. Critical design decisions: (a) session-level training instead of full-trajectory training is well-justified by GPU memory constraints (200K-token trajectories become tractable); (b) pairwise normalization for open-ended scoring over pointwise or ternary win/tie/lose is well-justified by empirical failure modes documented in Section 7.4; (c) excluding KL penalty from GRPO is stated but not ablated. Key Results: Quest-35B (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B base) on the full test sets: BrowseComp 64.6% (with discard-all, 45.5% otherwise) vs. OpenAI-DR 51.5%, Claude Opus 4.5 67.8%; BrowseComp-Plus 69.5% vs. OpenAI-DR not reported, Tongyi-DR 44.5%; Mind2Web 2 30.7% vs. OpenAI-DR 28.0%, Tongyi-DR 16.7%; WideSearch 60.6% vs. GPT-5 54.0%, Tongyi-DR 37.3%; HLE 37.2% vs. Claude Opus 4.5 43.2%, GPT-5 35.2%; GAIA 80.8% vs. GPT-5 76.4%; DeepResearch Bench 48.2% vs. OpenAI-DR 47.0%, Tongyi-DR 40.5%; LiveResearchBench 68.2% vs. GPT-5 73.1%, Tongyi-DR 56.3%. At equivalent 30B scale, Quest-30B beats Tongyi-DR and OpenResearcher on 4/8 benchmarks. Quest-2B-SFT scores 30.3% on HLE and 72.8% on GAIA, competitive with or exceeding OpenAI o3 (24.9% HLE, 70.5% GAIA). Ablations show SFT alone degrades open-ended tasks; MT recovers them; RL provides the largest boost for report synthesis but mildly hurts HLE and GAIA (alignment tax). Baselines are current and strong for open-weight comparisons; however, frontier proprietary agents (GPT-5, Claude Opus 4.5) have missing values across several benchmarks, making head-to-head comparisons incomplete. Ablations use sampled subsets (e.g., BrowseComp: 130 of 1,266), which may inflate variance. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The rubric-tree synthesis framework is genuinely novel as a unified mechanism for generating verifiably-graded training data across both objective and open-ended research tasks without human annotation. The structured three-bucket Context State as an explicit epistemic encoding of an agent's research state extends prior context management work (discard-all, keep-last-N) in a principled direction. The full open release (Quest-8K dataset, models 2B–35B, training scripts) is a practical contribution given that no prior open deep research agent released all three components together. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover the effect of each training stage (Vanilla → SFT → MT+SFT → MT+SFT+RL) and model size (2B, 4B, 9B, 35B). No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported for any benchmark result; most ablations use subsets rather than full test sets, and subset sizes (130 examples for BrowseComp) are small enough for noise to influence comparisons. Code, data (Quest-8K), and model weights (Quest-35B-RL at HuggingFace) are publicly released. A notable methodological concern: the training data is generated by web crawling and autonomous exploration, and the evaluated benchmarks (BrowseComp, HLE, GAIA) also draw from public web sources; no contamination analysis is presented. A second concern: BrowseComp results are reported under two different protocols (discard-all giving 64.6% vs. standard giving 45.5%); the headline Figure 1 number uses discard-all while the main table reports both, which requires careful reading to avoid conflation. The manual examination of evaluation scripts (50 samples, 4 annotators) found 6/50 rubric-related errors and 2/50 non-executable scripts — a non-trivial error rate that could introduce noisy rewards. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built open-weight model family evaluated by deployment on benchmarks). - Practical: Quest-2B and Quest-4B run on commodity GPUs; Quest-35B uses a MoE architecture (3B active parameters out of 35B total) so inference fits on hardware that can serve a standard 7B-dense model, making deployment practical. - Efficient: Training Quest-35B requires 32 H100 GPUs for ~5 days (MT) + ~1 day (SFT) + RL; inference relies on GPT-5-mini (via API) for context condensation and page visit summarization, introducing ongoing API cost and a proprietary external dependency that is not fully replaceable without retraining. - Effective: Evidence across 8 diverse benchmarks shows the method solves all three claimed capability dimensions; the clearest margin over open-weight baselines is on citation grounding (Mind2Web 2: 30.7% vs. 16.7% for Tongyi-DR) and report synthesis (DeepResearch Bench: 48.2% vs. 35.4% for OpenResearcher). - Scalable: The context management mechanism is designed to extrapolate to arbitrarily long horizons (demonstrated at >200 turns during inference despite training on ≤100-turn trajectories), but no benchmark in the evaluation explicitly stress-tests very-long-horizon tasks (hours-long research sessions); scaling to larger model sizes is explored only through SFT, not the full MT+SFT+RL recipe. The work delivers trained model weights (HuggingFace), training scripts (GitHub), and a synthetic dataset (Quest-8K) — a complete software artifact. Strengths: - The rubric-tree formalism is a clean unification of objective and open-ended task supervision; the hierarchical partial-credit score provides a richer RL signal than binary exact-match while remaining fully automated. - The structured Context State (trusted/untrusted/uncertain) goes beyond prior discard-all and keep-last-N strategies by preserving epistemic distinctions — a genuinely useful inductive bias for long-horizon research agents. - Section 7 documents four categories of unsuccessful attempts (search result prediction in MT, rubric-based error identification, DPO for report comparison, evaluation design failures) with hypotheses, which is unusually candid and provides actionable guidance for the field. - Full open release of models, data, and training code substantially lowers the barrier for follow-on research in a space dominated by proprietary systems. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that RL slightly sacrifices HLE and GAIA performance (alignment tax from specialization toward report synthesis behaviors). They acknowledge that small models (2B) underperform on open-ended tasks. The section on unsuccessful attempts acknowledges instability in DPO training for long-form comparisons and the failure of pointwise scoring. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The training pipeline depends on GPT-5-mini (context condensation, visit summarization, fact-checking reward computation, citation insertion) and Claude Sonnet 4.5 (data generation) at multiple stages. Despite being called "fully open," these proprietary API dependencies mean training cannot be reproduced without incurring API costs to external vendors; the fallback models (DeepSeek V3.2) are noted only for RL-stage API rate-limit handling, not as full replacements. - No contamination analysis: the web-crawled training data is temporally overlapping with several benchmarks, and the paper does not analyze whether benchmark-relevant content appeared in training queries or visited URLs. - The BrowseComp headline score (64.6%) is produced under the discard-all context strategy (not Quest's Context Condenser), chosen to match the evaluation protocol used by proprietary competitors — but this means the primary advertised result for the most prominent benchmark does not use Quest's own claimed contribution of structured context management. - Ablation subsets are small (130 examples) and no variance estimates are reported, making it difficult to assess whether differences of 1–3 percentage points are meaningful. - The base model (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B) is a mixture-of-experts architecture; comparisons with dense 30B models conflate different computational classes without discussion. Bottom Line: Quest is the strongest open-weight deep research agent published to date and provides the field's most complete reproducible training recipe; a researcher building or benchmarking web agents should read this paper, with the caveat that "fully open" overstates the case given the proprietary API dependencies embedded throughout the training pipeline.

BLINKG: A Benchmark for LLM-Integrated Knowledge Graph Generation

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BLINKG, a benchmark that evaluates LLMs' ability to generate declarative semantic mappings from heterogeneous data sources (CSV, JSON, XML) to ontology terms during knowledge graph construction, decomposed into eight independently scorable atomic subtasks. - Why it's important: KG construction mapping phases routinely exceed six person-months in large real-world projects, yet no standardized evaluation framework exists for comparing LLM-based mapping approaches — each prior proposal uses different tasks, datasets, and metrics, making cross-study comparison impossible. - How it does it: BLINKG defines three scenarios of increasing schema-to-ontology lexical distance (Basic / Schema-Aligned using LinkedGTFS / Schema-Distant using eProcurement Ontology + CODICE XML), evaluates six LLMs using task-level precision/recall/F1 scored via a combined Levenshtein + embedding similarity metric calibrated against expert annotations, and releases all gold standards and evaluation code openly. - The good: The benchmark exposes a diagnostic capability cliff: LLMs achieve near-perfect entity class identification (similarity > 0.95) in simple scenarios but score F1 = 0.00 on join conditions and transformation functions in schema-distant Scenario 3 — across all six models and all prompting strategies — providing actionable granularity no end-to-end benchmark delivers. - The missing: The benchmark uses real-world ontologies (GTFS, ePO) that may overlap with LLM training corpora, and the similarity threshold is calibrated on the same expert annotations used for evaluation — creating a circularity that no held-out validation corrects. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners working on LLM-assisted knowledge engineering or KG construction pipelines; less relevant outside the Semantic Web community, and the small gold standards (72 and 26 instances in the two realistic scenarios) limit statistical confidence in task-level findings.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BLINKG, a benchmark that evaluates LLMs' ability to generate declarative semantic mappings from heterogeneous data sources (CSV, JSON, XML) to ontology terms during knowledge graph construction, decomposed into eight independently scorable atomic subtasks. - Why it's important: KG construction mapping phases routinely exceed six person-months in large real-world projects, yet no standardized evaluation framework exists for comparing LLM-based mapping approaches — each prior proposal uses different tasks, datasets, and metrics, making cross-study comparison impossible. - How it does it: BLINKG defines three scenarios of increasing schema-to-ontology lexical distance (Basic / Schema-Aligned using LinkedGTFS / Schema-Distant using eProcurement Ontology + CODICE XML), evaluates six LLMs using task-level precision/recall/F1 scored via a combined Levenshtein + embedding similarity metric calibrated against expert annotations, and releases all gold standards and evaluation code openly. - The good: The benchmark exposes a diagnostic capability cliff: LLMs achieve near-perfect entity class identification (similarity > 0.95) in simple scenarios but score F1 = 0.00 on join conditions and transformation functions in schema-distant Scenario 3 — across all six models and all prompting strategies — providing actionable granularity no end-to-end benchmark delivers. - The missing: The benchmark uses real-world ontologies (GTFS, ePO) that may overlap with LLM training corpora, and the similarity threshold is calibrated on the same expert annotations used for evaluation — creating a circularity that no held-out validation corrects. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners working on LLM-assisted knowledge engineering or KG construction pipelines; less relevant outside the Semantic Web community, and the small gold standards (72 and 26 instances in the two realistic scenarios) limit statistical confidence in task-level findings. Problem & Motivation Knowledge engineers must map heterogeneous data source schemas — CSV columns, JSON paths, XML XPath expressions — to ontology terms in order to build knowledge graphs. In large-scale real-world projects this mapping phase alone regularly exceeds six person-months. Declarative mapping languages (RML, R2RML, SPARQL-Anything) reduce repetition but do not eliminate the semantic alignment work. LLMs have been proposed as accelerators, but existing proposals (Hofer et al. for RML from JSON, Schmidt et al. for YARRRML in manufacturing, Höltgen et al. for infrastructure RDF, Freund et al.'s ReMap) each define their own tasks, datasets, and success criteria, making cross-study comparison impossible. Prior benchmarks are inadequate: RODI targets RDB-only settings and evaluates mappings indirectly through SPARQL query accuracy; SemTab evaluates annotation of tabular data against pre-existing KGs, not ontology alignment for new graph construction. The result is a fragmented landscape with no agreed-upon tasks, gold standards, or metrics for LLM mapping evaluation. Key Idea The core insight is to decompose the monolithic KG mapping problem into eight independently scorable atomic subtasks — Ontology Class Identification, Subject URI Generation, Ontology Property Identification, Data Reference Identification, Related Entity Class and Joins, Language Annotation, Datatype Annotation, and Transformation Functions — and to evaluate LLMs on these subtasks across scenarios that vary in schema-to-ontology lexical distance, rather than measuring end-to-end pipeline quality or syntactic mapping language correctness. This decomposition exposes precisely where LLMs succeed or fail rather than collapsing all failure modes into a single end-to-end metric. Methodology BLINKG defines three scenarios. Scenario 1 (Basic) comprises eight atomic test cases over a minimal three-class toy ontology (Person, Sport, City) with artificial CSV/JSON/XML data derived from RML test cases; it tests each of the eight subtasks in isolation with 1–7 gold-standard instances per case. Scenario 2 (Schema-Aligned) uses the LinkedGTFS ontology (17 classes, 49 datatype properties, 10 object properties, 10 SKOS controlled vocabularies) with real Madrid Metro GTFS CSV feeds (13 files, 72 total gold instances); source and ontology have high lexical alignment since the ontology was derived bottom-up from the GTFS specification. Scenario 3 (Schema-Distant) uses a subset of the eProcurement Ontology (11 classes from the pan-European epo-core module) with CODICE XML procurement entries from Spain (26 gold instances); CODICE and ePO were developed independently, introducing significant structural and lexical divergence requiring deep domain expertise to bridge. Each LLM is prompted with a standardized natural-language template requesting a structured table output listing data references, ontology properties, entity classes, subject templates, joins, datatypes, language tags, and transformation functions. Temperature is fixed at 0.3 based on preliminary runs. Each prompt runs three times; results are macro-averaged. To fit context windows, Scenario 2 is split into 6 sub-scenarios and Scenario 3 into 2. Three evaluation modes are reported: expert (manual annotation), raw (automatic on raw output), and post-processed (automatic after heuristic cleaning — whitespace removal, discarding incoherent fragments, dropping misaligned columns). Correctness is binary: an annotation is correct if max(Levenshtein similarity, raw SBERT cosine, verbalized-ontology SBERT cosine) ≥ τ = 0.8, where τ was chosen by minimizing MAE against expert annotations. Zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting strategies are compared on Scenario 3 for DeepSeek-R1. Six LLMs are evaluated: DeepSeek-R1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o, OpenAI o3, LLaMA 3.3 70B Instruct, and Mixtral 8x22B Instruct. Three critical design decisions: (1) Tabular output rather than raw RML/R2RML — well justified, as LLMs are known to produce syntactically invalid mapping language files. (2) Threshold τ = 0.8 calibrated against expert annotations — problematic because it is selected on the same data it evaluates (no held-out calibration set). (3) Sub-scenario splitting for context-window limitations — pragmatic but introduces potential coherence loss not ablated. Key Results Scenario 1: Entity class identification similarity ≥ 0.95 for most models; several reach 1.00. Ontology property identification reliably high. Join condition is the hardest task, with similarity ranging 0.37 (LLaMA) to 0.69 (Gemini 2.5 Pro). Language annotation shows high variance: DeepSeek/Gemini > 0.83; Mixtral/LLaMA < 0.53. MAE between expert and post-processed F1 ranges 0.106 (DeepSeek) to 0.236 (Mixtral), indicating the automatic metric is a reasonable but imperfect proxy in simple settings. Scenario 2 (72 gold instances): Entity class and data reference post-processed similarity > 0.90 for top models (GPT-4o data reference: 0.91; DeepSeek entity class: 0.95). Ontology property F1 approaches 1.00 (post-processed) for DeepSeek and Gemini. Join condition: no model exceeds 0.85 F1 (post-processed) and most fall below 0.65. Function Name: all models below 0.20 similarity raw; post-processed jumps to 0.20 (a flat heuristic artifact). Function Output: below 0.41 post-processed for most. MAE between expert and post-processed F1 ranges 0.13 (GPT-4o) to 0.22 (Gemini, Mixtral). Scenario 3 (26 gold instances): No task exceeds mean similarity 0.53 post-processed for any model. Entity class best: DeepSeek 0.53, GPT-4o 0.42. Join condition F1 = 0.00 for all models in all evaluation modes. Function Output F1 = 0.00 for all models except o3 (0.25 post-processed). Subject Generation F1 = 0.00 for all models. Prompting strategy experiment (DeepSeek): few-shot raises data reference F1 from 0.38 to 0.86 and subject generation from 0.00 to 0.71, but join and function tasks remain at 0.00 regardless of strategy. MAE between expert and post-processed ranges 0.32 (o3) to 0.42 (DeepSeek), indicating the automatic metric substantially underestimates LLM performance in complex schema-distant settings. No non-LLM baselines are included. Baselines are limited to cross-model comparison within the six LLMs. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The primary contribution is a structured, openly released benchmark with gold standards across three scenarios. The finding that join conditions and transformation functions are a universal failure mode across six diverse LLMs — persistent even under few-shot prompting in schema-distant settings — is a genuine empirical finding. The evaluation methodology (task decomposition + multi-similarity scoring) is an incremental methodological contribution. The paper does not introduce a new method, algorithm, or system for KG construction itself. Rigor & Reproducibility All resources (ontologies, datasets, gold standards, evaluation library) are released at https://github.com/citiususc/blinkg under Apache 2.0. Only 3 prompt runs per scenario — standard deviations across runs are not reported, and significance tests are absent. No inter-annotator agreement is reported for the expert evaluation used as the gold calibration reference. The similarity threshold τ = 0.8 is selected by minimizing MAE against the same expert annotations it evaluates — this is a Goodhart's Law concern: the metric is tuned to its own benchmark. Context-window splitting into sub-scenarios is not evaluated as a confound. The benchmark uses real-world ontologies (GTFS, ePO) publicly available on the Web and potentially in LLM training corpora; the authors explicitly acknowledge data leakage as a limitation and recommend synthetic ontologies for future versions but do not control for it here. The paper is peer-reviewed, accepted to Transactions on Graph Data and Knowledge (received July 2025, accepted March 2026). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (evaluation of LLM systems against gold standards; the paper reports no deployment of a new system, only evaluation of existing LLMs). - Practical: The benchmark runs against API-accessible commercial and open-source LLMs with no specialized hardware beyond API access; any practitioner can reproduce the evaluation. - Efficient: Scenario 2 requires splitting into 6 sub-scenarios to fit LLM context windows, and running 6 models × 3 prompt repetitions over 72+26 instances is modest in compute but non-trivial in API cost; the evaluation library lowers the barrier for reuse. - Effective: The benchmark successfully differentiates model capabilities across difficulty levels and identifies specific failure subtasks (join conditions, transformation functions) that end-to-end evaluation would obscure; expert-vs-automatic MAE confirms the metric is a reasonable proxy in simpler scenarios. - Scalable: Gold standards are small (72 and 26 instances in the two realistic scenarios), limiting statistical confidence; the framework architecture is extensible through community contributions, but current coverage is insufficient to claim generalization to the full diversity of real-world KG mapping challenges. The work delivers a software artifact (benchmark suite with ontologies, datasets, gold standards, evaluation library) at the stated GitHub URL. Strengths - Task decomposition is the primary contribution: separating KG mapping into 8 independently scorable subtasks enables precise failure-mode diagnosis that RODI's SPARQL-based or SemTab's annotation-level evaluation cannot provide. - The three-scenario difficulty ladder with real-world ontologies (LinkedGTFS, ePO) and data sources (Madrid Metro GTFS, Spanish procurement CODICE XML) provides external validity beyond toy examples and covers both CSV and deeply nested XML. - The three-mode evaluation design (expert, raw, post-processed) reveals that raw automatic scoring systematically underestimates LLM performance — a finding with practical implications for how future LLM evaluations in this domain should be designed. - The prompting strategy ablation on Scenario 3 demonstrates concretely that few-shot prompting helps local selection tasks but fails entirely for compositional tasks (joins, transformations), providing actionable guidance for future research. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) data leakage risk from publicly available GTFS and ePO ontologies that may overlap with LLM training corpora, with a commitment to synthetic ontologies in future versions; (2) reliance on manual inspection or simplified metrics for semantic equivalence; (3) exclusion of advanced mapping constructs (RML-CC list generation, RML-Star, RML-IO) deferred to future versions; (4) fragility of proprietary LLM APIs — OpenAI o1 and Gemini 2.0 Pro were removed or restricted mid-evaluation, requiring repetition. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No non-LLM baselines: existing automated mapping tools (MIRROR, AutoMap4OBDA, BootOX, D2RQ) are not compared, leaving unanswered whether LLMs even outperform classical heuristic-based tools on Scenario 1. - Threshold circularity: τ = 0.8 is calibrated by minimizing MAE against the same expert annotations used for evaluation — this overfits the metric to the benchmark and should be validated on held-out data or via cross-validation. - Scenario 3 gold standard instability: with only 26 instances spread across 9 task types, individual task scores (e.g., Join Condition: 16 instances) are computed over counts too small for stable estimates; confidence intervals are absent. - Context-window splitting as an uncontrolled confound: decomposing Scenario 2 into 6 sub-scenarios removes global ontology context an LLM would otherwise use; the effect of this decomposition on results is never ablated. - Single temperature: only temperature 0.3 is reported in final experiments despite the claim that preliminary runs were conducted; no sensitivity analysis is included. - No inter-annotator agreement: the expert evaluation serves as the calibration anchor for τ, but no agreement metric (Cohen's κ, Fleiss's κ) is reported, so the reliability of the gold calibration reference is unknown. Bottom Line BLINKG is a useful, openly released benchmark that fills a genuine gap by providing the first task-decomposed, format-agnostic, multi-scenario evaluation framework for LLM-assisted KG mapping; knowledge graph construction researchers should read it for its diagnostic task decomposition and the finding that compositional mapping tasks (joins, transformation functions) are a universal current LLM failure mode, while keeping in mind that absent non-LLM baselines, small gold standards, and an uncorrected threshold circularity make the quantitative results preliminary rather than definitive.

Skill Weaving: Efficient LLM Improvement via Modular Skillpacks

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SkillWeave, a modular framework that decomposes a general-purpose LLM into domain-specific delta modules ("skillpacks") trained via full-parameter DPO and compressed via SkillZip — a joint weight-and-activation quantization scheme — enabling multi-domain LLM serving under fixed memory and latency budgets. - Why it's important: Practitioners serving LLMs across heterogeneous domains (math, coding, dialogue, agentic tasks) must choose between maintaining multiple full-model copies (prohibitive memory/swap latency) or scaling to large monolithic models (high cost); existing PEFT and delta-compression approaches either underperform or leave inference latency unsolved. - How it does it: Each domain receives a full-parameter DPO fine-tune; shared cross-task knowledge is then extracted via model merging and subtracted from each delta; the residual domain deltas are factorized via SVD with double smoothing (channel-wise outlier redistribution + rank-wise orthogonal rotation) and quantized to INT8/INT4 for hardware-native GEMM execution without runtime dequantization. - The good: A 10B SkillWeave model (8B backbone + 5× ~0.5B skillpacks) outperforms Gemma2-27B on MATH (62.5 vs 54.4) and all same-size baselines across most of 8 benchmarks, while delivering 4.2× inference speedup over a 32B monolith in agent deployment. - The missing: The headline comparison table (Table 1) uses oracle domain labels — a condition unavailable in real deployment; the learned router ablation is presented separately and shows negligible degradation, but under mixed-domain or adversarial inputs this gap is not stress-tested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers targeting multi-domain LLM deployment under GPU memory constraints; the SkillZip quantization design and the full-tune-then-compress principle are the most practically transferable contributions.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SkillWeave, a modular framework that decomposes a general-purpose LLM into domain-specific delta modules ("skillpacks") trained via full-parameter DPO and compressed via SkillZip — a joint weight-and-activation quantization scheme — enabling multi-domain LLM serving under fixed memory and latency budgets. - Why it's important: Practitioners serving LLMs across heterogeneous domains (math, coding, dialogue, agentic tasks) must choose between maintaining multiple full-model copies (prohibitive memory/swap latency) or scaling to large monolithic models (high cost); existing PEFT and delta-compression approaches either underperform or leave inference latency unsolved. - How it does it: Each domain receives a full-parameter DPO fine-tune; shared cross-task knowledge is then extracted via model merging and subtracted from each delta; the residual domain deltas are factorized via SVD with double smoothing (channel-wise outlier redistribution + rank-wise orthogonal rotation) and quantized to INT8/INT4 for hardware-native GEMM execution without runtime dequantization. - The good: A 10B SkillWeave model (8B backbone + 5× ~0.5B skillpacks) outperforms Gemma2-27B on MATH (62.5 vs 54.4) and all same-size baselines across most of 8 benchmarks, while delivering 4.2× inference speedup over a 32B monolith in agent deployment. - The missing: The headline comparison table (Table 1) uses oracle domain labels — a condition unavailable in real deployment; the learned router ablation is presented separately and shows negligible degradation, but under mixed-domain or adversarial inputs this gap is not stress-tested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers targeting multi-domain LLM deployment under GPU memory constraints; the SkillZip quantization design and the full-tune-then-compress principle are the most practically transferable contributions. Problem & Motivation Deploying LLMs across diverse domains — math reasoning, code generation, dialogue, agentic tool use — forces one of two costly strategies: maintain multiple full-parameter specialized models (multiplicative memory and model-swap latency costs) or rely on a single large monolithic model (high inference cost per query). PEFT methods like LoRA reduce training cost but systematically underperform full fine-tuning on domain-specific tasks. Existing delta-compression methods (BitDelta, DeltaCome, ASVD) compress the stored weight delta but require runtime dequantization back to FP16, leaving inference latency unchanged. Task interference and catastrophic forgetting further limit joint fine-tuning approaches. The paper targets the specific gap of achieving full-fine-tune quality with near-backbone inference cost across multiple concurrent domains. Key Idea The core insight is to decouple the quality phase from the efficiency phase: train each domain at full parameter capacity (capturing fine-grained domain improvements that LoRA cannot), then compress the resulting delta — not the original weights — using joint weight-and-activation quantization that enables direct INT8/INT4 GEMM execution. The enabling trick for the compression step is a two-stage outlier smoothing: first redistribute channel-wise activation outliers into the weight matrix (SmoothQuant-style), then apply an orthogonal rotation to the SVD factors to equalize rank-dimension energy, making both the activation and the low-rank weights amenable to uniform quantization without dequantization overhead. Methodology SkillWeave runs a structured three-stage pipeline. (1) Skillpack Building: for each of K domains, the base model Mθ₀ is prompted to self-generate responses on a partitioned seed dataset; a domain-specific rule-based filter (regex answer matching for math, sandbox execution for code, structural heuristics and reward-model scoring for dialogue) splits outputs into helpful/harmful preference pairs; online LN-DPO (length-normalized, no explicit reference log-ratio) fine-tunes the full model to produce Mₖ = M₀ + Δₖ. (2) Skillpack Compression (SkillZip): a shared component Δ_shared = Merge([Δ₁,...,Δₖ]) is absorbed into the backbone and subtracted from each delta, making residuals sparser and more domain-specific. Each residual delta is factorized via truncated SVD (W ≈ AB), then double-smoothed: channel-wise scaling of X redistributes activation outliers; orthogonal rotation Q disperses rank-concentrated singular energy. The objective minimizes ||XW - Dequant(X̂ÂB̂)|| with per-token/per-tensor quantization on X̂, per-tensor on Â, per-channel on B̂ — chosen specifically for hardware GEMM compatibility. Final GPTQ step reduces weights to INT4. (3) Skillpack Integration: at inference, the FP16 backbone runs constantly; a domain router (Qwen2.5-0.5B with a linear classification head, for general tasks) selects one skillpack; output = XᵢW + X̂ᵢÂᵢB̂ᵢ using S-LoRA-style batching. Critical design decisions: (a) full fine-tuning before compression vs. PEFT training — justified by ablation showing ~30% average advantage over PEFT across domains; (b) joint activation+weight quantization vs. weight-only — eliminates runtime dequantization, enabling 2.1× kernel decode speedup; (c) merge-before-compress — empirically reduces required compression ratio per delta. Key Results General capability (Table 1, LLaMA-3.1-8B base, 10B total): SkillWeave scores GSM8K 91.0 (+0.2 over best same-size baseline EMR-Merging at 16.7B), MATH 62.5 (+2.1 over Twin-Merging r1024 at 21B), HumanEval 75.0, MBPP 77.8, AlpacaEval2 52.8, IFEval 79.1, BBH 76.2, ARC-C 88.6 — ranking first in 4 of 8 benchmarks. Notably surpasses Gemma2-27B on MATH (62.5 vs 54.4) and GSM8K (91.0 vs 90.4). However, SkillWeave scores 52.8 on AlpacaEval2 versus FuseChat3.0's 64.2 — an 11.4-point deficit on dialogue that is attributed to FuseChat's stronger teacher models but not directly ablated. Agent deployment (Figure 5, Qwen2.5-7B backbone): SkillWeave achieves 4.2× throughput speedup over 32B monolith and 5.5× over 5×7B ensemble, while remaining within 3% and 5% of their respective task performance. SkillZip compression (Table 2): X8A8B8 configuration achieves average score 59.8 vs DeltaCome 59.7 and BitDelta 57.7, with 1.38× end-to-end latency improvement over DeltaCome. Kernel-level decode latency (batch=1): X8A8A8 achieves 0.0558ms vs FP16 0.1167ms (2.1× speedup). Routing: learned Qwen2.5-0.5B router achieves >0.999 F1 across all four domains (Table 3); oracle vs. learned routing performance gap is negligible (Table 4). Results at 1B scale (Table 5) show SkillWeave outperforms Twin-Merging and Self-Rewarding but underperforms FuseChat3.0 on most dimensions. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The full-tune-then-compress design principle as applied to per-domain LLM skillpacks is a genuinely novel framing that resolves the PEFT-vs-full-FT quality tradeoff. SkillZip's joint weight+activation quantization applied specifically to SVD-factorized task deltas, with the rank-wise rotation smoothing, extends SmoothQuant in a non-obvious direction. The surrounding pipeline (DPO self-improvement, model merging, SVD compression) assembles known techniques, but their integration and the hardware-aware inference design constitute a substantive contribution beyond a standard combination applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for both the SkillZip components (Table 2, testing merge/smooth/rotate combinations) and for the self-improvement algorithm choice (Table 1 ablation rows). No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple random seeds are reported anywhere in the paper. The headline Table 1 uses oracle domain labels — the paper discloses this in Section 3.3 and Appendix B.3, and provides a separate oracle-vs-learned router ablation (Table 4) showing negligible gap (MATH: 62.5 vs 62.3; all other benchmarks identical or within 0.2), but the main table numbers are technically achieved under a condition unavailable in unstructured deployment. Code and training scripts are released at an anonymous repository (https://anonymous.4open.science/r/anonymous-repo-BFE7) — available but not yet under a named project or permanent DOI. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.22205v1); results are not peer-reviewed. Training data for SkillWeave overlaps significantly with FUSECHAT-MIXTURE (disclosed in Appendix E.2), which complicates the comparison with FuseChat baselines since FuseChat additionally uses stronger external teacher models. AlpacaEval2 raw win rate (not length-controlled) is reported in the main table — a metric known to favor verbosity; LC scores are relegated to the appendix. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS — the paper builds and evaluates a deployed inference system. - Practical: All experiments run on single NVIDIA A100-80G GPUs; the 9.5B total parameter footprint (7B backbone + 5×0.5B skillpacks resident in memory) fits in a single commercially available high-memory GPU. - Efficient: SkillZip X8A8B8 achieves 2.1× kernel decode speedup over FP16 and 1.38× end-to-end speedup over DeltaCome; the routing model (Qwen2.5-0.5B) adds marginal overhead (one forward pass per request, not per token), with the dominant slowdown being GPU memory contention rather than computation. - Effective: Within 3% of a 5×7B task-specialized ensemble on AgentBench and within 5% of a 32B monolith; outperforms all same-size baselines on math and coding in general evaluation; underperforms FuseChat3.0 by 11.4 points on AlpacaEval2 dialogue, with the gap attributed to teacher strength rather than method limitation — this attribution is asserted but not controlled for. - Scalable: Tested at two backbone scales (1B and 8B) with up to 5 concurrent skillpacks; the paper claims the approach is "scalable" to additional domains but provides no experiment beyond K=5 skills, and does not analyze how compression quality degrades as K grows or as domain boundaries become less distinct. The system delivers a software artifact (code + training scripts at anonymous repo); trained model weights are not explicitly released. Strengths - The full-tune-then-compress paradigm is both conceptually clear and empirically well-supported: ablations in Table 2 and the single-skill rows in Table 1 confirm that full-parameter training before compression consistently outperforms LoRA training (+30% over ASVD, consistent advantage over PEFT across 8 benchmarks). - SkillZip's joint activation quantization is the technically strongest contribution: by eliminating runtime dequantization through hardware-compatible INT8/INT4 GEMM, it achieves speedups that prior weight-only delta-compression methods (BitDelta, DeltaCome) structurally cannot, while matching or exceeding their accuracy. - The rank-wise orthogonal rotation to handle SVD-induced outliers is a non-obvious and technically grounded solution; it addresses a real failure mode of applying per-tensor/per-channel quantization to SVD factors where energy concentrates in leading rank dimensions. - The agent deployment evaluation is more realistic than standard benchmark comparison: testing against both multi-model ensemble and large monolith under actual throughput and latency measurement on a real serving stack (S-LoRA + Gamma arrival process) gives deployment practitioners actionable numbers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify two limitations. First, evaluation covers benchmarks with clear domain boundaries; effectiveness in open-ended or weakly defined task settings (emergent user behaviors, mixed-domain agentic workflows) requires further investigation. Second, rule-based verification works well for math and code (exact answer or execution correctness) but fails for creative writing or abstract conversation where no correctness criterion exists, limiting self-improvement to structured domains. The authors frame both as future work directions rather than minimizing them — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The oracle routing assumption inflates headline numbers: while Table 4 shows negligible oracle-vs-learned router degradation on single-domain benchmarks, the claim does not hold for mixed-domain or adversarial prompts (the case study in Figure 4 actually shows that misrouting can sometimes help, but this is cherry-picked rather than systematically evaluated). - No ablation on K (number of domains): it is unknown whether adding a 6th or 10th skillpack degrades the shared backbone, the merge quality, or the compression fidelity of existing skillpacks. - The 11.4-point AlpacaEval2 gap versus FuseChat3.0 (52.8 vs 64.2) is not adequately explained or ablated; the authors attribute it to teacher strength but offer no controlled experiment (e.g., SkillWeave with the same FuseChat teacher data) to substantiate this. - The 1B backbone results (Table 5) show SkillWeave underperforms FuseChat3.0 on most dimensions; the comparison across model scales uses different baseline classes, making it difficult to isolate the method contribution from scale effects. - Latency experiments are single-GPU A100-80G only; multi-GPU tensor-parallel or pipeline-parallel deployments common in production are not addressed, and the eviction-and-reloading policy used as the 5×7B baseline is designed to make that baseline look worse. - The paper does not analyze what happens to previously trained skillpacks when a new domain is added — whether the backbone merge step must be redone, and whether that invalidates existing compressed skillpacks. Bottom Line A practitioner targeting memory-constrained multi-domain LLM deployment will find the full-tune-then-compress principle and SkillZip's hardware-aware INT8/INT4 delta execution directly actionable, but should treat the headline benchmark numbers with caution given the oracle-routing assumption and the unresolved dialogue performance gap.

LoMo: Local Modality Substitution for Deeper Vision-Language Fusion

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LoMo, a data curation method that converts text-only SFT samples into text–image–text interleaved sequences by rendering a middle span of the text prompt as an image, injecting implicit cross-modal alignment supervision into standard VLM fine-tuning without any architectural change. - Why it's important: Current VLMs drop 8.70–11.68 accuracy points when identical semantic content is delivered as a rendered image rather than text tokens, a fragility that undermines real-world robustness in document understanding, mixed-modality interfaces, and any deployment where linguistic content arrives through visual channels. - How it does it: By forcing the model to predict the same answer from a sequence where the semantically critical middle segment is a rendered image bracketed by text tokens, the standard NLL loss implicitly minimizes a KL divergence between the model's predictive distributions under textual and visual carriers — alignment becomes a task-level requirement with no extra objective. - The good: Under a rendered evaluation protocol (entire text question delivered as a pixel image), LoMo raises LLaVA-OV1.5-8B average accuracy across 13 benchmarks by +18.86 points (15.24→34.10), with DocVQA gaining +43.51, and a controlled ablation confirms that the gains are not attributable to simply having more multimodal training samples. - The missing: Evaluation is restricted to 8B–9B models trained for one epoch; whether gains persist or grow at 70B+ scale or after RL-based post-training, and whether the rendered test distribution matching the training renderer inflates rendered evaluation numbers, are not examined. - Read it or not: VLM practitioners who fine-tune on SFT corpora should read this — the method is plug-in compatible, introduces zero inference cost, and is supported by thorough ablations; the core idea is simple enough to implement in a weekend.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LoMo, a data curation method that converts text-only SFT samples into text–image–text interleaved sequences by rendering a middle span of the text prompt as an image, injecting implicit cross-modal alignment supervision into standard VLM fine-tuning without any architectural change. - Why it's important: Current VLMs drop 8.70–11.68 accuracy points when identical semantic content is delivered as a rendered image rather than text tokens, a fragility that undermines real-world robustness in document understanding, mixed-modality interfaces, and any deployment where linguistic content arrives through visual channels. - How it does it: By forcing the model to predict the same answer from a sequence where the semantically critical middle segment is a rendered image bracketed by text tokens, the standard NLL loss implicitly minimizes a KL divergence between the model's predictive distributions under textual and visual carriers — alignment becomes a task-level requirement with no extra objective. - The good: Under a rendered evaluation protocol (entire text question delivered as a pixel image), LoMo raises LLaVA-OV1.5-8B average accuracy across 13 benchmarks by +18.86 points (15.24→34.10), with DocVQA gaining +43.51, and a controlled ablation confirms that the gains are not attributable to simply having more multimodal training samples. - The missing: Evaluation is restricted to 8B–9B models trained for one epoch; whether gains persist or grow at 70B+ scale or after RL-based post-training, and whether the rendered test distribution matching the training renderer inflates rendered evaluation numbers, are not examined. - Read it or not: VLM practitioners who fine-tune on SFT corpora should read this — the method is plug-in compatible, introduces zero inference cost, and is supported by thorough ablations; the core idea is simple enough to implement in a weekend. Problem & Motivation Vision-language models (VLMs) treat text and image as modality-specific inputs — text as the linguistic query, images as the visual scene — because this is how virtually all standard training corpora (image captioning, VQA, OCR, web-interleaved data) are structured. This asymmetry means the model never needs to equate semantically identical content delivered as text tokens versus as pixel images. The practical symptom is severe: rendering the text question as an image while keeping all other inputs identical causes LLaVA-OV1.5-8B to drop 11.68 points, InternVL3.5-8B to drop 8.70 points, GLM-4.1V-9B to drop 10.09 points, and Qwen3.5-9B to drop 9.25 points across standard multimodal benchmarks. The authors measure this more precisely by extracting hidden states of text tokens and their rendered-image counterparts and computing cosine distances; accuracy drop correlates strictly monotonically with this cross-carrier distance, growing from 7.75% in the closest distance bin to 21.23% in the farthest, establishing a mechanistic link between representational distance and performance degradation. Key Idea The core insight is that the standard NLL fine-tuning objective, applied to an instance where a text span has been replaced by its rendered-image counterpart, implicitly decomposes into the standard SFT loss plus a KL divergence term between the model's predictive distribution conditioned on the original text and its distribution conditioned on the image-substituted version. No new loss function, no auxiliary head, and no contrastive pairs are needed; by merely reformatting the input while keeping the supervision target unchanged, cross-carrier representational alignment becomes a first-class task-level requirement. Methodology LoMo transforms a text-only instance (x, a) through three successive stages. (1) Structure-Aware Span Localization: For short inputs (≤3 sentences), the entire text is the target span. For longer inputs, a formula-aware chunker treats LaTeX expressions and common math commands as atomic units and extracts the middle one-third by character length, ensuring equation boundaries are never split. This produces (x_pre, x_mid, x_suf). (2) Visual Rendering: x_mid is routed to a LaTeX renderer if it contains mathematical expressions, otherwise to a standard text renderer (font size 20, Latin Modern Math at size 26); LaTeX failures automatically fall back to the text renderer. Margin trimming removes large whitespace. (3) Perceptual Distortion: One operation is sampled uniformly from {clean, rotate, blur, shadow/stain, wave} and applied to the rendered image, simulating real-world document capture noise. The final instance (x_pre, I', x_suf) replaces x while preserving the original answer a. Three critical design decisions are well-justified: the middle-span placement (ablation in Table 4 confirms +2.68 vs. +1.56 for prefix placement), the 50% rewrite ratio (Table 3 shows gains peak at 50% and fall to +1.80 at 100%), and formula-aware chunking (full-text rendering without span localization yields only +1.19 average gain, Table 2). Training uses 4M examples total (2M multimodal + 2M text-only, 50% of text-only rendered), LLaMA-Factory, FlashAttention 2, bf16, DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 1, 8×H200 GPUs, AdamW with cosine schedule (4e-5 to 1e-6), 1 epoch, vision tower frozen. Key Results Under Standard Evaluation (text question delivered as text): LoMo improves LLaVA-OV1.5-8B by +2.68 average (40.88→43.56) and Qwen3.5-9B by +2.82 average (54.43→57.25) across 13 benchmarks. Largest single-task gains on LLaVA: CountBench +8.15 (42.97→51.12), V* +3.99 (47.71→51.70), MM-IFEval +3.21 (58.40→61.61). Largest on Qwen: MMMU-Pro +6.35 (39.83→46.18), MM-IFEval +5.49 (51.74→57.23), DocVQA +6.10 (85.89→91.99).
