Improvements appear in 23 of 26 comparisons; three marginal regressions (MMMU −0.56 on LLaVA; MathVista −0.60, CC-OCR −0.30 on Qwen). Under Rendered Evaluation (text question delivered as pixel image): gains expand to +18.86 on LLaVA (15.24→34.10) and +11.92 on Qwen (43.26→55.18), roughly 7× and 4× the standard gains. DocVQA: +43.51/+44.49; CC-OCR: +25.40/+16.54; CountBench: +34.82/+12.32. On Qwen, the Standard→Rendered accuracy gap compresses from 11.17 points (Standard SFT) to 2.07 points (LoMo). Alignment metrics at 4M scale: LoMo reduces MIR by 0.122 below Standard SFT and reduces pairwise cross-modal distance from 0.52 to 0.49, while Standard SFT increases it from 0.52 to 0.57. Pure-text benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, GSM8K, HumanEval, LiveCodeBench, IFEval): LoMo matches or slightly improves Standard SFT (+0.28 and +0.58 average), confirming no pure-text regression. The baseline — Standard SFT on the identical 4M-instance corpus — is well-matched; no comparison against other cross-modal alignment methods (VCD, HA-DPO) is provided. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The use of in-context rendered-text insertion as an implicit cross-carrier alignment mechanism during standard SFT is genuinely novel; prior text-as-pixels work (Pix2Struct, Glyph, DeepSeek-OCR) treats pixel rendering as an efficiency substitute for tokens, not as a complementary alignment signal within a single training instance. The KL-divergence interpretation of the implicit loss (Eq. 5–6) is a clean and non-obvious theoretical contribution. The monotonic correlation between pairwise cross-carrier representational distance and accuracy drop is a new empirical finding, though the general modality gap concept originates in CLIP-era work. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and well-targeted: component ablation ruling out full-text rendering and isolating the contribution of perceptual distortion (Table 2); rewrite ratio sweep over 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% (Table 3); rendering position comparison of prefix/middle/suffix/multi-span (Table 4); controlled image-to-text ratio comparison at matched 1:1 ratio (Table 5, +2.45 gain confirms the gains are not merely from more multimodal samples); data scale ablation from 1M to 4M (Figure 4). No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds are reported anywhere — all results are single-run, which is standard in VLM fine-tuning but leaves borderline gains (1–2 points) unverified statistically. Two architecturally different backbones mitigate cherry-picking. No code, data, or model weights are stated to be released in the paper (a project page is listed but no explicit open-source statement). Evaluation uses deterministic decoding (T=0) via EvalScope, aiding reproducibility. No Goodhart's Law concern — benchmarks are standard held-out sets not used in training. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Data construction requires only CPU servers (two 128-core machines, ~20 hours for 4M examples); training runs on 8×H200 for one epoch — within reach of well-resourced labs and cloud budgets. Zero inference overhead. - Efficient: The rendering preprocessing is a one-time CPU cost; LoMo adds no new model parameters, no additional forward passes at inference, and minimal training compute overhead (interleaved tokens are a modest increase over text-only samples). - Effective: The controlled ratio ablation (Table 5) and component ablation (Table 2) together show that the gains are attributable to the interleaved cross-carrier formulation specifically, not to increased multimodal sample count or simple pixel exposure; 23/26 positive comparisons across two architectures is credible evidence. - Scalable: Tested only at 8B–9B scale; the authors explicitly disclaim verification at larger scales. Data scaling from 1M to 4M shows monotonically increasing benefit, but extrapolation beyond the tested range is unsupported by evidence. The work delivers a data curation procedure (reproducible from the paper's algorithm and hyperparameter appendix) but no software artifact, trained model weights, or released dataset. Strengths - The theoretical decomposition (Eq. 5–6) showing that NLL on LoMo-transformed data implicitly minimizes a KL divergence between text-carrier and visual-carrier predictive distributions is a clean, non-obvious insight that elevates this beyond a pure engineering contribution. - The controlled image-to-text ratio ablation (Table 5) explicitly anticipates and rules out the most obvious confound, demonstrating intellectual rigor that many data augmentation papers omit. - Architecture agnosticism and zero inference overhead make LoMo immediately applicable to any SFT pipeline; the data construction cost (~20 CPU-hours per 1M samples) is low enough for routine use. - Measuring both set-level (MIR) and pair-level (pairwise cosine distance) alignment metrics provides complementary evidence that the mechanism is doing what the theory claims — the finding that Standard SFT actually pushes paired text/image representations apart during training (0.52→0.57) is a striking and counter-intuitive result. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors provide a dedicated Limitations section and directly confront three scope boundaries: (1) LoMo is applied only during SFT, with pre-training and RL post-training integration left to future work; (2) the middle-span heuristic is not difficulty-aware or curriculum-style, leaving potential gains on the table; (3) validation is restricted to 8B–9B models due to compute constraints. This direct engagement with limitations is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No direct comparison against other cross-modal alignment methods (VCD, HA-DPO) on downstream task accuracy — the paper claims to address the same modality gap from the data side, making accuracy comparisons with these approaches a natural and missing baseline. - The rendered evaluation protocol uses the same font, size (20pt), and rendering pipeline for both LoMo training (rendered spans) and test-time question rendering, which may inflate rendered evaluation improvements through distribution matching; varying the test-time renderer (different fonts, resolutions) would disentangle genuine alignment from renderer-specific overfitting. - No breakdown of which SFT data categories (math, natural language, OCR) benefit most from rendering — this would guide practitioners on where to concentrate the rewrite budget and explain why visual perception and instruction following gain more than math reasoning. - Single-run results with no error bars; gains of 1–2 points on individual benchmarks (e.g., CC-OCR +2.21, DocVQA +1.72 on LLaVA standard evaluation) cannot be distinguished from run-to-run variance without multiple seeds. - No code, dataset, or model release stated, reducing immediate independent reproducibility. Bottom Line A practitioner fine-tuning VLMs on standard SFT corpora gains a concrete, zero-inference-cost technique with solid mechanistic grounding and thorough ablations; the method is immediately applicable and the evidence — particularly the dramatic rendered evaluation gains and the controlled ratio experiment — is credible enough to justify adoption pending replication at larger model scales.

Prism: Private Relational Data Synthesis with Language Models

dl.acm.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Prism, an end-to-end framework that adapts the PATE (Private Aggregation of Teacher Ensembles) framework to autoregressive LLM generation for differentially private synthesis of relational tabular data via a novel tuple distillation mechanism. - Why it's important: Existing DP synthesis methods either apply DP-SGD to LLMs — which corrupts pretrained representations and degrades utility — or rely on lower-capacity models (graphical models, GANs) that fail to capture complex attribute correlations under a tight privacy budget; Prism sidesteps both problems. - How it does it: An ensemble of teacher LLMs fine-tuned on disjoint data partitions vote token-by-token during autoregressive generation; trie-guided pruning constrains votes to schema-valid tokens and short-circuits deterministic completions, data-dependent adaptive noise scales Gaussian perturbation to histogram range, and probabilistic sampling with threshold filtering replaces argmax to preserve diversity. - The good: Prism outperforms eight DP synthesis baselines across five benchmark datasets at all tested DP budgets (ε ∈ {1.0, 3.2, 6.8, 10.0}), with a formal end-to-end RDP-based privacy proof that correctly handles the adaptive (data-dependent) noise mechanism. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to small-to-medium tables (up to 236K rows, 22 columns) with entirely enumerable attribute domains; the method's behavior on truly high-cardinality continuous attributes — which require pre-discretization that the paper never discusses — is unaddressed. - Read it or not: Database and privacy researchers working on DP data synthesis should read this — it delivers a clean, formally sound PATE-to-LLM adaptation with strong empirical results; practitioners needing fast synthesis should be aware the 8.8h runtime on a dataset of 32K rows using 8× A100s is a real deployment barrier.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Prism, an end-to-end framework that adapts the PATE (Private Aggregation of Teacher Ensembles) framework to autoregressive LLM generation for differentially private synthesis of relational tabular data via a novel tuple distillation mechanism. - Why it's important: Existing DP synthesis methods either apply DP-SGD to LLMs — which corrupts pretrained representations and degrades utility — or rely on lower-capacity models (graphical models, GANs) that fail to capture complex attribute correlations under a tight privacy budget; Prism sidesteps both problems. - How it does it: An ensemble of teacher LLMs fine-tuned on disjoint data partitions vote token-by-token during autoregressive generation; trie-guided pruning constrains votes to schema-valid tokens and short-circuits deterministic completions, data-dependent adaptive noise scales Gaussian perturbation to histogram range, and probabilistic sampling with threshold filtering replaces argmax to preserve diversity. - The good: Prism outperforms eight DP synthesis baselines across five benchmark datasets at all tested DP budgets (ε ∈ {1.0, 3.2, 6.8, 10.0}), with a formal end-to-end RDP-based privacy proof that correctly handles the adaptive (data-dependent) noise mechanism. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to small-to-medium tables (up to 236K rows, 22 columns) with entirely enumerable attribute domains; the method's behavior on truly high-cardinality continuous attributes — which require pre-discretization that the paper never discusses — is unaddressed. - Read it or not: Database and privacy researchers working on DP data synthesis should read this — it delivers a clean, formally sound PATE-to-LLM adaptation with strong empirical results; practitioners needing fast synthesis should be aware the 8.8h runtime on a dataset of 32K rows using 8× A100s is a real deployment barrier. Problem & Motivation Organizations store sensitive personal data in relational tables and need to share it for analytics and ML without violating privacy regulations (GDPR, HIPAA). The standard approach — privately training a generative model from scratch — forces a harsh privacy-utility trade-off: injecting DP noise into LLM weights (DP-SGD) distorts the pretrained representations that make LLMs useful for capturing complex attribute correlations, yielding poor utility. Non-LLM alternatives (Bayesian networks, GANs, autoencoders, diffusion models) lack the representational capacity to model high-dimensional joint distributions under a tight privacy budget. The specific gap: how to leverage LLMs' pretrained knowledge for relational synthesis while enforcing formal, provable DP without corrupting pretrained representations. Key Idea Perturb LLM outputs rather than weights: treat the problem as PATE-style knowledge distillation where k teacher LLMs vote at the token level during autoregressive generation, and add calibrated Gaussian noise to the token vote histogram before selection. The critical enabling trick is a token trie built per attribute domain that simultaneously enforces schema validity (pruning out-of-domain tokens), reduces the number of privacy-budget-consuming teacher queries (short-circuiting deterministic completions when a trie node has a single child), and compacts the vote histogram to improve the signal-to-noise ratio of the DP mechanism. Methodology Prism is a three-step pipeline. Step 1 (teacher training): partition the private table D into k disjoint equal subsets; serialize each tuple as "A1 is v1, A2 is v2, ..." and fine-tune k copies of a pretrained LLM using causal language modeling (CLM) cross-entropy loss. Step 2 (tuple distillation): for each token position in an autoregressive generation, query all k teachers for their top-token predictions; aggregate into a count histogram; apply three innovations — (a) Trie-guided pruning: for each attribute, build a token trie from the attribute domain; prune out-of-domain tokens from the histogram; short-circuit single-child trie nodes without a teacher query; (b) Data-dependent DP noise: compute histogram range r = max(c[τ]) − min(c[τ]), perturb r with Gaussian noise N(0,(σr)²) (sensitivity Δr = 2, DP-accounted), then linearly interpolate a noise scale σh ∈ [σh_min, σh_max] where high-range (strong consensus) histograms get larger σh (saves budget) and low-range gets smaller σh (preserves signal); add N(0,(σh)²) to each count (sensitivity Δh = √2); (c) Probabilistic sampling with threshold filtering: zero out noisy counts below 2σh, normalize the remaining histogram, and sample rather than taking the argmax. Repeat until the RDP accountant signals budget exhaustion, yielding differentially private distilled dataset D̂. Step 3 (student training): fine-tune a student LLM on D̂ (no additional privacy cost by post-processing property), then sample the final synthetic dataset D̃ by autoregressive generation from the student. Privacy analysis uses Rényi DP (RDP) composition. Sensitivity of the range query Δr = 2 (Lemma 2: disjoint partitions mean one tuple change affects at most one teacher, shifting max and min by at most 1). Histogram L2 sensitivity Δh = √2 (Lemma 3: worst case is a valid→valid token vote change). Per-round RDP: εr(α) = 2α/(σr)² for range release; εh_t(α|H_t) = α/(σh_t)² for histogram release conditioned on privatized history. The adaptive noise σh_t is a deterministic function of privatized history H_t, so RDP composes additively across rounds. Standard RDP-to-(ε,δ)-DP conversion completes the proof (Theorems 1–2). Three critical design decisions: (1) Token-level voting, not value-level — well-justified: LLM knowledge is encoded at the token level; treating multi-token categorical values (e.g., "Bachelors" → ["ba","che","lors"]) as atomic would require new vocabulary entries without pretrained representations. (2) Trie short-circuiting — well-justified: reduces queries from one-per-token to approximately one-per-value for categorical attributes, delivering substantial budget savings. (3) Probabilistic rather than deterministic argmax selection — well-justified and empirically validated: argmax induces mode collapse toward high-frequency values, artificially inflating recall while collapsing ranking metrics (AUROC, AUPRC). Key Results Experiment 1 (main comparison): Prism outperforms 8 baselines across 5 datasets (Abalone 4,177; Tax 30,000; Adult 32,561; Cardio 70,000; Diabetes 236,378 rows) at ε = 6.8, δ = 1e-5. Representative results — Adult F1: Prism 0.8222 vs. best baseline DP-LLM Full 0.8036; Adult AUROC: Prism 0.6941 vs. best baseline AIM 0.6916; Tax F1: Prism 0.5776 vs. next-best DP-LLM LoRA 0.5555; Diabetes F1: Prism 0.7939 vs. next-best AIM 0.7910. Prism consistently achieves highest AUROC and AUPRC across almost all datasets. Baseline set is current and strong: includes both DP-SGD LLM methods, GAN-based, diffusion-based, and graphical-model-based methods. Experiment 2 (ablation on Adult): All three components contribute. Removing probabilistic sampling + threshold filtering causes the largest degradation in ranking metrics (AUROC drops from 0.6941 to 0.6726), with spuriously high recall (0.9192) confirming mode collapse. Removing data-dependent DP and token pruning each cause smaller but consistent drops. Experiment 5 (varying ε on Adult): Prism is best or near-best at all four tested budgets ε ∈ {1.0, 3.2, 6.8, 10.0}; even at ε = 1.0 (strongest privacy), Prism outperforms all baselines. Experiment 7 (impact of noise): Prism w/o DP achieves AUROC 0.7098 vs. Prism 0.6941 — the noise slightly reduces ranking quality as expected, but F1 difference is small (0.8169 vs. 0.8222), suggesting the noise acts as mild regularization at the chosen scale. Experiment 11 (runtime): 8.8h total for Prism with 1800 teachers on Adult (8× A100s, 96 vCPUs, 1TB RAM), dominated by teacher fine-tuning (linear in k). 600 teachers achieves 4.1h with near-identical utility. Lightweight baselines run in < 1h; P3GM takes 8.4h. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the first adaptation of PATE to autoregressive LLM-based tabular data synthesis, with the trie-guided pruning + data-dependent adaptive noise + probabilistic sampling mechanism enabling tractable budget use. Each individual component (tries, data-dependent DP, probabilistic sampling) has prior art in the database literature; the contribution is the non-obvious combination needed to make token-level PATE work for this setting. The student-teacher LLM framing and LLMs-for-tabular-data are both established; what is new is the mechanism connecting them under formal DP. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present (Exp 2) and cover all three components, but only on the Adult dataset — the paper asserts "consistent trends across datasets" without showing ablation tables for other datasets, which is a reproducibility gap given that Cardio and Abalone show narrower advantages over baselines. Error bars (mean ± std of 3 runs) are reported throughout. The RDP-based privacy proof is complete and technically sound, correctly handling the data-dependent adaptive noise via the conditioning argument that σh_t is a deterministic function of privatized history H_t. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/golden-eggs-lab/prism. One methodological concern: the paper uses only downstream predictive utility (Train-on-Synthetic, Test-on-Real with 9 classifiers) as the evaluation metric; statistical fidelity metrics (marginal distributions, pairwise correlations, Jensen-Shannon divergences) are entirely absent, making it impossible to assess whether Prism preserves data structure beyond what classifiers capture. No empirical privacy auditing (membership inference, attribute inference attacks) is performed to validate that the formal DP guarantee translates to empirical resilience. Published as a peer-reviewed SIGMOD paper (Proc. ACM Manag. Data, received Oct 2025, accepted Feb 2026). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (builds and evaluates a complete end-to-end synthesis system). - Practical: Requires 8× A100 GPUs and 1TB RAM as tested; a reduced configuration (600 teachers) achieves nearly identical utility in 4.1h, lowering the hardware bar, but the memory footprint of 1800 fine-tuned LLM copies is still substantial and not characterized separately. - Efficient: Teacher fine-tuning dominates runtime and scales linearly with k; at 1800 teachers, Prism takes 8.8h vs. < 1h for lightweight alternatives — a 10–40× overhead that is a real deployment barrier for iterative workflows. - Effective: The TSTR evaluation consistently shows Prism synthetic data preserves downstream classification utility better than all 8 baselines at all tested DP budgets, directly solving the stated problem; the student model generalizes substantially better than direct release of the distilled set (29,035 unique tuples vs. 501 in teacher-sampling on Adult). - Scalable: Tested up to 236K rows and 22 attributes — moderate tabular scale, not stress-scale. The trie-based approach requires fully enumerable attribute domains, which is structurally incompatible with high-cardinality continuous attributes without pre-discretization; this discretization step and its privacy/utility implications are not addressed. Single-table scope only; multi-table and foreign key scenarios are acknowledged as out of scope. The paper delivers an open-source prototype at https://github.com/golden-eggs-lab/prism. Strengths - The RDP-based end-to-end privacy proof is technically non-trivial and correctly handled: the key step — showing that σh_t is a deterministic function of the privatized history H_t, enabling additive RDP composition — is not obvious and is cleanly formalized. - Trie-guided pruning simultaneously solves three problems: schema validity enforcement, vocabulary restriction to schema-plausible tokens, and dramatic reduction in teacher query count (often one query per multi-token categorical value), making the approach budget-efficient in a way that would not be apparent from the PATE starting point alone. - The mode-collapse hypothesis for argmax selection is empirically confirmed in Exp 2 with the characteristic signature: highest recall (0.9192) but worst AUROC/AUPRC — a crisp, interpretable result that validates a key design choice. - The evaluation at ε = 1.0 (very high privacy) is particularly informative: Prism still leads all baselines, suggesting the pretrained LLM's prior knowledge provides a floor of utility that other methods cannot match under strong noise. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly limit scope to single-table synthesis, noting multi-table and foreign key handling as "orthogonal." They acknowledge the runtime disadvantage vs. lightweight baselines and discuss it as a utility-cost trade-off. They note the consistent LLM architecture choice (same model for teachers and students) as a simplification. They address the pretraining exposure concern (§7.1) with an argument that DistilGPT2 was trained on unstructured web text unlikely to contain the benchmark tables in CSV form, and cite the Diabetes dataset (postdates OpenWebText) as additional evidence — though this argument is intuitive rather than rigorous. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The trie-based approach requires each attribute domain to be explicitly enumerated. For continuous numerical attributes (floating-point income, continuous age), this requires pre-discretization — but the paper never discusses how discretization is performed, what discretization granularity is used, or how granularity affects the privacy-utility trade-off. The benchmark datasets happen to have largely categorical attributes and low-cardinality integers; this structural dependency is a serious unacknowledged gap for real-world tables with continuous-valued columns. - Ablation study (Exp 2) is presented only for Adult, not for the other four datasets. The claim of "consistent trends across datasets" is unverified and particularly important for datasets where Prism's margin over baselines is smaller (e.g., Cardio, where PATE-CTGAN achieves F1 0.7332 vs. Prism 0.7533). - No empirical privacy audit is conducted (no membership inference or attribute inference attacks). The formal DP guarantee is sound, but the gap between provable and empirical privacy can be large depending on calibration; its absence is a standard omission in the DP synthesis literature but notable given the paper's emphasis on formal guarantees over empirical heuristics. - Statistical fidelity metrics (marginal distributions, pairwise correlations) are absent. Using only downstream classification utility as the sole metric is a proxy that can miss structural data quality issues — a synthetic dataset that inflates certain attribute combinations might score well on TSTR while being useless for other downstream tasks. - Experiment 7 shows Prism w/o DP achieves slightly lower F1 (0.8169) than Prism (0.8222), meaning adding DP noise marginally improves the primary metric. This counterintuitive result is not explained and could indicate that hyperparameters (noise scales) were tuned to implicitly benefit the Adult benchmark. Bottom Line Prism delivers a technically sound and empirically well-validated contribution — the first clean PATE-LLM integration for DP tabular synthesis with a rigorous privacy proof and consistent gains over eight baselines — but the steep compute cost, unenumerated continuous-attribute limitation, and absence of statistical fidelity metrics and empirical privacy auditing mean a practitioner should treat the results as promising rather than deployment-ready for arbitrary real-world tables.

From Raw Experience to Skill Consumption: A Systematic Study of Model-Generated Agent Skills

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a utility-grounded evaluation framework that systematically studies the full three-stage lifecycle of model-generated agent skills (experience generation → skill extraction → skill consumption) across five agentic task domains, six target models, and five extractor models, and derives a meta-skill prior that consistently improves skill quality at extraction time. - Why it's important: Model-generated skills are increasingly deployed in commercial agent platforms as a scalable alternative to hand-crafted skills, but practitioners have had no principled basis for choosing extractors or predicting when skills will help versus hurt — the study finds that 25% of extractor-target pairings exhibit negative transfer. - How it does it: By holding the target model fixed while varying only the extractor across a controlled cross-matrix experiment, then using automated contrastive analysis of high-gap skill pairs to identify three validated rubric dimensions (Failure Mechanism Encoding, Actionable Specificity, High-Risk Action Blacklist) that predict downstream utility, and operationalizing them as a system-prompt insertion at extraction time. - The good: The validated 3-dimension meta-skill improves downstream performance in all nine evaluated cells (+1.55 pp average) and raises LLM judge pairwise accuracy on skill pairs from 46.4% to 73.8%, while the intuitive plausibility rubric hurts performance in 6 of 9 cells (−0.59 pp average). - The missing: The validated rubric was derived from and tested on the same experimental cells (3 domains × 3 targets), with no held-out domain to confirm generalizability; the study also does not test multi-skill libraries, skill retrieval, or cross-domain transfer, which are the primary deployment scenarios. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners building agent skill libraries or researchers studying LLM agent self-improvement, as it provides the first comprehensive lifecycle analysis and a concrete, drop-in extraction improvement backed by systematic experiments.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a utility-grounded evaluation framework that systematically studies the full three-stage lifecycle of model-generated agent skills (experience generation → skill extraction → skill consumption) across five agentic task domains, six target models, and five extractor models, and derives a meta-skill prior that consistently improves skill quality at extraction time. - Why it's important: Model-generated skills are increasingly deployed in commercial agent platforms as a scalable alternative to hand-crafted skills, but practitioners have had no principled basis for choosing extractors or predicting when skills will help versus hurt — the study finds that 25% of extractor-target pairings exhibit negative transfer. - How it does it: By holding the target model fixed while varying only the extractor across a controlled cross-matrix experiment, then using automated contrastive analysis of high-gap skill pairs to identify three validated rubric dimensions (Failure Mechanism Encoding, Actionable Specificity, High-Risk Action Blacklist) that predict downstream utility, and operationalizing them as a system-prompt insertion at extraction time. - The good: The validated 3-dimension meta-skill improves downstream performance in all nine evaluated cells (+1.55 pp average) and raises LLM judge pairwise accuracy on skill pairs from 46.4% to 73.8%, while the intuitive plausibility rubric hurts performance in 6 of 9 cells (−0.59 pp average). - The missing: The validated rubric was derived from and tested on the same experimental cells (3 domains × 3 targets), with no held-out domain to confirm generalizability; the study also does not test multi-skill libraries, skill retrieval, or cross-domain transfer, which are the primary deployment scenarios. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners building agent skill libraries or researchers studying LLM agent self-improvement, as it provides the first comprehensive lifecycle analysis and a concrete, drop-in extraction improvement backed by systematic experiments. Problem & Motivation Language agents increasingly rely on reusable procedural artifacts called "skills" — distilled from past execution trajectories — to improve performance without retraining. Domain-level, model-generated skills are particularly attractive because they scale beyond labor-intensive hand-crafting and are now standard in several commercial agent platforms. However, prior evaluation work has studied only isolated stages of this lifecycle: SkillsBench, SWE-Skills-Bench, and Skills-in-the-Wild measure skill consumption with pre-authored or repository-sourced skills; SkillCraft studies extraction but restricts skills to executable function compositions not generalizable to broader domains. No prior work traverses the full experience-generation → extraction → consumption pipeline and asks whether model-generated skills reliably work, when they fail, and what structural properties determine utility. The result is that practitioners deploying skill libraries have no principled basis for choosing an extractor model, managing experience pool composition, or screening skills before deployment. Key Idea The core insight is that skill extraction capability is distinct from and uncorrelated with a model's task-solving capability — the same model can be a strong executor and a weak extractor, or vice versa — and that downstream skill utility is not predictable from surface textual properties such as fluency, format, or completeness. Instead, utility is governed by three specific content properties: encoding why agents fail with named mechanisms (not just that they fail), providing step-level procedures tied to domain objects and tools (not abstract process advice), and explicitly blacklisting high-risk action patterns. These properties can be operationalized as a compact meta-skill injected into the extractor's system prompt without modifying the extraction pipeline. Methodology The pipeline has three formal stages. Stage 1: a target model M executes tasks from a training split to produce an experience pool T_{M,D} containing both successful and failed trajectories. Stage 2: an extractor model E distills the pool into a single domain-level skill S_{E,M,D} via a two-stage framework — per-trajectory pattern extraction (up to K=3 success or failure patterns per trajectory, fully parallelizable) followed by tree-structured hierarchical consolidation (merging in groups of G=10 until a single consolidated pattern set remains, then converting to a schema-conformant skill via structured tool calls). Stage 3: the skill is inlined into M's system prompt and evaluated on held-out test tasks, with the metric being performance delta Δ(E,M,D) = Perf(M|skill,test) − Perf(M|test). Two aggregate metrics disentangle the two roles: Extraction Efficacy (EE) = average Δ across all targets for a fixed extractor, and Target Evolvability (TE) = average Δ across all extractors for a fixed target. The extraction framework intentionally uses minimal structure with no domain heuristics, leaving all abstraction decisions to the extractor model itself so that performance differences are attributable to extractor capability rather than pipeline engineering. Critical design decisions: (1) consolidating all experience into a single domain-level skill — well-justified for causal attribution but explicitly limits ecological validity; (2) minimal extraction scaffold — appropriate for isolating extractor differences but means the pipeline may underperform more engineered alternatives; (3) downstream Δ as the sole quality metric — well-justified by the finding that text-based quality signals are unreliable. The rubric discovery pipeline analyzes 17 high-gap skill pairs (same target, same domain, |Δ_A − Δ_B| ≥ 0.5pp), extracts per-pair differences via GPT-5.4, consolidates them into 7 candidate dimensions, and validates each by better-rate (proportion of pairs where the higher-Δ skill scores better on that dimension); three dimensions with better-rates 64–66% form the validated rubric. Key Results Across 5 domains, 6 targets, and 5 extractors, model-generated skills improve performance in 75% of extractor-target pairings and degrade it in 25%, with domain-level negative transfer rates ranging from 13% (SpreadsheetBench, SWE-bench-Verified) to 47% (ALFWorld). Extraction Efficacy is uncorrelated with model capability: on SpreadsheetBench, Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite achieves the highest EE (+5.86 pp) while GPT-5.4 — the strongest task-performer — ranks last (+1.67 pp). Target Evolvability varies sharply within domains: on ALFWorld, GPT-5.4 reaches TE = +4.93 while Qwen3.5-9B reaches TE = −1.69 from the same set of extractors. Experience composition studies (3 domains, 5 success ratios) show pure-failure pools are consistently worst; the optimal mix is domain-specific (ALFWorld peaks at failure-heavy pools due to informative dead-end states; SpreadsheetBench peaks near all-success). Format normalization (Friedman test) yields p > 0.34 for all 4 formats on all 6 targets (no detectable effect), while switching extractors yields p < 0.005 for 5/6 targets. LLM pairwise judgment without rubric achieves 46.4% accuracy overall, declining to 15.8% on high-gap pairs (δ ≥ 5pp) — the fluent skill is the worse performer. The validated meta-skill improves all 9 evaluation cells (+1.55 pp average, largest +3.7 pp on SpreadsheetBench); the 7-dimension plausibility rubric hurts 6/9 cells (−0.59 pp average). Validated rubric raises pairwise judge accuracy from 46.4% to 73.8%. Alternative harness validation (Claude Code, Codex) on SpreadsheetBench yields consistent directional results (+0.4 pp average), confirming results are not artifacts of the Python-script harness. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The three-stage lifecycle analysis spanning extractors, targets, and domains is genuinely novel in scope — no prior benchmark crosses all three stages with domain-level, model-generated skills. The finding that LLM judgment of skill quality inversely predicts actual utility at high performance gaps (15.8% accuracy at δ ≥ 5pp) is a striking empirical result with direct practical consequences. The automated rubric-discovery and validation pipeline that closes the loop from diagnostic analysis to a concrete extraction improvement is a methodologically novel contribution. The meta-skill itself is a straightforward prompt-engineering intervention, not a novel algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility All main table entries are averaged over 3 independent evaluation runs. The format experiment uses the Friedman test with σ-ratio effect size, providing a principled statistical basis. The experience composition study holds the extractor fixed and manipulates only the success-failure ratio, cleanly isolating one variable. Pairwise skill evaluation uses 9-vote majority voting with randomized presentation order to mitigate position bias, on 151 pairs exceeding a 0.5 pp gap threshold. Code is released at https://aka.ms/SkillLens. However, no confidence intervals or error bars appear in Table 1, making it difficult to determine whether individual small Δ values (e.g., ±0.5 pp) exceed run-to-run noise. The validated rubric was derived using pairs from the same experimental matrix it is subsequently tested on (the better-rate evaluation and the meta-skill evaluation draw from the same 9 (domain, target) cells), raising the risk of overfitting the rubric to the specific experimental distribution. No held-out domain is used to confirm generalizability of the rubric. This is an arXiv preprint (May 2026) without peer review; results should be treated with the corresponding confidence level. Prompt templates and hyperparameters are fully documented in appendices (B–H). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The extraction pipeline runs on API-accessible frontier models or locally served open-source models (Qwen3.5-35B, Qwen3.5-9B on 8 NVIDIA B200 GPUs via vLLM); the meta-skill is a prompt insertion requiring no additional infrastructure. - Efficient: Per-trajectory extraction is embarrassingly parallel; hierarchical consolidation scales as O(n log n) in trajectory count with fixed group size; the meta-skill adds zero inference-time compute overhead. - Effective: The meta-skill improves downstream performance in all 9 evaluated cells, but absolute effect sizes are modest (+1.55 pp average); the core finding that 25% of pairings exhibit negative transfer is robustly replicated across two alternative harnesses (Claude Code, Codex). - Scalable: The study consolidates all experience into a single domain-level skill and evaluates only in-distribution held-out tasks; performance with multi-skill libraries, larger experience pools, or cross-domain transfer is not evaluated and is explicitly deferred to future work — scalability claims are not substantiated experimentally. Software artifact: code available at https://aka.ms/SkillLens. Strengths - The controlled cross-matrix design (6 targets × 5 extractors × 5 domains) is the most comprehensive evaluation of the skill lifecycle to date and surfaces non-obvious empirical patterns — particularly that extraction capability is a distinct, non-monotone function of model capability across all domains tested. - The quantified finding that LLM judgment of skill quality inversely predicts actual utility at high performance gaps (15.8% accuracy at δ ≥ 5pp) is concrete, surprising, and practically consequential: it invalidates the common assumption that a capable LLM can screen a skill library reliably. - The automated rubric-discovery pipeline (contrastive pair analysis → candidate dimensions → better-rate validation → end-to-end meta-skill test) is methodologically coherent and avoids purely post-hoc rationalization by testing the rubric in downstream extraction rather than only in pairwise judgment. - The experience composition study (Section 5.1) cleanly isolates the effect of success-failure pool composition, yielding directly actionable guidance: all-failure pools are universally worst, and the optimal mix is domain-specific for identifiable reasons (ALFWorld failures reveal dead-end states; SpreadsheetBench successes encode stable procedural strategies). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly and directly acknowledge that consolidating all experience into a single domain-level skill injected via system prompt is a deliberate interpretability choice that sacrifices coverage — it excludes retrieval, multi-skill libraries, planning scaffolds, and skill interference. They frame scaling to richer harnesses and larger skill libraries as future work rather than a gap, and acknowledge that skills extracted from imperfect experience pools may carry biases or unsafe shortcuts that become harder to audit at scale. This transparent scoping is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The validated rubric was derived from pairs drawn from the same 3-domain, 3-target cells used for the meta-skill evaluation; no held-out domain tests whether the three dimensions generalize to BFCL or SEAL-0, where experience pool dynamics (tool-calling, multi-hop retrieval) differ substantially from the derivation domains. - No statistical significance tests appear for the main Δ matrix (Table 1); with only 3 evaluation runs per cell, many small positive and negative Δ values could fall within run-to-run noise, and the reported 75%/25% positive/negative split may shift under more runs. - The study does not report experience pool sizes per (target, domain) pair or investigate sensitivity to pool size — a critical practical variable, since experience collection is the most expensive step and pool sizes presumably vary widely across domains. - The experience generator and skill consumer are the same model M, confounding pool quality with target capability; a stronger design would decouple them (e.g., generate experience with a fixed strong model and vary only the extractor and consumer). - The behavioral impact analysis (Appendix D) characterizing why skill consumption helps GPT-5.4 and hurts Qwen3.5-9B is qualitative trajectory inspection on one domain (SpreadsheetBench); it is descriptive rather than causal and is not replicated across other domains or targets. Bottom Line Practitioners building model-generated skill libraries should read this paper — it provides the first rigorous evidence that extraction capability is model-specific and uncorrelated with task strength, that textual plausibility is a misleading quality signal, and that inserting a validated 3-dimension rubric into any extractor's system prompt measurably reduces negative transfer without modifying the underlying pipeline.

Auto-Dreamer: Learning Offline Memory Consolidation for Language Agents

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Auto-Dreamer, a Qwen3-14B consolidator trained with GRPO to perform offline cross-session region rewriting of language-agent memory banks, replacing accumulated session-specific entries with compact, provenance-grounded abstractions. - Why it's important: Language agents operating over task streams accumulate redundant, contradictory, and overspecific memories that bloat retrieval context and degrade performance; existing methods couple acquisition and consolidation into a single online step that lacks the global view needed to fix this. - How it does it: The consolidator treats a selected working region as read-only evidence, performs bounded tool-use rollouts (search_memory, get_source_trace, synthesize) over it and provenance-linked source trajectories, and replaces the region with a synthesized replacement set — making forgetting, deduplication, and abstraction structurally default rather than explicit decisions. - The good: Achieves 41.1% success on ScienceWorld (+7.0pp over UMEM, the strongest RL-trained baseline) using 12× less active memory, and transfers to held-out ALFWorld (60.2%) and WebArena (52.3%) without retraining, including across a Qwen-to-Gemini writer-backbone shift. - The missing: The ALFWorld margin over UMEM is only 1.8pp with overlapping bootstrap confidence intervals, the two strongest RL-trained baselines are excluded from the WebArena column due to context-window constraints, and no seed or task-order variance is reported for main results. - Read it or not: Researchers building multi-session language-agent memory systems should read this — the region-rewriting formulation and counterfactual utility reward are intellectually novel, the cross-domain transfer is a strong result, and the 10-baseline comparison is unusually thorough for the subfield.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Auto-Dreamer, a Qwen3-14B consolidator trained with GRPO to perform offline cross-session region rewriting of language-agent memory banks, replacing accumulated session-specific entries with compact, provenance-grounded abstractions. - Why it's important: Language agents operating over task streams accumulate redundant, contradictory, and overspecific memories that bloat retrieval context and degrade performance; existing methods couple acquisition and consolidation into a single online step that lacks the global view needed to fix this. - How it does it: The consolidator treats a selected working region as read-only evidence, performs bounded tool-use rollouts (search_memory, get_source_trace, synthesize) over it and provenance-linked source trajectories, and replaces the region with a synthesized replacement set — making forgetting, deduplication, and abstraction structurally default rather than explicit decisions. - The good: Achieves 41.1% success on ScienceWorld (+7.0pp over UMEM, the strongest RL-trained baseline) using 12× less active memory, and transfers to held-out ALFWorld (60.2%) and WebArena (52.3%) without retraining, including across a Qwen-to-Gemini writer-backbone shift. - The missing: The ALFWorld margin over UMEM is only 1.8pp with overlapping bootstrap confidence intervals, the two strongest RL-trained baselines are excluded from the WebArena column due to context-window constraints, and no seed or task-order variance is reported for main results. - Read it or not: Researchers building multi-session language-agent memory systems should read this — the region-rewriting formulation and counterfactual utility reward are intellectually novel, the cross-domain transfer is a strong result, and the 10-baseline comparison is unusually thorough for the subfield. Problem & Motivation Language agents deployed across streams of related tasks accumulate session-level memories through local writers, but these banks grow monotonically, accumulate near-verbatim duplicates, and encode contradictory or overspecific entries whose shared structure is only visible when looking across sessions simultaneously. The core gap is a consolidation problem: existing methods — retrieval-augmented stores, procedural skill libraries (Voyager, Memp), reflection methods (Reflexion, ExpeL), and RL-trained writers (UMEM, Mem-α) — all perform acquisition and consolidation in a single online pass with access only to the current session's evidence. This precludes discovering recurring patterns, abstracting reusable procedures, resolving contradictions across sessions, or making principled forgetting decisions. A secondary problem is that RL-trained writers optimize online construction or retrieval but do not learn which memories are load-bearing versus redundant, so they cannot trade off success against bank compactness. Key Idea The single core insight is that replacing CRUD-style per-entry memory updates with region rewriting — treating a selected working region as read-only evidence and synthesizing a fresh replacement set that supersedes it — makes compactness, abstraction, deduplication, and forgetting structurally default rather than behaviors that must be explicitly learned. In CRUD-style systems, old entries persist unless the controller explicitly edits or deletes them, so consolidation decomposes into many local retention decisions and redundant entries survive by default. In region rewriting, information survives only if it is re-synthesized into the replacement set, shifting the inductive bias toward compact abstractions without requiring the policy to learn to delete. This is grounded in the operational structure of complementary learning systems (CLS) theory — fast hippocampal acquisition, slow neocortical consolidation — adopted as a design principle rather than a biological claim. Methodology Auto-Dreamer is a two-timescale system. The fast path is a fixed prompted writer (Qwen3-14B on ALFWorld/ScienceWorld, Gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview on WebArena) that runs after each session, emitting zero or more typed INSERT_SEMANTIC or INSERT_PROCEDURAL entries with provenance pointers into an append-only bank; it does not read existing entries or resolve conflicts. The slow path is a learned consolidator Cθ (Qwen3-14B initialized, fine-tuned with GRPO) that triggers every k sessions (k=8 ALFWorld, k=10 ScienceWorld, k=5 WebArena). At each consolidation event, the working region R is defined as the union of newly written entries plus entries retrieved by the task agent during the interval. The consolidator performs a bounded tool-use rollout over (R, TR) using four tools — search_memory, check_memory, get_source_trace, synthesize — and terminates either explicitly or at a turn budget. The bank is updated as B = (B∖R) ∪ S. The read operator uses a frozen Qwen2.5-3B sentence encoder (2048-d) with FAISS IndexFlatIP retrieval at top-K=3 within a 1,500-token budget, refreshed every 8 environment steps during each episode. Training uses GRPO over 200 steps (batch 16, group N=8) on ScienceWorld trajectories only, with 8 H100 GPUs. The reward is r_g = U_V(S_g) + α·r_cf(S_g; V), where U_V is mean task return on a held-out evaluation set V using a local bank containing only the synthesized entries (B_g = S_g), and r_cf is a counterfactual term estimated by Monte Carlo random masking of entries (fraction ρ), penalizing redundant entries (masking them leaves performance unchanged) and rewarding load-bearing ones (masking them hurts). Three critical design decisions: (1) local-bank credit assignment for training, which aligns the reward unit with the policy's output unit but creates a distribution shift relative to full-bank deployment — partially addressed empirically; (2) provenance grounding requiring all synthesized entries to cite source_ids, which prevents hallucination but means information missed by the writer cannot be recovered; (3) heuristic region selection (new + recently retrieved), which is not ablated. Key Results Continual-memory deployment (bank starts empty, grows during evaluation, prequential protocol): - ScienceWorld (660 tasks): Auto-Dreamer 41.1% SR vs UMEM 34.1% (+7.0pp), ReasoningBank 30.9% (+10.2pp), no-memory 28.7% (+12.4pp); AUC 0.411 vs 0.353 (UMEM); bank 6,947 tokens vs 80,918 (UMEM), 155,117 (ReasoningBank). - ALFWorld (274 tasks): 60.2% [54.65, 65.59 bootstrap CI] vs UMEM 58.4% [53.15, 63.65] (+1.8pp, overlapping CIs), Mem-α 57.4% [52.04, 62.59]; bank 10,954 tokens vs 62,947 (UMEM, 6× less). - WebArena (117 tasks): 52.3% [43.5, 60.9]; leads all evaluated baselines; UMEM and Mem-α excluded due to context-window constraints, so comparison is against AWM/LightMem at 52.0% and Reflexion/ExpeL at ≤50.8%. Fixed-bank consolidation (controlled, single-pass): Auto-Dreamer 72.7% ALFWorld vs ExpeL 70.1% (+2.6pp); 44.3% ScienceWorld vs Mem0 42.0% (+2.3pp). Narrower margins than continual deployment, consistent with removal of cumulative retrieval-competition effects. Ablations: Writer-only (no consolidation) gives 48.0%/29.6%/44.2%; untrained pipeline (region rewriting without GRPO) gives 59.2%/31.4%/46.6% — showing that structure (region rewriting) accounts for most bank-size reduction and GRPO training adds 9.7pp on ScienceWorld and 5.7pp on WebArena. Without counterfactual reward, the bank grows rapidly during training without corresponding performance gains; with it, the bank first grows then contracts while performance is maintained. Cadence sensitivity: ScienceWorld drops ~10–15pp at k=1 or k=20 relative to k=10; ALFWorld is more robust (range ~5pp). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. The region-rewriting formulation of offline consolidation and the GRPO-trained multi-step tool-using consolidator with counterfactual credit assignment are genuinely novel. The two-timescale architecture is conceptually related to LightMem but differs fundamentally in that LightMem uses fixed prompted pipelines with per-entry CRUD decisions, while Auto-Dreamer learns a rewrite policy over regions. The CLS inspiration is well-established in cognitive science but has not been operationalized as a region-rewriting RL objective for agent memory. The application of GRPO to tool-using memory consolidation (as opposed to task execution) is novel in this setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: the writer-only vs. untrained vs. trained progression isolates the contributions of region rewriting and GRPO; the counterfactual reward ablation shows its specific effect on bank size vs. task performance; the cadence sweep characterizes a key hyperparameter. Bootstrap CIs (N=10,000) are reported in Appendix H — these reveal that the ALFWorld advantage over UMEM is not clearly statistically significant (overlapping 95% CIs). No seed or task-order variance is reported for main runs (acknowledged as a limitation). Prompts and hyperparameters are reported verbatim in the appendix. UMEM and Mem-α are excluded from WebArena and not retrained with larger context, which weakens the WebArena claim. The training distribution includes ScienceWorld evaluation (the consolidator is trained and evaluated on ScienceWorld tasks), creating an in-distribution advantage that is mitigated but not eliminated by the held-out task disjointness. Training uses environment simplifications (teleport, openDoors, self-watering pots) whose effect on learned generalization is not analyzed. No code/checkpoint release URL is provided; the paper references OpenTinker and verl frameworks implicitly. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.20616v1, May 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on 8 H100 GPUs for training and 4 GH200 GPUs for evaluation serving multiple model endpoints; the consolidator (Qwen3-14B) and task agent (Qwen3.5-9B or Gemini) require substantial GPU memory but are commercially available models. The consolidation step is offline (does not block task execution) and triggers periodically. - Efficient: Active memory banks are 6–24× smaller than comparable-performance baselines at retrieval time; the consolidator incurs additional LLM calls every k sessions, but per-episode total token cost across the stream (writer + consolidator + agent) is not reported, making full cost accounting impossible. - Effective: Evidence is strong on ScienceWorld (+7pp, large margin), moderate on ALFWorld (+1.8pp, overlapping CIs with the strongest baseline), and incomplete on WebArena (two strongest RL-trained baselines excluded). - Scalable: Tested on up to 660-task streams; no stress-scale test at 10×–100× episodes is reported. The formal relationship between the local-bank training objective and full persistent-bank performance under long-horizon composition is uncharacterized. Consolidation cadence must be tuned per domain (sensitivity varies substantially). No standalone software artifact is released; contribution is at paper and framework-integration level. Strengths - Region rewriting as a structural compactness primitive is a sharp idea: by making forgetting the default update semantics rather than something to be learned, the policy search space is substantially reduced and the training signal is cleaner. - The counterfactual utility reward correctly distinguishes load-bearing from redundant memories and ablations show it controls bank size without hurting task performance — a clean design choice with clear empirical support. - Transfer across task domains (ScienceWorld → ALFWorld → WebArena) and across writer backbones (Qwen → Gemini) without retraining is the strongest empirical result, providing genuine evidence that the consolidator learns a domain-agnostic procedure over textual memory entries. - Baseline coverage is unusually broad (10 systems, 7 families), implementation details are fully documented with commit hashes, and bootstrap CIs are reported — a higher standard of transparency than typical in this subfield. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) evaluation restricted to three text-based environments, no claims about structured state representations or multimodal settings; (2) robustness to alternative writers, schemas, or noisier provenance links is untested — information missed by the writer cannot be recovered; (3) retrieval budget (top-K=3) favors compact banks and may disadvantage methods that benefit from looser budgets; (4) local-bank training objective is a surrogate for deployment-time bank composition with no formal characterization of the gap; (5) no seed or task-order variance reported, and several ALFWorld margins are small. The authors confront these directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The ALFWorld advantage over UMEM (1.8pp) has overlapping bootstrap CIs and is not statistically significant by the paper's own reported intervals; the abstract's claim of leading on ALFWorld is technically accurate in point estimate but could mislead without this context. - UMEM and Mem-α are excluded from WebArena due to context-window constraints but not retrained — this omission removes the two strongest RL-trained baselines from the hardest benchmark and inflates the apparent WebArena advantage without acknowledgment of the confound. - The region selection policy (new entries + recently retrieved entries) is a heuristic that is never ablated; an alternative selection strategy (e.g., clustering-based or staleness-based) might substantially change results. - Total LLM token consumption per episode across the full pipeline (writer + consolidator + task agent) is not reported, making it impossible to assess whether the compact bank trades reduced retrieval cost for increased consolidation cost. - Training uses three environment simplifications (teleport, openDoors, self-watering pots) for ScienceWorld; the degree to which these simplify the task distribution and inflate the learned policy's apparent generalization is not discussed. - The cadence hyperparameter k is environment-specific and shows substantial sensitivity (ScienceWorld drops ~10–15pp at k=1 or k=20), but no adaptive or learned cadence policy is considered, which would be required for deployment to new domains without tuning. Bottom Line The region-rewriting formulation is a genuinely sharp structural contribution and the ScienceWorld results and cross-domain transfer are strong enough to make this paper worth reading for anyone designing memory systems for multi-session language agents — but the ALFWorld advantage is within noise, the WebArena comparison is incomplete, and practitioners should not treat the results as fully established until code is released and peer review is completed.

Conceptual Schema Inference for Tabular Datasets using Large Language Models

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces two LLM-based methods — GeSI (generative, decoder-based) and EmSI (embedding, encoder-based) — for automatically deriving conceptual schemas (entity types, type hierarchies, attributes, and inter-type relationships) from raw tabular datasets using only column headers and cell values, without external ontologies or task-specific training. - Why it's important: Data lakes and open data portals contain thousands of heterogeneous tables that cannot be navigated or queried without a global conceptual map; existing dataset discovery tools operate at the individual table or attribute level and provide no repository-wide semantic understanding. - How it does it: GeSI chains per-table LLM type-path inference → weighted merging → LLM-verified pruning → column-type hierarchy construction → bottom-up attribute inheritance → attribute-value-guided relationship and cardinality inference; EmSI clusters subjectness-weighted table embeddings into top-level types, then clusters columns into attributes, builds sub-hierarchies from Jaccard-similarity dendrograms with silhouette-based pruning, and identifies relationships via column-embedding similarity thresholds. - The good: GeSI achieves RI > 0.978 and Purity > 0.89 for conceptual attribute inference across all three evaluated benchmarks, and F1 scores of 0.675–0.967 for relationship inference, outperforming the embedding-based EmSI (best F1 ≤ 0.359) on every relationship metric. - The missing: No downstream task evaluation demonstrates that an inferred schema actually improves dataset search, data integration, or annotation quality; the only baselines for type inference and attributes are prior works from the same author group. - Read it or not: Database researchers working on data lake management, ontology learning, or LLM-assisted data integration will find the formal problem definition and the generative vs. embedding trade-off analysis valuable; practitioners should note that GeSI's attribute inference dominates runtime (>90% of total) and reaches ~2000 minutes at 10K tables with a local 14B model.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces two LLM-based methods — GeSI (generative, decoder-based) and EmSI (embedding, encoder-based) — for automatically deriving conceptual schemas (entity types, type hierarchies, attributes, and inter-type relationships) from raw tabular datasets using only column headers and cell values, without external ontologies or task-specific training. - Why it's important: Data lakes and open data portals contain thousands of heterogeneous tables that cannot be navigated or queried without a global conceptual map; existing dataset discovery tools operate at the individual table or attribute level and provide no repository-wide semantic understanding. - How it does it: GeSI chains per-table LLM type-path inference → weighted merging → LLM-verified pruning → column-type hierarchy construction → bottom-up attribute inheritance → attribute-value-guided relationship and cardinality inference; EmSI clusters subjectness-weighted table embeddings into top-level types, then clusters columns into attributes, builds sub-hierarchies from Jaccard-similarity dendrograms with silhouette-based pruning, and identifies relationships via column-embedding similarity thresholds. - The good: GeSI achieves RI > 0.978 and Purity > 0.89 for conceptual attribute inference across all three evaluated benchmarks, and F1 scores of 0.675–0.967 for relationship inference, outperforming the embedding-based EmSI (best F1 ≤ 0.359) on every relationship metric. - The missing: No downstream task evaluation demonstrates that an inferred schema actually improves dataset search, data integration, or annotation quality; the only baselines for type inference and attributes are prior works from the same author group. - Read it or not: Database researchers working on data lake management, ontology learning, or LLM-assisted data integration will find the formal problem definition and the generative vs. embedding trade-off analysis valuable; practitioners should note that GeSI's attribute inference dominates runtime (>90% of total) and reaches ~2000 minutes at 10K tables with a local 14B model. Problem & Motivation Data scientists working with data lakes, web tables, and open data portals face repositories of hundreds to thousands of tables from heterogeneous producers. These repositories exhibit both schema-level heterogeneity (the same attribute represented under different column names) and granularity variation (tables mixing entity records at different hierarchical levels). Existing methods for dataset discovery — keyword search, join discovery, table union search — operate at the level of individual tables or attribute pairs and cannot provide a global conceptual understanding: which entity types are present, what attributes characterize them, and what semantic relationships recur across types. Prior schema inference work targets semi-structured formats (XML, JSON, RDF) using syntactic similarity and is unsuitable for raw tabular data. Data profiling and join discovery enrich the logical schema but do not reduce complexity or resolve semantic inconsistencies. The gap is a method that automatically constructs an Entity-Relationship-style conceptual schema from arbitrary tabular repositories, without curated taxonomies, external ontologies, or task-specific labeled training data. Key Idea The central insight is that LLMs — both generative and embedding variants — contain sufficient world knowledge to map raw table columns to semantic entity types and to reason about hierarchical and relational structure, provided that inference is decomposed into tractable per-table or per-column subtasks and the outputs are merged, verified, and propagated bottom-up. For GeSI, the key trick is introducing an intermediate column type hierarchy as a shared semantic backbone: each column is anchored to its lowest-common-ancestor in this hierarchy, so columns that share a semantic domain (e.g., both Composer and Performer are typed as Musician) are clustered together before being resolved into distinct attributes via LLM role-naming. This prevents premature semantic collapse and is the direct source of GeSI's superior Purity in attribute inference. For EmSI, the key trick is replacing column identity by embedding similarity, using subjectness-weighted table embeddings to cluster tables that describe the same real-world concept regardless of their surface column names. Methodology GeSI is a three-step sequential pipeline. Step 1 infers a global type hierarchy: each table receives one or more type paths (e.g., Thing→Event→MusicEvent) via few-shot LLM prompts with optional constraints (ABS blacklists abstract top-level types; SLC forces second-level convergence; FET pre-extracts leaf types then generates full paths; FULL combines SLC and FET). Per-table paths are merged by edge frequency weighting and pruned via self-loop removal, inverse-edge resolution, and LLM-based is-a verification (each candidate edge A→B is validated by asking the LLM "Is A the parent type of B?"). Step 2 constructs a parallel column type hierarchy, maps each column to its lowest-common-ancestor semantic type, clusters co-typed columns per entity type, names attributes via LLM using column name + sampled values + table context, deduplicates synonymous attribute names (Fig. 7 prompt), and promotes attributes appearing in ≥θ=0.9 of child types up the hierarchy. Step 3 infers relationships by identifying attributes whose sampled values are named entities of other inferred types (two-pass top-K then refined selection), generating a predicate label, and inferring cardinality from three column statistics (null ratio ρ_null, multi-value ratio ρ_multi, uniqueness ratio ρ_uniq) plus sampled rows. LLM backbones evaluated: Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-14B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4. EmSI proceeds in four steps. Step 1 computes subjectness scores (via TableMiner+) for each column, constructs weighted table embeddings as weighted aggregations of column embeddings, and clusters tables via agglomerative clustering. Step 2 clusters all columns within each top-level type by column-embedding similarity; each cluster becomes a conceptual attribute; subject attributes are identified by named-entity membership and subjectness score ≥ 50%. Step 3 runs hierarchical clustering on tables within each top-level type using Jaccard similarity of their conceptual attribute sets, selects dendrogram slices with silhouette score within Δ=0.15 of the maximum, and promotes retained clusters to subtypes. Step 4 establishes a relationship between subject attribute cs_ai of type t_i and non-subject attribute ca_j of type t_j when more than δ_2 of ca_j's columns are within distance δ_1 of cs_ai's columns. Embedding backbones: Starmie, DeepJoin, SwAV (fine-tuned), SBERT, Unicorn (pretrained). Critical design decisions: (a) the hierarchy-first ordering in GeSI is well-justified as type context disambiguates attribute roles; (b) the frequency-weighted edge merging is reasonable but can produce overlapping type clusters (a Book table assigned to both Product and CreativeWork paths) that reduce RI; (c) the silhouette-based dendrogram pruning in EmSI is principled but Δ=0.15 was tuned on WDC and applied across all datasets, which risks overfitting to WDC structure. Key Results All experiments run on a single NVIDIA A100 (80 GB), 5 repetitions averaged. Four benchmarks: WDC (602 tables / 43 types), GDS (660 tables / 53 types), OD Small (158 tables / 24 types), OD Large (10,361 tables / 53 types — type/hierarchy only). Top-level types: GeSI achieves highest Purity on all benchmarks (0.963–0.993 best-variant), outperforming GeTT baseline by 2.8–8.1% and EmSI by 1.6–9.8%. EmSI achieves highest RI on 3/4 benchmarks (SwAV: 0.887 WDC, 0.894 GDS, 0.952 OD Small, 0.892 OD Large). GeSI RI lags primarily because its multi-path assignment creates overlapping clusters. Type hierarchy (PTCS): EmSI dominates (0.800–1.000 best-variant) vs. GeSI (0.633–0.868); EmSI outperforms GeSI's best by 9.3–15.3%. GeSI generates 24–213 more distinct types than EmSI but hierarchies are 0.5–1.0 levels shallower on three datasets. LLM-based verification improves GeSI's PTCS by +0.053 (GDS) to +0.153 (WDC). Prompt constraints (FET/FULL) improve RI by ~7% on WDC; ABS/SLC improve PTCS by 8–12% on WDC. Conceptual attributes: GeSI substantially outperforms EmSI and SI-LLM baseline: GeSI RI 0.978–0.995 / Purity 0.867–0.933 vs. EmSI best RI 0.933/Purity 0.803, SI-LLM best RI 0.966/Purity 0.742 (on WDC). GeSI Purity advantage over EmSI is up to +0.209. Relationships: GeSI best F1 0.729 (WDC), 0.807 (GDS), 0.967 (OD Small). EmSI best F1 0.359 (WDC), 0.177 (GDS), 0.222 (OD Small) — primarily recall-constrained (recall ≤ 0.243). GeSI worst variant outperforms EmSI best by +0.316 (WDC), +0.523 (GDS), +0.563 (OD Small). Cardinality: GeSI GPT-4 achieves 0.804–0.857 accuracy; Qwen2.5-14B peaks at 0.870 on WDC but drops to 0.712 on GDS. GPT-4 is most consistent across datasets. Scalability: Near-O(n) scaling for both methods on OD Large (1K–10K tables). GeSI (GPT-3.5) is ~1.4× slower than EmSI (SwAV) at 10K tables; ~20× slower at 1K tables. GeSI attribute inference accounts for >90% of GeSI runtime. EmSI attribute clustering is O(m²) in columns, becoming the primary bottleneck at large scale. Cost: GeSI with GPT-3.5 costs $1.23–$3.77 per benchmark dataset. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM: The paper formally defines repository-level conceptual schema inference from raw tabular data — distinguishing it from prior work on schema matching, logical schema profiling, and dataset discovery — and is the first to jointly target entity types, type hierarchies, conceptual attributes, and inter-type relationships under minimal metadata assumptions. NEW METHOD: GeSI's use of an intermediate column type hierarchy for attribute disambiguation is genuinely novel; EmSI's subjectness-weighted embedding clustering with silhouette-based hierarchical type inference is a new adaptation of known clustering methods to this problem. NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING: The paper establishes a quantified complementarity between generative and embedding approaches — generative dominates on attribute and relationship inference while embedding dominates on type hierarchy structural consistency — which was not previously characterized. The individual components (LLM prompting, embedding clustering, agglomerative clustering, silhouette pruning) are standard; the contribution lies in their composition and evaluation for this specific problem. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation studies are present and informative: prompt constraint variants are evaluated individually on WDC (Figs. 14–15), and LLM-based verification is ablated on all four datasets (Fig. 16), confirming both design choices contribute meaningfully. Results are averaged over 5 runs, but standard deviations are not reported, making it impossible to assess statistical significance of small differences. Code, prompts, and datasets are publicly released at https://github.com/PierreWoL/SILM/. Ground truth for WDC and GDS is based on Schema.org type annotations with manual attribute and relationship labeling; OD Small/Large ground truth derives Schema.org type paths by upward traversal, not by independent domain-expert labeling, which systematically biases evaluation toward Schema.org vocabulary and may advantage GeSI (which generates Schema.org-like type names from LLM priors). The PTCS metric is novel and defined in this paper without external validation that it correlates with human-judged schema utility. The only baselines for type/hierarchy and attribute inference are GeTT and SI-LLM, both prior works from the same author group; no comparison to non-LLM schema discovery systems (e.g., ALITE, Abstra, classical ontology learning) is included. EmSI's Δ and (δ₁, δ₂) thresholds are selected on WDC and applied to all other datasets, including WDC in the evaluation, which is a minor but real source of optimism in WDC results for EmSI. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: GeSI can run using OpenAI API or local open-weight models (Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-14B) on a single A100 (80 GB); EmSI requires the same hardware for embedding models. Both paths use commercially available hardware, though 80 GB VRAM is high-end. - Efficient: GeSI attribute inference is the dominant cost (>90% of total) with ~2000 minutes at 10K tables using Qwen2.5-14B; EmSI's O(m²) column clustering becomes the bottleneck beyond ~350K columns (currently ~2000 minutes at 10K tables). Neither is near real-time for large repositories. - Effective: GeSI demonstrably solves attribute inference (RI > 0.978, Purity > 0.89) and relationship inference (F1 up to 0.967) on benchmark data; EmSI solves type hierarchy structural consistency (PTCS up to 1.0). No downstream task evaluation confirms utility in actual data lake management. - Scalable: Near-linear scaling demonstrated up to 10,361 tables / 354,666 columns; EmSI's quadratic column complexity would become prohibitive at 10× this column count; GeSI's linear LLM-call count scales better but API cost and latency become significant. The work delivers a software artifact (code + prompts + datasets at GitHub), but no pre-trained models or CLI tool; integration requires engineering effort. Strengths - The formal problem definition (Definition 1–7) cleanly separates conceptual schema inference from related tasks and is the most precise treatment of this problem in the literature, enabling future comparisons. - GeSI's intermediate column type hierarchy construction for attribute disambiguation is a technically sound and empirically validated design choice: it prevents premature semantic collapse (correctly separating Composer and Performer as distinct roles vs. a broad Person attribute) in a way that neither SI-LLM nor EmSI achieves. - The PTCS metric addresses a genuine evaluation problem — multiple valid hierarchies exist for the same data — by measuring ancestor-descendant consistency rather than exact edge match, and is better suited to this task than standard clustering metrics alone. - The efficiency analysis at scale (1K–10K tables, Figs. 17–19) is unusually thorough for a methods paper of this type and provides actionable guidance on when to use each approach. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) GeSI fails on numeric and acronym-heavy columns lacking informative names (e.g., FIFA World Cup position codes "rcm", "st" incorrectly merged into a generic Number cluster); (2) GeSI sometimes produces near-synonymous sibling types (e.g., Musictrack and Song); (3) EmSI's relationship inference captures instance-level rather than conceptual-level relationships, limiting recall; (4) both methods may confuse observed cardinality in sampled data with true conceptual cardinality; (5) EmSI (Unicorn) failed to scale to GDS due to GPU memory limits from quadratic pairwise computation. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The most significant gap is the absence of any downstream task evaluation. The paper does not show that an inferred schema improves dataset search precision, reduces integration effort, or enables better annotation — which is the stated motivation. Schema quality measured against Schema.org ground truth is a proxy, not the target. - All baselines for type/hierarchy inference (GeTT) and attribute inference (SI-LLM) are prior works by the same group. No classical schema inference methods (ALITE, Abstra, schema summarization methods) or non-LLM ontology learning tools are included as comparators, making it impossible to assess how much improvement LLMs actually provide over simpler approaches. - The Schema.org-derived ground truth for OD Small/Large is systematically biased: paths are constructed by upward traversal of Schema.org, not by independent annotation. GeSI's LLM outputs are naturally aligned with Schema.org vocabulary (as it is in LLM pretraining data), which may inflate GeSI's scores relative to methods that do not share this bias. - The 5-run averaging without standard deviations prevents any statistical significance assessment, particularly relevant for closely competing configurations. - EmSI's O(m²) column clustering complexity is mentioned but not addressed; no approximation (e.g., ANN-based similarity) is proposed, which caps EmSI's practical applicability to repositories well below the scale of major data lakes. - The paper claims near-O(n) scaling for GeSI but does not evaluate attribute inference or relationship inference on OD Large (only type/hierarchy), so the claimed scalability is incomplete. Bottom Line Database researchers designing LLM-assisted data lake management systems will find this paper a rigorous and well-executed reference for both the formal problem of conceptual schema inference and the complementary strengths of generative versus embedding approaches; however, the absence of non-LLM baselines and downstream task validation means the practical value of the inferred schemas relative to simpler alternatives remains unestablished.

A Mechanistic Study of Tabular Foundation Models

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
