Key Idea The core insight is that the edge-supported KL merge-barrier signal on a simplicial 2-complex over experts admits an orthogonal Hodge decomposition into gradient, curl, and harmonic components, and the harmonic component is exactly the part that no vertex potential (per-expert score) or triangle-boundary correction (per-face adjustment) can explain. This makes the harmonic component a principled, topology-based certificate of irreducible mergeability obstruction. Turning this decomposition into a selection objective — covering the top-p% harmonic-critical edges and top-qT% triplet-critical triangles with a greedy submodular set function — is the key move that elevates Hodge theory from analysis tool to engineering criterion. Methodology Given a sparse MoE layer with n experts, HodgeCover proceeds in four stages. (1) Barrier construction: compute all pairwise KL merge barriers bij (O(n²) forward passes on 2,048 C4-train tokens) and up to 500 sampled triplet barriers bijk for 3-cliques of the median-barrier subgraph. (2) Complex construction: form the simplicial 2-complex K = (V, E, T) with the complete edge set and the curated triangle set; apply the 1-Hodge Laplacian L1 = ∂₁ᵀ∂₁ + ∂₂∂₂ᵀ using the unweighted form so the harmonic projector is topologically invariant across layers. Solve two sequential least-squares systems via Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse to extract bgrad, bcurl, bharm. (3) Critical simplex extraction: designate top-p% edges by |bharm,e| as harmonic-critical and top-qT% triangles by |bijk| as triplet-critical. (4) Greedy coverage: maximize the monotone submodular objective Φ(S) = Σsal(i) + λe|CE(S)|/|E| + λt|CT(S)|/|T| with the standard (1 − 1/e) greedy guarantee. Non-survivors are redirected to the nearest survivor under a Hodge-weighted barrier that penalizes routing through high-harmonic edges. HodgeCover+Wanda then applies unstructured Wanda weight pruning on survivors at residual sparsity r2 ≈ 0.575 (at 66% total compression with r1 = 0.20 expert drop). Key design decisions: (a) unweighted Laplacian to decouple topology from barrier normalization — well-justified; (b) complete edge set rather than thresholded subgraph to avoid discontinuous spectral behavior — well-justified with analysis; (c) triangle cap at 500 fixed by compute budget rather than sensitivity study — not ablated. Key Results Evaluation covers OLMoE-1B-7B (64 experts, 16 layers), Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B (256 experts, 40 layers), and Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B (256 experts, 48 layers) at 33% and 66% expert reduction rates. Baselines: REAP, REAM, MC-SMoE, STUN+Wanda (published), plus REAP+Wanda and REAM+Wanda computed by the authors. Benchmarks: WikiText-103 perplexity, C4 perplexity, and 9 downstream tasks (ARC-c/e, BoolQ, HellaSwag, MMLU 5-shot, PIQA, TruthfulQA-MC2, WinoGrande, GSM8K 8-shot). At 66% on Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B, HodgeCover+Wanda achieves 74.6% DS-Avg vs. 62.0% for STUN+Wanda (+12.6 pp) and 74.1% for the matched-control REAP+Wanda (+0.5 pp); GSM8K 85.5% vs. 22.1% (STUN+W) and vs. 87.6% (REAP, expert-count axis only). At 66% on Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B, HodgeCover+Wanda achieves 75.9% DS-Avg vs. 70.8% for STUN+Wanda and 75.4% for REAP+Wanda. On the expert-count axis alone (no Wanda), HodgeCover matches REAP within ±0.3 pp DS-Avg on all four Qwen cells. The ablation on Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B at 66% shows the Hodge step accounts for +5.74 pp over Triplet-Penalty soft (same triplet input, no Hodge) and +5.7 pp over Greedy-Barrier; removing triangles entirely costs −11.5 pp; using a binary triangle veto (Triplet-Hypergraph) collapses by −30.5 pp. Harmonic energy fraction ρharm ranges 29–62% of per-layer barrier energy across the three models, confirming the obstruction is non-trivial. Plan-time for the full HodgeCover pipeline on Qwen 3.5-35B is ~480 seconds vs. ~25 seconds for REAP — a 19× overhead, offline and amortized. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuinely novel contribution is operationalizing the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian as a parameter-selection objective for MoE compression — prior topological deep learning work uses Hodge decomposition only as an analysis diagnostic. The inexpressibility result (Remark 1, Proposition 2) formally separates HodgeCover from all per-expert-ranking methods. The empirical finding that harmonic energy constitutes 29–62% of per-layer barrier energy in production MoE models is also new. The application of greedy submodular coverage and the composition with Wanda are standard techniques applied in a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation structure is strong: four ablations systematically isolate the Hodge decomposition (vs. soft triplet penalty), the triangle term (vs. harmonic-edge-only), and hard vs. soft triplet constraints. The Hodge-step contribution of +5.74 pp on Qwen 3.5-35B at 66% is the sharpest number in the paper and is experimentally clean. Matched-control hybrids (REAP+Wanda, REAM+Wanda) are computed by the authors to control for Stage-2 Wanda differences — a creditable methodological choice. Error bars, multiple seeds, and significance tests are absent throughout. Hyperparameters (p = qT = 20%, λe = 1.0, λt = 0.5, α = 3.0) are set by qualitative inspection on a single layer of a single model; no empirical sensitivity grid is reported. MC-SMoE is evaluated without its mandatory KD pass (training on 2,048 tokens causes overfitting), which reduces its baseline strength and is disclosed. Calibration corpus is fixed at 2,048 C4-train tokens, matched across all methods. Code and model weights are not mentioned as released; the paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.13997v1). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (compression system evaluated by downstream benchmarks and perplexity). - Practical: Runs on 2× NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPUs (98 GB each); the barrier sweep and Hodge projection fit within standard research hardware at n ≤ 256 experts, though 256-expert Qwen models require ~196 GB GPU memory for the uncompressed checkpoint before compression. - Efficient: Plan-time is ~480 seconds for Qwen 3.5-35B (vs. ~25 s for REAP), a 19× overhead paid once offline; inference throughput of the compressed model matches REAP+Wanda to within 6% at 66%; the dead-expert ratio at 66% is 1–3 pp higher than REAM+Wanda on Qwen scales, a real cost acknowledged by the authors. - Effective: Achieves best C4 perplexity on every (model, rate) cell for the hybrid axis and best WikiText perplexity on every 66% cell; the +12.6 pp DS-Avg gain over STUN+Wanda on Qwen 3.5-35B at 66% is large and driven by real task performance (GSM8K, MMLU), not perplexity artifacts. - Scalable: Evaluated at up to 122B total parameters (Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B); the pairwise sweep scales as O(n²|D|d²) — manageable at n = 256 but not evaluated beyond this expert count; the authors defer multimodal and RL post-trained MoE checkpoints to future work. The method delivers a compression pipeline (not a library or CLI), with algorithmic pseudocode and full hyperparameter tables provided but no public code repository mentioned. Strengths - The Hodge-decomposition framing is mathematically precise and identifies a structural failure mode of all prior pairwise/triplet compressors through a clean inexpressibility argument (Remark 1, Proposition 2) — this is genuine novelty, not a relabeling. - The ablation isolating the Hodge decomposition from the triplet input (+5.74 pp over Triplet-Penalty on Qwen 3.5-35B at 66%) is the strongest evidence in the paper: the improvement comes from how triplet information is processed, not merely from having it. - Diagnostic measurements (ρharm ∈ [0.29, 0.62] across 104 layers of three production models, Fig. 2) provide evidence that the harmonic obstruction is pervasive in real MoE structures, not a pathological corner case, which directly motivates why the method should work. - The choice of unweighted Laplacian (barriers enter as signal, not as operator weights) is a non-obvious but well-justified design decision that keeps the harmonic projector topologically invariant across layers and model families. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) HodgeCover cannot recover the last few perplexity points that fine-tuning or knowledge distillation would address; (2) plan-time on 35B-parameter models is ~480 seconds (vs. ~25 s for REAP), though amortized across compression rates; (3) evaluation is restricted to language MoE; multimodal and RL post-trained checkpoints are deferred; (4) the triangle cap |T| = 500 is set by wall-clock budget. The authors confront these limitations directly and without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The hyperparameter sensitivity gap is significant: p, qT, λe, λt, α are set by qualitative inspection on one layer of one model and applied universally. The paper provides only analytical boundary behavior (saturation at p → 100%, vanishing at p → 0%) without any empirical sweep. Given that the ablation shows +5.74 pp difference from topological structure, the question of how sensitive this is to the 20% threshold is material. - The triangle cap at |T| = 500 is set by compute budget and is not ablated across values. Appendix A.9 notes that |T|/C(n,3) → 0 at Qwen scale, and that the harmonic component vanishes as |T| → C(n,3) — but where the method degrades on the path between these regimes is not measured. - At 33% compression, STUN+Wanda beats HodgeCover+Wanda on WikiText for OLMoE and Qwen 3.5-35B (e.g., 8.86 vs. 9.18 for Qwen 3.5-35B), and the paper explains this as a C4 perplexity tradeoff, but the explanation for why HodgeCover+Wanda is worse at 33% on WikiText is not mechanistically explored. - The Triplet-Penalty ablation was not run on Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B because the 35B run "cleared the ablation gating threshold" — this is an incomplete ablation on the largest model, where results might differ given the very different ρharm profile. - The matched-control REAP+Wanda closes most of STUN+Wanda's gap; the margin of HodgeCover+Wanda over REAP+Wanda at 66% is 0.3–0.8 pp DS-Avg on the two Qwen scales — real but modest, and driven by a 19× plan-time overhead versus REAP. Bottom Line A practitioner running aggressive (≥66%) learning-free compression of large production MoE models — particularly Qwen-family models — will find HodgeCover+Wanda the current best option on the aggressive frontier, at the cost of a 19× plan-time overhead over REAP; a researcher in topological methods applied to neural network compression will find the Hodge-decomposition framing and the inexpressibility result the most durable contributions.

OProver: A Unified Framework for Agentic Formal Theorem Proving

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OProver, a unified framework for agentic formal theorem proving in Lean 4 that trains a policy to iteratively revise failed proof attempts using retrieved compiler-verified proofs and raw Lean 4 compiler diagnostics, paired with OProofs, a corpus of 1.77M Lean statements, 6.86M compiler-verified proofs, and 1.07M serialized multi-round agentic proving trajectories. - Why it's important: Prior provers treat retrieval and compiler feedback as inference-time heuristics grafted onto a fixed model trained only on finalized proofs, creating a train–inference mismatch that leaves the richest source of supervision—failure traces—unexploited. - How it does it: The policy conditions on a compact state (theorem statement, top-k retrieved proofs, most-recent failed attempt, raw compiler diagnostics) and is trained end-to-end through continued pretraining followed by an iterative loop alternating SFT on round-level repair examples and GSPO reinforcement learning on hard unsolved cases, with newly verified proofs and repair trajectories recirculated into the corpus and retrieval index each round. - The good: OProver-32B, a 32B dense model, achieves Pass@32 of 93.3% on MiniF2F, 58.2% on ProverBench, and 11.3% on PutnamBench—outperforming or matching LongCat-Flash-Prover (560B MoE, ~17× total parameters) on three of five benchmarks, with ablations confirming that iterative compiler feedback contributes the dominant performance share (e.g., removing it drops MathOlympiad from 22.8 to 16.5 for the 32B model). - The missing: The ablation table never isolates retrieval augmentation alone (with feedback intact), so the independent contribution of RAG cannot be measured against the full system—only its marginal value on top of a feedback-free baseline is reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on LLM-based formal verification or agentic post-training pipelines; the co-evolution loop and compact-state formulation are clearly described and the corpus release (OProofs) provides a concrete artifact, but readers primarily interested in tactic-level or tree-search provers will find limited relevance.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OProver, a unified framework for agentic formal theorem proving in Lean 4 that trains a policy to iteratively revise failed proof attempts using retrieved compiler-verified proofs and raw Lean 4 compiler diagnostics, paired with OProofs, a corpus of 1.77M Lean statements, 6.86M compiler-verified proofs, and 1.07M serialized multi-round agentic proving trajectories. - Why it's important: Prior provers treat retrieval and compiler feedback as inference-time heuristics grafted onto a fixed model trained only on finalized proofs, creating a train–inference mismatch that leaves the richest source of supervision—failure traces—unexploited. - How it does it: The policy conditions on a compact state (theorem statement, top-k retrieved proofs, most-recent failed attempt, raw compiler diagnostics) and is trained end-to-end through continued pretraining followed by an iterative loop alternating SFT on round-level repair examples and GSPO reinforcement learning on hard unsolved cases, with newly verified proofs and repair trajectories recirculated into the corpus and retrieval index each round. - The good: OProver-32B, a 32B dense model, achieves Pass@32 of 93.3% on MiniF2F, 58.2% on ProverBench, and 11.3% on PutnamBench—outperforming or matching LongCat-Flash-Prover (560B MoE, ~17× total parameters) on three of five benchmarks, with ablations confirming that iterative compiler feedback contributes the dominant performance share (e.g., removing it drops MathOlympiad from 22.8 to 16.5 for the 32B model). - The missing: The ablation table never isolates retrieval augmentation alone (with feedback intact), so the independent contribution of RAG cannot be measured against the full system—only its marginal value on top of a feedback-free baseline is reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on LLM-based formal verification or agentic post-training pipelines; the co-evolution loop and compact-state formulation are clearly described and the corpus release (OProofs) provides a concrete artifact, but readers primarily interested in tactic-level or tree-search provers will find limited relevance. Problem & Motivation: Formal theorem proving in Lean 4 requires every proof step to pass mechanical kernel verification, creating a dense, semantically rich feedback signal at each failure. Despite this, most neural provers—including recent high-performing systems like Goedel-Prover-V2 and DeepSeek-Prover-V2—are trained exclusively on finalized compiler-verified proofs. When retrieval or compiler feedback is incorporated at all, it appears only at inference time as an external wrapper, not as part of the learned policy. This train–inference mismatch means the model is never optimized to interpret diagnostics or leverage retrieved lemma patterns. Additionally, existing Lean corpora (NuminaMath-LEAN, Lean-Workbook, Goedel-Pset, etc.) record only the final verified proof, discarding the failed attempts, retrieved context, and compiler messages that actually drove proof construction—the supervision needed to learn agentic self-correction. Key Idea: The core insight is that agentic theorem proving should be formulated as a bounded multi-round Markov decision process with a compact state X_t = (s, R_t, p_{t-1}, f_{t-1}), where s is the theorem statement, R_t is the top-k retrieved compiler-verified proofs, p_{t-1} is the previous proof attempt, and f_{t-1} is the raw Lean 4 compiler diagnostic—and that this exact state formulation should be used identically during rollout collection, SFT, and RL training so the training-time interface matches proving-time interaction perfectly. Crucially, rather than projecting compiler output into a hand-designed error taxonomy, OProver passes raw textual diagnostics directly to the policy, preserving fine-grained type-mismatch, goal, and identifier information that categorical encoding would lose. Methodology: OProver has three interlocking components. (1) OProofs construction proceeds via two branches: public Lean resources (NuminaMath-LEAN, Lean-Workbook, etc.) deduplicated and then agentically proved using existing open-source provers; and raw informal math statements mined from Common Crawl and GitHub via a FastText classifier, autoformalized into Lean 4 using CriticLean, then proved. Both branches retain only Lean-verified proofs. (2) Agentic proving at inference time runs up to R refinement rounds; on failure, raw compiler feedback f_{t-1} is fed back to the policy alongside the top-k retrieved proofs from retrieval memory M (using Qwen3-8B-Embedding, selected over Goedel-Prover-V2-8B via 66.7% GPT-4o judge preference on 1,000 retrieval queries). Successful proofs are immediately indexed back into M. (3) Training proceeds in two phases: a one-time continued pretraining on a 65B-token mixture (30% Lean/OProofs, 40% Nemotron-Math-4-Plus, 20% OpenCoder, 10% ProLong-64K) yielding OProver-Base; then iterative post-training alternating SFT (cross-entropy on target proof p_t only, sequence length 40,960) and GSPO RL (reward r_t = 0.8 + 0.2·1[format correct] if Lean-verified, else 0.0; advantages normalized group-relative across all n×R rounds per theorem; n=8 rollouts, R=4 for 8B, R=2 for 32B). DAPO-style group filtering excludes fully-solved and fully-failed groups. Each iteration re-indexes newly verified proofs into M and adds repair trajectories to the SFT pool. Key design decisions: compact state (only most-recent attempt, not full history) is justified by keeping context manageable while preserving the locally-relevant correction signal; raw compiler feedback rather than taxonomy encoding preserves fine-grained repair information; round-level decomposition of trajectories provides process-level supervision without consuming full interaction histories. Key Results: OProver-32B achieves Pass@32 (n=64) of 93.3% on MiniF2F (vs. 90.4% Goedel-Prover-V2-32B with self-correction, 90.2% LongCat-Flash-Prover with TIR), 58.2% on ProverBench (vs. 57.9% LongCat), and 11.3% on PutnamBench (vs. 10.4% LongCat), and ranks second on MathOlympiadBench (22.8% vs. 27.5% LongCat TIR) and ProofNet (33.2% vs. 36.1% LongCat TIR). Even OProver-8B exceeds Goedel-Prover-V2-32B across all five benchmarks. OProver-32B surpasses DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B on all five benchmarks despite 21× fewer parameters. Ablation (Table 3): removing iterative feedback (keeping only round 1) drops OProver-32B by 4.9 points on MiniF2F, 6.3 on MathOlympiad, 7.4 on ProofNet, 6.2 on ProverBench, and 4.3 on PutnamBench—the dominant contribution. Further removing retrieval adds 0.5–1.7 point additional drops. Iterative post-training (Figure 6): OProver-8B improves 12.3 points (79.5→91.8) over 3 post-training rounds on MiniF2F; OProver-32B improves 8.6 points (84.7→93.3) over 2 rounds, with no saturation observed. Test-time scaling (Figure 4): BestPass(B) improves consistently from B=8 to B=256 across all benchmarks, with optimal refinement depth R=16 on easier benchmarks and R=8 on PutnamBench (where low per-chain success probability penalizes narrow sampling). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is the unified co-evolution loop: training agentic feedback-conditioned repair as a learned policy (not inference-time wrapper) and coupling it with a corpus that continuously grows from the prover's own repair traces. The compact-state formulation and GSPO pooling across n×R rounds are concrete technical choices not seen in prior provers. OProofs is the first public Lean corpus to simultaneously include retrieved proof context, compiler feedback, and multi-round repair supervision (Table 1 shows all prior datasets lack at least one of these). The broader approach—iterative self-improvement via verified proofs and failure traces—is related to STP and Seed-Prover, but those systems iterate on statement–proof pairs without serialized multi-turn trajectories. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations in Table 3 cover the two core components (feedback and retrieval) across both model sizes and all five benchmarks—this is thorough by community standards. Figure 6 tracks all intermediate checkpoints across post-training iterations, establishing monotonic improvement. No error bars or significance tests are reported (standard in this community given the computational cost). Code and model weights are publicly released at github.com/multimodal-art-projection/OProver and huggingface.co/m-a-p/oprover. The retriever comparison uses GPT-4o as a pairwise judge over 1,000 queries—an appropriate but not infallible proxy. Autoformalization quality for Branch 2 (informal→Lean) is not independently validated; the corpus trusts CriticLean's output after compiler verification, which filters syntactic correctness but not semantic faithfulness. The baselines marked with † are taken from published reports rather than re-evaluated under OProver's protocol, which may conflate differences in evaluation setup. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: OProver-8B and -32B are dense models deployable on commodity GPU clusters; the Lean 4 verification server runs locally with a 240-second timeout per request, which is within reach of standard academic hardware. - Efficient: Multi-round inference at up to R=16 rounds multiplies generation cost by up to 16× over single-pass provers; at the optimal budget B=256 with R=8, total compute per theorem is substantial but bounded and comparable to best-of-N sampling used by competing systems. - Effective: Ablations confirm the method solves the stated problem (agentic repair of failed proofs), with compiler feedback providing 4.3–7.4 point Pass@32 gains over single-round generation across benchmarks. - Scalable: Test-time scaling shows consistent improvement from B=8 to B=256, with diminishing returns; the co-evolution loop has been run for 2–3 iterations and shows no saturation, but whether additional iterations continue yielding gains at comparable cost is untested. The paper delivers trained model weights and a software system (Lean server + retrieval pipeline), not just a paper-form result. Strengths: - The co-evolution loop design is well-motivated and concretely specified (Algorithm 1): each component (corpus, retrieval memory, SFT data, RL hard cases) maps to a specific routing rule, making the system reproducible. - OProofs fills a genuine gap: it is the only public Lean corpus providing all six supervision dimensions (formal statements, verified proofs, NL rationale, compiler feedback, retrieved proof context, multi-round repair) simultaneously. - The compact-state formulation (condition only on most-recent attempt + feedback, not full history) is a principled design choice that aligns training and inference exactly, avoiding the common failure where a model trained on finalized proofs encounters out-of-distribution multi-turn inputs at deployment. - The test-time compute analysis (§4.2) is unusually rigorous for the field: fixing total budget B and sweeping (R, k) allocation reveals the benchmark-dependent optimal depth, providing actionable guidance for practitioners. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not explicitly enumerate limitations in a dedicated section. The paper acknowledges that on PutnamBench, all methods—including OProver-32B—remain below 12% Pass@32, and that BestPass shows diminishing returns with budget. The paper notes that OProofs statistics cover only the Lean 4 theorem-proving component; auxiliary pretraining corpora are excluded from reported counts. These are factual scope statements rather than critical limitations directly confronted. The lack of an explicit limitations section is a mild credibility concern. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The ablation table (Table 3) removes feedback first, then both feedback and RAG—but never tests RAG-only removal (with feedback intact). The marginal contribution of retrieval is thus measured only on top of an already-degraded no-feedback baseline, potentially understating retrieval's independent value. - Baseline comparisons are not apples-to-apples: OProver uses multi-round rollouts (each sample = a complete trajectory of up to R rounds) while baselines use whole-proof single-pass sampling or best-of-N. Section 4.2 provides a budget-controlled comparison, but the baselines' own BestPass curves are not shown—only OProver's scaling is characterized. - The autoformalization quality of Branch 2 (Common Crawl + GitHub → Lean 4 via CriticLean) is validated only by Lean compiler acceptance, not by semantic faithfulness to the original informal claim. Semantically incorrect but syntactically valid formalizations could degrade proof quality silently. - LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o) for retriever selection is a proxy metric; direct task performance (Pass@k with and without each retriever) would be a stronger validation. - No Pass@1 numbers are reported anywhere; the paper reports exclusively Pass@32, which masks the practical single-attempt performance relevant to many real use cases. - The GSPO hyperparameter choice (n=8 rollouts, R=4 for 8B, R=2 for 32B during RL) is stated but not ablated, so the sensitivity of RL gains to these settings is unknown. Bottom Line: This is a technically solid and well-executed system paper: the co-evolution design is coherent, the OProofs corpus fills a documented gap, the ablations are honest, and the SOTA results on three of five benchmarks at 32B dense scale are genuine—but practitioners should note it is an unreviewed preprint, the multi-round inference cost is non-trivial relative to single-pass baselines, and the ablation design leaves retrieval's independent contribution unclear.

Generative Recursive Reasoning

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GRAM (Generative Recursive reAsoning Models), a framework that replaces the deterministic latent-state update in Recursive Reasoning Models with a learned stochastic Gaussian perturbation, enabling probabilistic multi-trajectory inference trained via amortized variational inference. - Why it's important: All existing RRMs follow a single fixed latent trajectory, making them structurally incapable of representing multiple valid solutions or escaping local minima — a fundamental failure mode on any constraint satisfaction task where the solution space is multimodal. - How it does it: At each recursion step, GRAM computes a deterministic update u_t, then samples a state-dependent Gaussian noise ε_t ~ N(μ_θ(u_t), σ²_θ(u_t)I) and adds it as a residual, inducing a distribution over reasoning trajectories that is trained with an ELBO objective and exploited at inference via parallel trajectory sampling. - The good: GRAM with N=20 parallel samples at 16 iterations achieves 97.0% on Sudoku-Extreme and outperforms TRM at 320 iterations (90.5%), reaching the same accuracy ceiling with a parallelizable rather than sequential compute budget. - The missing: All evaluation targets toy-scale synthetic benchmarks (9×9 grids, 8–10 node graphs, 28×28 images); there is no evaluation on any real-world reasoning task where GRAM's design principles would need to compete with autoregressive systems under realistic conditions. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers designing latent-space or recursive reasoning architectures for combinatorial or constraint satisfaction tasks; practitioners focused on LLM-scale reasoning will find the experimental scope too restricted to act on.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GRAM (Generative Recursive reAsoning Models), a framework that replaces the deterministic latent-state update in Recursive Reasoning Models with a learned stochastic Gaussian perturbation, enabling probabilistic multi-trajectory inference trained via amortized variational inference. - Why it's important: All existing RRMs follow a single fixed latent trajectory, making them structurally incapable of representing multiple valid solutions or escaping local minima — a fundamental failure mode on any constraint satisfaction task where the solution space is multimodal. - How it does it: At each recursion step, GRAM computes a deterministic update u_t, then samples a state-dependent Gaussian noise ε_t ~ N(μ_θ(u_t), σ²_θ(u_t)I) and adds it as a residual, inducing a distribution over reasoning trajectories that is trained with an ELBO objective and exploited at inference via parallel trajectory sampling. - The good: GRAM with N=20 parallel samples at 16 iterations achieves 97.0% on Sudoku-Extreme and outperforms TRM at 320 iterations (90.5%), reaching the same accuracy ceiling with a parallelizable rather than sequential compute budget. - The missing: All evaluation targets toy-scale synthetic benchmarks (9×9 grids, 8–10 node graphs, 28×28 images); there is no evaluation on any real-world reasoning task where GRAM's design principles would need to compete with autoregressive systems under realistic conditions. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers designing latent-space or recursive reasoning architectures for combinatorial or constraint satisfaction tasks; practitioners focused on LLM-scale reasoning will find the experimental scope too restricted to act on. Problem & Motivation Recursive Reasoning Models (RRMs) such as HRM and TRM use iterative latent-state refinement with shared transition functions, decoupling reasoning depth from parameter count and output length. This is appealing for constraint propagation, state tracking, and multi-step inference. However, all existing RRMs are deterministic: they follow a single fixed latent trajectory and converge to one prediction. This is structurally inadequate for problems with multiple valid solutions — such as N-Queens or graph coloring — where deterministic models exhibit mode collapse and coverage rates below 36% regardless of depth. More generally, any problem where a single refinement path can become trapped in a suboptimal attractor suffers from this single-trajectory design. The gap is the absence of principled multi-hypothesis exploration within the RRM paradigm. Key Idea The core insight is to reinterpret the deterministic latent update as the mean of a learned stochastic distribution: instead of z_t = u_t (a fixed next state), GRAM adds a state-dependent Gaussian perturbation ε_t ~ N(μ_θ(u_t), σ²_θ(u_t)I) to produce z_t = u_t + ε_t. This converts repeated recursive computation from a deterministic fixed point into a distribution over trajectories — a latent-variable generative model trained with an ELBO. The key distinguishing move is applying stochasticity at the level of the abstract high-level state (not output decoding, not random initialization), and training the noise distribution with a target-conditioned variational posterior so the model learns which directions to explore, not just random noise. Methodology GRAM maintains a hierarchical latent state z = (h, l): the high-level component h carries abstract reasoning state and is updated stochastically once per outer step; the low-level component l carries fine-grained computation and is refined K times deterministically per outer step (K=6 for Sudoku, K=4 otherwise; T=3 outer steps per supervision step). The full recursion is organized into N_sup=16 supervision steps applied sequentially, with the terminal state of each step passed as input to the next. Training maximizes an ELBO: L_ELBO = E_q[log p(y|z_T, x)] − KL(q_φ(ε|u, y) || p_θ(ε|u)). Because backpropagating through the full T_total = T × N_sup trajectory is prohibitive, gradients are propagated only through the final transition of each supervision step (truncated BPTT), a standard approximation validated empirically by showing that the surrogate objective tracks the full ELBO monotonically during training. At inference, GRAM samples N parallel trajectories from the learned prior and selects the best via majority voting or a Latent Process Reward Model (LPRM) — a value head v_ψ(z_t) trained jointly to predict trajectory accuracy from the latent state. Adaptive Computation Time (ACT) via a Q-learning halt head allows early termination. Three critical design decisions: (1) stochasticity injected only at the high level (ablation confirms low-level noise provides no benefit); (2) learned directional guidance μ_θ is essential in multi-solution settings (stochasticity-only collapses on N-Queens at 50.27%); (3) truncated ELBO surrogate consistently tracks full ELBO (Figure 8 confirms monotone co-decrease on both Sudoku and N-Queens). Key Results On Sudoku-Extreme, GRAM achieves 97.0% vs. TRM 87.4%, HRM 55.0%, Looped TF 61.3% (all at 16 supervision steps; GRAM 10M params vs HRM 27M, TRM 7M). On ARC-AGI-1: GRAM 52.0% vs TRM 44.6%, HRM 40.3%. On ARC-AGI-2: GRAM 11.1% vs TRM 7.8%, HRM 5.0%. Width scaling: N=20 parallel samples at 16 iterations outperforms all deterministic baselines at 320 iterations including TRM (97.0% vs 90.5%), with the advantage of parallelizability. On N-Queens 8×8: GRAM 99.7% accuracy and 90.3% solution coverage (20 samples) vs. best generative baseline MDLM 96.1%/87.2%; deterministic recursive baselines cover at most 36.1%. On 8-vertex Graph Coloring: GRAM 2.7 conflict edges vs AR 19.0 and TRM 109.3. Unconditional Sudoku generation: 99.05% validity with 10.9M params and 16 steps vs D3PM-Uniform-Big 91.33% with 55.1M params and 1,000 steps. Binarized MNIST: IS 2.04, FID 73.34 at 256 inference steps vs D3PM 1.86/74.03 at 1,000 steps. Baselines within the recursive model class are current and reproduced under identical settings. LLM comparisons (o3-mini-high, GPT-5.2, Grok-4-thinking, Gemini 3 Pro) are reported as reference points for benchmark difficulty, not controlled baselines. The surprising finding that Deepseek-R1 (671B parameters) scores 0% on Sudoku-Extreme — matching direct prediction baselines — underscores that these benchmarks probe a reasoning axis not addressed by pretraining scale alone. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. The genuinely novel contribution is framing stochastic latent-state dynamics (previously used in VRNNs, DSSMs, and Dreamer-family world models for temporal observation modeling) as computation rather than perception: latent transitions define possible reasoning trajectories, supporting conditional and unconditional generation from a single compact model. Width-based inference-time scaling via parallel trajectory sampling within the RRM paradigm is novel. The underlying components (variational inference, hierarchical latent dynamics, truncated BPTT, ACT) are individually established; the contribution lies in their coherent integration and reinterpretation as a reasoning framework. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Table 3a shows cumulative contribution of deep supervision, hierarchical recursion, and stochastic guidance; Table 3b isolates the roles of stochasticity vs. directional guidance. Error bars (mean ± std) are reported across runs in Table 1. The surrogate ELBO approximation is empirically validated by tracking both the training objective and the full ELBO throughout training (Figure 8), showing monotone co-decrease. Code is referenced at a website (ahn-ml.github.io/gram-website) but no explicit public repository or model release URL appears in the paper; this is a preprint (arXiv:2605.19376). Methodological concern: the KL coefficient β is tuned per task over a wide range (0.04–0.5) and no β sensitivity ablation is provided, indicating possible fragility. The ARC-AGI comparison with TRM is partially confounded by augmentation: TRM's published numbers use 1,000 augmentations per problem, while the paper's stated comparison is at a fixed epoch/hyperparameter budget; Appendix D.2 addresses this partially but the main table does not make augmentation regime explicit for all methods. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: Trained on 8× NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs; training times range from 1 hour (N-Queens 8×8) to 5 days (ARC-AGI). Inference with N=20 parallel samples is feasible on consumer GPU hardware. - Efficient: GRAM (10M params) is more parameter-efficient than HRM (27M) and comparable to TRM (7M). Parallel sampling multiplies inference FLOPs by N, but this is offset by the ability to reduce sequential recursion depth; N=20 at 16 iters achieves what deterministic baselines need 320 iters to reach, and the N trajectories are fully parallelizable unlike sequential depth. - Effective: Achieves best accuracy and coverage among comparable recursive reasoning models on all five task families tested; the structural advantage on multi-solution tasks (mode collapse of deterministic baselines is irreversible regardless of depth) is well-established. However, all evidence is on small-scale toy benchmarks. - Scalable: The sequential nature of deep supervision — N_sup gradient updates per example — is explicitly acknowledged as a barrier to scaling toward larger foundation models. No experiments beyond toy scale are presented; scalability to real-world problem sizes is claimed but not evidenced. The paper delivers a model and training procedure; code availability is unclear from the paper text alone. Strengths - Framing stochastic latent-state transitions as a distribution over reasoning trajectories (rather than observation uncertainty) is a conceptually clean contribution that bridges two previously separate literatures: probabilistic SSMs and recursive reasoning architectures. - Width-based inference scaling via parallel trajectories is practically useful because it is embarrassingly parallelizable, unlike sequential depth scaling, and the paper provides a concrete mechanism (LPRM) for trajectory selection without external verifiers. - The ablation precisely characterizes the roles of each component: deterministic guidance alone (variance → 0) causes catastrophic overfitting to the target (0% on both tasks); stochasticity without learned direction works for unimodal tasks but collapses on multi-solution problems (50.27% N-Queens); the full combination is required for robust performance. - The unconditional generation capability emerging from the same architecture — with validity and quality competitive with diffusion baselines at a fraction of the parameter count and step count — is a non-obvious and empirically well-supported finding. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state that the sequential nature of deep supervision limits training efficiency compared to Transformers, posing a significant barrier to scaling GRAM toward larger foundation models. They also note that multi-sample inference may increase compute and energy costs at scale, and that real-world deployment requires rigorous uncertainty calibration. These are confronted transparently rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All benchmarks are synthetic toy problems (9×9 Sudoku, 8–10 node graphs, 8×8/10×10 N-Queens, 28×28 images); there is no evidence the framework transfers to any real-world reasoning task, language understanding, or planning problem where practical impact is claimed. - The KL coefficient β is tuned per task across a 12× range (0.04 to 0.5) with no sensitivity analysis; this suggests significant hyperparameter fragility that is uncharacterized. - The augmentation regime is inconsistent across the TRM vs. GRAM ARC-AGI comparison in Table 8: TRM's published numbers rely on 1,000 augmentations per test instance, making the main table comparison potentially misleading until Appendix D.2 partially unpacks it. - Parallel trajectory sampling is functionally similar to Best-of-N with a learned process reward model — a well-studied technique in the LLM literature. No comparison to BoN with task-specific verifiers (which would be trivial to implement for Sudoku and graph coloring) is provided, leaving open whether the latent-trajectory structure adds anything beyond what external verification already enables. - No wall-clock latency comparison is reported; it is possible that GRAM N=20 at 16 steps is slower in wall time than TRM at 320 steps despite equivalent accuracy, depending on GPU utilization and memory overhead. Bottom Line A researcher building compact recursive or latent-space reasoning systems for constraint satisfaction or multi-solution combinatorial problems gains a concrete and well-ablated design principle from reading this paper; practitioners focused on LLM-scale reasoning or real-world applications will find the experimental scope too restricted to draw transferable conclusions.

Teaching Language Models to Think in Code

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces THINC (Thinking in Code), a training framework that restructures tool-integrated reasoning so that code itself serves as the primary reasoner — replacing the interleaved natural-language/code paradigm — through trajectory distillation, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning, producing THINC-4B which achieves 78.1% avg@16 across five competition-level math benchmarks. - Why it's important: Interleaved tool-integrated reasoning systems routinely let NL arithmetic errors propagate silently into code, use code only as a post-hoc verifier rather than a solver, and duplicate work between NL and code; these structural flaws corrupt final answers and degrade robustly under execution failures, limiting the ceiling of LLM mathematical reasoning. - How it does it: THINC constrains every trajectory to a single NL planning block followed by code-only reasoning blocks connected solely through interpreter execution outputs, then trains this format via 12.2k distilled trajectories (SFT) and three-stage GRPO/DAPO reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. - The good: THINC-4B achieves 78.1% avg@16 across five competition benchmarks, outperforming all TIR baselines including the much larger rStar2-Agent-14B and ReTool-32B, surpassing Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking on four of five benchmarks, and maintaining 64–69% recovery@3 under consecutive code failures versus near-collapse for interleaved baselines. - The missing: All experiments are confined to 1.7B and 4B parameter models and competition-level mathematics; whether the code-centric format transfers to larger scales or other tool-integrated domains (e.g., web search, multi-tool agentic tasks) is entirely untested. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on mathematical reasoning, tool-integrated LLM training, or RL post-training — the structural diagnosis of interleaved TIR is sharp and the ablation against ASTER-4B (matched base, teacher, and RL pipeline) isolates the trajectory format as the source of gain.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces THINC (Thinking in Code), a training framework that restructures tool-integrated reasoning so that code itself serves as the primary reasoner — replacing the interleaved natural-language/code paradigm — through trajectory distillation, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning, producing THINC-4B which achieves 78.1% avg@16 across five competition-level math benchmarks. - Why it's important: Interleaved tool-integrated reasoning systems routinely let NL arithmetic errors propagate silently into code, use code only as a post-hoc verifier rather than a solver, and duplicate work between NL and code; these structural flaws corrupt final answers and degrade robustly under execution failures, limiting the ceiling of LLM mathematical reasoning. - How it does it: THINC constrains every trajectory to a single NL planning block followed by code-only reasoning blocks connected solely through interpreter execution outputs, then trains this format via 12.2k distilled trajectories (SFT) and three-stage GRPO/DAPO reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. - The good: THINC-4B achieves 78.1% avg@16 across five competition benchmarks, outperforming all TIR baselines including the much larger rStar2-Agent-14B and ReTool-32B, surpassing Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking on four of five benchmarks, and maintaining 64–69% recovery@3 under consecutive code failures versus near-collapse for interleaved baselines. - The missing: All experiments are confined to 1.7B and 4B parameter models and competition-level mathematics; whether the code-centric format transfers to larger scales or other tool-integrated domains (e.g., web search, multi-tool agentic tasks) is entirely untested. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on mathematical reasoning, tool-integrated LLM training, or RL post-training — the structural diagnosis of interleaved TIR is sharp and the ablation against ASTER-4B (matched base, teacher, and RL pipeline) isolates the trajectory format as the source of gain. Problem & Motivation: Mathematical reasoning in LLMs has increasingly relied on tool-integrated reasoning (TIR), where NL thought blocks alternate with code execution across multiple turns. Practitioners in LLM post-training and competition-math solving face three recurring failure modes: (1) the model completes a derivation in NL, then runs code only to confirm — code adds no new computation; (2) when NL performs arithmetic, errors propagate as hard-coded constants into subsequent code blocks without detection; (3) NL and code duplicate each other's reasoning roles, with NL laying out the algorithm step-by-step and code merely transcribing it. These structural problems are intrinsic to the interleaved TIR format rather than being model-specific failures, and prior RL or prompting interventions have not addressed them at the trajectory-format level. Key Idea: The key insight is that code is already a symbolic reasoning system equivalent to mathematics in expressive power, so NL reasoning between code blocks is structurally redundant and error-prone. THINC eliminates NL from the reasoning chain entirely after an initial strategic planning step: the first block (t1) expresses only a high-level solution strategy, and every subsequent block is code whose inputs are exclusively the execution outputs of prior code blocks. This means every intermediate value is produced and verified by the interpreter by construction, NL errors cannot propagate, and the roles of planning (NL) and computation (code) are cleanly separated. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. First, trajectory distillation: using a 3-shot prompt demonstrating the THINC format, the authors sample one trajectory per problem from Qwen3.5-27B on Skywork-OR1 and OpenMathReasoning (English, positive-integer-answer problems). Trajectories are retained only if correct, error-free in every code block, contain ≥3 code blocks, and spend <50% of tokens in the planning thought block (|t1|/|τ| < 0.5). This yields 12,200 THINC-SFT trajectories. Second, supervised fine-tuning: Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 are fine-tuned on THINC-SFT using the standard next-token prediction objective (m_k = 1 for all tokens), context length 32K, learning rate 7×10⁻⁶ with cosine schedule, batch size 16, 3 epochs. Third, reinforcement learning: starting from SFT checkpoints, GRPO is applied on DAPO-Math-17k using the DAPO variant with token-level normalization and asymmetric clipping (ε_low = 0.20, ε_high = 0.28), no KL penalty, rollout batch size 128, G = 8 trajectories per prompt. Training runs three curriculum stages of increasing context budget and tool call limit (Stage 1: 280 steps, 16K context, 20 tool calls; Stage 2: 120 additional steps filtering trivial problems; Stage 3: from step 400, 32K context, 40 tool calls). The reward function is binary exact-match. Key design decisions: (1) retaining all tokens in the SFT loss (including execution outputs) — authors find no significant difference from masking them; (2) strict filtering criteria that enforce the code-centric format at distillation time rather than relying on RL alone; (3) the per-code-block stationless design where variables do not persist between turns, forcing re-imports and hardcoded values from prior results. Key Results: THINC-4B achieves 78.1% ± 4.4% avg@16 across AIME 2024 (88.3%), AIME 2025 (85.8%), AIME 2026 (86.0%), HMMT 2025 February (74.0%), and BeyondAIME (56.1%). This is the best result in the comparison table, outperforming all TIR baselines: ASTER-4B (73.8%), rStar2-Agent-14B (61.5%), ReTool-32B (50.7%), DemyAgent-4B (57.3%), CoRT-1.5B (57.0%). It also surpasses the NL-only Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking (75.2%) on four of five benchmarks, and outperforms its own distillation teacher Qwen3.5-27B (64.7% with 3-shot) by 13.4 points despite being 7× smaller. The most controlled ablation compares THINC-4B against ASTER-4B, which shares the same base model, teacher capacity, and RL pipeline but uses an interleaved trajectory structure: THINC-4B exceeds ASTER-4B by 4.1 points on average with fewer tool calls (6.1 vs. 11.1) and shorter responses (13.5k vs. 15.4k tokens). Code-grounding analysis shows 99.2% of THINC-4B final answers appear in at least one code block's execution output, versus 88.4% for ReTool and 74.3% for rStar2. Recovery@k (fraction reaching correct answer after k consecutive initial code failures) shows THINC-4B maintains 64–69% recovery for k = 1,2,3, dropping to 54.5% at k = 4 and 33.3% at k = 5, while ASTER drops from 52.1% to 18.5% and rStar2-Agent collapses to 0% at k = 5. OOD evaluation on GPQA-Diamond shows THINC-4B at 66.48% avg@16 and 91.41% best@16, compared to ASTER-4B at 63.42/90.40 and base Qwen3-4B-Thinking at 66.32/83.84. THINC-1.7B reaches 42.8% avg@16, outperforming Qwen3-1.7B (32.2%), the tool-prompted base (29.8%), and CoRT-1.5B (25.7%). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuinely novel contribution is the trajectory format design: restricting NL to a single strategic planning step and routing all subsequent reasoning through code-only blocks connected by execution outputs. The individual training components — trajectory distillation from a teacher model, SFT, GRPO/DAPO RL with verifiable rewards — are established techniques applied from prior TIR and post-training literature. The THINC-SFT dataset of 12,200 trajectories is a new artifact. The combination of format design + the empirical demonstration that this structural change explains the performance gap (via the ASTER-4B ablation) constitutes a substantive contribution beyond applying known methods to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper's key ablation — comparing THINC-4B against ASTER-4B under matched conditions (same base model, same teacher, same RL pipeline, differing only in trajectory format) — is the strongest piece of evidence isolating the format as the causal variable; this is methodologically sound. Error bars (95% confidence intervals) are reported throughout. The code-grounded answer rate (99.2%) and Recovery@k metric are well-designed behavioral analyses that go beyond accuracy numbers. No significant ablation is provided to show which of the three SFT filtering criteria (correctness, error-free execution, ≥3 code blocks, planning token ratio) individually matter — the filtering criteria are justified by intuition but not ablated. The claim that m_k = 1 vs. m_k = 0 (masking execution tokens) produces no significant difference is stated but not shown in detail. Code and models are declared as "will be released soon" — not yet publicly available at submission time. Training used 8× NVIDIA H200 GPUs. Multiple random seeds for RL training are not mentioned explicitly. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (training framework + trained models evaluated on benchmarks). - Practical: Training on 8× NVIDIA H200 GPUs is commercially accessible in cloud settings; inference runs within a 32K token budget using standard Python interpreter access (numpy, scipy, sympy). - Efficient: THINC-4B uses fewer tool calls (6.1 vs. 11.1 for ASTER) and generates shorter responses (13.5k vs. 15.4k tokens), giving lower inference cost than comparable interleaved baselines at the same model size. - Effective: 78.1% avg@16 on five competition-level benchmarks with 99.2% code-grounded answers provides strong evidence the method solves the stated problem of unreliable NL-mediated computation. - Scalable: Experiments are confined to 1.7B and 4B parameter models; whether the format and training recipe hold at 7B, 14B, or 70B+ is not tested and constitutes the single most important open question. The paper delivers two trained model checkpoints (pending release) and a 12.2k SFT dataset; no public library or CLI tool is provided. Strengths: - The three-failure-mode diagnosis of interleaved TIR (post-hoc verification, NL error propagation, role confusion) is precise and falsifiable, not merely motivational. - The ASTER-4B ablation is unusually clean: matching base model, teacher, and RL pipeline isolates trajectory format as the variable, providing stronger causal evidence than most TIR papers. - Recovery@k is a novel and informative metric that reveals robustness properties invisible to accuracy-only evaluation; the result that THINC maintains 64–69% recovery while interleaved systems degrade to near-zero is a concrete behavioral characterization. - The OOD evaluation on GPQA-Diamond shows the code-centric format generalizes beyond mathematical training distribution, which partially addresses the scope concern. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state two limitations: (1) experiments are restricted to 1.7B and 4B parameter models due to computational constraints; (2) evaluation is confined to competition-level mathematics. They name extension to larger models and other tool-integrated domains as natural future directions. They confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The SFT dataset is drawn from Skywork-OR1 and OpenMathReasoning, which overlap significantly with competition math benchmarks — the degree of train/test contamination across AIME and HMMT problems is not analyzed. - THINC's stateless code execution (each block is a fresh Python process, variables do not persist) is an unusual design choice that forces models to hardcode prior results; this may harm performance on problems requiring long stateful derivations, but no analysis of failure cases where statefulness would have helped is provided. - The SFT filtering criteria (particularly the 50% planning-token ratio threshold) are engineering choices presented without ablation; the sensitivity of downstream RL performance to these thresholds is unknown. - The comparison includes Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking, a much larger NL-only model, but does not compare against any large TIR system (e.g., a 70B TIR model); the ceiling of the interleaved TIR paradigm at large scale is therefore not established. - Code and models are not yet released, making independent replication impossible at time of publication. Bottom Line: Any practitioner building or training tool-integrated mathematical reasoning systems should read this paper — the ASTER-4B ablation credibly demonstrates that the trajectory format alone (not model size or RL recipe) drives a meaningful accuracy gain, and the Recovery@k analysis reveals a robustness advantage that standard evaluation metrics miss entirely.

Multi-Stream LLMs: Unblocking Language Models with Parallel Streams of Thoughts, Inputs and Outputs

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces multi-stream parallel generation, an instruction-tuning paradigm that trains decoder-only LLMs to simultaneously read from and write to multiple parallel token streams in a single forward pass, replacing the sequential single-stream chat format. - Why it's important: Current chat models must finish consuming input before generating output, cannot think while reading, and cannot react to new inputs mid-generation — a bottleneck that grows more costly as agentic systems incorporate longer tool chains, subagent loops, and chain-of-thought blocks. - How it does it: Each role (user, system, thinking, response, audit) is assigned a dedicated token stream with independent per-stream RoPE positional encoding and a cross-stream causal attention mask that allows every stream to attend to all other streams' tokens at strictly earlier timesteps. - The good: Token Number to First Target Token (TNFT) drops to 0 across all tested benchmarks — the model emits response tokens before the user finishes typing — and adding a parallel audit stream achieves 1.63× throughput speedup over sequential solving-then-auditing while accuracy is preserved or slightly improved. - The missing: All experiments use models up to 27B parameters trained on small synthetic instruction datasets (3–48k samples per task for 3 epochs); scaling to frontier model sizes with large post-training pipelines is not evaluated. - Read it or not: ML researchers working on LLM inference efficiency, real-time agentic systems, or AI safety monitorability should read this — it is a principled, multi-benefit architectural proposal with concrete experiments, though frontier-scale validation remains absent.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces multi-stream parallel generation, an instruction-tuning paradigm that trains decoder-only LLMs to simultaneously read from and write to multiple parallel token streams in a single forward pass, replacing the sequential single-stream chat format. - Why it's important: Current chat models must finish consuming input before generating output, cannot think while reading, and cannot react to new inputs mid-generation — a bottleneck that grows more costly as agentic systems incorporate longer tool chains, subagent loops, and chain-of-thought blocks. - How it does it: Each role (user, system, thinking, response, audit) is assigned a dedicated token stream with independent per-stream RoPE positional encoding and a cross-stream causal attention mask that allows every stream to attend to all other streams' tokens at strictly earlier timesteps. - The good: Token Number to First Target Token (TNFT) drops to 0 across all tested benchmarks — the model emits response tokens before the user finishes typing — and adding a parallel audit stream achieves 1.63× throughput speedup over sequential solving-then-auditing while accuracy is preserved or slightly improved. - The missing: All experiments use models up to 27B parameters trained on small synthetic instruction datasets (3–48k samples per task for 3 epochs); scaling to frontier model sizes with large post-training pipelines is not evaluated. - Read it or not: ML researchers working on LLM inference efficiency, real-time agentic systems, or AI safety monitorability should read this — it is a principled, multi-benefit architectural proposal with concrete experiments, though frontier-scale validation remains absent. Problem & Motivation Modern chat-format LLMs inherit a sequential single-stream design from early instruction-tuned models: all roles (user, system, model, tools) are concatenated into one token sequence, so every message must end before the next begins. In practice this means a deployed agent cannot act while thinking, cannot think while reading, and cannot receive new user input mid-generation without external intervention. This sequential bottleneck grows increasingly costly as agentic pipelines incorporate long tool call chains, subagent communications, and explicit reasoning blocks. Beyond latency, the shared embedding space creates a privilege-separation failure: injected user-content tokens and trusted system tokens are architecturally indistinguishable, enabling prompt injection attacks. Separately, chain-of-thought reasoning traces may be unfaithful or suppressed under output supervision pressure, limiting the ability of external monitors to detect misaligned reasoning. These problems affect ML engineers and AI safety researchers working on real-time coding agents, computer-use agents, and oversight systems for deployed LLMs. Key Idea The core insight is that the single-stream chat format is a historical convention, not a transformer requirement: a standard decoder-only model can be extended to generate H token sequences in parallel by (1) assigning each role a dedicated stream with independent positional encoding, (2) enforcing a cross-stream causal attention mask so each stream can attend to all other streams at strictly earlier timesteps, and (3) instruction-tuning on synthetic multi-stream dialogue data. This single change simultaneously enables overlapping read/think/write, provides architectural privilege separation between instruction sources, and exposes parallel internal reasoning traces to external monitors. Methodology The approach has four components. First, the multi-stream probability factorization: p_θ(y^(1),...,y^(H)) = Π_{h,t} p_θ(y_t^(h) | y_{<t}^(h), {y_{<t}^(h')}_{h'≠h}), satisfying intra-stream causality (each stream autoregressively generates its own past) and cross-stream causality (stream h attends to all other streams' tokens at strictly earlier timesteps t). Second, two transformer modifications: (a) stream-aware positional encoding — each stream maintains its own independent RoPE counter starting from zero, plus a learnable per-stream embedding e_h^s added to every token; (b) a binary stream causal mask M_{(h,t),(h',τ)} = 1 iff τ < t, generalizing the standard causal mask across streams. Third, training uses interleaved token packing (tokens reordered position-wise across streams rather than concatenated stream-end-to-end), which produces a near-lower-triangular attention layout compatible with FlashAttention's causal fast path, and standard cross-entropy loss (optional stream-contrastive variant upweights cross-stream-dependent tokens via log-probability shift). Synthetic multi-stream training data is constructed via a three-stage pipeline: wait-k stream-like generation (frontier LLMs produce responses beginning after only k source tokens), LLM-based causal verification (judge checks each assistant chunk references only temporally available user tokens), and two-level quality filtering (per-stream fluency/completeness and cross-stream role adherence). Experiments use Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B (efficiency), Qwen2.5-7B and Qwen3-4B (security), Qwen3-8B and Qwen3.5-27B (monitorability), all trained for 3 epochs on 4×B200 GPUs. Critical design decisions: interleaved vs. sequential packing is well-justified by FlashAttention compatibility; independent per-stream RoPE is ablated against 8 alternatives (2D RoPE variants, offset, rotation, NoPE) and wins on 3/4 IFEval metrics; the stream-contrastive training loss is described but its effect vs. standard CE is not prominently reported in main tables. Key Results Efficiency (Tables 1, 2, 6, 8, Figure 10): TNFT drops to 0 across all six benchmarks for both two-stream (Solving While Reading) and three-stream (Auditing While Solving) settings, versus Base (TNFT: 117–336) and Vanilla single-stream (TNFT: 103–358). End-to-end delay on LogicNLI falls from 45.95 tokens (Vanilla) to 38.18 (Ours, 1.7B) and from 58.31 to 47.39 (Ours, 4B). MSL is halved relative to Vanilla+Reflection on LogicNLI (2453 vs. 4207 for 1.7B). Parallel auditing achieves 1.63× throughput speedup over sequential solving-then-auditing (Figure 10). Task accuracy is largely preserved: GSM8K 89.51% (Stream 1.7B) vs. 90.37% Base / 90.60% Vanilla; MATH500 51.60% vs. 48.40% Base / 48.20% Vanilla; LogicNLI three-stream 65.65% vs. 64.95% Vanilla+Reflection (1.7B). Security (Table 3): indirect prompt injection ASR (StruQ-ID) drops from 76.00% to 42.23% (Qwen2.5-7B) and 80.69% to 41.91% (Qwen3-4B) without any adversarial training. NESSiE Safe & Helpful score rises from 8.21 to 28.21 (2.5-7B) and 17.95 to 23.07 (3-4B). However, Gandalf direct injection ASR rises from 97.57% to 98.19% (2.5-7B) and 88.81% to 95.71% (3-4B), a regression the paper does not discuss. Monitorability (Table 4): alignment-faking eval-awareness rises from 0.37 to 0.84 (Stream-8B, 95% CI 0.74–0.94), monitor accuracy from 0.30 to 0.43, and concern sub-vocalization from 0.07 to 0.42; gains are consistent at 27B scale. Baselines use current Qwen3-series models (2025) and are appropriate for the tested scale; no comparison against GPT-4-class frontier models. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. The unified formalization of multi-stream parallel generation with cross-stream causal attention, interleaved packing, and stream-aware RoPE, plus the synthetic data pipeline for converting sequential corpora into multi-stream training samples, is the primary contribution. Genuinely novel is the single-architecture treatment of latency, privilege separation, and monitorability as joint consequences of stream decomposition. What is a standard combination: wait-k-style data augmentation (established in simultaneous MT), LLM-judge quality filtering, and fine-tuning on synthetic data are existing techniques applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover positional encoding strategies (8 alternatives on IFEval, Table 7) but not the causal verification step, stream-contrastive loss vs. standard CE, or the effect of stream count H on accuracy. Security and monitorability experiments report standard deviations / 95% bootstrap CIs (n=1000) across 3 seeds; efficiency tables (Tables 1, 2, 6) do not report variance. Code, models, demo, and data are linked on the title page (GitHub, HuggingFace). Methodological concerns: (1) monitorability evaluation uses synthetic stress-test scenarios generated by the same Claude Opus 4.5–4.7 models that produced training data, creating potential circularity; (2) security experiments intentionally start from pretrained base models (not safety-tuned variants) to isolate architectural benefit, but leave the gap vs. properly safety-trained single-stream models unmeasured; (3) the paper is a preprint with no peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Trains on 4×NVIDIA B200 GPUs; inference uses standard decoder-only attention with a modified mask and position encoding, compatible with FlashAttention and standard serving stacks — commercially deployable at small-to-mid scale today. - Efficient: Theoretical latency is bounded by the longest stream (H× speedup over sequential decoding); empty idle tokens incur zero KV-cache entries; total token count increases but user-perceived latency (MSL) drops substantially. - Effective: TNFT=0 is a directly measured hard outcome, not a proxy; task accuracy is preserved within ~1–2% across most benchmarks; security and monitorability gains are measured on standard held-out benchmarks, not self-report. - Scalable: Tested at 1.7B–27B parameters; no experiments at 70B+ scale; authors explicitly acknowledge this gap and note that larger-scale post-training may be needed to fully demonstrate benefits. The paper delivers linked code, trained model weights, a demo, and a dataset (title page links), constituting a reusable software artifact beyond paper form. Strengths - The multi-benefit framing is the paper's most valuable intellectual contribution: a single architectural change (stream separation) simultaneously reduces latency, hardens privilege separation, and exposes internal reasoning to monitors — three problems normally addressed by three separate techniques. - TNFT=0 is a qualitatively different result, not an incremental improvement: the model produces first output tokens while still receiving input, enabling genuinely interrupt-driven interaction that is impossible in single-stream models. - The 33+ percentage-point reduction in StruQ-ID ASR without any adversarial training demonstrates that structural stream isolation provides a meaningful baseline security property purely from format design. - The monitorability experiments are carefully connected to active AI safety literature (alignment faking, covert behavior taxonomy from Meinke et al. 2025) and use realistic concern categories, giving the results practical relevance beyond benchmark gaming. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: models are small (up to 27B) and trained on limited synthetic instruction data relative to modern post-training pipelines; parallel streaming is not beneficial for all applications (text processing, limited-interaction pipelines, standard chat); the work focuses on dense attention patterns and alternative sparse patterns (striped, one-way, partial isolation) remain unexplored; MATH500 improvements in the three-stream setting are largely attributable to format correction rather than substantive parallel reflection. The authors confront these limitations directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Gandalf direct injection benchmark shows the Stream model performing worse than Vanilla on both model scales (ASR rises by ~0.6–7 points); the paper neither discusses nor explains this regression. - All security experiments compare against base models, not instruction-tuned or safety-tuned single-stream models; the actual deployment-relevant comparison (safety-tuned single-stream vs. stream model) is unmeasured. - Monitorability training data is generated by Claude Opus 4.5–4.7 and evaluated partly using gpt-5.4-mini as judge; the circularity between generating model and evaluation rubric is not discussed. - The effect of stream count H on task accuracy, security, and monitorability is not studied — all efficiency experiments use 2 or 3 streams; it is unclear whether benefits saturate or degrade as H grows. - No experiment verifies that multi-stream fine-tuned models retain full single-stream capability when collapsed to a single stream on tasks not included in the training mix. Bottom Line A practitioner building real-time agentic LLM systems or an AI safety researcher working on reasoning monitorability should read this — the idea is principled, the implementation is concrete, and the multi-benefit framing of one training change addressing latency, privilege separation, and oversight simultaneously is the kind of architectural insight that could influence how next-generation models are trained, pending frontier-scale validation.

GriNNder: Breaking the Memory Capacity Wall in Full-Graph GNN Training with Storage Offloading

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GriNNder, a framework that enables full-graph GNN training on a single GPU by offloading activations and gradients to NVMe SSD storage through a coordinated cache-(re)gather-bypass mechanism, with a custom lightweight graph partitioner and a gradient-engine that eliminates redundant snapshot storage. - Why it's important: Full-graph GNN training preserves complete neighborhood information (unlike mini-batch sampling) and is the gold standard for algorithmic validation, but storing all intermediate activations and gradients for graphs with tens to hundreds of millions of nodes exhausts GPU and host memory, forcing researchers to either use costly multi-GPU clusters or compromise with sampling. - How it does it: Structured Storage Offloading (SSO) exploits the power-law distribution of cross-partition dependencies to cache only frequently-needed activations in host memory at partition granularity, regathers activation snapshots on-demand during backpropagation rather than pre-storing them (eliminating α-fold data amplification), and bypasses host memory for low-reuse data via GPUDirect Storage. - The good: GriNNder trains 3-layer GCN on the 100M-node Papers graph in 9.07 min/epoch on a single 24GB GPU where HongTu runs out of swap memory entirely, and achieves throughput within 1.10× of a 4-server CAGNET cluster that costs ~40× more hardware. - The missing: The competitive comparison against distributed systems assumes a slow 10Gbps interconnect; the paper's own analytical projection shows that a modern 200Gbps InfiniBand HDR setup would make CAGNET 2.75× faster than GriNNder, meaning GriNNder's distributed parity claim is hardware-dependent. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML systems researchers and GNN practitioners working with graphs exceeding single-server memory, and for anyone building storage-offloading systems for irregular-access workloads.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GriNNder, a framework that enables full-graph GNN training on a single GPU by offloading activations and gradients to NVMe SSD storage through a coordinated cache-(re)gather-bypass mechanism, with a custom lightweight graph partitioner and a gradient-engine that eliminates redundant snapshot storage. - Why it's important: Full-graph GNN training preserves complete neighborhood information (unlike mini-batch sampling) and is the gold standard for algorithmic validation, but storing all intermediate activations and gradients for graphs with tens to hundreds of millions of nodes exhausts GPU and host memory, forcing researchers to either use costly multi-GPU clusters or compromise with sampling. - How it does it: Structured Storage Offloading (SSO) exploits the power-law distribution of cross-partition dependencies to cache only frequently-needed activations in host memory at partition granularity, regathers activation snapshots on-demand during backpropagation rather than pre-storing them (eliminating α-fold data amplification), and bypasses host memory for low-reuse data via GPUDirect Storage. - The good: GriNNder trains 3-layer GCN on the 100M-node Papers graph in 9.07 min/epoch on a single 24GB GPU where HongTu runs out of swap memory entirely, and achieves throughput within 1.10× of a 4-server CAGNET cluster that costs ~40× more hardware. - The missing: The competitive comparison against distributed systems assumes a slow 10Gbps interconnect; the paper's own analytical projection shows that a modern 200Gbps InfiniBand HDR setup would make CAGNET 2.75× faster than GriNNder, meaning GriNNder's distributed parity claim is hardware-dependent. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML systems researchers and GNN practitioners working with graphs exceeding single-server memory, and for anyone building storage-offloading systems for irregular-access workloads. Problem & Motivation: Full-graph GNN training processes all vertices and edges each iteration without sampling, which preserves full neighborhood information and avoids the accuracy variance of mini-batch approaches—making it the preferred training mode for algorithmic validation. However, storing all node activations and gradients across all L layers simultaneously requires memory scaling as O(|V| × |H| × L), easily reaching terabytes for graphs like the 100M-node Papers dataset. Existing single-server methods (e.g., HongTu) are limited to GPU + host DRAM capacity; distributed systems (CAGNET, Sancus) require multi-GPU clusters with high-speed interconnects that cost ~40× more per training run. Naïve application of storage offloading from LLM training fails because: (1) GNN activations exhibit irregular random-access patterns driven by graph topology rather than sequential layer dependencies; (2) existing frameworks (PyTorch autograd) store redundant "snapshot" activations that amplify storage I/O by a factor α (average expansion ratio, typically 8 for large graphs); (3) standard partitioners like MT-METIS consume up to 4.8× the graph's size in memory during partitioning itself, which exceeds available resources on the target single-server setting. Key Idea: The core insight is that cross-partition dependency patterns in real-world graphs follow a power-law distribution analogous to vertex degree distributions—a small number of partitions account for most dependencies of any given partition. Exploiting this skew, GriNNder caches only the hot partitions (covering the heavy tail of dependencies) in host memory at partition granularity rather than vertex granularity, enabling efficient sequential I/O instead of fine-grained random access. The second key idea is that activation snapshots required by backpropagation are a reorganization of gathered activations (GA0) which can be reconstructed on demand from the original cached activations during the backward pass, eliminating both snapshot storage and the associated α-fold I/O amplification. Methodology: GriNNder orchestrates a partition-by-partition training loop over three coordinated mechanisms. During the forward pass, for each partition p of layer l: (1) Cache loads layer l−1 activations from storage into host memory at partition granularity, with LRU eviction when host capacity is exhausted; (2) Gather fetches required source-vertex activations from the host cache into GPU; (3) the GPU executes the forward pass; (4) Bypass writes output activations directly to storage via GPUDirect Storage (GDS), skipping host memory to avoid cache pressure. During the backward pass, for each partition: (1) Cache provides layer activations from host; (2) Bypass loads gradients directly from storage; (3) Regather reconstructs the gathered-activation snapshot by replaying the gather operation from cached A0 rather than loading a pre-stored snapshot; (4) GPU computes gradients; (5) gradient scatter-accumulates to host memory as a write-back buffer. The host acts as a multi-layer cache with intra-layer reuse detection—partitions with high cross-layer reuse stay in host, those with low reuse bypass via GDS. Cache operates at partition granularity (typical partition size several GBs) to enable sequential I/O, avoiding the 16KiB page-level read amplification of vertex-level random access. The switching-aware partitioner minimizes memory footprint by storing only the CSR graph (SrcPtr, DstIdx) plus a single Dst's Partition array of O(2|V|+2|E|) total space, compared to METIS's O(2|V|+|E|+Σ(|E_i|+|V_i|)) requirement. It iteratively reassigns each vertex to the partition of its majority neighbors (label propagation), converging in 30–50 iterations. The balance penalty function Penalty(i,j) = |P_j|/(α_balance × |V|/p) with default α_balance=1.1 allows ~10% imbalance. I/O latency is hidden by overlapping PCIe transfers of the previous partition's activations with GPU computation on the current partition. Key Results: On a single-GPU workstation (AMD Ryzen9 7950X, 128GB DDR5, RTX A5000 24GB, 4TB PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD), GriNNder (GRD) versus key baselines on per-epoch training time for 3-layer GCN with hidden dim 256: Products (2.4M nodes): GRD=0.12 min vs HongTu=0.17, Betty=0.61, Ginex=9.00—GRD is 1.42× faster than HongTu and 30.98× faster than Betty. IGBM (10M nodes): GRD=0.93 min vs HongTu=6.46, Betty=28.71, Ginex=GPU OOM—6.97× faster than HongTu. Papers (100M nodes): GRD=9.07 min vs HongTu=Swap OOM, Betty=GPU OOM, Ginex=17.72—the only single-server method that completes without OOM.
