Against distributed baselines on Papers (4-server cluster, 10Gbps interconnect): GRD=9.07 vs CAGNET=10.01 (approximation with memory checkpointing added) and Sancus=GPU OOM—GriNNder achieves comparable throughput at 1/40 the hardware cost. Ablation (Table 3, IGBM, 3-layer GCN, reduced cache): HongTu=12.53 min/epoch, GRD-G (regathering only)=1.20, GRD-GC (regathering + caching)=1.41—demonstrating that regathering alone provides 6.84–12.34× improvement over HongTu across cache sizes. On synthetic Kronecker graphs (4.2M–33.6M nodes), GRD provides 1.41–12.50× speedup over HongTu, confirming scalability trends. Partitioning memory: MT-METIS requires 895 GB for Papers vs GRD's 36.72 GB (24.37× reduction). SSD write volume: HongTu writes 192.4 GB/epoch on IGBM vs GRD's 2.1 GB (91.6× reduction); on Papers, HongTu cannot run and GRD writes 647.62 GB/epoch. Multi-GPU (4× RTX A4090): scaling is approximately linear up to 4 GPUs on IGBM/Papers. GriNNder maintains accuracy identical to all exact baselines (only Sancus differs due to staleness). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The structured storage offloading framework for full-graph GNN training is genuinely novel—no prior system has leveraged storage as a memory tier for this specific problem. The gradient-engine activation regathering is a new mechanism that, unlike generic activation checkpointing (Chen et al., 2016) or HongTu's intermediate recomputation, eliminates snapshot storage by exploiting graph topology structure during backpropagation. The switching-aware partitioner is a new lightweight algorithm, though it applies established label-propagation ideas with a memory-minimization twist. The partition-wise cache management exploiting power-law cross-partition dependencies is new in the GNN storage context. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations in Table 3 isolate the contribution of regathering versus caching separately. Sensitivity analyses cover: effective cache size (Table 3), SSD bandwidth with PCIe Gen4/Gen5/RAID5 (Figure 13b), number of partitions (Appendix Q), number of layers and model architectures (Figure 12). Multiple models tested: GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE. Baselines are current and appropriate—Betty, Ginex, HongTu represent the state-of-single-server art, CAGNET and Sancus represent distributed full-graph training. SSD endurance is quantified (64.72TB total writes for 100 epochs on Papers = 0.23% of one SSD's endurance). Code is publicly released at https://github.com/AIS-SNU/GriNNder. Paper is peer-reviewed at MLSys 2026 (not a preprint). One concern: the distributed baseline comparison uses a 10Gbps interconnect that the authors themselves acknowledge is the communication bottleneck (80–98% of execution time), and the projected speedup at 200Gbps InfiniBand HDR gives CAGNET a 2.75× advantage—this is disclosed but buried in Section 9.1. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — GriNNder is a built, evaluated framework. - Practical: GriNNder runs on a commodity workstation (single RTX A5000 24GB GPU + 4TB NVMe SSD), hardware available for ~$3,300, well within academic lab budgets. - Efficient: Storage I/O is reduced by up to 91.6× versus HongTu on IGBM through regathering and caching; regather + recompute account for only 4.88% + 5.69% of backward pass time on IGBM, hidden by I/O overlap; the switching-aware partitioner adds only 0.07–0.39% overhead to total training time. - Effective: Evidence shows it solves the actual problem—completing full-graph training on the 100M-node Papers dataset where all competing single-server methods fail (OOM), with accuracy identical to exact baselines. - Scalable: Tested on graphs up to 100M nodes; synthetic Kronecker graphs up to 33.6M nodes confirm scaling trends; multi-GPU extension tested with 4 GPUs; the paper does not test graphs beyond 100M nodes or interconnects denser than PCIe 5.0, so billion-node performance remains a claim, not a measurement. GriNNder delivers a software artifact (PyTorch Geometric extension, PyPI-installable, open-source at the GitHub URL above). Strengths: - The regathering insight—that snapshot amplification is eliminable by treating the backward gather as a recomputation from cached original activations—is a genuine conceptual contribution that yields 8.5× I/O reduction with negligible compute cost (regather=4.88%, recompute=5.69% of backward time, both hidden by overlap). - The partition-wise caching design is well-matched to NVMe access patterns: operating at partition granularity (GBs) avoids the read amplification inherent to 16KiB page-level access for fine-grained vertex lookups, a non-obvious engineering insight backed by analysis. - The switching-aware partitioner achieves 7.10–24.37× memory reduction versus MT-METIS with competitive partition quality (close to METIS quality on Products/IGBM, slightly better partitioning efficiency than Spinner), using an algorithm that is both theoretically motivated and practically fast. - The system's non-invasive API (single inheritance + layer_forward method) enables zero-accuracy-change adoption for existing PyG code, a practical barrier reduction that is tested and confirmed. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: (1) host-vertex caching of hot vertices would further reduce I/O but requires vertex-level management that incurs fine-grained storage access and read amplification at the page level, making partition-level caching preferable for power-law graphs; (2) GriNNder is slower than distributed systems with modern high-bandwidth interconnects (200Gbps InfiniBand HDR projects CAGNET at 2.75× faster)—it is positioned as cost-effective, not universally fastest; (3) SSD writes on Papers are 647.62 GB/epoch, higher than on IGBM, because limited host memory relative to graph size forces more storage traffic; (4) multi-GPU overhead exists due to shared host memory and storage bandwidth. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The distributed baseline comparison, while disclosed in Section 9.1, is not front-and-center in the main results—Table 1 compares only against the 10Gbps-bottlenecked cluster, and the acknowledgment that modern InfiniBand (200Gbps) would yield 2.75× CAGNET advantage is tucked into the discussion. Readers not scrutinizing Section 9.1 may overestimate GriNNder's position relative to well-provisioned distributed systems. Additionally, the regathering mechanism requires host memory to cache A0 (original activations) for on-demand regathering; under extreme memory constraints where even A0 cannot fit in host (the "Papers with 0.25× cache" scenario in Table 3), the method degrades—this boundary condition is shown but not analyzed in depth. Finally, the evaluation uses only homogeneous graphs (node classification); GNNs for heterogeneous graphs, link prediction, or graph classification tasks may have different memory-access profiles that affect the power-law assumption. Bottom Line: GriNNder delivers a technically sound, deployable solution to a real and pressing problem—full-graph GNN training on hardware that academic labs actually own—and any researcher or engineer who wants to train on graphs beyond single-server GPU/DRAM limits without a cluster budget should read this paper.

Test-Time Speculation

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Test-Time Speculation (TTS), an online distillation algorithm that continuously adapts the speculative decoding draft model during inference by treating each verification round as a free knowledge-distillation training step. - Why it's important: State-of-the-art speculators (DFlash, EAGLE-3, PARD) see acceptance length collapse toward 1 within a few thousand output tokens on long-response tasks — EAGLE-3 reaches acceptance length 1.1 beyond 20K tokens on LiveCodeBench, yielding zero practical speedup — precisely for the agentic workflows and long-form reasoning tasks that dominate modern LLM deployments. - How it does it: The target model's verification step already computes its full token distribution over every draft token for free; TTS repurposes this as distillation supervision, taking one gradient step on a position-weighted KL-divergence loss from target to draft after every S speculation rounds, with the backward pass offloaded asynchronously to a CUDA side stream to avoid throughput regression. - The good: On Qwen3.5-122B with DFlash, TTS achieves up to 72% acceptance-length improvement and 1.54× mean throughput at update stride S=5, with the improvement compounding to 245% at the 20–30K token range on LiveCodeBench — directly where the baseline collapses. - The missing: All experiments are single-request; the paper does not evaluate how per-request draft adaptation interacts with multi-request batched serving, where a shared speculator cannot simultaneously be updated by concurrent requests. - Read it or not: Essential for ML systems engineers deploying speculative decoding for reasoning models or coding agents with long outputs; can be skipped by those running only short-context inference where existing speculators already work well.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Test-Time Speculation (TTS), an online distillation algorithm that continuously adapts the speculative decoding draft model during inference by treating each verification round as a free knowledge-distillation training step. - Why it's important: State-of-the-art speculators (DFlash, EAGLE-3, PARD) see acceptance length collapse toward 1 within a few thousand output tokens on long-response tasks — EAGLE-3 reaches acceptance length 1.1 beyond 20K tokens on LiveCodeBench, yielding zero practical speedup — precisely for the agentic workflows and long-form reasoning tasks that dominate modern LLM deployments. - How it does it: The target model's verification step already computes its full token distribution over every draft token for free; TTS repurposes this as distillation supervision, taking one gradient step on a position-weighted KL-divergence loss from target to draft after every S speculation rounds, with the backward pass offloaded asynchronously to a CUDA side stream to avoid throughput regression. - The good: On Qwen3.5-122B with DFlash, TTS achieves up to 72% acceptance-length improvement and 1.54× mean throughput at update stride S=5, with the improvement compounding to 245% at the 20–30K token range on LiveCodeBench — directly where the baseline collapses. - The missing: All experiments are single-request; the paper does not evaluate how per-request draft adaptation interacts with multi-request batched serving, where a shared speculator cannot simultaneously be updated by concurrent requests. - Read it or not: Essential for ML systems engineers deploying speculative decoding for reasoning models or coding agents with long outputs; can be skipped by those running only short-context inference where existing speculators already work well. Problem & Motivation Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by drafting multiple tokens with a small fast model and verifying them in one target-model forward pass. Its speedup scales with acceptance length — the average number of consecutive draft tokens accepted per round. Draft models (EAGLE-3, DFlash, PARD) are trained offline on short sequences (≤2K tokens from ShareGPT, UltraChat), but at inference time they must match the target on sequences reaching tens of thousands of tokens. As generation proceeds, the target model becomes increasingly confident (entropy decreasing with output length), while the draft model's cross-entropy on the target's top-1 token increases monotonically from 2.8 to 6.4 nats over 10K tokens (EAGLE-3 on Llama3.1-8B). The paper shows this train–inference distribution mismatch causes acceptance lengths to degrade consistently across four tasks (AIME, MATH-500, OlympiadBench, LiveCodeBench) and all three speculators, even with larger 122B target models. Because long-response tasks — agentic workflows, extended chain-of-thought reasoning, competitive programming — dominate real-world LLM deployment, this failure mode makes state-of-the-art speculative decoding effectively useless at the scale where it is most needed. Key Idea The verification step in standard speculative decoding already computes the target model's full conditional distribution p(x_{t+k}|x_{1:t+k-1}) over every draft token in a single forward pass — exactly the supervision signal needed for knowledge distillation from target to draft. TTS exploits this: after each round of draft-and-verify, before moving to the next round, it performs one gradient step on the draft using a KL-divergence loss between target and draft distributions over the just-evaluated canvas tokens. No additional target-model computation is required; the distillation signal is a byproduct of existing inference work. Methodology TTS augments the standard speculative decoding loop with an update step. (1) Draft q_t generates a canvas of C tokens. (2) Target p evaluates p(x_{t+k}|x_{1:t+k-1}) for all k in one forward pass. (3) Standard rejection sampling determines acceptance length τ. (4) Loss L_t(q) = KL̃(p||q) + λ·KL̃(q_t||q) is computed, where the first term is a position-weighted approximate KL-divergence (weights {w_k} decaying with position k, matching the draft's original training recipe) and the second is a regularization term that prevents the draft from drifting excessively from its current state. (5) One Adam gradient step updates q_t to q_{t+τ}. Three critical design decisions: (a) Exactly one gradient step per canvas — ablations show K>1 steps cause overfitting to the current canvas and reduce generalization to subsequent rounds; (b) Strided updates every S rounds — S=5 achieves the best throughput (1.83× wall-clock speedup on LiveCodeBench vs. DFlash) by amortizing the backward-pass cost while preserving 88% of the acceptance-length gain from S=1; (c) Asynchronous CUDA side-stream pipelining — the backward pass is dispatched to a dedicated CUDA stream overlapping with the next S-1 speculation rounds, with a synchronization barrier before the next canvas generation. Implementation is built on top of the public DFlash and EAGLE-3 codebases and integrated into SGLang, requiring nontrivial engineering to maintain KV cache consistency after in-flight speculator weight updates. Key Results TTS is evaluated on 8 benchmarks (AIME 2024/2025, MATH-500, OlympiadBench-Math, OlympiadBench-Physics, GPQA Diamond, TheoremQA, LiveCodeBench) eliciting up to 32K output tokens, across 5 models (Qwen3-4B/8B/32B, Qwen3.5-122B-A10B MoE, Llama3.1-8B) and 2 speculator architectures (DFlash, EAGLE-3). Hardware: Amazon EC2 G7e with 8×NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell (96GB GDDR7 each). Key numbers: vs. DFlash, TTS improves mean acceptance length by up to 72% (AIME 2025, Qwen3.5-122B: 2.9→4.9) and 41% on average. vs. EAGLE-3, up to 67% (AIME 2024, Llama3.1-8B: 1.2→2.0) and 34% on average. Throughput at S=5: 1.54× mean over DFlash on Qwen3.5-122B (best individual: MATH-500 at 49.7 tokens/sec vs. 29.7). The improvement scales with output length: on AIME 2024 (Qwen3-8B), the gain is 15% for 0–10K tokens, 78% for 10–20K, and 183% for 20–30K; on LiveCodeBench, 17% → 123% → 245%. Consistent with the diagnosis, gains are largest when training-inference context mismatch is most severe: EAGLE-3 on Llama3.1-8B (2K training context vs. 128K target context) sees 61% mean improvement, while EAGLE-3 on Qwen3-8B (both 40K context) sees ~20%. Output accuracy is unchanged by construction (TTS only adapts the draft; the target distribution determines final token selection). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The core novelty is identifying that the speculative decoding verification pass is structurally equivalent to on-policy distillation data generation and exploiting this for zero-cost online adaptation. This is genuinely non-obvious: prior online speculative decoding work (OSD, ATLAS) adapts the draft across requests using accumulated sequence buffers, not within a single generation, and assumes request streams decompose into separable domains. The asynchronous CUDA pipelining that makes TTS throughput-positive is an engineering contribution beyond the algorithmic idea. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover two key hyperparameters: optimization steps per round K (Section 6.5) and update stride S (Section 6.4), both with clear monotonic trends. Section 6.6 provides mechanistic validation by showing TTS restores cross-entropy divergence to near-zero. However, significant methodological gaps remain: no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported; each benchmark uses only 32 sequences (30 for AIME), too small to estimate variance reliably. No code repository is linked in the paper despite nontrivial systems engineering being a claimed contribution. Multi-seed results are not reported. Evaluation covers only math, science, and coding benchmarks; no long-form generation tasks with different statistical properties are included. The paper is an arXiv preprint (2605.09329v1, May 10, 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a working system integrated into SGLang, evaluated by deployment on real hardware. - Practical: Runs on commercially available multi-GPU instances (Amazon EC2 G7e), though 8×96GB Blackwell GPUs are high-end and not universally accessible; the algorithm itself has no fundamental hardware requirement beyond what speculative decoding already needs. - Efficient: At stride S=5 with asynchronous pipelining, TTS achieves 1.54× mean throughput over the DFlash baseline, meaning the backward-pass overhead is more than recovered by higher acceptance length; the break-even and net-positive throughput are both demonstrated empirically. - Effective: TTS demonstrably solves the stated problem — acceptance length increases with generation length rather than collapsing — and the mechanism (restored cross-entropy divergence) is verified diagnostically, not just as a black-box empirical result. - Scalable: Tested at model sizes up to 122B (MoE) and sequence lengths up to 32K tokens. No evaluation beyond 32K tokens. The per-request adaptation design does not directly address batched multi-request serving, where a shared speculator is updated by concurrent requests — a significant open scalability question for production deployment. The paper does not release a software artifact. Strengths - The zero-cost supervision insight is both simple and non-obvious: the key observation that verification already produces distillation samples required no new computation, only a new perspective on existing work, which is the hallmark of a genuine conceptual contribution. - Empirical coverage is broad for a systems paper: 5 model sizes (4B–122B, dense and MoE), 2 speculator architectures, 8 benchmarks, consistent results across all combinations — the phenomenon is robust, not cherry-picked. - Root-cause diagnosis (Sections 3 and 6.6) moves beyond "it works" to explain mechanistically why it works: the train-inference context mismatch is quantified (2.8→6.4 nats cross-entropy increase), and TTS is shown to directly restore it. - The throughput engineering (strided updates + asynchronous CUDA side-stream) is a real contribution that converts a theoretically appealing but practically backward-pass-bottlenecked method into one with demonstrated positive net throughput. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Section C.2 explicitly acknowledges two limitations: (1) the gradient update uses standard PyTorch kernels — custom fused kernels would reduce per-update latency and improve throughput at all strides; (2) the update stride S is static — an adaptive stride that decreases when acceptance length degrades and increases when it is stable would be more efficient. Both are deferred to future work. The authors also note they do not report accuracy metrics because output distribution is unchanged by construction. They do not acknowledge the small evaluation sample size, the absence of multi-request serving analysis, or the lack of code release. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Batch serving compatibility: TTS as described adapts per-request draft weights. In production, a single speculator serves many concurrent requests. The paper is silent on whether TTS can be used in this setting, whether adapted weights can be shared or must be per-request, and whether concurrent adaptations interfere. This is the most important practical gap. - Sample size: 32 sequences per benchmark is too small to report means without variance estimates. Results could be within noise for some benchmarks. - No ground-truth accuracy verification: AIME, MATH-500, and GPQA have known answers; verifying that TTS-generated outputs achieve identical accuracy to vanilla speculative decoding would empirically confirm the correctness guarantee rather than relying solely on the theoretical argument. - Task diversity: All 8 benchmarks are math, physics, or code — domains with heavy token repetition and structured reasoning patterns. Long-form creative or summarization tasks may have different draft degradation dynamics. - Code not released: SGLang integration and CUDA pipelining are non-trivial; without code, the systems contribution is not directly reproducible. Bottom Line TTS identifies a real and underappreciated failure mode of speculative decoding at long-context — the very regime where it is most economically valuable — and provides a mechanistically grounded fix with positive net throughput that systems engineers deploying reasoning or coding agents should evaluate immediately, with the caveat that production-scale batched serving compatibility remains an open problem the paper does not address.

Unleashing Scalable Context Parallelism for Foundation Models Pre-Training via FCP

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FCP (Flexible Context Parallelism), a context parallelism framework for foundation model pre-training that shards sequences into fixed-size blocks and assigns those blocks to GPUs via arbitrary peer-to-peer communication rather than a fixed ring topology, achieving simultaneous compute efficiency and workload balance. - Why it's important: Real-world pretraining corpora have highly skewed, lognormal sequence-length distributions (spanning 1K–512K tokens in production workloads), and existing ring-topology CP methods either over-shard short sequences — tanking GPU utilization — or segregate sequences by length — causing severe workload imbalance — but cannot do both well at once. - How it does it: FCP decouples sharding granularity from communication topology: a greedy LPT-variant scheduler bin-packs variable-cost blocks onto workers for load balance, and a congestion-free bipartite-matching solver orders block transfers so each GPU sends and receives at most one block per sub-stage, enabling full overlap of communication with computation. - The good: FCP achieves >90% attention MFU with <5% workload imbalance at 256 GPUs across three distinct sequence-length distributions, outperforming all four baselines (Ring Attention, ByteScale, WLB-LLM, MagiAttention) by 1.13×–2.21× in MFU — the ablation table shows a cumulative 2.6× MFU gain from baseline (0.29) to full system (0.75) on 128× GPU-X forward pass. - The missing: The paper reports only attention-module MFU, not end-to-end training throughput (tokens/sec) for the full model; whether the overhead of non-attention operations or optimizer steps erodes the headline gains is not quantified. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML systems engineers designing or evaluating context parallelism infrastructure for long-context foundation model training; skippable for those whose training workloads are dominated by uniform-length sequences.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FCP (Flexible Context Parallelism), a context parallelism framework for foundation model pre-training that shards sequences into fixed-size blocks and assigns those blocks to GPUs via arbitrary peer-to-peer communication rather than a fixed ring topology, achieving simultaneous compute efficiency and workload balance. - Why it's important: Real-world pretraining corpora have highly skewed, lognormal sequence-length distributions (spanning 1K–512K tokens in production workloads), and existing ring-topology CP methods either over-shard short sequences — tanking GPU utilization — or segregate sequences by length — causing severe workload imbalance — but cannot do both well at once. - How it does it: FCP decouples sharding granularity from communication topology: a greedy LPT-variant scheduler bin-packs variable-cost blocks onto workers for load balance, and a congestion-free bipartite-matching solver orders block transfers so each GPU sends and receives at most one block per sub-stage, enabling full overlap of communication with computation. - The good: FCP achieves >90% attention MFU with <5% workload imbalance at 256 GPUs across three distinct sequence-length distributions, outperforming all four baselines (Ring Attention, ByteScale, WLB-LLM, MagiAttention) by 1.13×–2.21× in MFU — the ablation table shows a cumulative 2.6× MFU gain from baseline (0.29) to full system (0.75) on 128× GPU-X forward pass. - The missing: The paper reports only attention-module MFU, not end-to-end training throughput (tokens/sec) for the full model; whether the overhead of non-attention operations or optimizer steps erodes the headline gains is not quantified. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML systems engineers designing or evaluating context parallelism infrastructure for long-context foundation model training; skippable for those whose training workloads are dominated by uniform-length sequences. Problem & Motivation Training foundation models on long-context sequences (up to 1M tokens) requires distributing self-attention computation across multiple GPUs via context parallelism (CP). In practice, pretraining datasets follow long-tailed, approximately lognormal sequence-length distributions — roughly 50% of sequences are short enough to fit on a single GPU without any CP, yet the tail reaches 512K tokens. This heterogeneity creates two conflicting demands: (1) compute efficiency requires each GPU to handle blocks large enough to saturate tensor-core arithmetic intensity (≥4K tokens on Hopper), so short sequences must not be over-sharded; (2) workload balance requires equalizing total computation across GPUs, but attention FLOPs scale as O(L²) while memory scales as O(L), so a naive equal-token assignment leaves GPUs processing long sequences dramatically overloaded. Existing designs sacrifice one for the other: ring attention fixes 2N shards per sequence regardless of length, causing severe MFU degradation for short sequences; length-grouped designs (ByteScale) partition by length but use separate rings per group, producing up to 70% workload imbalance for outlier sequences. Key Idea The central insight is that the ring topology — universally adopted by prior CP designs — is not a prerequisite for efficient communication; it is merely a convenient symmetry that limits the scheduling search space. By elevating the abstraction from sequence-level sharding to block-level sharding, FCP makes each fixed-size block (e.g., 4K tokens) an independent scheduling unit that can land on any GPU. This transforms the CP scheduling problem into a multi-dimensional bin-packing problem over block-sized units, solvable with a greedy LPT scheduler, while the resulting arbitrary data dependencies are managed by modeling inter-GPU communication as a bipartite graph and computing a minimum-stage congestion-free matching that guarantees full overlap of communication and computation. Methodology FCP has three main components operating on each training batch: (1) Block Distributor: Each input sequence is divided into fixed-size blocks (default 4K tokens). Blocks from short sequences that fall below block size are packed using the `varlen` API. A greedy variant of the Longest Processing Time (LPT) algorithm assigns blocks to workers, minimizing maximum computation load subject to a per-worker memory cap. Each block's compute cost accounts for both FLOPs and compute efficiency f(B), which depends on block size and GPU hardware. The scheduler runs in O(K log N) for K blocks and N GPUs — negligible latency. (2) Communication Planner: Given the block assignment, inter-GPU data dependencies are represented as an undirected bipartite graph (N send nodes × N receive nodes), where an edge i→j indicates a block must transfer from GPU i to GPU j. FCP proves that a single-stage congestion-free communication round is exactly a matching on this bipartite graph, and that the minimum number of congestion-free sub-stages equals the maximum degree Δ of the graph. The solver uses the Hopcroft–Karp algorithm to partition all edges into Δ disjoint matchings in O(|V|^{1/2} |E|) = O(N̂^{2.5}) time, where N̂ is the CP group size (typically hundreds). A bottom-up coalescer merges fine-grained sub-stages into coarser execution rounds without violating the congestion constraint, increasing block size at execution time to improve kernel efficiency while preserving scheduling flexibility. (3) Transparent Reshuffler: Since external frameworks (FSDP, TP, EP) manage sequence layout independently, the reshuffler performs on-the-fly layout permutation when entering and exiting the attention module. This all-to-all communication is bounded by total context length (linear in L) while computation is quadratic in L, guaranteeing it can always be overlapped. The reshuffling is interleaved with local attention computation via the block-level pipeline, incurring negligible overhead. The pipeline for each attention layer is: reshuffle → [pull remote block | compute local block]×Δ → push local blocks → reshuffle back. Zig-Zag ordering is applied to causal masks to balance intra-sequence compute and communication volume. Critical design decisions: (a) Fixed block size rather than variable: justified by hardware arithmetic-intensity requirements and the ability to reduce search space without loss of expressivity. (b) Arbitrary P2P communication rather than ring: justified by the proof that traffic volume is bounded and can always overlap with computation given sufficient block size. (c) Congestion-free matching rather than random ordering: critical to avoid network hotspots that degrade bandwidth below the overlap threshold. Key Results All experiments use a Llama-3-70B configuration (8 KV heads, 64 QO heads, head dimension 128), 4K block size, coalesce degree 16, evaluated on two anonymized GPU cluster types (GPU-X: compute/comm ratio 5920, GPU-Y: ratio 2500). Baselines: Ring Attention, ByteScale, WLB-LLM (oracle version with per-sequence/per-document oracle switching), MagiAttention. - Workload balance: FCP maintains <5% computation and communication imbalance across all GPU counts (16–256) and all three workload distributions (real-world lognormal, less long-tailed, bimodal). MagiAttention reaches 17% communication imbalance; ByteScale reaches 70% computation imbalance at 256 GPUs under the real-world distribution. - Attention MFU (compute efficiency, equal-length control): FCP achieves >90% normalized MFU at all GPU counts; Ring Attention degrades to ~20% at 256 GPUs; ByteScale degrades to ~30% at 256 GPUs. - Module-level MFU (real workload, weak scaling, 32K tokens/GPU): FCP consistently leads all baselines. At 256 GPUs on GPU-X, FCP forward MFU ≈ 0.65–0.70 while the best baseline (WLB-LLM or ByteScale) reaches ≈ 0.40–0.50 depending on distribution. On GPU-Y at up to 48 GPUs, FCP exceeds 70% of single-GPU FA4 MFU. - Ablation (128× GPU-X): Adding block-level pipelining (#1) raises forward MFU from 0.29 to 0.48 (+64%); congestion-free solver (#2) to 0.62 (+29%); coalescer (#3) to 0.69 (+10%); transparent reshuffler (#4) to 0.75 (+7%). Each component contributes meaningfully. - Block size sensitivity: 4K tokens is the sweet spot on GPU-X, achieving ~7% higher MFU than 2K or 8K. Below 2K, MFU collapses due to hardware underutilization; above 6K, scheduling granularity degrades balance. - Scalability: Near-linear scaling demonstrated to 256 GPUs; no testing beyond 256 GPUs is reported. Baselines are current and competitive. The WLB-LLM oracle baseline (which uses ground-truth performance data rather than an estimator) represents an unusually strong comparison. The paper was accepted to MLSys 2026, indicating peer review. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is the combination of: (a) block-granularity CP scheduling freed from ring-topology constraints, (b) the formulation of communication ordering as a bipartite matching problem with a constructive polynomial-time congestion-free solver, and (c) transparent integration via reshuffler. The individual components (LPT scheduling, bipartite matching, block-level pipelining, FlashAttention kernels) are known techniques; the novelty lies in their joint formulation as a complete CP system that provably achieves both objectives simultaneously. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation studies (Table 2) isolate each of the four components with cumulative add-one-at-a-time analysis on a fixed hardware configuration — this directly identifies which pieces drive the gain. Sensitivity analyses cover block size and per-GPU token count. Three workload distributions (real-world, lognormal synthetic, bimodal) test distributional generality. The paper evaluates both forward and backward passes. One significant reproducibility concern: the exact GPU hardware types are anonymized ("GPU-X" and "GPU-Y") due to confidentiality constraints, meaning the compute/communication ratios (5920 and 2500) cannot be independently verified. No public code repository is announced in the paper. The congestion-free solver's proof (Hall's theorem application) is included in Appendix A.2 and appears complete. No error bars or multiple-seed training runs are reported; performance measurements are likely deterministic (hardware utilization), so this is less concerning than for accuracy metrics. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS (FCP is a built framework evaluated by deployment on real clusters). - Practical: Runs on commercially available NVIDIA Hopper and Blackwell GPUs with standard InfiniBand or RoCE networking; excludes torus-topology accelerators (TPU v3). - Efficient: Implemented in ~4K lines of Python with minor modifications to FlashAttention3; communication overhead is bounded by O(L) (linear in context length) and fully overlapped with O(L²) computation for blocks above the hardware-efficiency threshold; only a small number of SMs (6 on GPU-X, 8 on GPU-Y) dedicated to communication. - Effective: Consistently achieves >90% attention MFU and <5% workload imbalance across all tested configurations; the ablation confirms each system component earns its complexity; however, only attention-module MFU is reported — full training throughput (tokens/sec, end-to-end) is not. - Scalable: Tested up to 256 GPUs with near-linear scaling demonstrated; the congestion-free solver runs in O(N̂^{2.5}) time and completes within seconds for groups of hundreds of workers; behavior beyond 256 GPUs is claimed but not measured. The paper delivers a software artifact (implemented system, ~4K lines Python) but no public release is announced. Strengths - The theoretical formulation of CP scheduling as bipartite matching with a constructive proof of the optimal number of congestion-free sub-stages is the strongest intellectual contribution — it converts a previously heuristic problem into one with a provably optimal solution at polynomial cost. - The LPT-based block distributor correctly handles the multi-dimensional nature of the bin-packing problem (memory AND compute), an important practical distinction from prior work that optimizes only computation. - Evaluation against an oracle version of WLB-LLM (with ground-truth performance data replacing the estimator) is an unusually honest comparison that rules out the possibility that FCP simply defeats a weak learned predictor. - The transparent reshuffler approach — overlapping layout permutation with local attention computation — solves a genuine deployment barrier: existing CP systems require intrusive framework modifications, and FCP's on-the-fly approach removes this obstacle. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) FCP requires arbitrary P2P communication and degrades on torus-based topologies (e.g., TPU v3); it is optimized for fat-tree or rail-optimized InfiniBand/RoCE networks. (2) The current implementation handles only causal and non-causal attention masks; irregular sparse patterns (e.g., block-sparse attention) are deferred to future work. (3) Block size selection is hardware-dependent, though the authors show it can be determined from cluster profile alone with only ~7% sensitivity across the 2K–6K range. These limitations are confronted directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Attention-only metrics: All performance numbers measure attention-module MFU or workload balance at the attention level. A full pretraining run involves FFN layers, normalization, embedding lookups, optimizer steps, and gradient communication (FSDP). The 1.13×–2.21× MFU improvement in attention does not translate directly to a proportional end-to-end training speedup; the actual wall-clock improvement for a complete training job is not reported. - Anonymized hardware: GPU-X and GPU-Y types are withheld; the compute/communication ratios (5920, 2500) are critical parameters for interpreting where FCP's overlap assumptions hold. Readers cannot reproduce or predict FCP's performance on their own hardware without profiling. - No training convergence validation: The paper does not verify that FCP produces numerically equivalent outputs to a reference attention implementation (or that small numerical differences from block reordering have no convergence impact). This is standard for CP papers but remains an implicit assumption. - Scaling ceiling not probed: The near-linear scaling claim is demonstrated only to 256 GPUs. At larger scales, the congestion-free solver complexity O(N̂^{2.5}) and the bipartite graph construction may become non-trivial; this is not analyzed. - No public code: Without a released codebase, independent replication requires reimplementing the scheduling algorithms and the FlashAttention3 integration from scratch. Bottom Line FCP solves a well-defined, practically important problem (CP under heterogeneous sequence lengths) with a theoretically grounded algorithm, strong empirical results on realistic workloads at meaningful scale, and an honest evaluation against strong baselines — practitioners building long-context pretraining infrastructure should read this paper, though they should independently verify end-to-end training throughput gains before committing to the integration.

Expressiveness Limits of Autoregressive Semantic ID Generation in Generative Recommendation

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper identifies fundamental expressiveness limitations in generative recommendation (GR) models arising from the decoding tree induced by autoregressive semantic ID (SID) generation, proves formal bounds on these limitations, and proposes Latte, a method that prepends a discrete latent token before each SID to convert the single decoding tree into a user-adaptive forest of trees. - Why it's important: SID-based GR models are increasingly deployed in production recommenders, but their autoregressive decoding imposes hidden structural constraints that prevent personalized preference reversals and non-transitive item similarity — degrading recommendation quality in ways invisible to standard training objectives. - How it does it: During training, a randomly sampled discrete latent token is prepended to each SID target sequence; at inference, the model generates this latent token first, creating multiple latent-conditioned generation paths with user-dependent effective tree distances that relax the structural correlation. - The good: Two formal theorems (rank-reversal bound via Cantelli's inequality; forced transitivity via the ultrametric property of tree distances) directly connect the tree structure to measurable expressiveness failures, and noise-baseline ablations confirm the gains are not from regularization. - The missing: All experiments use three medium-scale academic Amazon categories (up to 95K users, 26K items, SID depth m=3); the method is untested at industrial scale and against tokenization-level approaches that might address the root cause more directly. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers or engineers building SID-based generative recommenders — the theoretical framework in Section 3 formally characterizes a structural defect affecting the entire model class, and Latte is a low-cost drop-in mitigation.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper identifies fundamental expressiveness limitations in generative recommendation (GR) models arising from the decoding tree induced by autoregressive semantic ID (SID) generation, proves formal bounds on these limitations, and proposes Latte, a method that prepends a discrete latent token before each SID to convert the single decoding tree into a user-adaptive forest of trees. - Why it's important: SID-based GR models are increasingly deployed in production recommenders, but their autoregressive decoding imposes hidden structural constraints that prevent personalized preference reversals and non-transitive item similarity — degrading recommendation quality in ways invisible to standard training objectives. - How it does it: During training, a randomly sampled discrete latent token is prepended to each SID target sequence; at inference, the model generates this latent token first, creating multiple latent-conditioned generation paths with user-dependent effective tree distances that relax the structural correlation. - The good: Two formal theorems (rank-reversal bound via Cantelli's inequality; forced transitivity via the ultrametric property of tree distances) directly connect the tree structure to measurable expressiveness failures, and noise-baseline ablations confirm the gains are not from regularization. - The missing: All experiments use three medium-scale academic Amazon categories (up to 95K users, 26K items, SID depth m=3); the method is untested at industrial scale and against tokenization-level approaches that might address the root cause more directly. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers or engineers building SID-based generative recommenders — the theoretical framework in Section 3 formally characterizes a structural defect affecting the entire model class, and Latte is a low-cost drop-in mitigation. Problem & Motivation: Generative recommendation models score items by autoregressively generating sequences of discrete semantic ID tokens, with item probability P(i|u) = ∏_j P(c^(j)|c^(1:j-1), u). Because items sharing a long SID prefix share many conditional probability terms in this product, their predicted scores are necessarily correlated across users — items structurally close in the decoding tree receive similar generation probabilities regardless of user-specific preferences. This creates two concrete failure modes: (1) rank reversals — the scenario where Alice prefers item A over B while Bob prefers B over A — are suppressed for items with short tree distance, since their probabilities co-move; (2) intransitive item similarities cannot be represented, because the ultrametric property of tree distances forces the model to assign high similarity to any pair of items that are both similar to a third. Neither failure mode is a training artifact: they are structural consequences of how SID-based GR models compute probabilities. The problem is pervasive: on three Amazon datasets with PSID tokenization, 83–85% of test instances have at least one nearby item at tree distance 2. Key Idea: The structural coupling is caused by a fixed single decoding tree where every item pair has a constant tree distance. The key insight is that prepending a small discrete latent token to each SID before training creates a forest of |L| latent-conditioned decoding trees rather than one. At inference, different users can select different dominant latent tokens for the same pair of structurally close items (i, i'); when their dominant latent tokens differ, the effective tree distance becomes 2(m+1) — the maximum — breaking the universal correlation that suppresses rank reversals. The latent tokens thus introduce user-context-dependent tree distances without changing the underlying SID representation. Methodology: The paper proceeds in three phases. First, empirical characterization: 1,024 item pairs at each tree distance ∈ {2,4,6,8} are sampled, 512 users are drawn per pair, and Pearson correlation between P(i|u) and P(i'|u) across users is computed. This consistently shows ρ ≈ 1.0 at distance 2 and ρ > 0.8 at distance 4 across three datasets for TIGER. Second, theoretical analysis: Theorem 3.4 uses Cantelli's inequality to derive P(R(i,i')) ≤ 4σ²(1−ρ)/(μ²+2σ²(1−ρ)), showing rank reversal probability vanishes as ρ→1; Theorem 3.5 exploits the ultrametric inequality satisfied by tree distances (Lemma C.1) to prove that representing both i₁~i₂ and i₂~i₃ as high-similarity forces the model to assign high similarity to i₁~i₃ — precluding intransitive structure. Critical design decisions for Latte: (1) uniform random latent token sampling during training, motivated by the need for approximately uniform inference-time generation (validated in Table 10); (2) beam search aggregation at inference rather than exhaustive enumeration over all latent tokens; (3) small latent vocabulary M' ∈ {4,8}, as M'=1 or 2 causes accumulated latent-token prediction errors that outweigh the structural benefit. The model architecture is T5-style encoder-decoder (same as TIGER/PSID) with 4 stacked Transformer blocks and embedding dim 128; SID depth m=3, vocabulary M=256 per position. Key Results: On three Amazon Reviews 2023 datasets (Instruments: 57K users/25K items, Scientific: 51K users/26K items, Games: 95K users/26K items) with leave-one-out splitting: Latte achieves NDCG@10 of 0.0618/0.0470/0.0958 vs. PSID baseline of 0.0602/0.0445/0.0939, for per-dataset gains of +2.66%/+5.62%/+2.03% on NDCG@10, averaging 3.45% relative improvement across all metrics. All improvements are statistically significant (paired t-test, p < 0.05). Latte outperforms all 11 baselines including TIGER, ActionPiece, and LETTER. Latte generalizes across three tokenizers (OPQ: +1.68%; RQ-VAE: +2.02%; RQ-KMeans: +2.02% over PSID on Instruments NDCG@10). Noise ablation (Table 13) confirms Latte (0.0515 Games NDCG@10) outperforms Dropout (0.0499) and Swap (0.0462) augmentations, ruling out regularization as the explanation. Structural coupling (Kendall τ between tree distance and item similarity): Latte achieves −0.4451 vs. PSID −0.4611 on Scientific (less negative = less coupling). Inference overhead: 76s vs. 66s per epoch for PSID on a single A6000 GPU — a 15% increase. Multimodal extension on Million Playlist Dataset: dynamic modality ordering via latent tokens achieves NDCG@10 0.1353 vs. 0.1266 for the best fixed-order baseline. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW THEORETICAL RESULT + NEW METHOD. The decoding-tree formalism and formal proof that tree-induced correlations bound rank-reversal probability and force transitivity (Theorems 3.4/3.5) are genuinely novel; the Latte method is a simple but principled fix that follows directly from the analysis rather than being an ad hoc engineering trick. The framing of autoregressive recommendation decoding as tree traversal and the application of Cantelli's inequality and the ultrametric argument are not standard combinations in the recommender systems literature. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover latent vocabulary size (M' ∈ {0,1,2,4,8}), aggregation method (sum vs. max), tokenization strategy (OPQ/RQ-VAE/RQ-KMeans), and noise-based data augmentation alternatives. Statistical significance via paired t-tests is reported for all main results. Individual confidence intervals or standard deviations across seeds are not reported. Theoretical assumptions are explicitly stated: Assumption 3.3 (correlation monotonically decreases with tree distance) is empirically validated on TIGER and PSID; Assumption B.1 (equal variance across items) is a standard simplification. The uniform latent-token generation assumption used in Proposition C.2 is validated empirically only for the Games dataset (Table 10). Code is publicly released at https://github.com/hyp1231/Latte. Baselines reuse reported numbers from prior work [62,63] for most models, with PSID reimplemented from official code — a standard but not independently verified practice. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A6000 GPU; no multi-GPU or specialized hardware required. - Efficient: Adds one extra decoding step and M' additional token embeddings (embedding dim 128), increasing per-epoch inference time by ~15% (76s vs. 66s); memory overhead is negligible. - Effective: Gains are consistent across all 12 dataset/metric combinations, confirmed not to stem from noise regularization, and mechanistically validated via Kendall correlation reduction — the proposed mechanism operates as claimed. - Scalable: Tested only on datasets up to 95K users and 26K items with SID depth m=3; behavior at industrial scale (millions of users/items, deeper SID hierarchies, higher beam sizes) is neither tested nor analytically bounded. Software artifact: code released at https://github.com/hyp1231/Latte. Strengths: - The proofs of Theorems 3.4 and 3.5 are technically sound and non-trivial: exploiting Cantelli's inequality for the rank-reversal bound and the ultrametric property for forced transitivity are clean, reusable proof techniques that could apply to other sequence-decoding recommendation settings. - Empirical characterization (Figure 2) provides direct measurement of the assumed structural coupling across two model families on three datasets, making Assumption 3.3 credible rather than stipulated. - The noise ablation experiment (Table 13, comparing Dropout and Swap augmentations) directly addresses the most plausible alternative explanation for Latte's gains and rules it out quantitatively. - The multimodal latent-token-as-permutation-indicator extension (Section 6) demonstrates that the latent token framework is not a one-trick device but a general mechanism for injecting structured inductive biases into GR models. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state three explicit limitations (Appendix F): (1) modest additional inference cost from the extra latent token decoding step, relevant for latency-sensitive serving; (2) potential degeneration to the base model if latent token embeddings become near-identical during training; (3) incomplete removal of structural constraints — items sharing the same dominant latent-conditioned path still exhibit prefix coupling. They forthrightly describe Latte as a simple illustrative solution rather than an optimal one, and explicitly invite future work on stronger fixes. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The Kendall correlation improvement (e.g., −0.4451 vs. −0.4611 on Scientific) is statistically significant but numerically small, indicating substantial residual structural coupling that Latte does not resolve. The paper does not compare against tokenization-level interventions that could address the root cause — e.g., learning SIDs that explicitly break prefix coupling or using flat codebooks without hierarchical structure — which would be stronger baselines for the identified problem. All experiments use SID depth m=3; at greater depths (e.g., TIGER's m=4 which shows stronger coupling in Table 2), the structural constraints tighten and Latte's benefit could be different — but this is not tested. The theoretical analysis assumes SID token vocabularies and sequences are fixed; how results change under end-to-end jointly learned tokenization is not analyzed. Inference latency per query in a serving environment (not per training epoch) is not reported, making it difficult to assess production viability. Bottom Line: This is a well-executed paper that gives the generative recommendation community its first formal account of why SID-based autoregressive decoding structurally limits personalization, and delivers a simple, code-released mitigation that consistently improves a strong baseline — practitioners building GR pipelines should read Section 3 and benchmark Latte against their current base model.

Achieving Gold-Medal-Level Olympiad Reasoning via Simple and Unified Scaling

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SU-01, a 30B-A3B sparse MoE model trained via a four-stage post-training recipe — reverse-perplexity curriculum SFT, coarse RLVR, refined RL with generative proof rewards plus self-refinement and experience replay, and test-time verification-and-refinement scaling — that achieves gold-medal-level performance on IMO 2025, USAMO 2026, and IPhO 2024/2025. - Why it's important: Olympiad-level reasoning demands complete, auditable proofs rather than just correct final answers, and demonstrating that a compact ~30B active-parameter model can reach gold-medal thresholds with a simple, domain-unified recipe challenges the assumption that such capability requires frontier-scale compute or bespoke symbolic systems. - How it does it: The key mechanism is staged decomposition: SFT instills proof-search behavioral patterns via a reverse-perplexity curriculum; coarse RLVR scales search breadth under binary verifiable rewards; refined RL shifts optimization from answer correctness to proof quality using a generative reward model augmented with self-refinement prompts and experience replay; and TTS iterates the trained solve-verify-refine loop at inference to allocate additional compute to hard problems. - The good: With TTS, SU-01 scores 35 points on both IMO 2025 and USAMO 2026, meeting the IMO gold line and exceeding the USAMO gold line by 10 points, matching the highest reported human total among 340 USAMO competitors under conservative expert grading. - The missing: The generative proof reward model (DeepSeekMath-V2) is used to judge both mathematics and physics derivations but its calibration for IPhO-style physics proofs is not validated, leaving the quality of the refined RL training signal for the physics domain unexamined. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers working on post-training recipes for reasoning models — it is the most transparent public recipe achieving verified gold-medal olympiad performance from a compact model, with unusually strong external validation via official competition problems graded by gold-medal expert judges.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SU-01, a 30B-A3B sparse MoE model trained via a four-stage post-training recipe — reverse-perplexity curriculum SFT, coarse RLVR, refined RL with generative proof rewards plus self-refinement and experience replay, and test-time verification-and-refinement scaling — that achieves gold-medal-level performance on IMO 2025, USAMO 2026, and IPhO 2024/2025. - Why it's important: Olympiad-level reasoning demands complete, auditable proofs rather than just correct final answers, and demonstrating that a compact ~30B active-parameter model can reach gold-medal thresholds with a simple, domain-unified recipe challenges the assumption that such capability requires frontier-scale compute or bespoke symbolic systems. - How it does it: The key mechanism is staged decomposition: SFT instills proof-search behavioral patterns via a reverse-perplexity curriculum; coarse RLVR scales search breadth under binary verifiable rewards; refined RL shifts optimization from answer correctness to proof quality using a generative reward model augmented with self-refinement prompts and experience replay; and TTS iterates the trained solve-verify-refine loop at inference to allocate additional compute to hard problems. - The good: With TTS, SU-01 scores 35 points on both IMO 2025 and USAMO 2026, meeting the IMO gold line and exceeding the USAMO gold line by 10 points, matching the highest reported human total among 340 USAMO competitors under conservative expert grading. - The missing: The generative proof reward model (DeepSeekMath-V2) is used to judge both mathematics and physics derivations but its calibration for IPhO-style physics proofs is not validated, leaving the quality of the refined RL training signal for the physics domain unexamined. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers working on post-training recipes for reasoning models — it is the most transparent public recipe achieving verified gold-medal olympiad performance from a compact model, with unusually strong external validation via official competition problems graded by gold-medal expert judges. Problem & Motivation Mathematical and scientific olympiad problems stress-test long-horizon reasoning because solutions must be complete, logically rigorous, and survive strict grading — a correct final answer with a hidden gap or unjustified step earns no credit. Prior systems that reach gold-medal performance either rely on specialized symbolic or neuro-symbolic engines (AlphaGeometry, AlphaProof) or large-scale compute with massive search budgets (Gemini Deep Think). General-purpose reasoning models trained with RL have improved substantially on answer-verifiable benchmarks but their proof-quality remains weak on harder olympiad problems, as evidenced by large gaps between AnswerBench and IMO-ProofBench-Advanced scores even for strong post-trained models. The central question is whether a compact (~30B active parameter) model can be pushed to expert-level proof reasoning through a simple, domain-unified post-training recipe that does not require specialized formal verification infrastructure. Key Idea The core insight is that rigorous proof-seeking capability can be decomposed into three orthogonal improvements that must be layered sequentially: (1) proof-search behavior must first be instilled by SFT before RL can scale it (RL on a model without the right reasoning pattern produces answer-seeking, not proof-seeking); (2) coarse RLVR under binary verifiable rewards scales search coverage but cannot improve proof quality beyond answer correctness; (3) a generative reward model that scores full proof validity — not just final answers — is required to push toward complete proofs, and must be augmented with self-refinement (training the model to critique and repair its own drafts) and experience replay (preserving rare successful proofs long enough for learning) to overcome the sparsity of high-quality proof rollouts. A reverse-perplexity curriculum for SFT is a secondary but practically critical insight: ordering training examples by descending PPL under the current policy ensures the model encounters its most mismatched (hardest-to-imitate) trajectories first, enabling it to absorb the new reasoning pattern faster while reducing truncation rates from 7–8% (random order) to near 0%. Methodology Stage 1 (SFT): Starting from P1-30B-A3B, the model is fine-tuned on 338K trajectories (≤8,192 tokens) covering math (71.8K), STEM (62.9K), code (30.2K), and instruction-following (18.8K) for direct generation, plus 89.5K self-verify and 65.2K self-refine traces for self-improvement behavior. Trajectories are generated by DeepSeek-V3.2-Speciale and filtered for quality. Training runs for 4 epochs with batch size 128 and Adam lr=1×10⁻⁵ on 8 GPUs, with rollout shuffling disabled to preserve the reverse-perplexity ordering within each epoch. A truncation rate below 5% is used as an operational indicator of SFT convergence. Stage 2 (Coarse RL): Trains on 8,967 verifiable prompts using GSPO (Group Sequence Policy Optimization) with binary outcome rewards. Reward verification uses a layered pipeline: canonicalized text matching → Math-Verify rule-based checks → gpt-oss-120b generative verification for residual cases. The unnormalized group-relative advantage (no standard-deviation normalization) is used: Â_i = r(q,o_i) − μ_G_q. The MoE router is frozen during replay to prevent replay-induced instability. Training runs 96 steps with 8 rollouts per prompt, batch size 128, 160K maximum response length. Stage 3 (Refined RL): Shifts the reward from answer correctness to proof quality using DeepSeekMath-V2 as a binary generative proof reward that scores the full reasoning path for mathematical validity, rigor, and completeness. Two memory mechanisms are added: (a) self-refinement converts failed rollout groups (group average reward < τ_ref=0.5) into critique-and-repair prompts at target ratio η_ref=0.20, stored in a refinement buffer and mixed with fresh prompts; (b) experience replay (ExGRPO-inspired) admits queries to a replay buffer only when 0 < n+(q) < 2 (hard but occasionally solvable), stores the lowest-entropy successful trajectory as the replay candidate, and retires queries once n+(q) ≥ 4. Replay injects at ratio ρ=0.25 over the non-refinement batch. The combined objective (Eq. 2) mixes fresh GSPO updates with replayed trajectory updates. Anti-hack preprocessing replaces malformed model outputs before reward model scoring. Refined RL runs 104 steps after 96 coarse steps. Stage 4 (TTS): At inference, the model runs an iterative solve-verify-refine loop following Huang & Yang (2025). Each run allows up to MAX_EXPLORATION_ROUNDS=30 solve-verify-refine cycles. A candidate is accepted after MAX_VERIFICATION_TRUE_ROUNDS=5 consecutive passed verifications or terminated after MAX_VERIFICATION_FALSE_ROUNDS=10 consecutive failures. Up to MAX_RUNS=10 independent runs are launched per problem; accepted candidates from stable runs are selected. Generation uses temperature 1.0, top-p 0.95, maximum 160K tokens per generation, served via SGLang. Key Results On answer-verifiable benchmarks, SU-01 reaches 77.5% on AnswerBench (behind Nemotron-Cascade-2's 80.5%), 59.8% on AMO-Bench (best similar-size, ahead of Qwen3.6-35B-A3B's 58.8%), and 94.6%/93.3% on AIME 2025/2026 (best similar-size). FrontierScience-Olympiad overall: 61.5%. On non-verifiable proof benchmarks, SU-01 direct generation reaches 57.6% on IMO-ProofBench Overall (best similar-size; Gemini 3.1 Pro Thinking is 72.6%, GPT-5.5-High is 80.7%); TTS lifts this to 70.2% (91.0% Basic, 49.5% Advanced). On IPhO 2024/2025, SU-01 scores 23.5/25.3 (direct/TTS), exceeding the gold lines of 20.8/19.7 — best similar-size model. On official IMO 2025 with TTS, SU-01 scores 35 points (gold line = 35), solving P2, P4, P5 fully. On USAMO 2026 with TTS, SU-01 scores 35 points (gold line = 25), matching the highest reported human total among 340 competitors. On FrontierScience-Research (out-of-domain chemistry, biology), SU-01 reaches 11.7% overall (best similar-size), with 10% on Chemistry and Biology despite no RL training signal from those domains. Stage ablation (Figure 4) shows: SFT lowers AnswerBench (69.2→59.5) while raising ProofBench-Advanced (6.2→14.8); Coarse RL recovers AnswerBench (76.7) and raises ProofBench-Advanced (25.2); Refined RL mainly improves ProofBench-Advanced (38.1); TTS raises ProofBench-Advanced further (49.5). Baselines are current strong systems including Nemotron-Cascade-2, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B, and frontier-size GPT-5.5-High and Gemini 3.1 Pro Thinking. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The combination of reverse-perplexity SFT curriculum, two-level RL (coarse verifiable → refined generative proof reward), and integrated self-refinement + experience replay for proof-quality specialization is novel as a unified recipe. Individual components — GSPO, RLVR, generative reward models (DeepSeekMath-V2), ExGRPO-style replay, TTS verification loops — are existing ideas from prior work; the contribution is their principled composition and the empirical demonstration that this recipe, applied to a 30B-A3B backbone with 340K SFT trajectories and 200 RL steps, is sufficient for gold-medal olympiad reasoning without domain-specific symbolic systems. Rigor & Reproducibility Stage-by-stage ablations are presented (Figure 4) showing each component's contribution to both answer-verifiable (AnswerBench) and proof-oriented (ProofBench-Advanced) metrics. The reverse-perplexity curriculum is ablated against random and ascending-PPL orderings (§6.3), with large effects: descending-PPL reaches 55.8 AnswerBench and 40.0 AMO-Bench vs. 39.5/31.0 for random, and near-zero truncation vs. 7–8% for random. Evaluation runs are repeated 4–8 times depending on benchmark. IMO and USAMO competition results are graded by three independent gold-medal experts with worst score taken as conservative final. Code and model weights are publicly released (project page and GitHub links on first page). Contamination is addressed by decontaminating the RL prompt pool. Concerns: (1) The generative reward model (DeepSeekMath-V2) is used to score physics proofs, but its calibration for IPhO-style physics derivations is not validated — a poorly calibrated physics reward corrupts refined RL training for that domain. (2) TTS results for IMO/USAMO are graded by human experts while direct-generation results use automatic grading, introducing evaluation protocol inconsistency that makes it impossible to cleanly isolate TTS compute gain. (3) Experience replay threshold (n+(q) ≥ 4 retirement, ρ=0.25 ratio) and self-refinement ratio (η_ref=0.20) are not ablated. (4) This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: SFT trains on 8 GPUs and RL on 64 GPUs using the SLiMe/THUDM framework; inference is served with SGLang. Accessible to well-resourced academic labs but not individual researchers. - Efficient: Training cost is substantially lower than comparably performing systems: Nemotron-Cascade-2 uses ~26M SFT samples and multi-stage cascade RL; DeepSeek-V3.2 requires 943.7B token continued pretraining plus specialized distillation and mixed RL. SU-01 uses 338K SFT trajectories and 200 RL steps. TTS inference cost is high — median initial solution 106K tokens, refinement 83K tokens — making per-problem cost significant at competition time. - Effective: Strong evidence across multiple proof-oriented benchmarks including official olympiad problems graded by domain experts, not only automated metrics. - Scalable: The recipe is tested exclusively on the 30B-A3B backbone; no experiments with different model sizes, dense architectures, or weaker/stronger backbones are reported. Scaling behavior is claimed but not measured. The paper delivers model weights, training code (SLiMe/slime framework), and 57 pages of model-generated competition solutions as public artifacts. Strengths - The reverse-perplexity curriculum is a concretely validated training insight: it reduces truncation from 7–8% to ~0% and recovers substantially more post-trained backbone capability than random or easy-first orderings, with a clear mechanistic explanation (the model must first absorb unfamiliar proof patterns before consolidating on familiar ones). - The staged decomposition of proof capability is empirically clean: ProofBench-Advanced tracks independently of AnswerBench across stages, providing evidence that answer-seeking and proof-seeking are genuinely different optimization targets that require different training signals. - IMO/USAMO competition results under conservative triple-expert grading (worst score reported) are among the most externally credible evaluations in the olympiad reasoning literature, substantially more rigorous than benchmark-only comparisons. - Cross-domain transfer to FrontierScience-Research chemistry and biology (10% on each, best similar-size despite zero RL signal from those domains) provides genuine evidence for scientific reasoning generalization beyond the training distribution. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state that the model performs better on problems admitting rigid formal representations (number theory, coordinate geometry, analytic reformulations) and is less reliable when the core challenge involves preserving combinatorial structure or proving finely tuned process invariants. They cite concrete failures: IMO 2025 P6 (invalid column-permutation reduction in a combinatorics argument) and USAMO 2026 P2 (gaps in a delicate global strategy argument). They also acknowledge that the generative reward model is "more expensive and more vulnerable to judge artifacts" and describe anti-hack preprocessing to mitigate reward hacking. They do not directly confront the evaluation protocol inconsistency between TTS (human grading) and direct generation (automatic) for competition results. Analyst-Identified Gaps - DeepSeekMath-V2 is a mathematical proof reward model trained on math data; its ability to reliably score IPhO physics derivations (dimensional analysis, physical modeling, multi-step derivations) is never validated. A miscalibrated reward corrupts refined RL for physics in ways that answer-based benchmarks may not detect. - No ablation of the experience replay retirement threshold (n+(q) ≥ 4), replay ratio (ρ=0.25), or refinement ratio (η_ref=0.20); sensitivity to these hyperparameters is unknown. - The same recipe is applied to one backbone (P1-30B-A3B); there is no evidence it transfers to other model families, dense architectures, or different parameter scales — the "simple and unified" framing implies generality that is not demonstrated. - Biology scores on FrontierScience-Research remain at 25% (direct generation), and the paper does not analyze why chemistry (69.4%) benefits far more from transfer than biology (25%), despite both being outside the RL training domains. - Coarse RL evaluation protocol uses gpt-oss-120b as a fallback verifier for cases failing rule-based checks — this introduces a closed-source model as a potential confounder in the training reward, and its error rate is not reported. Bottom Line Researchers working on post-training recipes for reasoning models should read this — it is the clearest public recipe for achieving verified gold-medal olympiad performance from a compact model, with unusually rigorous external validation, and the reverse-perplexity curriculum and staged RL decomposition are immediately applicable insights.

Efficient Training on Multiple Consumer GPUs with RoundPipe

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RoundPipe, a pipeline parallelism schedule that breaks the "weight binding" constraint in consumer GPU LLM fine-tuning by treating GPUs as a stateless worker pool and dispatching computation stages in round-robin order, achieving near-zero-bubble pipeline throughput. - Why it's important: Fine-tuning on 8×RTX 4090 consumer GPUs costs ~80% less than equivalent A100 datacenter hardware, but existing pipeline schedules waste up to 30% of GPU time on bubbles caused by LM-head load imbalance, severely limiting throughput and maximum trainable model size. - How it does it: CPU offloading already transfers weights to host DRAM on demand, so RoundPipe exploits this to decouple stages from GPUs — any GPU can receive any stage's weights and execute it — enabling asymmetric forward/backward stage splitting that equalizes per-stage execution time and eliminating both structural and imbalance bubbles simultaneously. - The good: 1.48–2.16× throughput improvement over Mobius (the strongest consumer-GPU baseline) on 8×RTX 4090 across 1.7B–32B models, and is the only system capable of LoRA fine-tuning a 235B MoE model on 24 GB GPUs with 31K sequence length. - The missing: The system is inherently single-server (all stages share one host memory pool); multi-node scaling is unaddressed and would reintroduce the network bottlenecks the design was built to avoid. - Read it or not: Systems researchers and practitioners building LLM fine-tuning infrastructure on consumer GPU servers should read this; the core insight is clean and the open-source library is immediately usable.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RoundPipe, a pipeline parallelism schedule that breaks the "weight binding" constraint in consumer GPU LLM fine-tuning by treating GPUs as a stateless worker pool and dispatching computation stages in round-robin order, achieving near-zero-bubble pipeline throughput. - Why it's important: Fine-tuning on 8×RTX 4090 consumer GPUs costs ~80% less than equivalent A100 datacenter hardware, but existing pipeline schedules waste up to 30% of GPU time on bubbles caused by LM-head load imbalance, severely limiting throughput and maximum trainable model size. - How it does it: CPU offloading already transfers weights to host DRAM on demand, so RoundPipe exploits this to decouple stages from GPUs — any GPU can receive any stage's weights and execute it — enabling asymmetric forward/backward stage splitting that equalizes per-stage execution time and eliminating both structural and imbalance bubbles simultaneously. - The good: 1.48–2.16× throughput improvement over Mobius (the strongest consumer-GPU baseline) on 8×RTX 4090 across 1.7B–32B models, and is the only system capable of LoRA fine-tuning a 235B MoE model on 24 GB GPUs with 31K sequence length. - The missing: The system is inherently single-server (all stages share one host memory pool); multi-node scaling is unaddressed and would reintroduce the network bottlenecks the design was built to avoid. - Read it or not: Systems researchers and practitioners building LLM fine-tuning infrastructure on consumer GPU servers should read this; the core insight is clean and the open-source library is immediately usable. Problem & Motivation Consumer GPU servers (8×RTX 4090, 24 GB VRAM each, PCIe 4.0 at 32 GB/s) are ~80% cheaper than A100/H100 datacenter hardware but impose two hard constraints. First, VRAM is insufficient for training models beyond a few billion parameters without CPU offloading — an 8B model requires 128 GB for model states alone. Second, PCIe interconnect offers less than 20% of NVLink bandwidth, making collective communications for data parallelism prohibitively expensive; prior work shows DeepSpeed spends ~70% of training time on communication on PCIe servers. The state of the art for this setting, Mobius, uses pipeline parallelism with CPU offloading to replace collectives with P2P activation transfers, but inherits the "weight binding" problem: stages are fixed to GPUs, so any load imbalance (e.g., the LM head is disproportionately large) produces either structural bubbles (coarse partitioning) or imbalance bubbles (fine-grained partitioning). In practice, bubble ratios for models like Llama-3.1-8B on looped schedules reach 30%, significantly degrading effective throughput. Key Idea The core insight is that CPU offloading, already required for consumer GPU training, implicitly removes the necessity to bind a pipeline stage to a fixed physical GPU: since model weights reside in host memory and are transferred to a GPU on demand, those same weights can be redirected to a different GPU at no extra cost. This enables treating all GPUs as interchangeable stateless workers and dispatching stage computations in round-robin order, which in turn allows asymmetric stage splitting — separate layer partitions for forward and backward passes sized to equalize per-stage wall-clock time — simultaneously eliminating both structural and imbalance bubbles without the memory overhead of zero-bubble or asynchronous schedules. Methodology RoundPipe has three algorithmic layers. The Computation Dispatch Paradigm stores all model weights, gradients, optimizer states, and stage-boundary activations in CPU host DRAM; GPU workers are stateless and execute whichever stage is dispatched to them. The RoundPipe Schedule divides M micro-batches into rounds of M_R ≥ N micro-batches; within each round, S_f forward stages followed by S_b backward stages form a linear dispatch sequence where stage slot i goes to GPU (g0+i) mod N, and the starting GPU g0 rotates between rounds to maintain continuous flow. Asymmetric partitioning uses 3 transformer layers per forward stage and 1 layer per backward stage (since backward with recomputation costs ~3× forward), with the first backward stage fusing forward and backward to avoid redundant recomputation. Asynchronous staleness-1 optimizer updates (via a separate optimizer worker) eliminate warm-up and cool-down bubbles at iteration boundaries. Three critical implementation challenges are addressed: (1) a priority-aware multi-stream transfer engine using four CUDA streams per device — activation transfers (critical path) proceed first, while parameter/gradient transfers are chunked via longest-processing-time-first scheduling and packed into idle windows between activation slots, preventing head-of-line blocking; (2) a fine-grained per-layer event-based consistency protocol that replaces a global FP32→FP16 weight-copy barrier with per-layer threading events, allowing the optimizer worker to release early layers before deeper layers finish synchronizing; and (3) an O(L³) automatic stage partitioning algorithm that enumerates O(L²) candidate t_max values from contiguous layer-time sums, then for each runs a greedy O(L) feasibility check against memory and time constraints. Key Results This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.27085v1, April 2026). On 8×RTX 4090 (consumer hardware): RoundPipe achieves 1.48–2.16× throughput improvement over Mobius and 4.7–7.3× longer maximum sequence length vs. the next-best non-TP baseline; RoundPipe-sync (no async optimizer) achieves 1.15–1.63× over Mobius. RoundPipe is the only system that can LoRA fine-tune Qwen3-235B (235B-A22B MoE) on 24 GB GPUs with 31K sequence length — all baselines OOM. Near-linear throughput scaling is demonstrated from 1–8 GPUs. Maximum sequence length is invariant to GPU count (e.g., 49K tokens for LLaMA-3.1-8B regardless of 1 or 8 GPUs). On 8×A800 SXM (datacenter NVLink hardware): RoundPipe delivers 0.98–1.47× vs. SOTA datacenter baselines, and the 4090 RoundPipe achieves at least 76% of A800 SOTA throughput across all models. Ablation via pipeline simulator: RoundPipe-sync reduces bubble ratio 23–55% relative to the best looped baseline; full async RoundPipe drives absolute bubble ratio below 4.5%. Fine-grained consistency protocol saves 2.6–14 seconds per iteration vs. blocking copy, scaling with trainable parameter count. Baselines are current and representative: DeepSpeed ZeRO-2, FSDP, ZeRO-Infinity, Megatron-PP, Megatron-TP, and Mobius on both hardware configurations. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The computation dispatch paradigm — using CPU offloading as the enabling mechanism to decouple pipeline stages from GPUs — is a genuine non-obvious insight that recasts a hardware constraint as a scheduling opportunity. The asymmetric forward/backward stage splitting is a concrete method contribution not present in prior pipeline schedules. The open-source Python library is a real system artifact. Individual components (CPU offloading, round-robin scheduling, async optimizer, event-based synchronization) are individually known; the novelty is in their co-design specifically for the consumer GPU / PCIe-bottlenecked setting.
