Prior benchmarks (Claw-Eval, ClawArena, SkillsBench) are human-authored, requiring hundreds of person-hours, and freeze into static artifacts vulnerable to benchmark contamination as agents absorb them through training. Training pipelines (OpenClaw-RL, MetaClaw) are similarly bounded by whatever tasks users happen to perform. The gap is that neither evaluation nor training can scale alongside agent capabilities when environment construction is a manual bottleneck. Key Idea: The core insight is that modern agent environments have an asymmetry: they are semantically infinite from the agent's perspective (unbounded NL context, tool outputs, conversation history) but implementationally finite (a small set of in-memory mock service databases). Exploiting this asymmetry, the paper proposes a declarative formalism E = (P, M, C) — task spec, interaction interface, evaluation functional — that separates what to verify from how to verify it. An LLM can generate a valid (P, M, C) triple without reasoning about state transitions; the deterministic Validator enforces correctness structurally. This makes automated generation tractable where prior approaches required per-task grader logic. Methodology: The pipeline has three modules. The Parser converts a natural language request into structured "intent atoms" (actions, objects, constraints) via a single LLM call. The Generator has three sub-workflows: task generation (LLM writes task.yaml with tools, fixtures, scoring components), service generation (designs new FastAPI mock servers when needed), and fixture generation (mounts files into containers). The Validator runs 12 structural checks (required fields, weight sums, check type validity, LLM judge weight cap at 55%, safety/scoring contradiction detection) and semantic coverage checks (every intent atom must appear in P, M, or C). Execution uses Docker containers with `--network none`, mock services injecting 25% random HTTP 429/500 errors to test robustness, and three harness integration tiers (native plugin, MCP server, SKILL.md+curl). The GradingEngine applies 15 check types (audit-based, output-based, file-based, plus LLM judge) weighted by a safety gate that zeroes the entire score on any forbidden action. Critical design decisions: (1) capping LLM judge weight at 55% to keep the majority of every score deterministic — well-justified to reduce grading variance; (2) deduplication via action rotation and passing last 10 task names to the LLM — reasonable but heuristic; (3) using server-side audit logs rather than agent-reported actions to prevent credit for described-but-not-performed actions — well-justified. Key Results: On quality: Auto-ClawEval-Mini achieves Coherence 0.59 vs. 0.51 for Claw-Eval and Clarity 3.54 vs. 3.38, with 100% validity, generated in 1.8 hours vs. 208 person-hours. On agent performance across 1,040 environments: completion scores range from 34% (MiniMax M2.5) to 76% (MiniMax M2.7 — oddly, the smaller model variant scores worse), with no model saturating the benchmark. GPT-5.4 achieves the highest mean score (58.8). Safety (≥83%) and robustness (100% across all models) are near-perfect, making completion the primary discriminating axis. Across 8 harnesses, all structured harnesses outperform the ReAct Agent Loop baseline (53.3%), with NemoClaw reaching 69.0 (+15.7 pp). Counter-intuitively, Tier 3 SKILL.md harnesses (NemoClaw 69.0, Hermes 66.9) outperform several Tier 2 MCP harnesses (ZeroClaw 57.1, PicoClaw 53.2), suggesting harness integration tier does not determine performance. Auto-ClawEval and Mini scores differ by <2% across all models and harnesses. The system produces 1,040 environments for $80 in API costs (claude-sonnet-4.6). A false-negative audit of 52 high-effort low-score trajectories finds 0 genuine grading errors. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The declarative (P, M, C) environment formalism and the three-module generation pipeline are genuinely novel for the claw-like agent domain; the broader idea of automated benchmark generation via LLM (c.f. SWE-smith, RandomWorld, Agent World Model, Endless Terminal) is an active area, and this paper's specific contribution is the application to multi-harness API-calling agents with structured verification. The cross-harness evaluation design revealing that harness engineering matters as much as model choice is an empirical finding with practical significance. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations on individual pipeline components (Parser, Validator, Generator sub-workflows) are absent — it is not possible to determine how much each module contributes to final quality. No error bars or significance tests are reported on coherence/clarity comparisons (small N for quality study: 104 tasks). The Pass³ aggregation (requiring success in 3 independent runs) is used for methodology description but not clearly applied as the primary reported metric in Tables 3–4. A notable methodological concern: the coherence metric is assessed by an LLM judge that can directly read ClawEnvKit's explicit P↔M↔C YAML structure but cannot inspect Claw-Eval's rubrics embedded in task-specific Python graders — the authors acknowledge this explicitly, which partially deflates the coherence advantage claim. Code and benchmark are publicly released at https://github.com/xirui-li/ClawEnvKit. This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. All experiments ran on a single Apple M-series Mac with Docker Desktop. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built tool and infrastructure evaluated by deployment. - Practical: Runs on a single Apple M-series Mac with Docker Desktop; no GPU required; estimated $20–50 per 1,040-task run using Haiku. - Efficient: Generates 1,040 environments in 18 hours at $80 API cost vs. 208 person-hours for 104 human-curated tasks; evaluation cost scales linearly with task count and model API pricing. - Effective: The evidence that generated environments are evaluation-grade is moderately strong: 0% false negatives in audited cases and comparable agent score distributions to Claw-Eval; but the coherence comparison is partially confounded by judge visibility into task format. - Scalable: Tested at 1,040 environments across 8 harnesses and 5 model families; the authors claim unlimited scalability but do not demonstrate what happens at 10,000+ environments or whether task diversity degrades at scale due to the 24-category constraint anchored to Claw-Eval's service library. The work delivers a software tool (ClawEnvKit) and a benchmark dataset (Auto-ClawEval), both released publicly. Strengths: - The declarative (P, M, C) formalism is well-motivated and cleanly separates environment specification from verification, enabling LLM generation without explicit state-space reasoning — a genuine architectural insight. - The server-side audit log grading design (rather than agent-reported actions) closes a subtle but important loophole in agent evaluation where models receive credit for hallucinated tool calls. - The LLM judge weight cap at 55% and the Pass³ aggregation are thoughtful engineering choices that reduce non-determinism in grading without eliminating qualitative assessment. - The cross-harness evaluation (8 frameworks, 4 model families) is more comprehensive than any prior benchmark in this space and yields the non-obvious finding that harness tier does not strictly determine performance. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront three limitations: (1) mock services cannot replicate production API messiness (auth flows, schema drift, subscription-tier rate limits); (2) coverage is limited to 24 categories lacking GUI automation, voice, multi-agent delegation, and domain-specific workflows; (3) tasks are capped at 20 tool-calling rounds, not designed for long-horizon multi-session workflows. The authors also acknowledge the coherence metric advantage is partly a measurement artifact of judge visibility into structured format. This directness is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The entire "claw-like agent" ecosystem (OpenClaw, NanoClaw, IronClaw, etc.) uses pseudonymous names for what appear to be real systems (OpenClaw maps to open-source Claude Code-style CLI agents; the paper explicitly includes Claude Code and Codex as Tier 2 MCP harnesses alongside these fictional names). This opacity makes it impossible to independently verify the harness results or replicate experiments without access to the named systems. - All 1,040 Auto-ClawEval environments are seeded from the same 104 Claw-Eval scenarios (10 variants per scenario), meaning task diversity is anchored to human-curated scenarios — the system is a diversity-amplifier, not a truly unconstrained generator. The benchmark's coverage is bounded by Claw-Eval's original 24 categories and 15 services. - No ablation shows what fraction of generated tasks fail validation and require regeneration, which would reveal how often the Generator produces invalid environments and the true cost of validation. - The MiniMax M2.7 (more capable) vs. M2.5 result is anomalous: M2.7 scores 49.4 on Auto-ClawEval but M2.5 scores 43.6, yet on Auto-ClawEval-Mini, M2.7 scores only 44.9 vs. M2.5's 51.4 — a rank reversal with no discussion. - Diversity within generated task categories is not quantified; 10 variants per scenario might produce semantically redundant tasks that over-represent narrow sub-patterns. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating CLI-style LLM agent harnesses gains concrete infrastructure and a ready-to-use benchmark from this work, but should weight the coherence/clarity claims conservatively given the measurement confound, and should note that the pseudonymous ecosystem naming complicates independent replication.

Claw-Eval-Live: A Live Agent Benchmark for Evolving Real-World Workflows

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Claw-Eval-Live, a live benchmark for LLM workflow agents that derives its task distribution from public workflow demand signals (ClawHub Top-500 skill popularity) and grades agents on observable execution evidence rather than final-text plausibility. - Why it's important: Static agent benchmarks freeze task distributions at publication time and score only final responses, causing silent drift from real automation demand and masking agents that produce fluent output without actually executing required actions. - How it does it: A refreshable signal layer (updated quarterly from ClawHub popularity snapshots) is separated from a fixed time-stamped release snapshot; tasks are selected via MILP to maximize pilot-model discrimination while covering all 22 task families, and graded deterministically from execution traces, audit logs, and post-run workspace state. - The good: The result that no model clears 70% pass rate — with HR/management workflows near-zero and workspace repair near-ceiling — provides a concrete and actionable capability map that a static, author-curated task set would not have surfaced. - The missing: GPT-5.4 is simultaneously an evaluated model and the LLM judge for semantic grading dimensions — a direct conflict of interest the paper acknowledges but addresses only by limiting judge scope, with no cross-judge validation using a model absent from the leaderboard. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners building or benchmarking workflow agents who need a quantified breakdown of where frontier models fail on real enterprise tasks; less necessary for those focused on code agents or single-turn evaluation.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Claw-Eval-Live, a live benchmark for LLM workflow agents that derives its task distribution from public workflow demand signals (ClawHub Top-500 skill popularity) and grades agents on observable execution evidence rather than final-text plausibility. - Why it's important: Static agent benchmarks freeze task distributions at publication time and score only final responses, causing silent drift from real automation demand and masking agents that produce fluent output without actually executing required actions. - How it does it: A refreshable signal layer (updated quarterly from ClawHub popularity snapshots) is separated from a fixed time-stamped release snapshot; tasks are selected via MILP to maximize pilot-model discrimination while covering all 22 task families, and graded deterministically from execution traces, audit logs, and post-run workspace state. - The good: The result that no model clears 70% pass rate — with HR/management workflows near-zero and workspace repair near-ceiling — provides a concrete and actionable capability map that a static, author-curated task set would not have surfaced. - The missing: GPT-5.4 is simultaneously an evaluated model and the LLM judge for semantic grading dimensions — a direct conflict of interest the paper acknowledges but addresses only by limiting judge scope, with no cross-judge validation using a model absent from the leaderboard. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners building or benchmarking workflow agents who need a quantified breakdown of where frontier models fail on real enterprise tasks; less necessary for those focused on code agents or single-turn evaluation. Problem & Motivation: LLM agent benchmarks face two structural problems. First, static benchmarks freeze a task mixture at publication time; as enterprise tool stacks evolve and automation priorities shift, that mixture becomes stale while remaining reproducible — the benchmark measures yesterday's demand. Second, grading final responses cannot verify whether an agent actually called the required APIs, updated the correct records, or repaired the intended artifact; a polished output can conceal complete execution failure. Both problems compound in deployed business workflows, which combine cross-service state manipulation (CRM lookups, calendar writes, ticket updates) with local workspace repair (file edits, shell commands, test verification). Existing benchmarks address one surface or the other, and none derive their task distribution from external demand signals. Key Idea: The benchmark separates construction into two layers with different time scales: a refreshable signal layer that reads workflow popularity from ClawHub Top-500 skill downloads and translates it into a distributional prior over task families, and a fixed release layer that materializes that prior into executable tasks with fixtures, controlled services, and deterministic graders. The release is reproducible; the construction pipeline is rerunnable. This decoupling allows future releases to absorb demand shifts without invalidating prior evaluations. Methodology: The signal-to-task pipeline has five stages. Stage 1 collects a time-stamped ClawHub Top-500 snapshot, treating each skill as a public workflow demand signal. Stage 2 clusters related signals into workflow patterns by user goal, target artifact, and execution surface (33 patterns collapse to 6 signal families). Stage 3 converts normalized signal mass into family weights (w_f = sum of pattern masses in family / total mass) used as distributional priors for task seeding. Stage 4 expands weighted patterns into executable task candidates, each with a YAML task definition, RESTful mock services that load fixture data and record audit logs, and a task-specific grader.py inheriting from AbstractGrader. Candidates pass pilot screening only if they are runnable, reproducible, and produce score variation. Stage 5 selects the public release using a MILP over 157 screened candidates: maximize pairwise pilot-ordering preservation across K=5 pilot models, subject to N=105 tasks, at least one task per fine-grained family, and zero-discrimination exclusions. Grading uses a deterministic-first hybrid: tool dispatch logs verify data retrieval (15–20% of task score), ground-truth fixture comparison verifies data accuracy (40–60%), and service audit trails or post-run workspace state verify action completion (10–20%); GPT-5.4 judges semantic dimensions only when deterministic signals are insufficient. Workspace repair tasks use fully deterministic script-first verification. Pass threshold is τ=0.80; models are ranked by Pass Rate, ties broken by Overall Completion Score (mean score × 100). Key Results: The best model, Claude Opus 4.6, reaches 66.7% pass rate (70/105 tasks) with overall completion 83.6; GPT-5.4 follows at 63.8% and 81.7. No model clears 70%. The leaderboard spans 22.9 percentage points from top (Opus 4.6) to bottom (Doubao Seed 2.0, 43.8%). Task family structure is more informative than aggregate rank: Development/Terminal tasks (18 tasks) score 100% for Opus 4.6, GPT-5.4, and Sonnet 4.6; HR/People tasks (9 tasks) average only 6.8% across all 13 models with several at 0.0%; MGMT is all-fail under the public pass rule. The service-backed surface (87 tasks) sees a best pass rate of 59.8% (Opus 4.6), while workspace repair (18 tasks) sees all models above 72.2% with several near 100%. Under the public pass rule, 27 tasks are all-fail and 19 are all-pass, concentrating discrimination in a middle band. Among top-ranked models, GPT-5.4 consumes the fewest tokens (1.26M vs. 3.32M for Opus 4.6) at an estimated API cost of $6.27 vs. $31.61. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK / NEW SYSTEM. The core novelty is the signal-driven, refreshable benchmark construction pipeline — deriving task distributions from external popularity data rather than author judgment is a principled and uncommon design choice. The MILP-based discrimination-aware subset selection is a concrete methodological contribution that makes curation auditable. The combination of service-backed and workspace repair under one signal-calibrated release is a scope contribution. The grading methodology (deterministic-first, LLM-for-semantics-only) is not novel in principle but is applied carefully. The individual controlled services and mock fixtures are engineering execution rather than conceptual contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are presented on the signal-to-task pipeline itself — neither the effect of different signal sources nor alternative MILP objective formulations are evaluated. No inter-rater agreement or reliability statistics are reported for the LLM judge component. The full 105×13 score matrix is released as raw_matrix_v3.csv, and the project page (claw-eval-live.github.io) is referenced, but the completeness of the public artifact release is not specified in the paper. The GPT-5.4 judge conflict is acknowledged but resolved only by scope restriction; no validation against human annotation or an independent judge model is presented. The ClawHub Top-500 signal source is proprietary and not independently verifiable, which limits external replication of the construction pipeline's validity claim. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS profile — the paper presents and evaluates a benchmark infrastructure. - Practical: The evaluation infrastructure runs against controlled mock services and sandboxed workspaces accessible via API, requiring no specialized hardware. - Efficient: Full evaluation of 13 models over 105 tasks costs $0.56–$31.61 per model at list prices and 79–241 minutes wall time; the MILP over 157 binary variables is tractable with off-the-shelf solvers. - Effective: The benchmark produces a 22.9 pp spread across models and reveals structured failure patterns by task family, demonstrating it measures meaningful capability differences beyond general language fluency. - Scalable: The quarterly refresh pipeline scales linearly with screened candidates; MILP tractability at larger candidate pool sizes is not analyzed. Task counts per family remain small (1–17), making family-level statistics noisy as the benchmark stands. The benchmark delivers a software artifact (YAML task definitions, grader.py files, mock service fixtures, score matrix CSV) and a rerunnable construction pipeline. Strengths: - Signal-driven task construction is a principled antidote to author-snapshot bias; anchoring the task distribution to an external popularity proxy makes the distributional prior externally inspectable and updateable. - MILP-based discrimination-aware subset selection converts subjective curation into an auditable optimization with explicit size, coverage, and ordering constraints — a methodological transparency step that most benchmark papers skip. - Deterministic-first hybrid grading reduces judge gaming surface area: the LLM judge cannot override hard evidence from service audit logs or workspace state, and its scope is explicitly bounded to semantic dimensions only. - The execution surface comparison (workspace repair near-ceiling vs. service-backed workflows below 60% for every model) is a concrete, actionable finding that reshapes where agent research effort should focus. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge GPT-5.4 judge bias, noting they mitigate it by limiting LLM judging to semantic dimensions and grounding every judge input in collected traces and rubrics. They note the 105-task release is modest in scale and position each release as a time-stamped snapshot rather than a permanent capability definition. ClawHub signals are explicitly described as proxies for workflow salience, not ground-truth deployment frequency, economic value, or task difficulty. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The live benchmark's core value proposition depends entirely on ClawHub Top-500 popularity data, which is proprietary, potentially gameable, and not independently verifiable. If skill downloads reflect developer curiosity or marketing rather than enterprise automation demand, the external grounding claim is weakened and the paper provides no validation of signal validity. - GPT-5.4 judge bias is not resolved: the paper presents no comparison against a neutral judge (e.g., a model absent from the leaderboard) or human annotation, so the magnitude of judge self-favoritism on semantic dimensions remains unmeasured. - 44 of 105 released tasks are non-discriminative under the public pass rule (27 all-fail, 19 all-pass), reducing the effective evaluation set to approximately 61 tasks — smaller than the headline count implies. - The paper presents only one release; the defining property of a live benchmark (tracking demand shifts across releases and demonstrating that rankings change) is claimed but not empirically demonstrated. - Several task families have extremely small counts (SEC: 1, R: 1, DOC: 1, C: 2), making family-level pass rates highly sensitive to single-task scoring artifacts and statistically unreliable. - The pass threshold τ=0.80 is not justified, ablated, or compared to alternatives; different thresholds would shift the all-fail/all-pass balance and potentially reorder the leaderboard. - No human baseline is reported for any task family, making it impossible to determine whether MGMT (all-fail) and HR (<7% average) represent genuine agent capability limits or flawed task design. Bottom Line: A practitioner evaluating workflow agents for enterprise deployment gains a useful structured failure map and a more honest grading methodology from this work, but the live/refreshable claim rests on a proprietary signal source that cannot be independently validated, the single-release paper cannot demonstrate the benchmark's defining temporal property, and the effective discrimination base of ~61 tasks is too narrow to support confident fine-grained conclusions.

Synthetic Computers at Scale for Long-Horizon Productivity Simulation

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Synthetic Computers at Scale, a methodology for generating persona-grounded, artifact-rich computer environments with realistic file hierarchies and cross-file dependency graphs, and running month-long productivity simulations on them to produce experiential learning signals for AI agents. - Why it's important: Training long-horizon productivity agents requires realistic, user-specific work context (files, history, collaborator dynamics), which is impossible to collect at scale from real computers due to privacy constraints — without this, synthetic data degenerates into generic toy workflows. - How it does it: A persona is progressively elaborated into a user profile, filesystem policy, and artifact dependency graph; an LLM-based setup agent generates objectives and simulated collaborators; a work agent then executes 20 simulated workdays navigating the filesystem and coordinating with collaborators, producing both process trajectories and final deliverables. - The good: Occupation-specific skills extracted from 900 training simulations improve agent performance from 61.6% to 68.6% rubric score in-domain (83/100 test computers), and the same skills transfer out-of-domain to GDPVal (105 wins vs. 67 losses on 220 tasks, p=0.005 two-sided) despite GDPVal being structurally very different from the simulation setting. - The missing: No ablation isolates which components — dependency graph, collaborator simulation, weekly planning — actually drive the gains; the entire system is evaluated only as a monolith. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building long-horizon agent training pipelines or synthetic data infrastructure; practitioners interested only in benchmark leaderboard results can skip it.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Synthetic Computers at Scale, a methodology for generating persona-grounded, artifact-rich computer environments with realistic file hierarchies and cross-file dependency graphs, and running month-long productivity simulations on them to produce experiential learning signals for AI agents. - Why it's important: Training long-horizon productivity agents requires realistic, user-specific work context (files, history, collaborator dynamics), which is impossible to collect at scale from real computers due to privacy constraints — without this, synthetic data degenerates into generic toy workflows. - How it does it: A persona is progressively elaborated into a user profile, filesystem policy, and artifact dependency graph; an LLM-based setup agent generates objectives and simulated collaborators; a work agent then executes 20 simulated workdays navigating the filesystem and coordinating with collaborators, producing both process trajectories and final deliverables. - The good: Occupation-specific skills extracted from 900 training simulations improve agent performance from 61.6% to 68.6% rubric score in-domain (83/100 test computers), and the same skills transfer out-of-domain to GDPVal (105 wins vs. 67 losses on 220 tasks, p=0.005 two-sided) despite GDPVal being structurally very different from the simulation setting. - The missing: No ablation isolates which components — dependency graph, collaborator simulation, weekly planning — actually drive the gains; the entire system is evaluated only as a monolith. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building long-horizon agent training pipelines or synthetic data infrastructure; practitioners interested only in benchmark leaderboard results can skip it. Problem & Motivation: Long-horizon AI agents operating over entire user computers — navigating filesystems, reading artifacts, coordinating with collaborators, and producing multi-week deliverables — need training data grounded in realistic user-specific environments. Real trajectories cannot be collected at scale because personal computers contain private enterprise documents, project state, and interaction history. Prior synthetic data approaches generate isolated tasks (e.g., GDPVal, OSWorld) rather than the full work context that conditions realistic productivity behavior: prior files, evolving project state, collaborator feedback, and cross-artifact dependencies. The gap is not missing tasks but missing environments. Key Idea: The core insight is "synthesize the context, not only the task." Rather than sampling a task and generating a prompt, the methodology first builds a user-specific computer — complete with a directed dependency graph of correlated artifacts reflecting realistic professional work history — and then conditions both objectives and agent behavior on that computer. The dependency graph (topologically ordered using Kahn's algorithm) ensures each file is generated with reference to its predecessors, so later artifacts genuinely build on earlier ones rather than being independent samples conditioned only on the persona. Methodology: The pipeline has two major stages. Computer creation: (1) A persona (brief professional description) is expanded into a detailed user profile covering identity, career stage, responsibilities, collaborators, tool preferences, and document habits. (2) A filesystem policy is derived, specifying drive layout, naming conventions, storage patterns, and organization style. (3) A filesystem plan enumerates files with paths, artifact types, timestamps, origins, and cross-file dependency edges. (4) Files are instantiated in dependency order — web-downloadable artifacts retrieved first, user-specific artifacts generated by an LLM agent equipped with Office-creation skills (MiniMax minimax-docx/xlsx, pptx-generator, minimax-pdf), each conditioned on its predecessor artifacts. Long-horizon simulation: A setup agent reads the user profile and computer state and infers month-long productivity objectives comprising 4–5 deliverable work packages with per-week milestones and expected output artifacts. The setup agent also generates simulated collaborators (managers, clients, peers, compliance officers) with roles, backgrounds, communication styles, and private reference files not initially visible to the work agent. A work agent then executes weekly planning cycles followed by 20 daily agent sessions, each session restoring context from the activity log and collaborator exchanges, producing files and sending messages. The simulation runtime uses Claude Code SDK; the work agent is Claude Sonnet 4.6, the setup agent Claude Opus 4.6. In-domain evaluation uses occupation-specific skills extracted from retrospective trajectory reports generated by an LLM analyzer, frequency-ranked across the 900 training simulations, and compiled into occupation-specific behavioral guides by a skill-creator agent. These skills are injected as context for the work agent on held-out test computers. Key Results: 1,000 synthetic computers were created and simulated. Pre-simulation computers averaged 112 files; post-simulation 197 files. Productivity artifacts (DOCX, XLSX, PDF, PPTX) account for 67.8% of all files. Each simulation averaged 2,272 agent turns and 8.59 hours wall-clock time with 5.5 simulated collaborators and ~31 communications per run. Rubric-based evaluation on 100 held-out computers: baseline score 61.6%, occupation-skill-augmented score 68.6% (+7.0 pp); skill-augmented wins 83/100 paired comparisons. Scaling trend: 10/100/500/900 training computers yield 50%/64%/75%/83% win rates, indicating the improvement monotonically increases with training coverage. Out-of-domain evaluation on GDPVal (220 tasks; avg 31 turns, 17 minutes, vs. the simulations' 2,272 turns and 8.59 hours): Sonnet-4.6 wins 105, loses 67 (p₂=0.005); Opus-4.6 wins 99, loses 71 (p₂=0.038); Haiku-4.5 wins 104, loses 80 (p₂=0.090, not significant two-sided). The retrospective analysis reveals that the dominant failure mode across all deliverables is cross-document numerical inconsistency, not failure to produce files. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The combination of persona-grounded filesystem synthesis, artifact dependency graph generation, and month-long multi-deliverable agent simulation with simulated collaborators is new. The finding that trajectory-derived occupation skills transfer out-of-domain to a structurally very different benchmark (GDPVal) is a non-obvious empirical result. However, the individual components — LLM-based persona expansion (building on Ge et al. 2024), skill extraction from trajectories, and rubric-based LLM judgment — are all combinations of existing ideas applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: This is a preprint technical report, not peer-reviewed. No ablations isolate individual components (dependency graph, collaborator simulation, weekly planning structure, retrospective analysis quality). The rubric generation procedure is somewhat principled — five runs per computer are used to generate and merge draft rubrics, reducing idiosyncratic rubric bias — but the rubric judge (Claude Opus 4.6) is the same model family as the agents being evaluated, raising potential circular evaluation concerns. Error bars, confidence intervals, and significance tests are reported for the GDPVal out-of-domain comparison (sign tests) but not for the in-domain rubric scores. The 4.2% tool-error rate in agent turns (213 errors across 5,114 turns in the appendix example) is described qualitatively but not systematically analyzed across all 1,000 simulations. The dataset (100 synthetic computers, 500 retrospective reports) is publicly released on HuggingFace at huggingface.co/datasets/microsoft/synthetic-computers-at-scale; trained model weights and extracted skills are not released. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (a methodology and system evaluated by running agents on synthetic computers). - Practical: The pipeline runs on Claude Code SDK with commercially available models (Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.6) and MiniMax open-source Office skills; no specialized hardware is required, but API access and sufficient quota are prerequisites. - Efficient: Each simulation averages 8.59 hours wall-clock time and 2,272 agent turns, making 1,000 simulations extremely expensive; the paper does not report total compute cost, token counts, or API spend, which is a significant omission for practitioners considering scaling. - Effective: The +7.0 pp in-domain improvement and statistically significant GDPVal gains are real signal; however, the absolute baseline score of 61.6% on LLM-judged rubrics leaves open how much of the gain reflects genuine productivity improvement versus rubric-gaming by the same model family. - Scalable: The paper claims the methodology can scale to billions of computers given sufficient compute, with personas available at that scale from prior work; however, only 1,000 computers are demonstrated, and the compute cost per simulation makes billion-scale deployment a distant extrapolation unsupported by current evidence. The work delivers a dataset artifact (100 synthetic computers, 500 reports) and a methodology description, but not a library, CLI, or trained model. Strengths: - The dependency graph over files is a genuine conceptual advance over treating synthetic artifacts as independent samples; it produces correlated, coherent work histories that better reflect real computer environments. - The collaborator simulation — including private reference materials that are only revealed through interaction, and intentional errors introduced by junior collaborators for the agent to catch — creates rich grounding challenges not present in existing benchmarks. - The out-of-domain transfer to GDPVal is the most credible result in the paper: GDPVal tasks are structurally very different (31 turns vs. 2,272 turns, no full computer context), yet the skills derived from computer-grounded simulations significantly improve performance, suggesting the extracted lessons generalize beyond the specific simulation setup. - The retrospective analysis appendix (Appendix A) provides unusually detailed, domain-specific diagnosis of agent failure modes — cross-document inconsistency, blank messages, end-of-context degradation — which are actionable and non-obvious. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly identify three future directions that implicitly acknowledge current limitations: (1) artifacts have uniform visual style across computers despite varying content (insufficient personalization of formatting); (2) filesystems are too clean and lack realistic noise (temporary downloads, abandoned files, clutter); (3) simulated collaborators are mostly reactive rather than having their own evolving state, deadlines, and proactive agendas. The authors also acknowledge that the web-downloadable file check is performed after planning, so some planned web downloads fall back to synthesis. These are treated as opportunities rather than deficiencies, but they represent real fidelity gaps relative to real computers. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No component ablation: it is unknown whether the dependency graph, the collaborator simulation, the weekly planning structure, or simply the quantity of synthetic data drives the in-domain improvement; a practitioner cannot know which components to implement. - Circularity in evaluation: the rubric judge (Opus 4.6), the work agent (Sonnet 4.6), and the skill-extraction model are all from the same Claude family; the GDPVal judge is also Claude Opus 4.6; this creates a risk that the extracted skills teach the agent to produce outputs that Opus judges as better without necessarily representing true productivity improvement. - Occupation imbalance: the 1,000 personas are heavily skewed toward Management & Executive (17.7%), Tech (13.7%), and Engineering (13.4%); occupations like Healthcare Support (2.2%), Production & Trades (2.0%), and Office & Admin (0.9%) are severely underrepresented, limiting the diversity claims. - No comparison to alternative synthetic data baselines: there is no comparison to simpler approaches such as task-conditioned single-turn generation, OSWorld-style sandbox simulation, or other agentic data generation pipelines. - Compute cost is entirely absent: 1,000 simulations × 8.59 hours of Claude Opus/Sonnet usage is a substantial and unreported expense; the scalability claim requires this information to evaluate. - The rubric merging procedure (5 runs per computer) relies on the setup agent's objectives and collaborator expectations as ground truth, but these are themselves LLM-generated and may share systematic biases. Bottom Line: This paper makes a credible case that synthesizing realistic work context — not just tasks — is the right framing for long-horizon agent training data, and the out-of-domain GDPVal transfer results are the strongest evidence for this claim; researchers building productivity agent training pipelines should read it, though practitioners will need to wait for ablations, compute cost disclosure, and component-level analysis before adopting the full methodology.

Efficient Agent Evaluation via Diversity-Guided User Simulation

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DIVERT (Diversity-Induced EValuation via branching of Trajectories), a snapshot-based evaluation framework that replaces linear Monte Carlo rollouts for multi-turn LLM agent evaluation with tree-structured branching from LLM-selected critical junction turns, guided by cosine-similarity-based diversity maximization over generated user responses. - Why it's important: Linear restart-from-root rollout evaluation of customer-facing agents wastes tokens on identical early prefixes, fails to expose rare deep failure modes, and becomes prohibitively expensive as agents scale to longer horizons and frontier API costs rise. - How it does it: DIVERT serializes the full orchestrator state (dialogue history, agent state, environment/tool state, random seed) at each user turn, uses an LLM to identify the junction whose modification would maximally change downstream agent behavior, generates K=3 candidate user responses conditioned on context, selects the maximally dissimilar one via all-MiniLM-L6-v2 cosine similarity, and resumes execution from the snapshot. - The good: Consistent improvement in failure discovery efficiency (errors per 100K agent tokens) across all three τ-bench domains (Airline, Retail, Telecom) and three models (GPT-OSS-120B, Gemini-2.5-Flash, LLaMA-4-Maverick), with even a single branch improving over the restart baseline while yielding a net 929.94-token saving per branch after overhead. - The missing: The LLM junction chooser is validated only by downstream ablation, not by ground-truth causal analysis of which turns actually drive failures; no significance tests or multiple seeds are reported on the main results; and results are confined to three τ-bench service domains. - Read it or not: Practitioners running multi-turn LLM agent evaluations against frontier API models should read this, as DIVERT is a drop-in protocol that demonstrably improves cost-coverage tradeoffs; those working on single-turn evaluation, training, or agentic coding tasks will find the contribution orthogonal to their setting.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DIVERT (Diversity-Induced EValuation via branching of Trajectories), a snapshot-based evaluation framework that replaces linear Monte Carlo rollouts for multi-turn LLM agent evaluation with tree-structured branching from LLM-selected critical junction turns, guided by cosine-similarity-based diversity maximization over generated user responses. - Why it's important: Linear restart-from-root rollout evaluation of customer-facing agents wastes tokens on identical early prefixes, fails to expose rare deep failure modes, and becomes prohibitively expensive as agents scale to longer horizons and frontier API costs rise. - How it does it: DIVERT serializes the full orchestrator state (dialogue history, agent state, environment/tool state, random seed) at each user turn, uses an LLM to identify the junction whose modification would maximally change downstream agent behavior, generates K=3 candidate user responses conditioned on context, selects the maximally dissimilar one via all-MiniLM-L6-v2 cosine similarity, and resumes execution from the snapshot. - The good: Consistent improvement in failure discovery efficiency (errors per 100K agent tokens) across all three τ-bench domains (Airline, Retail, Telecom) and three models (GPT-OSS-120B, Gemini-2.5-Flash, LLaMA-4-Maverick), with even a single branch improving over the restart baseline while yielding a net 929.94-token saving per branch after overhead. - The missing: The LLM junction chooser is validated only by downstream ablation, not by ground-truth causal analysis of which turns actually drive failures; no significance tests or multiple seeds are reported on the main results; and results are confined to three τ-bench service domains. - Read it or not: Practitioners running multi-turn LLM agent evaluations against frontier API models should read this, as DIVERT is a drop-in protocol that demonstrably improves cost-coverage tradeoffs; those working on single-turn evaluation, training, or agentic coding tasks will find the contribution orthogonal to their setting. Problem & Motivation: Multi-turn customer-facing LLM agents must be evaluated under diverse, stochastic user behaviors to expose reliability failures, but standard evaluation adopts linear Monte Carlo rollout from the initial state. This forces repeated regeneration of near-identical early conversation segments (authentication, greetings, routine diagnostic questions), incurring unnecessary token cost. Because these early prefixes are semantically similar but not token-identical across rollouts, KV-cache reuse is also structurally impossible. More critically, standard user simulators are biased toward cooperative, high-probability behaviors, making it unlikely that rare but realistic user patterns — fabricated credentials, ambiguous intent, insistent repetition — will be sampled often enough to surface the agent failures they trigger. As agents operate over increasingly long horizons and frontier API costs rise (Claude Opus at $25/M output tokens, GPT-5 at $10/M), this inefficiency becomes a practical bottleneck for comprehensive reliability assessment. Key Idea: Agent conversations are inherently tree-structured: many trajectories share a long common prefix and diverge at only a small number of critical decision points. The core insight is that restarting evaluation from the root discards this structure, forcing redundant recomputation of shared prefixes and precluding targeted exploration of behaviors that emerge only mid-conversation. By snapshotting the full execution state at LLM-identified junction turns and resuming with maximally diverse but intent-consistent user responses, DIVERT reuses computation on shared prefixes while directing new computation toward unexplored regions of the interaction space. Methodology: DIVERT operates as a four-stage pipeline on top of an initial pool of R full rollouts. (1) Initial rollouts and caching: R complete trajectories are executed per task, with the full orchestrator state (dialogue history, agent scratchpad, user simulator context, environment/tool database, random seed, routing metadata) serialized as state.pkl + metadata.json immediately before each user turn. (2) Junction selection: an LLM receives the full serialized trajectory and outputs the index of the user turn whose modification would produce maximal downstream behavioral change while preserving intent; temperature is 0.7 with a fallback to the previous turn on parse failure; overhead averages 103.48 tokens per branch. (3) Diversity-guided user response generation: K=3 candidate responses are generated independently at temperature 0.7, each conditioned on full conversation history and user backstory; the candidate with the lowest cosine similarity to the original response under all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings is selected as the divergent input; total candidate generation overhead is 326.04 tokens per branch. (4) Snapshot-based resumption: the cached state is restored and execution proceeds forward with only the modified user turn injected, until termination (success, 100-turn limit, or 10 errors). Branching is iterative: branched trajectories are eligible for further branching up to a configurable budget. Total DIVERT overhead per branch is 429.52 tokens against an average full-trajectory cost of 5,763.52 tokens, yielding a net savings of 929.94 tokens per branch after overhead. Two primary metrics: errors per 100K agent tokens (efficiency) and task failure count (unique tasks with at least one observed failure). Key Results: On the Airline domain with GPT-OSS-120B, the 8-rollout baseline achieves 14.7 errors/100K and 41 task failures (out of 50) at 1.5M tokens; adding 4 DIVERT branches raises efficiency to 16.4 errors/100K and coverage to 44 failures at 2.1M tokens. Reallocating budget from 8 rollouts to 2 rollouts + 8 branches achieves 19.7 errors/100K and 44 task failures at only 1.4M tokens — higher efficiency and equal coverage at lower cost than the 8-rollout baseline. On Gemini-2.5-Flash (Telecom), the 8+0 baseline yields 21.0 errors/100K; adding 4 branches raises this to 24.8. Results are consistent across LLaMA-4-Maverick and in heterogeneous agent/simulator pairings. The heatmaps (Figure 3) confirm that increasing rollouts alone shows clear saturation, while increasing branches continues to expose new failing tasks. Diversity validation (Table 2) confirms that selected candidates have lower mean utterance similarity (0.711) than 2nd (0.769) and 3rd (0.817) candidates, and this ordering propagates to downstream trajectory divergence (0.743 vs. 0.799 vs. 0.849). Exact prefix sharing under DIVERT reaches 34.80% (Airline), 42.50% (Retail), 58.35% (Telecom) versus ~0.5-0.6% for standard independent rollouts. Ablation (Table 3) shows junction chooser alone lifts efficiency (13.6 → 15.1 errors/100K) but slightly reduces task failure count (78 → 75); adding directed generation restores and extends both (15.8, 80); diversity selection adds a further marginal improvement (16.2, 81). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. Applying snapshot-based tree-structured branching to agent evaluation (as opposed to training, planning, or reasoning, where MCTS ideas already appear) is the primary novel contribution. The junction-selection-plus-diversity-generation pipeline for evaluation is new. The individual components — cosine-similarity diversity, LLM-as-selector, sentence-transformer embeddings, τ-bench evaluation protocol — are all standard; the novelty lies in their composition into a branching evaluation harness with full execution-state serialization. Rigor & Reproducibility: An ablation study (Table 3) cleanly decomposes each component's contribution. Three models and three domains are tested, including heterogeneous agent/simulator pairings. A fixed random seed (42) is used throughout — no variance estimates, error bars, or significance tests are reported, which is a notable weakness given that τ-bench results are known to vary across seeds. The intent preservation analysis uses GPT-OSS-120B as both the generator and the judge, a mild circularity. Code is promised "upon publication" but is not yet publicly available. KV-cache benefit is reported as structural prefix overlap, not as measured latency or throughput. Evaluations are bounded in scope due to frontier API costs, which the authors explicitly acknowledge. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Runs on commercially available infrastructure; the diversity selection step requires only a lightweight sentence-transformer on CPU (demonstrated on M1 Pro); the framework adds LLM API calls for junction selection and candidate generation but requires no specialized hardware. - Efficient: Total DIVERT overhead is 429.52 tokens per branch (<8% of a full trajectory), with a net saving of 929.94 tokens per branch after accounting for the overhead; as a fraction of frontier agent API cost, the overhead is 0.08-0.20%. - Effective: Consistently improves both errors/100K and task failure count across all tested domains and models, with even a single branch producing measurable gains; the ablation shows each component adds incrementally to the result. - Scalable: Tested at τ-bench scale (up to 278 tasks × 8 rollouts × 14 branches). No stress testing at 10× task count or with significantly longer trajectories; branching is currently limited to user turns only — extension to tool outputs and environment dynamics is acknowledged as future work. The paper delivers a described software system with full state-serialization specification (pickle + JSON schema), but code is not yet released. Strengths: - The observation that trajectories are tree-structured and that restart-from-root discards this structure is precise and well-motivated; it cleanly separates DIVERT from prior work on user simulation diversity, which focuses on the simulator's behavior rather than the evaluation protocol's structure. - Multi-domain, multi-model empirical validation with consistent results across Airline/Retail/Telecom and GPT-OSS-120B/Gemini-2.5-Flash/LLaMA-4-Maverick, including heterogeneous pairings, makes the findings hard to attribute to a single model's idiosyncrasies. - The cost accounting is unusually thorough: exact token breakdowns, shared-prefix overlap analysis, per-domain diversity validation at both candidate and trajectory levels, and concrete monetary estimates all appear in the appendix, enabling independent reproducibility assessment. - The ablation reveals a non-obvious result: junction chooser alone hurts task failure count despite improving token efficiency, showing that prefix reuse without directed diversity narrows rather than expands coverage — this mechanistic insight is the paper's most informative finding. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that branching is currently restricted to user turns and does not vary tool outputs or environment dynamics, which limits counterfactual depth. They identify the LLM-based junction chooser and cosine-similarity diversity metric as potentially replaceable with perplexity-based or alternative signals. Large-scale sweeps over closed frontier APIs were not conducted due to cost. KV-cache reuse is quantified as a structural proxy, not a measured speedup. These limitations are acknowledged directly and constructively. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The LLM junction chooser is validated only by downstream ablation, not by ground-truth causal analysis (e.g., counterfactual experiments that identify which turns actually drive failure); it is not demonstrated that the LLM picker outperforms simple heuristics such as always branching at the last user turn before a policy-relevant tool call. The intent preservation analysis uses GPT-OSS-120B as both the generator and the judge, and the finding (branched messages miss intent 25.27% vs. 28.12% for originals) is a modest difference whose significance is not tested. All experiments use a single fixed seed with no confidence intervals — given that τ-bench pass rates are known to be noisy at small rollout counts, the quantitative margins claimed deserve confirmation across multiple seeds. No experiment demonstrates additive benefit when combining DIVERT with an adversarial user simulator (the paper claims orthogonality but does not show synergy). Results are confined to service-domain τ-bench tasks; performance on open-domain web agents, coding agents, or tasks with much longer horizons is untested. Bottom Line: A practitioner evaluating costly multi-turn LLM agents at scale gains a concrete, deployable protocol that consistently improves failure discovery per token with negligible overhead — but the absence of variance estimates and the narrow τ-bench scope mean the quantitative magnitude of gains should be treated as indicative rather than definitive until code is released and replicated.

GREATER: Gradients Over Reasoning Makes Smaller Language Models Strong Prompt Optimizers

proceedings.iclr.cc · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GREATER, a prompt optimization method that uses numerical loss gradients computed through chain-of-thought reasoning to iteratively refine discrete prompt tokens for small open-source LLMs, eliminating dependence on large proprietary optimizer models. - Why it's important: Existing automated prompt optimization methods require expensive closed-source models like GPT-4 to generate textual feedback, making prompt optimization for small deployed models inaccessible without ongoing API costs and external model dependencies. - How it does it: GREATER generates candidate prompt tokens via the target model's own forward probabilities, produces a reasoning chain with the current prompt, extracts answer logits through a fixed extractor prompt appended after the reasoning, and backpropagates cross-entropy loss through the full sequence to select the best token replacement per position. - The good: On 5 randomly selected BBH tasks, GREATER optimized with only Llama-3-8B achieves 71.6 average accuracy versus 62.8 for the best GPT-4-optimized baseline (APE), and 75.4 with Gemma-2-9B versus 69.8 for APO-GPT-4 — a small model beating a large-model optimizer. - The missing: The method requires white-box gradient access, restricting it to open-source models, and no experiments are conducted on models larger than 9B parameters during optimization, leaving the method's scaling behavior as the optimizer entirely untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners optimizing open-source small LLMs on reasoning tasks who want a gradient-based alternative to GPT-4-dependent textual feedback; those using API-only model stacks or needing statistically validated results should look elsewhere.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GREATER, a prompt optimization method that uses numerical loss gradients computed through chain-of-thought reasoning to iteratively refine discrete prompt tokens for small open-source LLMs, eliminating dependence on large proprietary optimizer models. - Why it's important: Existing automated prompt optimization methods require expensive closed-source models like GPT-4 to generate textual feedback, making prompt optimization for small deployed models inaccessible without ongoing API costs and external model dependencies. - How it does it: GREATER generates candidate prompt tokens via the target model's own forward probabilities, produces a reasoning chain with the current prompt, extracts answer logits through a fixed extractor prompt appended after the reasoning, and backpropagates cross-entropy loss through the full sequence to select the best token replacement per position. - The good: On 5 randomly selected BBH tasks, GREATER optimized with only Llama-3-8B achieves 71.6 average accuracy versus 62.8 for the best GPT-4-optimized baseline (APE), and 75.4 with Gemma-2-9B versus 69.8 for APO-GPT-4 — a small model beating a large-model optimizer. - The missing: The method requires white-box gradient access, restricting it to open-source models, and no experiments are conducted on models larger than 9B parameters during optimization, leaving the method's scaling behavior as the optimizer entirely untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners optimizing open-source small LLMs on reasoning tasks who want a gradient-based alternative to GPT-4-dependent textual feedback; those using API-only model stacks or needing statistically validated results should look elsewhere. Problem & Motivation Automated prompt engineering methods that improve small LLM performance — APO, PE2, TextGrad — all rely on a much larger, more capable oracle model to diagnose errors and propose prompt refinements via "textual gradients." Zhang et al. (2024) empirically confirmed that small LMs cannot generate reliable optimization feedback, creating a hard dependency: improving an 8B production model requires paying for GPT-4 API calls. Prior gradient-based discrete prompt methods (AutoPrompt, PEZ) exist but are architecturally tied to simple classification verbalizers (fixed label token targets) and cannot extend to tasks requiring multi-step reasoning chains, which is exactly where smaller models need the most help. Key Idea Route the optimization loss signal through the model's own chain-of-thought reasoning rather than directly against output tokens. By generating reasoning r, then appending a fixed extractor prompt to force the model to emit answer logits, GREATER backpropagates cross-entropy loss through the concatenated [input ⊕ prompt ⊕ reasoning ⊕ extractor] sequence. Gradients then reflect how a change to a prompt token would alter the reasoning path that leads to the correct answer — a more direct signal than the downstream-only gradient, and computed entirely within the model being optimized rather than outsourced to a larger model. Methodology GREATER optimizes prompt token positions sequentially for T=10^5 steps. At each position i, three phases execute: (1) Candidate Proposal — compute top-k (k=10) next tokens from f_LLM conditioned on q=5 random task inputs, then take their intersection to get domain-relevant candidates; create a one-hot indicator ε_i over this small candidate set, extracted from the LM's embedding table. (2) Reasoning Generation and Extraction — generate reasoning r ~ f_LLM(x ⊕ p), then compute ŷ = f_LLM(x ⊕ p ⊕ r ⊕ p_extract), reusing KV-cache from the generation step for efficiency. (3) Gradient-Driven Selection — compute loss L = CE(ŷ, y) + λ·Perplexity(p) with λ=0.2, backpropagate to ∂L/∂ε_i, pick top-μ=3 candidates by highest negative gradient, evaluate each via a full forward pass on training data, and select the one with lowest loss. Dynamic prompt length is supported by checking for end tokens. Critical design decisions: the intersection operation enforces cross-sample relevance of candidates (well-motivated but unablated); the extractor prompt architecture isolates answer logits from free-form generation (well-justified by CoT literature); the top-3 forward-pass reranking adds robustness against gradient imprecision (reasonable but unablated). All experiments run on 2× A100 80GB GPUs. Key Results On BBH (21 tasks, 100-sample test): GREATER achieves 76.6% with Gemma-2-9B and 68.7% with Llama-3-8B, versus best textual-feedback baselines of 72.9% (TextGrad/Gemma) and 62.7% (APO/Llama). On GSM8K (1319-sample test): 89.4%/Gemma and 82.6%/Llama versus ZS-CoT baselines of 88.6%/79.6%. On FOLIO (203-sample validation): 69.1%/Gemma and 62.6%/Llama (ties PE2 for Llama). Compared directly to GPT-4-optimized prompts on 5 BBH tasks: GREATER (Llama) averages 71.6 versus 62.8 for APE-GPT4; GREATER (Gemma) averages 75.4 versus 69.8 for APO-GPT4. On the 1B parameter Llama-3.2-1B, GREATER averages 53.6 versus 46.0 for PE2-GPT4 on 5 BBH tasks. Ablation: removing gradient-over-reasoning and using direct-output gradients drops performance from 70 to 56 on movie recommendation and from 70 to 33 on tracking shuffled objects (Llama-3-8B). Optimization time is ~5 hours per task versus ~14 hours for TextGrad on the same hardware. Baselines include the most recent competitive methods (TextGrad 2024, PE2 2023) and are tested with both same-model and GPT-4 optimizers, making the comparison fair and strong. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The paper extends gradient-based discrete prompt search (AutoPrompt-style token replacement) to chain-of-thought reasoning tasks by routing the optimization loss through the generated reasoning chain via an answer-extractor architecture. The combination of LM-guided candidate generation, reasoning-conditioned loss, and gradient-over-chain-of-thought is novel; prior gradient methods optimized against answer logits without reasoning, and prior reasoning-integrated prompt methods used textual feedback rather than numerical gradients. The extension is non-trivial because naive backpropagation through next-token targets ignores reasoning entirely and empirically performs poorly. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation for the gradient-over-reasoning component is present (Figure 3) but covers only 2 of 21 BBH tasks and 2 models — insufficient to establish generality of the component contribution. No ablations on k, q, T, or λ hyperparameters are provided. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests appear anywhere in the paper; with ~100-sample test splits, differences of 1–2 accuracy points are likely within noise. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/psunlpgroup/GreaTer. Two BBH tasks (word sorting, dyck languages) are excluded from evaluation with the justification that small LLM outputs are "difficult to reliably evaluate" — this exclusion is not principled and removes tasks where the method might underperform. The perplexity regularization term is described as "less important" without ablation to verify this claim. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Optimization requires white-box gradient access to the model and 2× A100 80GB GPUs; inference uses only the resulting prompt string and runs on any hardware that can load the model. - Efficient: Optimization costs ~5 hours per task (versus ~14 hours for TextGrad) but scales linearly with the number of tasks and models, requiring ~210 A100-hours to reproduce Table 1 across 21 tasks and 2 models. - Effective: Consistently outperforms textual-feedback baselines across all three benchmark suites with both tested models, and matches or beats GPT-4-optimized prompts in direct comparison. - Scalable: Tested only on 8B–9B parameter models as the optimizer; Table 4 shows the performance advantage narrows when GREATER prompts transfer to Gemma-2-27B, but GREATER is never run as the optimizer on a model larger than 9B. The work delivers a code artifact and optimized prompt strings; no trained model weights or datasets are released beyond the GitHub repository. Strengths - The gradient-over-reasoning insight concretely breaks the dependency on closed-source oracle LLMs, enabling self-contained prompt optimization with the same model that runs at inference — a meaningful practical advance for open-source deployments. - The ablation in Figure 3 cleanly isolates the contribution of routing loss through the reasoning chain versus directly against output tokens, with 14–37 point drops when reasoning is removed, providing strong evidence for the core design choice. - Transferability experiments across both model families (Llama↔Gemma) and up to 27B show that gradient-optimized prompts generalize beyond the optimization model, which textual feedback methods do not always achieve. - The O(NL²) complexity argument and empirical 5-vs-14 hour comparison demonstrate that GREATER is not only more effective but computationally cheaper than TextGrad. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note that optimized prompts can exhibit grammatical errors or informal tone (Section 5.6, Table 11/14 examples) and propose dynamic top-k selection as a mitigation without follow-up experiments. Performance saturates on FOLIO for Llama-3-8B, attributed to a model-capability ceiling. No other limitations are explicitly discussed. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The white-box gradient access requirement — which restricts the method entirely to open-source models and excludes all API-only deployments (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) — is never acknowledged as a limitation, which is a significant omission given the paper's framing around competing with GPT-4-based optimizers. - No statistical significance testing on any reported number; with 100-sample test sets, many reported gaps (e.g., 89.4 vs 88.6 on GSM8K for Gemma) are likely within noise. - The intersection over q=5 candidates (Eq. 4) can produce very few or zero candidates at certain positions; the algorithm's behavior in this degenerate case is not described. - DSPy (Khattab et al., 2023), a well-known self-optimizing pipeline that could serve as a comparison point, is cited in related work but absent from all experimental tables. - The evaluation is restricted to multiple-choice or extractable-answer reasoning tasks where the extractor prompt architecture works naturally; applicability to open-ended generation is not discussed. - Optimization costs (5 hours/task on 2× A100 80GB) make per-task optimization impractical at scale; the paper does not address this or suggest amortization strategies. - All FOLIO results for Llama-3-8B show GREATER tied with PE2-GPT4 at 62.6%, which is less impressive than the framing suggests for this dataset. Bottom Line A practitioner optimizing open-source 8–9B parameter LLMs on reasoning tasks with white-box access will find a clean, code-released method that outperforms GPT-4-based textual feedback optimizers at a fraction of the cost; practitioners without gradient access or working beyond the reasoning-task domain will need to look elsewhere.

Intern-Atlas: A Methodological Evolution Graph as Research Infrastructure for AI Scientists

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Intern-Atlas, a large-scale methodological evolution graph built from 1,030,314 AI papers that explicitly encodes method-level lineage relationships, bottleneck-to-mechanism transitions, and typed causal edges, together with three graph operators for lineage reconstruction, idea evaluation, and idea generation. - Why it's important: Existing scholarly infrastructure (Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, OpenAlex) indexes papers and citation counts but leaves method evolution entirely implicit — an acute problem for AI research agents that cannot reliably reconstruct causal method topologies from unstructured text. - How it does it: An LLM-based two-phase extraction pipeline classifies every citation edge into one of seven causal types, attaches verbatim bottleneck-mechanism-tradeoff evidence records to each causal edge, and a deterministic post-checker discards edges whose quoted spans fail substring match against the source paper; a Self-Guided Temporal MCTS (SGT-MCTS) then traverses the resulting causal subgraph to reconstruct evolution chains. - The good: SGT-MCTS achieves Node Recall of 84.8% and Edge Recall of 79.0% against expert-curated survey chains, compared to 44.9% / 23.2% for the strongest beam-search baseline — a large margin on a meaningful external benchmark. - The missing: The primary idea generation evaluation scores outputs using Intern-Atlas's own evaluator, creating a circularity that inflates the method's apparent advantage; independent task-specific benchmarks (e.g., actual downstream experiment quality, accepted paper prediction) are absent. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building AI research agents, automated literature review systems, or structured knowledge graphs over scientific corpora — the infrastructure design, verbatim-grounded evidence schema, and SGT-MCTS formulation are all practically reusable.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Intern-Atlas, a large-scale methodological evolution graph built from 1,030,314 AI papers that explicitly encodes method-level lineage relationships, bottleneck-to-mechanism transitions, and typed causal edges, together with three graph operators for lineage reconstruction, idea evaluation, and idea generation. - Why it's important: Existing scholarly infrastructure (Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, OpenAlex) indexes papers and citation counts but leaves method evolution entirely implicit — an acute problem for AI research agents that cannot reliably reconstruct causal method topologies from unstructured text. - How it does it: An LLM-based two-phase extraction pipeline classifies every citation edge into one of seven causal types, attaches verbatim bottleneck-mechanism-tradeoff evidence records to each causal edge, and a deterministic post-checker discards edges whose quoted spans fail substring match against the source paper; a Self-Guided Temporal MCTS (SGT-MCTS) then traverses the resulting causal subgraph to reconstruct evolution chains. - The good: SGT-MCTS achieves Node Recall of 84.8% and Edge Recall of 79.0% against expert-curated survey chains, compared to 44.9% / 23.2% for the strongest beam-search baseline — a large margin on a meaningful external benchmark. - The missing: The primary idea generation evaluation scores outputs using Intern-Atlas's own evaluator, creating a circularity that inflates the method's apparent advantage; independent task-specific benchmarks (e.g., actual downstream experiment quality, accepted paper prediction) are absent. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building AI research agents, automated literature review systems, or structured knowledge graphs over scientific corpora — the infrastructure design, verbatim-grounded evidence schema, and SGT-MCTS formulation are all practically reusable. Problem & Motivation: Scholarly platforms treat papers as atomic units connected by untyped citation links, which suffices for human researchers who reconstruct method genealogies mentally. AI research agents — systems that automate hypothesis generation, retrieval, and ideation — cannot perform this reconstruction reliably: their parametric memory underrepresents low-frequency methodological knowledge, and they cannot distinguish genuine research gaps from gaps in their own representation. The result is that every agent system (AI Scientist, CoI, SPARK, CycleResearcher) reconstructs transient method-context chains from scratch at launch, with no shared persistent structured layer. The paper frames this as a historical infrastructure transition analogous to PDB preceding AlphaFold and ImageNet preceding CNNs: the emergence of a new computational consumer makes latent structure an explicit requirement. Key Idea: The core insight is that citation edges are not semantically uniform — a citation can mean extends, improves, replaces, adapts, uses_component, compares, or background — and that attaching verbatim bottleneck-mechanism evidence records to the strong-causal subset of edges converts a flat citation graph into a queryable causal topology of methodological evolution. Grounding every causal edge in a direct quote from the citing paper serves two purposes: it prevents the LLM from hallucinating bottleneck motivations, and it provides auditable provenance for downstream operators. Methodology: The system operates in two stages. Stage 1 (graph construction) processes 1,030,314 AI papers from major venues (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, ACL, KDD, etc., 1965–2025) through: (i) entity resolution — a hand-curated seed of 247 methods expanded by LLM scanning, yielding 8,155 canonical methods with 9,545 aliases and 3,173,187 stub nodes for out-of-corpus references; (ii) Phase-1 edge typing — Qwen3.6-35B-A3B classifies each citation into seven causal types, tuned for high recall; (iii) Phase-2 evidence extraction — a four-field record (bottleneck, mechanism, tradeoff, each as verbatim quoted spans, plus LLM confidence) is populated for every non-background edge; (iv) a deterministic post-checker discards edges failing substring match, year-ordering, or bidirectional conflicts. Stage 2 defines three operators over the resulting graph: (a) SGT-MCTS lineage reconstruction modifies UCT with a graph-aware prior combining edge confidence and temporal coherence (peaked at 1–3 year publication gaps), ranks chains by length, mean confidence, and MCTS visit count, and re-runs from branch points to surface parallel evolutions; (b) graph-grounded idea evaluation computes five dimension scores (Novelty, Feasibility, Significance, Validity, Clarity) entirely from deterministic graph statistics — topological disconnection, duplicate-risk detection via BGE + cross-encoder reranking, method maturity curves, time-decayed in-degree — combined via a non-linear cross-dimensional regularizer with four conjunctive penalties; (c) strategy-driven idea generation extracts four structural gap patterns (open axes, recent improvement directions, sacrifice axes, disconnected pairs) and maps each to a generation strategy with required evidence certificate exact-matched against stored bottleneck records. Critical design decisions: excluding `uses_component` from lineage traversal (preserving the distinction between methodological inheritance and compositional reuse) is well-justified; the zero-trainable-parameter evaluator core is a deliberate reproducibility commitment; the TC function calibrated on post-2015 AI literature is a scope limitation the authors acknowledge. Key Results: On the method-evolution benchmark derived from 30 survey papers (2,268 nodes, 1,462 edges, 133 chains), Intern-Atlas achieves NMR=91.0%, ERR=89.7%, PSC=92.0%. SGT-MCTS achieves NR=84.8, ER=79.0, CAS=84.8 versus Beam@10's 44.9 / 23.2 / 44.9 — roughly 2× improvement across all metrics. On the Strata Dataset (1,200 papers, 300 per tier), mean overall idea scores are 8.48 (top-tier ICLR/ICML/NeurIPS) > 7.83 (core) > 6.85 (workshop) > 5.84 (rejected), showing monotonic stratification. Spearman correlation with 10 AI PhD raters on 100 profiles: 0.81 for Intern-Atlas vs. 0.58 for pure LLM-as-Judge (overall); Novelty 0.84 vs. 0.52. For idea generation scored by Intern-Atlas's own evaluator: overall 7.20 vs. best baseline (Semantic Scholar) 6.18; blind pairwise win rates 88.0% vs. No-KB, 82.0% vs. OpenAlex, 81.0% vs. BM25 RAG. The production Phase-1 extraction model (Qwen3.6-35B) achieves 70.4% edge-type classification accuracy; the audit model (Claude Sonnet 4.6) achieves 93.0% — a 22.6-point gap that the paper acknowledges but does not deeply analyze in terms of downstream impact. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD (SGT-MCTS). The methodological evolution graph concept is genuinely novel as a standing queryable infrastructure at this scale; method-entity-centric knowledge graphs over scientific literature exist (Papers With Code, S2ORC) but none attach structured verbatim causal evidence records per citation. SGT-MCTS is a focused adaptation of MCTS with a temporal-coherence prior — novel in application though algorithmically derivative. The deterministic graph-based idea evaluator without trainable parameters is a non-obvious design choice that distinguishes it from prior LLM-as-judge approaches. Rigor & Reproducibility: The primary ablation for SGT-MCTS (beam search and random walk baselines) is sound and the margins are large. The idea generation evaluation contains a circularity: the same Intern-Atlas evaluator used to score idea quality is the one whose output is being evaluated — human pairwise win rates partially compensate, but the expert panel size (10 raters, 100 queries) is modest. The benchmark of 30 survey papers is relatively small and biased toward well-known, heavily-cited lineages; performance on niche or nascent areas is not tested. The Phase-1 production model accuracy of 70.4% is concerning — the paper argues downstream operators treat edge types as routing signals and localized misclassifications only shift ranking quality, but no ablation measures the effect of edge-type noise on lineage reconstruction or evaluation quality. Error bars and seeds: the strata evaluation reports means ± 1 s.d. at n=300, which is adequate. The graph and pipeline are stated to be released as open infrastructure; no repository URL is provided in the main text. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.28158v1, April 30, 2026), not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (built infrastructure evaluated by deployment and benchmarks). - Practical: The pipeline requires running Qwen3.6-35B-A3B across 1M+ papers for extraction, which demands significant GPU infrastructure, though the released artifact does not require re-running extraction to use the graph. - Efficient: Extraction cost is paid once; per-query operators use BM25 + deterministic graph traversal with B=200 MCTS rollouts, which is lightweight at inference time, though total construction compute is not stated. - Effective: SGT-MCTS recovers expert-curated evolution chains with NR=84.8% and the evaluator correlates 0.81 with PhD-expert ratings — both are meaningful but measured on AI-domain benchmarks only, leaving chemistry, physics, and biology generalization untested. - Scalable: The graph currently covers 1.03M papers; there is no ablation measuring quality degradation or construction cost at larger scales, and temporal coherence is calibrated only for post-2015 AI publication cadences. The paper delivers a software artifact (graph + pipeline, open-release) and a reusable extraction schema with verbatim evidence grounding. Strengths: - The verbatim-grounded evidence record per causal edge is a strong design choice: it provides deterministic auditability, enables post-hoc verification, and prevents hallucinated motivations from propagating through generation. - The SGT-MCTS lineage reconstruction improvement over beam search is large and measured against an independently constructed external benchmark (30 survey papers), not a self-generated reference. - The non-trivial decision to separate `uses_component` (compositional reuse) from the strong-causal subset for lineage traversal is well-reasoned and distinguishes methodological inheritance from module borrowing. - The zero-trainable-parameter evaluator design, while limiting flexibility, is auditable and reproducible in a way that learned scorers are not — a genuine trade-off acknowledged and justified.
