Author-Acknowledged Limitations: Phase-1 edge-type classification accuracy of 70.4% (production model) reflects difficulty in distinguishing fine-grained causal relations; the 14-axis bottleneck taxonomy is fixed at release and will require future revision; the TC function is calibrated on post-2015 AI literature and would need recalibration for other fields or research cadences; the four generation strategies cover only the most frequent topological patterns observed. The authors directly confront these, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) Circular evaluation: idea generation quality is measured primarily via Intern-Atlas's own evaluator — a system cannot validly use its own scoring function to demonstrate superiority in the same task; the human win-rate study partially compensates but does not eliminate this concern. (2) The production extraction model achieves only 70.4% Phase-1 accuracy yet the paper provides no ablation showing how edge-type noise affects downstream lineage recall or evaluation quality — this is a missing chain of evidence. (3) The benchmark of 30 surveys is small and drawn from highly cited, well-documented lineages (ConvNet backbones, Transformer families) where the graph is most likely to succeed; a stress test on emerging or interdisciplinary areas is absent. (4) The strata evaluation conflates paper quality with idea quality — accepted top-tier papers are not randomly higher-quality ideas than rejected ones; venue-level selection effects (reviewers, area chairs, topic fit) confound the signal. (5) No cross-domain evaluation: the graph, extraction taxonomy, and TC function are all calibrated on AI literature; claims of general scientific infrastructure are not supported by evidence outside this domain. Bottom Line: Intern-Atlas delivers a well-engineered, large-scale infrastructure artifact with a principled evidence-grounding design and strong lineage reconstruction results, and practitioners building AI research agents will find concrete and reusable components here — but the circular idea-generation evaluation and the untested 70.4% extraction accuracy leave the downstream utility claims undersubstantiated.

How Open Must Language Models be to Enable Reliable Scientific Inference?

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a structured framework identifying three fundamental inferential threats that closed language models pose to scientific research — versioning instability, credit assignment ambiguity, and insufficient information access — and maps which levels of model openness resolve or mitigate each threat. - Why it's important: A large and growing body of scientific research uses closed proprietary models (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude) as subjects of study, but those models silently update, embed undisclosed compound-system components, and withhold token probabilities and internal states, making any published findings potentially non-reproducible and non-generalizable by design. - How it does it: The paper constructs a taxonomic argument by defining three inferential goals (model evaluation, model comparison, model interpretability), deriving what information each requires, and showing systematically that closed models cannot supply that information while open-weight models can. - The good: The credit assignment section is the sharpest contribution: it precisely delineates why the behavior of a closed model endpoint cannot be attributed to the language model it contains — because decoding strategy, system prompts, guardrails, and API pipelines are all unspecified and uncontrollable, making any behavioral finding a property of the compound system, not the model. - The missing: The paper does not quantify how frequently published NLP research actually falls into each threat category, nor does it survey the literature to estimate what fraction of recent closed-model papers would have reached different conclusions with open-weight alternatives — empirical grounding that would substantially strengthen the normative argument. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers who use or review studies involving closed LLMs; it provides the clearest formalization to date of why "we used GPT-4" is scientifically insufficient as a methods statement.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a structured framework identifying three fundamental inferential threats that closed language models pose to scientific research — versioning instability, credit assignment ambiguity, and insufficient information access — and maps which levels of model openness resolve or mitigate each threat. - Why it's important: A large and growing body of scientific research uses closed proprietary models (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude) as subjects of study, but those models silently update, embed undisclosed compound-system components, and withhold token probabilities and internal states, making any published findings potentially non-reproducible and non-generalizable by design. - How it does it: The paper constructs a taxonomic argument by defining three inferential goals (model evaluation, model comparison, model interpretability), deriving what information each requires, and showing systematically that closed models cannot supply that information while open-weight models can. - The good: The credit assignment section is the sharpest contribution: it precisely delineates why the behavior of a closed model endpoint cannot be attributed to the language model it contains — because decoding strategy, system prompts, guardrails, and API pipelines are all unspecified and uncontrollable, making any behavioral finding a property of the compound system, not the model. - The missing: The paper does not quantify how frequently published NLP research actually falls into each threat category, nor does it survey the literature to estimate what fraction of recent closed-model papers would have reached different conclusions with open-weight alternatives — empirical grounding that would substantially strengthen the normative argument. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers who use or review studies involving closed LLMs; it provides the clearest formalization to date of why "we used GPT-4" is scientifically insufficient as a methods statement. Problem & Motivation: Scientific research on language models has rapidly shifted from open-weight models (BERT, T5, GPT-2) to closed proprietary systems accessed only through APIs or chat interfaces. Researchers using ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude as experimental subjects cannot control or even observe model versioning, the compound-system components layered around the core LM, or the full output probability distribution. The gap is not merely inconvenient: it threatens the three inferential goals that underlie most LM research — evaluating what a model does, comparing models, and understanding why a model behaves as it does. Prior work on model transparency (Mitchell et al. 2019; Bommasani et al. 2023) addressed accountability and governance; this paper focuses specifically on the epistemic validity of scientific inference. Key Idea: The central insight is that a closed model is not a language model but a compound system: a core LM wrapped in system prompts, decoding strategies, guardrails, retrieval APIs, and user personalization — none disclosed — and subject to silent, undated updates. Scientific findings about a closed model endpoint are therefore findings about an unknown, unstable, multi-component black box, not about a language model. Open weights resolve this because a researcher can instantiate a fixed, isolated LM and query its full output distribution, separating the LM from the compound system. Methodology: The paper's methodology is analytic rather than experimental. It defines terms precisely (closed model, open-weight model, model evaluation, model comparison, model interpretability), then derives for each inferential goal what information is required and whether closed or open-weight models supply it. Three problem categories are established: (1) The Versioning Problem — closed models update silently and irregularly, so the same label (e.g., "GPT-4o") does not refer to a stable artifact; the paper cites a 60% performance swing between March and June 2023 versions of GPT-3.5/4 on a prime-detection benchmark. (2) The Credit Assignment Problem — the behavioral output of a closed endpoint conflates the core LM with decoding strategy (unspecified), system prompt (hidden), guardrails (undocumented), and compound-system APIs, making attribution to the LM impossible. (3) The Insufficient Information Problem — closed models provide only text output; log-probabilities are rarely available; internal states are never available; yet many evaluation paradigms (multiple-choice benchmarks, surprisal-based psycholinguistic tests, calibration estimation) require per-token probabilities rather than top-prediction strings. The paper also identifies limited legitimate uses for closed models: existence proofs, upper baselines against open models, and sociotechnical research where ecological validity demands studying deployed systems. Key Results: The paper produces no quantitative experimental results. The evidence is primarily documentary: citations to platform changelogs showing undisclosed GPT updates; citations to empirical studies showing LM sensitivity to prompt formatting, temperature, and decoding strategy; citations showing that task reformulation to fit closed-model query formats systematically advantages instruction-tuned models over smaller open models (Hu and Levy 2023; Hu and Frank 2024). The 60% prime-detection performance variance across GPT versions (Chen et al. 2024c) is the most concrete data point. The paper also notes that no closed model since the original GPT-3 and Jurassic-1 has provided user-selectable token probabilities. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (secondary). The paper does not introduce a new method, dataset, or system. It contributes a precise taxonomic formalization of inferential threats that prior work on AI transparency had gestured at without fully developing. The three-problem framework (versioning, credit assignment, insufficient information) and the mapping onto specific inferential goals is the genuine intellectual contribution; the individual observations (models update, system prompts exist) are individually known but not previously organized into a coherent inferential framework with actionable scope conditions. Rigor & Reproducibility: No experiments are run, so standard reproducibility criteria (code, seeds, error bars) do not apply. The argument's rigor rests on the logical derivation and on cited empirical evidence from third parties. The taxonomy is internally consistent. The paper does not engage in self-evaluation on its own dataset, so Goodhart's Law concerns do not arise. The main weakness is selective citation: the paper cites primarily supportive evidence (sensitivity to prompts, performance instability) and does not seriously engage with the counterargument that closed models' instability may be overstated for many use cases, or that practical mitigation strategies (locking API version strings, recording execution dates) may be sufficient for some research goals. No code or data artifact is released, as none is produced. This is a preprint (arXiv:2603.26539v1, March 2026); it has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: THEORETICAL (the paper makes formal claims about what information is and is not available from model types, with no primary experimental claim). - Sound: The core logical structure — you cannot attribute compound-system behavior to a component if the other components are unobserved — is sound; the versioning argument is well-supported by cited empirical evidence. The framing is careful to distinguish what closed models "cannot" do by definition from what they "do not currently" do in practice. - General: The argument holds for any closed model that embeds an LM in an undisclosed compound system with silent versioning; it is appropriately scoped and does not overclaim for open-weight models, noting that even open weights do not guarantee reliable inference. - Tight: The framework identifies necessary (not sufficient) conditions: open weights are necessary but not sufficient for reliable mechanistic interpretability research, which also requires training data, checkpoints, and gradients. This is clearly stated. - Implementable: The paper yields a concrete decision procedure: for any research study, map the inferential goal to the framework's table (Appendix A, Table 1), identify the threats, and select a model class that resolves them. The recommendations are actionable for authors, reviewers, and editors. The paper delivers no software artifact; it stops at paper form, though it provides a summary decision table. Strengths: - The credit assignment problem section is genuinely clarifying: the observation that decoding strategy alone — an active research area with multiple non-equivalent strategies — is unspecified in every major closed model API is a precise, underappreciated point. - The scope conditions are honest: the paper explicitly carves out legitimate uses for closed models (existence proofs, sociotechnical research) rather than arguing for blanket prohibition. - The framework is extensible: the three-problem taxonomy provides a vocabulary that reviewers and editors can operationalize without requiring deep technical background. - The distinction between "open weights resolve the versioning problem" and "open weights do not guarantee it" (a hosted open-weight endpoint could still update) reflects unusual care in avoiding overclaim. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that the paper focuses only on model evaluation, comparison, and interpretability research on pretrained models, excluding human-computer interaction and some sociotechnical questions. They note the analysis was written from the perspective of external researchers without insider access. They acknowledge the three problems are not exhaustive — training data contamination presents additional inferential threats not fully treated. They explicitly state the discussion of mitigations beyond open weights (training data, checkpoints, gradients) is not exhaustive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper's central normative argument — that closed models are generally unsuitable for scientific use — rests on logical derivation rather than empirical demonstration of how frequently this actually produces wrong conclusions in published literature. A survey of published NLP papers using closed models, assessing what fraction report findings that cannot be reproduced or are likely confounded by compound-system effects, would substantially strengthen the case. The paper also largely ignores the practical economics: running competitive open-weight models at the scale needed to study frontier capabilities requires infrastructure unavailable to most academic groups, which its §5.2 discussion only partially addresses. Additionally, the paper treats closed model APIs as monolithic, but some (e.g., OpenAI's Completions endpoint with logprobs, Anthropic's token counting) partially mitigate the insufficient information problem; the granularity of this partial mitigation is not analyzed. Finally, the paper does not address the possibility that future closed model providers could resolve all three problems while remaining commercially closed, which would undercut the paper's practical recommendation to treat open weights as the default. Bottom Line: A practitioner who designs or reviews empirical NLP research using closed models will gain an immediately usable framework for identifying and documenting inferential threats — the credit assignment and versioning sections alone justify reading.

STEM: Structure-Tracing Evidence Mining for Knowledge Graphs-Driven Retrieval-Augmented Generation

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces STEM (Structure-Tracing Evidence Mining), a KG-RAG framework that reframes multi-hop question answering as schema-guided subgraph matching by projecting natural language queries into KG-aligned schema graphs and using a Triple-Dependent GNN to inject global structural priors into edge-level retrieval scoring. - Why it's important: LLM-based KGQA systems routinely fail on complex multi-hop questions because they generate relations that are semantically plausible but topologically absent in the target KG (schema hallucination), and retrieve fragmented paths rather than complete evidence subgraphs, causing systematic accuracy and coverage losses. - How it does it: Two fine-tuned 8B models (SGDA, SAGB) convert natural language queries into KG-symbolic schema graphs; a 6-layer Triple-Dependent GNN over those triples produces a Global Guidance Subgraph whose entity and triple membership biases the cosine-similarity-based isomorphic search at each step. - The good: STEM+GPT-4o achieves 90.94% Hit@1 / 76.18% F1 on WebQSP and 74.09% Hit@1 / 65.33% F1 on CWQ — SOTA on 3 of 4 metrics — while running ~6× faster than the strongest prompting baseline (FiDeLiS). - The missing: All training and evaluation is confined to Freebase-based benchmarks; no zero-shot or cross-KG transfer experiments are run, so generalizability to heterogeneous KGs is entirely undemonstrated despite real-world deployment claims. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building structured KG-RAG systems, particularly those dealing with schema heterogeneity or multi-answer retrieval; less useful if your focus is open-domain or schema-free RAG.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces STEM (Structure-Tracing Evidence Mining), a KG-RAG framework that reframes multi-hop question answering as schema-guided subgraph matching by projecting natural language queries into KG-aligned schema graphs and using a Triple-Dependent GNN to inject global structural priors into edge-level retrieval scoring. - Why it's important: LLM-based KGQA systems routinely fail on complex multi-hop questions because they generate relations that are semantically plausible but topologically absent in the target KG (schema hallucination), and retrieve fragmented paths rather than complete evidence subgraphs, causing systematic accuracy and coverage losses. - How it does it: Two fine-tuned 8B models (SGDA, SAGB) convert natural language queries into KG-symbolic schema graphs; a 6-layer Triple-Dependent GNN over those triples produces a Global Guidance Subgraph whose entity and triple membership biases the cosine-similarity-based isomorphic search at each step. - The good: STEM+GPT-4o achieves 90.94% Hit@1 / 76.18% F1 on WebQSP and 74.09% Hit@1 / 65.33% F1 on CWQ — SOTA on 3 of 4 metrics — while running ~6× faster than the strongest prompting baseline (FiDeLiS). - The missing: All training and evaluation is confined to Freebase-based benchmarks; no zero-shot or cross-KG transfer experiments are run, so generalizability to heterogeneous KGs is entirely undemonstrated despite real-world deployment claims. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building structured KG-RAG systems, particularly those dealing with schema heterogeneity or multi-answer retrieval; less useful if your focus is open-domain or schema-free RAG. Problem & Motivation LLMs used for knowledge graph question answering face three interrelated failure modes. First, schema hallucination: LLMs decomposing queries or generating reasoning plans lack prior knowledge of the target KG's relational vocabulary, so they produce semantically plausible but topologically nonexistent relations (e.g., generating "fly into" when the KG encodes "location.location.nearby_airports"). Second, evidence fragmentation: stepwise beam-search or path-matching approaches operate with only local semantic similarity, making them vulnerable to hub-node explosion and producing disconnected reasoning chains rather than coherent evidence subgraphs. Third, interactive methods that invoke the LLM at every search step scale poorly — especially for multi-answer questions requiring parallel path traversal. These problems collectively degrade Hit@1, F1, and ground-truth coverage, particularly on the more complex CWQ benchmark. Key Idea The key insight is to treat query planning and KG retrieval as a structure translation problem rather than a semantic search problem: first fine-tune specialized models to translate natural language queries into KG-symbolic schema graphs that are topologically valid (bridging the semantic-structural gap), then use a GNN conditioned on those schema triples to compute a soft global structural prior that re-weights edge scores during subgraph isomorphism search — combining structural reasoning and semantic matching rather than relying on either alone. Methodology The pipeline has five stages. (1) SGDA (Schema-Grounded Decomposition Agent): a fine-tuned Qwen3-8B decomposes the query into atomic relational assertions using beam search (B=4) and classifies the query as Precision (single deterministic answer) or Breadth (multiple answers). Trained on ~25k standard examples augmented with ~210k synthetic examples from Structure-to-Query Reverse Generation, which samples subgraphs from the KG and uses Gemini 2.5 Pro to generate aligned questions, enabling pattern generalization rather than fact memorization. (2) SAGB (Symbol-Aligned Graph Builder): a separate fine-tuned Qwen3-8B maps each textual assertion to the KG's symbolic relation vocabulary (e.g., "hockey position" → "ice_hockey.hockey_player.hockey_position"), trained on ~29k entries. Together SGDA+SAGB form the Semantic-to-Structural Projection pipeline. (3) Triple-GNN: a 6-layer message-passing GNN conditioned on query triples (average-pooled embedding EQ), using DistMult aggregation, that outputs per-entity probability scores; top-K entities (K=|T|×4) are connected into the Global Guidance Subgraph. (4) Structure-Tracing Subgraph Retrieval: starts from the highest-scoring anchor entity and recursively matches schema graph edges to KG edges via T-Score = cosine_sim(encoded triple pair) + ITri, where entity-level bias IEnt multiplies scores of Guidance Graph entities by 1.5, and triple-level bias ITri adds 0.5 for Guidance Graph triples. Precision strategy uses greedy edge selection; Breadth strategy retains all edges above threshold θ=0.6. (5) Generation: DFS linearization of the retrieved subgraph into reasoning chains, fed to a separate LLM (Llama-3.1-8B/70B or GPT-4o). Three critical design decisions: pattern-learning over fact-memorization in SGDA/SAGB training (well-justified by ablation), modest bias values (λ=1.5, τ=0.5) to avoid Structural Over-Interpretation (justified by grid search), and beam size B=4 for schema diversity (justified by ablation). Key Results STEM+GPT-4o: 90.94% Hit@1, 76.18% F1 on WebQSP (1,628 test); 74.09% Hit@1, 65.33% F1 on CWQ (3,531 test). Beats RoG+GPT-4o by 2.85%/4.48% Hit@1 on WebQSP/CWQ. Beats FiDeLiS+GPT-4o-turbo by ~6.5% Hit@1 on WebQSP, with improvements across all CWQ metrics. STEM+Llama-3.1-8B outperforms RoG+Llama-3.1-70B on CWQ (68.76% vs 67.43% Hit@1), demonstrating retrieval quality independent of model scale. Ablation (Table 7): removing both GNN biases drops CWQ Hit@1 by 10.18 points; retrieval coverage analysis (Table 12) shows guidance raises ground-truth path coverage from 65.07% to 73.68% on WebQSP and 59.8% to 70.39% on CWQ. Error attribution (Table 26): 83.01% of cases show correct retrieval producing correct answers; retrieval failures cause 57.3% of all incorrect answers. STEM runs at 5.77s/query vs FiDeLiS at 34.97s (~6× faster). Noteworthy weakness: STEM trails GNN-RAG on CWQ 2-hop questions (66.73% vs 69.4% F1, Table 3) — unexplained. ProgRAG+GPT-4o-mini achieves 90.4% Hit@1 on WebQSP without fine-tuning, matching STEM+GPT-4o — a competitive result the paper does not analyze deeply. Baselines are current and representative (FiDeLiS, GNN-RAG, ProgRAG, RoG). Three independent runs reported with averages. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the integrated pipeline: schema-aware query projection via fine-tuned models that learn KG relation patterns from reverse-generated data, combined with triple-conditioned GNN global guidance injected as soft biases into isomorphism search. The subgraph matching extends SimGRAG; the GNN architecture follows GFM-RAG's query-dependent design; decomposition is inspired by CoG. What is original is the training strategy (pattern learning via reverse generation), the Precision/Breadth strategy classification, and the dual-level (entity + triple) structural bias mechanism — a standard combination of existing ideas but with a novel integration that demonstrably closes the semantic-structural gap. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: schema pipeline comparison (Table 6), GNN bias ablations (Table 7), coverage rate ablations (Table 12), augmented data ablation (Table 13), and hyperparameter sweeps for K, B, λ, τ, and θ. Results averaged over 3 full runs; no per-run variance or confidence intervals reported. Code is publicly released (https://github.com/PennyYu123/STEM_RAG); synthetic dataset Dsyn (214k entries) to be released. Key methodological concern: bias hyperparameters (λ, τ) and threshold θ were tuned on validation splits of the same benchmarks used for evaluation — standard but worth noting. The Dsyn augmentation data is generated from the same Freebase-based KGs as the evaluation benchmarks, creating potential schema overlap that could inflate generalization estimates. Comparing fine-tuned STEM against zero-shot prompting baselines in the main table (Table 1) without clear visual separation is misleading despite the footnote acknowledgment. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL / SYSTEMS - Practical: All components run on commercially available hardware; paper uses 4×NVIDIA H100 for training and single H100 for inference evaluation; the Qwen3-8B and embedding components are accessible open-source models. - Efficient: Per-query latency averages 5.77s (WebQSP) with Precision mode at ~4.9s and Breadth at ~8.4s on a single H100; the 2×B=8 SGDA/SAGB forward passes dominate; GNN forward pass is preindexable; substantially better than interactive LLM methods but slower than pure retrieval baselines (GNN-RAG: 2.64s). - Effective: Direct retrieval coverage measurement (Table 12) and error attribution matrices (Tables 25, 26) provide strong evidence that the retrieval mechanism itself works — 83.01% of correct retrievals produce correct answers — rather than relying solely on end-to-end QA metrics. - Scalable: Only tested on Freebase-based WebQSP and CWQ; no experiments on larger or structurally different KGs; the authors explicitly state STEM is not zero-shot and its transferability to unseen KG schemas is unvalidated. Delivers: code repository, fine-tuned model weights (implicitly via training configs), and synthetic dataset Dsyn. Strengths - The Structure-to-Query Reverse Generation strategy for training SGDA/SAGB is practically effective: the 210k synthetic dataset improves schema graph Recall by 15% on WebQSP and Precision by 15% on CWQ (Figure 7), enabling pattern generalization that raw training data alone cannot provide. - The dual-level GNN bias mechanism (entity + triple, with modest magnitudes) strikes a principled balance between structural guidance and semantic flexibility; the grid-search analysis of Structural Over-Interpretation explains concretely why hard structural constraints fail. - The error attribution matrices (Tables 25, 26) provide rare transparency into pipeline failure modes, revealing that upstream planning errors cause only 16.4% of QA failures while retrieval accounts for 57.3% — actionable for future work. - The Precision/Breadth strategy classification (>90% accuracy per Table 15) is a simple but effective mechanism for handling multi-answer questions, yielding ~4–9% F1 improvements in multi-answer subsets (Table 2). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state three limitations directly: (1) planning deviations can still produce schema graphs that entirely miss the factual KG structure, causing retrieval failure; (2) STEM requires domain-specific fine-tuning and access to the target KG's schema, making it inapplicable zero-shot to unseen KGs; (3) Breadth strategy increases latency. The authors frame these as known trade-offs rather than minimizing them — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-KG confinement is deeper than acknowledged: the Dsyn synthetic data is derived from the same Freebase KG graphs as WebQSP/CWQ, so the SGDA/SAGB effectively memorize Freebase relational patterns; performance on Wikidata, DBpedia, or enterprise KGs is purely speculative. - The CWQ hop=2 underperformance versus GNN-RAG (66.73% vs 69.4% F1, Table 3) is noted but not analyzed — a 2-hop query is the simplest multi-hop case, and underperformance here suggests a structural vulnerability in the schema projection that is not explained. - ProgRAG+GPT-4o-mini achieves 90.4% Hit@1 on WebQSP without any fine-tuning, nearly matching STEM+GPT-4o; this result receives no comparative analysis and raises the question of whether the complexity of STEM's pipeline is necessary for WebQSP. - The efficiency comparison for GPT-4o variants conflates API call latency (variable, not controllable) with local model inference latency; all latency figures use local Llama models and should not be directly attributed to the GPT-4o variant results. - No measurement of false-positive rate in retrieved subgraphs (precision of retrieval, not just coverage/recall of ground-truth paths) — the Breadth strategy's threshold-based branching likely introduces irrelevant triples that inflate context for the LLM. Bottom Line A practitioner building KGQA over Freebase-style structured KGs gains concrete value from STEM's schema projection pipeline and retrieval coverage improvements, but should treat cross-KG and zero-shot generalization claims as unvalidated until tested beyond Freebase benchmarks.

Defragmenting Language Models: An Interpretability-based Approach for Vocabulary Expansion

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FragMend, an interpretability-based vocabulary expansion method that leverages "subword detokenization"—LLMs' internal progressive reconstruction of subwords across transformer layers—to select vocabulary items and initialize their embeddings, achieving up to 3× more token reduction than the prior interpretability baseline while preserving downstream performance. - Why it's important: Speakers of languages written in non-Latin scripts (e.g., Odia, Burmese) face a 5–10× tokenization premium over English speakers, directly inflating API costs and degrading model performance, and the standard BPE-based remedy forces a poor performance–efficiency tradeoff that interpretability-based methods can substantially improve. - How it does it: For each candidate subword affix extracted from a small unlabeled corpus, FragMend runs the Patchscopes activation-patching framework to detect whether the LLM internally reconstructs that subword; successfully detokenized subwords are added to the vocabulary with embeddings initialized via an orthogonal Procrustes transformation from the hidden state at the earliest detokenization layer. - The good: Interpretability-based item selection (vs. BPE) shifts the entire performance–efficiency Pareto frontier: BPE-expanded models can lose 60+ F1 points for languages like Odia at equivalent budget, while FragMend achieves 20–60% token reduction with single-digit performance drops on SIB200 and Belebele, using only 1000 training sequences and a single H200 GPU. - The missing: No evaluation on generative tasks (the method is only tested on topic classification and reading comprehension), and hypernetwork-based baselines (ZeTT, HYPEROFA) are explicitly deferred to future work—leaving the strongest competitors unaddressed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on multilingual tokenization, vocabulary adaptation, or mechanistic interpretability; practitioners needing generation-quality guarantees should wait for generative task evaluation.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FragMend, an interpretability-based vocabulary expansion method that leverages "subword detokenization"—LLMs' internal progressive reconstruction of subwords across transformer layers—to select vocabulary items and initialize their embeddings, achieving up to 3× more token reduction than the prior interpretability baseline while preserving downstream performance. - Why it's important: Speakers of languages written in non-Latin scripts (e.g., Odia, Burmese) face a 5–10× tokenization premium over English speakers, directly inflating API costs and degrading model performance, and the standard BPE-based remedy forces a poor performance–efficiency tradeoff that interpretability-based methods can substantially improve. - How it does it: For each candidate subword affix extracted from a small unlabeled corpus, FragMend runs the Patchscopes activation-patching framework to detect whether the LLM internally reconstructs that subword; successfully detokenized subwords are added to the vocabulary with embeddings initialized via an orthogonal Procrustes transformation from the hidden state at the earliest detokenization layer. - The good: Interpretability-based item selection (vs. BPE) shifts the entire performance–efficiency Pareto frontier: BPE-expanded models can lose 60+ F1 points for languages like Odia at equivalent budget, while FragMend achieves 20–60% token reduction with single-digit performance drops on SIB200 and Belebele, using only 1000 training sequences and a single H200 GPU. - The missing: No evaluation on generative tasks (the method is only tested on topic classification and reading comprehension), and hypernetwork-based baselines (ZeTT, HYPEROFA) are explicitly deferred to future work—leaving the strongest competitors unaddressed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on multilingual tokenization, vocabulary adaptation, or mechanistic interpretability; practitioners needing generation-quality guarantees should wait for generative task evaluation. Problem & Motivation: Languages written in non-Latin scripts are severely penalized by BPE-based tokenizers, which reflect the Latin-script dominance of pre-training data. Odia requires ~10× more tokens than English for equivalent text; Hindi ~4.5×; Gujarati ~6.8×—even when officially supported by the model. This token over-fragmentation translates directly into higher API costs, higher latency, and worse downstream task performance for affected language communities. Since retraining an LLM with an equitable tokenizer is computationally infeasible, vocabulary expansion—adding target-language items to an existing tokenizer and fine-tuning only the corresponding embeddings—is the practical remedy. Prior work treats frequency-based item selection (BPE merges on a target-language corpus) as an unquestioned default, focusing research effort solely on how to initialize embeddings for the newly added items. This paper challenges that assumption and asks whether interpretability-driven selection can produce a better efficiency–performance tradeoff. Key Idea: LLMs maintain an internal lexicon far richer than their surface vocabulary: when a multi-token word is processed, hidden representations at intermediate layers progressively merge the subword tokens, reconstructing a coherent representation of the full word—a process called "detokenization." The paper extends this finding to subwords: the model also detokenizes subword affixes (e.g., reconstructing "Lay" from "L"+"ay" before reconstructing "Layla" from all three tokens). Subwords that are successfully detokenized by the model's internal machinery are semantically grounded within the model's existing representational space; adding them to the vocabulary and initializing their embeddings from the corresponding activation provides a far better starting point than frequency-based candidates initialized heuristically. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages; this work fixes Stage 3 (LAPT fine-tuning of only the new embeddings, all other weights frozen) and systematically studies Stages 1 and 2. For Stage 1 (Item Selection), the Patchscopes framework (Ghandeharioun et al., 2024) is used: (i) a first forward pass computes hidden activations h^l_{ti} at every layer l for all subword tokens of a candidate word/affix; (ii) a second pass patches each activation as the input to a repetition-seeking prompt ("In [language]: X, X, X, X,") and checks whether the model outputs the target word/affix. A word is "detokenized" if it appears in any output across subwords and layers. FragMend extends the candidate set from unique full words in a training corpus (Tokens2Words) to all unique token affixes (prefixes, infixes, suffixes) of every word, enabling 2–3× more vocabulary additions from the same corpus. Because subwords appear in multiple words, a reduction strategy is required: either average candidate initializations (R_m) or select the earliest-layer instance (R_e). Two additional design choices address "vocabulary expansion perversity" (an HF tokenizer implementation artifact where newly added short items disrupt existing merges): a minimum-length filter removing items shorter than 3 UTF-8 characters (T), and full-word preference (P) when a subword coincides with a standalone word. Ablations identify T as the dominant choice (+15 pts in some languages), yielding preferred config R_e,T,P. For Stage 2 (Embedding Initialization), input and output embeddings for new items are initialized by applying a learned orthogonal Procrustes transformation T_{l,E} / T_{l,U} to the hidden state at the earliest successful detokenization layer. The transformation is fit on in-vocabulary items to map layer-l activations to embedding space. This is compared against Random, FVT (constituent-subword average), and FOCUS (fastText-weighted combination) baselines. Key Results: Primary model: Qwen3-30B-A3B; secondary: Qwen3.5-{0.8B,2B,4B,9B} and TinyAya-Global-3B. Evaluation benchmarks: SIB200 (topic classification, F1), Belebele (reading comprehension, accuracy), bits-per-byte on Glot500c. Training corpus: Glot500c, 14 languages, |C_train| ∈ {1k, 10k} sequences. Three seeds (most experiments). Pareto analysis (Figure 1c / Figure 6): For all five highly over-fragmented languages shown (amh, fas, guj, hin, ory), both Tokens2Words and FragMend dominate the BPE Pareto frontier regardless of BPE initialization method. BPE achieves higher token reduction at maximum budget but at performance costs up to 60+ F1/accuracy points for Odia (SIB200). For Hindi, Tokens2Words outperforms BPE on both dimensions simultaneously. Initialization comparison (Figures 3, 12): With Tokens2Words-selected items, Tokens2Words initialization outperforms FVT, Random, and FOCUS for most non-Latin script languages (~20 pts over baselines in pronounced cases); FVT leads for Latin-script languages. FOCUS is consistently weakest, consistent with prior literature. FragMend vs. Tokens2Words (Figure 4a, Table 5): FragMend is strictly more token-efficient for every language tested. For guj, hin, srp the efficiency gain comes with zero performance drop. For ory (Belebele): FragMend R_e,T,P reaches 63.0% vs. original 81.9% with ~40–55% token reduction; Tokens2Words reaches 73.0% with ~10–15% reduction. For mya: FragMend 61.6–62.2% vs. original 76.0% with ~40–60% reduction. FragMend fails to help for out-of-distribution languages (amh, gla, sot) due to weak model activations. Model scale (Figures 4b–c, 22–23): Token reduction increases monotonically from 0.8B to 9B parameters (4B Qwen3.5 exception: thinking-token generation inhibits detokenization). Larger models benefit more, supporting the hypothesis that interpretability-based methods will grow more effective as models improve. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The core novelty is twofold: (1) empirically establishing that item selection (Stage 1), not initialization, is the fundamental bottleneck in vocabulary expansion—a claim backed by Pareto analysis across five languages and multiple initialization strategies; (2) the discovery that subword-level detokenization is a generalizable phenomenon occurring across diverse language families, extending prior full-word detokenization findings (Kaplan et al., 2025). FragMend's specific combination of subword affix mining, Patchscopes probing, earliest-layer reduction, and length filtering is new. The orthogonal Procrustes embedding initialization and LAPT fine-tuning are from Kaplan et al. 2025, applied to a new (subword) setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Most experiments use three random seeds ({42, 20, 1984}); BPE Pareto uses one seed but seed stability is shown separately (Figure 7b–d). Error bars are visible in scale analysis figures (Figures 22–23) but not in main bar charts. Ablation of all three FragMend design choices is systematic (Tables 1 and 5), with clear magnitude estimates for each component's contribution—the length filter dominates. Code is publicly released at github.com/utahnlp/fragmend (there is a second anonymized URL in Appendix D, suggesting simultaneous double-blind submission; this is an arXiv preprint, arXiv:2604.16656v1). Evaluation uses standard public benchmarks (SIB200, Belebele, Glot500c). The Pareto analysis caps BPE's budget at the number of unique words in C_train to equalize comparison—a reasonable but potentially flattering constraint for interpretability methods at larger budgets. Hypernetwork-based baselines (ZeTT, HYPEROFA) are explicitly deferred. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Runs on a single H200 GPU (or MIG slice); requires only 1000 unlabeled target-language sentences and no retraining of model weights—deployable by any lab with a single high-end GPU and access to a small monolingual corpus. - Efficient: Patchscopes batch size of 2 (vs. 16 for Tokens2Words) makes FragMend 8× slower per candidate; the expanded candidate set (all subword affixes) is much larger than unique words, so total compute is substantially higher than Tokens2Words, though the paper does not report wall-clock times. - Effective: Achieves the claimed goal—meaningful token reduction with preserved task accuracy on classification and reading comprehension—but generative task performance (translation, instruction-following, summarization) is not evaluated, leaving a gap for production deployments. - Scalable: Tested across 4 model sizes (0.8B to 9B) and one 30B MoE model; method scales favorably with model size. Evaluation covers only 14 languages; extension to all 111 over-fragmented languages and to multi-language simultaneous expansion is untested. The paper delivers a software artifact (GitHub code) and a paper-form method; it does not release pre-expanded models or tokenizers. Strengths: - Reframes the vocabulary expansion problem: showing that item selection is the binding constraint shifts focus from the well-studied initialization problem to an under-studied one, and the Pareto evidence is initialization-independent (holds for Random, FVT, and FOCUS initializations of BPE items). - Subword detokenization as an empirical finding contributes to mechanistic interpretability literature: the layerwise progressive merging across both high- and low-resource, Latin- and non-Latin-script languages is a substantive observation about how LLMs organize lexical knowledge. - Practical accessibility: 1000 training sequences is attainable even for genuinely low-resource languages; the pipeline requires no API access, no bilingual dictionary, no auxiliary embedding space, and freezes all parameters except the new embeddings. - Clean, systematic ablation (Table 1/5): the contribution of each design choice (reduction strategy, length filter, full-word preference) is quantified, the dominant factor is identified, and the preferred configuration is argued from first principles. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The method's effectiveness depends on the quality of the LLM's internal representations; small models (TinyAya-3B, Qwen3.5-4B) show diminished gains due to the "curse of multilinguality" and fewer layers for detokenization. Languages not officially supported by the model (amh, gla, sot, mri in some experiments) show consistently poor detokenization and thus poor initialization. The tuning strategy (LAPT, embeddings-only) is fixed; more sophisticated multi-parameter tuning could improve all methods. The authors frame these honestly and do not minimize them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Hypernetwork-based baselines (ZeTT/Minixhofer et al. 2024; HYPEROFA/Özeren et al. 2025) are explicitly deferred but are published, available methods—their omission leaves the strongest competitive comparison open. No generative task evaluation means it is unknown whether vocabulary expansion with interpretability-based initialization helps or hurts open-ended generation, translation, or instruction-following—the tasks most affected by API costs for end users. The "vocabulary expansion perversity" fix (minimum 3 UTF-8 character length) is an empirically chosen threshold with no theoretical justification and may not generalize across scripts with very different character byte sizes (Burmese characters are 3 bytes each, so a 3-character Burmese subword ≥ 9 bytes). The paper evaluates single-target-language expansion in isolation; simultaneous multi-language expansion (the practical deployment scenario) is not studied. The Patchscopes prompt template ("In [language]: X, X, X, X,") is English-anchored; its cross-lingual reliability for non-Latin languages that the model does not officially support is not ablated. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on multilingual tokenization or LLM adaptation for low-resource non-Latin-script languages gains concrete, deployable methodology and a clear argument for why to look beyond BPE for item selection—this paper is worth reading; those in generative NLP or seeking production-ready comparisons should treat it as a promising preprint awaiting generative-task and hypernetwork-baseline evaluation.

Self-Evolving LLM Memory Extraction Across Heterogeneous Tasks

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BEHEMOTH, an 18-dataset benchmark for heterogeneous memory extraction, and CluE, a cluster-based self-evolving framework that groups training examples by extraction scenario to stably improve memory extraction prompts across diverse task types. - Why it's important: Deployed LLM assistants encounter wildly different interaction types — personalization chats, math problem-solving, agentic tool use — and no single fixed extraction rule generalizes across them; this work is the first to formalize and benchmark that generalization challenge. - How it does it: CluE summarizes each training example's extraction scenario, clusters examples by those scenarios rather than by surface task or domain, performs per-cluster failure/success analysis, then synthesizes cross-cluster insights into a unified updated extraction prompt. - The good: CluE achieves +9.04% relative gain over the Simple baseline across 18 datasets and consistently improves all three task categories simultaneously (+12.34% personalization, +8.39% problem-solving, +7.22% agentic), whereas every competing self-evolving framework improves one category at the expense of another. - The missing: All experiments use the same model for both memory extraction and target-query answering; no evaluation tests whether prompts evolved with one backbone transfer to a different extraction model. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building persistent LLM assistants or studying prompt optimization under heterogeneous data distributions; the BEHEMOTH benchmark and the cluster-by-scenario insight are both reusable contributions.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BEHEMOTH, an 18-dataset benchmark for heterogeneous memory extraction, and CluE, a cluster-based self-evolving framework that groups training examples by extraction scenario to stably improve memory extraction prompts across diverse task types. - Why it's important: Deployed LLM assistants encounter wildly different interaction types — personalization chats, math problem-solving, agentic tool use — and no single fixed extraction rule generalizes across them; this work is the first to formalize and benchmark that generalization challenge. - How it does it: CluE summarizes each training example's extraction scenario, clusters examples by those scenarios rather than by surface task or domain, performs per-cluster failure/success analysis, then synthesizes cross-cluster insights into a unified updated extraction prompt. - The good: CluE achieves +9.04% relative gain over the Simple baseline across 18 datasets and consistently improves all three task categories simultaneously (+12.34% personalization, +8.39% problem-solving, +7.22% agentic), whereas every competing self-evolving framework improves one category at the expense of another. - The missing: All experiments use the same model for both memory extraction and target-query answering; no evaluation tests whether prompts evolved with one backbone transfer to a different extraction model. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building persistent LLM assistants or studying prompt optimization under heterogeneous data distributions; the BEHEMOTH benchmark and the cluster-by-scenario insight are both reusable contributions. Problem & Motivation: As LLM assistants become persistent across sessions, they must extract and store useful information from prior conversations as memory. Existing extraction systems use static, domain-specific rules: personalization agents extract user preferences, agentic systems extract reusable strategies, problem-solving systems extract solution patterns. A general-purpose assistant, however, encounters all of these simultaneously, and no fixed extraction schema works across the full breadth. The field lacks a benchmark that spans this heterogeneity, and existing self-evolving frameworks — which iteratively refine the extraction prompt from task feedback — were designed for homogeneous distributions. Under heterogeneous training data, these methods exhibit a characteristic failure: updating too frequently causes successive batches of dissimilar examples to pull the prompt in conflicting directions; updating too infrequently washes out fine-grained task signals by averaging over dissimilar scenarios. Key Idea: The core insight is that memory extraction tasks should be clustered by extraction scenario (what type of information must be extracted and what makes extraction hard) rather than by surface task, domain, or dataset label. This allows per-cluster analysis to produce targeted, non-conflicting prompt improvements that can then be synthesized into a single coherent prompt covering all scenarios. Two examples from different datasets (e.g., an agentic trajectory and a math solution) may both require extracting multi-step procedural knowledge, and grouping them together surfaces shared extraction challenges that neither dataset-specific nor fully mixed analysis would reveal. Methodology: The paper formalizes single-step memory extraction as a triple (source conversation c, target query q, reward function R): an extraction model LLM_e produces a memory string m conditioned on an extraction prompt P; a generation model LLM_g answers q given m; quality is measured by R(answer). BEHEMOTH is constructed by repurposing 18 existing datasets into this formulation across three categories — personalization (5 datasets: MemBench-High/Low, PersonaMem-v2, PrefEval, LongMemEval), problem-solving (7: Game of 24, AIME, MMLU Pro Physics/Engineering, MathEquationBalancer, BigCodeBench, GPQA-Diamond), and agentic (6: AlfWorld, FEVER, PDDL, BabyAI, ScienceWorld, ToolBench). Problem-solving and agentic datasets lack multi-turn conversations, so LLM_g is prompted to generate source conversations from task instances; this introduces synthetic data at the source-conversation level. Training uses 20 examples per dataset (330 total, randomly shuffled); held-out OOD test sets are one dataset per category (LongMemEval, GPQA-Diamond, ToolBench). CluE operates in rounds. In each round: (1) Summarizer — an LLM reads each training log and writes a 2–3 sentence extraction scenario summary focusing on what type of information is needed and what makes extraction hard, abstracting away domain specifics; (2) Cluster Manager — an LLM assigns each example to one of up to 7 clusters based on extraction scenario similarity, with the ability to create, merge, or split clusters as distributions evolve; (3) Cluster Analyzer — for each cluster, an LLM with tool access inspects that cluster's training logs, identifies success and failure patterns, and produces targeted prompt recommendations; (4) Proposer — an LLM synthesizes per-cluster analyses into one improved extraction prompt, resolving conflicts by scoping recommendations to appropriate memory categories. CluE uses the same batch size (35) and tournament-based evaluation as MemEvolve, adding 221 optimization LLM calls over 5 rounds (~5.5h wall time). Evaluation uses Macro Accuracy (uniform average across datasets) and Relative Gain (geometric mean of per-dataset improvement ratios over Simple baseline), with three evaluation runs to report mean ± standard deviation. Key Results: On in-distribution test sets starting from the Simple seed (Qwen3-32B), CluE achieves 50.01% macro accuracy and +9.04% relative gain over Simple (46.00%). The three competing self-evolving methods all exhibit category tradeoffs: GEPA reaches +14.08% agentic but −2.16% personalization; MemEvolve reaches +10.76% personalization but −3.25% agentic; ACE shows +8.04% problem-solving but −4.38% personalization. CluE is the only method with positive relative gain in all three categories simultaneously. Starting from the stronger Survey seed, all baseline methods produce negative or flat overall relative gains (ACE: −1.44%, MemEvolve: −0.74%, GEPA: 0%), while CluE achieves +6.54% overall and positive gains in all three categories. On OOD test sets, CluE achieves 63.07% on LongMemEval (vs. 56.82% for MemEvolve), 48.48% on GPQA-Diamond (above the Simple baseline of 47.14%), and 26.67% on ToolBench. With Gemini-3-Flash as extraction backend, CluE achieves +3.40% relative gain; all three baselines either regress or show marginal improvement (ACE: −3.93%, MemEvolve: −0.29%, GEPA: +0.93%). In the continual memory setting, CluE (50.83 on Game of 24; 67.25 on AlfWorld) outperforms MemEvolve (43.33; 62.57). Qualitatively, CluE's evolved prompt contains 936 tokens — shorter than ACE's 1,403 tokens — and develops a structured 5-category memory taxonomy with per-category extraction guidelines. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The formalization of heterogeneous memory extraction as a distinct generalization challenge is a new problem framing. BEHEMOTH is a new benchmark (repurposed but systematically unified under one protocol). CluE's cluster-by-extraction-scenario evolution strategy is a new method in the self-evolving prompt optimization space. The core novelty is the scenario-clustering insight, which is non-obvious: prior self-evolving methods were aware of task heterogeneity but attempted to handle it through architectural variants (validation sets in GEPA, atomic operations in ACE) rather than by restructuring the feedback aggregation itself. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are limited — the paper does not ablate the effect of removing any single CluE component (e.g., Summarizer vs. Cluster Manager vs. Proposer), so it is not established which pipeline stage is responsible for performance gains. Error bars (standard deviations over three runs) are reported for evolving framework results but not for static prompt baselines, which are run once. The evaluation is downstream-utility-driven (avoiding LLM-as-judge bias), which is a sound design choice. The benchmark construction has a potential concern: problem-solving and agentic source conversations are LLM-generated by LLM_g (Qwen3-32B), meaning the source conversations and the answering model share characteristics; this could inflate performance for all methods on those categories relative to real-world deployment. Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/ayyyq/heterogeneous-memory-extraction. This is a preprint; peer review has not been completed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (system evaluated through experiments). - Practical: CluE requires only API-level or locally hosted LLM access (tested with Qwen3-32B and Gemini-3-Flash); no specialized hardware beyond what runs the extraction and generation models. - Efficient: CluE costs ~5.5h wall time and 221 optimization LLM calls over 5 rounds, marginally more than MemEvolve (~5.0h, 30 calls) but substantially less than ACE (~12.4h, 610 calls); the overhead is one-time prompt optimization, not per-inference cost. - Effective: Evidence shows CluE improves downstream task performance across all three task categories on both in-distribution and OOD test sets, with the advantage holding in a continual memory setting on two tasks; the proxy (single-step extraction evaluated by downstream reward) is well-motivated but not identical to real multi-session deployment. - Scalable: Training uses only 330 examples; scaling to larger training sets or to the full memory lifecycle (storage, retrieval, management) is explicitly left to future work and not tested. The work delivers code, data, and evolved prompts as software artifacts. Strengths: - The cluster-by-extraction-scenario insight is intellectually crisp and solves a concrete failure mode (conflicting gradient-like signals under heterogeneous data) that the authors demonstrate empirically in competing methods. - BEHEMOTH's unified evaluation protocol for 18 heterogeneous datasets under a single downstream-utility metric is a genuine infrastructure contribution that enables fair comparison across methods with different extraction philosophies. - The stronger-seed experiments (Table 4) are a particularly stringent test: CluE is the only method that improves over a well-engineered Survey prompt, while all others degrade it — a result that distinguishes CluE's stability from methods that simply overwrite good rules. - The evolved prompt analysis (Appendix B.3) traces exactly how clusters merge/split over rounds and how cluster insights map to taxonomy sections, providing mechanistic transparency uncommon in prompt optimization papers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that BEHEMOTH's 18 datasets may not cover the full complexity of real-world deployments, which can involve longer interaction histories and greater task diversity. They explicitly scope the work to memory extraction (the first stage of the memory lifecycle) and acknowledge that generalizing to storage, retrieval, and management remains open. They also note that continual evolution directly under the continual heterogeneous regime is left to future work. These are stated directly in Section 7, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No component ablation: the paper does not isolate which of the four CluE stages (Summarizer, Cluster Manager, Cluster Analyzer, Proposer) drives gains. A version without the Summarizer (clustering raw logs directly) or without per-cluster analysis would clarify the contribution. - Synthetic source conversations: problem-solving and agentic source conversations are generated by the same Qwen3-32B model used as LLM_g. This creates a distributional shortcut where the extraction model and the generation model share stylistic priors. No experiment tests CluE on truly human-generated problem-solving or agentic conversations. - Only 20 training examples per dataset: while the shuffled regime is realistic, all methods are trained on a tiny signal. It is not established whether the relative ordering of methods holds at larger training scales. - CluE with Gemini-3-Flash shows −5.93% agentic regression (Table 7), which the paper attributes to ceiling effects but does not analyze in depth. This is a meaningful failure mode for a method claiming robust cross-category generalization. - The cross-model generalization test changes only the extraction backend, not the optimization model; a test where evolution is performed with one LLM and the evolved prompt is deployed with a different backbone would be more informative for real adoption. Bottom Line: Practitioners building general-purpose persistent LLM assistants will find both the BEHEMOTH benchmark and the cluster-by-scenario evolution strategy directly useful, and the consistent cross-category gains distinguish this work meaningfully from prior self-evolving methods that trade off categories against each other.

SweetSpot: An Analytical Model for Predicting Energy Efficiency of LLM Inference

icpe2026.spec.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SweetSpot, an analytical model derived from Transformer FLOPs and memory-access complexity that predicts per-token energy efficiency of LLM inference as a closed-form function of input and output sequence lengths, and analytically locates the efficiency-maximizing (n_in, n_out) configuration. - Why it's important: LLM inference accounts for up to 90% of model lifecycle energy, yet commercial providers charge linearly per token — a pricing and deployment model this work shows is physically incorrect, with best-vs-worst configurations differing by 33.41× in energy efficiency. - How it does it: It derives per-token energy E_tok(n_in, n_out) = θ0 + θ1·n²_in/n_out + θ2·n_in + θ3·n_in/n_out + θ4·n_out + θ5/n_out by separately accounting for quadratic-input prefill FLOPs, linear-in-context decode FLOPs, and KV-cache memory traffic, then fits six scalar parameters per model to measured GPU power traces. - The good: Across all 13 models, SweetSpot achieves a mean MAPE of 1.79% (±0.61) versus 31.40% for the next-best baseline, with all five core parameters (θ0–θ4) statistically significant at p < 0.001 in 100% of models. - The missing: The model fixes batch size at 1024 and is only fitted and evaluated on the same grid of (n_in, n_out) pairs used to derive the parameters — there is no held-out generalization test, so true prediction accuracy on unseen configurations is undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners deploying LLM inference infrastructure who need an analytical handle on energy-optimal prompt and generation length targeting; less essential for researchers focused on training-time efficiency or hardware design.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SweetSpot, an analytical model derived from Transformer FLOPs and memory-access complexity that predicts per-token energy efficiency of LLM inference as a closed-form function of input and output sequence lengths, and analytically locates the efficiency-maximizing (n_in, n_out) configuration. - Why it's important: LLM inference accounts for up to 90% of model lifecycle energy, yet commercial providers charge linearly per token — a pricing and deployment model this work shows is physically incorrect, with best-vs-worst configurations differing by 33.41× in energy efficiency. - How it does it: It derives per-token energy E_tok(n_in, n_out) = θ0 + θ1·n²_in/n_out + θ2·n_in + θ3·n_in/n_out + θ4·n_out + θ5/n_out by separately accounting for quadratic-input prefill FLOPs, linear-in-context decode FLOPs, and KV-cache memory traffic, then fits six scalar parameters per model to measured GPU power traces. - The good: Across all 13 models, SweetSpot achieves a mean MAPE of 1.79% (±0.61) versus 31.40% for the next-best baseline, with all five core parameters (θ0–θ4) statistically significant at p < 0.001 in 100% of models. - The missing: The model fixes batch size at 1024 and is only fitted and evaluated on the same grid of (n_in, n_out) pairs used to derive the parameters — there is no held-out generalization test, so true prediction accuracy on unseen configurations is undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners deploying LLM inference infrastructure who need an analytical handle on energy-optimal prompt and generation length targeting; less essential for researchers focused on training-time efficiency or hardware design. Problem & Motivation: LLM inference dominates datacenter AI workloads and is estimated to constitute 90% of model lifecycle energy consumption, yet its energy cost is modeled and priced as a simple linear function of input plus output tokens. This linear assumption conflicts with the known computational structure of Transformers: the prefill phase incurs quadratic cost in input length due to attention, while the decode phase incurs linear per-step cost that grows with context length. No prior analytical model correctly characterizes the resulting non-monotonic efficiency landscape. Production systems are therefore unable to reason about energy trade-offs when deciding on prompt length, output budget, or batching strategy. Key Idea: Per-token energy has a minimum with respect to output length for any fixed input length: the quadratic prefill cost is amortized over more tokens as n_out grows (reducing per-token cost), but the linear decode cost eventually dominates (increasing per-token cost). Setting the derivative of E_tok with respect to n_out to zero yields a closed-form optimal output length n*_out = sqrt((θ1·n²_in + θ3·n_in + θ5) / θ4), which defines the efficiency sweet spot as a function of input length and six fitted model-specific scalars. Methodology: The model is built in two stages. First, a FLOPs-only version derives E_tok by computing per-layer prefill FLOPs (dominated by quadratic attention: 4n²_in·d per layer) and decode FLOPs (linear in context length per generated token), summing over L layers, and normalizing by n_out to get a five-term polynomial in n_in and n_out. Second, the full SweetSpot model adds a memory-access analysis of the KV-cache traffic during prefill (reading/writing n²_in·n_q elements per attention layer) and decode (reading (n_in + t − 1) cached KV entries per step), introducing a sixth term θ5/n_out from constant prefill memory overhead. Parameters θ0–θ5 are fitted per model using nonlinear least squares against measured GPU energy traces. Four baselines are compared: constant E_tok (Baseline 1), E_tok ∝ 1/n_out (Baseline 2), E_tok ∝ 1/(n_in + n_out) (Baseline 3), and the Wilkins et al. linear total-energy model normalized by n_out (Baseline 4). Critical design decisions: (1) per-output-token normalization to expose the amortization structure — well-justified theoretically; (2) memory traffic decomposition alongside FLOPs — justified by the 18.6% average MAPE reduction it delivers; (3) single-GPU, fixed-batch-size evaluation to isolate workload geometry effects — reasonable but limits scope. Key Results: SweetSpot achieves a mean MAPE of 1.79% (±0.61) across 13 LLMs ranging from 1B to 9B parameters, compared to 130.45%/130.49% for the output-only baselines, 106.25% for the (n_in + n_out) baseline, and 31.40% for the Wilkins et al. linear model. All five primary parameters (θ0–θ4) are significant at p < 0.001 in all 13 models; θ5 (memory constant term) is significant at p < 0.001 in 8/13 models. The aggregated heatmap across 13 models shows peak efficiency universally at n_in = 64, n_out ∈ [128, 256], with worst-case at n_in = 4096, n_out = 64, a 33.41× (±4.90) gap. GQA models are substantially more efficient than comparable MHA models: OPT 1.3B (MHA) is 42% less efficient than Qwen2 1.5B (GQA 6×) despite fewer parameters. Llama 3.2 1B reaches 120.71 tokens/joule at its peak; Falcon-RW 7B reaches only 18.78 tokens/joule at its peak. Predicted vs. measured peak configurations (Table 5) match well in n_in (universally 64) but show notable error in n_out prediction for some models (e.g., Gemma 2 9B: predicted 226, measured 64). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The novel contribution is the derivation of a physically-grounded analytical form for per-token energy efficiency that correctly captures the non-linear prefill–decode interplay and yields a closed-form optimality condition; prior work either used purely linear total-energy models or coarse empirical metrics. The empirical finding that efficiency peaks cluster at short inputs (n_in = 64) and medium outputs (128–256 tokens) across diverse architectures — with a 33× best-vs-worst gap — is the key new result. The fitting methodology (nonlinear least squares applied to an analytically-motivated function form) is standard. Rigor & Reproducibility: The dataset is publicly released on Zenodo (doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18714476). Fitted θ parameters for all 13 models are provided in Table 5, enabling reproduction of peak-configuration predictions. Parameter significance is reported via p-values across all 13 models (Table 4). However, there is no train/test split: all (n_in, n_out) grid points are used both for fitting and evaluation, so the reported MAPE measures in-sample fit rather than predictive generalization to unseen sequence lengths. No error bars are reported on the MAPE values across experimental repetitions (only across models). The experiment fixes max_batch_size = 1024 and N_req = 1000 — these choices are justified empirically (Figure 1, Figure 5) but their interaction with the analytical model is not incorporated into the model structure. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (measurements on real hardware plus an analytical model fit to those measurements). - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU using TensorRT-LLM v0.21.0; the measurement and fitting pipeline requires only pynvml power sampling and standard nonlinear least squares — commercially accessible. - Efficient: Model fitting is negligible overhead (six-parameter regression per LLM); online use requires only evaluating a closed-form expression, so deployment cost is essentially zero. - Effective: SweetSpot achieves 1.79% MAPE in-sample and correctly identifies peak efficiency configurations to within ≤100 tokens of measured optima for most models, though Gemma 2 9B is a notable exception (predicted 226 vs. measured 64 tokens); evidence supports the main empirical claim with that caveat. - Scalable: Only tested on 1B–9B parameter models on a single H100; the paper explicitly states that larger models may exhibit different memory footprint behavior and are deferred to future work — no extrapolation evidence is provided. The paper delivers no software artifact; it provides a model formulation, fitted parameter tables, and a public synthetic dataset. Strengths: - The analytical derivation is physically principled: starting from first-principles FLOPs and memory-access accounting of the Transformer architecture and arriving at a model form that matches data at 1.79% MAPE is a genuine contribution, not a black-box regression. - The closed-form optimality condition (Eq. 6) is actionable: it gives production systems a tractable formula for computing energy-optimal output length given an input length and six model-specific constants. - Coverage across 13 LLMs spanning 4 attention mechanism variants (MHA, GQA 2×/4×/6×/7×) and 3 parameter scales, all validated on the same hardware under controlled conditions, makes the efficiency landscape results credible and broadly applicable within the tested scope. - The identification that GQA grouping ratio (not just parameter count) is a primary determinant of energy efficiency is a concrete, actionable finding for architecture selection in energy-constrained deployments. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: (1) the model scope is limited to 1B–9B models, which may not generalize to larger LLMs; (2) experiments use a single hardware platform (H100), limiting hardware generalizability; (3) only TensorRT-LLM is evaluated, excluding vLLM, DeepSpeed, and others; (4) batch size is not incorporated into the model and is fixed at 1024, which Section 6.2 shows substantially affects efficiency values and peak locations. These are confronted directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The MAPE evaluation is in-sample: the same 8×8 grid of (n_in, n_out) pairs used to fit the six θ parameters is also used to compute MAPE. A proper evaluation would hold out a fraction of the grid (or interpolate to off-grid lengths) to test generalization — without this, the 1.79% figure measures fitting quality, not predictive accuracy. - Table 5 reveals that for Gemma 2 9B, the predicted peak n_out is 226 while the measured peak is 64 — a qualitative error (wrong quadrant of the efficiency surface). The paper does not discuss this failure case. - All experiments use synthetically generated datasets with zero-variance fixed token lengths. Real-world inference request distributions have high variance in both dimensions, and efficiency under such workloads may differ from the controlled grid. - The 33.41× efficiency claim compares the absolute best configuration (n_in=64, n_out≈256) to the absolute worst (n_in=4096, n_out=64). Real workloads rarely span this entire range, and the practical gain from alignment strategies targeting the sweet spot rather than the actual workload distribution is not quantified. - The model is validated only at the 64–4096 token range in powers of two; behavior at intermediate lengths (e.g., 300 or 800 tokens) is not directly verified. Bottom Line: This is a clean, well-scoped piece of work that provides the first physically-grounded analytical model for LLM inference energy efficiency with strong in-sample fit (1.79% MAPE) and a practically deployable closed-form optimality criterion — worth reading for anyone building energy-aware LLM serving systems, with the caveat that the evaluation conflates fitting and generalization performance.

An Agentic Approach to Metadata Reasoning

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Metadata Reasoner, an LLM-orchestrated agentic system that selects a sufficient and minimal set of tables from an enterprise data lake to answer a given analytical task. - Why it's important: Ranking-based retrieval (vector search, BM25, Pneuma) scores tables individually and cannot reason about multi-table combinations, join validity, data quality, or lineage — causing it to fail when tasks require integrating several sources from noisy, large-scale data lakes. - How it does it: The system stages metadata across three tiers — discrimination-oriented vector embeddings for initial candidate retrieval, LLM-generated statistical summaries attached at retrieval time, and on-demand tool calls (data_finder, joinability_check, column_profiler) — so the LLM agent consumes only the metadata fragments needed at each reasoning step. - The good: On KramaBench, the system achieves 83.16% average F1 vs. 50.77% for vector search and 45.12% for Pneuma, with the gap widest in the 1,498-table Astronomy domain (72.31% vs. 32.80%); downstream Text-to-SQL accuracy improves from 56.38% to 71.28%. - The missing: Inference cost and latency of the multi-step agentic loop are never reported, making it impossible to assess the efficiency trade-off against single-pass retrieval baselines. - Read it or not: Database engineers and agentic-systems researchers building data discovery pipelines should read it; the three-tier metadata staging design and the failure-case taxonomy are both concrete and actionable.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Metadata Reasoner, an LLM-orchestrated agentic system that selects a sufficient and minimal set of tables from an enterprise data lake to answer a given analytical task. - Why it's important: Ranking-based retrieval (vector search, BM25, Pneuma) scores tables individually and cannot reason about multi-table combinations, join validity, data quality, or lineage — causing it to fail when tasks require integrating several sources from noisy, large-scale data lakes. - How it does it: The system stages metadata across three tiers — discrimination-oriented vector embeddings for initial candidate retrieval, LLM-generated statistical summaries attached at retrieval time, and on-demand tool calls (data_finder, joinability_check, column_profiler) — so the LLM agent consumes only the metadata fragments needed at each reasoning step. - The good: On KramaBench, the system achieves 83.16% average F1 vs. 50.77% for vector search and 45.12% for Pneuma, with the gap widest in the 1,498-table Astronomy domain (72.31% vs. 32.80%); downstream Text-to-SQL accuracy improves from 56.38% to 71.28%. - The missing: Inference cost and latency of the multi-step agentic loop are never reported, making it impossible to assess the efficiency trade-off against single-pass retrieval baselines. - Read it or not: Database engineers and agentic-systems researchers building data discovery pipelines should read it; the three-tier metadata staging design and the failure-case taxonomy are both concrete and actionable. Problem & Motivation: Enterprise data lakes contain thousands of heterogeneous tables. When an LLM agent must answer an analytical question (SQL query, ML prediction task), it must first identify the right subset of tables. The problem is harder than single-document retrieval: the agent must find combinations of tables that jointly cover all required entities and attributes, verify that tables can be joined correctly, reason about data quality and lineage, and do so without overwhelming the context window. Existing approaches — keyword-based retrieval, vector search, or hybrid systems like Pneuma — rank individual tables by topic relevance and cannot capture whether a retrieved set is jointly sufficient, joinable, or free of quality defects. Expanding the context window to include all metadata is computationally prohibitive and degrades reasoning quality due to attention dilution in long contexts. Key Idea: The central insight is that different types of metadata are optimal at different stages of reasoning: coarse vector retrieval needs discrimination-oriented embeddings that highlight inter-table differences rather than shared high-level descriptions; per-table reasoning needs attached statistical summaries; and join/value verification needs on-demand tool calls. By routing each metadata type to the stage where it is most informative, the agent avoids both context saturation and the latency of precomputing all pairwise metadata signals (e.g., all join paths). Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. (1) Query decomposition: a Gemini-3-Flash LLM agent decomposes the analytical task into named entities, measures, temporal scope, and granularity constraints, producing a structured search plan. (2) Discrimination-oriented semantic search: metadata embeddings are constructed via a two-stage process — tables are grouped by semantic relatedness, and an LLM generates group-relative descriptions that emphasize distinguishing features (e.g., specific temporal ranges for geomagnetic forecast tables with identical schemas).
