A state-aware deduplication mechanism suppresses repeated metadata from tables already surfaced, replacing them with recurrence indicators. (3) Reasoning over attached metadata: each retrieved table is accompanied by schema information plus LLM-generated statistical summaries (min/max, top-K values, null ratios, cardinality). (4) On-the-fly tool calls: three specialized tools are invoked when attached metadata is insufficient — column_profiler() for fine-grained column histograms, data_finder() for deterministic value-level presence verification, and joinability_check() for foreign-key overlap between specific candidate pairs. The agent iterates between search and tool calls until it declares a sufficient and minimal set. Critical design decisions: the partition between attached vs. on-the-fly metadata is explicitly tunable (and validated by ablation); the search engine itself uses the same high-performance vector engine (text-embedding-005 with semantic reranking) as the baselines, so improvements are attributable to the agentic reasoning layer, not embedding quality alone. Key Results: On KramaBench (6 real-world domains, up to 1,498 tables), the full system achieves 83.16% average F1 (84.44% recall, 86.63% precision) using an average of 10.10 reasoning steps per task, versus 50.77% for vector search and 45.12% for Pneuma. The gain is largest in the high-cardinality Astronomy domain: 72.31% vs. 32.80% (vector) and 27.70% (Pneuma). On a synthetically scaled BIRD data lake with injected partitions, duplicates, and quality degradation (up to 208 tables per database), the system achieves 85.5% average F1 vs. 30.0% for Top-10 vector retrieval; 99.0% of selected tables are noise-free vs. 64.4% for the baseline. In a downstream Text-to-SQL experiment, MR-selected tables yield 71.28% SQL execution accuracy vs. 56.38% for Top-10 vector results across five BIRD databases, with individual gains ranging from +8.62 to +21.47 points. Ablation confirms that attached metadata provides the largest single improvement (73.43% → 79.66% F1) while simultaneously reducing steps from 13.51 to 8.36; adding on-the-fly tools without attached metadata causes steps to nearly triple (24.14) due to trial-and-error search. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The orchestration of three staged metadata tiers within an agentic loop for data-source selection is the primary contribution; discrimination-oriented embedding construction (group-aware meta-prompting to generate contrastive table descriptions) is a secondary technical novelty. Individual components — LLM tool use, vector search, statistical table profiling — are known; the contribution lies in their principled integration and the empirical demonstration that staged metadata access is both more accurate and more efficient than providing all metadata upfront or on-demand alone. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover four system variants and are run over five seeds with standard deviations reported. Baselines include a strong vector-search engine (text-embedding-005 with semantic reranker) and Pneuma (a recent LLM-hybrid system), both evaluated at their best retrieval threshold K — this is a fair comparison protocol. KramaBench is an external benchmark not created by the authors, providing independent evaluation. The synthetic BIRD dataset is author-created, which introduces Goodhart's Law risk: the noise types injected (lineage suffixes, quality flags) are precisely those the system is designed to reason about, so strong performance on this benchmark may partly reflect design alignment rather than general robustness. No code, model weights, or data are stated as publicly released. The backbone model (Gemini-3-Flash) is a proprietary Google model referenced via an arXiv preprint, making exact reproduction impossible. Inference cost and latency are not reported, which is a significant omission for an agentic system that makes multiple LLM calls per task. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Requires cloud API access to a large LLM (Gemini-3-Flash) and embedding model (text-embedding-005); not deployable on commodity on-premise hardware without equivalent API infrastructure. - Efficient: Average 10.10 LLM calls per task for the full system; the paper does not report wall-clock time or token cost, making it impossible to quantify the efficiency overhead relative to single-pass retrieval baselines. - Effective: The evidence is strong on both benchmarks — 32-point F1 gain over the best baseline on KramaBench, 99% noise-free table selection on synthetic BIRD, and a 14.9-point average gain in downstream SQL execution accuracy. - Scalable: The largest tested environment is 1,498 tables (Astronomy domain); enterprise data warehouses commonly contain tens of thousands of tables. Scalability beyond ~1,500 tables is claimed but not tested. The system exists as a working implementation using the Google ADK framework but is not released as a public artifact. Strengths: - The three-tier metadata staging design is principled and directly motivated by the context saturation problem; the ablation provides clean evidence that each tier contributes independently, with attached metadata providing the best accuracy-efficiency trade-off. - Discrimination-oriented embedding construction (group-aware meta-prompting to generate contrastive descriptions) is a concrete, reusable technique that yields +15 point Recall@5 gains in the Astronomy domain vs. schema-only embeddings at no inference-time cost. - Using KramaBench as the primary benchmark (external, multi-domain, real-world data lake with up to 1,498 tables) provides credible independent evidence; the 32-point margin over Pneuma is large and consistent across domains. - The failure-case taxonomy in Section 5.6 (5 failure modes: missing relation dependencies, granularity mismatch, multi-stage planning failure, redundant relation selection, incomplete partition retrieval) is analytically rigorous and provides a clear agenda for follow-on work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly analyze 126 failure cases from KramaBench and attribute them to five concrete failure modes (Section 5.6). They acknowledge limitations in handling multi-stage dependent query planning, granularity inference, exhaustive partition retrieval, and redundant relation filtering. Future work directions include unstructured data support, web search integration, and metadata quality improvement. The authors do not acknowledge the cost/latency issue, the synthetic benchmark circularity risk, or the replication barrier posed by the proprietary backbone. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Inference cost not quantified: the agentic loop averages 10.10 LLM calls per query; comparing to baselines that make one call without reporting token budget or latency makes the efficiency trade-off opaque and is the most important missing analysis for practitioners. - Synthetic BIRD benchmark circularity: the authors designed the noise injection scheme and the system's quality-reasoning tools simultaneously; strong performance on this benchmark is weaker evidence than performance on an independently constructed noisy benchmark. - Proprietary backbone: all experiments use Gemini-3-Flash (and Gemini-3-Pro for SQL generation), which is unreleased; no ablation with open-weights models (e.g., Llama, Mistral) is provided, making it unknown whether the approach generalizes beyond Google's models. - No human upper-bound on KramaBench: 83.16% F1 is uncontextualized — it is unclear whether the remaining 17% error reflects inherent task ambiguity or system fixable failures. - Scale gap: the 1,498-table Astronomy domain is the largest tested; there is no evidence the approach holds at 10,000–100,000 table scale typical of large enterprise data warehouses, where the planning and deduplication logic may not scale. - Downstream SQL experiment confounds table selection with justification quality: the MR configuration also passes the natural-language justification to the SQL generator, which is not available in the vector search condition, potentially inflating the downstream accuracy improvement attributable to table selection alone. Bottom Line: This is a solid systems paper with a principled design and convincing empirical results on real-world benchmarks; database engineers building LLM-powered data discovery pipelines will find both the three-tier metadata staging architecture and the discrimination-oriented embedding construction directly applicable, but the absence of cost analysis and the reliance on unreleased proprietary models leave the most important deployment questions unanswered.

Thermal-GEMs: Generalized Models for Building Thermal Dynamics

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the first comprehensive empirical comparison of multi-source general models (MSGMs) versus time series foundation models (TSFMs) for building indoor temperature forecasting, validated on real-world data across three datasets and four MSGM architectures, and identifies a threshold of 16 same-sensor source buildings for MSGMs to consistently outperform TSFMs. - Why it's important: Buildings require months-to-years of sensor data to fit accurate thermal models, yet each building is unique — an unsolved cold-start problem that blocks deployment of MPC and fault-detection systems in real estates. - How it does it: MSGMs are trained on shuffled windows from up to 200 heterogeneous source buildings and applied zero-shot (or with minimal fine-tuning) to unseen target buildings, with a seasonal four-split evaluation scheme that averages performance across spring, summer, autumn, and winter. - The good: MSGMs reduce average MAE by 33–63% over single-source TL across all 40 real-world Ecobee target buildings, with larger gains in lower-data regimes, and the threshold experiment (1–128 source buildings) provides a directly actionable deployment decision rule. - The missing: TSFMs are tested exclusively in zero-shot mode — no LoRA or curriculum fine-tuning is evaluated, which prior work has shown changes the ranking, leaving the MSGM vs. fine-tuned TSFM comparison unaddressed. - Read it or not: Practitioners deploying building energy management systems and researchers working on data-efficient time series forecasting for the built environment should read this; it provides concrete, actionable guidance on when to choose an MSGM over a TSFM based on available source buildings.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the first comprehensive empirical comparison of multi-source general models (MSGMs) versus time series foundation models (TSFMs) for building indoor temperature forecasting, validated on real-world data across three datasets and four MSGM architectures, and identifies a threshold of 16 same-sensor source buildings for MSGMs to consistently outperform TSFMs. - Why it's important: Buildings require months-to-years of sensor data to fit accurate thermal models, yet each building is unique — an unsolved cold-start problem that blocks deployment of MPC and fault-detection systems in real estates. - How it does it: MSGMs are trained on shuffled windows from up to 200 heterogeneous source buildings and applied zero-shot (or with minimal fine-tuning) to unseen target buildings, with a seasonal four-split evaluation scheme that averages performance across spring, summer, autumn, and winter. - The good: MSGMs reduce average MAE by 33–63% over single-source TL across all 40 real-world Ecobee target buildings, with larger gains in lower-data regimes, and the threshold experiment (1–128 source buildings) provides a directly actionable deployment decision rule. - The missing: TSFMs are tested exclusively in zero-shot mode — no LoRA or curriculum fine-tuning is evaluated, which prior work has shown changes the ranking, leaving the MSGM vs. fine-tuned TSFM comparison unaddressed. - Read it or not: Practitioners deploying building energy management systems and researchers working on data-efficient time series forecasting for the built environment should read this; it provides concrete, actionable guidance on when to choose an MSGM over a TSFM based on available source buildings. Problem & Motivation: Building operators who wish to deploy model-predictive control or fault-detection systems face a universal cold-start problem: each building has its own thermal signature driven by construction year, envelope parameters, occupancy, climate zone, and HVAC configuration, yet accurate ML-based thermal models require months to years of logged sensor data that does not exist at installation time. Transfer learning from a single source building mitigates this partially but inherits sensitivity to source-target mismatch. Multi-source general models (MSGMs) trained across many buildings promise better generalization, but prior work (Raisch et al. 2025) demonstrated MSGMs only on synthetic data. Independently, time series foundation models pre-trained on large heterogeneous corpora have been proposed as an alternative for sparse-data settings, but no head-to-head comparison between MSGMs and TSFMs for building thermal dynamics existed, and the practically critical question — how many same-sensor source buildings does a deployer need before MSGMs beat TSFMs — was unanswered. Key Idea: The core insight is that a model pre-trained on shuffled sliding windows from hundreds of heterogeneous buildings learns a general-purpose thermal dynamics representation that transfers zero-shot to unseen buildings better than both single-building pre-training and domain-agnostic TSFMs, because the MSGM is exclusively exposed to building physics while TSFMs are diluted across unrelated domains. The secondary insight is that this advantage has a data threshold: below ~16 same-sensor buildings, the MSGM's domain specificity cannot overcome the TSFM's breadth. Methodology: The pipeline compares three strategies — single-source TL, MSGM, and zero-shot TSFM — on 1-hour-ahead indoor temperature forecasting (4 × 15-minute steps, 96-step lookback). Three datasets are used: D1 EcoBee (real-world, 932 North American homes, 5 climate zones), D2 Ideal Households (real-world, 32 UK homes, 255 rooms), D3 European Households (non-public simulated, 450 Central European homes built 1949–present). Each dataset is split into source (88–96%) and target (4–11%) buildings; the target set is stratified by location. Data is downsampled to 15-minute intervals. Four MSGM architectures are evaluated: LSTM (baseline from prior work), standard Transformer with cosine positional embedding, Mamba (structured state-space model), and xLSTM. Hyperparameters are tuned per architecture per dataset using Bayesian optimization with Optuna; each configuration is trained 4 times and the best validation-RMSE model is selected. Source training uses up to 200 time series (180 train / 20 val), AdamW optimizer. Three TSFMs are evaluated zero-shot: Chronos-2 (encoder Transformer, synthetic pretraining), TimesFM 2.5 (decoder-only autoregressive, univariate mode), Toto (decoder-only, 151M parameters, 2T training points). Evaluation follows a four-way seasonal fine-tuning split: each year is partitioned into spring/summer/autumn/winter segments, each with a variable training set (0.5–2.5 months), 0.5-month validation, and 3-month test. Final MAE and RMSE are averaged across four seasons per target building. Three critical design decisions: (1) weight initialization (not layer freezing) for fine-tuning — justified by prior ablation in [38]; (2) zero-shot deployment mode for MSGMs on real-world data, motivated by the finding that fine-tuning increases error; (3) reporting best-of-4 runs for MSGM with ≤16 sources in the sparsity experiment — favorably biases MSGM in low-source regime. Key Results: Architecture ablation (zero-shot, all-in-one scenario): LSTM and Transformer outperform Mamba and xLSTM; Mamba is the clear outlier. Architecture choice among LSTM/Transformer/xLSTM has minor effect. Best architecture is dataset-dependent: Transformer for European Households, LSTM for EcoBee and Ideal. Average 1-hour MAE is ~0.1 K on Ideal and Euro datasets, ~0.25 K on EcoBee. MSGM vs. single-source TL (EcoBee, real-world): MSGM outperforms single-source TL on all 40 target buildings across all four data-sparsity scenarios. Zero-shot: MSGM MAE 0.254 K vs. single-source 0.683 K (63% reduction); RMSE 0.382 vs. 1.089 K (65% reduction). With 2.5 months of fine-tuning data, single-source TL MAE drops to 0.475 K, still well above the zero-shot MSGM at 0.269 K. Surprisingly, fine-tuning the MSGM on real-world data consistently increases error regardless of fine-tuning data volume; the authors hypothesize seasonal overfitting but do not verify this. MSGM vs. TSFMs (all three datasets, zero-shot): MSGM outperforms all three TSFMs on average across all datasets. TSFMs exceed the MSGM only on 12.5% of EcoBee buildings and 8% of Euro buildings by MAE; they lose on all Ideal buildings. Chronos wins best average rank across TSFMs and datasets. Unexpectedly, univariate TimesFM outperforms multivariate Chronos and Toto on the Ideal dataset — the authors attribute this to multivariate TSFMs failing to capture building thermal inertia from heating power signals. Same-sensor building sparsity (1–128 sources, 2× power steps): MSGM performance improves monotonically with source count, with diminishing returns above 64 sources. MSGM crosses the TSFM baseline at 16 sources (EcoBee, Euro) and 32 sources (Ideal) for MAE; at only 2–8 sources for RMSE. The Ideal dataset requires more sources because rooms from the same building introduce within-building homogeneity that limits diversity benefit. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW BENCHMARK EVALUATION. The genuine novelty is the first real-world validation of multi-source TL for building thermal dynamics and the first head-to-head MSGM vs. TSFM comparison on this task. The source-count threshold experiment is a novel empirical contribution with direct practical value. The architectures, TSFMs, and transfer learning protocol are all drawn from existing work; this paper's contribution is the empirical synthesis and the actionable threshold finding. Rigor & Reproducibility: The seasonal fine-tuning split with 4-way averaging is a meaningful robustness improvement over single-season evaluations in prior work. Models are trained 4 times from different random seeds; the best is selected rather than averaged, which inflates reported MSGM performance. No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported for cross-building comparisons, making it impossible to judge whether the MSGM's wins on individual buildings are meaningful or noise. The EcoBee target set is only 40 buildings (4.3% of available data). For the source-count experiment with ≤16 sources, best-of-4-trials reporting selects the luckiest random sample, creating an optimistic bias for MSGMs in the critical low-data regime. Code is promised on GitHub [anonymous — anonymized for review]. The European Households dataset is non-public, limiting full reproducibility for that split. Both real-world datasets (EcoBee, Ideal) are publicly available. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL — multiple trained models evaluated across real and simulated building datasets. - Practical: Training runs on a single H100 GPU (94 GB VRAM); inference for MSGMs takes 0.0002–0.0004s per forecast, well within 15-minute control cycle requirements for real deployment. - Efficient: MSGMs are 100× faster at inference than TSFMs (0.03–0.05s per forecast for TSFMs vs. sub-millisecond for MSGMs); MSGM pretraining takes 1.2–20.4 hours depending on architecture and dataset, a one-time cost amortized over many target buildings. - Effective: Evidence on real-world buildings (EcoBee, Ideal) shows that zero-shot MSGMs substantially outperform single-source TL and TSFMs for 1-hour temperature forecasting; the surprising finding that fine-tuning hurts performance on real-world data is unexplained and limits confident deployment of the fine-tuning pathway. - Scalable: Only tested up to 128 source buildings and 1-hour forecast horizons; longer prediction horizons needed for MPC (typically 6–24 hours) are not evaluated and represent an untested assumption. The work delivers trained model weights (promised via GitHub) but no software library or CLI tool for end-user deployment. Strengths: - First real-world validation closes the critical gap left by prior work (Raisch et al. 2025) that relied on simulated data only, substantially strengthening the case for MSGM deployment. - The source-count threshold finding (16 buildings for MAE, 2–8 for RMSE) gives practitioners a concrete, actionable decision rule that was previously absent from the literature. - The four-season evaluation design is a genuine methodological improvement over prior work that ignores seasonal effects; averaging across all four seasons reduces cherry-picking risk. - The zero-shot MSGM fine-tuning degradation finding is a counter-intuitive and practically important result not previously reported in the building thermal dynamics literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) TSFMs were tested in zero-shot only and naive fine-tuning increases errors, leaving advanced fine-tuning (LoRA, curriculum learning) as future work; (2) single-source TL experiments were capped at 3 months of fine-tuning data; (3) same-sensor building sparsity constrains MSGM applicability, and feature-invariant MSGMs that handle heterogeneous sensors are a future direction. These are genuine limitations confronted directly, which adds credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The explanation for fine-tuning degradation (seasonal overfitting) is a post-hoc hypothesis; no ablation disentangles seasonal overfitting from distribution shift or regularization mismatch, and this finding fundamentally affects the practical deployment recommendation. - Reporting the best of 4 random source samples for ≤16-source MSGM experiments introduces optimistic bias precisely where the result is most commercially sensitive; median-of-4 would be more defensible. - Forecast horizon is fixed at 1 hour (4 × 15-minute steps); practical MPC requires 6–24-hour horizons, and the relative performance of MSGMs vs. TSFMs likely changes at longer horizons where TSFMs' broader temporal representations may become more competitive. - The Ideal dataset's source-count anomaly (needing more sources due to within-building room homogeneity) exposes that the diversity measure for source buildings is building count rather than thermal diversity — a potentially misleading metric when rooms and buildings are mixed. - No significance testing is applied to per-building win rates (12.5% TSFM wins on EcoBee), making it unclear whether those wins are systematic or random. - Single-source TL uses LSTM only, not the best MSGM architecture per dataset; it is possible the architecture choice, not the multi-source pretraining, drives part of the observed gap. Bottom Line: Any practitioner deploying data-driven building thermal models who can assemble 16+ same-sensor buildings for pretraining will find specific, real-world-validated guidance here — but the unexplained fine-tuning degradation and the absence of longer-horizon and fine-tuned-TSFM evaluations mean the deployment recipe is still incomplete.

A Mechanistic Analysis of Looped Reasoning Language Models

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proves theoretically and demonstrates empirically that looped (recurrent-depth) language models tend toward cyclic fixed-point behavior in latent space, and shows that each recurrence within the loop replicates the same "stages of inference" observed in feedforward Transformers. - Why it's important: Looped LLMs are a promising test-time scaling paradigm, but their internal mechanics have been poorly understood — clarity here enables principled architectural design (norm choice, input injection, sandwich layers) and explains why some models generalize to unseen recurrence counts while others degrade. - How it does it: The paper combines a formal Lipschitz contraction argument (showing attention patterns stabilize when inputs converge to a cyclic fixed point) with empirical measurement of ColSum concentration, sink rates, and mixing scores across six pretrained looped models (Ouro 1.4B, Huginn-0125, Retrofitted Llama/OLMo/TinyLlama) run up to 128 recurrences on 256 GSM8k examples. - The good: The finding that Ouro 1.4B — trained from scratch with recurrence — spontaneously develops feedforward-like stages of inference in each recurrence block, with no explicit training signal encouraging this, is the most striking result and strongly supports the claim that these stages are emergent from the Transformer architecture itself. - The missing: The paper cannot explain analytically why specific architectural choices (pre-norm + input injection) produce stable fixed points, nor why stages of inference emerge; it empirically identifies what correlates but leaves the causal mechanism for future work. - Read it or not: Researchers designing or analyzing looped/recurrent Transformer architectures should read this; it provides actionable guidance on norm and injection choices and a principled lens for understanding recurrent depth that is absent from prior work.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proves theoretically and demonstrates empirically that looped (recurrent-depth) language models tend toward cyclic fixed-point behavior in latent space, and shows that each recurrence within the loop replicates the same "stages of inference" observed in feedforward Transformers. - Why it's important: Looped LLMs are a promising test-time scaling paradigm, but their internal mechanics have been poorly understood — clarity here enables principled architectural design (norm choice, input injection, sandwich layers) and explains why some models generalize to unseen recurrence counts while others degrade. - How it does it: The paper combines a formal Lipschitz contraction argument (showing attention patterns stabilize when inputs converge to a cyclic fixed point) with empirical measurement of ColSum concentration, sink rates, and mixing scores across six pretrained looped models (Ouro 1.4B, Huginn-0125, Retrofitted Llama/OLMo/TinyLlama) run up to 128 recurrences on 256 GSM8k examples. - The good: The finding that Ouro 1.4B — trained from scratch with recurrence — spontaneously develops feedforward-like stages of inference in each recurrence block, with no explicit training signal encouraging this, is the most striking result and strongly supports the claim that these stages are emergent from the Transformer architecture itself. - The missing: The paper cannot explain analytically why specific architectural choices (pre-norm + input injection) produce stable fixed points, nor why stages of inference emerge; it empirically identifies what correlates but leaves the causal mechanism for future work. - Read it or not: Researchers designing or analyzing looped/recurrent Transformer architectures should read this; it provides actionable guidance on norm and injection choices and a principled lens for understanding recurrent depth that is absent from prior work. Problem & Motivation: Looped Transformers — architectures that reapply a shared block of Transformer layers multiple times at inference — have emerged as a mechanism for scaling test-time compute, with models like Huginn, Ouro, and retrofitted LLaMA variants showing reasoning improvements with more recurrences. Despite growing empirical success, the internal dynamics of these models are poorly understood: it is unclear how the latent states evolve across recurrences, how this differs from feedforward computation, and why some looped models generalize gracefully to more test-time recurrences than they were trained on while others do not. The field lacked both a theoretical account of limiting behavior and an empirical characterization of whether looped models discover the same computational structure ("stages of inference") that feedforward models are known to exhibit. Key Idea: The core insight is that cyclic fixed-point convergence — where each individual layer within the recurrent block converges to its own distinct fixed point, rather than all layers collapsing to the same state — is both the dominant empirical behavior across trained looped models and a sufficient condition for attention patterns to stabilize across recurrences. This means: once the latent trajectory is cycling, the mixing behavior (what each attention head attends to) becomes constant recurrence-over-recurrence, and the recurrent block effectively replicates the same computational stages at each iteration, mirroring a feedforward model in miniature. Methodology: The paper proceeds in two main parts. The first (Section 4) establishes cyclic fixed-point behavior. Proposition 4.1 proves by induction that if an (l,k)-recurrent block S_k reaches a fixed point X' = S_k(X'), then each of the k constituent blocks B_1,...,B_k individually reaches a (possibly distinct) fixed point via a cyclic permutation argument. Proposition 4.2 proves, under a bounded-input assumption and using the 1/2-Lipschitz bound on softmax (Nair, 2025), that attention patterns S_ℓ(X_{ℓ,t}) converge at a rate ≤ L_sm · (2Bκ_ℓ/√d) · ‖X_{ℓ,t} − X_{ℓ,t-1}‖, so attention stabilizes as the residual stream converges. Empirical validation uses Frobenius norm between attention matrices across recurrences (Fig. 2), residual stream difference norms (Fig. 3), and cosine similarity to approximate fixed points computed at recurrence 128 (Fig. 4), on 256 random GSM8k test examples. The second part (Section 5) measures stages of inference using ColSum concentration C = 1 − H_col ∈ [0,1] (normalized entropy of column sums of attention matrices), plus sink rates and mixing scores, across realized depth in each recurrent block, comparing these profiles against feedforward baselines (Llama 3.2 1B, OLMo-2 1B, etc.). Section 5.1 investigates whether stages self-organize during training using small from-scratch looped models (configurations (2,4⊗4,2), (2,8⊗4,2), (2,12⊗4,2)) trained on a Nanochat-derived setup with dimension 512, constant 4-recurrence schedule, cross-entropy on the final output. Section 5.2 tests stability to out-of-distribution recurrence counts (up to 128) for models trained on ≤32. Key design decisions examined: norm position (pre-norm vs. Huginn/Ouro sandwich norms), input injection (present vs. absent), and presence of sandwich (prelude/coda) layers. Key Results: Across Huginn-0125 (3.5B) and Retrofitted Llama/OLMo/TinyLlama (0.8–1B), attention matrices of the same layer at different recurrences show strong diagonal structure in Frobenius norm heatmaps, confirming cyclic fixed-point behavior; these models reach approximate fixed points within the first 1–20 recurrences. Ouro 1.4B does not reach a strict fixed point but converges to small successive differences. Non-fixed-point behaviors (orbits, sliders) are rare: without a system prompt, only ~0.02% of tokens exhibit non-fixed-point behavior; with the Huginn "Long Persona" prompt, this rises to 0.14% of tokens (2.81% of examples). Input injection is necessary for stable fixed-point behavior across all norm types except pre-norm; without input injection, all non-pre-norm models fail to converge stably. Stages of inference (characteristic ColSum concentration profiles across block depth) observed in feedforward models are faithfully reproduced within each recurrence of Ouro 1.4B, Retrofitted Llama, and Retrofitted OLMo — with Ouro trained from scratch, ruling out inheritance from a feedforward base model. Huginn-0125 does not reproduce these stages, attributed to its norm structure (normalizing residual stream magnitude, preventing the massive-activation growth that drives compression behavior and hence sink formation). Small ablation (zeroing massive-activation MLP output in Retrofitted Llama) eliminates its stages of inference. Models with stable fixed points (Retrofitted Llama, Huginn) maintain consistent stages of inference at 128 recurrences; Ouro's stages drift significantly at out-of-distribution recurrence counts. Experiments are evaluated solely on GSM8k (256 examples) and HellaSwag (256 examples, Appendix E.4); no task-accuracy numbers are reported — this is purely an internal-dynamics study. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FORMULATION. The formal cyclic fixed-point framework (Propositions 4.1–4.2) is a new result that was not previously established for cyclic recurrent architectures. The application of stages-of-inference analysis to looped models, and the empirical demonstration that these stages emerge spontaneously from training, is a new empirical finding. The work is not a new method or system; it uses ColSum concentration and mixing metrics introduced by prior work (Queipo-de Llano et al., 2025) applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: The theoretical propositions are fully proved in the appendix (Appendix A), with stated assumptions (bounded inputs, shared weight matrices, submultiplicative norms). Empirical results are averaged over 256 random GSM8k test examples (same subset throughout), and small training experiments use 3 random seeds (Fig. 6). No error bars or significance tests are reported for the main empirical claims in the paper body, though standard deviations are shown in some appendix figures. Code is referenced (adapted from github.com/TrelisResearch/nanochat/tree/recursive) but no dedicated repository for this paper's analysis code is stated. Models are all publicly available on HuggingFace (Table 2). A methodological concern: the non-fixed-point detection algorithm (Algorithm 1) has hyperparameters (τ=0.05, ρ=0.9) selected manually via visual inspection, which the authors themselves flag. The paper evaluates only on GSM8k and HellaSwag; no claim is made about task performance, only internal dynamics — this is appropriately scoped. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile (internal dynamics analysis of existing trained models, plus small training experiments). - Practical: All experiments run on existing pretrained models obtainable from HuggingFace; inference with 128 recurrences on models up to 3.5B is feasible on standard GPU hardware. - Efficient: The analysis itself is post-hoc observation (no training cost beyond small from-scratch experiments at dimension 512); the study does not propose any new inference procedure, so no efficiency comparison is applicable. - Effective: The study demonstrates its claims about internal dynamics convincingly across six models, two datasets, and up to 128 recurrences; however, it does not demonstrate that architectural choices improving fixed-point stability also improve task performance. - Scalable: All pretrained models studied are ≤3.5B parameters; whether the cyclic fixed-point / stages-of-inference picture holds for larger models (7B+, 70B+) is untested and not claimed. The work delivers no software artifact, trained model, or dataset beyond the analysis; it is a paper-form empirical and theoretical study. Strengths: - The formal proof that cyclic fixed-point convergence implies per-layer fixed points (Proposition 4.1) and that attention patterns stabilize under this convergence (Proposition 4.2) grounds the empirical observations in a principled theoretical framework — prior work had only observed this empirically. - Demonstrating that Ouro 1.4B, trained from scratch with recurrence and without any feedforward model as a base, spontaneously develops stages of inference that mirror Llama's feedforward profile is a striking and non-obvious result that substantially strengthens the claim of emergent structure. - The paper cleanly isolates the role of architectural choices (norm type × input injection) through controlled randomly-initialized experiments, separating the effect of pretrained weights from architectural inductive bias. - The connection between fixed-point stability and generalization to out-of-distribution recurrence counts is practically actionable: it explains empirically observed failure modes of Ouro at test-time recurrence extrapolation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The analysis is restricted to cyclic recurrence (one shared block looped repeatedly) and does not extend to sequential recurrence with multiple distinct recurrent blocks, for which the authors refer readers to Pappone et al. (2025). The authors acknowledge they have not established analytically why specific architectural choices lead to stable fixed points, nor whether stable fixed-point behavior is desirable or restrictive for reasoning tasks. The non-fixed-point detection algorithm hyperparameters were tuned manually. The paper also acknowledges that stages of inference for Huginn-0125 are absent, and attributes this to norm structure, but does not fully resolve this — it is labeled a negative result. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper measures internal dynamics exclusively on a 256-example subset of GSM8k (reasoning) and HellaSwag (commonsense), and never reports whether the architectural choices that improve fixed-point stability (pre-norm + input injection) also improve task accuracy — this disconnect between mechanistic findings and downstream performance is the central unaddressed question. The small-scale training experiments (dimension 512, Nanochat-derived) may not reflect behavior at the scale of the pretrained models being analyzed; the authors note this caveat, but the gap from toy scale to 1–3.5B parameters is large. The claim that massive activations are required for stages of inference to form rests on a single ablation (zeroing one MLP output) on one model; a more systematic ablation across models would be needed to establish this. No comparison is made between looped models and deep feedforward models at matched parameter count, which would clarify whether the stages-of-inference correspondence is coincidental or structurally meaningful. Bottom Line: A mechanistically careful and theoretically grounded study that gives the first principled account of why looped Transformers behave as they do internally; practitioners designing recurrent-depth architectures will find the norm/injection guidance actionable, though the missing link to task performance means the work is explanatory rather than prescriptive.

ClawMark: A Living-World Benchmark for Multi-Turn, Multi-Day, Multimodal Coworker Agents

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ClawMark, a benchmark of 100 multi-turn, multi-day, multimodal office-workflow tasks scored by 1,537 deterministic Python checkers over five stateful sandboxed services, designed to evaluate persistent coworker agents under exogenously mutating environments. - Why it's important: Existing agent benchmarks run within single static episodes and are largely text-centric, leaving the coworker regime — where agents must track independently evolving state across multiple workdays and parse raw multimodal evidence — essentially unmeasured by any prior benchmark. - How it does it: Each task simulates 2–6 in-universe workdays against live sandboxed services, with between-turn state mutations injected as both announced loud events and unannounced silent mutations, then scored by deterministic Python functions against post-turn service state rather than LLM-as-judge. - The good: The failure taxonomy (pooled 10,759 checker evaluations across 7 models) reveals that silent-change detection (56.5% per-evaluation fail rate) and backend writeback (53.6%) are the dominant failure modes — nearly 2× the benchmark-wide average — providing a precise, actionable diagnostic that LLM-as-judge evaluation would miss. - The missing: Only 100 tasks across 13 scenarios (one scenario has a single task), all run under a single agent harness (OpenClaw); run-to-run variance is quantified for only 2 of 7 models, leaving statistical confidence in close-cluster rankings unestablished for 5 models. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or evaluating persistent LLM agents — the benchmark uniquely identifies two specific, measurable failure modes (silent-change detection, backend writeback) that prior benchmarks structurally cannot surface.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ClawMark, a benchmark of 100 multi-turn, multi-day, multimodal office-workflow tasks scored by 1,537 deterministic Python checkers over five stateful sandboxed services, designed to evaluate persistent coworker agents under exogenously mutating environments. - Why it's important: Existing agent benchmarks run within single static episodes and are largely text-centric, leaving the coworker regime — where agents must track independently evolving state across multiple workdays and parse raw multimodal evidence — essentially unmeasured by any prior benchmark. - How it does it: Each task simulates 2–6 in-universe workdays against live sandboxed services, with between-turn state mutations injected as both announced loud events and unannounced silent mutations, then scored by deterministic Python functions against post-turn service state rather than LLM-as-judge. - The good: The failure taxonomy (pooled 10,759 checker evaluations across 7 models) reveals that silent-change detection (56.5% per-evaluation fail rate) and backend writeback (53.6%) are the dominant failure modes — nearly 2× the benchmark-wide average — providing a precise, actionable diagnostic that LLM-as-judge evaluation would miss. - The missing: Only 100 tasks across 13 scenarios (one scenario has a single task), all run under a single agent harness (OpenClaw); run-to-run variance is quantified for only 2 of 7 models, leaving statistical confidence in close-cluster rankings unestablished for 5 models. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or evaluating persistent LLM agents — the benchmark uniquely identifies two specific, measurable failure modes (silent-change detection, backend writeback) that prior benchmarks structurally cannot surface. Problem & Motivation Existing LLM agent benchmarks — WebArena, OSWorld, tau-bench, TheAgentCompany — evaluate within a single static session where the environment does not change independently between steps. Real professional workflows span multiple workdays: emails arrive overnight, calendar entries shift, files are silently overwritten, and critical evidence is distributed across audio, video, scanned PDFs, and spreadsheets rather than clean pre-transcribed text. No prior benchmark jointly evaluates agents on (a) multi-day persistence, (b) exogenous between-turn environment mutation, and (c) raw multimodal evidence, while maintaining deterministic, reproducible scoring. This gap means current benchmarks cannot diagnose whether a model fails because it cannot parse a scanned PDF, fails to refresh stale state after a silent overnight mutation, or commits a compliance violation that prose-based judges overlook. Key Idea The core insight is that coworker evaluation requires treating the environment as a first-class participant that mutates independently of the agent between turns — not merely as a passive substrate that the agent writes to. Operationally: inject both announced loud events and unannounced silent mutations into five live sandboxed services between turns, then score by deterministic Python checkers querying post-turn service state, so an agent that reasons correctly but never commits the result to the right backend fails, and a compliance violation that does not appear in the prose trajectory but does appear in service state is caught deterministically. Methodology Each task is defined in a single `task.py` specifying per-turn wake-up prompts, service seed hooks, between-turn inject layers (loud events announced in prompts; silent mutations injected into services without notification), and a weighted checker rubric of 6–29 deterministic Python functions. Five sandboxed services run per task in an isolated docker-compose group: Docker-mounted filesystem, GreenMail SMTP/IMAP, Notion-compatible knowledge base, Google-Sheets-compatible spreadsheet, and Radicale CalDAV server. Tasks run 2–6 turns (mean 3.6), with 1,072 raw multimodal artifacts across the corpus. Scoring uses two metrics: weight-normalized task score (Eq. 1, continuous [0,1]) and strict Task Success requiring every checker to pass (Eq. 2, binary). 55 of 1,537 checkers are red-line constraints (premature-decision, compliance-bypass, data-exfiltration, irreversible-write) implemented as high-weight ordinary rubric entries — no separate evaluation path. The four-phase construction pipeline proceeds: task authoring (authors write task.py under three invariants: every silent mutation tied to at least one checker, every cross-modal contradiction spans two modalities, every red-line is a state check not prose match); evidence sourcing via web collection, original recording, or AI synthesis; a 3–5 round review loop combining human multimodal integrity inspection, checker-hacking audits, task-checker correspondence checks, and trajectory review by a Codex-class reviewer agent running against two reference model trajectories; and a release gate requiring bit-identical checker verdicts and diagnostic messages across two independent re-runs. Tasks failing the gate more than twice are redesigned or dropped. Seven frontier models (Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.4 high, Kimi K2.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview, Qwen 3.6 Plus, Kimi K2.5) ran under a single harness (OpenClaw) with provider-default inference and extended thinking enabled where supported. Three critical design decisions: (1) Bit-identical re-run gate operationally enforces the no-LLM-as-judge claim — well-justified as the main validity guarantee. (2) Dual metrics (weighted score + Task Success) jointly reported — justified because weighted score rewards partial progress while Task Success answers the deployment-relevant all-or-nothing question. (3) All seven models run under one harness to isolate model-side differences — appropriate for leaderboard comparison but limits external validity, as the paper explicitly acknowledges. Key Results Weighted scores (single-sweep): Claude Sonnet 4.6 at 75.8, Claude Opus 4.6 at 74.6, GPT-5.4 at 72.0, Kimi K2.6 at 68.4, Gemini 3.1 Pro at 68.2, Qwen 3.6 Plus at 57.2, Kimi K2.5 at 56.0. Under strict Task Success the ordering changes: Opus 4.6 leads at 20.0%, Sonnet at 14.0%, GPT-5.4 at 9.0%, Kimi K2.5 at 0.0%. The top three models cluster within 3.8 pp weighted score — the authors flag this band as tentative given single-sweep evaluation. Hardest scenario: Project Management, all models below 44.0, mean across 7 models ≈ 35.1. Per-scenario best distributed across four models, not dominated by one. Day-by-day trajectory on the 73 three-turn tasks: 6 of 7 models drop on Day 2 (first exogenous mutation). Largest Day-1→Day-2 declines: Opus 4.6 (80.6→69.0, −11.5 pp), Sonnet 4.6 (83.1→72.6, −10.5 pp), Kimi K2.6 (75.4→65.8, −9.6 pp). Qwen 3.6 Plus is the lone exception (+1.2 pp). Recovery by Day 3 is partial; 6 of 7 models remain below Day-1 baseline. Failure taxonomy (10,759 pooled evals, 3,404 failures, 31.6% overall): silent-change detection 56.5% fail rate, backend writeback 53.6% — both nearly 2× average. Backend writeback is the largest absolute bucket (567 failures, 16.7% of all failures). Red-line incidents are concentrated: Sonnet 4.6 and GPT-5.4 at 3.6%, Qwen 3.6 Plus at 14.5% (4× frontier top-3). pm_task2: all 7 models trip at least one red-line. Run-to-run stability (Appendix D, 3 sweeps for Kimi K2.6 and GPT-5.4): 2.8 pp range and 1.0 pp range respectively, small relative to the 19.8 pp cross-model spread. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark corpus and harness (multi-day tasks, exogenously mutating sandboxed services, deterministic scoring) constitute the primary contribution; the empirical finding — that silent-change detection and backend writeback fail at nearly 2× the benchmark-wide rate, and that compliance violations are surface-invisible but state-detectable — is a novel, actionable diagnostic. Individual components (sandboxed services, deterministic scoring, multi-turn evaluation) are established; novelty lies in their specific combination enforced by construction discipline (bit-identical re-run gate, checker-hacking audits) that makes the no-LLM-as-judge guarantee credible rather than aspirational. Rigor & Reproducibility Scoring determinism is operationally enforced, not just claimed: tasks are admitted only after bit-identical checker verdicts across two independent re-runs against frozen service state. Run-to-run variance is quantified for only 2 of 7 models (Kimi K2.6 and GPT-5.4, Appendix D); the remaining 5 have single-sweep results, so rankings within the top-3 cluster (3.8 pp spread) carry no stated statistical confidence. No error bars, significance tests, or confidence intervals are reported for model comparisons. Checker-hacking audits during construction actively guard against rubric items satisfiable by keyword emission rather than backend state change — methodological discipline rare in benchmark papers. Silent/loud annotation uses author-supplied comments in task.py and is acknowledged as "sensitive to borderline cases at the per-scenario level." The benchmark, harness, construction pipeline, and 700 execution traces are released publicly (github.com/evolvent-ai/ClawMark). This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.23781v1, April 2026), not peer-reviewed. A broken section cross-reference ("§??") in Table 2's caption indicates the manuscript was not fully finalized before posting. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built benchmark harness evaluated by deployment against seven frontier models. - Practical: The harness runs on standard x86 infrastructure via docker-compose with 4–8 concurrent compose stacks; no specialized hardware is required beyond sufficient RAM to run five concurrent sandboxed services per task. - Efficient: Full sweeps take 18.9–33.3 wall-clock hours across 100 tasks; the dominant cost is LLM API calls (214M–339M input tokens per model), not benchmark infrastructure — but this makes large-scale regression testing expensive for researchers without frontier-model API budgets. - Effective: The deterministic checkers evaluate actual service state, not proxy signals; the insurance_task1 case study demonstrates they catch compliance violations that pass prose-level evaluation, confirming the benchmark measures the intended property. - Scalable: The current corpus is 100 tasks; the construction pipeline is released to support growth, but the 3–5 round human review loop per task is a significant labor bottleneck, and no test of harness performance at 10× task count is reported. The work delivers a software artifact: benchmark tasks, deterministic evaluation harness, construction pipeline, and 700 execution traces. Strengths - The no-LLM-as-judge guarantee is operationally enforced by a bit-identical re-run release gate, closing the main validity gap in agent evaluation where scoring inconsistency routinely dominates noise. This is technically concrete, not a design aspiration. - The loud-event / silent-mutation distinction is a precise, testable formalization of a property real coworkers encounter (proactive state refresh vs. passive notification response), and the failure taxonomy validates it empirically: silent-change detection fails at 56.5%, nearly 2× the average, giving the distinction practical bite rather than leaving it as a conceptual claim. - Red-line checkers catch compliance violations defined by service state rather than prose, demonstrated concretely in insurance_task1: Kimi K2.5 passes 7 turn-3 prose-level checks while approving a claim before the technical report arrives, a violation visible only in the backend state and costing −9.3 pp. - Checker-hacking audits during construction — explicitly reviewing rubric items to ensure they cannot be satisfied by surface keyword emission — represent methodological discipline that most benchmark papers omit and that directly strengthens the benchmark's construct validity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) single-sweep results for 5 of 7 models with run-to-run variance bounded only for Kimi K2.6 and GPT-5.4; rankings within the 3.8 pp top-3 cluster are explicitly flagged as tentative. (2) Silent/loud classification uses author-supplied comments and is "sensitive to borderline cases." (3) EDA contains one task and its row is flagged as a case-level result, not a stable trend. (4) All models run under OpenClaw; no claim of empirical equivalence across different agent frameworks. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive, though the caveats appear only in footnotes and subsections rather than a consolidated limitations section. Analyst-Identified Gaps - With 1–6 tasks in several scenarios (Clinical Assistant: 4, EDA: 1, Legal Assistant: 6), per-scenario conclusions are statistically fragile. The authors flag EDA but not the other small-N scenarios; Legal Assistant (6 tasks, GPT-5.4 scores 59.5 vs. Sonnet's 84.1) may reflect a single task's characteristics rather than a generalizable gap. - All seven models run under OpenClaw with six model-specific harness patches applied; the authors disclaim cross-harness equivalence but do not assess whether those patches differentially affect model scores, leaving open whether the observed gaps partly reflect harness compatibility rather than intrinsic model capability. - The construction pipeline relies on a Codex-class reviewer agent for trajectory review, creating a potential circularity: if the reviewer agent shares failure modes with the evaluated models, those design flaws pass the release gate and systematically inflate scores for all models on the same tasks. No human validation of the reviewer agent's coverage is reported. - Day-2 performance drops are attributed to exogenous state changes, but the confound with longer prompt context (more history at Day 2) and accumulated tool-call errors is not disentangled. A condition with equivalent context length but no exogenous mutation would isolate the causal contribution of the mutation itself. - No human-performance baseline is reported, making it impossible to assess whether the benchmark is well-calibrated for difficulty or whether some scenarios are trivially solvable by a human professional (which would contextualize the low Task Success rates). - The task distribution across scenarios (15 Research Assistant tasks vs. 1 EDA task) is uneven and unmotivated; it is unclear whether the corpus represents real coworker workload distributions or reflects authoring convenience. Bottom Line ClawMark fills a genuine and well-defined gap — it is the only benchmark combining multi-day tasks, exogenous state mutation, full multimodal evidence, and deterministic scoring — and its failure taxonomy delivers the most operationally precise diagnostic of coworker-agent weaknesses currently available; practitioners building persistent agents should read it, though the 100-task corpus size and single-harness evaluation limit the statistical weight of its model rankings.

Large Language Models Explore by Latent Distilling

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Exploratory Sampling (ESamp), a decoding algorithm that reweights next-token probabilities at each generation step using prediction error from a lightweight online-trained MLP (Latent Distiller) mapping first-layer hidden states to final-layer hidden states, steering generation toward semantically novel regions. - Why it's important: Test-time scaling via candidate sampling is bottlenecked by semantic redundancy — increasing k in Pass@k yields diminishing returns when candidates share the same underlying reasoning path, limiting the ability of selection mechanisms (voting, reranking, verification) to find correct solutions. - How it does it: A 2-layer MLP is trained online during generation via MSE loss; high prediction error signals a novel representation mapping; the KL-regularized RL framework yields a closed-form logit reweighting logit_new = (1+β)·logit_ref − β·logit_dist, where the Distiller is shared across all K parallel sequences, providing implicit cross-sequence exploration coordination. - The good: On GPT-OSS-20B, ESamp achieves Pass@8 performance comparable to baseline Pass@64 on AIME benchmarks; a noise ablation replacing the error vector with magnitude-matched Gaussian noise collapses performance to vanilla level, confirming the error direction encodes structured semantic information rather than acting as random perturbation. - The missing: All experiments use models ≤32B parameters with context ≤8K tokens; the closest competitor (OverRIDE) is an anonymous under-review submission, weakening the primary comparison; gains on GPQA-Diamond are marginal and the limitation to multiple-choice scientific reasoning is not explicitly discussed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on test-time scaling, diverse decoding, or LLM serving — the latent-space RND-inspired novelty signal is a non-obvious insight with strong ablation support and a practical open-source implementation.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Exploratory Sampling (ESamp), a decoding algorithm that reweights next-token probabilities at each generation step using prediction error from a lightweight online-trained MLP (Latent Distiller) mapping first-layer hidden states to final-layer hidden states, steering generation toward semantically novel regions. - Why it's important: Test-time scaling via candidate sampling is bottlenecked by semantic redundancy — increasing k in Pass@k yields diminishing returns when candidates share the same underlying reasoning path, limiting the ability of selection mechanisms (voting, reranking, verification) to find correct solutions. - How it does it: A 2-layer MLP is trained online during generation via MSE loss; high prediction error signals a novel representation mapping; the KL-regularized RL framework yields a closed-form logit reweighting logit_new = (1+β)·logit_ref − β·logit_dist, where the Distiller is shared across all K parallel sequences, providing implicit cross-sequence exploration coordination. - The good: On GPT-OSS-20B, ESamp achieves Pass@8 performance comparable to baseline Pass@64 on AIME benchmarks; a noise ablation replacing the error vector with magnitude-matched Gaussian noise collapses performance to vanilla level, confirming the error direction encodes structured semantic information rather than acting as random perturbation. - The missing: All experiments use models ≤32B parameters with context ≤8K tokens; the closest competitor (OverRIDE) is an anonymous under-review submission, weakening the primary comparison; gains on GPQA-Diamond are marginal and the limitation to multiple-choice scientific reasoning is not explicitly discussed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on test-time scaling, diverse decoding, or LLM serving — the latent-space RND-inspired novelty signal is a non-obvious insight with strong ablation support and a practical open-source implementation. Problem & Motivation: Test-time scaling approaches — generating K candidate solutions and applying selection via reranking, majority voting, or verification — are fundamentally capped by the semantic diversity of the candidate pool. Standard stochastic sampling (temperature, top-p, min-p) generates surface-level lexical variation but candidates frequently converge on the same underlying reasoning path, especially for structured tasks like competition math. If all K candidates fail via the same systematic error, selection mechanisms cannot recover the correct answer. Structured search (Tree of Thoughts) achieves genuine exploration but multiplies compute by the branching factor. The gap is a mechanism that efficiently diversifies the reasoning strategies in a candidate batch without proportional overhead. Key Idea: The core insight adapts Random Network Distillation (RND) from RL exploration to LLM decoding: a lightweight MLP trained online during generation will exhibit high prediction error on representation mappings it has not yet seen. By training this MLP to map first-layer hidden states to final-layer hidden states using all parallel sequences in the batch, the distiller learns what reasoning paths the batch has already explored. Projecting the prediction error through the frozen LM head produces a structured logit correction that amplifies tokens leading to under-explored semantic regions. A single shared distiller across K parallel sequences implicitly coordinates exploration — once sequence A claims a semantic region, the distiller fits that mapping and suppresses subsequent sequences from revisiting it. Methodology: The pipeline runs two interleaved CUDA streams per decode step. The Latent Distiller (LD) is a 2-layer Gated SwiGLU MLP with 384-dim intermediate and input/output dimensions matching the host LLM hidden size. At each decode step, Layer 1 of the LLM outputs h_1; the LD asynchronously predicts ĥ_L = f_φ(h_1) while Layers 2..L run on the main stream. Both h_L and ĥ_L are projected through the frozen LM head to produce logit_ref and logit_dist. The reweighted sampling distribution is logit_new = (1+β)·logit_ref − β·logit_dist (Eq. 7), where β=0.25 is the default exploration strength. After sampling, the LD backward pass runs on the GPU during the CPU-bound post-processing interval using batch-averaged MSE loss over all parallel sequences: L(φ) = (1/|B|)Σ ‖h_L − f_φ(h_1)‖². Three critical design choices: (1) Using first-layer hidden states maximizes the GPU-overlap window — this is dictated by system constraints but also concentrates the representation space at the shallowest, most context-agnostic level. (2) The (1+β) fusion formula (vs. naive logit_ref − β·logit_dist) preserves relative probability mass of the base model; the ablation shows naive subtraction at β=0.5 collapses Pass@64 from 46.7% to 13.3% on AIME25. (3) A shared Distiller across all K sequences creates an implicit "first-come, first-served" scheduler without any explicit cross-sequence communication overhead. Key Results: On AIME 2024/2025 (30 competition math problems each), ESamp achieves Pass@64 of 80.0% vs. vanilla 70.0% for Qwen3-8B on AIME24; on GPT-OSS-20B, ESamp Pass@8 matches or exceeds baseline Pass@64 on both AIME benchmarks. On LiveCodeBench v5 (167 code problems), ESamp Pass@1 reaches 51.8% for GPT-OSS-20B at context 4096 vs. vanilla 43.4%, and 61.5% at context 16384 vs. vanilla 52.2%. On GPQA-Diamond (198 science multiple-choice), gains are marginal: ESamp 63.2% vs. vanilla 62.4% Pass@1 for GPT-OSS-20B. In creative writing (BookCorpus, 2000 stories), ESamp achieves the highest Vendi diversity score (1.67 vs. vanilla 1.62) and lowest perplexity (3.55 vs. vanilla 4.08) simultaneously — the only method that improves both metrics. Throughput overhead is 1.81% in standard serving (B=32, K=1) and 4.25% in test-time scaling (B=32, K=16) on an RTX4090 with Qwen3-8B. The noise ablation on Qwen3-8B (Table 10) shows ESamp Pass@16/Pass@64 of 57.0%/63.9% vs. magnitude-matched noise 52.4%/60.0% vs. vanilla 53.6%/58.9% on AIME25, confirming the error direction's structured content. β=0.25 is robust across Qwen3-4B/8B/14B without per-model tuning. Baselines are current: vanilla temperature, Min-p (ICLR 2025), FIRE (NAACL 2025), Tree of Thoughts (NeurIPS 2023), Contrastive Decoding (ACL 2023), and OverRIDE (under review at ICLR). All results are from a preprint (April 2026); not peer reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is adapting RND from RL exploration to per-step LLM decoding via online latent distillation in continuous representation space, combined with a batch-shared Distiller that implicitly coordinates multi-sequence exploration without explicit cross-sequence communication. The KL-regularized framework is adapted from Mudgal et al. 2024; the RND analogy from Burda et al. 2019. The vocabulary-space version of the same idea (ablation C.4) is substantially inferior (ESamp 57.0% vs. Vocab-Space 37.4% Pass@16 on AIME25), confirming that the continuous representation space is essential, not a coincidental choice. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough: β sensitivity, logit fusion formulation, noise ablation (C.3), latent vs. vocabulary space (C.4), multi-seed stability over 3 seeds (C.5), baseline hyperparameter grids with 3-point sweeps (C.6), entropy-adaptive baseline comparison (C.7), composability with FIRE and Self-Consistency (C.8), Distiller architecture (C.9), and shared vs. per-prompt Distillers (C.10). Three-seed results for Qwen3-8B report standard deviations (e.g., AIME25 ESamp Pass@64: 67.8 ± 1.9 vs. vanilla 58.9 ± 0.9). Code is open-sourced at github.com/LinesHogan/tLLM; main paper codebase released "upon acceptance." Primary concern: OverRIDE is cited as an anonymous under-review submission — this is the closest competitor and the comparison cannot be independently verified. Secondary concern: AIME 2024/2025 has only 30 problems each; Pass@k curves from 30 problems carry high variance, and confidence intervals for the Pass@k plots are not shown. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods evaluated via experiments) + SYSTEMS (the tLLM framework is a deployed software artifact). - Practical: Runs on commodity inference hardware (RTX4090, A100); Distiller adds <200MB VRAM for an 8B model and can theoretically be reduced to ~50MB. No modification to LLM weights is required. - Efficient: 4.25% throughput reduction in the test-time scaling scenario (B=32, K=16); Distiller forward pass requires <0.5ms vs. 15-20ms available window (Llama-3-8B on A100), providing a large overlap margin that may shrink for smaller or faster models. - Effective: The noise ablation directly demonstrates that the improvement is not from added stochasticity; ESamp improves Pass@k across 4 models and 4 benchmarks, while competitors fail on at least one domain (FIRE hurts LiveCodeBench, OverRIDE shows mixed results). - Scalable: Tested up to 32B parameters and K=64 samples; behavior at 70B+ models or >8K token chains is not evaluated. The per-prompt vs. shared Distiller trade-off (Table 18) is task-dependent and may require re-tuning at scale. Throughput benefits diminish under fully saturated GPU utilization (authors note this explicitly). Software artifact: Open-sourced as ESamp within the tLLM framework, built on vLLM v1 with CUDA graph support, FlashInfer sampling, and a producer-consumer abstraction for future runtime-adaptation algorithms. Strengths: - The noise ablation (Section C.3) is the paper's most credible piece of evidence: replacing the Distiller error vector with Gaussian noise of matched magnitude recovers vanilla-level performance, directly falsifying the hypothesis that ESamp is merely injecting beneficial noise and confirming the direction of the error vector carries structured semantic content. - The (1+β) logit fusion formula has a concrete, testable justification: the naive subtraction variant collapses Pass@64 from 46.7% to 13.3% at β=0.5 (Table 2), a 33-point gap that illustrates why probability mass preservation matters for coherence. - ESamp simultaneously achieves the highest diversity (Vendi) and highest coherence (lowest PPL) in creative writing — breaking the diversity-quality trade-off rather than just shifting it — which is the central empirical claim and is backed by both automatic metrics and LLM-as-judge evaluation on 2000 prompts. - The engineering depth is unusual for an algorithms paper: custom CUDA kernels, GPU ring buffers, CUDA Event synchronization (not CPU barriers), mixed-batch guardrails, and a Triton grouped prediction backend are all described, benchmarked, and open-sourced. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: (a) ESamp targets candidate-set coverage rather than single-sample accuracy, and small Pass@1 regressions occur in some settings (Qwen2.5-7B AIME25: 6.0% vs. vanilla 6.6%); (b) throughput benefits diminish under fully saturated GPU utilization; (c) the per-prompt vs. shared Distiller trade-off is task-dependent and adaptive strategies are left as future work; (d) the theoretical vanishing redundancy condition (Prop. A.2) relies on empirically supported but unproved assumptions (Rapid Fitting, Local Generalization). The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The primary competitor OverRIDE is an anonymous under-review submission ("Anonymous, 2025") whose hyperparameters are taken from its OpenReview supplementary — if the paper is rejected, revised, or withdrawn, the key comparison cannot be reproduced. AIME 2024/2025 each contain exactly 30 problems; Pass@k estimates from 30 problems are noisy (e.g., the difference between 31.7% and 30.3% Pass@16 is 0.4 problems in expectation), but confidence intervals for the Pass@k curves are not shown. Gains on GPQA-Diamond are marginal (<1% across all models) — ESamp's benefit appears concentrated on open-ended multi-step reasoning (math, code) rather than factual multiple-choice science, a limitation not explicitly discussed. The paper does not test whether the latent distillation property holds at 70B+ scale or whether the 384-dim MLP remains a useful predictor of final-layer representations for very deep models. The case study in Section E.6 uses a single math problem evaluated by a single LLM judge (Gemini Flash) — illustrative but not statistically meaningful. Bottom Line: A practitioner building test-time scaling pipelines for reasoning models gains a concrete, low-overhead diversification method with unusually thorough ablation support; the latent RND framing is a genuinely non-obvious insight, and the open-source implementation makes it immediately usable.

AutoResearchBench: Benchmarking AI Agents on Complex Scientific Literature Discovery

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AutoResearchBench, a 1,000-query benchmark for evaluating AI agents on autonomous scientific literature discovery, comprising two task types — Deep Research (identify the single paper satisfying a conjunction of obfuscated full-text constraints, or establish that none exists) and Wide Research (exhaustively retrieve all papers satisfying a multi-part scientific specification) — over a corpus of 3M+ arXiv papers. - Why it's important: Existing agentic benchmarks (BrowseComp, GAIA) measure general web navigation and score above 80% for frontier models, yet they do not test whether agents can perform genuine multi-hop reasoning over full scientific text, verify fine-grained technical constraints, or determine when a literature search is complete — capabilities that are central to autonomous research. - How it does it: Queries are constructed via a full-text-first human–machine pipeline that mines subtle constraints from appendices, ablation tables, and citation chains (never headline contributions), applies two-level fuzzification to suppress keyword shortcuts, enforces minimal sufficiency so removing any clue admits alternative papers, and validates each instance through a four-stage adversarial pipeline (LLM shortcut screening, ReAct agent stress testing, timed human search, corpus-level uniqueness audit). - The good: All evaluated frontier models — including Claude-Opus-4.6, Gemini-3.1-Pro, and GPT-5.4 — fail to exceed 10% on either task, with most falling below 5%, and a manual audit confirms 96% of model "false positives" in Wide Research are genuine true negatives, establishing both a large unsolved performance gap and high label quality. - The missing: The benchmark is restricted to computer science papers in a fixed arXiv corpus; whether difficulty is intrinsic to scientific reasoning or is partly an artifact of the specific corpus, domain, or DeepXiv tool is not disentangled. - Read it or not: Essential for researchers building or evaluating autonomous scientific agents; researchers focused on general web browsing agents or non-CS literature will find the scope too narrow to apply results directly.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AutoResearchBench, a 1,000-query benchmark for evaluating AI agents on autonomous scientific literature discovery, comprising two task types — Deep Research (identify the single paper satisfying a conjunction of obfuscated full-text constraints, or establish that none exists) and Wide Research (exhaustively retrieve all papers satisfying a multi-part scientific specification) — over a corpus of 3M+ arXiv papers. - Why it's important: Existing agentic benchmarks (BrowseComp, GAIA) measure general web navigation and score above 80% for frontier models, yet they do not test whether agents can perform genuine multi-hop reasoning over full scientific text, verify fine-grained technical constraints, or determine when a literature search is complete — capabilities that are central to autonomous research. - How it does it: Queries are constructed via a full-text-first human–machine pipeline that mines subtle constraints from appendices, ablation tables, and citation chains (never headline contributions), applies two-level fuzzification to suppress keyword shortcuts, enforces minimal sufficiency so removing any clue admits alternative papers, and validates each instance through a four-stage adversarial pipeline (LLM shortcut screening, ReAct agent stress testing, timed human search, corpus-level uniqueness audit). - The good: All evaluated frontier models — including Claude-Opus-4.6, Gemini-3.1-Pro, and GPT-5.4 — fail to exceed 10% on either task, with most falling below 5%, and a manual audit confirms 96% of model "false positives" in Wide Research are genuine true negatives, establishing both a large unsolved performance gap and high label quality. - The missing: The benchmark is restricted to computer science papers in a fixed arXiv corpus; whether difficulty is intrinsic to scientific reasoning or is partly an artifact of the specific corpus, domain, or DeepXiv tool is not disentangled. - Read it or not: Essential for researchers building or evaluating autonomous scientific agents; researchers focused on general web browsing agents or non-CS literature will find the scope too narrow to apply results directly. Problem & Motivation: Autonomous research agents repeatedly need to find scientific literature — not topically similar papers, but papers satisfying a conjunction of precise technical constraints whose decisive evidence resides in method details, ablation tables, figure captions, appendices, and citation chains rather than titles or abstracts. This task is qualitatively distinct from general web search on two axes: complexity (technical, fast-moving, domain-specific evidence distributed across full documents) and open-endedness (the number of qualifying papers is unknown and may be zero, requiring agents to reason about exploration completeness and when to abstain). Prior academic-specific datasets (RealScholarQuery, SPAR) are too small and lack interactive search environments; broad agentic benchmarks (GAIA, BrowseComp) do not require full-paper reading or open-ended completeness reasoning. The gap leaves unclear how close current agents are to being useful as autonomous literature reviewers. Key Idea: The core technical insight driving Deep Research task design is the minimal-sufficiency constraint principle: each query is built from full-text evidence such that the conjunction of clues uniquely locates exactly one paper in a 3M-paper corpus, removing any single clue admits alternative papers, and no lexical reformulation retrieves the target via shallow keyword matching. This forces genuine multi-hop evidence integration rather than paraphrastic retrieval. For Wide Research, the complementary idea is entity-graph-based query construction: shared multidimensional attributes (method, dataset, results) are extracted from a candidate pool, encoded as a strict logical conjunction, and used to define a query whose complete answer set must be exhaustively recovered — testing set-boundary reasoning, not just recall-oriented search. Methodology: The benchmark contains 600 Deep Research and 400 Wide Research queries spanning eight CS domains (CV 30.7%, ML 20.2%, NLP 12.5%, Multimodal 11.5%, others). For Deep Research, under-exposed high-quality papers (10–100 citations, excluding surveys) are selected; annotators read full text including supplementary material and extract verifiable constraints from secondary methodological choices, proof details, and fine-grained empirical observations — explicitly avoiding headline contributions. Constraints are fuzzified at topic and detail levels and pruned to the minimal sufficient set. No-answer instances (10% of Deep queries) are created by perturbing one core constraint so no valid paper exists. Four-stage adversarial verification retains an instance only if: (1) GPT-5.4 reformulations cannot retrieve the target shallowly, (2) Claude-Sonnet-4.6 and Gemini-3-Flash ReAct agents fail to solve it, (3) human annotators cannot solve it within 10 minutes using the same tools, and (4) corpus-level audit confirms exactly one valid paper. For Wide Research, candidate pools are domain-sourced, an entity graph is constructed, a strict-conjunction query is formulated and verified, answer sets are expanded via 7,136 LLM-generated search queries yielding 31,734 candidates that are screened to 23,217 by abstract filtering, 20,251 verified for full text, and 4,887 admitted by unanimous three-LLM majority vote; human experts audit 50% of passing papers and require ≥75% precision per query. Evaluation runs all models in a standardized ReAct loop (max 30 turns, soft context budget 1.1×10^5 tokens, temperature 0.6) over the DeepXiv full-text search tool. Deep Research is scored by exact-match accuracy; Wide Research by IoU = |Ŷ ∩ Y| / |Ŷ ∪ Y|. Critical design decisions: (1) full-text-first constraint mining over metadata-only — well-justified by the task's requirement that evidence be non-surfaceable via abstracts; (2) minimal-sufficiency pruning — well-justified as it prevents benchmark contamination by over-specified queries; (3) fixed ReAct evaluation loop shared across all models — appropriate for isolating model capability but does not distinguish model vs. tool-interaction effects. Key Results: Best Deep Research accuracy: Claude-Opus-4.6 at 9.39% (28.1 avg turns, 642s). Best Wide Research IoU: Gemini-3.1-Pro at 9.31% (4.55 avg turns, 235s). Most evaluated models score below 5% on both tasks. GPT-5.4 achieves 7.44% Deep accuracy in only 6.1 turns versus DeepSeek-V3.2's 4.21% in 28.8 turns, showing that more search iterations do not compensate for weak evidence integration. End-to-end systems: GPT Deep Research 11/50 queries correct (Deep), Alphaxiv 0/50, AI-studio Gemini-3.1-Pro 7/50. Thinking mode (extended reasoning) is inconsistently helpful: Qwen3-Max THINK 2.33% vs NOTHINK 3.24% (Deep); Gemini-3-Flash THINK 1.83% vs NOTHINK 2.75% (Deep). DeepXiv full-text search outperforms open-web (Jina) search by 1.45 percentage points averaged across four models on Deep Research (5.42% vs 3.97%).
