Test-time scaling (pass@k up to k=16) helps Deep Research more than Wide Research, indicating that Deep failures are brittle trajectory decisions while Wide failures are recall bottlenecks that repeated runs do not resolve. Claude-Sonnet-4.6 achieves the highest recall on Wide Research (0.3408) but the lowest precision (0.0586), versus Seed-2.0-Pro's highest precision (0.1787) with low recall (0.1304). Manual audit of model over-predictions: 96% were genuine true negatives confirming annotation completeness. Dataset construction cost: ~$6,700 API + ~580 expert-hours; per-model evaluation cost ~$27. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark construction methodology (full-text-first, minimal-sufficiency, four-stage adversarial verification) is technically non-obvious and represents a genuine advance over metadata-based or backward-constructed academic search benchmarks. The empirical finding that all frontier models fail to exceed 10% — while exceeding 80% on BrowseComp — quantifies a previously unmeasured and substantial capability gap. The task formulations (exact-match conjunctive paper identification; open-ended exhaustive set retrieval) are applied to a new domain but draw on established paradigms from information retrieval and agentic web search. Rigor & Reproducibility: Dataset quality is validated by a rigorous, multi-stage human-LLM pipeline and independently confirmed by the 96% true-negative audit. Code, dataset, and evaluation pipeline are publicly released (https://github.com/CherYou/AutoResearchBench). No ablations are presented on construction design choices (fuzzification depth, 10% no-answer ratio, constraint count per query), so the sensitivity of task difficulty to these parameters is unknown. Inter-annotator agreement rates are not reported, making independent assessment of annotation reliability difficult. The benchmark is a preprint and has not undergone peer review. Evaluation relies on the DeepXiv platform, which is not a standard public service; reproducibility is contingent on continued platform access. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: The evaluation environment requires API access to frontier LLMs and the DeepXiv full-text search platform; LLM APIs are commercially available, but DeepXiv's operational continuity for external researchers is not guaranteed. - Efficient: Per-model evaluation costs approximately $27 in API fees; construction required ~$6,700 and ~580 expert-hours, both within reach of well-resourced academic groups but not trivially reproducible. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrably measures a capability distinct from general web browsing (90+ percentage point performance gap versus BrowseComp), and the ground-truth quality audit supports confidence in the labels; however, evaluation is mediated by a specific search tool (DeepXiv), so scores reflect model × tool performance rather than model capability in isolation. - Scalable: The corpus is fixed at 3M+ CS papers; extension to other disciplines, dynamic corpora, and multi-modal evidence is explicitly deferred and would require substantial re-annotation investment. The work delivers a dataset, evaluation pipeline, and code as software artifacts. Strengths: - The four-stage adversarial verification pipeline for Deep Research — including agent-based stress testing and timed human adversarial search — is methodologically rigorous and substantially reduces the risk of benchmark contamination by trivially solvable or ambiguously posed queries. - The dual-task design captures two genuinely distinct and practically important literature-search demands (precise identification vs. exhaustive coverage), whereas prior benchmarks address only one or neither. - The structured nine-category error taxonomy with quantified per-model error distributions (e.g., Claude Opus 4.5 Wide Research dominated 85.3% by precision-unconstrained expansion) provides actionable diagnostic signal beyond aggregate scores. - The controlled 3M-paper full-text corpus with a standardized search tool enables apples-to-apples comparison across models, avoiding the confounds inherent in open-web evaluation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state the benchmark is restricted to CS papers in a fixed corpus, leaving cross-domain science, multi-modal evidence, and continually evolving literature for future work. They acknowledge that exhaustive answer sets for Wide Research may still be incomplete at corpus boundaries despite rigorous construction. Calibration, abstention behavior, and more efficient long-horizon search strategies are identified as open problems. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - All evaluations use a shared fixed ReAct loop and system prompt; the results measure model × tool × prompt interaction rather than isolated model capability, yet no ablation isolates these factors. - The 10% no-answer rate in Deep Research is an arbitrary design parameter; no analysis is provided of whether models are appropriately calibrated to abstain at this rate, or how this choice affects overall benchmark difficulty and model rankings. - Inter-annotator agreement statistics for human verification stages are absent, limiting independent assessment of annotation consistency. - IoU for Wide Research treats all set sizes equally; a query with 2 correct papers and one missed is penalized identically to a query with 20 papers and one missed, yet the latter is trivially harder — no stratified analysis by set size is provided. - The benchmark relies on a proprietary platform (DeepXiv) for both construction and evaluation; long-term reproducibility and community access are unconfirmed. Bottom Line: Researchers building or evaluating autonomous scientific literature agents should read this paper — the benchmark establishes a clear, reproducible performance floor and provides the most rigorous diagnostic of current agentic scientific search failures available, even if corpus scope and tool-dependency limit generalization.

Understanding Evolving Graph Structures for Large Discrete-Time Dynamic Graph Representation

www.vldb.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces UnderGS, a scalable GPU-resident framework for discrete-time dynamic graph (DTDG) representation that replaces adjacency-matrix neighbor management with a temporal-cohesive neighbor store, achieving up to 9× training speed-up and 31.36% average accuracy improvement over nine baselines across eight datasets. - Why it's important: Adjacency-matrix-based neighbor management for DTDGs consumes O(T|V|²) memory, prevents GPU-native batch processing, and captures only binary connectivity — making existing methods unable to scale to graphs with millions of edges or to faithfully represent structural evolution across snapshots. - How it does it: A GPU key-value store retains the top-K neighbors per node scored by a composite temporal influence score combining structural cohesiveness (node importance via subgraph density), path-length decay (α₁^(l-1)), and snapshot recency decay (α₂^(t-t')), together with a late-snapshot gradient aggregation mechanism that prevents information leakage during batch training. - The good: UnderGS is the only evaluated method that scales to the Stack-Overflow (2.6M nodes, 63M edges, 92 snapshots) and Wiki-Talk (1.1M nodes, 7.8M edges) datasets without out-of-memory errors, while simultaneously outperforming all baselines on both AUC and MRR tasks. - The missing: All experiments run on a single RTX 3090 (24 GB); the paper makes no claim about multi-GPU performance, and scalability beyond this hardware — or behavior on graphs with denser temporal dynamics than the tested datasets — is unevaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and engineers working on DTDG representation learning, temporal GNNs, or scalable graph ML infrastructure; it provides a principled, hardware-aware alternative to adjacency-matrix neighbor management with strong empirical evidence.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces UnderGS, a scalable GPU-resident framework for discrete-time dynamic graph (DTDG) representation that replaces adjacency-matrix neighbor management with a temporal-cohesive neighbor store, achieving up to 9× training speed-up and 31.36% average accuracy improvement over nine baselines across eight datasets. - Why it's important: Adjacency-matrix-based neighbor management for DTDGs consumes O(T|V|²) memory, prevents GPU-native batch processing, and captures only binary connectivity — making existing methods unable to scale to graphs with millions of edges or to faithfully represent structural evolution across snapshots. - How it does it: A GPU key-value store retains the top-K neighbors per node scored by a composite temporal influence score combining structural cohesiveness (node importance via subgraph density), path-length decay (α₁^(l-1)), and snapshot recency decay (α₂^(t-t')), together with a late-snapshot gradient aggregation mechanism that prevents information leakage during batch training. - The good: UnderGS is the only evaluated method that scales to the Stack-Overflow (2.6M nodes, 63M edges, 92 snapshots) and Wiki-Talk (1.1M nodes, 7.8M edges) datasets without out-of-memory errors, while simultaneously outperforming all baselines on both AUC and MRR tasks. - The missing: All experiments run on a single RTX 3090 (24 GB); the paper makes no claim about multi-GPU performance, and scalability beyond this hardware — or behavior on graphs with denser temporal dynamics than the tested datasets — is unevaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and engineers working on DTDG representation learning, temporal GNNs, or scalable graph ML infrastructure; it provides a principled, hardware-aware alternative to adjacency-matrix neighbor management with strong empirical evidence. Problem & Motivation: Discrete-time dynamic graphs model systems (retail, finance, social networks) that evolve through discrete snapshots, where each snapshot is a static graph. The central challenge for representation learning is neighbor management: existing approaches either maintain a full adjacency matrix per snapshot — consuming O(T|V|²) memory and restricting scalability — or preprocess multiple adjacency matrices into node sequences for sequence models, incurring hours of preprocessing and CPU–GPU transfer overhead (e.g., FALCON requires 2.2 hours on Reddit-title). Both lines fail simultaneously: snapshot-focused GNN approaches lose cross-snapshot temporal context, while sequence-based approaches lose intra-snapshot structural context. Neither line scales to graphs with millions of nodes or hundreds of snapshots because they cannot batch-process efficiently on a single GPU. The gap is a neighbor management strategy that captures structural evolution, preserves temporal context, and fits in GPU memory without preprocessing. Key Idea: The core insight is to replace the adjacency matrix with a GPU-resident key-value store that, for each node, retains exactly K neighbors selected by a temporal influence score — a single scalar that fuses three quantities: structural importance of the neighbor (cohesiveness of its local subgraph), path-length discount (closer hops score higher), and snapshot recency discount (more recent interactions score higher). Because the store caps at K entries per node, total memory drops from O(T|V|²) to O(|V|K), independent of snapshot count. The store is maintained incrementally as new snapshots arrive: L-hop traversals compute influence scores for candidates; new neighbors displace incumbents if their scores are higher; a snapshot decay pass reduces all stored scores at snapshot boundaries. This design allows direct GPU access for message passing without preprocessing, and the influence score serves as an aggregation weight replacing degree normalization. Methodology: UnderGS consists of four interacting components. (1) Temporal influence score: For node vᵢ(t) and neighbor vⱼ(t') interacting over l hops, Iᵢⱼ(t) = Cⱼ(t') · α₁^(l-1) · α₂^(t-t'), where Cⱼ(t') is the node importance defined as a sum of exponential terms over the interaction counts within vⱼ's subgraph, α₁ ∈ (0,1) is the path-decay hyperparameter, and α₂ ∈ (0,1) is the snapshot-decay factor. Node importance distinguishes structurally cohesive neighbors from peripheral ones, which degree alone cannot. The paper proves three formal properties: Snapshot Decay (Theorem 4.6 — more recent neighbors are preferred when structurally comparable), Cohesive Priority (Theorem 4.7 — neighbors in cohesive subgraphs are retained over sparser ones), and Path Decay (Theorem 4.8 — direct neighbors score higher than equidistant distant ones). (2) Temporal-cohesive neighbor store: A GPU key-value store with O(1) lookup and update per node; initialized from scratch and updated incrementally per batch or snapshot; reduces storage from O(T|V|²) to O(|V|K). K=16 and L=2 in all experiments. (3) Time-aware filter: For nodes with sparse current-snapshot interactions, historical neighbors can dominate. A Bernoulli mask with probability ρ (tuned by dataset density: ρ=0.3 for high density, ρ=0.5 for low density) randomly zeros out historical neighbor influence scores before aggregation, shifting focus toward current interactions. (4) Late-snapshot gradient aggregation: Each snapshot G(t) is partitioned into B(t) mini-batches; losses are computed independently per batch using binary cross-entropy on link prediction labels, but gradients are accumulated across all batches of the snapshot and applied once after the full snapshot is processed (θ ← θ − η · (1/B(t)) ΣᵦᵢB(t) ∇L^b_{G(t)}). This prevents later batches from being influenced by parameter updates from earlier batches within the same snapshot (information leakage). The framework is compatible with six neural network architectures (GCN, GIN, GAT, GraphSAGE, MLP Mixer, Transformer), using influence scores as aggregation weights in lieu of degree normalization; message embedding Mᵢⱼ(t) = [Zⱼ(t-1) ‖ eᵢⱼ(t') ‖ φ(t-t')]Wₘ concatenates node embedding, edge features, and a time encoding. Key Results: Evaluated on 8 datasets spanning multiple domains and scales (UCI-Message: 1,899 nodes; Stack-Overflow: 2.6M nodes, 63M edges, 92 snapshots). Baselines are 9 models: 4 GNN-based (EvolveGCN-H, EvolveGCN-O, Roland, WinGNN) and 5 sequence-based (DGNN-LSTM, DGNN-GRU, DGNN-Transformer, SimpleDyG, FALCON). All experiments use a single GeForce RTX 3090 (24 GB RAM). Key quantitative outcomes: (1) AUC (link prediction): UnderGS achieves best results on 7/8 datasets; up to 13.56% improvement over the best baseline; e.g., 99.97% AUC on Bitcoin-OTC vs. 99.75% for second-best. Only Roland is competitive on small datasets. (2) MRR (link ranking): UnderGS achieves best on all 8 datasets; average 59.20% improvement; e.g., 95.01% MRR on Bitcoin-Alpha vs. 36.20% for second-best (SimpleDyG). (3) Training efficiency: Up to 9× overall speed-up vs. best baseline; up to 6× per-epoch speed-up. (4) Scalability: 4 of 9 baselines (DGNN variants, FALCON) OOM on 5+ datasets; only UnderGS completes all 8 including Stack-Overflow and Wiki-Talk. (5) vs. TGL (multi-GPU framework): UnderGS achieves 94× per-epoch sampling speed-up, 10× overall training speed-up, and 24.07% better AUC+MRR on average — while running on a single GPU. Ablation (Table 3): removing snapshot decay drops MRR by up to 82.30%; removing path decay drops by up to 26.02%; replacing node importance with k-core drops MRR by 9.29% on average; the time-aware filter contributes 4.35% on average. Training pipeline ablation (Table 4): pipeline yields 6.93–10.40× overall speed-up and 3.65–10.44× per-epoch speed-up vs. snapshot-by-snapshot training. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The temporal influence score combining structural cohesiveness, path decay, and snapshot decay as a unified neighbor selection criterion is genuinely new; the GPU-resident incremental neighbor store that replaces adjacency matrices in GNN training is a novel infrastructure contribution; the late-snapshot gradient aggregation addressing information leakage in DTDG mini-batch training is a targeted engineering advance. The individual components (influence-weighted aggregation, GPU key-value stores, gradient accumulation) are known; the novelty is their integration into a principled, theoretically grounded neighbor management framework for DTDGs. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough: 5 structural variants tested across all 8 datasets (Table 3); training pipeline ablation on 2 datasets (Table 4); compatibility tested across 6 neural networks with and without influence scores (Figures 6–7). Experiments are repeated 10 times and mean ± standard deviation is reported. Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/dani0108/UnderGS (PVLDB Artifact Availability badge confirmed). The paper was published in PVLDB Vol. 19, No. 5, 2026 (peer-reviewed). Hyperparameters (K=16, L=2, α₁=0.1, α₂=0.05, filter ratio 40%) are reported; ρ is density-dependent (ρ=0.3 or 0.5) which introduces a tuning step not fully automated. The embedding dimension is fixed at 128 and batch size at 200 for all models, ensuring fair comparison. Theoretical proofs for the three core properties are deferred to a technical report (Sections 2.1 and 2.2 thereof); the main paper contains proof sketches, which limits independent verification from the paper alone. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (built framework evaluated by deployment on real datasets). - Practical: Runs on a single commodity GPU (RTX 3090, 24 GB) on all 8 tested datasets including the 63M-edge Stack-Overflow graph. - Efficient: Neighbor store reduces memory from O(T|V|²) to O(|V|K); eliminates preprocessing (2.2 hours for FALCON on Reddit-title); achieves 6–9× training speed-up over the best baselines; per-epoch sampling is 94× faster than TGL. - Effective: Outperforms all 9 baselines on AUC (7/8 datasets) and MRR (8/8 datasets) with statistically meaningful margins (error bars reported); ablations confirm each component contributes materially, not just the aggregate. - Scalable: Tested at up to 2.6M nodes and 63M edges (Stack-Overflow), 7.8M edges with 324 snapshots (Wiki-Talk); no experiment exceeds this scale; behavior at 10×–100× scale on billion-edge graphs is not tested, though the O(|V|K) theoretical bound is favorable. The paper delivers a software artifact (GitHub repository with source code and data). Strengths: - The temporal influence score is principled and theoretically grounded with three proven properties (Theorems 4.6–4.8), giving the neighbor selection mechanism a formal foundation absent in prior adjacency-matrix approaches. - The scalability gap versus competitors is substantial and not marginal: UnderGS is the only method completing all 8 datasets on a single GPU, including graphs where every alternative OOMs; the 94× sampling speed-up over TGL (a multi-GPU system) is the most striking empirical result. - Compatibility with six heterogeneous neural architectures (message-passing and non-message-passing) without architectural modifications demonstrates genuine generality of the neighbor store abstraction. - The late-snapshot gradient aggregation addresses a concrete information-leakage failure mode in DTDG batch training that prior work ignores, and ablation (Table 4) shows it yields 6.93–10.40× speed-up over snapshot-by-snapshot training. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper does not include a dedicated limitations section. The conclusion notes that UnderGS targets single-GPU settings, contrasting it with multi-GPU approaches; no explicit failure modes or scope boundaries are stated. The density-dependent filter probability ρ is acknowledged implicitly as a dataset-specific hyperparameter but not discussed as a limitation. The authors do not discuss behavior on highly skewed degree distributions (power-law graphs), adversarially structured temporal graphs, or directed weighted graphs beyond those tested. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Hyperparameter sensitivity for ρ: The time-aware filter probability is set per dataset based on an undisclosed density threshold (10⁻⁴). The paper does not report a sensitivity analysis for ρ, leaving unclear how sensitive performance is to this choice and how a practitioner would set it without labeled validation data. - Single-hardware evaluation: All experiments use one specific GPU. The O(|V|K) memory claim is theoretically favorable, but the paper does not demonstrate behavior when the neighbor store itself exceeds GPU memory (e.g., billion-node graphs), nor does it discuss CPU-offload strategies for this regime. - Transductive setting only: All downstream tasks (link prediction, link ranking) are evaluated on edges within the training graph's node set. Inductive generalization to entirely unseen nodes at inference time is not evaluated, limiting applicability to dynamic graphs with frequent node arrivals. - Theoretical proof completeness: The three key theorems are proved in a separate technical report not included in the PVLDB submission; the main paper contains only proof sketches, which a peer reviewer cannot independently verify from the paper alone. - No comparison to hyperbolic or geometry-aware DTDG methods (e.g., the authors' own prior work on hyperbolic temporal graphs, refs [23, 47]) which have shown strong performance on sparse social graphs — an omission that slightly narrows the baseline comparison. Bottom Line: A practitioner building DTDG representation learning pipelines gains a concretely useful contribution here — UnderGS offers a principled, GPU-efficient replacement for adjacency-matrix neighbor management with strong empirical support across 8 diverse datasets and publicly released code, making it the current method of choice for single-GPU DTDG training at scale.

Where does output diversity collapse in post-training?

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates, through a controlled comparison of three parallel post-training lineages of the Olmo 3 7B model, that output diversity collapse is determined by training data composition—not post-training algorithm—and cannot be recovered at inference time. - Why it's important: Inference-time scaling methods (pass@k, majority voting, self-consistency) depend on output diversity; if post-training eliminates that diversity, the scaling benefits are lost, and value-laden tasks risk homogenized outputs. - How it does it: The paper exploits Olmo 3's public checkpoints across three lineages—Think (narrow two-teacher CoT distillation), Instruct (broad multi-source), and RL-Zero (RL directly from base)—that share the same SFT→DPO→RL recipe but differ in data, then measures diversity at each checkpoint across 15 tasks with four metrics, and surgically removes CoT at inference to separate format from weights. - The good: The CoT suppression experiment (Think-not-thinking) is definitive: removing chain-of-thought format costs 18–32% accuracy on hard tasks but leaves SBERT diversity unchanged at every stage, proving the collapse is weight-embedded, not format-imposed. - The missing: The study is purely observational within existing checkpoints; no direct data composition intervention is run, so the causal claim that "more teachers → less collapse" is supported by cross-lineage comparison rather than a controlled experiment. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on LLM post-training, inference-time compute scaling, or alignment diversity; it provides concrete numbers and a clean experimental argument that inference-side fixes for diversity collapse are insufficient.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates, through a controlled comparison of three parallel post-training lineages of the Olmo 3 7B model, that output diversity collapse is determined by training data composition—not post-training algorithm—and cannot be recovered at inference time. - Why it's important: Inference-time scaling methods (pass@k, majority voting, self-consistency) depend on output diversity; if post-training eliminates that diversity, the scaling benefits are lost, and value-laden tasks risk homogenized outputs. - How it does it: The paper exploits Olmo 3's public checkpoints across three lineages—Think (narrow two-teacher CoT distillation), Instruct (broad multi-source), and RL-Zero (RL directly from base)—that share the same SFT→DPO→RL recipe but differ in data, then measures diversity at each checkpoint across 15 tasks with four metrics, and surgically removes CoT at inference to separate format from weights. - The good: The CoT suppression experiment (Think-not-thinking) is definitive: removing chain-of-thought format costs 18–32% accuracy on hard tasks but leaves SBERT diversity unchanged at every stage, proving the collapse is weight-embedded, not format-imposed. - The missing: The study is purely observational within existing checkpoints; no direct data composition intervention is run, so the causal claim that "more teachers → less collapse" is supported by cross-lineage comparison rather than a controlled experiment. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on LLM post-training, inference-time compute scaling, or alignment diversity; it provides concrete numbers and a clean experimental argument that inference-side fixes for diversity collapse are insufficient. Problem & Motivation: Post-trained LLMs produce less varied outputs than their base counterparts, and this homogenization undermines inference-time scaling methods—pass@k, majority voting, and self-consistency—that require diverse samples to aggregate over. The problem extends to creative and value-laden tasks where output homogenization risks imposing a single perspective. Prior work attributed collapse to specific algorithms (DPO in narrative generation, SFT in reasoning, reward in creative tasks) without disentangling whether the algorithm or the training data caused the drop, and without testing whether the chain-of-thought generation format itself constrains diversity at inference. Key Idea: When two models share identical post-training algorithms (SFT→DPO→RL) but differ only in data—narrow two-teacher CoT distillation versus broad multi-source instruction tuning—diversity collapses at different stages and by different magnitudes, proving that data composition, not algorithm, determines when and how sharply collapse occurs. The secondary insight is that suppressing CoT at inference on a collapsed model does not restore diversity, demonstrating the collapse is baked into learned weights rather than imposed by generation format. Methodology: The study evaluates 13 Olmo 3 7B checkpoints across three post-training lineages. Think uses SFT on ∼2.3M synthetic CoT traces from two teachers (QwQ-32B and DeepSeek-R1), followed by DPO on ∼200K Delta Learning pairs and GRPO-based RL on ∼105K prompts. Instruct initializes from Think-SFT, then trains on ∼2.2M multi-source examples (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4.1) that strip reasoning traces, uses the same prompt pool for DPO but with GPT-judged pairs, and the same RL stage. RL-Zero applies RL directly to Base, bypassing SFT and DPO, in four domain-specific variants. Four complementary metrics are measured per checkpoint on 15 tasks across six categories: EAD (lexical, unique n-grams normalized for length bias), SBERT (semantic, mean pairwise cosine distance with all-mpnet-base-v2), NLI (logical, pairwise entailment/contradiction scoring with roberta-large-mnli), and Vendi Score (effective number of distinct semantic modes). K=16 outputs per prompt at T=0.6 are generated; for Think-lineage models, reasoning traces are stripped before computing metrics. A Think-not-thinking condition prefills an empty `<think>\n</think>` block to force direct answers, isolating format from weights. Quality-filtered diversity decomposes aggregate Da (SBERT on all outputs) into Dc (SBERT on correct-only subset) and Vc (Vendi Score on correct outputs) for six verifiable tasks. Three critical design decisions: (1) using matched temperature T=0.6 for all models including Base, validated in Appendix H to introduce only ∼11% SBERT reduction versus the 62% collapse observed at SFT—an order-of-magnitude smaller effect; (2) stripping CoT traces before any metric computation so diversity reflects final answers only; (3) using C13 13-gram matching to flag six benchmarks with 7–30% training overlap, though results on contaminated and clean tasks are consistent. Key Results: Think-SFT loses 62% of Base SBERT diversity on average across 15 tasks, versus 38% for Instruct-SFT. Despite initializing from the already-collapsed Think-SFT, Instruct-SFT recovers a median 40% of lost diversity due to its multi-source data. DPO removes 23% further diversity in Instruct versus only 4% in Think (which has little remaining to remove). RL-Zero retains ≥71% of Base diversity with a median of 94%. Both supervised lineages converge to similar final floors: Think retains 38% and Instruct 34% of Base diversity. The Vendi Score drops from ∼3.4 effective semantic modes (Base) to ∼1.8 at final checkpoints, with near-total collapse on math (GSM8K: 1.3 modes, MATH-Algebra: 1.4). EAD (lexical diversity) remains stable or increases even as SBERT drops, a dissociation replicated across all tasks—aligned models use varied vocabulary to express semantically identical content. Value-pluralism tasks show the steepest Think collapse (PRISM −78%, TruthfulQA −79%). The CoT suppression experiment finds zero diversity recovery on any task-stage combination: Think-not-thinking SBERT tracks Think within noise at every stage, while accuracy drops substantially (IFEval −8%, GSM8K −18%, HumanEval −32%, MATH-Geometry −32%). Quality decomposition reveals a task-dependent split: on IFEval, 83.4% of the diversity drop persists in Dc (genuine narrowing among correct outputs); on HumanEval, less than 10% is genuine (most collapse reflects elimination of wrong outputs). Think gains only +0.4 percentage points from majority voting on GSM8K (16 near-identical correct answers), while Base gains +24% and RL-Zero +22–26%. On TruthfulQA, majority voting hurts all models (majority vote@16 < accuracy@1) because collapse amplifies the dominant misconception. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The contribution is a controlled dissociation of post-training algorithm effects from data composition effects on output diversity, plus the CoT-suppression falsification test. The experimental apparatus (multi-lineage comparison with identical recipes but different data, plus out-of-distribution inference interventions) is methodologically sharper than prior work, which confounded method and data. The quality-filtered decomposition framework is a useful analytical tool but draws on established ideas. The finding that both supervised lineages converge to the same diversity floor regardless of training trajectory is counter-intuitive and practically significant. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are not applicable in the traditional sense—this is an observational study across existing checkpoints rather than a system with tunable components. The four-metric setup (EAD, SBERT, NLI, Vendi Score) provides complementary views, and the finding that EAD diverges from SBERT is itself a meaningful result. Training-evaluation contamination is measured and flagged; results are consistent across clean and contaminated benchmarks. Temperature sensitivity is assessed in a dedicated appendix and shown to be small relative to the observed effects. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/ckarouzos/where-diversity-collapses/. The LLM-as-judge evaluation (gpt-4.1-mini via OpenAI Batch API) for non-verifiable tasks introduces vendor dependence and known limitations for creative/value-laden tasks, which the authors acknowledge. No error bars or significance tests are reported on the main SBERT diversity comparisons; the study relies on consistent directional patterns across 15 tasks as evidence of reliability. This is a preprint under review. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All experiments use vLLM and lighteval on standard HPC hardware; no proprietary infrastructure required beyond API access to gpt-4.1-mini for judge evaluation. - Efficient: The evaluation requires 13 models × 15 tasks × 500 prompts × 16 samples = ∼1.56M forward passes, plus embedding and NLI inference; substantial but within reach of academic compute with HPC access (acknowledged in the paper). - Effective: The evidence convincingly demonstrates that collapse is weight-embedded and data-driven for the specific Olmo 3 7B family; the practical implication—avoid narrow-teacher SFT—is well-supported within this scope. - Scalable: Tested exclusively at 7B; whether the same stage-attribution pattern holds at 70B+ is not examined and remains an open question. The convergence to a similar diversity floor across lineages might differ at scale. The work delivers a code artifact and data analysis rather than a deployable system or trained model. Strengths: - The parallel-lineage design is the strongest feature: Think and Instruct share identical algorithms and differ only in data, providing a cleaner causal structure for attribution than any prior single-lineage analysis. - The CoT-suppression experiment directly falsifies the hypothesis that generation format drives diversity collapse, resolving a confound present in all prior Think-vs-Instruct comparisons. - The quality-filtered decomposition (Da vs. Dc vs. Vc) is a concrete methodological contribution that resolves a genuine debate in the field about whether diversity collapse is "just quality control" or genuine homogenization—and shows it is task-dependent. - Breath of evaluation across 15 tasks spanning six qualitatively different categories produces a coherent picture of task-dependent diversity-quality tradeoffs. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note they measure final-answer diversity rather than reasoning-path diversity, which may underestimate collapse in the Think lineage where CoT traces are stripped. They distinguish distributional diversity (measured here) from representational diversity (which perspectives are preserved), noting that a model could appear collapsed distributionally while retaining key stances, or vice versa. LLM-judge evaluation of creative and value-laden tasks has known limitations, and those results are presented as supplementary context rather than primary evidence. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The study is observational within existing checkpoints; no direct data composition intervention (e.g., training on 4 vs. 2 vs. 10 teachers with fixed data volume) is run, so the causal mechanism connecting teacher breadth to collapse magnitude is inferred rather than established. The two teachers used for Think (QwQ-32B, DeepSeek-R1) come from the same training lineage and likely occupy a narrow region of output space; the paper does not characterize the diversity of teacher outputs themselves, which would strengthen the mechanistic interpretation. The study covers only 7B-scale models from a single family (Olmo 3); the convergence-to-same-floor finding could be specific to this architecture, pretraining mix, or scale. The RL-Zero diversity advantage (median 94% of Base retained) comes at a massive accuracy penalty (49.8–61% on GSM8K vs. 93% Think), and this tradeoff is described but not deeply analyzed—it is unclear whether entropy-regularized RL could preserve some RL-Zero diversity without the quality collapse. The paper does not examine whether simple interventions like increasing the number of SFT teachers from 2 to 8 would shift the diversity floor or just the trajectory. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on inference-time compute scaling, preference optimization, or model alignment will extract concrete and actionable findings here: diversity collapse is a data problem, not an algorithm problem, and you cannot fix it at inference time—read it.

Rethinking On-Policy Distillation of Large Language Models: Phenomenology, Mechanism, and Recipe

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper identifies two governing conditions for on-policy distillation (OPD) success — thinking-pattern consistency between student and teacher, and the teacher possessing genuinely new knowledge beyond what the student has seen — reveals the causal token-level mechanism (progressive alignment on shared high-probability overlap tokens), and proposes two practical recipes (off-policy cold start and teacher-aligned prompt selection) to recover failing OPD runs. - Why it's important: OPD is deployed in production post-training pipelines for Qwen3, MiMo, and GLM-5, yet practitioners use teacher benchmark scores as a proxy for distillation quality — a proxy this paper demonstrates is unreliable and can predict the wrong outcome. - How it does it: Controlled reverse-distillation experiments hold the student fixed while varying the teacher, and token-level metrics (overlap ratio, overlap-token advantage, entropy gap) are tracked throughout training to distinguish successful from failing runs causally, not just correlationally. - The good: The reverse distillation finding — that a weaker 1.5B teacher and a strictly stronger 7B teacher from the same model family drive the student to virtually identical (poor) outcomes despite the 7B outperforming on benchmarks — is a concrete, hard-to-dismiss counter-example to the dominant intuition that stronger teachers are always better. - The missing: All experiments use mathematical reasoning benchmarks (AIME 2024, AIME 2025, AMC 2023) with models in the 1.5B–7B range; generalization to code, open-ended tasks, or frontier-scale models is entirely untested. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers designing LLM post-training pipelines should read this; it provides precisely defined diagnostic metrics and empirically validated recipes, though its math-only scope and absence of RL baselines leave the most practically decisive comparison open.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper identifies two governing conditions for on-policy distillation (OPD) success — thinking-pattern consistency between student and teacher, and the teacher possessing genuinely new knowledge beyond what the student has seen — reveals the causal token-level mechanism (progressive alignment on shared high-probability overlap tokens), and proposes two practical recipes (off-policy cold start and teacher-aligned prompt selection) to recover failing OPD runs. - Why it's important: OPD is deployed in production post-training pipelines for Qwen3, MiMo, and GLM-5, yet practitioners use teacher benchmark scores as a proxy for distillation quality — a proxy this paper demonstrates is unreliable and can predict the wrong outcome. - How it does it: Controlled reverse-distillation experiments hold the student fixed while varying the teacher, and token-level metrics (overlap ratio, overlap-token advantage, entropy gap) are tracked throughout training to distinguish successful from failing runs causally, not just correlationally. - The good: The reverse distillation finding — that a weaker 1.5B teacher and a strictly stronger 7B teacher from the same model family drive the student to virtually identical (poor) outcomes despite the 7B outperforming on benchmarks — is a concrete, hard-to-dismiss counter-example to the dominant intuition that stronger teachers are always better. - The missing: All experiments use mathematical reasoning benchmarks (AIME 2024, AIME 2025, AMC 2023) with models in the 1.5B–7B range; generalization to code, open-ended tasks, or frontier-scale models is entirely untested. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers designing LLM post-training pipelines should read this; it provides precisely defined diagnostic metrics and empirically validated recipes, though its math-only scope and absence of RL baselines leave the most practically decisive comparison open. Problem & Motivation On-policy distillation uses the student's own sampled rollouts as training contexts and leverages the teacher's per-token log-probabilities as dense reward, avoiding the exposure-bias problem of supervised fine-tuning on fixed teacher sequences. Despite rapid adoption in industry post-training pipelines, OPD exhibits a striking failure mode: a stronger teacher can completely fail to improve the student while a weaker teacher succeeds. No prior work has systematically identified the conditions under which OPD succeeds or fails, nor explained the token-level mechanism through which teacher supervision steers the student. Practitioners therefore lack principled tools to select teachers, configure runs, or diagnose failures — and the implicit assumption that higher teacher benchmark scores guarantee better distillation is empirically wrong. Key Idea The central insight is that OPD's effective gradient signal is concentrated almost entirely on the small set of tokens that appear in both the student's and the teacher's high-probability top-k sets (the "overlap" region), which carries 97–99% of the combined probability mass. For OPD to succeed, (1) this overlap must be non-negligible from the outset (thinking-pattern consistency), and (2) the teacher must assign different probabilities to those shared tokens than the student already learned (new knowledge). When both conditions hold, the overlap set grows self-reinforcingly — each reverse-KL update pushes more student mass toward teacher-preferred tokens within the overlap, enlarging it further — creating a virtuous cycle. When either condition fails, the overlap stagnates and gradient updates point in incoherent directions despite the teacher's reward being globally informative. Methodology The paper is organized into three empirical components. In phenomenology (§3), the authors design controlled OPD experiments holding the student fixed and varying the teacher across two model families: (i) Qwen3-1.7B-Base with Qwen3-4B (non-thinking) vs. Qwen3-4B-Base-GRPO, and (ii) R1-Distill-1.5B with R1-Distill-7B vs. Skywork-OR1-Math-7B. A reverse-distillation experiment uses JustRL-1.5B (an RL-trained 1.5B model) as the student, distilling from its own pre-RL checkpoint (R1-Distill-1.5B) and from R1-Distill-7B, to disentangle thinking-pattern alignment from benchmark performance. In mechanism (§4), three dynamic metrics — overlap ratio M_overlap (fraction of shared top-k tokens), overlap-token advantage M_adv (average log-ratio within overlap), and entropy gap ΔH (|H(q_t)−H(p_t)|) — are tracked throughout training. An ablation partitions the top-k support into overlap and non-overlap subsets and trains on each in isolation to test causal sufficiency. In recipe (§5), off-policy cold start (SFT on teacher rollouts before OPD) and teacher-aligned prompts (matching the prompt template or content from the teacher's post-training data) are each tested as interventions on otherwise-failing configurations. In §6, reward reliability is probed by training across six maximum response lengths (0.5K–15K tokens) and measuring teacher continuation accuracy from student-generated prefixes at truncation depths of 1K, 4K, 8K, 16K tokens. The loss throughout is per-token reverse KL over student-generated trajectories. All math experiments use DAPO-Math-17K or subsets of DeepMath, evaluated at avg@16 (temperature 0.7, top-p 0.95) on AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and AMC 2023. Key Results Thinking-pattern consistency: Skywork-OR1-Math-7B (post-RL teacher) achieves a 16.9% gap recovery rate vs. 5.3% for same-family R1-Distill-7B in the DeepSeek family; in the Qwen family, Qwen3-4B-Non-Thinking-RL-Math achieves 58.6% vs. 15.6% for Qwen3-4B (non-thinking), despite the RL teachers sharing the same base checkpoint as the student. Reverse distillation: both R1-Distill-1.5B and R1-Distill-7B as teachers drive JustRL-1.5B to the same regressed accuracy level (~0.28 on AIME 2025), with indistinguishable training curves despite the 7B scoring higher than 1.5B on benchmarks. Overlap sufficiency: Overlap Top-k recovers full Student Top-k performance on all three benchmarks, while Non-Overlap Top-k consistently underperforms; overlap ratio rises from ~72% to ~91% in successful runs. Off-policy cold start: SFT-initialized OPD outperforms pure OPD throughout training and maintains a higher overlap ratio and lower entropy gap from the start. Reward degradation: teacher continuation advantage over student prefixes decreases monotonically from +0.37 at 1K tokens to +0.02 at 16K tokens; moderate response lengths (3K–7K tokens) yield the strongest downstream accuracy, while 10K–15K exhibits late-stage collapse with entropy spikes and overlap ratio drops. Sampled-token OPD matches Top-4 through Top-64 on all three benchmarks; Top-1 consistently underperforms due to biased mode-concentrated selection. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FORMULATION. The two governing conditions and the associated diagnostic metrics are new empirical contributions — prior OPD work focused on demonstrating promise across varied objectives without analyzing when or why it fails. The reverse distillation design is an original controlled experiment not seen in the literature. The overlap-token mechanism provides a new formulation of what OPD optimizes in practice. This is a mechanistic study of an existing technique rather than a new training algorithm; the genuinely novel contribution is the causal evidence (from ablations and controlled reversals) rather than a new method. Rigor & Reproducibility The overlap vs. non-overlap ablation directly tests the causal claim (not just observational correlation). The reverse distillation experiment is a well-designed internal control isolating thinking-pattern from benchmark performance. Multiple model families (DeepSeek, Qwen) and three benchmarks provide empirical breadth. avg@16 reduces sample variance at the problem level, though no formal significance tests, confidence intervals, or error bars appear anywhere in the paper. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/thunlp/OPD; trained model weights and full training datasets are not released. The paper is an arXiv preprint (April 2026), not peer-reviewed. One Goodhart's Law concern: all evaluation benchmarks (AIME, AMC) are in the same mathematical reasoning domain as the training data; the recipes may be optimizing for the evaluation domain rather than generalizable OPD behavior. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All experiments use 1.5B–7B parameter models and standard GPU training; no specialized hardware is required, and the recommended sampled-token OPD is the cheapest computational variant. - Efficient: The finding that sampled-token OPD matches Top-4 through Top-64 directly simplifies implementation; the off-policy cold start adds a SFT phase but the paper does not report its compute cost relative to additional RL training. - Effective: The overlap-token ablation provides causal evidence rather than correlation, and the recovery recipes show measurable gains across all three benchmarks on previously failing configurations. - Scalable: Not evaluated beyond 7B parameter models or 200 training steps; the reward degradation finding at long trajectories (>7K tokens) is a concrete limitation for long-horizon and agentic settings that the authors explicitly acknowledge but do not resolve. The work delivers code (scripts and training configs); no pre-trained model weights, no new dataset, and no standalone library. Strengths - The reverse distillation experiment is an elegant natural experiment that simultaneously validates both conditions — by showing a weaker and a stronger same-family teacher produce identical outcomes, the paper rules out benchmark performance as a confound in a single controlled design. - The overlap-token sufficiency ablation provides interventional (not just observational) evidence that the overlap set is the causal gradient locus, distinguishing this analysis from descriptive diagnostic papers. - The reward degradation analysis (§6) identifies a fundamental ceiling for OPD in long-horizon settings: teacher continuation advantage collapses from +0.37 at 1K to +0.02 at 16K prefix depth, with collapse propagating backward through the trajectory — an important finding for practitioners targeting agentic or extended chain-of-thought settings. - The three diagnostic metrics (overlap ratio, overlap-token advantage, entropy gap) are precisely defined and consistently discriminate successful from failing runs across model families and teacher-student pairs, providing monitoring tools that a practitioner can implement directly. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: (1) all experiments are on mathematical reasoning benchmarks and generalization to code and open-ended tasks remains open; (2) isolating the new-knowledge condition from pre-training corpus differences (vs. tokenizer or architecture differences) requires controlled pre-training ablations that are prohibitively expensive; (3) the anisotropy hypothesis for why globally informative rewards fail locally is suggestive but not directly verified; (4) teacher-aligned prompts substantially reduce student entropy, requiring mixing with out-of-distribution prompts to preserve exploration. These are confronted explicitly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paper does not compare OPD-with-recipe against RL (GRPO/PPO) with the same compute budget; this is the most practically decisive comparison for post-training pipeline designers, and its absence leaves the central design question — when to choose OPD over RL — unanswered. - All students are base or lightly RL-trained models at 1.5B–7B scale; whether thinking-pattern alignment plays the same role at 70B+ is unknown and cannot be inferred from the current experiments. - The AIME/AMC evaluation domain matches the training data domain exactly; no out-of-domain generalization test is reported, which creates a risk that the recipes optimize for benchmark-specific patterns rather than general OPD behavior. - "Thinking pattern" is operationalized through the observable overlap ratio metric but is not defined a priori; a practitioner cannot determine compatibility before training begins, limiting the prescriptive value of the framework. - The off-policy cold start recipe's total compute cost (SFT + OPD) versus just running more RL from the base model is never reported, which is necessary for a cost-benefit assessment. Bottom Line Practitioners designing OPD post-training pipelines gain concrete diagnostic tools and empirically validated recipes from this work, anchored by a well-designed reverse-distillation experiment that overturns the "stronger teacher is always better" assumption — but the math-only evaluation scope, absence of RL baselines, and untested scaling behavior mean the work answers "why does OPD fail?" more definitively than "should you use OPD at all?"

Expert Upcycling: Shifting the Compute-Efficient Frontier of Mixture-of-Experts

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces expert upcycling, a method that progressively expands a trained E-expert MoE model into an mE-expert model mid-training by duplicating existing experts and extending the router, while holding top-K routing fixed so per-token inference cost is unchanged. - Why it's important: Training large MoE models from scratch is expensive because all expert weights, gradients, and optimizer states must reside in memory regardless of sparsity, and all-to-all communication overhead consumes 45–50% of training time — expert upcycling captures the quality benefits of a larger expert count without paying the full from-scratch training cost. - How it does it: The upcycling operator duplicates existing expert weights (non-uniformly, guided by gradient-norm importance scores) and copies their router entries with small bias noise, providing a warm initialization that starts near the source model's loss; subsequent continued pre-training (CPT) then breaks symmetry among duplicates through stochastic gradient diversity and loss-free load balancing. - The good: In the 7B→13B total parameter experiment (32→64 experts), the upcycled model matches Fixed-64 on validation loss (1.263 vs. 1.267) and average benchmark accuracy (56.4 vs. 56.7) across 11 tasks while saving 32% of GPU hours (27,888 vs. 41,328). - The missing: Experiments are limited to doubling expert count (m=2) at scales up to 7B total parameters — no validation that the approach holds at m>2 or at frontier scales (100B+ total parameters) where router collapse and communication dynamics differ qualitatively. - Read it or not: Read it if you train or plan to train sparse MoE models and want a practical, theoretically grounded strategy for growing expert capacity without restarting training; skip if your interest is in dense model scaling or inference-time efficiency.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces expert upcycling, a method that progressively expands a trained E-expert MoE model into an mE-expert model mid-training by duplicating existing experts and extending the router, while holding top-K routing fixed so per-token inference cost is unchanged. - Why it's important: Training large MoE models from scratch is expensive because all expert weights, gradients, and optimizer states must reside in memory regardless of sparsity, and all-to-all communication overhead consumes 45–50% of training time — expert upcycling captures the quality benefits of a larger expert count without paying the full from-scratch training cost. - How it does it: The upcycling operator duplicates existing expert weights (non-uniformly, guided by gradient-norm importance scores) and copies their router entries with small bias noise, providing a warm initialization that starts near the source model's loss; subsequent continued pre-training (CPT) then breaks symmetry among duplicates through stochastic gradient diversity and loss-free load balancing. - The good: In the 7B→13B total parameter experiment (32→64 experts), the upcycled model matches Fixed-64 on validation loss (1.263 vs. 1.267) and average benchmark accuracy (56.4 vs. 56.7) across 11 tasks while saving 32% of GPU hours (27,888 vs. 41,328). - The missing: Experiments are limited to doubling expert count (m=2) at scales up to 7B total parameters — no validation that the approach holds at m>2 or at frontier scales (100B+ total parameters) where router collapse and communication dynamics differ qualitatively. - Read it or not: Read it if you train or plan to train sparse MoE models and want a practical, theoretically grounded strategy for growing expert capacity without restarting training; skip if your interest is in dense model scaling or inference-time efficiency. Problem & Motivation: Training MoE language models with large expert counts from scratch is computationally expensive in a specific way: all expert weights, gradients, and optimizer states must reside in GPU memory even though only K of E experts are active per token, so memory and inter-device all-to-all communication both scale with total parameter count E, not active parameters K. Scaling laws show that lowering the activation ratio K/E improves quality-per-FLOP, which pushes practitioners toward very large E — but the cost of training from scratch grows proportionally. There is no existing method that grows expert count within an already-sparse MoE while preserving inference cost (top-K fixed), closing the gap to a from-scratch larger model within a limited training budget. Key Idea: Duplicate experts from a trained E-expert MoE checkpoint to construct a warm-initialized mE-expert model. Because the duplicated experts inherit learned weight vectors and the router is extended with near-identical entries (plus small bias noise), the expanded model starts at approximately the same loss as the source — far below random initialization. Loss-free load balancing then ensures every replica receives gradient signal, and stochastic gradient diversity drives replicas to specialize over subsequent CPT. The core insight is that warm initialization makes Term II of the quality-gap bound negative (closer to optimum than random init), which more than compensates for the time spent in a less expressive model class (Term I). Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. Phase 1: train an E-expert MoE for τ steps using a Warmup–Stable–Decay schedule with loss-free load balancing. Phase 2: apply the upcycling operator U_m — assign replication counts {r_e} to experts (uniform: r_e=m for all e; utility-based: higher counts for experts with larger gradient-norm scores ‖g_e‖²), copy each expert r_e times, extend the router by copying source logits and adding small uniform bias noise ε~U(−δ,δ) to replicated slots only. Phase 3: continue pre-training the mE-expert model for T−τ steps. The theoretical framework uses the online convex optimization (OCO) regret-telescoping technique to decompose the quality gap L̄_up − L̄_fs into (I) a capacity term proportional to (Σ_{t<τ} η_t / Σ_all η_t) × (L_E − L_mE) — penalizing time in the smaller model class — and (II) an initialization gain (‖θ^U_+ − θ_+‖² − ‖θ^0_+ − θ_+‖²) / (2Ση_t) — which is negative when the operator places new parameters closer to the optimum than random init does. Critical design decisions: (a) Warm initialization via exact copying rather than perturbation — justified empirically: Spearman ρ=0.80 between initial and final validation loss across 65 runs confirms that better initialization consistently produces better final models. (b) Gradient-norm utility scores ‖g_e‖² for non-uniform replication — justified theoretically via first-order Taylor expansion and empirically via ablation. (c) Loss-free load balancing throughout — critical because duplicated experts start with identical weights and would receive zero gradient without explicit routing bias adjustment. Key Results: Main experiment: 20-layer interleaved MoE, 7B→13B total / 1B active non-embedding parameters (32→64 experts, TopK=2, trained on 380B tokens). At 100% CPT (380B additional tokens), upcycled achieves validation loss 1.263 vs. Fixed-64's 1.267 and Fixed-32's 1.301; average accuracy 56.4 vs. 56.7 (Fixed-64) and 52.9 (Fixed-32) across 11 benchmarks (MMLU, BBH CoT, GSM8K, IFEval, HellaSwag, ARC-Challenge, ARC-Easy, PIQA, OpenBookQA, SciQ, Social IQA). GPU hours: 27,888 (upcycled) vs. 41,328 (Fixed-64), a 32% saving. At 50% CPT, upcycled already matches Fixed-64 on validation loss (1.305 vs. 1.308) and 4 of 6 reported benchmarks. Full MoE generalization: 256→512 experts, TopK=8, 154M–1B total parameters — gap closure 92.9–95.3% across all sizes. Utility-based duplication vs. uniform: at 25% CPT, gap closure 26.5% vs. 8.2% (3.2× improvement); advantage narrows at 100% CPT. Sparse upcycling (dense→MoE) comparison: expert upcycling (MoE→MoE) outperforms sparse upcycling at all activation ratios, with the gap widening from 0.026 at 25% activation ratio to 0.241 at 3.13%. Transition timing: upcycling at τ/T ≤ 0.25 achieves 94–100% efficiency under fixed total budget. CPT budget sweep: at least 50% CPT budget required for strong gap closure; 10% CPT achieves only 34.7% efficiency. Heuristic diversity methods (20 variants across expert and router levels) uniformly fail to exceed copy-paste by more than 10⁻³ in validation loss. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The method of progressively growing MoE expert count within the MoE regime while holding top-K fixed is genuinely novel relative to prior work: sparse upcycling converts dense→MoE, SPARKLING grows MoE width but increases active parameters, and progressive training methods (Net2Net, stacking) grow dense networks. The gradient-norm-guided non-uniform duplication is a meaningful new operator. The empirical finding that warm initialization quality (not diversity) determines final model quality — formalized by the Spearman ρ=0.80/0.86 correlation — is a concrete, non-obvious contribution that extends to adjacent work on model initialization. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and structured around the theory, covering transition timing (5 points), CPT budget (5 points), operator design (6 strategies), activation ratio (4 levels), and architecture (interleaved vs. full MoE). The theory-to-experiment mapping is explicit: each ablation tests a specific prediction of Theorem 3.1. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported — standard practice for pretraining-scale experiments but a limitation for the 7B main result. The authors use disjoint pre-training and CPT data splits, which correctly avoids data leakage. Ablation experiments use DCLM; the 7B main experiment uses a curated internal mixture emphasizing instruction following, reasoning, and math — this data mixture difference means the 7B result may not be directly comparable to the ablation recipe. Code and training configurations are released at https://github.com/amazon-science/expert-upcycling. This is a preprint; the results have not undergone peer review. The convexity assumption underlying Theorem 3.1 is explicitly acknowledged as non-literal and the authors point to empirical justification from [45, 4] for the theory's predictive validity. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on NVIDIA A100 GPU clusters using standard data parallelism and tensor parallelism; no specialized hardware required beyond what frontier MoE training already demands. - Efficient: The 7B→13B experiment measures smE ≈ 1.9×sE per step; expert upcycling saves ~32% GPU hours vs. fixed-size training when τ≈2/3T, and ~67% when starting from an existing checkpoint. The operator itself (gradient computation + expert duplication) is a one-time cost at transition step τ. - Effective: At 100% CPT, the upcycled model matches Fixed-64 on both validation loss and 11 downstream benchmarks; the evidence that it solves the stated problem (quality-matched, compute-efficient capacity expansion) is direct and convincing at the tested scale. - Scalable: The method is validated only at up to 7B total parameters and expansion factor m=2 (doubling); the authors explicitly acknowledge that scaling to frontier models, larger m, or distribution shift between pre-training and CPT may introduce router collapse or balancing brittleness. No experiments at 70B+ total parameters are reported. The software artifact (code + training configs) is publicly released. Strengths: - The theoretical framework is tightly coupled to the experimental design: Term I and Term II of Theorem 3.1 each have dedicated ablation tables, and the framework correctly predicts the qualitative reversal (upcycling efficiency increases with pre-training duration in MoE→MoE, opposite to dense→MoE) observed in Table 3a vs. the scaling-law findings of Liew et al. [32]. - The counterintuitive finding that diversity-inducing initialization (20 methods) uniformly fails to beat copy-paste is well-documented across expert and router levels; the Spearman rank-correlation analysis (ρ=0.80/0.86, n=65 runs) quantitatively establishes that initialization quality dominates over initialization diversity, which is a non-obvious and broadly applicable insight. - The comparison to sparse upcycling (dense→MoE) across four activation ratios is a direct competitive test that contextualizes when expert upcycling is preferable; the widening gap at lower activation ratios (0.026→0.241) is a clean, predictable result grounded in Term I. - Code and training configurations are released, enabling practical adoption. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that experiments validate m=2 (doubling) only; larger expansion factors and frontier model scales may introduce new failure modes (router collapse, balancing brittleness). They acknowledge that distribution shift between pre-training and CPT is an unaddressed risk. The theoretical convexity assumption is directly confronted and its non-literalness acknowledged. These are confronted credibly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 7B main experiment uses a curated CPT data mixture biased toward instruction following and math, while ablations use DCLM — this confounds the data effect with the architectural effect, making it unclear how much of the strong 7B result is attributable to the CPT data quality versus the upcycling method itself. No evaluation of inference throughput or latency is provided; the paper focuses on training compute, but the implication that inference cost is unchanged assumes identical deployment parallelism, which may not hold when expert count doubles and communication patterns change. Downstream benchmark evaluation is absent for all ablation runs (only validation loss), making it impossible to verify that gap closure in perplexity translates to downstream improvement at the 1B ablation scale — the authors justify this by citing [54] on emergent abilities, but this is a weak defense given that several benchmarks (ARC, HellaSwag) are reliably informative at 1B. No analysis of expert specialization post-upcycling (e.g., expert utilization entropy, routing overlap between duplicated pairs) is provided, leaving the claimed symmetry-breaking mechanism unobserved directly. Bottom Line: A practitioner training or upgrading MoE models gains concrete value from this work: the practical recipe (upcycle at τ/T ≤ 0.25, budget ≥ 50% CPT, use gradient-norm importance scores) is actionable, the 32% compute saving is well-supported at 7B scale, and the code is public — but the absence of experiments beyond m=2 and 7B total parameters means adopters at frontier scale are extrapolating beyond the evidence.

Learning the Signature of Memorization in Autoregressive Language Models

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LT-MIA (Learned Transfer MIA), the first transferable learned membership inference attack for fine-tuned language models, trained exclusively on transformers yet transferring zero-shot to architecturally unrelated models. - Why it's important: Copyright holders, privacy regulators, and data governance practitioners need to determine whether specific text was used to fine-tune a deployed model, but every prior attack is a hand-crafted heuristic bounded by the designer's intuition and incapable of improvement through learning. - How it does it: Fine-tuning any model on any corpus creates unlimited labeled membership data by construction; training a lightweight transformer classifier over diverse model-dataset combinations forces it to learn only the memorization signal shared across architectures — the relational shift in per-token distributions between fine-tuned target and pre-trained reference — while filtering architecture-specific artifacts. - The good: LT-MIA achieves 0.963/0.972/0.936 AUC on unseen Mamba-2.8B/RWKV-4-3B/RecurrentGemma-2B respectively, exceeding its own held-out transformer mean (0.908 AUC) and delivering 2.8× higher TPR@0.1% FPR versus the strongest baseline (EZ-MIA) on transformers. - The missing: The attack is validated only on supervised cross-entropy fine-tuning; extension to RLHF, DPO, instruction tuning, and pretraining membership — arguably more important practical threat models — is untested. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone working on LLM privacy, copyright auditing, or security; the architecture-invariance finding is a fundamental result that reframes what constitutes a necessary defense.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LT-MIA (Learned Transfer MIA), the first transferable learned membership inference attack for fine-tuned language models, trained exclusively on transformers yet transferring zero-shot to architecturally unrelated models. - Why it's important: Copyright holders, privacy regulators, and data governance practitioners need to determine whether specific text was used to fine-tune a deployed model, but every prior attack is a hand-crafted heuristic bounded by the designer's intuition and incapable of improvement through learning. - How it does it: Fine-tuning any model on any corpus creates unlimited labeled membership data by construction; training a lightweight transformer classifier over diverse model-dataset combinations forces it to learn only the memorization signal shared across architectures — the relational shift in per-token distributions between fine-tuned target and pre-trained reference — while filtering architecture-specific artifacts. - The good: LT-MIA achieves 0.963/0.972/0.936 AUC on unseen Mamba-2.8B/RWKV-4-3B/RecurrentGemma-2B respectively, exceeding its own held-out transformer mean (0.908 AUC) and delivering 2.8× higher TPR@0.1% FPR versus the strongest baseline (EZ-MIA) on transformers. - The missing: The attack is validated only on supervised cross-entropy fine-tuning; extension to RLHF, DPO, instruction tuning, and pretraining membership — arguably more important practical threat models — is untested. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone working on LLM privacy, copyright auditing, or security; the architecture-invariance finding is a fundamental result that reframes what constitutes a necessary defense. Problem & Motivation Membership inference attacks (MIAs) determine whether a specific text appeared in a model's training data, with applications in copyright enforcement, privacy auditing under GDPR, and data governance compliance. All existing MIA methods for language models are hand-crafted heuristics — loss thresholding, Min-K%, Zlib normalization, reference-based calibration (RefLoss), and EZ-MIA — each designed by human intuition with performance bounded by the designer's insight. Learned MIAs outperform heuristics in other domains (image classifiers) but require training many shadow models with known membership, which is prohibitively expensive at LLM scale. The implicit assumption blocking learned MIA from scaling to language models has been that shadow model training is a prerequisite. Key Idea The single enabling insight is that fine-tuning any model on any corpus produces unlimited labeled membership data by construction — the identity of members and non-members is known exactly — eliminating the shadow model bottleneck entirely. The cost of generating training data is only forward passes through existing fine-tuned models, not training new ones. A second, independent insight is that training diversity (many model-dataset combinations at fixed total data volume) filters architecture-specific artifacts — tokenizer idiosyncrasies, logit scale biases — leaving only the invariant memorization signal: the relational shift in per-token likelihood a fine-tuned model assigns relative to its pre-trained baseline. This invariance holds because gradient descent on cross-entropy loss is the only shared mechanism across transformers, state-space models, linear attention, and gated recurrence. Methodology The LT-MIA pipeline has three stages. First, per-token feature extraction: given black-box access to the fine-tuned target model and its pre-trained reference (same architecture), 154-dimensional feature vectors are extracted at each of T=128 token positions. Features span three groups — target model features (45 channels: per-token cross-entropy loss, top/bottom-20 logits, ground-truth logit and log-normalized rank, global loss mean/std broadcast to all positions), reference model features (45 channels, same structure), and comparison features (64 channels: per-token loss difference, log-likelihood ratio, cross-model ranks for top-20 and bottom-20 tokens from each model). Logit features are locally normalized by subtracting the within-group maximum; rank features are log-transformed. Second, a lightweight transformer encoder (2 layers, 4 heads, feedforward dim 256, <500K total parameters) processes the T×154 feature sequence with sinusoidal positional encodings; a learned query computes a weighted sum over encoder outputs fed to a 2-layer MLP classifier predicting P(member). Third, training: 10 transformer-base models fine-tuned on 3 text corpora yield 30 model-dataset combinations (540,000 samples); binary cross-entropy is optimized with AdamW (lr=3×10⁻⁴, batch 1024, 30 epochs). Critical design decisions: (1) sequence modeling over positional features rather than aggregation to a scalar — contributes 5.0 AUC points over mean-pooled MLP, well-justified by classifier architecture ablation; (2) 30 diverse combinations over 1 combination at identical data volume — reduces train-to-eval AUC gap from 20.2 to 0.2 points, well-justified by diversity ablation; (3) comparison features dominate over target-only or reference-only features — permutation importance confirms Comparison > Target > Reference hierarchy identically across all four architectural families, substantiating the relational interpretation. Key Results On 7 held-out transformers × 4 datasets (280 evaluation combinations), LT-MIA achieves 0.908 mean AUC versus EZ-MIA's 0.887 (+2.1 points) and RefLoss's 0.760 (+14.8 points); Wilcoxon signed-rank test p < 0.001 versus all baselines. At strict thresholds: LT-MIA achieves TPR@1% FPR of 0.375 versus EZ-MIA's 0.230 (1.6× improvement) and TPR@0.1% FPR of 0.171 versus 0.061 (2.8×). EZ-MIA's TPR@1% collapses to near-zero on WikiText-103 for most models while LT-MIA remains robust. Zero-shot transfer to non-transformers: Mamba-2.8B 0.963 AUC, RWKV-4-3B 0.972, RecurrentGemma-2B 0.936 — all exceeding the transformer holdout mean and the strongest baseline on each family. Domain transfer to code: 0.865 AUC on Swallow-Code despite training only on English natural language text. Even simple heuristics transfer: Loss achieves 0.867 AUC on Mamba, RefLoss reaches 0.892, confirming the signature is architecture-invariant independently of detection method. A notable weakness: on WikiText-103 with large models (Llama-2-7B: 0.781, Pythia-6.9B: 0.796) LT-MIA falls below its own average because the reference model already assigns high likelihood to Wikipedia text, reducing the relational signal. All experiments ran on a single NVIDIA H200 GPU in ~30 GPU-hours. This is a preprint; results are not peer reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The method genuinely removes the shadow model bottleneck for learned MIA at LLM scale — not a straightforward combination of existing ideas. The empirical finding that the memorization signature is architecture-invariant (transferring zero-shot across models sharing no computational mechanisms) is a substantive scientific result, not merely an engineering demonstration applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and specific: permutation importance for feature groups (across all four architectural families), classifier architecture comparison (Transformer vs. MLP vs. Logistic Regression, Table 3/11), and training diversity (Table 12/Figure 4). The diversity ablation uses fixed total data volume, correctly isolating the diversity effect. Significance testing (Wilcoxon signed-rank, p < 0.001) is reported for the main comparison. Complete per-model-per-dataset results for all 70 evaluation combinations are in the appendix (Tables 4–9). Zero overlap between training and evaluation model-dataset combinations is explicitly documented (Table 10). Code and trained classifier are publicly available (URL linked in paper but not printed in the PDF text). One concern: the classifier checkpoint is selected by validation AUC on held-out samples from the training distribution; the zero-shot transfer experiments compensate for this by using entirely different architectures and datasets, but validation set leakage into hyperparameter selection is not analyzed. Fine-tuning configuration (3 epochs, batch 16, sequence length 128, LoRA r=16 for most models) is narrow; behavior under different fine-tuning regimes is not tested. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Requires only black-box inference access (full vocabulary logits) to the fine-tuned target and its pre-trained reference checkpoint; applicable to any open-weight model on commercially available hardware; closed-weight APIs are out of scope. - Efficient: Feature extraction costs one forward pass per model per sample; the classifier is <500K parameters and trivially fast; total experimental cost was ~30 GPU-hours on a single H200 for 70 fine-tuning runs, feature extraction, and classifier training. - Effective: Demonstrates measurable improvement over all baselines on the stated problem (fine-tuning membership detection for open-weight models), most pronounced at low FPR operating points relevant to real auditing at scale; effectiveness under deployment conditions beyond cross-entropy fine-tuning is undemonstrated. - Scalable: Tested on models up to 7B parameters; the paper explicitly notes that gains from 100 or 1,000 training combinations remain unexplored, scaling laws for the classifier are an open question, and transfer to pretraining-scale membership inference is a different and harder problem. The work delivers a trained classifier artifact plus code. Strengths - The enabling insight — fine-tuning provides unlimited labeled data by construction — is simple, correct, and non-obvious; prior work missed it because the shadow model paradigm was treated as a definitional requirement for learned MIA rather than an approximation to labeled data. - Architecture-invariance is rigorously substantiated: permutation importance shows an identical feature hierarchy (Comparison > Target > Reference) independently across four families sharing no computational mechanisms, making the result qualitatively robust rather than anecdotal. - The diversity-versus-scale ablation is methodologically clean (fixed total data volume, 1 vs. 30 combinations) and directly explains the transfer mechanism while providing practical guidance. - Low-FPR evaluation (TPR@1% and TPR@0.1% FPR) exposes where EZ-MIA's calibration collapses in practice, providing a more honest assessment of operational utility than AUC alone. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) performance degrades when the reference model already assigns high likelihood to the target text (WikiText-103 with large models — fundamental to all reference-based attacks); (2) requires same-architecture pre-trained reference, available for open-weight models but not closed APIs; (3) validated only on supervised fine-tuning with cross-entropy loss; RLHF, DPO, instruction tuning, continual pretraining remain untested; (4) code-domain transfer underperforms natural language (0.865 vs. 0.908). The authors confront these limitations directly and do not minimize them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All target models are fine-tuned with a specific, relatively aggressive regime (3 epochs, batch 16, sequence length 128). Fine-tuning for 1 epoch or with very large batch sizes (which reduce per-sample gradient signal) could attenuate the memorization signature substantially; this is not tested. - The threat model assumes the adversary knows the architecture family to select the correct reference checkpoint.
